diseases.name: Chlamydia
diseases.management: Per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days is now the preferred treatment for urogenital, rectal, and pharyngeal chlamydia (superior microbiologic cure vs azithromycin, especially for rectal infection). (2) Azithromycin 1 g single dose is an alternative — preferred in pregnancy and when adherence is a concern. (3) Test-of-cure recommended for rectal chlamydia treated with azithromycin (14 days post-treatment). (4) Retest for reinfection at 3 months. (5) Treat sex partners. (6) Screen all sexually active women 25 years annually.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Azithromycin for rectal chlamydia without test-of-cure. Fluoroquinolones as first-line.

treatments.notes: preferred; superior to azithromycin for rectal infection
preferred in pregnancy; acceptable when adherence is a concern
for doxycycline and azithromycin intolerance

diseases.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
diseases.management: Per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Empiric treatment in sexually active women with unexplained lower abdominal/pelvic pain + cervical motion/uterine/adnexal tenderness. (2) Outpatient (mild-moderate): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose + doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days + metronidazole 500 mg BID × 14 days (metronidazole now routinely recommended). (3) Inpatient: cefotetan 2 g IV q12h or cefoxitin 2 g IV q6h + doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV q12h; alternative: clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + gentamicin loading then maintenance. (4) Hospitalize if: pregnant, tubo-ovarian abscess, unable to tolerate PO, surgical emergency not excluded, failed outpatient therapy. (5) IUD does NOT need to be removed. (6) Treat sex partners.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Removing IUD in PID (not necessary). Fluoroquinolone monotherapy (inadequate anaerobic coverage). Delay in empiric treatment.

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM × 1 + doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days + metronidazole 500 mg BID × 14 days
treatments.notes: metronidazole now routinely added

treatments.dosage: cefoxitin 2 g IV q6h + doxycycline 100 mg q12h
treatments.duration: until 24 hrs improved then PO × 14 days total
treatments.notes: transition to PO after 24 hrs improvement

treatments.dosage: clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + gentamicin 2 mg/kg load then 1.5 mg/kg q8h
treatments.duration: until 24 hrs improved then PO × 14 days total
treatments.notes: alternative parenteral regimen

diseases.name: Candidiasis
diseases.management: Per the 2016 IDSA Candidiasis Guideline and 2025 ECMM/ISHAM/ASM Global Guideline: (1) Oropharyngeal candidiasis (mild): clotrimazole troches 10 mg 5×/day or miconazole buccal tablet 50 mg daily × 7–14 days; nystatin suspension as alternative. Moderate-severe: fluconazole 100–200 mg daily × 7–14 days. (2) Vulvovaginal candidiasis (uncomplicated): topical azole (miconazole, clotrimazole) × 1–7 days or fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose. (3) Recurrent VVC (≥3 episodes/year): fluconazole 150 mg weekly × 6 months; oteseconazole (FDA-approved for RVVC in non-reproductive-potential females). (4) Esophageal candidiasis: fluconazole 200–400 mg daily × 14–21 days; echinocandin for fluconazole-refractory. (5) Invasive candidiasis: echinocandin first-line (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin); fluconazole for step-down if susceptible.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fluconazole in pregnancy (teratogenic risk). Echinocandins for CNS or ocular candidiasis (poor penetration). Probiotics for VVC prevention (no evidence).

treatments.notes: first-line systemic
topical
equally effective as topical azoles
FDA-approved for RVVC prevention

treatments.dosage: caspofungin 70 mg load then 50 mg daily, or micafungin 100 mg daily
treatments.duration: 14 days after first negative blood culture
treatments.notes: first-line for candidemia

diseases.name: Herpes Simplex (HSV-1/HSV-2)
diseases.management: Per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) First episode genital herpes: valacyclovir 1 g BID × 7–10 days, or acyclovir 400 mg TID × 7–10 days, or famciclovir 250 mg TID × 7–10 days. (2) Recurrent episodes: valacyclovir 500 mg BID × 3 days or 1 g daily × 5 days; acyclovir 800 mg TID × 2 days. (3) Suppressive therapy (≥6 outbreaks/year or discordant couples): valacyclovir 500 mg–1 g daily or acyclovir 400 mg BID. (4) Severe/disseminated (immunocompromised): IV acyclovir 5–10 mg/kg q8h. (5) Diagnosis: type-specific PCR of lesion preferred over culture.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Valacyclovir 500 mg daily for suppression in patients with >9 outbreaks/year (use 1 g daily). Culture alone for diagnosis (PCR preferred).

treatments.notes: better bioavailability than acyclovir
equally effective
reduces transmission by 48%
for immunocompromised or disseminated

diseases.name: Herpes Zoster
diseases.management: Per the 2013 NEJM Review and ATA guidelines: (1) Antiviral therapy within 72 hours of rash onset: valacyclovir 1 g TID × 7 days (preferred — better bioavailability), or famciclovir 500 mg TID × 7 days, or acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day × 7 days. (2) Treat all immunocompromised patients and those ≥50 years regardless of timing. (3) Severe/disseminated: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h. (4) Pain management: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, gabapentin/pregabalin for acute neuritis; TCAs, gabapentin, pregabalin, or capsaicin for postherpetic neuralgia. (5) Prevention: Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) for all adults ≥50 years and immunocompromised adults ≥19 years (2 doses).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroids alone without antivirals. Antivirals started >72 hours after rash in immunocompetent patients with mild disease (limited benefit). Live zoster vaccine (Zostavax — discontinued).

treatments.notes: preferred; superior to acyclovir for pain reduction

less convenient dosing

diseases.name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia
diseases.management: Per the 2019 ATS/IDSA Guideline and 2025 ATS Update: (1) Outpatient, healthy, no comorbidities: amoxicillin, doxycycline, or macrolide (only if local pneumococcal resistance 25%). (2) Outpatient with comorbidities: respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy OR β-lactam (amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalosporin) + macrolide or doxycycline. (3) Inpatient, non-severe: β-lactam (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam) + macrolide (preferred) OR respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy. (4) Inpatient, severe (ICU): β-lactam + macrolide (preferred over β-lactam + FQ). (5) Duration: minimum 3 days; stop when clinically stable ≥48 hours. (6) Corticosteroids: may reduce mortality in severe CAP (dexamethasone 6 mg daily × 4 days). (7) Test for influenza and COVID-19 when circulating. (8) Do NOT routinely add MRSA or Pseudomonas coverage unless risk factors + local data support it.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Macrolide monotherapy if local pneumococcal resistance ≥25%. Routine MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage without risk factors. Fluoroquinolone as first-line when β-lactam + macrolide is tolerated. Procalcitonin to withhold initial antibiotics. | Fluoroquinolones as first-line in healthy outpatients (C. difficile risk, tendon rupture, resistance). Routine anti-MRSA/anti-Pseudomonas coverage without risk factors. Corticosteroids for non-severe CAP (2025 ATS: recommend against). Prolonged antibiotic courses beyond clinical stability.

treatments.notes: narrow-spectrum
covers atypicals

treatments.dosage: amox-clav 875 mg BID + azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2–5
treatments.notes: combination therapy
may reduce 28-day mortality in severe CAP

diseases.name: Infective Endocarditis
diseases.management: Per the 2020 ACC/AHA VHD Guideline and 2023 Duke-ISCVID Criteria: (1) Diagnosis: Modified Duke Criteria (updated 2023 to include PET/CT, cardiac CT, and expanded microbiology). (2) Obtain ≥3 sets blood cultures before antibiotics. (3) TTE first; TEE if TTE nondiagnostic, prosthetic valve, or suspected complications. (4) Native valve, MSSA: nafcillin/oxacillin 2 g IV q4h × 6 weeks (preferred over vancomycin). (5) Native valve, MRSA: vancomycin or daptomycin × 6 weeks. (6) Streptococcal IE: penicillin G or ceftriaxone × 4 weeks (2 weeks if add gentamicin). (7) Enterococcal IE: ampicillin + ceftriaxone (preferred in elderly/renal impairment) or ampicillin + gentamicin × 6 weeks. (8) Oral step-down: POET trial showed non-inferiority of oral switch after ≥10 days IV in stable left-sided IE. (9) Early surgery for: uncontrolled infection, HF from valvular dysfunction, large vegetations (>10 mm) with embolic events, or abscess. (10) Multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vancomycin for MSSA IE (inferior outcomes). Empiric antibiotics before blood cultures. Delay in surgical consultation for complicated IE. Daptomycin for left-sided IE (inactivated by surfactant in lungs). | Combination of antistaphylococcal penicillin + gentamicin or rifampin for native valve MSSA IE (no benefit, increased toxicity). Avoid DOACs in prosthetic valve IE (use warfarin if mechanical valve). Avoid daptomycin for left-sided IE caused by MSSA when beta-lactams can be used.

treatments.notes: preferred over vancomycin for MSSA
target AUC/MIC 400–600
can shorten to 2 weeks with gentamicin

treatments.dosage: ampicillin 2 g q4h + ceftriaxone 2 g q12h
treatments.notes: preferred in elderly/renal impairment; avoids aminoglycoside toxicity

diseases.name: Clostridioides difficile Infection
diseases.management: CDI management per the 2021 IDSA/SHEA Focused Update and 2026 AGA Clinical Practice Update: (1) Diagnosis: clinical symptoms (acute diarrhea, typically after antibiotics) + laboratory confirmation; multistep testing preferred (GDH/toxin EIA then NAAT if discordant). (2) Non-severe initial CDI: fidaxomicin 200 mg BID × 10 days (preferred — lower recurrence than vancomycin) or oral vancomycin 125 mg QID × 10 days. Metronidazole should NOT be used except in fulminant disease. (3) Severe CDI (WBC >15,000 or Cr >1.5 mg/dL): fidaxomicin or vancomycin at standard doses. (4) Fulminant CDI (shock, ileus, megacolon): high-dose vancomycin 500 mg QID PO/NG + IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h + rectal vancomycin if ileus; surgical consultation. (5) First recurrence: fidaxomicin or vancomycin taper. (6) Second or subsequent recurrence: fecal microbiota-based therapy (FMT, fecal microbiota spores live-brpk [Vowst], fecal microbiota live-jslm [Rebyota]) after antibiotic treatment. (7) Discontinue inciting antibiotic if possible. (8) Do NOT perform test of cure.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Metronidazole for non-fulminant CDI (inferior). Test-of-cure after treatment. Vancomycin prophylaxis during systemic antibiotics. Probiotics for CDI prevention. PPIs without clear indication in CDI patients.

treatments.notes: preferred; lower recurrence than vancomycin
acceptable; lower cost

treatments.dosage: 125 mg QID × 10–14 days → BID × 7 days → daily × 7 days → q2–3 days × 2–8 weeks
treatments.notes: for first recurrence

treatments.dosage: 200 mg BID days 1–5, then 200 mg daily on alternate days 7–25

diseases.name: Type 1 Diabetes
diseases.management: Per the 2021 ADA/EASD Consensus Report and 2026 ADA Standards of Care: (1) Insulin therapy is essential — basal-bolus regimen (MDI) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/pump). (2) Basal insulin: glargine, detemir, or degludec (analog insulins preferred — less hypoglycemia than NPH). (3) Bolus insulin: lispro, aspart, or glulisine before meals. (4) HbA1c target: 7% for most adults; individualize. (5) CGM recommended for all patients on insulin (reduces hypoglycemia, improves TIR). (6) Hybrid closed-loop (automated insulin delivery) systems reduce hypoglycemia and improve TIR. (7) Adjunctive therapies: SGLT2 inhibitors (sotagliflozin) may reduce HbA1c but increase DKA risk — use with caution. (8) Hypoglycemia treatment: 15 g fast-acting carbohydrate for glucose 70 mg/dL; glucagon for severe hypoglycemia (nasal or auto-injector preferred). (9) Screen for complications annually.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sulfonylureas (no role in T1D). SGLT2 inhibitors without DKA risk counseling. Withholding basal insulin during illness (precipitates DKA). Overtreatment of hypoglycemia (rebound hyperglycemia).

treatments.notes: analog insulins preferred; less hypoglycemia than NPH
bolus insulin for prandial coverage

diseases.name: Type 2 Diabetes
diseases.management: Per the 2025 ADA Standards, 2022 ADA/EASD Consensus, 2024 ACP Guideline, and 2025 JAMA Review: (1) HbA1c target: 10%, or catabolic features. (9) Deprioritize sulfonylureas and insulin when SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA available (inferior for mortality/morbidity).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sulfonylureas as first-line when SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA available (inferior outcomes). Insulin before GLP-1 RA trial. SGLT2i in patients with recurrent DKA or type 1 diabetes without careful monitoring. Metformin if eGFR 30.

treatments.notes: first-line without cardiorenal comorbidities; titrate slowly
independent of HbA1c; cardiorenal benefit

diseases.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
diseases.management: Per the 2024 ADA/EASD/JBDS/AACE/DTS Consensus Report and 2026 ADA Standards: (1) Diagnostic criteria: glucose >250 mg/dL (or euglycemic DKA 12. (2) IV fluids: NS 1–1.5 L/hr initially; switch to 0.45% NS when Na normalizes; add D5 when glucose 200. (3) Insulin: regular insulin 0.1–0.14 U/kg/hr IV infusion (no bolus needed if 0.14 U/kg/hr); OR subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin q1–2h for mild DKA. (4) Potassium: replace if K 5.3 (20–40 mEq/L in IV fluids); hold insulin if K 3.3 until corrected. (5) Transition to SC insulin: give basal insulin 2–4 hours before stopping IV insulin to prevent rebound. (6) Bicarbonate: NOT routinely recommended (consider only if pH 6.9). (7) Identify and treat precipitant (infection, medication non-adherence, new-onset T1D).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bicarbonate unless pH 6.9. Stopping IV insulin before SC insulin has been given for 2–4 hours. Insulin without potassium replacement if K 3.3. Excessive fluid resuscitation in young adults (cerebral edema risk).

treatments.notes: switch to 0.45% NS when Na normalizes; add D5 when glucose 200
no bolus needed at 0.14 U/kg/hr rate
safe and cost-effective for mild DKA
hold insulin if K 3.3

treatments.dosage: give 2–4 hrs before stopping IV insulin
treatments.notes: prevents rebound hyperglycemia and recurrent DKA

diseases.name: Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State
diseases.management: Per the 2024 ADA/EASD Consensus Report: (1) Diagnostic criteria: glucose >600 mg/dL, serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, minimal ketones. (2) More severe volume depletion than DKA (average 9 L deficit). (3) Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the priority: NS 1–1.5 L/hr initially; switch to 0.45% NS when Na normalizes. (4) Insulin: lower doses than DKA (0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr); some experts recommend fluids first before insulin to avoid rapid osmolality shifts. (5) Potassium replacement as in DKA. (6) Monitor serum osmolality — avoid correcting >3 mOsm/kg/hr (risk of cerebral edema). (7) Mortality higher than DKA (0.7–1%). (8) Mixed DKA-HHS has highest mortality.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Rapid correction of osmolality (>3 mOsm/kg/hr — cerebral edema risk). High-dose insulin before adequate fluid resuscitation. Premature discharge without transition to SC insulin and education.

treatments.notes: average 9 L deficit
lower rate than DKA; some start fluids first
same principles as DKA

diseases.name: Hypertension
diseases.management: Hypertension (SBP ≥130 or DBP ≥80 mmHg) management begins with lifestyle modification for all patients: weight loss, DASH-type diet with sodium reduction and potassium supplementation, regular physical activity, and moderation/elimination of alcohol. Pharmacotherapy is initiated for SBP ≥140/DBP ≥90 in all adults, or at SBP 130–139/DBP 80–89 in those at high CVD risk (diabetes, CKD, established CVD, 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%, or age ≥65 with SBP ≥130). Target is 130/80 mmHg. For stage 2 hypertension (>20/10 mmHg above target), start two first-line agents from different classes.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ACE inhibitor + ARB combination (hyperkalemia, renal injury, no added benefit). Avoid ACEi/ARBs in pregnancy.

treatments.notes: preferred in uncomplicated HTN
preferred thiazide-like diuretic, longer acting
calcium channel blocker, good in elderly/Black patients
ACE inhibitor, preferred in diabetes/CKD/proteinuria
ARB, use when ACEi not tolerated (cough/angioedema)
add-on for resistant hypertension

diseases.name: Hypertensive Emergency
diseases.management: Hypertensive emergency is defined as BP >180/120 mmHg with evidence of acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, AKI, acute HF/pulmonary edema, aortic dissection, ACS, ICH). Requires ICU admission with continuous BP monitoring and parenteral antihypertensive therapy. For aortic dissection: reduce SBP to 120 mmHg within 20 minutes with beta-blocker + vasodilator. For most other emergencies without a compelling condition: reduce SBP by no more than 25% in the first hour, then to 160/100 over 2–6 hours, then cautiously to 130–140 over 24–48 hours. Severe hypertension (>180/120) WITHOUT target organ damage (formerly "urgency") should NOT receive aggressive IV lowering — reinstitute/intensify oral medications in the outpatient setting.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Hydralazine in encephalopathy, ICH, and ischemic stroke (unpredictable BP drops, reflex tachycardia). Avoid beta-blockers in acute HF. Avoid aggressive IV lowering in severe HTN without target organ damage.

treatments.notes: most emergencies; more effective than labetalol at reaching short-term BP target

treatments.dosage: 20 mg IV bolus then 1–2 mg/min infusion

diseases.name: HFrEF
diseases.management: HFrEF (EF ≤40%) management centers on the "4 pillars" of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT): ARNI (preferred) or ACEi/ARB, evidence-based beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid antagonist (MRA), and SGLT2 inhibitor. All four should be initiated early and titrated to target doses within ~3 months of diagnosis. Compared with no treatment, quadruple therapy reduces the risk of death by ~73% over 2 years. Loop diuretics are used for congestion/symptom relief but have no mortality benefit. Additional therapies for persistent symptoms include hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate (especially in self-identified Black patients), ivabradine (sinus rhythm, HR ≥70 on max beta-blocker), and vericiguat (higher-risk patients). Reassess EF 3–6 months after achieving target GDMT to determine need for ICD/CRT.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Metoprolol tartrate (use succinate only for HFrEF). Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) — negative inotropic effect. NSAIDs — fluid retention, worsening HF. Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone, rosiglitazone) — fluid retention.

treatments.notes: ARNI preferred over ACEi/ARB; 36-hr washout from ACEi before switching

diseases.name: HFpEF
diseases.management: HFpEF (EF ≥50%) is a heterogeneous syndrome driven by comorbidities including hypertension, diabetes, obesity, AF, CAD, and CKD. Management focuses on: (1) SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line disease-modifying therapy (reduced HF hospitalization/CV death by 18–21% vs placebo in EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER trials); (2) loop diuretics for congestion/symptom relief; (3) exercise training and weight loss (clinically meaningful improvements in functional capacity and quality of life); (4) aggressive comorbidity management (BP control, glycemic control, AF management, OSA treatment); (5) MRAs may reduce HF hospitalizations, particularly in those with lower-range EF (50–60%); (6) ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) may benefit those with EF closer to 50%. No therapy has demonstrated mortality reduction in HFpEF.
diseases.notes: Avoid: No specific drugs to avoid unique to HFpEF beyond general HF precautions (avoid NSAIDs, limit fluid/sodium). Non-dihydropyridine CCBs are NOT contraindicated in HFpEF (unlike HFrEF).

treatments.notes: SGLT2i; Class I recommendation
SGLT2i; Class I recommendation
loop diuretic for congestion relief

diseases.name: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
diseases.management: ADHF management begins with rapid identification of precipitants (medication nonadherence, ischemia, arrhythmia, infection, dietary indiscretion). Classify by clinical profile: wet/dry (congestion) and warm/cold (perfusion). Initial treatment focuses on decongestion with IV loop diuretics — starting IV dose should be ≥2.5× the chronic daily oral dose (DOSE trial). SGLT2 inhibitors should be added early to improve diuretic efficiency. Vasodilators (nitroglycerin, nitroprusside) are considered when hypertension precipitates the episode. Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) are reserved for cardiogenic shock/hypoperfusion. Before discharge, rapidly initiate/optimize oral GDMT (4 pillars for HFrEF). Close follow-up within 7–14 days post-discharge is critical.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aggressive IV BP lowering in the absence of hypertensive emergency. Avoid initiating or up-titrating beta-blockers during acute decompensation (may worsen hemodynamics). Avoid NSAIDs. Avoid non-dihydropyridine CCBs in HFrEF.

treatments.notes: cornerstone of decongestion

diseases.name: Stable Angina
diseases.management: Stable angina (chronic coronary disease with exertional chest pain) management includes: (1) lifestyle modification (exercise, smoking cessation, weight management); (2) preventive pharmacotherapy — aspirin 75–100 mg daily, high-intensity statin, BP control; (3) antianginal therapy for symptom relief with beta-blocker, CCB, or long-acting nitrate as first-line (all Class I); (4) if symptoms persist, add a second agent from a different class; (5) ranolazine for refractory symptoms despite first-line agents; (6) sublingual nitroglycerin for acute episodes; (7) revascularization (PCI or CABG) for refractory symptoms or high-risk anatomy. The 2023 AHA/ACC CCD Guideline emphasizes that no single antianginal class is superior — selection should be individualized based on HR, BP, LV function, and comorbidities.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Ivabradine in patients with CCD and normal LV function — potentially harmful (Class III: Harm per 2023 AHA/ACC). Short-acting nifedipine (reflex tachycardia, hypotension). Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors within 24 hrs of nitrates (48 hrs for tadalafil).

treatments.notes: beta-blocker; preferred if prior MI or HFrEF
dihydropyridine CCB; preferred if low HR, Raynaud, or vasospastic angina
non-DHP CCB; avoid in HFrEF
long-acting nitrate; provide 12-hr nitrate-free interval

diseases.name: NSTEMI
diseases.management: NSTEMI management includes: (1) immediate dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) — aspirin loading 162–325 mg then 81 mg daily + P2Y12 inhibitor; (2) parenteral anticoagulation (UFH, enoxaparin, or bivalirudin); (3) risk stratification — early invasive strategy (angiography within 24 hrs) for moderate-to-high risk patients (elevated troponin, ECG changes, GRACE score >140); (4) conservative/ischemia-guided strategy for low-risk patients; (5) ~60% undergo PCI, ~10% CABG, ~30% medical therapy alone; (6) post-discharge: DAPT for 12 months (may shorten to 1–3 months if high bleeding risk), high-intensity statin, beta-blocker (if LV dysfunction), ACEi/ARB, cardiac rehabilitation. The 2025 ACC/AHA ACS Guideline reinforces early invasive approach for high-risk NSTEMI.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prasugrel in patients with prior stroke/TIA (net harm). NSAIDs other than aspirin. Routine GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors upstream (use selectively at time of PCI). Calcium channel blockers (verapamil/diltiazem) if wide-complex tachycardia of unknown origin.

diseases.name: STEMI
diseases.management: STEMI requires emergent reperfusion. Primary PCI (door-to-balloon ≤90 min at PCI-capable hospital, or first medical contact-to-device ≤120 min with transfer) is the preferred strategy and reduces mortality by ~2% absolute vs fibrinolysis. If PCI cannot be achieved within 120 min, administer fibrinolytic therapy (tenecteplase preferred — single weight-based IV bolus; half-dose for age ≥75) followed by transfer for angiography within 3–24 hrs. DAPT: aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel). Anticoagulation with UFH or bivalirudin during PCI. Drug-eluting stents are standard. Post-STEMI: beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, high-intensity statin, DAPT × 12 months, cardiac rehab, assess LVEF at 40 days for ICD eligibility.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fibrinolytics if active bleeding, recent stroke (3 months), intracranial hemorrhage, aortic dissection, or uncontrolled HTN. Avoid routine supplemental O2 if SpO2 ≥90%. Avoid NSAIDs. Avoid verapamil/diltiazem if LV dysfunction.

treatments.notes: preferred reperfusion strategy
fibrinolytic; half-dose if ≥75 yrs; now also FDA-approved for acute ischemic stroke
give immediately
preferred P2Y12 inhibitor for STEMI
preferred for STEMI undergoing PCI; avoid if prior stroke/TIA

diseases.name: Atrial Fibrillation
diseases.management: AF management follows the 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline framework of 4 stages. Key pillars: (1) Lifestyle/risk factor modification for ALL stages — weight loss, exercise, alcohol reduction, OSA treatment, BP control; (2) Stroke prevention — anticoagulation with DOAC (preferred over warfarin) for patients with estimated annual stroke risk ≥2% (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 in men, ≥3 in women); DOACs reduce stroke by 60–80% vs placebo; aspirin is NOT recommended for stroke prevention; (3) Rate control — target resting HR 110 bpm (lenient) or 80 bpm (strict) with beta-blockers, diltiazem/verapamil, or digoxin; (4) Rhythm control — early rhythm control is now emphasized; catheter ablation is first-line for symptomatic paroxysmal AF and for AF with HFrEF; antiarrhythmic drugs (flecainide, propafenone, sotalol, dofetilide, amiodarone) selected based on structural heart disease and comorbidities.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aspirin monotherapy for stroke prevention (inferior to anticoagulation, similar bleeding risk). Flecainide/propafenone in structural heart disease or ischemic heart disease. Dronedarone in permanent AF or NYHA III–IV HF. Verapamil/diltiazem in pre-excited AF (WPW).

treatments.dosage: 5 mg (2.5 mg if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, Cr ≥1.5)
treatments.notes: DOAC for stroke prevention; preferred over warfarin

diseases.name: Atrial Flutter
diseases.management: Atrial flutter (typical = CTI-dependent, ~300 bpm atrial rate with sawtooth pattern) management parallels AF in many respects. Key principles: (1) Anticoagulation follows the same stroke risk assessment and DOAC recommendations as AF; (2) Rate control is often MORE difficult than in AF due to less concealed AV nodal conduction — higher doses of beta-blockers, diltiazem, or verapamil (or combinations) may be needed; (3) Catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus (CTI) is highly effective (>95% success) and is recommended as first-line for symptomatic or rate-refractory typical flutter (Class I); (4) Acute cardioversion: IV ibutilide or dofetilide for pharmacological cardioversion; synchronized electrical cardioversion (often at lower energy, 50–100 J) for hemodynamically unstable patients; rapid atrial pacing if pacing wires available.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Flecainide/propafenone without AV nodal blocker (can organize flutter to slower rate with 1:1 conduction → hemodynamic collapse). Avoid beta-blockers, diltiazem, verapamil in pre-excited flutter. Avoid Class IC agents in structural heart disease.

treatments.notes: definitive treatment for typical (CTI-dependent) flutter; >95% success rate
rate control; higher doses often needed

diseases.name: SVT (Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia)
diseases.management: PSVT (most commonly AVNRT or AVRT) management: (1) Acute — vagal maneuvers first (modified Valsalva 43% effective); if unsuccessful, IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push → 12 mg → 12 mg (91% effective); if adenosine fails, IV verapamil 5 mg or diltiazem 20 mg; synchronized cardioversion for hemodynamically unstable patients; (2) Long-term prevention — catheter ablation is first-line (94–99% single-procedure success, Class I recommendation); pharmacotherapy (beta-blockers, CCBs, or antiarrhythmics) for patients who decline or are not candidates for ablation; (3) Etripamil (intranasal CCB, FDA-approved 2025) is a new self-administered option for acute PSVT termination.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Verapamil/diltiazem in wide-complex tachycardia of unknown origin (may be VT → hemodynamic collapse). Adenosine in pre-excited AF/flutter (WPW). IV beta-blockers + IV CCBs simultaneously (severe bradycardia/hypotension).

treatments.notes: first intervention; 43% effective

diseases.name: Ventricular Tachycardia
diseases.management: VT management depends on hemodynamic stability and underlying substrate. (1) Acute — hemodynamically unstable: immediate synchronized cardioversion (or defibrillation if pulseless VT/VF); hemodynamically stable sustained monomorphic VT: IV procainamide (preferred per ESC, PROCAMIO trial), IV amiodarone, or IV lidocaine; VT storm: IV amiodarone + beta-blocker (propranolol or esmolol), sedation, consider urgent catheter ablation; (2) Long-term — ICD for secondary prevention in patients with structural heart disease and sustained VT/VF (Class I); antiarrhythmic drugs (amiodarone or sotalol) to reduce recurrent VT/ICD shocks; catheter ablation for recurrent VT despite AAD therapy (VANISH trial: ablation superior to escalated AAD); (3) Idiopathic VT (structurally normal heart): beta-blockers or verapamil (for fascicular VT); catheter ablation curative in most cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Verapamil/diltiazem in VT with structural heart disease (hemodynamic collapse). Class IC agents (flecainide, propafenone) in ischemic heart disease or structural heart disease. Prophylactic lidocaine or high-dose amiodarone in suspected AMI without VT (Class III: Harm).

treatments.notes: for hemodynamically unstable VT or pulseless VT/VF
until VT terminates or max dose | preferred for stable monomorphic VT (PROCAMIO trial); avoid in severe HF

treatments.dosage: 150 mg IV over 10 min → 1 mg/min × 6 hrs → 0.5 mg/min

diseases.name: Bradyarrhythmia / Heart Block
diseases.management: Bradyarrhythmias include sinus node dysfunction (SND) and atrioventricular (AV) block. Management depends on symptoms and etiology. (1) Identify and correct reversible causes: medications (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, amiodarone), electrolyte abnormalities (hyperkalemia), hypothyroidism, increased vagal tone, ischemia, Lyme disease. (2) Acute symptomatic bradycardia: IV atropine 0.5 mg q3–5 min (max 3 mg); if refractory, transcutaneous pacing or IV isoproterenol/dopamine infusion. (3) Permanent pacemaker (PPM) indications per 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block, high-grade AV block, or third-degree AV block not due to reversible causes — PPM recommended regardless of symptoms (Class I); symptomatic SND with documented bradycardia-symptom correlation (Class I); symptomatic first-degree or Mobitz type I AV block when symptoms are clearly attributable to the block (Class IIa). (4) Conduction system pacing (His-bundle or left bundle branch area pacing) is increasingly preferred over traditional RV pacing to preserve ventricular synchrony.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Atropine in infranodal block (may worsen block by increasing sinus rate). Avoid beta-blockers, non-DHP CCBs, digoxin, and amiodarone in patients with symptomatic bradycardia unless pacemaker in place. Isoproterenol in acute MI (increases myocardial oxygen demand).

treatments.notes: for symptomatic bradycardia; ineffective in infranodal block and transplanted hearts

treatments.dosage: set rate 60–80 bpm, increase output until capture
treatments.duration: bridge to transvenous pacing or PPM
treatments.notes: for hemodynamically unstable bradycardia unresponsive to atropine
temporizing measure; avoid in ischemia
for symptomatic bradycardia if atropine ineffective
Class I for Mobitz II, high-grade, or third-degree AV block; Class I for symptomatic SND

diseases.name: Pericarditis
diseases.management: Pericarditis (most commonly idiopathic/viral) management per the 2025 ACC Expert Consensus: (1) First-line is dual anti-inflammatory therapy — NSAID (ibuprofen 600–800 mg TID or aspirin 650 mg TID) + colchicine (0.5 mg BID, or 0.5 mg daily if 70 kg); taper NSAIDs after symptoms resolve and CRP normalizes; colchicine for 3 months (first episode) or ≥6 months (recurrence). (2) Exercise restriction for ≥1 month (max HR 100 bpm) until clinical remission. (3) Gastroprotection with PPI when using NSAIDs. (4) Corticosteroids (low-dose prednisone 0.25–0.5 mg/kg/day) only if NSAIDs/colchicine contraindicated or refractory — associated with increased recurrence risk if used in first episode. (5) For colchicine/steroid-refractory recurrent pericarditis: IL-1 blockers — rilonacept (FDA-approved) or anakinra; AIRTRIP trial showed recurrence reduced from 90% to 18% with anakinra. (6) Hospitalize for large effusion/tamponade, severe refractory pain, or suspected purulent/tuberculous pericarditis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroids as first-line (increased recurrence). High-dose corticosteroids (>0.5 mg/kg/day) unless specific indication. Anticoagulants in acute pericarditis with large effusion (risk of hemopericardium). NSAIDs in post-MI pericarditis — use aspirin instead.

treatments.notes: taper after symptom resolution and CRP normalization
preferred when patient already on or has indication for aspirin
reduces recurrence by ~50% (ICAP trial)

diseases.name: Myocarditis
diseases.management: Myocarditis management depends on etiology and hemodynamic status. (1) Supportive care: standard HF therapy (ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, diuretics) for LV dysfunction; avoid NSAIDs (may worsen myocardial inflammation in animal models). (2) Hemodynamically unstable/fulminant: ICU, mechanical circulatory support (ECMO, Impella, VAD) as bridge to recovery or transplant — fulminant myocarditis has paradoxically good long-term prognosis if patient survives acute phase. (3) Immunosuppressive therapy is etiology-specific per 2024 ACC Expert Consensus: Giant cell myocarditis — high-dose IV methylprednisolone + cyclosporine + antithymocyte globulin; Eosinophilic myocarditis — corticosteroids (reduced in-hospital mortality from 65.7% to 9.9%); Cardiac sarcoidosis — corticosteroids ± methotrexate; Immune checkpoint inhibitor myocarditis — stop ICI + high-dose IV methylprednisolone; Autoimmune — corticosteroids ± steroid-sparing agents. (4) Lymphocytic/viral myocarditis — no proven benefit of immunosuppression; supportive care. (5) Activity restriction for 3–6 months. (6) Cardiac MRI is the primary noninvasive diagnostic tool; EMB for specific indications (fulminant, refractory, suspected GCM).
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (may worsen myocardial inflammation). Immunosuppression in virus-positive lymphocytic myocarditis without ruling out active viral replication (EMB with viral PCR recommended before immunosuppression). Resuming ICI therapy after ICI-associated myocarditis (high recurrence risk).

treatments.notes: ACEi for LV dysfunction
beta-blocker; start low, titrate slowly
for congestion

treatments.dosage: 1 g/day IV × 3 days → prednisone 1 mg/kg/day taper
treatments.notes: for GCM, eosinophilic, ICI-associated, sarcoid myocarditis

diseases.name: Aortic Stenosis
diseases.management: AS management per 2020 ACC/AHA VHD Guideline: (1) No medical therapy slows AS progression — statins do not prevent progression. (2) Surveillance: mild AS — echo q3–5 years; moderate — q1–2 years; severe asymptomatic — q6–12 months. (3) Intervention indicated for: symptomatic severe AS (dyspnea, angina, syncope) — Class I; asymptomatic severe AS with LVEF 50% — Class I; asymptomatic very severe AS (Vmax ≥5 m/s) with low surgical risk — Class IIa. (4) Choice of intervention: SAVR vs TAVR — shared decision-making based on age, surgical risk, anatomy, durability considerations; TAVR indications expanding (now approved for all risk categories); SAVR preferred in younger patients (65) due to valve durability and in those with bicuspid anatomy or concomitant cardiac surgery needs. (5) Balloon aortic valvuloplasty is palliative/bridge only. (6) Low-flow, low-gradient AS requires dobutamine stress echo to differentiate true severe AS from pseudo-severe AS.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vasodilators (ACEi, nitrates) were historically avoided but are now considered safe in compensated severe AS with careful monitoring. Avoid rapid diuresis (preload-dependent). No medical therapy prevents AS progression.

treatments.notes: preferred in younger patients (65), bicuspid valve, or concomitant CABG/other valve surgery
approved for all surgical risk categories; preferred in elderly/high-risk patients
bridge to SAVR/TAVR or palliation in non-surgical candidates

diseases.name: Aortic Regurgitation
diseases.management: Chronic AR management per 2020 ACC/AHA VHD Guideline: (1) Surveillance with serial echocardiography — severe AR with normal LV function: echo q6–12 months. (2) Medical therapy: vasodilators (ACEi/ARB, dihydropyridine CCB) for hypertension management; standard HF therapy if LV dysfunction develops. (3) Surgical intervention (AVR) indicated for: symptomatic severe AR — Class I; asymptomatic severe AR with LVEF ≤55% — Class I; asymptomatic severe AR with LV dilation (LVESD >50 mm or indexed >25 mm/m²) — Class IIa. (4) Acute severe AR (endocarditis, aortic dissection, trauma): medical stabilization with vasodilators (nitroprusside) and inotropes → urgent surgery; IABP is contraindicated. (5) Aortic root replacement if concomitant aortic root dilation (≥55 mm, or ≥45 mm in Marfan/bicuspid).
diseases.notes: Avoid: IABP in acute severe AR (worsens regurgitation by augmenting diastolic pressure). Beta-blockers in acute severe AR (prolong diastole, worsen regurgitation). Watchful waiting in symptomatic severe AR (high mortality without intervention).

treatments.notes: for hypertension in chronic AR; may slow LV dilation
vasodilator for hypertension in chronic AR
for symptomatic severe AR or asymptomatic with LVEF ≤55% or LV dilation
for acute severe AR with hemodynamic compromise

diseases.name: Mitral Regurgitation
diseases.management: MR is classified as primary (degenerative — leaflet/chordal pathology) or secondary (functional — LV dilation/dysfunction). (1) Primary MR: surgery (repair preferred over replacement) indicated for symptomatic severe MR (Class I) or
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vasodilator therapy in normotensive asymptomatic primary MR with normal LV function - no proven benefit (Class III: No Benefit per ACC/AHA); may paradoxically worsen MR by decreasing LV size and mitral closing force, increasing prolapse | Phenylephrine and purely vasoconstrictive agents in acute MR - increase LV afterload and worsen regurgitant volume | MV replacement when repair is feasible for degenerative disease - repair has superior short- and long-term outcomes across all age ranges; replacement should not be performed for isolated posterior leaflet pathology unless repair was attempted and failed (Class III: Harm) | Proceeding to TEER or surgery for secondary MR without full GDMT optimization - secondary MR is often responsive to medical therapy (approx 40-60% improve with GDMT alone); premature intervention may expose patients to procedural risk without benefit

treatments.notes: Reduces afterload, redirects flow from regurgitant pathway to aorta, increases forward output; limited by hypotension; combine with dobutamine if hypotensive
Alternative titratable vasodilator; similar mechanism to nitroprusside

diseases.name: Aortic Dissection
diseases.management: Aortic dissection is classified as Type A (ascending aorta) or Type B (descending aorta distal to left subclavian). All patients require immediate ICU admission, invasive BP monitoring, and aggressive medical therapy to reduce aortic wall stress. Target SBP 100–120 mmHg and HR 60–80 bpm. IV beta-blocker is first-line — must be initiated BEFORE vasodilators to prevent reflex tachycardia. Type A dissection: emergent surgical repair (Class I) — mortality without surgery approaches 50% at 48 hours. Type B dissection: uncomplicated — optimal medical therapy (OMT) with lifelong antihypertensives; complicated (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain/HTN, rapid expansion) — TEVAR is first-line endovascular intervention. Long-term: ACEi/ARBs beneficial for chronic BP management; serial imaging surveillance (CT/MRI at 1, 3, 6, 12 months then annually).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vasodilators (nitroprusside, nicardipine, hydralazine) BEFORE beta-blocker (reflex tachycardia increases aortic wall stress). Avoid beta-blockers in acute severe aortic regurgitation (blocks compensatory tachycardia). Avoid anticoagulation unless specific indication (may worsen intramural hematoma).

treatments.notes: ultra-short acting beta-blocker; preferred for rapid titration
combined alpha/beta-blocker
add after beta-blocker for additional BP control
ONLY after adequate beta-blockade; risk of cyanide toxicity
Class I for all acute Type A dissections
for malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain/HTN
target SBP 120 mmHg; serial imaging surveillance

diseases.name: Peripheral Arterial Disease
diseases.management: PAD management per the 2024 ACC/AHA PAD Guideline centers on: (1) Cardiovascular risk factor modification — smoking cessation (most important modifiable risk factor), high-intensity statin, BP control (target 130/80), glycemic control in diabetes; (2) Antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy — single antiplatelet therapy (clopidogrel preferred or aspirin) for symptomatic PAD (Class I); low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin reduces MACE and MALE (COMPASS trial) but increases bleeding (Class I); (3) Symptom management — supervised exercise therapy (SET) for claudication (Class I, 36-session program); cilostazol for claudication symptoms if exercise insufficient; (4) Revascularization — for lifestyle-limiting claudication refractory to exercise/medical therapy, or critical limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) with rest pain, ulcers, or gangrene; endovascular vs surgical approach based on anatomy and patient factors.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Full-intensity anticoagulation without another indication (no benefit, increased bleeding). Pentoxifylline (insufficient evidence of benefit). Cilostazol in heart failure (Class III contraindication). Dual antiplatelet therapy without recent revascularization (uncertain benefit).

treatments.notes: preferred single antiplatelet for symptomatic PAD
alternative single antiplatelet

treatments.dosage: rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin 100 mg daily
treatments.notes: COMPASS trial; reduces MACE and MALE; Class I
high-intensity statin for all PAD patients
Class I for claudication; improves walking distance

diseases.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
diseases.management: DVT management per ACCP/ASH/AHA guidelines: (1) Initiate anticoagulation promptly upon diagnosis or high clinical suspicion. DOACs are preferred over warfarin for non-cancer DVT (Class I). (2) Two oral-only DOAC regimens available: apixaban 10 mg BID × 7 days → 5 mg BID, or rivaroxaban 15 mg BID × 21 days → 20 mg daily. Alternative: parenteral anticoagulation (LMWH) bridged to dabigatran 150 mg BID or edoxaban 60 mg daily, or warfarin (INR 2–3). (3) Duration: provoked by major transient risk factor (surgery) — 3 months; unprovoked — indefinite if low/moderate bleeding risk; cancer-associated — DOAC preferred (or LMWH), minimum 6 months, often indefinite. (4) Extended secondary prevention: apixaban 2.5 mg BID or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily after ≥6 months of full-dose therapy. (5) Most DVT patients can be managed as outpatients. (6) Compression stockings for symptom relief (no longer routinely recommended for PTS prevention). (7) The COBRRA trial (2026) showed apixaban had lower clinically relevant bleeding than rivaroxaban (3.5% vs 5.3%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Full-intensity anticoagulation without confirmed diagnosis (unless high clinical suspicion and imaging delayed). DOACs in antiphospholipid syndrome (use warfarin). DOACs in pregnancy (use LMWH). Routine IVC filter placement in patients who can receive anticoagulation.

treatments.notes: oral-only DOAC regimen; lower bleeding than rivaroxaban (COBRRA trial)
oral-only DOAC regimen

diseases.name: Pulmonary Embolism
diseases.management: PE management per the 2026 AHA/ACC PE Guideline uses a new 5-category risk stratification (A–E). (1) Anticoagulation is the cornerstone — DOACs preferred over VKAs (Class I); LMWH preferred over UFH for parenteral therapy (Class I). (2) Category A (asymptomatic/incidental): outpatient anticoagulation. Category B (symptomatic, low severity score): early discharge with outpatient anticoagulation. Category C (elevated biomarkers ± RV dysfunction): hospitalize, close monitoring, rescue reperfusion if deterioration. Category D (incipient cardiopulmonary failure): hospitalize, consider advanced therapies. Category E (persistent hypotension/cardiogenic shock): systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hrs) is first-line; catheter-directed therapy, mechanical thrombectomy, or surgical embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated or fails. (3) PE response teams (PERTs) recommended. (4) Duration: provoked by major transient factor — 3 months; unprovoked or persistent risk factor — extended/indefinite anticoagulation (Class I). (5) Screen for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary disease at every visit for ≥1 year.

treatments.notes: DOAC preferred; oral-only regimen

diseases.name: Croup
diseases.management: Per the 2026 AAFP Review and 2019 NEJM Review: (1) Most common in children 6 months–3 years; caused by parainfluenza virus. (2) Characteristic barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness; abrupt onset at night. (3) Diagnosis is clinical — radiography and labs typically unnecessary. (4) Corticosteroids are first-line for ALL severities: dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO single dose (max 12 mg) — reduces symptoms at 2 hrs, shortens hospital stays, reduces return visits. Prednisolone is an alternative. (5) Moderate-severe croup: add nebulized racemic epinephrine 0.5 mL of 2.25% solution (or L-epinephrine 5 mL of 1:1000) — rapid onset but short-lived (observe 2–4 hrs for rebound). (6) Mild croup: dexamethasone alone; exposure to outdoor cold air (50°F) for 30 min may reduce symptoms. (7) Humidified mist: no proven benefit. (8) Recurrent croup: evaluate for anatomic abnormality or GERD.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics (viral etiology). Humidified mist (no proven benefit). Cough suppressants. Discharging within 2 hrs of epinephrine without observation (rebound risk).

treatments.notes: Cochrane review: reduces symptoms at 2 hrs; shorter hospital stays

if unable to take oral corticosteroid; onset 1–2 hours

diseases.name: Bronchiolitis
diseases.management: Per the 2014 AAP Guideline (reaffirmed), 2022 Lancet Review, and 2023 AAFP Review: (1) Most common in infants 12 months; RSV accounts for 60–80%. (2) Diagnosis is clinical — viral testing and radiography NOT routinely recommended. (3) Treatment is SUPPORTIVE ONLY — no curative therapy exists. (4) Supplemental oxygen if SpO₂ 90% (AAP threshold). (5) Hydration: oral preferred; nasogastric or IV if unable to maintain oral intake. (6) Nasal suctioning for comfort. (7) NOT recommended: bronchodilators (no benefit; adverse effects), epinephrine (no inpatient benefit), systemic or inhaled corticosteroids (no benefit), antibiotics (unless concurrent bacterial infection), chest physiotherapy, nebulized hypertonic saline (low-quality evidence of modest benefit). (8) High-flow nasal cannula: may be used as rescue therapy for hypoxic infants failing standard oxygen. (9) Hospitalize if: SpO₂ 90%, nasal flaring/grunting, apnea, age ≤2 months, dehydration, poor feeding. (10) Prevention: nirsevimab (long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody) for all infants 8 months entering first RSV season; palivizumab for high-risk infants; RSV maternal vaccine.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bronchodilators (no benefit; adverse effects including tachycardia). Corticosteroids (no benefit). Antibiotics without bacterial co-infection. Routine chest X-ray. Routine viral testing. Chest physiotherapy. Deep nasal suctioning. | Bronchodilators (no benefit, adverse effects). Epinephrine (no inpatient benefit). Systemic or inhaled corticosteroids (no benefit). Antibiotics (unless concurrent bacterial infection). Routine chest X-ray or viral testing. Chest physiotherapy (no benefit).

treatments.dosage: supplemental O₂ if SpO₂ 90%; NG/IV fluids if unable to feed
treatments.notes: no pharmacotherapy shortens course

treatments.dosage: maintain SpO2 >90%, ensure adequate fluid intake

diseases.name: Pediatric Asthma
diseases.management: Per the 2020 NAEPP Focused Update (NHLBI): (1) Stepwise approach by age group (0–4, 5–11, ≥12 years). (2) Ages 0–4: Step 1 — PRN SABA; Step 2 — daily low-dose ICS + PRN SABA (preferred) or short-course ICS at onset of RTI; Step 3 — daily medium-dose ICS; Step 4 — medium-dose ICS-LABA. (3) Ages 5–11: Step 1 — PRN SABA; Step 2 — daily low-dose ICS; Step 3 — daily medium-dose ICS or low-dose ICS-LABA; Step 4 — medium-dose ICS-LABA; Step 5 — high-dose ICS-LABA ± biologic (omalizumab approved ≥6 years). (4) Ages ≥12: same as adult stepwise; ICS-formoterol as single maintenance and reliever therapy (SMART) strongly recommended for moderate-severe persistent asthma (Step 3–4). (5) Acute exacerbation: albuterol q20 min × 3, systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day, max 60 mg × 3–5 days), ipratropium for severe exacerbation. (6) Assess control q2–6 weeks; step up if needed, step down if well-controlled ≥3 months. (7) Allergen mitigation for sensitized patients. (8) FDA boxed warning on montelukast (neuropsychiatric events).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Montelukast as first-line (FDA boxed warning — neuropsychiatric events). LABA monotherapy without ICS. Oral corticosteroids long-term without specialist consultation. Undertreating persistent asthma.

treatments.notes: preferred controller; step up if not controlled
single inhaler for maintenance and reliever; strong recommendation ages ≥12

treatments.dosage: 2–4 puffs q20 min × 3 (MDI + spacer) or 2.5–5 mg nebulized
treatments.notes: SABA for quick relief
for moderate-severe exacerbations
for moderate-severe allergic asthma uncontrolled on Step 4–5

diseases.name: Pediatric Urinary Tract Infection
diseases.management: Per the 2011 AAP Guideline (reaffirmed 2016) and 2021 Pediatrics Review: (1) Suspect UTI in febrile infants/children without a source. (2) Diagnosis requires BOTH positive urinalysis AND urine culture with significant growth (≥50,000 CFU/mL from catheterized specimen or SPA). Bag specimens are unreliable for culture. (3) Oral antibiotics are equally efficacious as IV for uncomplicated febrile UTI in children >1 month. (4) Duration: 7–14 days for pyelonephritis (febrile UTI); 2–4 days for cystitis (afebrile UTI). (5) Antibiotic choice based on local resistance patterns: cephalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, TMP-SMX, or cefixime for oral; ceftriaxone or gentamicin for IV. Nitrofurantoin NOT for pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration). (6) Renal ultrasound: recommended for all children ≤24 months with first febrile UTI. (7) VCUG: NOT routine after first UTI; consider if abnormal ultrasound, atypical pathogen, complex course, or recurrent febrile UTI. (8) Antibiotic prophylaxis: NOT routinely recommended; consider selectively for high-risk patients (high-grade VUR, recurrent febrile UTI). (9) Prompt treatment within 48 hrs reduces renal scarring risk.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bag urine specimens for culture (high contamination). Nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis (poor renal tissue penetration). Routine VCUG after first uncomplicated febrile UTI. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis. Delay in treatment beyond 48 hrs (increases scarring risk).

treatments.notes: adjust based on culture
based on local resistance
short IV course then transition to oral is equally effective
check local resistance; avoid in infants 2 months

diseases.name: Contact Dermatitis
diseases.management: Per the 2015 AAAAI Practice Parameter and 2021 Clinical Reviews: (1) Two forms: irritant contact dermatitis (ICD, 80%) and allergic contact dermatitis (ACD, 20%). (2) Cornerstone of treatment: identify and AVOID the causative irritant or allergen. (3) Mild-moderate localized: mid- to high-potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.1% or clobetasol 0.05%) BID × 2–3 weeks. Avoid potent fluorinated TCS on face, eyelids, folds. (4) Severe or extensive (>20% BSA): systemic corticosteroids — prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day tapered over 2–3 weeks (especially for severe Toxicodendron/poison ivy dermatitis; rapid taper causes rebound). (5) Emollients to restore skin barrier. (6) Antihistamines for pruritus (sedating antihistamines may help with sleep). (7) Topical calcineurin inhibitors: off-label for steroid-resistant ACD or sensitive areas. (8) Patch testing: if causative allergen unknown or treatment fails. (9) Chronic refractory: phototherapy, oral retinoids, or immunosuppression.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Potent fluorinated TCS on face/eyelids/folds. Rapid corticosteroid taper in severe rhus dermatitis (rebound). Prolonged systemic steroids for chronic dermatitis. Topical antibiotics (neomycin, bacitracin — common contact allergens themselves).

treatments.notes: mid-high potency for body; low potency for face/folds
for >20% BSA or severe rhus dermatitis; do NOT taper rapidly
restore skin barrier
cornerstone of management; patch testing if allergen unknown

diseases.name: Tinea / Dermatophyte Infections
diseases.management: Per the 2025 AAFP Review and 2014 Cochrane Review: (1) Tinea corporis, cruris, pedis: topical antifungals first-line — terbinafine 1% cream daily × 1–2 weeks (most effective) or clotrimazole 1% BID × 2–4 weeks. Butenafine 1% daily is also effective. (2) Oral antifungals for extensive disease, failed topical therapy, immunocompromised, or severe moccasin-type tinea pedis: terbinafine 250 mg daily × 2–4 weeks or itraconazole 200 mg daily × 1–2 weeks. (3) Tinea capitis: REQUIRES oral antifungals (topicals do not penetrate hair follicle). Terbinafine 250 mg daily × 4–6 weeks (first-line for Trichophyton). Griseofulvin 500–1000 mg daily × 6–12 weeks (first-line for Microsporum). Add antifungal shampoo (ketoconazole 2% or selenium sulfide) to reduce transmission. (4) Onychomycosis: oral terbinafine 250 mg daily × 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails) — highest cure rate. Itraconazole pulse therapy as alternative. Topical efinaconazole or ciclopirox for mild cases. (5) Confirm diagnosis with KOH prep or culture before oral therapy. (6) Rising terbinafine resistance with T. indotineae — consider itraconazole if suspected.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical corticosteroids alone (exacerbate tinea — "tinea incognito"). Topical antifungals alone for tinea capitis (ineffective). Oral antifungals without confirming diagnosis (KOH/culture). Combination antifungal-steroid creams long-term.

treatments.notes: highest cure rate among topicals
widely available OTC
confirm diagnosis before starting; monitor LFTs
first-line for Microsporum species

diseases.name: Scabies
diseases.management: Per the 2024 JAMA Review and 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Caused by Sarcoptes scabiei. Classic findings: burrows, excoriated papules in interdigital web spaces, wrists, axillae, buttocks, genitalia. (2) First-line: permethrin 5% cream applied to ALL areas from neck down (scalp to toes in infants/young children), left on 8–14 hrs, then washed off. Repeat in 1–2 weeks. Highest cure rate (80.6% at 3–6 weeks). (3) Alternative: oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg, repeated in 14 days. Preferred when topical application is difficult (inability to self-administer, lack of bathing access, extensive atopic dermatitis). Not FDA-approved for scabies but widely used. Limited safety data in pregnancy and children 15 kg. (4) Crusted (Norwegian) scabies: combination oral ivermectin + topical permethrin + topical keratolytic (urea or salicylic acid); more frequent dosing. (5) Treat ALL close contacts simultaneously, even if asymptomatic. (6) Decontamination: launder clothing/linens in hot water ≥122°F for 10 min or seal in plastic bag ≥72 hrs. (7) Post-scabetic pruritus may persist 2–4 weeks after successful treatment (does not indicate treatment failure).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Single-dose treatment without repeat (eggs may survive). Treating only symptomatic individuals (treat all contacts). Lindane as first-line (neurotoxicity, seizures). Assuming treatment failure if pruritus persists 4 weeks (post-scabetic itch is common).

treatments.dosage: apply neck to toes (scalp to toes in infants) for 8–14 hrs
treatments.notes: highest cure rate; safe in pregnancy and children ≥2 months
preferred when topical difficult; take with food; limited data in pregnancy and children 15 kg
crusted scabies requires aggressive combined therapy

diseases.name: Drug Eruption
diseases.management: Per the 2012 NEJM Review, 2024 JAMA Dermatology Consensus, and 2019 Lancet Review: (1) Most common type: maculopapular (exanthematous) drug eruption — onset days to weeks after drug initiation. (2) Cornerstone: identify and DISCONTINUE the causative drug. (3) Mild maculopapular eruption: antihistamines for pruritus + topical corticosteroids. May resolve even if drug continued ("treat-through" approach in select cases with monitoring). (4) Severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs) — SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP — are medical emergencies requiring immediate drug withdrawal. (5) DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms): immediate drug cessation + systemic corticosteroids (first-line for severe); very high-potency topical corticosteroids for mild-moderate DRESS. Monitor for organ involvement (liver, kidney, heart). (6) AGEP (Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis): drug withdrawal usually sufficient; topical corticosteroids; systemic corticosteroids for severe cases. Self-limited (resolves in 1–2 weeks). (7) Fixed drug eruption: recurs at same site with re-exposure; avoid causative drug. (8) Common culprits: antibiotics (amoxicillin, sulfonamides), anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Re-exposure to causative drug (especially for SCARs). Rapid corticosteroid taper in DRESS (rebound/relapse). Ignoring signs of SCAR progression (mucosal involvement, fever, eosinophilia, organ dysfunction). Thalidomide for SJS/TEN (increased mortality).

treatments.dosage: immediately for SCARs; assess risk-benefit for mild eruptions
treatments.notes: cornerstone of management
for maculopapular eruption, mild DRESS, AGEP
symptomatic relief
for severe DRESS with organ involvement

diseases.name: Endometriosis
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review and 2021 Lancet Review: (1) Affects ~10% of reproductive-age women; average diagnostic delay 7–10 years. (2) Diagnosis: clinical symptoms + imaging (ultrasound/MRI); laparoscopy no longer required before initiating empiric therapy. (3) First-line: combined hormonal contraceptives (continuous dosing preferred) + NSAIDs, OR progestins (norethindrone acetate 5 mg daily, dienogest 2 mg daily, depot medroxyprogesterone 150 mg q3 months, or levonorgestrel IUD 52 mg). All hormonal treatments have similar efficacy for pain reduction. (4) Second-line: GnRH agonists (leuprolide 3.75 mg IM monthly) or GnRH antagonists (elagolix 150–200 mg daily; relugolix combination tablet) — always with hormonal add-back therapy to prevent bone loss. (5) Third-line: aromatase inhibitors (letrozole) + progestin. (6) Surgery: for refractory symptoms, large endometriomas (>3 cm), deep infiltrating endometriosis causing organ dysfunction. Laparoscopic excision preferred. Post-operative hormonal therapy to prevent recurrence. (7) Fourth-line (definitive): hysterectomy ± bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for completed childbearing with refractory symptoms. (8) Infertility: refer to reproductive endocrinology; ovarian suppression does NOT improve fertility.
diseases.notes: Avoid: GnRH agonists without add-back therapy (bone loss, menopausal symptoms). Ovarian suppression to improve fertility (ineffective). Danazol as first-line (androgenic side effects). Opioids for chronic endometriosis pain.

treatments.notes: continuous dosing superior to cyclic for pain
effective; low cost
effective for dysmenorrhea; local effect

treatments.dosage: 150 mg daily (moderate pain) or 200 mg BID (severe/dyspareunia)
treatments.duration: up to 24 months (150 mg) or 6 months without add-back
treatments.notes: GnRH antagonist; add-back therapy recommended at higher dose

treatments.dosage: 3.75 mg IM monthly or 11.25 mg q3 months

diseases.name: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding
diseases.management: Per the 2022 Cochrane Network Meta-Analysis and ACOG Practice Bulletin: (1) Definition: menstrual blood loss >80 mL per cycle or bleeding that interferes with quality of life. (2) Evaluate for structural causes (PALM: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy) and non-structural causes (COEIN: Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not classified). (3) First-line medical therapy: levonorgestrel IUD 52 mg (most effective medical treatment — reduces blood loss by ~95%). (4) Other medical options: tranexamic acid 1.3 g TID × 5 days during menses (antifibrinolytic; reduces blood loss ~50%); combined oral contraceptives (reduce blood loss ~40%); oral progestins (norethindrone, medroxyprogesterone); NSAIDs during menses. (5) Surgical options (if medical therapy fails or structural cause): endometrial ablation (for completed childbearing), myomectomy (for fibroids with desire for fertility), hysterectomy (definitive). (6) Iron supplementation for iron deficiency anemia. (7) Acute severe bleeding: high-dose estrogen (conjugated estrogen 25 mg IV q4–6h) or high-dose oral progestin to stabilize endometrium.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tranexamic acid + combined hormonal contraceptives (increased VTE risk). Endometrial ablation in women desiring future pregnancy. Delay in evaluating for structural or coagulation causes. Hysterectomy without trial of medical therapy.

treatments.notes: most effective medical treatment; reduces blood loss ~95%
antifibrinolytic; reduces blood loss ~50%; do not combine with CHC
reduces blood loss ~40%; also provides contraception

treatments.dosage: naproxen 500 mg BID or mefenamic acid 500 mg TID
treatments.notes: reduces blood loss ~20–40%
for iron deficiency anemia

diseases.name: Contraception
diseases.management: Per the 2024 CDC US Selected Practice Recommendations and ACOG Guidelines: (1) Most effective (>99%): LARC — levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena 52 mg, Kyleena 19.5 mg, Skyla 13.5 mg), copper IUD (Paragard), etonogestrel implant (Nexplanon). (2) Highly effective (91–99%): combined oral contraceptives, progestin-only pills, patch (Xulane), vaginal ring (NuvaRing), depot medroxyprogesterone (Depo-Provera 150 mg IM q3 months). (3) Moderately effective (76–88%): condoms, diaphragm, fertility awareness methods. (4) Emergency contraception: ulipristal acetate 30 mg PO (most effective oral EC, up to 5 days); levonorgestrel 1.5 mg PO (up to 3 days); copper IUD (most effective EC overall, up to 5 days). (5) US MEC categories guide contraceptive selection based on medical conditions. (6) Quick start: can start any hormonal method on any day of cycle with reasonable certainty of non-pregnancy; backup method × 7 days. (7) No routine labs needed before starting most contraceptives (BP check for CHC). (8) CHC contraindications: migraine with aura, uncontrolled HTN, VTE history, smoking ≥35 years, breast cancer.
diseases.notes: Avoid: CHC in migraine with aura (stroke risk). CHC in smokers ≥35 years (VTE/MI risk). CHC in uncontrolled HTN. Depot medroxyprogesterone >2 years without reassessing BMD risk.

treatments.notes: >99% effective; also treats HMB; can be used in nulliparous

diseases.name: Ulcerative Colitis
diseases.management: UC management per the 2020 AGA Guideline and 2023 JAMA/Lancet reviews: (1) Mild-to-moderate UC: 5-ASA (mesalamine) is first-line — combination oral + rectal 5-ASA superior to either alone; rectal 5-ASA preferred for proctitis. (2) Moderate-to-severe UC: biologics are first-line — infliximab and vedolizumab preferred in biologic-naïve patients (AGA); adalimumab, golimumab, ustekinumab, tofacitinib, upadacitinib, ozanimod, etrasimod also options. (3) Combination therapy (biologic + immunomodulator) more effective than monotherapy. (4) Acute severe UC (ASUC): IV methylprednisolone 40–60 mg/day; if no response by day 3 (Oxford criteria) → infliximab rescue or colectomy. (5) Maintenance: biologic or immunomodulator; 5-ASA for mild disease. (6) Colectomy for refractory disease, toxic megacolon, perforation, dysplasia/malignancy (~15% of patients). (7) Colorectal cancer surveillance colonoscopy starting 8 years after diagnosis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Thiopurine monotherapy for induction (too slow). Methotrexate for UC maintenance (no efficacy). Corticosteroids for maintenance. Delay in colectomy for toxic megacolon or perforation. Cyclosporine as first-line rescue (infliximab preferred in most centers). | Thiopurine monotherapy for induction (too slow). Methotrexate for UC maintenance (no efficacy). Corticosteroids for maintenance. Delay in colectomy for toxic megacolon or perforation. Cyclosporine as first-line rescue (infliximab preferred in most centers).

treatments.notes: combination oral + rectal superior to either alone

diseases.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
diseases.management: IBS management per the 2021 JAMA Review, ACG Guideline, and AGA Clinical Practice Update: (1) Positive diagnosis using Rome IV criteria (recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week for ≥3 months, associated with defecation, change in stool frequency/form). (2) Limited testing: CBC, CRP, celiac serology (tTG-IgA); colonoscopy only if alarm features or age-appropriate screening. (3) First-line for all subtypes: dietary modification (low-FODMAP diet, soluble fiber [psyllium]), exercise, stress reduction. (4) IBS-C: soluble fiber → osmotic laxative (PEG) → secretagogue (linaclotide, lubiprostone, plecanatide, tenapanor). (5) IBS-D: loperamide → bile acid sequestrant (cholestyramine) → rifaximin 550 mg TID × 14 days → eluxadoline or alosetron (women with severe IBS-D only). (6) Pain-predominant: antispasmodics (hyoscine, dicyclomine) → low-dose TCA (amitriptyline 10–25 mg) → SSRI/SNRI. (7) Psychological therapies (CBT, gut-directed hypnotherapy) effective for refractory IBS.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine colonoscopy without alarm features. Opioids (narcotic bowel syndrome). Insoluble fiber/bran (may worsen bloating). Alosetron in men or mild IBS-D (restricted use). Unnecessary food allergy testing. | Routine colonoscopy without alarm features. Opioids (narcotic bowel syndrome). Insoluble fiber/bran (may worsen bloating). Alosetron in men or mild IBS-D (restricted use). Unnecessary food allergy testing.

diseases.name: Celiac Disease
diseases.management: Celiac disease management per the 2023 ACG Guideline Update and 2026 NEJM Review: (1) Diagnosis: IgA tTG as initial screening test + total IgA level; if tTG positive → EGD with duodenal biopsies (≥4 biopsies from D2, ≥1 from D1). No-biopsy approach in children: tTG ≥10× ULN + positive EMA in second sample. In adults, biopsy remains standard, though tTG ≥10× ULN has high PPV (~99.8%). (2) Treatment: strict lifelong gluten-free diet (GFD) — avoid wheat, barley, rye; oats are safe for most patients. (3) Refer to GI dietitian experienced in celiac disease. (4) Screen for nutritional deficiencies: iron, folate, B12, vitamin D, calcium, zinc. (5) Bone density assessment (DEXA) at diagnosis if risk factors present. (6) Monitor tTG-IgA at 6 and 12 months, then annually — declining titers indicate dietary adherence. (7) Repeat biopsy at 1–2 years to confirm mucosal healing (especially in adults). (8) Nonresponsive celiac disease: most common cause is inadvertent gluten exposure; rule out refractory celiac disease (type I vs type II — type II has risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Reliance on serology alone for diagnosis in adults (biopsy still recommended). Starting GFD before diagnostic workup is complete (reduces sensitivity of testing). Routine oat avoidance (safe for most; ensure uncontaminated). Corticosteroids for uncomplicated celiac disease (GFD is sufficient). | Reliance on serology alone for diagnosis in adults (biopsy still recommended). Starting GFD before diagnostic workup is complete (reduces sensitivity of testing). Routine oat avoidance (safe for most; ensure uncontaminated). Corticosteroids for uncomplicated celiac disease (GFD is sufficient).

treatments.dosage: strict avoidance of wheat, barley, rye
treatments.notes: only proven treatment; clinical response in weeks, mucosal healing in months-years

diseases.name: Infectious Diarrhea / Food Poisoning
diseases.management: Infectious diarrhea management per the 2017 IDSA Guideline and 2022 AAFP Review: (1) Most acute diarrhea is self-limited (viral >bacterial); supportive care is mainstay. (2) Rehydration: oral rehydration solution preferred; IV fluids for severe dehydration, shock, or inability to tolerate PO. (3) Symptomatic therapy: loperamide for acute watery diarrhea in immunocompetent adults (avoid in bloody/febrile diarrhea); bismuth subsalicylate as alternative. (4) Stool testing: multiplex PCR or culture if bloody diarrhea, fever >38.5°C, severe abdominal pain, immunocompromised, recent travel, or duration >7 days. (5) Empiric antibiotics: only for febrile dysentery with toxicity, severe traveler's diarrhea, or sepsis — azithromycin 1000 mg single dose or 500 mg daily × 3 days (preferred); fluoroquinolone alternative (avoid in SE Asia due to Campylobacter resistance). (6) Pathogen-specific therapy: Shigella — azithromycin or fluoroquinolone; Salmonella — antibiotics only if bacteremia, age extremes, immunocompromised, or prosthetic devices; Campylobacter — azithromycin; EHEC (E. coli O157:H7) — do NOT give antibiotics (may increase HUS risk). (7) Public health reporting for outbreaks.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics for non-bloody, non-febrile acute diarrhea (self-limited). Antibiotics for EHEC/E. coli O157:H7 (may increase HUS risk). Loperamide in bloody/febrile diarrhea. Routine stool testing for uncomplicated acute diarrhea 7 days. Antimotility agents in children 18 years. | Antibiotics for non-bloody, non-febrile acute diarrhea (self-limited). Antibiotics for EHEC/E. coli O157:H7 (may increase HUS risk). Loperamide in bloody/febrile diarrhea. Routine stool testing for uncomplicated acute diarrhea 7 days. Antimotility agents in children 18 years.

treatments.notes: mainstay of treatment for all causes

diseases.name: Viral Hepatitis A
diseases.management: Hepatitis A is self-limited; no specific antiviral therapy. Supportive care: rest, hydration, avoid hepatotoxins (alcohol, acetaminophen in excess). Fulminant hepatitis A (1%) requires ICU care and liver transplant evaluation. Prevention: HAV vaccination (2-dose series) for all children, travelers, chronic liver disease, MSM, PWID, homeless.

treatments.notes: self-limited in >99%
for all at-risk populations

diseases.name: Viral Hepatitis B (Chronic)
diseases.management: Chronic HBV management per the 2018 AASLD Guidance and 2026 AAFP Review: (1) Treatment indications: HBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL + elevated ALT + significant fibrosis/inflammation; immune-active phase; cirrhosis with any detectable HBV DNA. (2) First-line antivirals: entecavir 0.5 mg daily (1 mg if lamivudine-experienced), tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) 300 mg daily, or tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 25 mg daily — all have high barrier to resistance. (3) TAF preferred over TDF in patients with renal impairment or osteoporosis risk. (4) Peginterferon alfa-2a 180 mcg SC weekly × 48 weeks — finite duration; preferred in young patients seeking treatment discontinuation. (5) Treatment endpoints: HBsAg loss (functional cure — rare, ~1%/year); HBeAg seroconversion in HBeAg-positive patients. (6) Most patients require indefinite NA therapy. (7) HCC surveillance: ultrasound ± AFP q6 months in cirrhosis or high-risk populations. (8) Acute HBV: >95% recover spontaneously; antivirals only for acute liver failure or protracted course >4 weeks.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Lamivudine monotherapy (high resistance rate ~70% at 5 years). Interferon in decompensated cirrhosis (hepatotoxicity). Adefovir (inferior to entecavir/tenofovir). Stopping NA therapy without close monitoring (risk of severe flare). | Lamivudine monotherapy (high resistance rate ~70% at 5 years). Interferon in decompensated cirrhosis (hepatotoxicity). Adefovir (inferior to entecavir/tenofovir). Stopping NA therapy without close monitoring (risk of severe flare).

treatments.notes: high barrier to resistance; preferred NA

diseases.name: Viral Hepatitis C
diseases.management: Chronic HCV management per the AASLD/IDSA HCV Guidance and 2021 ACP Best Practice Advice: (1) Treat ALL patients with chronic HCV (treat-all strategy). (2) Pangenotypic DAA regimens achieve SVR12 >95%: glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) × 8 weeks (treatment-naïve without cirrhosis) or 12 weeks (compensated cirrhosis); sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) × 12 weeks. (3) Treatment-experienced or DAA-failure: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir/voxilaprevir (Vosevi) × 12 weeks. (4) Decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C): sofosbuvir/velpatasvir ± ribavirin × 12 weeks (avoid protease inhibitors). (5) Pre-treatment: genotype (if using genotype-specific regimen), HBV coinfection screening (risk of HBV reactivation during DAA therapy), assess fibrosis stage. (6) SVR12 = cure; lifelong HCC surveillance if cirrhosis even after SVR. (7) No vaccine available.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis (hepatotoxicity). Interferon-based regimens (obsolete). Stopping HCC surveillance after SVR in patients with cirrhosis. Initiating DAA without HBV screening (reactivation risk). | Protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis (hepatotoxicity). Interferon-based regimens (obsolete). Stopping HCC surveillance after SVR in patients with cirrhosis. Initiating DAA without HBV screening (reactivation risk).

treatments.notes: pangenotypic; treatment-naïve
pangenotypic; for all genotypes including decompensated cirrhosis

diseases.name: Alcoholic Hepatitis
diseases.management: Alcoholic hepatitis management per the 2024 ACG Guideline and 2021 JAMA Review: (1) Assess severity: MELD >20 = severe AH (high short-term mortality). (2) Abstinence is cornerstone of treatment. (3) Nutrition: 35 kcal/kg/day with 1.2–1.5 g/kg protein; thiamine, B12, zinc supplementation. (4) Screen for infection before starting corticosteroids (blood, urine, ascites cultures, CXR). (5) Severe AH (MELD >20): prednisolone 40 mg/day × 28 days; assess response at day 7 with Lille score — continue if 0.45 (responder), discontinue if ≥0.45 (non-responder). Maximum benefit at MELD 25–39. (6) IV N-acetylcysteine as adjunct to corticosteroids (strong recommendation, ACG). (7) Pentoxifylline is NOT recommended (no benefit). (8) Early liver transplant for highly selected patients with severe AH unresponsive to medical therapy. (9) Treat AUD: naltrexone, acamprosate (avoid disulfiram in ALD).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Pentoxifylline (no benefit — strong recommendation against, ACG). Disulfiram in ALD (hepatotoxicity). Corticosteroids with uncontrolled infection, GI bleeding, or hepatorenal syndrome. Nephrotoxic drugs including NSAIDs and diuretics. Corticosteroids for MELD >50 (uncertain benefit, high mortality). | Pentoxifylline (no benefit — strong recommendation against, ACG). Disulfiram in ALD (hepatotoxicity). diseases.name: Cirrhosis
diseases.management:
Cirrhosis management per the 2023 JAMA Review, 2021 AASLD Guidance, and 2016 NEJM Review: (1) Treat underlying etiology (alcohol cessation, HCV DAAs, HBV antivirals, weight loss for MASLD). (2) Compensated cirrhosis: nonselective beta-blockers (carvedilol preferred) for clinically significant portal hypertension — reduces decompensation and death (PREDESCI trial: 16% vs 27%). (3) Variceal screening: EGD at diagnosis; band ligation for medium-large varices; NSBB (propranolol, nadolol, carvedilol) for primary prophylaxis. (4) Ascites: sodium restriction (2 g/day) + combination spironolactone (100 mg) + furosemide (40 mg) daily (combination superior to sequential: 76% vs 56% resolution); large-volume paracentesis with albumin replacement (6–8 g/L removed) for tense ascites. (5) HCC surveillance: US ± AFP q6 months. (6) Vaccinations: HAV, HBV, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19. (7) Avoid NSAIDs (AKI risk), ACEi/ARBs in decompensated cirrhosis (hypotension), sedatives. (8) Acetaminophen safe up to 2 g/day. (9) Liver transplant evaluation when MELD ≥15.

(continued from previous batch) (9) Hepatorenal syndrome-AKI (HRS-AKI): terlipressin + IV albumin is first-line (FDA-approved); terlipressin reverses HRS-AKI in 36–44% of patients. Norepinephrine + albumin is alternative (requires ICU). Midodrine + octreotide is inferior. Monitor for respiratory failure (especially in ACLF-3). All patients with HRS-AKI should be evaluated urgently for liver transplant. (10) Acetaminophen safe up to 2 g/day. (11) Avoid NSAIDs, ACEi/ARBs in decompensated cirrhosis, aminoglycosides, IV contrast when possible. (12) MELD ≥15 → liver transplant evaluation.

treatments.notes: NSBB; reduces portal pressure more than propranolol; PREDESCI trial
titrate to HR 55–60; alternative NSBB
combination superior to sequential; max spironolactone 400 mg + furosemide 160 mg

diseases.name: GERD
diseases.management: GERD management per the 2022 ACG Guideline: (1) Lifestyle modifications for all — weight loss in overweight/obese (strong recommendation), avoid meals 2–3 hrs before bedtime, elevate head of bed, avoid trigger foods, smoking cessation. (2) Empiric PPI once daily 30–60 min before a meal × 8 weeks for typical symptoms (heartburn, regurgitation) without alarm features. (3) PPIs superior to H2RAs for healing erosive esophagitis (strong recommendation). (4) If symptoms respond, attempt to discontinue or switch to on-demand PPI (unless LA grade C/D esophagitis or Barrett's — indefinite PPI). (5) Use lowest effective PPI dose for maintenance. (6) PPI non-responders: do NOT routinely add medical therapies; perform endoscopy and/or reflux monitoring off therapy. (7) Vonoprazan (P-CAB) is FDA-approved for erosive esophagitis and GERD; achieves faster, more potent acid suppression than PPIs. (8) Surgical option: laparoscopic fundoplication for select patients with objective GERD documentation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine addition of prokinetics, baclofen, or sucralfate to PPI non-responders without objective GERD testing. Long-term PPI without reassessing indication. Barium swallow as sole diagnostic test for GERD.

treatments.notes: standard-dose PPI; equivalent efficacy across PPIs at appropriate doses
alternative PPI
P-CAB; faster onset, no meal timing requirement; FDA-approved for erosive esophagitis and GERD
