for patients with objective GERD who prefer surgery over lifelong PPI

diseases.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
diseases.management: PUD management per the 2024 JAMA Review and ACG guidelines: (1) Test all patients for H. pylori — urea breath test or stool antigen preferred (not serology). (2) PPI therapy: duodenal ulcer — omeprazole 20 mg daily × 4 weeks; gastric ulcer — omeprazole 40 mg daily × 4–8 weeks. (3) Eradicate H. pylori if positive (see H. pylori entry). (4) Discontinue NSAIDs if possible; if NSAIDs required, co-prescribe PPI and use lowest effective NSAID dose or celecoxib. (5) Smoking cessation (impairs ulcer healing). (6) Endoscopy for patients ≥60 years with new symptoms, alarm features (weight loss, bleeding, dysphagia), or persistent symptoms after H. pylori treatment. (7) Repeat endoscopy for gastric ulcers to rule out malignancy. (8) GI bleeding: see GI Bleed entry.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Serology alone for H. pylori diagnosis (cannot distinguish active from past infection). NSAIDs without gastroprotection in patients with ulcer history. Antacids as sole therapy (temporary symptom relief only).

treatments.notes: PPI heals 87% duodenal, 84% gastric ulcers
eradication heals >90% of H. pylori-associated ulcers
H2RA; if PPI intolerant
mucosal protectant; less effective than PPI

diseases.name: H. pylori Infection
diseases.management: H. pylori eradication per the 2026 JAMA Review and ACG/Maastricht/Toronto guidelines: (1) First-line: bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) × 14 days is preferred due to rising clarithromycin resistance. (2) Alternatives: vonoprazan dual therapy (vonoprazan + amoxicillin × 14 days) — high eradication rates even with clarithromycin-resistant strains; vonoprazan triple therapy; rifabutin triple therapy (Talicia — FDA-approved); PPI triple therapy (only if local clarithromycin resistance 15%). (3) Concomitant therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin + metronidazole × 14 days) is an alternative when bismuth unavailable. (4) Second-line: use regimen not previously used — BQT or levofloxacin triple; avoid repeating clarithromycin or levofloxacin. (5) Test of cure: urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after therapy completion and ≥2 weeks after stopping PPI.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Clarithromycin-based triple therapy in areas with >15% clarithromycin resistance. Repeating same antibiotic regimen after failure. Doxycycline substitution for tetracycline in BQT (inferior). Serology for test of cure.

treatments.dosage: PPI BID + bismuth QID + tetracycline 500 mg QID + metronidazole 500 mg TID-QID
treatments.notes: preferred first-line; eradication ~85–90%

treatments.dosage: vonoprazan 20 mg BID + amoxicillin 1000 mg TID
treatments.notes: high efficacy even with clarithromycin resistance

treatments.dosage: vonoprazan 20 mg + clarithromycin 500 mg + amoxicillin 1000 mg BID
treatments.notes: P-CAB-based triple

treatments.dosage: omeprazole 40 mg + amoxicillin 1000 mg + rifabutin 50 mg TID
treatments.notes: FDA-approved; effective against clarithromycin-resistant strains

treatments.dosage: PPI BID + clarithromycin 500 mg BID + amoxicillin 1000 mg BID
treatments.notes: only if local clarithromycin resistance 15%

treatments.dosage: PPI BID + clarithromycin 500 mg + amoxicillin 1000 mg + metronidazole 500 mg BID
treatments.notes: non-bismuth quadruple; ~91% eradication

diseases.name: Gastritis
diseases.management: Gastritis management depends on etiology: (1) H. pylori gastritis: eradicate H. pylori (see H. pylori entry); PPI × 4–8 weeks. (2) NSAID/aspirin-related: discontinue offending agent if possible; PPI × 4–8 weeks; if NSAID must continue, co-prescribe PPI. (3) Stress-related mucosal disease (critically ill): PPI prophylaxis for high-risk ICU patients (mechanical ventilation >48 hrs, coagulopathy, prior GI bleed, TBI, burns >35% BSA). (4) Autoimmune gastritis (pernicious anemia): vitamin B12 supplementation (IM or high-dose oral); monitor for iron deficiency; endoscopic surveillance for gastric neuroendocrine tumors. (5) Alcohol-related: cessation + PPI. (6) Bile reflux gastritis: sucralfate or ursodiol may help.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term PPI without reassessing indication. NSAIDs in active gastritis. Routine stress ulcer prophylaxis in non-ICU patients (no benefit, C. difficile risk).

treatments.notes: acid suppression for symptom relief and mucosal healing
treats underlying cause

treatments.dosage: 1000 mcg IM monthly or 1000–2000 mcg PO daily
treatments.notes: for pernicious anemia
mucosal protectant

diseases.name: Acute Pancreatitis
diseases.management: Acute pancreatitis management per the 2018 AGA Guideline and 2021 JAMA Review: (1) Diagnosis: ≥2 of 3 criteria — characteristic abdominal pain, lipase/amylase ≥3× ULN, imaging findings. (2) Fluid resuscitation: goal-directed IV crystalloid (LR preferred over NS); the WATERFALL trial (2022) showed aggressive fluids (20 mL/kg bolus + 3 mL/kg/hr) increased fluid overload without benefit — moderate resuscitation (1.5 mL/kg/hr) is now preferred. (3) Early oral feeding as tolerated (within 24 hrs); if not tolerated, nasogastric/nasojejunal tube feeding preferred over TPN. (4) Analgesia: multimodal (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, opioids PRN). (5) No role for prophylactic antibiotics (even in predicted severe/necrotizing pancreatitis). (6) ERCP within 24 hrs only if concurrent cholangitis; not routine for biliary pancreatitis without cholangitis. (7) Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild biliary pancreatitis (reduces recurrence). (8) Necrotizing pancreatitis: conservative management initially; intervene (step-up approach: percutaneous drain → endoscopic/minimally invasive necrosectomy) only for infected necrosis, typically ≥4 weeks after onset.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prophylactic antibiotics (no benefit in preventing infected necrosis). Aggressive IV fluids (WATERFALL trial: increased fluid overload). Routine ERCP without cholangitis. Early surgical intervention for sterile necrosis. TPN when enteral nutrition is feasible.

treatments.notes: goal-directed; LR preferred; avoid aggressive fluids
reduces length of stay; no need for NPO or clear liquids first
multimodal analgesia
NSAID; opioid-sparing
for mild biliary pancreatitis; reduces recurrence from 18% to 5%
minimally invasive preferred over open necrosectomy

diseases.name: Chronic Pancreatitis
diseases.management: Chronic pancreatitis management: (1) Alcohol and smoking cessation (most important — slows progression). (2) Pain management: stepwise approach — acetaminophen → NSAIDs → low-dose TCA (amitriptyline) or pregabalin/gabapentin → opioids (last resort); pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) may reduce pain by negative feedback on CCK. (3) Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (PEI): PERT with meals — lipase 40,000–50,000 units per meal, 25,000 per snack; add PPI if inadequate response (acid degrades enzymes). (4) Endocrine insufficiency: type 3c diabetes — insulin often required; avoid metformin if malnutrition. (5) Nutritional support: small frequent meals, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K), medium-chain triglycerides. (6) Endoscopic therapy for dominant strictures or stones (ESWL + ERCP). (7) Surgery (Frey, Puestow, Whipple) for refractory pain with ductal dilation or mass.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids as first-line for pain (dependence, narcotic bowel syndrome). High-fat meals without PERT. Delay in PERT initiation. Metformin in malnourished patients with type 3c diabetes.

treatments.dosage: 40,000–50,000 lipase units per meal, 25,000 per snack
treatments.notes: for PEI; titrate to stool normalization
add if PERT response inadequate; prevents acid degradation of enzymes
first-line analgesic
neuropathic pain component
most important intervention to slow progression

diseases.name: Acute Cholecystitis
diseases.management: Acute cholecystitis management per the 2022 JAMA Review and Tokyo Guidelines 2018: (1) Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 1–3 days of diagnosis) is first-line — associated with fewer complications (11.8% vs 34.4%), shorter LOS (5.4 vs 10 days), and lower costs vs delayed surgery. (2) Pre-operative: NPO, IV fluids, antibiotics (for moderate-severe or septic patients), analgesia. (3) Antibiotics: ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam for moderate-severe; mild uncomplicated may not require antibiotics if early cholecystectomy performed. (4) Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube: reserved for patients with prohibitively high surgical risk (higher complication rate than cholecystectomy — 65% vs 12%). (5) Pregnancy: early laparoscopic cholecystectomy recommended in all trimesters (lower maternal-fetal complications: 1.6% vs 18.4% with delayed). (6) Elderly (>65 years): cholecystectomy associated with lower 2-year mortality (15.2%) vs nonoperative management (29.3%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delayed cholecystectomy beyond 7–10 days from symptom onset (increased complications). Percutaneous cholecystostomy as first-line (higher complication rate than surgery). Conservative management in elderly without clear contraindication to surgery.

treatments.notes: first-line for all patient populations; lower complications than delayed

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h
treatments.notes: for moderate-severe cholecystitis
broad-spectrum; for severe or healthcare-associated

diseases.name: Acute Cholangitis
diseases.management: Acute cholangitis management per the Tokyo Guidelines 2018: (1) Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) present in 50–70%; Reynolds' pentad adds altered mental status and hypotension. (2) Severity grading: Grade I (mild) — responds to antibiotics alone; Grade II (moderate) — requires early biliary drainage; Grade III (severe) — organ dysfunction, requires ICU + urgent drainage. (3) Empiric antibiotics: ceftriaxone + metronidazole, piperacillin-tazobactam, or carbapenem for severe/healthcare-associated. (4) Biliary drainage: ERCP with sphincterotomy ± stone extraction is first-line; percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) if ERCP fails or unavailable; EUS-guided drainage as alternative. (5) Definitive treatment of underlying cause (choledocholithiasis, stricture, malignancy) after acute episode resolves.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in biliary drainage for moderate-severe cholangitis (increases mortality). Antibiotics alone for Grade II–III cholangitis (drainage essential). Empiric narrow-spectrum antibiotics in severe disease.

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h
treatments.notes: for community-acquired cholangitis
broad-spectrum alternative
for severe cholangitis or MDR risk
first-line drainage; perform within 24–48 hrs for moderate-severe
if ERCP fails or unavailable

diseases.name: Choledocholithiasis
diseases.management: Choledocholithiasis management: (1) Risk stratification: high risk (dilated CBD >6 mm + elevated bilirubin + visible CBD stone on imaging) → ERCP; intermediate risk → EUS or MRCP to confirm before ERCP; low risk → proceed to cholecystectomy with intraoperative cholangiogram. (2) ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the gold standard for CBD stone removal. (3) Cholecystectomy should follow ERCP (same admission if possible) to prevent recurrent biliary events. (4) Laparoscopic CBD exploration during cholecystectomy is an alternative to pre-operative ERCP in experienced centers. (5) For large stones (>15 mm): mechanical lithotripsy, electrohydraulic lithotripsy, or cholangioscopy-guided laser lithotripsy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ERCP for low-risk patients (unnecessary procedural risk). Delay in cholecystectomy after ERCP (increased recurrent biliary events). Routine ERCP without risk stratification.

treatments.notes: gold standard for CBD stone removal
same admission after ERCP to prevent recurrence
to confirm CBD stones before ERCP

diseases.name: Acute Appendicitis
diseases.management: Acute appendicitis management: (1) Appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred) remains the gold standard — lower complication rates, faster recovery than open. (2) Antibiotics-first strategy: the CODA trial (2020) showed that antibiotics alone (IV ertapenem → oral ciprofloxacin + metronidazole × 10 days) was non-inferior to appendectomy for uncomplicated appendicitis at 30 days, but 29% required appendectomy within 90 days. Antibiotics-first is a reasonable option for uncomplicated appendicitis in patients who wish to avoid surgery. (3) Complicated appendicitis (perforation, abscess, phlegmon): if stable, initial antibiotics + percutaneous drainage of abscess >3 cm → interval appendectomy at 6–8 weeks; if unstable or peritonitis → urgent appendectomy. (4) Pre-operative antibiotics: single dose of cefazolin + metronidazole or cefoxitin.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgery for uncomplicated appendicitis beyond 24 hrs (increased perforation risk). Open appendectomy as first approach (laparoscopic preferred). Routine interval appendectomy after successful antibiotics-first without recurrence (shared decision-making).

treatments.notes: gold standard; lower complications than open

treatments.dosage: ertapenem 1 g IV daily × ≥24 hrs → ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID + metronidazole 500 mg TID PO × 10 days
treatments.notes: CODA trial; 29% require appendectomy within 90 days
for stable patients with abscess >3 cm

diseases.name: Diverticulitis
diseases.management: Diverticulitis management per the 2025 JAMA Review, 2022 ACP Guideline, and 2021 AGA Clinical Practice Update: (1) Uncomplicated (85% of cases): observation with pain control (acetaminophen) and dietary modification; antibiotics NOT routinely needed in immunocompetent patients with mild disease (ACP conditional recommendation). (2) Antibiotics indicated for: persistent fever, rising WBC, age >80, pregnancy, immunocompromised, comorbidities (cirrhosis, CKD, HF, poorly controlled DM), or signs of sepsis. First-line oral: amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin + metronidazole × 4–7 days. IV: ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam. (3) Complicated diverticulitis: abscess >3 cm → percutaneous drainage + IV antibiotics; perforation with peritonitis → emergent laparotomy with colonic resection. (4) Outpatient management appropriate for 95% of uncomplicated cases. (5) Colonoscopy 6–8 weeks after resolution to exclude malignancy (especially if first episode, age >40).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine antibiotics for uncomplicated diverticulitis in immunocompetent patients (no benefit per Cochrane review). NSAIDs (may worsen diverticulitis). Opioids (increase perforation risk). Routine elective sigmoid resection after single uncomplicated episode.

treatments.notes: no antibiotics needed in immunocompetent patients with mild disease
for patients meeting antibiotic criteria

treatments.dosage: cephalexin 500 mg QID + metronidazole 500 mg TID
treatments.notes: alternative oral regimen

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h

diseases.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
diseases.management: SBO management per the EAST Practice Management Guideline: (1) Adhesions cause 65% of SBO; other causes: hernias (10%), neoplasms (5%), Crohn's (5%). (2) Immediate surgery for: peritonitis, clinical deterioration (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis, continuous pain), or CT findings of bowel ischemia (closed-loop obstruction, pneumatosis, portal venous gas). (3) Non-operative management for partial or complete SBO without signs of strangulation: NPO, IV fluids, NG decompression, electrolyte correction. (4) Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin): both diagnostic and therapeutic — if contrast reaches colon on abdominal X-ray at 8–24 hrs, SBO will likely resolve; if not, surgery indicated. Administer at 48 hrs if SBO has not resolved. (5) Surgery if no resolution by days 3–5. (6) Laparoscopic approach preferred when feasible (shorter LOS, fewer complications). (7) Admit to surgical service (lower mortality than medical service).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgery when signs of strangulation present. Gastrografin in closed-loop obstruction (requires CT first). Admission to medical service (surgical service associated with better outcomes). Prolonged non-operative management beyond 5 days without reassessment. | Delay in surgery when strangulation suspected. Oral intake before resolution confirmed. Ignoring rising lactate or worsening exam. Prolonged nonoperative management beyond 48–72 hrs without improvement.

treatments.dosage: NPO, IV fluids, NG decompression, electrolyte correction
treatments.notes: 60–85% of adhesive SBO resolves without surgery
if contrast in colon at 8–24 hrs → SBO resolving; if not → surgery
for peritonitis, ischemia, closed-loop, or failed conservative management

diseases.name: Crohn Disease
diseases.management: Crohn disease management per the 2025 AGA Living Guideline and 2024 ACG Guideline: (1) Moderate-to-severe CD: biologic therapy is first-line — TNF antagonists (infliximab, adalimumab) with or without immunomodulator (azathioprine/6-MP) for combination therapy; anti-IL-23 agents (risankizumab, guselkumab, mirikizumab); ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23); vedolizumab (anti-integrin); upadacitinib (JAK inhibitor, after TNF failure in US). (2) The 2025 AGA guideline recommends upfront advanced therapy over step-up approach. (3) Mucosal healing is the treatment target. (4) Mild CD: budesonide 9 mg daily × 8 weeks for ileal/right colonic disease; 5-ASA has limited role. (5) Corticosteroids for induction only — NOT for maintenance. (6) Perianal fistulizing CD: infliximab + surgical drainage; seton placement. (7) Stricturing CD: endoscopic balloon dilation or surgical resection. (8) Smoking cessation (worsens CD).

treatments.dosage: infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 wks then q8wk + azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg daily
treatments.notes: combination therapy superior to monotherapy (SONIC trial)

treatments.dosage: 160 mg SC then 80 mg at wk 2 then 40 mg q2wk
treatments.notes: TNF antagonist; monotherapy acceptable

diseases.name: Hepatic Encephalopathy
diseases.management: HE management per the 2026 ACG Clinical Guideline and 2016 NEJM Review: (1) Identify and treat precipitants: infection (diagnostic paracentesis mandatory), GI bleeding, dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatremia, hypokalemia), constipation, sedatives, dietary protein excess (rare). (2) Acute OHE (grade 2): lactulose 15–30 mL PO q2h until 2 soft bowel movements, then titrate to 2–3 BMs/day. Grade 3–4 HE: lactulose via NG tube or lactulose enema (200 g in 700 mL water/NS, retain 30–60 min, q4–6h). (3) PEG 3350 (4 L) is an alternative to lactulose for acute HE (conditional recommendation, ACG). (4) Add rifaximin to lactulose for acute OHE (conditional recommendation, ACG). (5) Secondary prophylaxis: lactulose (strong recommendation) + rifaximin 550 mg BID (reduces recurrence by 58%, NNT 4; reduces HE-related hospitalization by 50%, NNT 9). (6) Do NOT routinely check serum ammonia to guide treatment (conditional recommendation against). (7) Do NOT restrict protein (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day recommended). (8) Do NOT perform routine brain imaging unless new focal neurologic deficits.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Serum ammonia to guide treatment decisions (conditional recommendation against, ACG). Protein restriction (worsens sarcopenia, does not improve HE). Routine brain imaging without focal deficits. Sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids). Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics without evidence of infection. Lactulose overuse causing dehydration/electrolyte disturbances (precipitates HE recurrence).

treatments.dosage: 15–30 mL (10–20 g) q2h until 2 soft BMs, then 15–30 mL 2–4×/day
treatments.notes: titrate to 2–3 soft BMs/day; strong recommendation (ACG)
retain 30–60 min; for patients at aspiration risk
faster HE resolution than lactulose in one RCT; conditional recommendation (ACG)
add to lactulose; 58% risk reduction in OHE recurrence; NNT 4
titrate to 2–3 soft BMs/day; strong recommendation (ACG)
do NOT restrict protein

diseases.name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
diseases.management: SBP management per the 2021 AASLD Practice Guidance and 2021 NEJM Review: (1) Diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN count >250/mm³ on diagnostic paracentesis; inoculate blood culture bottles at bedside. (2) Empiric antibiotics: IV third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 2 g daily or cefotaxime 2 g q8h) is first-line for community-acquired SBP. Healthcare-associated/nosocomial or recent broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure: broader coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem). (3) IV albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1 + 1 g/kg on day 3 — reduces renal impairment (10% vs 33%) and in-hospital mortality (10% vs 29%) vs antibiotics alone. (4) Assess response: repeat paracentesis at 48 hrs; PMN decrease 25% from baseline → broaden antibiotics, evaluate for secondary peritonitis. (5) Duration: 5–7 days. (6) Secondary prophylaxis (after first SBP episode): ciprofloxacin 500 mg daily or TMP-SMX DS daily — indefinite until transplant or death. (7) Primary prophylaxis: ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily for GI bleeding (up to 5 days); norfloxacin/ciprofloxacin for high-risk patients (ascites protein 1.5 g/dL + advanced cirrhosis). (8) Hold NSBBs if MAP 65 mmHg or AKI develops.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in empiric antibiotics when PMN >250/mm³. Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis in patients already on quinolone prophylaxis who develop SBP (resistance). PPIs in cirrhosis with ascites when not clearly indicated (increase SBP risk 4.3-fold). NSBBs during active SBP with hypotension or AKI.

treatments.notes: third-generation cephalosporin; first-line empiric therapy
equivalent to ceftriaxone; historically most studied
for nosocomial SBP or recent broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure
reduces renal impairment and mortality; greatest benefit if bilirubin >4 or Cr >1
after first SBP episode
alternative to ciprofloxacin
reduces infections and improves survival in cirrhosis with GI bleeding

diseases.name: Upper GI Bleed
diseases.management: UGIB management per the 2026 ESGE Guideline Update, 2024 Aliment Pharmacol Ther Review, and ACG/AASLD guidelines: (1) Risk stratification: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) ≤1 → consider outpatient management. (2) Resuscitation: IV crystalloids; restrictive RBC transfusion at Hgb 7 g/dL (target 7–9 g/dL); transfuse at Hgb 8 g/dL if cardiovascular disease; transfuse before Hgb reaches 7 if hemodynamically unstable. Do NOT give FFP or platelets based on INR/platelet targets. (3) Pre-endoscopy: erythromycin 250 mg IV 30 min before EGD (improves visualization); PPI may be considered but does not improve clinical outcomes; tranexamic acid NOT recommended. (4) Cirrhosis: prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily × up to 5 days) + vasoactive drugs (octreotide 50 mcg bolus then 25–50 mcg/hr, or terlipressin) started immediately. (5) Endoscopy within 24 hrs (not emergent 6 hrs unless hemodynamically unstable despite resuscitation). (6) Peptic ulcer with high-risk stigmata (Forrest Ia/Ib/IIa): endoscopic therapy (clips, thermal, OTS clips); epinephrine injection NOT as monotherapy. (7) Post-endoscopy: high-dose PPI (80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr × 72 hrs or intermittent BID-QID × 72 hrs) for high-risk ulcers. (8) Variceal bleeding: EVL for esophageal varices; cyanoacrylate for gastric varices; pre-emptive TIPS within 72 hrs for high-risk (Child-Pugh B ≥8 with active bleeding or C 10–13). (9) Rebleeding: repeat endoscopy → TAE → surgery. (10) Resume antithrombotics early when clinically indicated.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tranexamic acid (no benefit, potential harm). FFP/platelet transfusion based on INR/platelet targets in cirrhosis (no evidence of benefit). Emergent endoscopy (6 hrs) before adequate resuscitation (may worsen outcomes). Epinephrine injection as sole endoscopic therapy (must combine with second modality). Liberal transfusion strategy (Hgb threshold 9 g/dL — associated with higher mortality). Delay in antibiotics/vasoactive drugs in cirrhosis.

treatments.notes: goal: restore end-organ perfusion

treatments.dosage: transfuse at Hgb 7 g/dL (or 8 if CVD)
treatments.notes: restrictive strategy reduces mortality (RR 0.65) and rebleeding (RR 0.58)
prokinetic; improves visualization; reduces need for repeat EGD
may reduce high-risk stigmata; does not improve clinical outcomes
prophylactic antibiotics reduce infections and mortality in cirrhosis with UGIB
vasoactive drug; continue until bleeding controlled

treatments.dosage: pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion or 40 mg IV BID-QID

diseases.name: Lower GI Bleed
diseases.management: LGIB management per the 2023 ACG Guideline Update and 2021 ACR Appropriateness Criteria: (1) Most common cause: diverticulosis (40%); other causes: angiodysplasia, hemorrhoids, ischemic colitis, colorectal neoplasia, IBD. (2) ~80% of LGIB is self-limited. (3) Resuscitation: IV crystalloids; restrictive RBC transfusion (Hgb 7 g/dL threshold, extrapolated from UGIB data). (4) Hemodynamically stable: colonoscopy after bowel preparation within 24 hrs of admission (diagnostic and therapeutic); early colonoscopy (24 hrs) has not demonstrated improved patient-oriented outcomes vs elective inpatient colonoscopy. (5) Hemodynamically unstable or massive bleeding: CT angiography (CTA) is imaging of choice → if positive, transcatheter arterial embolization (TAE). (6) Endoscopic hemostasis: clips, thermal therapy, epinephrine injection for identified bleeding source (diverticular bleed, angiodysplasia). (7) Surgery: last resort for uncontrolled bleeding or failed endoscopic/radiologic intervention. (8) Anticoagulant/antiplatelet management: resume antithrombotics as soon as clinically indicated based on thromboembolic risk.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine urgent colonoscopy (24 hrs) without evidence of improved outcomes. Technetium-99m RBC scintigraphy as routine first-line (inferior to CTA). Surgery before attempting endoscopic/radiologic intervention. Delay in resuming antithrombotics when clinically indicated. Subtotal colectomy without localization of bleeding source (high morbidity).

treatments.notes: same principles as UGIB
extrapolated from UGIB data
diagnostic and therapeutic; early colonoscopy not shown to improve outcomes
imaging of choice for active/severe hemorrhage or when colonoscopy not feasible
following positive CTA; preferred over surgery
for diverticular bleed, angiodysplasia

diseases.name: Sepsis / Septic Shock
diseases.management: Sepsis management per the 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC) Guidelines: (1) Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction (SOFA ≥2) caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors required to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. (2) Screening: use NEWS, MEWS, or SIRS — NOT qSOFA alone (strong recommendation against qSOFA as single screening tool). (3) Measure blood lactate if sepsis suspected. (4) Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, but do NOT delay antibiotics. (5) Administer IV antibiotics as soon as possible — ideally within 1 hour for septic shock, within 3 hours for sepsis without shock. (6) Initial fluid resuscitation: at least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid within first 3 hours (weak recommendation, downgraded from strong). Use balanced crystalloids (LR) over NS (weak recommendation). (7) Vasopressors: norepinephrine first-line; add vasopressin (up to 0.03 U/min) as second agent; add epinephrine if needed. Avoid dopamine except in select patients with bradycardia. (8) MAP target 65 mmHg. (9) Corticosteroids: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for refractory shock (vasopressor-dependent despite adequate fluids). (10) Source control within 6–12 hours. (11) De-escalate antibiotics based on cultures. (12) Short-course antibiotics (7 days for most infections). (13) Lung-protective ventilation if ARDS (6 mL/kg IBW). (14) VTE prophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPI for high-risk), glycemic control (target 180 mg/dL).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine as first-line vasopressor (increased arrhythmia risk). qSOFA as sole screening tool. Hydroxyethyl starch (HES) for resuscitation (increased AKI and mortality). Albumin as initial resuscitation fluid (no benefit over crystalloids for most patients). Delay in antibiotics for culture results.

treatments.notes: balanced crystalloids preferred over NS
first-line vasopressor
add when NE dose 0.25–0.5 mcg/kg/min
if MAP not achieved with NE + vasopressin
for vasopressor-dependent shock
de-escalate based on cultures

diseases.name: Cellulitis
diseases.management: Cellulitis management per the 2014 IDSA SSTI Guideline and 2026 AAFP Review: (1) Cellulitis is a clinical diagnosis — cultures of blood or skin are NOT routinely recommended. (2) Nonpurulent, mild cellulitis: oral antibiotics covering streptococci and MSSA — cephalexin or penicillin VK × 5 days. Addition of TMP-SMX does NOT improve cure rates for nonpurulent cellulitis. (3) Moderate cellulitis (systemic signs): IV antibiotics — cefazolin or oxacillin. (4) Severe cellulitis (SIRS, immunocompromised, penetrating trauma, MRSA risk): vancomycin or linezolid + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem. (5) Purulent cellulitis/abscess: I&D is primary treatment; add TMP-SMX or clindamycin for MRSA coverage (NNT 11 for TMP-SMX after I&D). (6) Elevation of affected area. (7) Treat predisposing factors (edema, tinea pedis, lymphedema). (8) Recurrence rate ~29%; consider prophylactic penicillin for recurrent cellulitis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine blood/skin cultures for uncomplicated cellulitis. TMP-SMX added to cephalexin for nonpurulent cellulitis (no benefit). Bilateral cellulitis diagnosis (rare — consider venous stasis dermatitis). Fluoroquinolones as first-line.

treatments.notes: covers streptococci and MSSA
anti-staphylococcal penicillin

for systemic signs requiring hospitalization
for severe infection or confirmed MRSA

diseases.name: Erysipelas
diseases.management: Erysipelas is a superficial skin infection involving the upper dermis and lymphatics, almost always caused by Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes). Distinguished from cellulitis by sharply demarcated, raised borders and more superficial involvement. Treatment is identical to nonpurulent cellulitis: oral penicillin or cephalexin × 5 days for mild cases; IV penicillin G or cefazolin for moderate-severe. Elevation of affected limb. Treat predisposing factors (lymphedema, tinea pedis).
diseases.notes: Avoid: MRSA coverage for classic erysipelas (GAS is the cause). Fluoroquinolones.

treatments.notes: narrow-spectrum; targets GAS
broader coverage
for systemic signs

diseases.name: Necrotizing Fasciitis
diseases.management: Necrotizing fasciitis management per the 2014 IDSA Guideline and 2024 JAMA Surgery Review: (1) Surgical emergency — urgent surgical debridement is the most important determinant of outcome. (2) Clinical features: pain out of proportion to exam, rapid progression, crepitus, skin necrosis, systemic toxicity. NECROSIS score (SBP ≤120, WBC ≥15,000, violaceous skin) has 100% PPV if all 3 present. (3) Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Gram-positive (including MRSA), Gram-negative, and anaerobes: vancomycin or linezolid + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + clindamycin (for toxin suppression). (4) Return to OR every 24–36 hours for re-exploration until no further debridement needed. (5) Continue antibiotics until source control achieved and afebrile for 48–72 hours. (6) ICU admission. (7) Mortality 17–34% even with optimal care.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgical debridement for imaging (CT/MRI). Narrow-spectrum antibiotics. Conservative management without surgery. Reliance on LRINEC score alone (poor sensitivity).

treatments.dosage: vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h + pip-tazo 4.5 g q6h + clindamycin 600–900 mg q8h
treatments.notes: clindamycin for toxin suppression

treatments.dosage: vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h + meropenem 1 g q8h + clindamycin 600–900 mg q8h
treatments.notes: carbapenem for resistant Gram-negatives

treatments.dosage: aggressive excision of all necrotic tissue
treatments.notes: most important intervention; do NOT delay for imaging

diseases.name: Skin Abscess
diseases.management: Skin abscess management per the 2014 IDSA Guideline and 2026 AAFP Review: (1) Incision and drainage (I&D) is the primary treatment — curative in most cases. (2) Packing does NOT appear to reduce recurrence for abscesses up to 5 cm. Loop drainage is preferred over traditional I&D. (3) Adjunctive antibiotics after I&D reduce treatment failure (NNT 11 for TMP-SMX): TMP-SMX 1–2 DS BID × 7 days or clindamycin 300 mg TID × 7 days. (4) MRSA is the most common pathogen in community-acquired abscesses. (5) Wound cultures are not routinely needed (predictable microbiology). (6) Large or deep abscesses may require surgical consultation. (7) Point-of-care ultrasound reliably differentiates cellulitis from abscess (LR+ 8.6, LR− 0.07).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics alone without I&D (insufficient). Routine wound cultures. Packing for small abscesses ≤5 cm.

treatments.notes: primary treatment; packing not needed for abscesses ≤5 cm
reduces treatment failure; NNT 11
MRSA coverage; more GI side effects

diseases.name: Osteomyelitis
diseases.management: Osteomyelitis management per the 2022 WikiGuidelines Consensus and 2015 IDSA NVO Guideline: (1) Obtain bone biopsy with culture before starting antibiotics when feasible (gold standard for diagnosis and antibiotic selection). (2) Empiric antibiotics: ceftriaxone ± vancomycin (add vancomycin if MRSA risk factors). (3) Culture-directed therapy: MSSA → nafcillin/oxacillin or cefazolin IV → oral dicloxacillin, cephalexin, or TMP-SMX; MRSA → vancomycin IV → oral TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or linezolid. (4) Duration: 4–6 weeks total; IV-to-oral switch is safe and effective (OVIVA trial: oral non-inferior to IV at 1 year). (5) Add rifampin for prosthetic joint/implant-associated infections (reduces biofilm). (6) Surgical debridement for necrotic bone, hardware-associated infection, or failure of medical therapy. (7) Diabetic foot osteomyelitis: ampicillin-sulbactam or amoxicillin-clavulanate for polymicrobial coverage.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Rifampin monotherapy (rapid resistance). Empiric antibiotics before bone biopsy when biopsy is feasible (delays diagnosis). Prolonged IV therapy when oral step-down is appropriate. Aminoglycosides as sole therapy (poor bone penetration).

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g daily ± vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h
treatments.notes: add vancomycin if MRSA risk
anti-staphylococcal penicillin

diseases.name: Septic Arthritis
diseases.management: Septic arthritis management per the 2020 French SFR/SPILF Recommendations and 2021 AAFP Review: (1) Medical emergency — joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis is mandatory (WBC >50,000/mm³ with >90% PMNs is highly suggestive; crystal analysis to rule out gout/pseudogout). (2) Blood cultures before antibiotics. (3) Empiric IV antibiotics covering S. aureus and streptococci: cefazolin or nafcillin (MSSA); vancomycin if MRSA risk. Add Gram-negative coverage for elderly, immunocompromised, or GU/GI source. Consider N. gonorrhoeae in sexually active young adults. (4) Joint drainage: serial arthrocentesis vs arthroscopic lavage vs open surgical drainage — no clear superiority; hip and shoulder joints often require surgical drainage. (5) Duration: 2–4 weeks (gonococcal: 7–14 days). (6) Oral step-down after clinical improvement (non-inferior to prolonged IV per OVIVA-related data). (7) Early physical therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in joint aspiration and antibiotics. Corticosteroids as adjunct (guidelines recommend against). Empiric antibiotics before synovial fluid aspiration when feasible. Prolonged immobilization (early PT recommended).

treatments.notes: covers MSSA and streptococci
for MRSA risk factors
for disseminated gonococcal infection
hip and shoulder often require surgical drainage
after clinical improvement

diseases.name: Bacterial Meningitis
diseases.management: Bacterial meningitis management per the 2026 AAFP Review, IDSA Guidelines, and ESCMID Guidelines: (1) Medical emergency — initiate empiric antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation. (2) Empiric therapy by age: neonates → ampicillin + cefotaxime (or gentamicin); 1 month–50 years → ceftriaxone + vancomycin; >50 years or immunocompromised → ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (Listeria coverage). (3) Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days — give 15–20 min before or with first dose of antibiotics; reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis and hearing loss in children. (4) LP before antibiotics if no contraindication; do NOT delay antibiotics for imaging — antibiotics do not sterilize CSF for 2–6 hours. CT before LP only if: immunocompromised, history of CNS disease, new-onset seizure, papilledema, altered consciousness, or focal neurologic deficit. (5) Duration: N. meningitidis 7 days; S. pneumoniae 10–14 days; Listeria 21 days; GBS 14–21 days. (6) Close contacts of meningococcal meningitis: chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in antibiotics for LP or imaging. CT before LP in all patients (only if specific indications). Dexamethasone after antibiotics have been given (benefit lost). Oral step-down for bacterial meningitis (IV throughout). Empiric antibiotics without Listeria coverage in patients >50 or immunocompromised.

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g q12h + vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h
treatments.notes: empiric; narrow based on culture

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 2 g q12h + vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h + ampicillin 2 g q4h
treatments.notes: ampicillin for Listeria coverage

treatments.dosage: ampicillin 50 mg/kg q6–8h + cefotaxime 50 mg/kg q6–8h
treatments.notes: covers GBS, E. coli, Listeria
give before or with first antibiotic dose; reduces mortality (pneumococcal) and hearing loss

treatments.dosage: rifampin 600 mg q12h × 2 days, OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg single dose, OR ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose

diseases.name: Encephalitis
diseases.management: Encephalitis management per the 2026 Lancet Review and IDSA Guidelines: (1) Empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for all suspected encephalitis (HSV-1 is most common treatable cause; greatest benefit within 24–48 hours of symptom onset). (2) Duration: ≥14 days for HSV encephalitis (21 days if immunocompromised). (3) Diagnosis: CSF HSV PCR (sensitivity >95%); MRI brain (temporal lobe involvement classic for HSV). (4) Other specific antivirals: ganciclovir for CMV encephalitis; acyclovir/valacyclovir for VZV. (5) Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2): immunotherapy (IVIG, corticosteroids, plasma exchange) — early administration associated with improved outcomes. (6) Supportive care: seizure management, ICP monitoring, airway protection. (7) Epilepsy more common after infectious encephalitis than autoimmune.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in empiric acyclovir. Withholding acyclovir pending PCR results. Corticosteroids for infectious encephalitis without concurrent antimicrobials. Phenytoin as first-line anti-seizure (drug interactions, side effects).

treatments.notes: start empirically in all suspected encephalitis; do not wait for PCR
for CMV encephalitis in immunocompromised
early immunotherapy improves outcomes
high-dose pulse steroids

diseases.name: Uncomplicated UTI (Cystitis)
diseases.management: Uncomplicated UTI management per the 2011 IDSA/ESMID Guideline and 2024 AAFP Rapid Evidence Review: (1) Diagnosis in women: clinical symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency) without vaginal discharge are sufficient — urine culture NOT routinely needed. (2) Urine culture indicated for: recurrent UTI, treatment failure, history of resistant isolates, atypical presentation, men, or age ≥65. (3) First-line antibiotics: nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID × 5 days; fosfomycin 3 g single dose; TMP-SMX 160/800 mg BID × 3 days (if local resistance 20%); pivmecillinam 400 mg TID × 3 days (FDA-approved 2025). (4) Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis (FDA black box warnings, collateral damage). (5) Men with UTI: always obtain urine culture; treat × 7 days; consider prostatitis. (6) Symptomatic treatment with NSAIDs and delayed antibiotics may be considered (low complication risk). (7) Prevention of recurrent UTI: increased fluid intake (≥1.5 L/day), cranberry products, methenamine hippurate, vaginal estrogen (postmenopausal).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis (FDA black box; reserve for complicated UTI). Routine urine culture for uncomplicated cystitis in women. Treating asymptomatic bacteriuria (except in pregnancy or pre-urologic procedure).

treatments.notes: first-line; low resistance; avoid if CrCl 30
convenient; slightly lower efficacy than nitrofurantoin

diseases.name: Pyelonephritis
diseases.management: Pyelonephritis management per the 2011 IDSA Guideline and 2018 NEJM Review: (1) Always obtain urine culture and susceptibility testing. (2) Outpatient (mild-moderate, no nausea/vomiting): ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 7 days (if local FQ resistance 10%); levofloxacin 750 mg daily × 5 days; TMP-SMX 160/800 mg BID × 14 days (if susceptible). If FQ resistance >10%, give initial one-time IV dose of ceftriaxone 1 g or aminoglycoside before starting oral therapy. (3) Inpatient (severe, sepsis, unable to tolerate PO): ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV daily; piperacillin-tazobactam; fluoroquinolone IV; carbapenem for MDR risk. (4) Duration: FQ 5–7 days; beta-lactam 7 days; TMP-SMX 7–14 days. (5) Imaging (CT) if no improvement within 48 hours (rule out obstruction, abscess). (6) Nitrofurantoin and oral fosfomycin should NOT be used (inadequate tissue levels).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Nitrofurantoin (inadequate tissue levels). Oral fosfomycin (inadequate renal tissue levels). Empiric FQ if local resistance >10% without initial parenteral dose. Delay in imaging if no improvement at 48 hours.

treatments.notes: if local FQ resistance 10%
alternative FQ

diseases.name: Prostatitis
diseases.management: Prostatitis management per the 2025 JAMA Review and 2026 CID State-of-the-Art Review: (1) Acute bacterial prostatitis (ABP): UTI with systemic illness + prostatic tenderness on DRE. First-line: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for moderate-severe (piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftriaxone, or ciprofloxacin); oral ciprofloxacin for mildly ill. Duration: minimum 2 weeks (some experts recommend 4 weeks). Prostatic abscess (3–6% of ABP): drainage if >1–2 cm. (2) Chronic bacterial prostatitis (CBP): recurrent UTIs from same strain; diagnosis via 4-glass or 2-glass Meares-Stamey test. First-line: fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin) × minimum 4–6 weeks (best prostatic penetration). Alternatives: TMP-SMX, doxycycline, fosfomycin × 4–6 weeks. (3) Chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CP/CPPS): no infection identified; first-line α-blockers (tamsulosin) for urinary symptoms; NSAIDs for pain; pregabalin; pollen extract.

treatments.notes: good prostatic penetration
for hospit

diseases.name: Streptococcal Pharyngitis
diseases.management: GAS pharyngitis management per the 2012 IDSA Guideline, ACP/CDC Advice, and 2011 NEJM Review: (1) Diagnosis: use Centor criteria to guide testing — 0–1 criteria: no testing/treatment; ≥2 criteria: rapid antigen detection test (RADT); treat if positive. Throat culture backup for negative RADT in children (not routinely needed in adults). (2) First-line: penicillin V or amoxicillin × 10 days. (3) Penicillin allergy: cephalexin (non-type I allergy), azithromycin, or clindamycin. (4) Antibiotics shorten symptoms by 1–2 days and prevent acute rheumatic fever. (5) Analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) for all patients. (6) Tonsillectomy not recommended solely for recurrent pharyngitis in adults.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics without positive streptococcal test. Fluoroquinolones. Routine throat culture in adults with negative RADT. Treatment of chronic GAS carriers (low risk of complications/transmission).

treatments.notes: narrow-spectrum; no resistance reported
equally effective; better taste for children
for adherence concerns
first-generation cephalosporin
macrolide resistance ~30% in US

diseases.name: Acute Otitis Media
diseases.management: AOM management per the 2013 AAP Guideline and 2025 NEJM Review: (1) Diagnosis requires: symptoms + moderate-severe TM bulging, or new-onset otorrhea, or mild bulging with recent ear pain or intense TM erythema. (2) Observation (watchful waiting) without antibiotics is appropriate for: children ≥6 months with mild unilateral AOM without otorrhea; reassess in 48–72 hours. (3) When antibiotics indicated: high-dose amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID × 10 days (children 2 years or severe) or 5–7 days (≥2 years, mild). (4) Amoxicillin-clavulanate if: amoxicillin in past 30 days, concurrent conjunctivitis, or treatment failure. (5) Pain management: ibuprofen or acetaminophen for all patients. (6) Tympanostomy tubes for recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in 12 months).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Decongestants and antihistamines (no benefit, potential harm). Routine antibiotics for mild unilateral AOM in children ≥6 months. Azithromycin as first-line (inferior efficacy).

treatments.notes: first-line; covers S. pneumoniae
for treatment failure, recent amoxicillin use, or conjunctivitis-otitis

diseases.name: Acute Rhinosinusitis
diseases.management: Acute rhinosinusitis management per the 2025 AAO-HNS Guideline Update, 2016 NEJM Review, and 2025 AAFP Review: (1) 90–98% of cases are viral; only 0.5–2% of URIs develop ABRS. (2) Diagnose ABRS when: symptoms persist ≥10 days without improvement, OR symptoms worsen within 10 days after initial improvement ("double worsening"). (3) Watchful waiting (without antibiotics) is recommended for ALL patients with uncomplicated ABRS (strong recommendation, AAO-HNS 2025 — extended from prior guideline which limited to "mild" cases). (4) If antibiotics chosen: amoxicillin with or without clavulanate × 5–7 days (first-line). Doxycycline or respiratory fluoroquinolone for penicillin allergy. (5) Supportive care: intranasal corticosteroids, saline irrigation, analgesics. (6) No imaging for uncomplicated ARS. (7) Refer to ENT if recurrent (≥4 episodes/year) or concern for complications.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics for viral rhinosinusitis (10 days of symptoms without worsening). Imaging for uncomplicated ARS. Oral decongestants >5 days. Antihistamines (unless allergic component). Oral corticosteroids.

treatments.notes: strong recommendation (AAO-HNS 2025); safety-net prescription acceptable

treatments.dosage: amoxicillin 1000 mg TID or amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID
treatments.notes: amoxicillin alone is as effective as amoxicillin-clavulanate (AAFP)
alternative first-line
NNT 14 for symptom improvement

diseases.name: Influenza
diseases.management: Influenza management per the 2024 WHO Guideline, 2022 Lancet Review, and CDC recommendations: (1) Diagnosis: rapid molecular assay (RT-PCR) or rapid antigen test; home-based tests now available. (2) Antiviral treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset: oseltamivir (most widely used), baloxavir (single-dose, superior viral load reduction), zanamivir (inhaled), peramivir (IV). (3) Treat ALL hospitalized patients and high-risk outpatients regardless of symptom duration. (4) Baloxavir has greater efficacy against influenza B than oseltamivir and may reduce complications more effectively. (5) Supportive care: antipyretics, hydration. (6) Annual influenza vaccination is the primary prevention strategy. (7) Post-exposure prophylaxis: oseltamivir or baloxavir for high-risk contacts.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in antiviral treatment beyond 48 hours in high-risk patients (still treat if hospitalized). Antibiotics for uncomplicated influenza (strong recommendation against, WHO). Corticosteroids for non-severe influenza (conditional recommendation against, WHO).

treatments.notes: most widely studied; safe in pregnancy; reduces symptoms by ~1 day

treatments.dosage: weight-based single dose (40 mg if 80 kg, 80 mg if ≥80 kg)

diseases.name: COVID-19
diseases.management: COVID-19 management per the 2025 IDSA Guideline Update and 2026 ACP Practice Points (Version 3): (1) Outpatient, high-risk, mild-moderate: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) within 5 days of symptom onset (preferred — reduces mortality and hospitalization); molnupiravir as alternative. Remdesivir 3-day IV course for high-risk outpatients. (2) Hospitalized, non-severe: remdesivir × 5 days; dexamethasone 6 mg daily × 10 days if supplemental O2 required. (3) Hospitalized, severe/critical (mechanical ventilation): dexamethasone 6 mg daily × 10 days; tocilizumab or baricitinib if progressive despite steroids. (4) Drug interactions: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor — check interactions (statins, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants). (5) Renal dosing: nirmatrelvir dose reduction if eGFR 30–60; contraindicated if eGFR 30. (6) Molnupiravir: EUA only; not for pregnancy (mutagenic potential).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir with eGFR 30 (contraindicated). Hydroxychloroquine (no benefit, potential harm). Ivermectin (no benefit). Molnupiravir in pregnancy. Dexamethasone in patients NOT requiring supplemental O2 (may increase mortality).

treatments.notes: reduces mortality and hospitalization; CYP3A4 inhibitor — check interactions; dose reduce if eGFR 30–60
FDA-approved for all ages ≥1.5 kg

RECOVERY trial: reduces 28-day mortality in patients on O2 or ventilation

diseases.name: HIV / AIDS
diseases.management: HIV management per the 2024 IAS-USA Panel Recommendations and NIH Guidelines: (1) Start ART as soon as possible after diagnosis — ideally within 7 days, including same-day initiation if ready and no active OI. (2) Recommended initial regimens (all InSTI-based): bictegravir/TAF/FTC (Biktarvy) — single tablet, once daily; dolutegravir + TAF/FTC or TDF/FTC; dolutegravir/lamivudine (Dovato) — 2-drug regimen, only if HIV RNA 500,000 and no HBV coinfection, genotype available. (3) If InSTI resistance suspected (e.g., after cabotegravir PrEP failure): boosted darunavir + TXF/XTC. (4) Monitor: HIV RNA at 2–4 weeks, then q4–8 weeks until undetectable, then q3–6 months; CD4 count; renal function; lipids; HBV/HCV coinfection. (5) PrEP: TDF/FTC or TAF/FTC daily oral; cabotegravir IM q2 months (superior to oral PrEP). (6) Doxy-PEP: doxycycline 200 mg within 72 hours after condomless sex for MSM/transgender women with recent STI history — reduces syphilis, chlamydia, gonorrhea.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dolutegravir/lamivudine without genotype results or with HBV coinfection. Efavirenz-based regimens as first-line (inferior tolerability). Abacavir without HLA-B5701 testing. Stopping ART without medical indication. Delay in ART initiation.

treatments.notes: single-tablet regimen; high barrier to resistance
equally effective; flexible NRTI backbone
2-drug regimen; only if HIV RNA 500,000, no HBV, genotype available
PrEP for HIV prevention; Descovy not studied for receptive vaginal sex
long-acting injectable PrEP; superior to oral TDF/FTC
reduces syphilis, chlamydia in MSM/transgender women

diseases.name: Malaria
diseases.management: Malaria management per the 2025 NEJM Review and WHO Guidelines: (1) Uncomplicated P. falciparum (chloroquine-resistant or unknown): artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) is first-line ACT — 3-day course with fatty food. Alternatives: atovaquone-proguanil × 3 days; quinine + doxycycline/clindamycin × 7 days. (2) Chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum (limited regions): chloroquine × 3 days. (3) P. vivax/ovale: chloroquine (or ACT if chloroquine-resistant area) + primaquine 14 days or tafenoquine single dose for radical cure (prevent relapse) — requires G6PD testing before primaquine/tafenoquine. (4) Severe malaria: IV artesunate (first-line) 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12, 24 hours then daily; follow with full oral ACT course. Monitor for delayed hemolysis × 4 weeks after IV artesunate. (5) Prophylaxis: atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine based on destination.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Chloroquine for P. falciparum from chloroquine-resistant areas. Primaquine/tafenoquine without G6PD testing. Artemisinin monotherapy (promotes resistance). Mefloquine in SE Asia (resistance). Delay in IV artesunate for severe malaria.

treatments.notes: take with fatty food; most widely used ACT
for returned travelers; contraindicated in pregnancy

treatments.dosage: 600 mg base then 300 mg at 6, 24, 48 hrs
treatments.notes: for P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, and sensitive P. falciparum

diseases.name: Lyme Disease
diseases.management: Lyme disease management per the 2020 IDSA/AAN/ACR Guideline: (1) Erythema migrans (early localized): doxycycline 100 mg BID × 10 days (preferred — also covers Anaplasma coinfection and prevents early Lyme); amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 14 days; cefuroxime 500 mg BID × 14 days. Azithromycin is second-line only. (2) Early disseminated (multiple EM, cranial nerve palsy, carditis without high-degree AV block, arthritis): oral doxycycline or amoxicillin. (3) Lyme meningitis or high-degree AV block: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily × 14–21 days (can switch to oral after stabilization). (4) Lyme arthritis: oral doxycycline or amoxicillin × 28 days; if refractory, IV ceftriaxone × 14–28 days. (5) Single-dose doxycycline 200 mg prophylaxis within 72 hours of tick removal in endemic areas (tick attached ≥36 hours). (6) 7-day doxycycline may be sufficient for solitary EM (non-inferior to 14 days in European RCT).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Azithromycin as first-line (second-line only). Prolonged antibiotic courses beyond guideline recommendations. Serologic testing without clinical suspicion. Treatment based on positive serology alone without compatible clinical syndrome.

treatments.notes: preferred; also covers Anaplasma; 7 days may be sufficient
for children 8 years, pregnancy
for penicillin/doxycycline intolerance
can switch to oral after stabilization
within 72 hrs of tick removal; tick attached ≥36 hrs in endemic area

diseases.name: Syphilis
diseases.management: Syphilis management per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines and 2025 JAMA Review: (1) Early syphilis (primary, secondary, early latent): benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM single dose. (2) Late latent syphilis or tertiary (without neurosyphilis): benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM weekly × 3 weeks. (3) Neurosyphilis, ocular, or otic syphilis: aqueous crystalline penicillin G 18–24 million units/day IV (3–4 million units q4h) × 10–14 days. (4) Penicillin allergy: doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days (early) or 28 days (late); ceftriaxone 1 g IM/IV daily × 10 days. No alternative to penicillin in pregnancy — desensitize if allergic. (5) Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction in ~30% (treat with antipyretics). (6) Screen: all sexually active persons 15–44 at least once; pregnant patients 3 times (first prenatal, third trimester, delivery). (7) Doxy-PEP: doxycycline 200 mg within 72 hours post-exposure for MSM/transgender women with recent STI.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Azithromycin for syphilis (resistance documented). Alternatives to penicillin in pregnancy (insufficient data — desensitize). Benzathine penicillin G for neurosyphilis (inadequate CSF levels).

treatments.notes: 90–100% treatment success; no resistance reported
only regimen with adequate CSF penetration
for penicillin allergy
limited data; avoid if severe penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity risk)

diseases.name: Gonorrhea
diseases.management: Gonorrhea management per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Uncomplicated urogenital, anorectal, or pharyngeal gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (150 kg) or 1 g IM (≥150 kg). (2) If chlamydia not excluded: add doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days. (3) Azithromycin co-treatment is NO LONGER routinely recommended (antimicrobial stewardship). (4) Alternative (if ceftriaxone unavailable): gentamicin 240 mg IM + azithromycin 2 g PO single dose; or cefixime 800 mg PO single dose. (5) Test of cure: only for pharyngeal gonorrhea (14 days after treatment). (6) Retest for reinfection at 3 months.

treatments.notes: monotherapy; stable MICs in US
co-treatment for presumptive chlamydia

treatments.dosage: gentamicin 240 mg IM + azithromycin 2 g PO
treatments.notes: for cephalosporin allergy
less effective for pharyngeal gonorrhea

diseases.name: Vaginal Candidiasis
diseases.management: Per the 2016 IDSA Guideline and 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Candida albicans causes ~90% of cases. (2) Uncomplicated VVC (~90% of cases): topical azole (clotrimazole, miconazole) × 1–7 days OR single-dose fluconazole 150 mg PO — both achieve >90% response. Oral and topical are equally effective. (3) Severe acute VVC: fluconazole 150 mg PO every 72 hrs × 2–3 doses. (4) Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year): induction with fluconazole 150 mg PO every 72 hrs × 3 doses, then maintenance fluconazole 150 mg weekly × 6 months. Oteseconazole (FDA-approved for RVVC prevention in non-reproductive-potential females). (5) C. glabrata (azole-resistant): intravaginal boric acid 600 mg daily × 14 days; alternatives include nystatin suppositories or topical flucytosine ± amphotericin B cream. (6) Pregnancy: topical azoles only (7-day course preferred); avoid oral fluconazole (associated with spontaneous abortion and birth defects). (7) Confirm diagnosis with KOH prep or culture before treating recurrent episodes.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oral fluconazole in pregnancy (teratogenic risk). Oral azoles for C. glabrata (often resistant). Oteseconazole in reproductive-potential females (embryofetal toxicity). Empiric treatment without confirming diagnosis in recurrent cases.

treatments.notes: >90% response; equally effective as topical
OTC available; preferred in pregnancy

treatments.dosage: 150 mg q72h × 3 doses then 150 mg weekly
treatments.notes: ~90% suppression during maintenance; ~50% relapse after stopping

diseases.name: Asthma Exacerbation
diseases.management: Acute asthma exacerbation management per GINA 2023 and the 2020 NAEPP EPR-4 Guideline: (1) Assess severity (mild/moderate vs severe vs life-threatening) using vital signs, SpO2, PEF/FEV1, ability to speak, accessory muscle use. (2) Immediate treatment: supplemental O2 to target SpO2 93–95% in adults; repeated SABA (albuterol 4–10 puffs MDI q20min × 1 hr or continuous nebulization); systemic corticosteroids within the first hour. (3) Add ipratropium for moderate-severe exacerbations (reduces hospitalizations). (4) IV magnesium sulfate for severe exacerbations not responding to initial therapy. (5) High-dose ICS within the first hour may reduce need for hospitalization. (6) Discharge criteria: PEF/FEV1 60–80% predicted, symptom improvement, SpO2 >94%. (7) At discharge: oral prednisone 50 mg/day × 5–7 days (adults), initiate or step up ICS controller, follow-up within 2–7 days.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine antibiotics (unless bacterial infection suspected). Sedatives/anxiolytics (respiratory depression). Routine chest X-ray (unless alternative diagnosis suspected). Excessive O2 targeting SpO2 >95% (may cause hypercapnia). Short-term ICS dose increase alone for worsening symptoms (not effective).

treatments.dosage: 4–10 puffs MDI q20min × 3 doses or 2.5–5 mg nebulized q20min × 3
treatments.notes: SABA; continuous nebulization for severe
add to SABA; reduces hospitalizations
give within first hour; no taper needed for short course
if unable to take PO; not superior to oral route

diseases.name: Asthma (Chronic)
diseases.management: Chronic asthma management per GINA 2023 and the 2020 NAEPP EPR-4 follows a stepwise approach. GINA Track 1 (preferred): ICS-formoterol as reliever at ALL steps — as-needed only in Steps 1–2 (mild), with daily maintenance in Steps 3–5 (MART = maintenance and reliever therapy). Track 2 (alternative): as-needed SABA across all steps with regular ICS (Step 2) or ICS-LABA (Steps 3–5). Key principles: (1) SABA-only treatment is no longer recommended — all patients should receive ICS-containing therapy; (2) MART with ICS-formoterol reduces severe exacerbations by ≥60% vs SABA alone in mild asthma; (3) Step 3–4: low-to-medium dose ICS-formoterol MART preferred; (4) Step 5: add LAMA (tiotropium), consider biologics for severe asthma (anti-IgE, anti-IL5/5R, anti-IL4R, anti-TSLP); (5) Assess control, adherence, inhaler technique, and comorbidities at every visit; (6) Step down after 3 months of good control.
diseases.notes: Avoid: SABA-only treatment (increased exacerbation and mortality risk). Short-term ICS dose increase for worsening symptoms (no benefit). Montelukast as first-line over ICS (inferior). Long-acting beta-agonist monotherapy without ICS (increased mortality risk).

treatments.notes: as-needed only; reduces exacerbations vs SABA alone
single inhaler for both maintenance and relief
with SABA PRN for relief
add to ICS-LABA for uncontrolled asthma
LTRA; less effective than ICS; FDA black box warning for neuropsychiatric events

diseases.name: COPD Exacerbation
diseases.management: COPD exacerbation management per the 2026 GOLD Report: (1) Bronchodilators — increase dose/frequency of SABA ± short-acting anticholinergic (ipratropium); (2) Systemic corticosteroids — prednisone 40 mg/day × 5 days (Evidence A); (3) Antibiotics × 5 days if purulent sputum, prior positive sputum culture, or requiring mechanical ventilation (Evidence A) — first-line: amoxicillin-clavulanate, macrolide, or doxycycline; fluoroquinolone or broader coverage if MDR risk; (4) Controlled O2 to target SpO2 88–92%; (5) High-flow nasal therapy (HFNT) is first-line ventilatory support for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure; NIV for hypercarbic respiratory failure or HFNT failure — NIV reduces intubation, improves survival (Evidence A); (6) VTE prophylaxis; (7) At discharge: initiate/optimize LABD maintenance therapy; consider adding ICS if ≥1 moderate/severe exacerbation and elevated blood eosinophils.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Methylxanthines (theophylline/aminophylline) — not recommended due to increased side effects without clear benefit. High-flow O2 targeting SpO2 >92% (risk of CO2 retention). Routine antibiotics without indication.

treatments.notes: increase dose/frequency of short-acting bronchodilators
systemic corticosteroids; equally effective as longer courses
for purulent sputum or requiring ventilation

alternative first-line antibiotic
for repeated exacerbations or suspected resistance
for hypercarbic respiratory failure; reduces intubation and mortality

diseases.name: COPD (Chronic)
diseases.management: Chronic COPD management per the 2026 GOLD Report and 2020 ATS Guideline follows a group-based approach. GOLD groups: Group A (low symptoms, few exacerbations) — bronchodilator; Group B (high symptoms, few exacerbations) — LAMA + LABA; Group E (exacerbation-prone) — LAMA + LABA, consider adding ICS if blood eosinophils ≥300. Key principles: (1) Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow FEV1 decline; (2) LAMA/LABA combination is preferred over monotherapy (strong recommendation, ATS); (3) Triple therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA) for patients with exacerbations despite dual bronchodilator therapy, especially if eosinophils ≥300 — reduces exacerbations and mortality; (4) ICS withdrawal may be considered if no exacerbations in past year; (5) Non-pharmacological: pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19, pertussis, zoster), supplemental O2 if PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%; (6) Roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor) for frequent exacerbators with FEV1 50% and chronic bronchitis phenotype; (7) Azithromycin 250 mg daily for exacerbation prevention in former smokers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ICS monotherapy (not recommended; always combine with LABD). Maintenance oral corticosteroids (conditional recommendation against, ATS). Theophylline as first-line (narrow therapeutic index, limited benefit). Routine antibiotics beyond azithromycin prophylaxis.

treatments.notes: cornerstone bronchodilator
preferred over monotherapy (ATS strong recommendation)
reduces exacerbations and mortality; IMPACT and ETHOS trials

if PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% at rest

diseases.name: Hospital-Acquired / Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
diseases.management: HAP/VAP management per the 2016 IDSA/ATS Guideline: (1) Empiric antibiotic selection based on local antibiogram, risk factors for MDR pathogens, and mortality risk; (2) Low MDR risk: monotherapy with piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, levofloxacin, or meropenem; (3) High MDR risk: two anti-gram-negative agents from different classes (e.g., anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside); (4) Add MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid) if risk factors present or unit MRSA prevalence >10–20%; (5) De-escalate based on culture results; (6) Duration: 7 days for most patients (including non-fermenting GNRs); use procalcitonin to guide discontinuation; (7) Obtain respiratory cultures before starting antibiotics; semiquantitative cultures of noninvasive samples preferred.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aminoglycosides or colistin as monotherapy (use only in combination when alternatives unavailable). Prolonged antibiotic courses >7 days without clear indication. Empiric dual gram-negative coverage without MDR risk factors. Routine use of biomarkers alone for diagnosis.

treatments.notes: anti-pseudomonal coverage; monotherapy if ≥90% gram-negative susceptibility on antibiogram
anti-pseudomonal cephalosporin

treatments.useWhen: low MDR risk or high MDR risk

diseases.name: Aspiration Pneumonia
diseases.management: Aspiration pneumonia management per the 2019 ATS/IDSA Guideline and NEJM review: (1) Distinguish aspiration pneumonitis (chemical, often self-limited within 24–48 hrs, antibiotics usually not needed) from aspiration pneumonia (bacterial infection requiring antibiotics). (2) Standard CAP antibiotics provide adequate coverage for most aspiration pneumonia — specific anti-anaerobic therapy is NOT routinely needed, as most standard regimens (beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors, carbapenems, fluoroquinolones) already cover anaerobes. (3) Community-acquired aspiration: ampicillin-sulbactam, ertapenem, or respiratory fluoroquinolone. (4) Hospital-acquired aspiration: follow HAP/VAP guidelines based on MDR risk. (5) Add clindamycin ONLY if high risk of predominantly anaerobic infection (severe periodontal disease with necrotizing pneumonia or lung abscess). (6) Duration: 5–7 days for uncomplicated; longer for lung abscess or empyema. (7) Address underlying risk factors: dysphagia evaluation, aspiration precautions, oral hygiene.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine antibiotics for aspiration pneumonitis (self-limited; reassess at 48 hrs). Routine anaerobic-specific coverage (standard CAP regimens sufficient). Metronidazole monotherapy for aspiration pneumonia (inferior to clindamycin for anaerobic pulmonary infection). Routine clindamycin addition without clear anaerobic risk.

treatments.notes: covers aerobes and anaerobes
carbapenem with anaerobic coverage; no Pseudomonas coverage
respiratory fluoroquinolone
respiratory fluoroquinolone with anaerobic coverage

diseases.name: Pulmonary Edema (Cardiogenic)
diseases.management: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema management: (1) Position upright. (2) Respiratory support — NIPPV (CPAP or BiPAP) is first-line; reduces intubation and improves survival (multiple systematic reviews); HFNC as alternative; intubate if NIPPV fails or patient obtunded. (3) Vasodilator therapy — nitroglycerin is first-line; high-dose bolus NTG (400–800 mcg IV bolus, then infusion) is safe and effective for SCAPE (sympathetic crashing acute pulmonary edema); clevidipine or nicardipine if refractory hypertension. (4) Diuretics — IV furosemide for patients with evidence of systemic volume overload (peripheral edema, weight gain, cardiomegaly); NOT routinely for SCAPE without fluid overload (often fluid redistribution, not overload). (5) Treat underlying cause (ACS, arrhythmia, valvular emergency). (6) Avoid morphine (associated with increased intubation and mortality in observational studies).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Morphine/opioids (associated with increased intubation and mortality). Beta-blockers in acute setting (may worsen cardiogenic shock). Routine diuresis in SCAPE without volume overload. Excessive IV fluids.

treatments.notes: reduces intubation and mortality
rapid afterload reduction; first-line for SCAPE
titrate to response | for systemic volume overload; not routine in SCAPE without fluid overload

diseases.name: Pleural Effusion
diseases.management: Pleural effusion management: (1) Determine etiology — leading causes: heart failure (36%), infection, malignancy, PE. (2) Imaging: CXR for laterality/size; point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) strongly recommended for detection, characterization, and procedural guidance. (3) Thoracentesis indications: new unilateral effusion >1 cm on decubitus or >2 cm on US; effusion not explained by HF/cirrhosis/renal failure; effusion not improving with diuresis. Small bilateral effusions in decompensated HF do NOT require thoracentesis. (4) Pleural fluid analysis: Light's criteria to differentiate exudate vs transudate; send cell count, Gram stain, culture, cytology, protein, LDH, pH. (5) Parapneumonic effusion: simple (pH >7.2, culture negative) — antibiotics alone; complicated (pH 7.2, positive culture/Gram stain, or purulent) — chest tube drainage ± intrapleural tPA/DNase. (6) Malignant effusion: indwelling pleural catheter or pleurodesis for recurrent symptomatic effusions.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Thoracentesis for small bilateral effusions in decompensated HF (treat with diuretics first). Chest tube for hepatic hydrothorax (high complication rate, protein/electrolyte depletion). Removing >1.5 L in single thoracentesis without monitoring (re-expansion pulmonary edema risk). Routine pleural biopsy without first sending cytology.

treatments.dosage: remove up to 1–1.5 L per session
treatments.notes: for symptomatic relief; POCUS guidance strongly recommended
if pH >7.2, culture negative, non-loculated

treatments.dosage: tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg in 30 mL NS
treatments.notes: MIST2 trial; reduces surgical referral
outpatient management; spontaneous pleurodesis in ~45%

treatments.dosage: 4–5 g talc slurry via chest tube or poudrage via thoracoscopy

diseases.name: Pneumothorax
diseases.management: Pneumothorax management per the 2024 ERS/EACTS/ESTS Guideline and BTS/ACCP guidelines: (1) Classify as primary spontaneous (PSP), secondary spontaneous (SSP), traumatic, or iatrogenic. (2) PSP — minimally symptomatic: conservative management (observation, supplemental O2, analgesia) is reasonable; PSP resolution at 8 weeks is ~94% with conservative care vs ~99% with chest tube, with fewer complications and lower recurrence (PSP trial, NEJM 2020). Large or symptomatic PSP: needle aspiration first (success rate 50–70%); if aspiration fails → small-bore chest tube (≤14 Fr) with Heimlich valve or water seal. (3) SSP — all patients should be hospitalized; most require chest tube drainage due to poor pulmonary reserve; air leak >48 hrs → surgical referral. (4) Tension pneumothorax: immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular line or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) → chest tube. (5) Recurrence prevention: VATS with pleurodesis ± bullectomy for recurrent PSP or persistent air leak (>5–7 days). Smoking cessation reduces recurrence risk. Supplemental O2 accelerates pleural air reabsorption by 4-fold.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Large-bore chest tubes for uncomplicated pneumothorax (no benefit over small-bore, more painful). Routine suction (not shown to improve outcomes). Clamping chest tubes before removal (no evidence of benefit). Delay in decompression of tension pneumothorax.

treatments.notes: O2 accelerates reabsorption 4-fold; observe 4–6 hrs
success rate 50–70%; can discharge if successful
attach to Heimlich valve or water seal
for recurrent PSP, persistent air leak >5–7 days, or bilateral PSP

treatments.dosage: 14G needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS anterior axillary
treatments.notes: life-saving; follow with chest tube

diseases.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
diseases.management: OSA management per the ACP Guideline, AASM, and AAFP 2024 review: (1) Diagnosis: polysomnography is gold standard; home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) acceptable for suspected moderate-to-severe OSA without significant cardiopulmonary comorbidities. AHI ≥15 or AHI 5–14 with symptoms → treat. (2) CPAP is first-line therapy — most effective at reducing AHI to 4 hrs/night for >70% of nights; adherence ~75%. (3) Weight loss for all overweight/obese patients (strong recommendation, ACP). (4) Alternatives for CPAP-intolerant patients: mandibular advancement device (MAD) for mild-moderate OSA (AHI 30); hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire) for moderate-severe OSA with BMI 32 who fail CPAP; positional therapy for position-dependent OSA. (5) Surgical options: uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP), maxillomandibular advancement — generally not first-line. (6) Lifestyle: avoid alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids before sleep; sleep hygiene. No OSA therapy has demonstrated mortality benefit in RCTs.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol before sleep (worsen upper airway collapse). Sedative hypnotics in untreated OSA. UPPP as first-line (variable success, significant morbidity). Routine screening in asymptomatic patients (USPSTF: insufficient evidence).

treatments.notes: most effective therapy; improves ESS, reduces AHI; no proven mortality benefit

treatments.dosage: intensive lifestyle modification, GLP-1 RA, or bariatric surgery
treatments.notes: strong recommendation (ACP); reduces AHI
for mild-moderate OSA (AHI 30) or CPAP-intolerant patients
for moderate-severe OSA, BMI 32, CPAP-intolerant; no complete concentric collapse on DISE

diseases.name: Interstitial Lung Disease
diseases.management: ILD encompasses >200 disorders; management depends on subtype. Per ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT guidelines and JAMA 2024 review: (1) IPF: antifibrotic therapy is standard of care — nintedanib or pirfenidone (strong recommendation); both slow FVC decline by ~44–57%; nerandomilast (PDE4B inhibitor, FDA-approved 2025) can be used alongside or instead of standard antifibrotics. Immunosuppression (prednisone + azathioprine + NAC) is HARMFUL in IPF (PANTHER trial — increased mortality). (2) CTD-ILD (e.g., SSc-ILD): mycophenolate mofetil (strong recommendation); tocilizumab, nintedanib, rituximab, cyclophosphamide (conditional recommendations). (3) Progressive pulmonary fibrosis (PPF) of any cause: nintedanib (strong recommendation); pirfenidone (conditional). (4) Hypersensitivity pneumonitis: antigen avoidance + corticosteroids ± immunosuppression. (5) Sarcoidosis-related ILD: see Sarcoidosis entry. (6) Supportive care for all: pulmonary rehabilitation, supplemental O2 if SpO2 88%, vaccinations, lung transplant referral for advanced disease (refer at diagnosis for UIP pattern; refer when FVC 80% or DLCO 40% for other ILDs).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prednisone + azathioprine + NAC in IPF (PANTHER trial: increased mortality and hospitalization). Immunosuppression in IPF without confirmed inflammatory component. Delay in antifibrotic initiation for IPF. Corticosteroids for acute exacerbation of IPF (no convincing evidence).

treatments.notes: tyrosine kinase inhibitor; slows FVC decline; main SE: diarrhea; monitor LFTs
antifibrotic; main SE: nausea, photosensitivity; monitor LFTs
PDE4B inhibitor; FDA-approved 2025; can be used with or instead of nintedanib/pirfenidone; FIBRONEER-IPF trial
strong recommendation for SSc-ILD (ATS); immunomodulatory

treatments.dosage: 162 mg SC weekly or 8 mg/kg IV q4wk
treatments.notes: IL-6 inhibitor; FDA-approved for SSc-ILD; conditional recommendation
anti-CD20; conditional recommendation for CTD-ILD

diseases.name: Sarcoidosis
diseases.management: Sarcoidosis management per JAMA 2022 review and JACC 2020 review: (1) Not all patients require treatment — up to 80% of Stage I and 30–80% of Stage II achieve spontaneous remission. (2) Treatment indications: significant symptoms (cough, dyspnea), progressive pulmonary disease (worsening PFTs or imaging), or dangerous organ involvement (cardiac, neurologic, ocular, hypercalcemia). (3) First-line: oral prednisone 20–40 mg/day × 2–6 weeks, then taper over 6–18 months; 20 mg appears near top of dose-response curve with less toxicity. (4) Second-line (steroid-sparing): methotrexate 10–15 mg/week or azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day — for patients requiring prednisone ≥10 mg/day or with steroid intolerance. (5) Third-line: anti-TNF agents (infliximab 3–5 mg/kg IV q4–8 weeks). (6) Stage IV fibrocystic disease: antifibrotics may be considered; up to 70% develop precapillary pulmonary hypertension → treat with PAH-targeted therapies. (7) PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) and herpes zoster prophylaxis (acyclovir) if prednisone ≥20 mg/day for ≥1 month with additional immunosuppression. (8) Surveillance: spirometry, DLCO, CXR q3–12 months.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Treatment of asymptomatic Stage I sarcoidosis (high spontaneous remission rate). Prolonged high-dose corticosteroids without steroid-sparing agent (cumulative toxicity). Corticosteroids for Stage IV fibrocystic disease (unlikely to benefit). Immunosuppression without infection prophylaxis when indicated.

treatments.notes: for symptomatic pulmonary sarcoidosis with infiltrates and abnormal PFTs
steroid-sparing agent; monitor CBC, LFTs; supplement with folic acid

diseases.name: Tuberculosis
diseases.management: TB management per the 2016 ATS/CDC/IDSA Guideline (updated 2025): (1) Active drug-susceptible TB: standard 6-month regimen — intensive phase: isoniazid (INH) + rifampin (RIF) + pyrazinamide (PZA) + ethambutol (EMB) × 2 months; continuation phase: INH + RIF × 4 months. EMB can be stopped once susceptibility to INH and RIF confirmed. Daily dosing preferred (strong recommendation). Extend continuation phase to 7 months (total 9 months) if cavitary disease AND positive cultures at 2 months. (2) Alternative 4-month regimen: INH + rifapentine + moxifloxacin + PZA (4HPMZ) — endorsed by WHO/CDC for eligible adults ≥12 years with drug-susceptible TB. (3) Pyridoxine (B6) 25–50 mg/day with INH for patients at risk of neuropathy (diabetes, HIV, pregnancy, alcoholism, renal failure). (4) DOT recommended for all patients. (5) LTBI treatment (preferred): 3 months weekly INH + rifapentine (3HP); 4 months daily rifampin (4R); 3 months daily INH + rifampin (3HR). 6–9 months INH is alternative (lower completion rates). (6) MDR-TB: 6-month all-oral regimen — bedaquiline + pretomanid + linezolid ± moxifloxacin (BPaL ± M).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prednisone + azathioprine + NAC for TB (this is an IPF caution — included for clarity). Monotherapy for active TB (promotes resistance). Pyrazinamide beyond 2 months (hepatotoxicity without benefit). Fluoroquinolones empirically for respiratory symptoms without ruling out TB (may mask diagnosis and delay treatment). Ethambutol without baseline visual acuity testing (optic neuritis risk).

treatments.dosage: INH 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg), RIF 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg), PZA 25 mg/kg, EMB 15–20 mg/kg
treatments.notes: standard 4-drug intensive phase; DOT recommended

treatments.dosage: INH 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg), RIF 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg)
treatments.duration: 4 months (or 7 months if cavitary + culture-positive at 2 months)
treatments.notes: continuation phase
shorter regimen for eligible adults ≥12 yrs; endorsed by WHO/CDC 2022
