all-oral regimen for RR/MDR-TB; recommended by WHO 2022

diseases.name: Pulmonary Hypertension (Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension — WHO Group 1)
diseases.management: PAH management per the 2022 ESC/ERS Guideline and 2019 CHEST Guideline update: PH is defined as mPAP >20 mmHg; PAH (Group 1) requires PAWP ≤15 mmHg and PVR ≥3 WU on RHC. (1) Risk stratification guides therapy — low/intermediate/high risk based on WHO FC, 6MWD, BNP/NT-proBNP, hemodynamics. (2) Vasoreactivity testing for IPAH/HPAH/DPAH — responders: high-dose CCBs (nifedipine, diltiazem, amlodipine); reassess at 3–4 months. (3) Non-vasoreactive, low-to-intermediate risk: initial oral combination therapy — ERA + PDE5i (ambrisentan + tadalafil, or macitentan + tadalafil) is Class I. (4) If not at low-risk status at 3–6 months: add prostacyclin pathway agent (selexipag PO, inhaled treprostinil/iloprost, or parenteral prostacyclin). (5) High-risk at presentation: triple therapy including IV prostacyclin (epoprostenol — only PAH drug with proven mortality benefit). (6) Supportive: diuretics for RV volume overload, O2 if hypoxemic, supervised exercise rehabilitation, anticoagulation (individualized). (7) Lung transplant referral for refractory disease. (8) Opsynvi (macitentan + tadalafil fixed-dose combination) FDA-approved for PAH FC II–III.
diseases.notes: Avoid: CCBs in non-vasoreactive patients (may cause hypotension, syncope, death). Riociguat + PDE5i combination (contraindicated). Bosentan + sildenafil combination (no benefit on morbidity/mortality — COMPASS-2 trial). Initial triple oral therapy with macitentan + tadalafil + selexipag (Class III, ESC/ERS). Pregnancy in PAH (extremely high mortality).

treatments.notes: initial oral combination therapy; AMBITION trial; Class I
fixed-dose combination; FDA-approved for PAH FC II–III; Class I

sGC stimulator; do NOT combine with PDE5i

diseases.name: Hyperkalemia
diseases.management: Per the 2023 AAFP Guideline and 2025 AHA Guidelines: (1) Emergent treatment for K ≥6.5 mEq/L or ECG changes: IV calcium gluconate 1–2 g over 2–5 min (stabilizes cardiac membrane; no effect on K level). (2) Shift K intracellularly: regular insulin 10 U IV + D50 25 g (onset 15–30 min); nebulized albuterol 10–20 mg (additive effect); sodium bicarbonate only if metabolic acidosis present. (3) Remove K from body: loop diuretics (if volume overloaded); sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) 10 g TID × 48 hrs or patiromer 8.4 g daily (newer binders preferred over SPS); hemodialysis for refractory cases or ESRD. (4) Identify and treat cause (medications, AKI, acidosis). (5) Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) is no longer recommended due to questionable efficacy and GI adverse events.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) — questionable efficacy, risk of intestinal necrosis. Bicarbonate alone without acidosis. Insulin without dextrose (hypoglycemia risk). Beta-blockers (worsen hyperkalemia).

treatments.notes: no effect on K level; stabilizes cardiac membrane

treatments.dosage: regular insulin 10 U IV + D50 25 g
treatments.notes: monitor for hypoglycemia; give dextrose first if glucose 250
additive with insulin; synergistic effect
preferred over SPS; onset 1 hr
for ESRD, refractory hyperkalemia, or ongoing K release

diseases.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
diseases.management: Per the 2025 KDIGO Guideline and 2016 AAFP Review: (1) Defined by heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia (3 g/dL), peripheral edema, hyperlipidemia. (2) Adults: renal biopsy recommended to determine histologic subtype (membranous nephropathy and FSGS most common in adults). (3) All patients: sodium restriction, fluid restriction, loop diuretics for edema, ACEi/ARB to reduce proteinuria. (4) Immunosuppressive therapy guided by biopsy: corticosteroids for minimal change disease; rituximab, cyclophosphamide, or calcineurin inhibitors for membranous nephropathy or FSGS. (5) VTE prophylaxis if albumin 2.5 g/dL (high thrombosis risk). (6) Statin for hyperlipidemia. (7) Children: empiric prednisone 60 mg/m²/day × 4–6 weeks then taper (steroid-sensitive in 75–90%); biopsy only if steroid-resistant.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine anticoagulation without risk assessment. NSAIDs (worsen renal function). Excessive fluid restriction without monitoring.

treatments.notes: reduces proteinuria
combine with albumin infusion if severe
steroid-sensitive in 75–90%
for frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent NS

diseases.name: Nephrolithiasis
diseases.management: Per the 2014 ACP Guideline and 2026 AHRQ Systematic Review: (1) Acute renal colic: NSAIDs (ketorolac 30 mg IV or ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO) first-line for pain; opioids if NSAIDs insufficient. (2) Medical expulsive therapy: tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily for distal ureteral stones 5–10 mm (facilitates passage). (3) Stones 5 mm: usually pass spontaneously. Stones >10 mm or obstructing: urologic intervention (SWL, ureteroscopy, or PCNL). (4) Prevention of recurrence: increased fluid intake (>2.5 L/day); diet with normal-high calcium, low sodium, low animal protein. (5) Pharmacologic prevention (recurrent calcium stones): thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg or chlorthalidone 25 mg daily), potassium citrate, allopurinol (if hyperuricosuria). (6) 24-hour urine collection for metabolic evaluation in recurrent stone formers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Excessive calcium restriction (paradoxically increases stone risk). Excessive vitamin C supplementation (increases oxalate). Prolonged opioid use for renal colic.

treatments.dosage: ketorolac 30 mg IV or ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO
treatments.notes: superior to opioids for renal colic
for distal ureteral stones 5–10 mm
reduces calcium stone recurrence ~50%
reduces stone recurrence ~75%

diseases.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review and 2024 AGA Clinical Practice Update: (1) Diagnosis: ferritin 45 ng/mL (AGA threshold) or 30 ng/mL; transferrin saturation 20% if inflammation present (ferritin 100 in inflammatory states). (2) Identify and treat cause: GI bleeding (bidirectional endoscopy in men and postmenopausal women), heavy menstrual bleeding, celiac disease, H. pylori. (3) Oral iron: ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily or every other day (every-other-day dosing improves absorption). (4) IV iron: for oral intolerance, malabsorption (celiac, post-bariatric), CKD, HF, IBD, pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester), ongoing blood loss. (5) Check response: reticulocyte count at 1–2 weeks; hemoglobin at 4–8 weeks (expect ≥1 g/dL rise). (6) Continue iron 3–6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Calcium, coffee, tea within 1 hour of oral iron (decrease absorption). Delaying endoscopy in men/postmenopausal women with IDA. Transfusion for stable, asymptomatic anemia.

treatments.notes: every-other-day dosing improves absorption

treatments.medication: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or iron sucrose)
treatments.dosage: ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg × 2 doses 1 week apart; or iron sucrose 200 mg × 5 doses
treatments.notes: for oral intolerance, malabsorption, CKD, HF, IBD, pregnancy

diseases.name: Sickle Cell Crisis
diseases.management: Per the 2014 NHLBI Evidence-Based Report and 2022 JAMA Review: (1) Acute vaso-occlusive crisis: rapid initiation of opioids (morphine or hydromorphone) — do NOT delay; NSAIDs as adjunct; IV fluids at maintenance rate (avoid overhydration). (2) Incentive spirometry for all hospitalized patients (prevents acute chest syndrome). (3) Acute chest syndrome: exchange transfusion if severe (target HbS 30%); empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin); supplemental O2. (4) Disease-modifying therapy: hydroxyurea for all patients with HbSS or Sβ0-thalassemia starting at 9 months of age (reduces VOC, ACS, hospitalizations). (5) Additional agents: L-glutamine (reduces pain crises); crizanlizumab (anti-P-selectin; reduces VOC frequency). (6) Voxelotor withdrawn from market (safety concerns). (7) Curative: hematopoietic stem cell transplant (matched sibling donor); gene therapy emerging. (8) Prophylactic penicillin until age 5; annual TCD screening ages 2–16.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Voxelotor (withdrawn — safety concerns). Meperidine (seizure risk). Overhydration (pulmonary edema risk). Undertreating pain.

treatments.notes: reduces VOC, ACS, hospitalizations; start at 9 months

treatments.dosage: morphine 0.1–0.15 mg/kg IV q2–4h or hydromorphone
treatments.notes: rapid initiation; do not delay
reduces hospitalizations by 33%
anti-P-selectin; reduces VOC from 2.98 to 1.63/year

diseases.name: Immune Thrombocytopenia
diseases.management: Per the 2019 NEJM Review and 2019 ASH Guidelines Update: (1) Treat if platelets 30,000 or significant bleeding. (2) First-line: corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks then taper; or dexamethasone 40 mg/day × 4 days). (3) Severe/life-threatening bleeding: IVIG 1 g/kg × 1–2 days + corticosteroids + platelet transfusion. (4) Second-line (if corticosteroid-refractory or dependent): TPO-receptor agonists (eltrombopag 50 mg daily or romiplostim 1–10 mcg/kg SC weekly) — preferred over splenectomy in first 12 months. Rituximab 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 doses (off-label). Fostamatinib (SYK inhibitor) for chronic ITP. (5) Splenectomy: delay ≥12 months; reserve for refractory to medical therapy. (6) Anti-D immunoglobulin: option in Rh-positive, non-splenectomized patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Splenectomy in first 12 months (allow time for spontaneous remission). Platelet transfusion unless life-threatening bleeding. Antiplatelet agents.

treatments.notes: or dexamethasone 40 mg/day × 4 days
rapid platelet rise within 24–48 hrs

treatments.dosage: eltrombopag 50 mg daily PO or romiplostim 1–10 mcg/kg SC weekly
treatments.notes: TPO-RAs; sustained response in 60–80%
off-label; ~60% initial response; ~50% relapse at 1–2 years

diseases.name: Immune TTP
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review and 2020 ISTH Guidelines: (1) Medical emergency — mortality >90% untreated. (2) Suspect if MAHA + thrombocytopenia + ADAMTS13 10%. (3) First-line: therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) daily (1–1.5 plasma volumes) + corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg or methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3 days) + rituximab 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 doses. (4) Caplacizumab 11 mg IV then 11 mg SC daily (anti-vWF nanobody): reduces time to platelet normalization and early recurrence; increases bleeding risk. Continue until ADAMTS13 recovery. (5) Do NOT transfuse platelets (worsens thrombosis) unless life-threatening bleeding. (6) Monitor ADAMTS13 in remission; preemptive rituximab if ADAMTS13 90% with modern triplet therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Platelet transfusion (worsens thrombosis — "fuel on fire"). Delay in initiating TPE. Stopping caplacizumab before ADAMTS13 recovery.

treatments.notes: start before ADAMTS13 results if high suspicion

treatments.dosage: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3 days
treatments.notes: combined with TPE
reduces relapse; OR 0.4 for relapse vs no rituximab

treatments.dosage: 11 mg IV before first TPE then 11 mg SC daily
treatments.notes: reduces exacerbation risk by 29%; increases bleeding

diseases.name: Ischemic Stroke
diseases.management: Per the 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline and 2020 NEJM Review: (1) IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg; 10% bolus, rest over 60 min) within 4.5 hrs of symptom onset. Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg IV push is an emerging alternative (FDA-approved 2025). (2) Mechanical thrombectomy for anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion within 6 hrs (or up to 24 hrs if favorable perfusion imaging). (3) BP management: permissive hypertension (do not lower unless >220/120 or >185/110 if thrombolysis candidate). (4) Minor/nondisabling stroke or high-risk TIA: dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) within 24 hrs × 21 days then single agent. (5) Cardioembolic stroke (AF): anticoagulation with DOAC (start within 4–14 days depending on infarct size). (6) Symptomatic carotid stenosis ≥50%: carotid endarterectomy or stenting. (7) Admit to stroke unit; swallow screen before oral intake.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticoagulation within 24 hrs of thrombolysis. Aggressive BP lowering in acute stroke (unless thrombolysis candidate). Aspirin within 24 hrs of alteplase.

treatments.dosage: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg; 10% bolus, rest over 60 min)
treatments.notes: time is brain; door-to-needle 60 min
FDA-approved 2025; single bolus; may be preferred before thrombectomy

treatments.frequency: within 6 hrs (up to 24 hrs with favorable imaging)
treatments.notes: functional independence 46% vs 26.5% with medical therapy alone
reduces stroke risk from 7.8% to 5.2%

diseases.name: Status Epilepticus
diseases.management: Per the 2020 ESETT Trial and 2025 Lancet Neurology Review: (1) Definition: seizure lasting ≥5 min (generalized tonic-clonic). (2) First-line (0–5 min): benzodiazepines — lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg), or midazolam 10 mg IM if no IV access, or diazepam 0.15 mg/kg IV. (3) Second-line (5–20 min): fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg), or valproate 40 mg/kg IV (max 3000 mg) — ESETT trial showed equivalent efficacy. Lacosamide non-inferior to fosphenytoin (TRENdS trial). (4) Refractory SE (>30 min): continuous infusion of midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital; intubation and ICU. (5) Super-refractory SE (>24 hrs): ketamine, barbiturates, immunotherapy if autoimmune etiology suspected. (6) Identify and treat underlying cause throughout.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Phenytoin IV push (arrhythmia, tissue necrosis — use fosphenytoin). Delay in benzodiazepine administration. Inadequate dosing of second-line agents.

treatments.notes: terminates SE in ~65%
faster administration than IV lorazepam

treatments.dosage: fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (max 4500 mg), or valproate 40 mg/kg (max 3000 mg)
treatments.notes: ESETT: equivalent efficacy (~45% response)

treatments.dosage: midazolam 0.2 mg/kg bolus then 0.1–2 mg/kg/hr or propofol 1–2 mg/kg bolus then 20–65 mcg/kg/min
treatments.notes: requires intubation and ICU

diseases.name: Multiple Sclerosis
diseases.management: Per the 2018 AAN Guideline and 2021 JAMA Review: (1) Start DMT early in all patients with relapsing MS. (2) Moderate-efficacy DMTs (lower risk): interferon beta, glatiramer acetate, teriflunomide, dimethyl fumarate. (3) High-efficacy DMTs: natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, alemtuzumab, cladribine. (4) For highly active RRMS: consider high-efficacy DMT first-line. (5) Ocrelizumab: only DMT approved for primary progressive MS. (6) Acute relapses: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days; PLEX if steroid-refractory. (7) Monitor for PML risk with natalizumab (JCV serology q3–6 months). (8) Symptom management: fatigue (amantadine), spasticity (baclofen, tizanidine), neuropathic pain (gabapentin), bladder dysfunction.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Natalizumab if JCV antibody index >1.5 (high PML risk). Fingolimod without first-dose cardiac monitoring. Stopping DMT without plan (rebound activity with natalizumab/fingolimod).

treatments.notes: anti-CD20; only DMT for PPMS

diseases.name: Parkinson Disease
diseases.management: Per the 2021 AAN Guideline and 2020 JAMA Review: (1) Levodopa-carbidopa is the most effective symptomatic therapy and should not be delayed. (2) Initial therapy options: levodopa (preferred for most, especially elderly), dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole — consider in younger patients), MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline — mild symptoms). (3) Motor fluctuations ("wearing off"): add COMT inhibitor (entacapone), MAO-B inhibitor, or dopamine agonist. (4) Dyskinesia: amantadine 137–274 mg ER daily. (5) Advanced disease: deep brain stimulation (STN or GPi) for medication-resistant tremor, motor fluctuations, or dyskinesias; levodopa-carbidopa enteral suspension (Duopa). (6) Non-motor symptoms: depression (SSRIs), dementia (rivastigmine), psychosis (pimavanserin, quetiapine, clozapine), constipation, orthostatic hypotension. (7) Exercise and rehabilitation are essential across all stages.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine agonists in elderly or cognitively impaired (hallucinations, impulse control disorders). Metoclopramide and typical antipsychotics (worsen parkinsonism). Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic therapy (neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome).

treatments.notes: most effective; start low, go slow

diseases.name: Myasthenia Gravis
diseases.management: Per the 2016 International Consensus Guidance and 2024 Nordic Guideline: (1) Pyridostigmine 60 mg TID (titrate to effect) — first-line symptomatic therapy. (2) If inadequate: corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, taper to lowest effective dose). (3) Steroid-sparing agents: azathioprine (first-line IS), mycophenolate, methotrexate, tacrolimus. (4) Thymectomy: for thymoma (all patients) and non-thymoma AChR+ patients age ≤50–65 (MGTX trial: improved outcomes). (5) Myasthenic crisis: IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days or plasma exchange (5 sessions); ICU monitoring; intubation if FVC 15–20 mL/kg. (6) Newer therapies: complement inhibitors (eculizumab for refractory AChR+ gMG), FcRn blockers (efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab). (7) Avoid medications that worsen MG: aminoglycos
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fluoroquinolones, macrolides, aminoglycosides (worsen NMJ transmission). Beta-blockers, magnesium, muscle relaxants. Penicillamine. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (can trigger de novo MG). | Fluoroquinolones, macrolides, aminoglycosides (worsen NMJ transmission). Beta-blockers, magnesium, muscle relaxants. Penicillamine. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (can trigger de novo MG).

diseases.name: Major Depressive Disorder
diseases.management: Per the 2023 ACP Guideline and 2025 CANMAT Guideline: (1) Mild MDD: CBT monotherapy preferred. (2) Moderate-severe MDD: monotherapy with CBT or a second-generation antidepressant (SGA), or combination CBT + SGA. (3) First-line SGAs: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) or SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine). Bupropion and mirtazapine also first-line. (4) No substantial differences in efficacy among SGAs; choose based on side-effect profile, comorbidities, cost, and patient preference. (5) Allow 4–6 weeks at adequate dose before deeming ineffective. (6) If inadequate response: optimize dose → switch to different SGA → augment (add aripiprazole, lithium, or bupropion). (7) Duration: continue ≥6–12 months after remission; longer for recurrent episodes. (8) Monitor for suicidality, especially in young adults during first weeks.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar depression (risk of mania). MAOIs with serotonergic drugs (serotonin syndrome). Paroxetine in pregnancy (cardiac defects). Abrupt discontinuation of SSRIs/SNRIs (withdrawal syndrome). | Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar depression (risk of mania). MAOIs with serotonergic drugs (serotonin syndrome). Paroxetine in pregnancy (cardiac defects). Abrupt discontinuation of SSRIs/SNRIs (withdrawal syndrome).

treatments.notes: SSRI; well-studied; safe in cardiac disease

diseases.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
diseases.management: Per the 2026 JAMA Internal Medicine Review and 2015 NEJM Review: (1) First-line: SSRIs or SNRIs (escitalopram, paroxetine, duloxetine, venlafaxine — all FDA-indicated for GAD). Sertraline and fluoxetine also effective. (2) CBT is equally effective as first-line, alone or combined with pharmacotherapy. (3) Start at low dose; titrate over 4–6 weeks; full effect may take 2–3 months. (4) Second-line/adjunct: buspirone 15–60 mg/day, pregabalin (not FDA-approved in US for GAD but widely used elsewhere). (5) Benzodiazepines: effective but risk of dependence; reserve for short-term use or treatment-resistant cases with careful monitoring. (6) Duration: ≥12 months after remission; relapse common with early discontinuation. (7) Monitor with GAD-7 scale.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term benzodiazepines as first-line (dependence, cognitive effects). Abrupt benzodiazepine discontinuation (withdrawal seizures). Beta-blockers for GAD (ineffective for generalized anxiety; may help performance anxiety). | Long-term benzodiazepines as first-line (dependence, cognitive effects). Abrupt benzodiazepine discontinuation (withdrawal seizures). Beta-blockers for GAD (ineffective for generalized anxiety; may help performance anxiety).

treatments.notes: FDA-indicated for GAD

diseases.name: Bipolar Disorder
diseases.management: Per the 2023 JAMA Review and 2018 CANMAT/ISBD Guidelines: (1) Acute mania: first-line monotherapy — lithium, valproate, quetiapine, aripiprazole, risperidone, cariprazine. Combination therapy (lithium or valproate + atypical antipsychotic) for severe mania. (2) Bipolar depression: quetiapine monotherapy (first-line), lurasidone, cariprazine, lamotrigine. Lithium also effective. (3) Maintenance: lithium (gold standard — reduces suicide risk), valproate, quetiapine, lamotrigine (prevents depressive episodes), aripiprazole. (4) Discontinue antidepressants and stimulants during mania. (5) Lithium monitoring: serum levels (0.6–1.0 mEq/L maintenance), renal function, thyroid function q6 months. (6) Valproate: monitor LFTs, CBC; teratogenic (avoid in women of childbearing potential). (7) Acute agitation: lorazepam, haloperidol, or olanzapine IM.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antidepressant monotherapy (risk of mania switch). Valproate in pregnancy. Carbamazepine + oral contraceptives (reduces efficacy). Abrupt lithium discontinuation (rebound mania). | Antidepressant monotherapy (risk of mania switch). Valproate in pregnancy. Carbamazepine + oral contraceptives (reduces efficacy). Abrupt lithium discontinuation (rebound mania).

treatments.notes: gold standard; reduces suicide risk; monitor levels, renal, thyroid

diseases.name: Schizophrenia
diseases.management: Per the 2019 Psychopharmacology Review and 2025 Japanese Expert Consensus: (1) Second-generation antipsychotics (SGAs) are first-line. (2) For positive symptoms: risperidone, olanzapine, paliperidone, aripiprazole. (3) For negative symptoms/cognitive impairment: aripiprazole, brexpiprazole, cariprazine. (4) Adequate trial: 4–6 weeks at therapeutic dose. (5) If inadequate response to 2 adequate trials: clozapine (gold standard for treatment-resistant schizophrenia). (6) Clozapine monitoring: weekly CBC for first 6 months (agranulocytosis risk ~1%), then biweekly, then monthly. (7) Long-acting injectable antipsychotics (LAIs): for poor adherence or patient preference (paliperidone palmitate, aripiprazole lauroxil). (8) Metabolic monitoring for all SGAs: weight, glucose, lipids at baseline and periodically. (9) Avoid polypharmacy with multiple antipsychotics when possible.
diseases.notes: Avoid: First-generation antipsychotics as first-line (higher EPS, tardive dyskinesia). Antipsychotic polypharmacy without clozapine trial. Olanzapine in patients with metabolic syndrome without monitoring. Abrupt discontinuation. | First-generation antipsychotics as first-line (higher EPS, tardive dyskinesia). Antipsychotic polypharmacy without clozapine trial. Olanzapine in patients with metabolic syndrome without monitoring. Abrupt discontinuation.

treatments.notes: effective for positive symptoms; prolactin elevation

diseases.name: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome
diseases.management: Per the 2020 ASAM Guideline and 2025 NEJM Review: (1) Assess severity with CIWA-Ar scale: mild (10), moderate (10–18), severe (≥19). (2) Mild (CIWA 10, no risk factors): supportive care ± gabapentin or carbamazepine. (3) Moderate (CIWA 10–18): benzodiazepines first-line. Symptom-triggered dosing preferred over fixed schedule (reduces total dose and duration). (4) Severe (CIWA ≥19): benzodiazepines first-line; phenobarbital as adjunct or alternative. (5) Benzodiazepine options: diazepam 10–20 mg or chlordiazepoxide 50–100 mg (long-acting, self-tapering); lorazepam or oxazepam preferred in liver disease (no active metabolites). (6) ICU: midazolam, phenobarbital, or dexmedetomidine for refractory cases. (7) Thiamine 100 mg IV/IM before glucose (prevent Wernicke's); high-dose thiamine (500 mg IV TID × 3 days) if Wernicke's suspected. (8) Seizure prophylaxis: benzodiazepines are effective; phenytoin is NOT effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Phenytoin for alcohol withdrawal seizures (ineffective). Alcohol as treatment. Undertreating severe withdrawal (risk of DT, death). Fixed-schedule dosing when symptom-triggered is feasible. | Phenytoin for alcohol withdrawal seizures (ineffective). Alcohol as treatment. Undertreating severe withdrawal (risk of DT, death). Fixed-schedule dosing when symptom-triggered is feasible.

treatments.notes: long-acting; self-tapering; symptom-triggered preferred
long-acting; rapid onset
no active metabolites; preferred in hepatic impairment

diseases.name: Opioid Use Disorder / Overdose
diseases.management: Per the 2021 VA/DoD Guideline and 2024 CRISM Guideline: (1) Medications for OUD (MOUD) are first-line — buprenorphine and methadone are equally recommended as first-line. (2) Buprenorphine: sublingual 8–24 mg/day; can be prescribed in office-based settings; partial agonist — blocks effects of other opioids. Low-dose initiation ("microdosing") allows starting without full withdrawal. (3) Methadone: full agonist; dispensed at OTPs; start 20–30 mg, titrate slowly; effective for high-potency fentanyl use. (4) Extended-release naltrexone: 380 mg IM monthly; requires 7–10 days opioid-free; lower retention than agonist therapy. (5) Acute overdose: naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN; repeat q2–3 min; observe ≥4 hrs (longer for long-acting opioids). (6) MOUD reduces overdose death by ~50%. (7) Psychosocial treatment alone is inferior to MOUD. (8) Buprenorphine monotherapy (without naloxone) preferred in pregnancy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Psychosocial treatment alone without MOUD (inferior outcomes). Abrupt opioid cessation without MOUD (high relapse/overdose risk). Naltrexone initiation without adequate opioid-free period (precipitated withdrawal). Undertreating with methadone (60 mg maintenance). | Psychosocial treatment alone without MOUD (inferior outcomes). diseases.name: Gout
diseases.management: Per the 2022 NEJM Review and 2021 Lancet Review: (1) Acute flare: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg BID), colchicine (1.2 mg then 0.6 mg 1 hr later; total 1.8 mg day 1), or corticosteroids (prednisone 30 mg/day × 5 days). Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for monoarticular flare. (2) Urate-lowering therapy (ULT) indications: ≥2 flares/year, tophi, radiographic damage, urolithiasis, CKD, or urate >8 mg/dL. (3) First-line ULT: allopurinol — start 100 mg daily (50 mg if eGFR 60), titrate by 100 mg/month to target urate 6 mg/dL (max 800 mg/day). (4) Alternative ULT: febuxostat 40–80 mg daily (increased CV risk vs allopurinol — CARES trial). (5) Anti-inflammatory prophylaxis during ULT initiation: colchicine 0.6 mg daily or naproxen 250 mg daily × 3–6 months. (6) ULT can be started during an acute flare. (7) Pegloticase for refractory tophaceous gout.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aspirin (alters urate excretion). Febuxostat as first-line over allopurinol (CV risk). Stopping ULT during acute flare. Thiazide diuretics if possible (raise urate). | Aspirin (alters urate excretion). Febuxostat as first-line over allopurinol (CV risk). Stopping ULT during acute flare. Thiazide diuretics if possible (raise urate).

treatments.dosage: 1.2 mg then 0.6 mg 1 hr later (day 1); then 0.6 mg daily-BID
treatments.notes: most effective within 24 hrs of flare onset

diseases.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
diseases.management: Per the 2023 Lancet Review and 2018 JAMA Review: (1) Start DMARD therapy within 3 months of symptom onset — early treatment is critical. (2) First-line: methotrexate 10–25 mg/week + folic acid 1 mg daily. Hydroxychloroquine for low disease activity. (3) Treat-to-target: aim for remission or low disease activity; reassess q3 months and escalate if target not met. (4) If methotrexate inadequate: add biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor, IL-6R antagonist, abatacept, rituximab) or targeted synthetic DMARD (JAK inhibitor). (5) All bDMARDs/tsDMARDs more effective when combined with methotrexate. (6) Short-term glucocorticoids (prednisone ≤10 mg/day × ≤3 months) as bridge therapy when starting/changing DMARDs. (7) JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib): effective but increased CV/VTE/malignancy risk in patients ≥65 or with CV risk factors (ORAL Surveillance trial). (8) Screen for TB, hepatitis B before biologics.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Glucocorticoids as long-term monotherapy. Combining biologics (increased infection risk). JAK inhibitors in patients ≥65 with CV risk factors without discussing risks. Delaying DMARD initiation. | Glucocorticoids as long-term monotherapy. Combining biologics (increased infection risk). JAK inhibitors in patients ≥65 with CV risk factors without discussing risks. Delaying DMARD initiation.

treatments.notes: anchor drug; monitor CBC, LFTs, creatinine q3 months

diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
diseases.management: Per the 2023 EULAR Recommendations and 2024 JAMA Review: (1) Hydroxychloroquine for ALL patients (≤5 mg/kg/day) — reduces mortality by 54%, prevents flares, organ damage, thrombosis. (2) Glucocorticoids as bridge therapy; minimize to ≤5 mg/day prednisone equivalent; withdraw if possible. (3) Immunosuppressive agents for moderate-severe disease: methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil. (4) Biologics: belimumab (anti-BLyS; FDA-approved for active SLE and lupus nephritis), anifrolumab (anti-type I IFN receptor; for moderate-severe SLE). (5) Lupus nephritis: mycophenolate or low-dose IV cyclophosphamide as anchor + add-on belimumab or voclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor). (6) Severe/refractory: cyclophosphamide, rituximab (off-label). (7) Ophthalmologic screening for HCQ retinopathy: baseline, then annually after 5 years.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Glucocorticoids >5 mg/day long-term (organ damage). Mycophenolate and methotrexate in pregnancy (teratogenic). NSAIDs in lupus nephritis. Stopping HCQ (flare risk). | Glucocorticoids >5 mg/day long-term (organ damage). Mycophenolate and methotrexate in pregnancy (teratogenic). NSAIDs in lupus nephritis. Stopping HCQ (flare risk).

diseases.name: Anaphylaxis
diseases.management: Per the 2020 AHA Guidelines and 2022 Anaphylaxis CPG: (1) Epinephrine IM is the ONLY first-line treatment — administer immediately. (2) Adult dose: epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg IM (1:1000) into mid-anterolateral thigh; repeat q5–15 min if no response. Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg). (3) Intranasal epinephrine 2 mg: FDA-approved 2024 as alternative. (4) Position: supine with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress → sitting). (5) Adjuncts: IV NS bolus 1–2 L for hypotension; albuterol nebulizer for bronchospasm; H1 antihistamine (diphenhydramine) and H2 blocker (famotidine) — adjuncts only, NOT first-line. (6) Corticosteroids NOT routinely recommended (no evidence of benefit for acute anaphylaxis). (7) Observe ≥4 hours (biphasic reactions in ~5%). (8) Discharge with epinephrine auto-injector prescription and anaphylaxis action plan. (9) Refractory: IV epinephrine infusion 0.1–1 mcg/kg/min; glucagon if on beta-blockers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antihistamines or corticosteroids as first-line (delayed onset, do not treat anaphylaxis). Delay in epinephrine administration. IV epinephrine bolus in non-arrest patients (arrhythmia risk — use infusion). Discharging without epinephrine auto-injector prescription. | Antihistamines or corticosteroids as first-line (delayed onset, do not treat anaphylaxis). Delay in epinephrine administration. IV epinephrine bolus in non-arrest patients (arrhythmia risk — use infusion). Discharging without epinephrine auto-injector prescription.

diseases.name: Preeclampsia / Eclampsia
diseases.management: Per the 2020 ACOG Practice Bulletin and 2022 NEJM Review: (1) Definitive treatment is delivery. (2) Timing of delivery: ≥37 weeks for preeclampsia without severe features; ≥34 weeks for severe features (or earlier if maternal/fetal deterioration). (3) Antihypertensive therapy: treat severe hypertension (≥160/110) urgently — IV labetalol 20 mg then 40 mg then 80 mg q10 min, or IV hydralazine 5–10 mg q20 min, or oral nifedipine 10–20 mg q20 min. (4) Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia and eclampsia: 4–6 g IV loading dose over 15–20 min then 1–2 g/hr maintenance infusion. Monitor patellar reflexes, urine output, respiratory rate. (5) Antidote for magnesium toxicity: calcium gluconate 1 g IV. (6) Antenatal corticosteroids if 34 weeks (fetal lung maturity). (7) Aspirin 81–162 mg daily starting 12–16 weeks for prevention in high-risk women. (8) Postpartum: monitor BP closely days 3–6 (peak); continue antihypertensives as needed.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ACEi/ARBs in pregnancy (teratogenic). Delay in treating severe hypertension. Magnesium without monitoring (toxicity risk). Dexamethasone for HELLP syndrome (no benefit).

treatments.dosage: 20 mg then 40 mg then 80 mg q10 min (max 300 mg)
treatments.notes: or nifedipine 10–20 mg PO q20 min

diseases.name: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus
diseases.management: Per the 2026 ADA Standards of Care and 2023 AAFP Review: (1) Lifestyle modification first: medical nutrition therapy (MNT) + moderate exercise. (2) Glucose targets: fasting 95 mg/dL, 1-hr postprandial 140 mg/dL, 2-hr postprandial 120 mg/dL. (3) If lifestyle insufficient (30% of patients): insulin is preferred pharmacotherapy (does not cross placenta). (4) Metformin: effective alternative; crosses placenta; may increase childhood weight; SMFM considers it reasonable if patient prefers. ADA 2026 recommends against metformin/glyburide as first-line. (5) Glyburide: third-line; crosses placenta; less effective than insulin or metformin. (6) Monitor fetal growth with ultrasound. (7) Delivery timing: 39–40 weeks if well-controlled on diet; 37–39 weeks if on medication. (8) Postpartum: screen for T2DM at 4–12 weeks with 75-g OGTT or HbA1c; then every 1–3 years lifelong.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Glyburide as first-line (inferior outcomes). Metformin/glyburide as first-line per ADA 2026. Delaying pharmacotherapy when >20% glucose values abnormal.

treatments.dosage: individualized diet (≥175 g complex carbs/day) + 30 min moderate exercise

diseases.name: Postpartum Hemorrhage
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review, 2021 NEJM Review, and 2017 ACOG Practice Bulletin: (1) Definition: cumulative blood loss ≥1000 mL or bleeding with hemodynamic instability. (2) Most common cause: uterine atony (70–80%). (3) First-line: bimanual uterine massage + oxytocin 10–40 U in 1 L NS IV infusion. (4) Second-line uterotonics: methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM (avoid in HTN), carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) 0.25 mg IM q15 min (avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 mcg SL/PR. (5) Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hrs of birth (WOMAN trial: reduced bleeding-related mortality by 19%). (6) If uterotonics fail: intrauterine balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), uterine artery embolization. (7) Hysterectomy for life-threatening refractory hemorrhage. (8) Massive transfusion protocol: 1:1:1 ratio pRBC:FFP:platelets. (9) Retained products: manual extraction or suction curettage.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in uterotonic administration. Methylergonovine in hypertension. Carboprost in asthma. Tranexamic acid >3 hrs after birth (no benefit).

treatments.dosage: 10–40 U in 1 L NS IV infusion
treatments.notes: mainstay; immediate uterine response

diseases.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
diseases.management: Per the 2018 ACOG Practice Bulletin: (1) >90% are tubal. (2) Hemodynamically unstable or ruptured: emergent surgical management (laparoscopic salpingectomy preferred). (3) Stable, unruptured: methotrexate IM or laparoscopic surgery — decision guided by clinical/lab/imaging data + patient preference. (4) Methotrexate candidates: hemodynamically stable, no rupture, hCG ideally 5000 mIU/mL, no fetal cardiac activity, mass 3.5 cm, able to follow up. (5) Single-dose protocol: MTX 50 mg/m² IM; check hCG day 4 and 7 (expect ≥15% decline between days 4–7). Two-dose protocol for higher hCG levels. (6) ~70% success rate with single-dose MTX. (7) Salpingostomy vs salpingectomy: salpingectomy preferred unless contralateral tube compromised and future fertility desired. (8) Expectant management: only if hCG low and declining. (9) Delay pregnancy ≥3 months after MTX.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Methotrexate if fetal cardiac activity present, hCG >5000, or unable to follow up. Delay in surgery for ruptured ectopic. Conception within 3 months of MTX.

treatments.frequency: single dose (may repeat day 7 if inadequate decline)
treatments.notes: ~70% success; check hCG days 4 and 7

diseases.name: Bacterial Vaginosis
diseases.management: Per the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines: (1) Treat symptomatic patients. (2) First-line: metronidazole 500 mg PO BID × 7 days, OR metronidazole 0.75% gel intravaginally daily × 5 days, OR clindamycin 2% cream intravaginally at bedtime × 7 days. (3) Alternatives: secnidazole 2 g PO single dose, tinidazole 2 g PO daily × 2 days, or clindamycin 300 mg PO BID × 7 days. (4) Pregnancy: metronidazole 500 mg PO BID × 7 days or clindamycin 300 mg PO BID × 7 days (treat all symptomatic pregnant women — associated with preterm birth). (5) Recurrent BV (≥2 episodes): metronidazole gel intravaginally twice weekly × 4–6 months. (6) Alcohol avoidance during metronidazole is no longer considered necessary per updated evidence. (7) Routine treatment of male sex partners not yet recommended, though emerging evidence suggests benefit.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tinidazole in pregnancy. Douching. Intravaginal clindamycin with latex condoms (oil-based). Secnidazole in pregnancy (insufficient data).

diseases.name: Atopic Dermatitis
diseases.management: Per the 2024 AAD Guideline and 2021 NEJM Review: (1) All stages: emollients (moisturizers) as foundation; avoid triggers. (2) Mild: topical corticosteroids (low-mid potency for face/folds; mid-high potency for body). (3) Moderate: topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for sensitive areas; crisaborole (PDE4 inhibitor); ruxolitinib cream (topical JAK inhibitor). (4) Proactive therapy: intermittent TCS or TCI to frequently relapsing areas (e.g., twice weekly) to prevent flares. (5) Moderate-severe refractory to topicals: narrowband UVB phototherapy. (6) Severe/refractory: systemic therapy — dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα; first-line biologic), tralokinumab (anti-IL-13). JAK inhibitors: abrocitinib, upadacitinib (oral; rapid onset; monitor for infections, VTE, malignancy). (7) Older immunosuppressants (methotrexate, cyclosporine, azathioprine): off-label; used when biologics unavailable. (8) Sedating antihistamines (hydroxyzine) for nocturnal pruritus only; non-sedating antihistamines have limited benefit for AD itch.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oral corticosteroids long-term (rebound flares). Superpotent TCS on face/folds. Non-sedating antihistamines for AD itch (ineffective). Topical antibiotics without clinical infection.

treatments.notes: mainstay; proactive use twice weekly prevents flares
for face, eyelids, folds; steroid-sparing

treatments.dosage: 600 mg SC load then 300 mg q2 weeks
treatments.notes: anti-IL-4Rα; strong recommendation per AAD; conjunctivitis in ~10%

diseases.name: Plaque Psoriasis
diseases.management: Per the 2020 JAMA Review and AAD-NPF Guidelines: (1) Mild (3–5% BSA): topical corticosteroids (mainstay) ± vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene). Combination calcipotriene/betamethasone is superior to either alone. Calcineurin inhibitors for face/folds. (2) Moderate (3–10% BSA): topicals ± narrowband UVB phototherapy. (3) Severe (>10% BSA or significant QoL impact): systemic therapy. (4) Biologics are first-line for moderate-severe psoriasis per AAD-NPF: IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab — highest PASI 90 rates), IL-23 inhibitors (risankizumab, guselkumab — durable response, infrequent dosing), TNF-α inhibitors (adalimumab, infliximab), IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab). (5) Oral systemics: methotrexate 10–25 mg/week (traditional first-line), apremilast (PDE4 inhibitor; mild-moderate), deucravacitinib (TYK2 inhibitor), cyclosporine (short-term only), acitretin. (6) Screen for psoriatic arthritis (present in ~30%), metabolic syndrome, depression.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term cyclosporine (nephrotoxicity; max 1–2 years). Superpotent TCS on face/folds. Systemic corticosteroids (rebound flare, pustular psoriasis). Biologics without TB screening.

diseases.name: Kawasaki Disease
diseases.management: Per the 2024 AHA Scientific Statement and 2026 NEJM Trial: (1) Diagnosis: fever ≥5 days + ≥4 of 5 criteria (rash, bilateral conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy) OR fever + fewer criteria with coronary aneurysms. (2) First-line: IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion over 8–12 hrs + aspirin. (3) Aspirin: moderate-high dose (30–50 mg/kg/day) during acute phase until afebrile 48–72 hrs, then low-dose (3–5 mg/kg/day) for 6–8 weeks (or indefinitely if coronary abnormalities). (4) Adjunctive corticosteroids: recommended for patients at high risk of IVIG resistance (Kobayashi score ≥5) — prednisolone reduces coronary artery lesions. (5) IVIG-resistant (persistent fever ≥36 hrs after IVIG): second dose IVIG, or infliximab 5 mg/kg IV, or IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg × 1–3 days. (6) Long-term management stratified by coronary artery Z-scores. (7) Defer live vaccines 11 months after IVIG.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Ibuprofen with aspirin (inhibits antiplatelet effect). Live vaccines within 11 months of IVIG. Delay in IVIG beyond day 10 of illness.

treatments.notes: reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 20–25% to 3–5%; most effective within 10 days of fever onset
then low-dose 3–5 mg/kg/day × 6–8 weeks
for Kobayashi score ≥5; reduces coronary lesions
anti-TNF; for persistent fever after IVIG

diseases.name: Febrile Seizure
diseases.management: Per the 2019 AAFP Review and 2021 Cochrane Review: (1) Definition: seizure in child 6 months–5 years with fever ≥100.4°F without CNS infection. (2) Simple febrile seizure: generalized, 15 min, does not recur within 24 hrs. Complex: >15 min, focal, or recurs within 24 hrs. (3) Simple febrile seizure in well-appearing child: NO routine labs, neuroimaging, or EEG needed. Evaluate for source of fever. (4) Seizure lasting >5 min: benzodiazepine (rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg, or midazolam intranasal/buccal). (5) Prognosis: excellent — no increased mortality, no negative effects on intellect or behavior. (6) Recurrence risk: ~30% after first febrile seizure. (7) Routine antiepileptic prophylaxis is NOT indicated (risks outweigh benefits). (8) Antipyretics do NOT prevent febrile seizures. (9) Parent education and reassurance are the cornerstone of management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine antiepileptic prophylaxis (adverse effects outweigh benefits). Routine neuroimaging or EEG for simple febrile seizures. Antipyretics to prevent febrile seizures (ineffective). Lumbar puncture unless meningitis suspected.

treatments.dosage: rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg or intranasal midazolam
treatments.notes: for seizures lasting >5 min
excellent prognosis; no routine testing needed

diseases.name: Neonatal Sepsis
diseases.management: Per the 2024 Lancet Review and WHO Guidelines: (1) Early-onset sepsis (EOS, 72 hrs): most common pathogens — GBS, E. coli, Listeria. Empiric: ampicillin + gentamicin. (2) Late-onset sepsis (LOS, >72 hrs): most common — CoNS, S. aureus, gram-negatives. Empiric: flucloxacillin or vancomycin + gentamicin. (3) Prompt initiation of antibiotics is crucial — do not delay for culture results. (4) Narrow spectrum based on culture and sensitivity. (5) Duration: 7–10 days for confirmed bacteremia; 14–21 days for meningitis. Rule out sepsis (negative cultures, well-appearing): stop antibiotics at 36–48 hrs. (6) Supportive care: thermoregulation, respiratory support, fluid/nutritional support, cardiovascular support as needed. (7) Avoid routine empiric use of broad-spectrum antibiotics (cephalosporins) — promotes resistance. (8) IVIG, granulocyte transfusions NOT supported by current evidence.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Empiric cephalosporins (promotes resistance). Prolonged antibiotics without confirmed infection. IVIG for sepsis treatment (no benefit). Delay in antibiotic initiation.

treatments.dosage: ampicillin 50 mg/kg q8–12h + gentamicin 4–5 mg/kg q24–36h
treatments.duration: 7–10 days (bacteremia); stop at 36–48 hrs if cultures negative
treatments.notes: covers GBS, E. coli, Listeria

treatments.dosage: vancomycin 15 mg/kg q8–12h + gentamicin per protocol
