diseases.name: Acetaminophen Overdose
diseases.management: Per the 2023 AASLD Guidance, 2023 ACG Guideline, and 2023 JAMA Consensus Statement: (1) Activated charcoal 1 g/kg (max 50 g) if within 4 hrs of ingestion. (2) N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the only effective antidote. If given within 8–10 hrs, virtually prevents hepatotoxicity. (3) IV NAC protocol (preferred): 150 mg/kg load over 15–60 min, then 50 mg/kg over 4 hrs, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hrs (total 300 mg/kg over ~21 hrs). (4) Oral NAC: 140 mg/kg load then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 additional doses (72-hr protocol). (5) Use Rumack-Matthew nomogram for single acute ingestion to guide NAC initiation. (6) Stopping criteria: APAP 10 mcg/mL, INR 2 (or 1.5), ALT improving or normal, clinically well. Continue NAC at 6.25 mg/kg/hr if criteria not met. (7) Acute liver failure (coagulopathy + encephalopathy): ICU; consider liver transplant referral. (8) High-risk ingestion (≥30 g): extended NAC protocol.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Stopping NAC after fixed 21-hr protocol without reassessing (common error). Relying solely on APAP level without clinical assessment. Delay in NAC initiation. Acetazolamide (no role).

treatments.dosage: 150 mg/kg load over 15–60 min → 50 mg/kg over 4 hrs → 100 mg/kg over 16 hrs
treatments.notes: preferred route; continue at 6.25 mg/kg/hr if ALT rising or INR >2

treatments.dosage: 140 mg/kg load then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses
treatments.notes: antiemetics as needed
reduces absorption; may benefit beyond 4 hrs with NAC

diseases.name: Salicylate Toxicity
diseases.management: Per the 2020 NEJM Review: (1) Activated charcoal 1–2 g/kg (max 100 g) — most effective within 2 hrs; consider multi-dose activated charcoal for large ingestions or enteric-coated preparations. (2) IV fluids: LR or NS 10–20 mL/kg/hr until urine output 1–1.5 mL/kg/hr. (3) Urine alkalinization (essential): IV sodium bicarbonate bolus 1 mmol/kg then infusion (3 ampules NaHCO₃ in 1 L D5W) — target urine pH >7.5. Increasing urine pH from 6.1 to 8.1 increases salicylate clearance 18-fold. (4) Correct hypokalemia aggressively (prevents urine alkalinization). (5) Dextrose for CNS hypoglycorrhachia (can occur with normal serum glucose). (6) Hemodialysis indications: salicylate level >90 mg/dL regardless of symptoms, >80 mg/dL with renal impairment, severe symptoms (cerebral edema, ARDS, AKI, refractory acidosis), or clinical deterioration despite standard therapy. (7) Intubation: CAUTION — avoid decrease in minute ventilation (worsens acidemia → tissue salicylate redistribution). Give NaHCO₃ 2 mmol/kg before intubation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Intubation without pre-treatment NaHCO₃ (loss of compensatory hyperventilation → fatal acidemia). Acetazolamide (worsens toxicity). Forced diuresis (pulmonary edema risk). Decreasing minute ventilation in intubated patients.

treatments.dosage: 1 mmol/kg IV bolus then infusion (3 ampules in 1 L D5W)
treatments.duration: until SA 7.5; monitor ABG; correct hypokalemia

treatments.notes: multi-dose for large ingestions or enteric-coated
for SA >90 mg/dL, AKI, cerebral edema, ARDS, refractory acidosis

diseases.name: Tricyclic Antidepressant Overdose
diseases.management: Per the 2023 and 2025 AHA Guidelines: (1) Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, ventricular arrhythmias, hypotension. Anticholinergic effects → seizures, altered mental status, tachycardia. (2) First-line: IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms, ventricular arrhythmias, or hypotension — bolus 1–2 mEq/kg, titrate to QRS narrowing and hemodynamic improvement. Target pH 7.45–7.55 (do not exceed 7.55). (3) Continuous NaHCO₃ infusion or repeat boluses after initial response. (4) Seizures: benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam). Avoid phenytoin. (5) Hypotension: IV fluids first, then norepinephrine if refractory. (6) Intubated patients: mechanical hyperventilation as adjunct to NaHCO₃ (dual therapy achieves alkalemia faster and QRS narrowing twice as fast). (7) Refractory cardiogenic shock: consider VA-ECMO. (8) Lidocaine for refractory wide-complex tachycardia. (9) Monitor serum sodium (155 mEq/L) and potassium (hypokalemia common with alkalinization).

treatments.duration: until QRS 100 ms and hemodynamically stable
treatments.notes: target pH 7.45–7.55; max Na 155 mEq/L

diseases.name: Beta-Blocker / Calcium Channel Blocker Overdose
diseases.management: Per the 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline and 2020 AHA Scientific Statement: (1) Both cause profound bradycardia, hypotension, and cardiogenic shock. CCBs also cause hyperglycemia; beta-blockers cause hypoglycemia. (2) Decontamination: activated charcoal if within 1–2 hrs; whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release preparations. (3) IV calcium: first-line for CCB overdose — calcium chloride 1–2 g IV (via central line) or calcium gluconate 3–6 g IV; may repeat. (4) Glucagon: 3–10 mg IV bolus over 3–5 min then 3–5 mg/hr infusion — reasonable for both BB and CCB overdose; causes nausea/vomiting. (5) High-dose insulin euglycemic therapy (HIET): insulin 1 U/kg IV bolus then 0.5 U/kg/hr infusion + dextrose to maintain glucose 100–200 mg/dL — improves hemodynamics and survival; monitor potassium. (6) Vasopressors: norepinephrine or epinephrine for refractory hypotension. (7) IV lipid emulsion (Intralipid 20%): 1.5 mL/kg bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min for lipophilic agents (verapamil, propranolol). (8) Temporary pacing for refractory bradycardia. (9) VA-ECMO for refractory cardiogenic shock.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in HIET initiation (most effective early). Isoproterenol (may worsen hypotension). Inadequate dextrose supplementation with HIET (hypoglycemia). Calcium alone without HIET in severe cases.

treatments.dosage: calcium chloride 1–2 g IV or calcium gluconate 3–6 g IV
treatments.notes: first-line for CCB; low risk

treatments.dosage: 3–10 mg IV bolus over 3–5 min then 3–5 mg/hr infusion
treatments.notes: causes nausea/vomiting; transient effect
monitor glucose (dextrose infusion) and potassium
for lipophilic agents (verapamil, propranolol)

diseases.name: Burns
diseases.management: Per the 2019 NEJM Review and 2025 WMS Guideline: (1) Airway: intubate early if burns >40% TBSA, head/mouth burns, smoke inhalation, altered consciousness, hoarseness. (2) Breathing: 100% O₂ until CO poisoning ruled out (carboxyhemoglobin level); pulse oximetry unreliable. (3) Circulation/Fluid resuscitation: burns ≥20% TBSA require IV fluids. Parkland formula: 4 mL/kg/% TBSA burned in first 24 hrs (half in first 8 hrs). Modified Brooke: 2 mL/kg/% TBSA. Use LR. Titrate to urine output 0.5 mL/kg/hr (adults), 1 mL/kg/hr (children 30 kg). (4) Wound care: cool running water × 20 min (within 3 hrs); cover with clean dressings; silver sulfadiazine or mafenide acetate for partial/full-thickness burns. (5) Pain: IV opioids for moderate-severe burns; multimodal analgesia. (6) Tetanus prophylaxis. (7) Transfer to burn center: >10% TBSA partial-thickness, any full-thickness, face/hands/feet/genitalia/joints, inhalation injury, electrical/chemical burns, significant comorbidities.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Ice or ice water (worsens tissue injury). Over-resuscitation (pulmonary edema, compartment syndrome). Relying on pulse oximetry for CO poisoning (falsely normal). Delay in intubation for airway burns.

treatments.dosage: 4 mL/kg/% TBSA in 24 hrs (half in first 8 hrs)
treatments.notes: target UOP 0.5 mL/kg/hr adults
reduces depth and improves outcomes
multimodal approach preferred

diseases.name: Surgical Site Infection Prevention
diseases.management: Per the 2016 WHO Guidelines, 2014 IDSA Guidelines, and 2023 JAMA Review: (1) Administer antibiotics within 60 min before skin incision (within 120 min for vancomycin/fluoroquinolones). (2) Cefazolin is the most commonly used agent for most clean and clean-contaminated procedures. (3) Colorectal surgery: cefazolin + metronidazole (or cefoxitin alone) for anaerobic coverage. (4) Weight-based dosing: cefazolin 2 g for 1500 mL. (6) Single dose is sufficient for most procedures — do NOT continue antibiotics after wound closure (no benefit; increases C. difficile risk and AKI). Exception: cardiac surgery (up to 48 hrs). (7) Beta-lactam allergy: clindamycin or vancomycin. (8) MRSA-positive patients undergoing high-risk surgery: add vancomycin to standard prophylaxis; intranasal mupirocin decolonization.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prolonged prophylaxis beyond wound closure (no benefit; increases C. difficile, AKI). Fluoroquinolones as routine prophylaxis (resistance, C. difficile). Delay in antibiotic administration beyond 60 min pre-incision.

treatments.dosage: 2 g IV (120 kg); 3 g IV (≥120 kg)
treatments.frequency: single dose; redose q4h if ongoing
treatments.notes: within 60 min of incision
or cefoxitin 2 g alone

treatments.frequency: single dose; start 120 min before incision
treatments.notes: infuse over 60 min; add to (not replace) cefazolin if MRSA risk

diseases.name: Perforated Viscus
diseases.management: Per the 2023 WSES Guidelines and ACS Textbook: (1) Surgical emergency — prompt recognition and resuscitation are critical. (2) Initial management: NPO, IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem for severe sepsis), pain management. (3) CT abdomen/pelvis: primary imaging modality — demonstrates free air, fluid, and site of perforation. Unstable patients should go directly to OR without delay for imaging. (4) Surgical management: exploratory laparotomy (or laparoscopy in select cases); resect perforated segment; primary anastomosis if hemodynamically stable with limited contamination; diversion (stoma) if unstable, diffuse peritonitis, or high-risk anastomosis. (5) Damage control surgery: for hemodynamically unstable patients with diffuse peritonitis — abbreviated laparotomy, temporary abdominal closure, ICU resuscitation, then planned return to OR. (6) Antibiotics: continue until signs of infection resolve (typically 4–7 days for established peritonitis). (7) Specific etiologies: perforated peptic ulcer (Graham patch), perforated diverticulitis (Hartmann procedure or primary anastomosis ± diversion), perforated appendicitis (appendectomy).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgical consultation. CT scan in hemodynamically unstable patients (go directly to OR). Prolonged nonoperative management of free perforation. Primary anastomosis in unstable patients with diffuse contamination.

treatments.dosage: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h or meropenem 1 g IV q8h
treatments.notes: cover gram-negatives and anaerobes
resect perforated segment; primary anastomosis vs stoma based on clinical status
for hemodynamic instability with diffuse peritonitis

diseases.name: Surgical VTE Prophylaxis
diseases.management: Per the 2012 ACCP Guidelines and 2020 AHA Policy Statement: (1) Risk-stratify all surgical patients using Caprini score. (2) Very low risk (Caprini 0): early ambulation only. (3) Low risk (Caprini 1–2): mechanical prophylaxis (IPC preferred). (4) Moderate risk (Caprini 3–4): pharmacologic prophylaxis — LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) or LDUH (heparin 5000 U SC q8–12h), or IPC if high bleeding risk. (5) High risk (Caprini ≥5): pharmacologic + mechanical prophylaxis. (6) Orthopedic surgery (TKA/THA): LMWH, fondaparinux, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, or aspirin (after initial 5 days of DOAC); extended prophylaxis up to 35 days. (7) Cancer surgery (abdominopelvic): extended prophylaxis with LMWH × 4 weeks. (8) High bleeding risk: mechanical prophylaxis (IPC) until bleeding risk decreases, then add pharmacologic. (9) Start pharmacologic prophylaxis 6–12 hrs postoperatively (or per surgeon preference).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Pharmacologic prophylaxis in very low risk patients (Caprini 0). Delay in initiating prophylaxis. Graduated compression stockings alone (inferior to IPC). Stopping prophylaxis prematurely in high-risk patients.

treatments.duration: until ambulatory or discharge (extended 4 weeks for cancer surgery)
treatments.notes: start 6–12 hrs postop
less effective than LMWH for orthopedic surgery

treatments.useWhen: low risk or high bleeding risk
treatments.notes: preferred mechanical method
start 6–10 hrs postop

diseases.name: Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting
diseases.management: Per the 2020 Consensus Guidelines and 2021 Cochrane Network Meta-Analysis: (1) Risk factors (Apfel score): female sex, non-smoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, postoperative opioids. Each factor adds ~20% baseline risk. (2) Low risk (0–1 factors): no prophylaxis or single agent. (3) Moderate risk (2 factors): 1–2 antiemetics. (4) High risk (3–4 factors): multimodal prophylaxis (≥2 agents from different classes) + reduce baseline risk. (5) Reduce baseline risk: TIVA with propofol (avoid volatile anesthetics), minimize opioids (multimodal analgesia), minimize nitrous oxide. (6) First-line prophylactic agents: ondansetron 4 mg IV at end of surgery + dexamethasone 4–8 mg IV at induction — most studied and effective combination. (7) Additional agents: droperidol 0.625–1.25 mg IV, aprepitant 40 mg PO (NK1 antagonist — most effective single agent), scopolamine patch, promethazine. (8) Rescue antiemetic: use agent from different class than prophylaxis; ondansetron 4 mg IV if not given prophylactically. (9) NK1 receptor antagonists (aprepitant) are the most effective single agents for preventing vomiting.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Volatile anesthetics in high-risk patients (use TIVA). Droperidol without cardiac monitoring (QT prolongation). Same-class rescue antiemetic as prophylaxis. Ignoring risk stratification.

treatments.dosage: 4 mg IV at end of surgery
treatments.notes: 5-HT3 antagonist; most commonly used
synergistic with ondansetron; does not increase infection risk
NK1 antagonist; most effective single agent for vomiting

treatments.frequency: apply evening before or 2 hrs before surgery
treatments.notes: for patients at high risk; anticholinergic side effects

diseases.name: Postoperative Pain
diseases.management: Per the 2012 ASA Guidelines and 2023 WSES Guidelines: (1) Multimodal analgesia is the standard of care — reduces opioid consumption and opioid-related side effects. (2) Core regimen (unless contraindicated): scheduled acetaminophen 1 g q6h + NSAID (ibuprofen 400–600 mg q6h or ketorolac 15–30 mg IV q6h) + procedure-specific regional anesthesia/local infiltration. (3) Opioids as rescue only — lowest effective dose for shortest duration. (4) Adjuncts: gabapentin/pregabalin preoperatively (reduces opioid requirements), dexamethasone (anti-inflammatory + antiemetic), IV lidocaine infusion (abdominal surgery). (5) Regional techniques: neuraxial analgesia (epidural), peripheral nerve blocks, wound infiltration with local anesthetic — procedure-specific. (6) PCA (patient-controlled analgesia): for moderate-severe pain when oral route unavailable. (7) Transition to oral analgesics as soon as tolerated. (8) Opioid-sparing protocols with acetaminophen + NSAIDs provide equivalent pain control with fewer adverse effects for many procedures.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioid-only analgesia (more side effects, less effective than multimodal). Ketorolac >5 days (renal toxicity). Meperidine (seizure risk, active metabolite). Prescribing excessive opioids at discharge.

treatments.notes: foundation of multimodal analgesia

treatments.dosage: ibuprofen 400–600 mg q6h or ketorolac 15–30 mg IV q6h (max 5 days)
treatments.notes: avoid in renal impairment, GI bleeding risk
reduces systemic opioid requirements

treatments.dosage: morphine 2–4 mg IV PRN or oxycodone 5–10 mg PO PRN
treatments.notes: lowest effective dose; taper as pain improves

diseases.name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
diseases.management: Per the 2022 Cochrane Review and 2025 International Review: (1) Medical emergency — mortality SJS ~10%, TEN ~30%. (2) Immediately discontinue the causative drug (most common: allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, NSAIDs, antibiotics). (3) Transfer to burn unit or ICU for supportive care: wound care (non-adherent dressings, avoid silver sulfadiazine), fluid resuscitation, nutritional support, pain management, temperature regulation. (4) Eye care: ophthalmology consultation within 24 hrs; preservative-free lubricants, topical corticosteroids, amniotic membrane transplantation for severe ocular involvement. (5) Systemic therapy (controversial, no consensus): cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day (meta-analysis suggests survival benefit), systemic corticosteroids (short pulse — some evidence of benefit), IVIG (controversial — inconsistent evidence), etanercept (single SC dose — emerging evidence). (6) Combination therapy (corticosteroids + IVIG or corticosteroids + cyclosporine) may be more effective than monotherapy. (7) SCORTEN score for prognosis. (8) Thalidomide is CONTRAINDICATED (increased mortality).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Thalidomide (CONTRAINDICATED — increased mortality). Silver sulfadiazine (sulfonamide — may be causative agent). Delay in drug discontinuation. Delay in ophthalmology consultation.

treatments.notes: most important intervention; earlier discontinuation improves survival

treatments.dosage: wound care, fluid resuscitation, nutrition, pain management
treatments.notes: similar to burn management
meta-analysis suggests survival benefit
some evidence of benefit; controversial
inconsistent evidence; may be more effective combined with corticosteroids

diseases.name: Urticaria / Angioedema
diseases.management: Per the 2026 AAFP Review and 2014 AAAAI Guideline: (1) Acute urticaria (6 weeks): usually self-limited. First-line: second-generation H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine). Short course of oral corticosteroids if no response. (2) Chronic urticaria (≥6 weeks): stepped approach — Step 1: second-generation H1 antihistamine at standard dose. Step 2: increase dose up to 4× standard (FDA-approved). Step 3: add omalizumab (anti-IgE; FDA-approved for chronic spontaneous urticaria) or cyclosporine. (3) H2 blockers and leukotriene receptor antagonists: conflicting evidence; may be tried as adjuncts. (4) Avoid NSAIDs, heat, tight clothing (triggers). (5) Angioedema without urticaria: consider hereditary angioedema (C4 level, C1-INH level/function) or ACEi-induced angioedema. (6) ACEi-induced angioedema: discontinue ACEi permanently; do NOT switch to another ACEi; ARBs generally safe. (7) Long-term corticosteroids NOT recommended for chronic urticaria.
diseases.notes: Avoid: First-generation antihistamines as first-line (sedation, impairment). Long-term corticosteroids for chronic urticaria. Topical antihistamines or corticosteroids (ineffective). NSAIDs (may trigger urticaria).

treatments.dosage: cetirizine 10 mg, loratadine 10 mg, or fexofenadine 180 mg
treatments.notes: 50% achieve control at standard dose
anti-IgE; FDA-approved for chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to antihistamines
for severe acute urticaria unresponsive to antihistamines

diseases.name: Acne Vulgaris
diseases.management: Per the 2024 AAD Guidelines and 2021 JAMA Review: (1) Mild comedonal: topical retinoid (adapalene, tretinoin, tazarotene) ± benzoyl peroxide. (2) Mild-moderate inflammatory: topical retinoid + benzoyl peroxide + topical antibiotic (clindamycin). Fixed-dose combinations preferred (adapalene/BPO, BPO/clindamycin). (3) Moderate-severe inflammatory: add oral antibiotic (doxycycline 100 mg daily — first-line; minocycline alternative) to topical regimen. Limit oral antibiotics to 3–4 months; always combine with BPO to reduce resistance. (4) Hormonal therapy (females): combined oral contraceptives or spironolactone 50–100 mg daily. (5) Severe/nodulocystic or treatment-resistant: oral isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (cumulative dose 120–150 mg/kg). iPLEDGE program required; teratogenic (pregnancy category X). (6) Topical azelaic acid and salicylic acid: alternatives. (7) Maintenance: topical retinoid ± BPO long-term.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical or oral antibiotics as monotherapy (resistance). Oral antibiotics >3–4 months. Isotretinoin without iPLEDGE enrollment. Combining oral tetracyclines with isotretinoin (pseudotumor cerebri).

treatments.notes: fixed-dose combination preferred; comedolytic + antimicrobial
always combine with BPO; limit duration to reduce resistance
off-label; monitor potassium; contraindicated in pregnancy
iPLEDGE required; teratogenic; monitor LFTs, lipids

diseases.name: Impetigo
diseases.management: Per the 2014 IDSA Guidelines and 2026 AAFP Review: (1) Most common in children 2–5 years. Caused by S. aureus and/or Group A Streptococcus. (2) Nonbullous (70%): honey-colored crusted lesions. Bullous (30%): flaccid bullae. (3) Mild, limited lesions: topical mupirocin 2% ointment BID × 5 days (first-line). (4) Extensive, multilesional, or outbreaks: oral antibiotics × 7 days — dicloxacillin or cephalexin (covers MSSA). If MRSA suspected: TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin. (5) Ecthyma (deeper ulcerative form): oral antibiotics required. (6) Hygiene measures: hand washing, avoid sharing towels, exclude from school/daycare until 12–24 hrs after starting antibiotics.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical antibiotics for extensive disease. Oral antibiotics without culture if MRSA suspected. Sharing towels/personal items. Returning to school before 12–24 hrs of treatment.

treatments.notes: first-line for limited disease
covers MSSA and GAS
or doxycycline, clindamycin

diseases.name: Hypothyroidism
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review, 2024 Lancet Review, and 2014 ATA Guideline: (1) Hashimoto thyroiditis causes ~85% of primary hypothyroidism. (2) Diagnosis: elevated TSH + low free T4 = overt; elevated TSH + normal free T4 = subclinical. (3) First-line: levothyroxine (synthetic T4) — full replacement dose 1.6 mcg/kg/day; take on empty stomach 30–60 min before breakfast. (4) Start lower doses (25–50 mcg/day) in elderly and patients with cardiovascular disease. (5) Monitor TSH 6–8 weeks after dose change, then annually. (6) Subclinical hypothyroidism: treat if TSH ≥10 mIU/L; consider treatment if TSH 5–10 in patients 65 with symptoms. Do NOT treat subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly (≥65) with TSH 10 — no benefit shown. (7) Pregnancy: increase dose by 30% once confirmed; target TSH per trimester-specific ranges. (8) Avoid calcium, iron, PPIs within 4 hours of levothyroxine.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Desiccated thyroid extract as first-line (variable T3 content). Liothyronine (T3) monotherapy. Treating subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly with TSH 10. Switching levothyroxine brands without retesting TSH.

treatments.dosage: 1.6 mcg/kg/day (full replacement); start 25–50 mcg in elderly/CVD
treatments.notes: take on empty stomach; monitor TSH q6–8 weeks until stable

diseases.name: Hyperthyroidism / Graves Disease
diseases.management: Per the 2016 ATA Guideline and 2023 Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology Review: (1) Three treatment options: antithyroid drugs (ATDs), radioactive iodine (RAI), or thyroidectomy. (2) ATDs (methimazole preferred): first-line worldwide; remission in ~40–50% after 12–18 months. Start methimazole 10–30 mg daily; titrate to euthyroidism. (3) RAI: definitive therapy; results in hypothyroidism in >80%; avoid in moderate-severe Graves ophthalmopathy. (4) Thyroidectomy: for large goiters, suspected malignancy, or severe ophthalmopathy. (5) Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) for symptom control in all patients until euthyroid. (6) PTU: reserved for first trimester of pregnancy (methimazole is teratogenic in first trimester) and thyroid storm. (7) Monitor CBC at baseline and if fever/sore throat (agranulocytosis risk ~0.2%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Methimazole in first trimester of pregnancy (teratogenic). RAI in moderate-severe Graves ophthalmopathy. Continuing ATDs without monitoring for agranulocytosis. Beta-blockers alone without definitive therapy.

treatments.notes: preferred ATD; titrate to euthyroidism; check CBC if fever

treatments.duration: first trimester then switch to methimazole
treatments.notes: hepatotoxicity risk; reserved for specific situations
symptom control; also blocks T4→T3 conversion
results in hypothyroidism >80%; avoid in active Graves ophthalmopathy

diseases.name: Thyroid Storm
diseases.management: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening exacerbation of thyrotoxicosis (mortality 10–30%). (1) PTU 500–1000 mg loading dose then 250 mg q4h PO/NG (preferred over methimazole — blocks T4→T3 conversion). (2) Iodine solution (SSKI or Lugol's) 1 hour AFTER PTU to block thyroid hormone release. (3) Propranolol 60–80 mg q4h PO or esmolol IV for heart rate control. (4) Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h (blocks T4→T3 conversion, treats relative adrenal insufficiency). (5) Cooling measures for hyperthermia (avoid aspirin — displaces T4 from binding proteins). (6) Identify and treat precipitant (infection, surgery, iodine load). (7) ICU admission.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aspirin for fever (displaces T4 from TBG). Iodine BEFORE PTU (can worsen thyrotoxicosis). Methimazole as sole agent (does not block T4→T3 conversion).

treatments.notes: blocks synthesis AND T4→T3 conversion
give 1 hour AFTER PTU
or esmolol IV if unable to take PO
blocks T4→T3; treats relative adrenal insufficiency

diseases.name: Cushing Syndrome
diseases.management: Per the 2023 JAMA Review: (1) Most common cause is exogenous glucocorticoid use. (2) Screening: 24-hour urinary free cortisol (2–3 samples), late-night salivary cortisol (2–3 samples), 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. (3) Determine ACTH-dependent vs independent: morning plasma ACTH. (4) ACTH-dependent (80–85%): pituitary MRI; bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling if MRI equivocal. (5) First-line treatment: surgical resection of causative tumor (transsphenoidal surgery for Cushing disease — ~80% remission). (6) Medical therapy (if surgery fails/not feasible): steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat); pituitary-directed (pasireotide, cabergoline). (7) Bilateral adrenalectomy for refractory cases. (8) Post-surgical adrenal insufficiency requires glucocorticoid replacement with slow taper.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Screening all patients with diabetes for Cushing syndrome (high false-positive rate). Dexamethasone suppression test in patients on estrogen (false positive). Bilateral adrenalectomy without exhausting other options (Nelson syndrome risk).

treatments.notes: ~80% remission; post-op adrenal insufficiency expected
steroidogenesis inhibitor; monitor LFTs
11β-hydroxylase inhibitor; FDA-approved for Cushing disease

diseases.name: Adrenal Insufficiency / Crisis
diseases.management: Per the 2025 JAMA Review and 2016 Endocrine Society Guideline: (1) Diagnosis: early-morning cortisol (10 = AI unlikely). Measure ACTH and DHEAS simultaneously. (2) Primary AI: low cortisol + high ACTH + low DHEAS. Secondary AI: low cortisol + low/normal ACTH. (3) Treatment: hydrocortisone 15–25 mg daily in 2–3 divided doses (largest dose on waking) OR prednisone 3–5 mg daily. (4) Primary AI: add fludrocortisone 0.05–0.3 mg daily for mineralocorticoid replacement. (5) Sick-day rules: double or triple glucocorticoid dose during illness/stress. (6) Adrenal crisis: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus then 50 mg IV q6–8h + aggressive IV NS resuscitation. (7) All patients: medical alert bracelet, injectable hydrocortisone for emergencies.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dexamethasone for daily replacement (too potent, Cushingoid risk). Hormonal monitoring to guide dose (use clinical assessment). Stopping glucocorticoids abruptly. Failing to stress-dose during illness/surgery.

treatments.dosage: 15–25 mg daily (e.g., 15 mg AM, 5 mg early PM)
treatments.notes: bioidentical to cortisol; individualize dose
longer-acting; once-daily dosing
mineralocorticoid replacement; not needed in secondary AI

treatments.dosage: 100 mg IV bolus then 50 mg q6–8h
treatments.notes: + aggressive IV NS resuscitation

diseases.name: Pheochromocytoma
diseases.management: Per the 2019 NEJM Review and NCCN Guidelines: (1) Diagnosis: plasma free metanephrines (sensitivity 89–100%) or 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines. Elevations >3× ULN are diagnostic. (2) Imaging: adrenal CT/MRI after biochemical confirmation. (3) Preoperative preparation: alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BID titrated up, or doxazosin 1 mg daily titrated up) for ≥7–14 days + high-sodium diet + liberal fluids. (4) Beta-blockade ONLY AFTER adequate alpha-blockade (for tachycardia). (5) Surgical resection: minimally invasive adrenalectomy is standard. (6) ~40% have germline mutations — genetic testing recommended for all. (7) Long-term surveillance: annual biochemical testing (metanephrines) indefinitely.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Beta-blockers BEFORE alpha-blockade (unopposed alpha stimulation → hypertensive crisis). Biopsy of suspected pheochromocytoma. Medications that precipitate crisis (metoclopramide, glucagon, TCAs, MAOIs). Combination alpha/beta blockers (e.g., labetalol) as sole agent.

treatments.dosage: 10 mg BID, titrate to 30 mg TID
treatments.notes: nonselective alpha-blocker; more postop hypotension

treatments.dosage: 1 mg daily, titrate to 10 mg BID
treatments.notes: selective alpha-1 blocker; longer half-life
ONLY after adequate alpha-blockade; target HR ~80 bpm

diseases.name: Primary Hyperparathyroidism
diseases.management: Per the 2022 JAMA Review and 2014 Fourth International Workshop Guidelines: (1) Diagnosis: elevated calcium + elevated or inappropriately normal PTH. (2) Parathyroidectomy indications (asymptomatic): calcium >1 mg/dL above ULN, T-score ≤−2.5, CrCl 250 mg/d (women) or >300 mg/d (men), age 95%. (4) Medical management (if surgery not pursued): cinacalcet 30 mg BID (lowers calcium, not PTH-mediated bone loss); bisphosphonates or denosumab for skeletal protection. (5) Monitor calcium, creatinine, BMD annually if observation chosen. (6) Ensure vitamin D sufficiency (≥20 ng/mL). (7) Adequate calcium intake (not excessive — 800–1000 mg/day).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Thiazide diuretics (worsen hypercalcemia). Excessive calcium intake (>1000 mg/day). Dehydration. Lithium (can cause hyperparathyroidism).

treatments.notes: indicated for symptomatic or asymptomatic meeting criteria
lowers calcium; does not improve BMD
improves BMD; does not lower calcium

diseases.name: Hypercalcemia
diseases.management: Per the 2022 JAMA Review and 2023 Endocrine Society Guideline: (1) Mild/asymptomatic: avoid exacerbating factors (dehydration, thiazides, excessive calcium/vitamin D). (2) Severe (≥14 mg/dL) or symptomatic: IV NS 200–300 mL/hr initially → calcitonin 4 IU/kg q12h (rapid onset, tachyphylaxis in 48 hrs) → zoledronic acid 4–5 mg IV over 15 min (onset 2–4 days, duration weeks). (3) Denosumab if renal impairment or bisphosphonate-refractory. (4) Treat underlying cause: parathyroidectomy for PHPT; chemotherapy for malignancy. (5) Furosemide ONLY after adequate volume resuscitation (not routine). (6) Glucocorticoids for granulomatous disease, lymphoma, vitamin D-mediated hypercalcemia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Furosemide before adequate hydration. Thiazide diuretics. Immobilization. Excessive calcium/vitamin D supplementation.

treatments.notes: correct dehydration first
rapid onset; tachyphylaxis after 48 hrs
most effective for sustained correction
onset 4–10 days | for bisphosphonate-refractory or renal impairment

diseases.name: Hypocalcemia
diseases.management: (1) Acute/symptomatic (tetany, seizures, QTc prolongation): IV calcium gluconate 1–2 g (10–20 mL of 10% solution) over 10–20 min, then continuous infusion 0.5–1.5 mg/kg/hr. Monitor for cardiac arrhythmias. (2) Chronic: oral calcium carbonate 1–3 g/day in divided doses + calcitriol 0.25–2 mcg/day. (3) Correct hypomagnesemia (magnesium is required for PTH secretion and action). (4) Hypoparathyroidism: calcium + calcitriol ± recombinant PTH (Natpara) for refractory cases. (5) Monitor serum calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, 24-hour urine calcium. (6) Target low-normal calcium to avoid hypercalciuria and nephrolithiasis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: IV calcium chloride via peripheral line (tissue necrosis risk). Rapid IV calcium push (cardiac arrest risk). Calcium without calcitriol in hypoparathyroidism (ineffective).

treatments.notes: prefer gluconate over chloride (less tissue necrosis if extravasation)
calcitriol bypasses need for PTH-dependent activation

treatments.dosage: magnesium sulfate 2 g IV or magnesium oxide 400 mg PO
treatments.notes: correct hypomagnesemia first

diseases.name: Osteoporosis
diseases.management: Per the 2023 NEJM Review, 2023 ACP Guideline, and 2024 ASBMR/BHOF Position Statement: (1) Screen: DXA for women ≥65, men ≥70, or younger with risk factors. (2) Treat if: T-score ≤−2.5, hip/vertebral fracture, or FRAX ≥3% hip / ≥20% MOF. (3) High fracture risk: bisphosphonates first-line (alendronate 70 mg weekly, risedronate 35 mg weekly, or zoledronic acid 5 mg IV annually). Denosumab 60 mg SC q6 months if bisphosphonate contraindicated. (4) Very high fracture risk: anabolic agents first (teriparatide or abaloparatide × 2 years, or romosozumab × 1 year) → then transition to antiresorptive. (5) Bisphosphonate holiday: consider after 5 years oral or 3 years IV if no longer high risk; reassess q2–4 years. (6) Denosumab: do NOT stop without transitioning to bisphosphonate (rebound vertebral fractures). (7) Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day + vitamin D 800–1000 IU/day for all.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Stopping denosumab without bisphosphonate transition (rebound fractures). Bisphosphonates if eGFR 30–35. Romosozumab if recent cardiovascular event. Ibandronate for hip fracture prevention (no evidence).

treatments.notes: take on empty stomach with water; sit upright 30 min
most potent bisphosphonate

treatments.duration: ongoing (do not stop without bisphosphonate transition)
treatments.notes: RANKL inhibitor; safe in renal impairment
anabolic; follow with antiresorptive
anti-sclerostin; follow with antiresorptive; avoid if recent MI/stroke

diseases.name: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuresis
diseases.management: Per the 2023 NEJM Review and 2022 JAMA Review: (1) Identify and treat underlying cause (malignancy, medications, CNS/pulmonary disease). (2) Severely symptomatic (seizures, coma): 3% hypertonic saline 100–150 mL bolus over 10–20 min; may repeat × 2; goal raise Na 4–6 mEq/L in first 6 hours. (3) Correction limits: ≤8 mEq/L in 24 hours (≤6 in high-risk for ODS: hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, Na 105). (4) Non-emergent: fluid restriction (first-line, 1–1.5 L/day). (5) Second-line: salt tablets + furosemide, urea 15–60 g/day, or tolvaptan 15 mg daily (start in hospital; monitor Na closely). (6) SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) emerging as option. (7) Isotonic saline worsens SIAD — do NOT use.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Isotonic saline (worsens SIAD). Overcorrection >8 mEq/L in 24 hrs (osmotic demyelination syndrome). Tolvaptan in liver disease. Vaptans in hypovolemic hyponatremia.

treatments.notes: goal raise Na 4–6 mEq/L in 6 hrs; limit ≤8 mEq/L in 24 hrs
mainstay; ~50% respond
V2 receptor antagonist; start in hospital; monitor Na q6h initially
osmotic diuresis; dissolve in juice for palatability

diseases.name: Diabetes Insipidus
diseases.management: (1) Central DI: desmopressin (DDAVP) 10–40 mcg intranasally BID or 0.1–0.4 mg PO BID-TID. Titrate to control polyuria while avoiding hyponatremia. (2) Nephrogenic DI: treat underlying cause (lithium, hypercalcemia); thiazide diuretics (paradoxical antidiuresis) + amiloride (if lithium-induced); low-sodium diet; NSAIDs (indomethacin) as adjunct. (3) Monitor serum sodium, urine output, urine osmolality. (4) Ensure free access to water at all times. (5) Diagnosis: water deprivation test or desmopressin trial; copeptin measurement emerging as preferred diagnostic test.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Desmopressin in nephrogenic DI (ineffective). Fluid restriction without free water access (dangerous). Overcorrection of hypernatremia (>10 mEq/L per 24 hrs).

treatments.dosage: 10–40 mcg intranasal BID or 0.1–0.4 mg PO BID-TID
treatments.notes: titrate to urine output; monitor for hyponatremia
paradoxical antidiuresis; combine with amiloride if lithium-induced
blocks lithium entry via ENaC

diseases.name: Acute Kidney Injury
diseases.management: Per KDIGO 2012 AKI Guideline: (1) Diagnosis: increase in serum creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hrs, or ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, or urine output 0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6 hrs. (2) Identify and treat cause: prerenal (volume depletion, hypotension), intrinsic (ATN, AIN, glomerulonephritis), postrenal (obstruction). (3) Volume resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid for prerenal AKI. (4) Discontinue nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast). (5) Avoid hyperglycemia, maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg. (6) Renal replacement therapy (RRT): for refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, volume overload, uremic symptoms. (7) No benefit of dopamine, fenoldopam, or mannitol for AKI prevention/treatment. (8) Contrast-induced AKI prevention: IV NS hydration pre/post procedure; hold metformin if eGFR 30.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine for renal protection (no benefit). Furosemide to prevent AKI (no benefit; may use for volume overload). Nephrotoxic contrast without hydration. Delay in relieving urinary obstruction.

treatments.notes: balanced crystalloid (LR) may be preferred over NS
resume ACEi/ARB after recovery
for refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, volume overload, uremia

diseases.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
diseases.management: Per the 2024 KDIGO Guideline Update and 2026 AHA/ACC/ADA/ASN CKM Guideline: (1) Classify by eGFR and albuminuria (KDIGO heat map). (2) First-line: ACEi or ARB for CKD with albuminuria (UACR ≥30 mg/g) or hypertension. (3) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) for CKD with eGFR ≥20 and UACR ≥200 (or any CKD with HF) — reduces CKD progression, CV death, HF hospitalization. (4) Finerenone for CKD + T2D + albuminuria (on max tolerated ACEi/ARB). (5) GLP-1 RA for CKD + T2D with suboptimal glycemic control. (6) BP target 130/80. (7) Statin for all CKD patients ≥50 years. (8) Avoid NSAIDs. (9) Refer to nephrology if eGFR 30, severe albuminuria, or rapid decline. (10) Manage complications: anemia (iron, ESAs), metabolite bone disease (phosphate binders, vitamin D), metabolic acidosis (sodium bicarbonate).
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (accelerate CKD). Combining ACEi + ARB (hyperkalemia, no benefit). Stopping ACEi/ARB for mild creatinine rise (30%). Metformin if eGFR 30.

treatments.notes: for CKD with albuminuria or HTN; do not combine ACEi + ARB
for eGFR ≥20; expect initial eGFR dip (protective); reduces CKD progression 30–40%
for CKD + T2D + albuminuria on max ACEi/ARB; monitor K+
for serum bicarb 22; may slow CKD progression

diseases.name: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
diseases.management: ARDS management centers on identifying and treating the underlying cause while providing lung-protective ventilation (4–8 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure 30 cm H₂O, driving pressure 15 cm H₂O). Conservative fluid strategy once off vasopressors. For moderate-to-severe ARDS (P/F 150), prone positioning ≥12–16 hr/day is strongly recommended. Neuromuscular blockade may be used in early severe ARDS. Higher PEEP for moderate-to-severe disease. Corticosteroids conditionally recommended within 14 days of onset. VV-ECMO for severe refractory cases (P/F 80) at experienced centers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: IV beta-2 agonists (harm, no benefit). HFOV (strongly against). Prolonged recruitment maneuvers (strongly against). Steroids ≥14 days after onset. Surfactant, statins, ketoconazole — all failed in trials.

treatments.notes: use when moderate-to-severe ARDS within 14 days of onset

treatments.dosage: 20 mg daily × 5 days then 10 mg daily × 5 days
treatments.notes: use when early ARDS (within 24 hr)
use when early severe ARDS with P/F 150 and refractory dyssynchrony
use when refractory hypoxemia despite prone positioning

diseases.name: Cardiogenic Shock
diseases.management: Critical reduction in cardiac output with end-organ hypoperfusion (SBP 90 mmHg). Requires early recognition, treatment of underlying cause (urgent revascularization for MI), multidisciplinary shock team. IV inotropic support to maintain perfusion (Class I). PA catheter may guide therapy. Norepinephrine preferred vasopressor. Temporary MCS when pharmacologic therapy fails. Triage to MCS-capable centers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine (more arrhythmias). Aggressive IV fluid boluses (worsen congestion). Negative inotropes (beta-blockers, non-DHP CCBs) — hold in acute shock.

treatments.notes: use when hypotension
use when low CO with adequate BP
use when dobutamine-refractory or on beta-blockers
use when combined support needed

diseases.name: Hypovolemic / Hemorrhagic Shock
diseases.specialty: Trauma Surgery / Critical Care / Emergency Medicine
diseases.management: Damage-control resuscitation: simultaneous hemorrhage control and volume restoration. Limit crystalloid ≤3 L in first 6 hours. Massive transfusion protocol with 1:1:1 ratio (plasma:platelets:RBCs). Permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90 mmHg) until definitive control (except TBI). TXA within 3 hours. Empirical calcium after every 4 units blood. Correct lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Excessive crystalloid (>3 L). Hydroxyethyl starch (renal injury, coagulopathy). Hypertonic saline (no benefit).

treatments.notes: target Hb ≥7 g/dL (≥8 in CAD); 1:1:1 ratio
corrects coagulopathy
maintain >50,000

treatments.dosage: 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8 hr
treatments.notes: do not give >3 hr post-injury
prevents citrate toxicity
use when fibrinogen 150–200 mg/dL

diseases.name: Cardiac Tamponade
diseases.management: Medical emergency from pericardial fluid compressing the heart. Diagnosis: Beck's triad + echo (diastolic RV collapse, RA inversion, plethoric IVC). Definitive treatment: echo-guided pericardiocentesis. Surgical drainage for aortic dissection, free wall rupture, trauma, purulent pericarditis. Temporize with gentle IV fluid bolus and vasopressors. Avoid positive-pressure ventilation and sedation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Positive-pressure ventilation. IV diuretics. Vasodilators. IV sedation.

treatments.notes: drain slowly to avoid decompression syndrome
gentle volume expansion only
use when post-drainage to prevent recurrence
use when inflammatory effusion

diseases.name: Cardiac Arrest / Post-ROSC Care
diseases.management: Post-cardiac arrest syndrome management: secure airway, support cardiopulmonary function, identify/treat cause. Target MAP ≥65 mmHg (preferably >80). SpO₂ 92–98% (avoid hyperoxia). Emergent coronary angiography for STEMI. Temperature management: targeted normothermia (36°C) or mild hypothermia (32–36°C) for ≥24 hr; prevent fever. Multimodal neuroprognostication delayed ≥72 hr after normothermia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Hyperoxia (PaO₂ >300). Routine corticosteroids. Premature neuroprognostication (72 hr). Routine prophylactic antibiotics.

treatments.notes: target MAP ≥65–80 mmHg
prevent fever >37.5°C; rewarm ≤0.5°C/hr
use when post-ROSC seizures

diseases.name: Rhabdomyolysis
diseases.management: Skeletal muscle breakdown releasing myoglobin, K⁺, PO₄, CK. Primary complication: AKI. Confirm with CK >5× ULN. Priorities: remove cause, aggressive IV crystalloid (goal UOP 200–300 mL/hr), monitor/correct electrolytes (hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia), watch for compartment syndrome and DIC. RRT for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, uremia, fluid overload. Urine alkalinization controversial.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Loop diuretics (no benefit; worsen depletion). Routine calcium for asymptomatic hypocalcemia. Nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast).

treatments.duration: until CK trending down and UOP adequate
treatments.notes: goal UOP 200–300 mL/hr; adjust for comorbidities

treatments.dosage: 150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 200 mL/hr
treatments.notes: controversial; may worsen hypocalcemia
do not routinely correct asymptomatic hypocalcemia

diseases.name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
diseases.management: Surgical emergency, mortality 30–50%. CTA is gold standard. Management by etiology: arterial embolism → embolectomy; arterial thrombosis → thrombectomy/bypass; MVT → systemic heparin; NOMI → supportive care + catheter-directed papaverine. Endovascular preferred in stable patients without peritonitis. Peritonitis → emergent laparotomy. Second-look laparotomy at 24–48 hr for extensive involvement. Supportive: fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in revascularization. Vasopressors when possible (worsen NOMI). Systemic thrombolysis for MVT.

treatments.notes: titrate to aPTT 1.5–2.5× control
cover gram-negatives and anaerobes

treatments.route: endovascular preferred if stable without peritonitis

diseases.name: Large Bowel Obstruction
diseases.management: Most commonly caused by CRC (~60%), volvulus (~15%), diverticular stricture. CT abdomen/pelvis confirms diagnosis. Initial: NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, electrolyte correction. Competent ileocecal valve → closed-loop obstruction → risk of cecal perforation (>12 cm). Emergent surgery for peritonitis/perforation/ischemia. For malignant obstruction: emergent surgery (Hartmann's or primary anastomosis) or SEMS as bridge to surgery for left-sided lesions.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay when cecal diameter >12 cm. Barium enema if perforation suspected. SEMS with perforation/peritonitis.

treatments.notes: use when left-sided malignant obstruction without perforation

diseases.name: Sigmoid / Cecal Volvulus
diseases.management: Sigmoid volvulus (~75% of colonic volvulus): endoscopic detorsion via sigmoidoscopy (success ~80%), followed by semi-elective sigmoid resection same admission (recurrence 40–60%). Emergent surgery if detorsion fails or gangrene/perforation. Cecal volvulus: right hemicolectomy (definitive); endoscopic decompression generally unsuccessful.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Endoscopic detorsion for cecal volvulus (low success). Delay after sigmoid detorsion.

treatments.notes: success ~80%; plan semi-elective resection

diseases.name: Incarcerated / Strangulated Hernia
diseases.management: Strangulated hernias require emergent surgical repair. Incarcerated (non-strangulated): trial of manual reduction if no strangulation signs; follow with urgent elective repair. Surgical approach: open or laparoscopic with bowel viability assessment; resect nonviable bowel. Broad-spectrum antibiotics if bowel compromise suspected.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Manual reduction when strangulation suspected. Delay in surgery for strangulated hernias.

treatments.notes: cover gram-negatives and anaerobes

diseases.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (incl. rupture)
diseases.management: Asymptomatic AAA 5.5 cm (men) / 5.0 cm (women): surveillance + risk factor modification. Elective repair when ≥5.5 cm, growth >1 cm/yr, or symptomatic. EVAR preferred when anatomy suitable. Ruptured AAA: permissive hypotension (SBP 70–90), MTP, emergent repair (EVAR if feasible).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aggressive fluid resuscitation in rupture. Delay for symptomatic/ruptured AAA.

treatments.notes: lower perioperative mortality; requires long-term surveillance
cardiovascular risk reduction

diseases.name: Acute Limb Ischemia
diseases.management: Presents with "6 Ps" (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesias, paralysis). Rutherford classification guides management. Emergent evaluation by vascular specialist. Immediate systemic anticoagulation with heparin (Class I). Revascularization strategy: catheter-directed thrombolysis (Class I for salvageable limb), surgical thromboembolectomy (Class IIa for embolic), percutaneous mechanical thrombectomy (Class IIa adjunct). Amputation for nonsalvageable limb. Monitor for compartment syndrome post-revascularization.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in anticoagulation. Delay in revascularization for threatened limbs. | Delay in anticoagulation. Delay in revascularization for threatened limbs.

treatments.notes: prevents clot propagation
Class I recommendation
Class IIa
monitor for compartment syndrome

diseases.name: Ischemic Colitis
diseases.management: Most common form of intestinal ischemia; typically non-occlusive, affecting watershed areas. Median age 70. Presents with lower abdominal pain and hematochezia. CT with contrast for initial evaluation; colonoscopy within 48 hr if no peritoneal signs (minimal insufflation, CO₂ preferred). >95% non-occlusive → antithrombotic therapy not needed. Most cases resolve with supportive care: bowel rest, IV fluids, stop offending medications. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics in severe cases. Surgery for peritonitis, perforation, or gangrene. 15% develop bowel necrosis. Right-sided or pancolitis has mortality ~21%.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs, digoxin, constipation-inducing drugs (predisposing factors). Antithrombotic therapy (not needed in non-occlusive disease). Barium enema if perforation suspected. | NSAIDs, digoxin, constipation-inducing drugs (predisposing factors). Barium enema if perforation suspected.

treatments.notes: bowel rest
not routine for mild cases

diseases.name: Toxic Megacolon
diseases.management: Rare but severe complication of colonic inflammation (IBD, C. difficile, CMV). Defined by radiographic colonic distention >6 cm + systemic toxicity + inflammatory etiology. Requires coordination between medical and surgical services. Aggressive medical therapy: IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 60 mg/day or hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h), IV fluids, transfusions, electrolyte correction, bowel rest, NG decompression, DVT prophylaxis. Avoid anticholinergics, opioids, antidiarrheals. Frequent re-evaluation. Surgery indicated if: no improvement after 48–72 hr of steroids, perforation, massive hemorrhage, increasing toxicity, or progressive dilation. Subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy is standard surgical approach. Perforation mortality 27–57%.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticholinergics, opioids, antidiarrheals (worsen dilation). Barium enema. Colonoscopy (perforation risk). Delay in surgery when deteriorating. | Anticholinergics, opioids, antidiarrheals (worsen dilation). Colonoscopy (perforation risk). Delay in surgery when deteriorating.

treatments.notes: use when IBD-related toxic megacolon

treatments.medication: IV vancomycin (rectal) + oral vancomycin + IV metronidazole
treatments.dosage: vancomycin 500 mg q6h rectal + 500 mg q6h oral + metronidazole 500 mg q8h IV
treatments.notes: fulminant C. difficile protocol

treatments.route: for failure of medical therapy, perforation, hemorrhage

treatments.dosage: vancomycin 500 mg q6h oral + rectal; metronidazole 500 mg q8h IV
treatments.notes: fulminant C. difficile protocol

diseases.name: Perianal Abscess / Anal Fissure
diseases.management: Perianal abscess: requires incision and drainage (I&D) — antibiotics alone are insufficient. Antibiotics added only for cellulitis, immunosuppression, prosthetic valves, or systemic signs. ~30–50% develop fistula-in-ano. Anal fissure: most are posterior midline. Acute fissures (6 weeks): conservative management with fiber, sitz baths, stool softeners. Chronic fissures: topical nitroglycerin 0.4% or topical diltiazem 2% (first-line); botulinum toxin injection (second-line); lateral internal sphincterotomy (definitive for refractory cases).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics alone for abscess (must drain). Opioids for fissure pain (worsen constipation). | Antibiotics alone for abscess (must drain). Opioids for fissure pain (worsen constipation).

treatments.notes: do not delay; antibiotics alone insufficient
headache common (up to 50%)
fewer headaches than nitroglycerin
use when topical therapy fails
small risk of incontinence

diseases.name: Hemorrhoids
diseases.management: Classified as internal (above dentate line, grades I–IV) or external. Conservative management first: high-fiber diet (25–30 g/day), adequate hydration, stool softeners, sitz baths, topical agents. Office procedures for grades I–III: rubber band ligation (most effective office procedure), sclerotherapy, infrared coagulation. Surgical hemorrhoidectomy for grade III–IV or failed office procedures. Thrombosed external hemorrhoids: excision within 72 hr of onset provides best relief; after 72 hr, conservative management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Straining. Prolonged sitting on toilet. Excessive wiping. | Straining. Prolonged sitting on toilet.

treatments.notes: reduces bleeding and recurrence
most effective office procedure

treatments.duration: for grade III–IV or failed office procedures
treatments.notes: gold standard; more pain but lower recurrence
after 72 hr, conservative management preferred

diseases.name: Pilonidal Disease
diseases.management: Chronic inflammatory condition of the natal cleft, most common in young hirsute males. Acute pilonidal abscess: incision and drainage. Chronic/recurrent disease: surgical excision with various closure techniques (primary closure, Limberg/Karydakis flap, open healing by secondary intention). Off-midline closure techniques have lower recurrence rates than midline closure. Minimally invasive options include endoscopic pilonidal sinus treatment (EPSiT) and pit-picking procedures. Postoperative wound care and hair removal (shaving or laser) reduce recurrence.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Midline closure (higher recurrence). Neglecting postoperative hair removal. | Midline closure (higher recurrence). Neglecting postoperative hair removal.

treatments.notes: lateral to midline incision preferred
lower recurrence than midline closure
faster recovery; higher recurrence

diseases.name: Traumatic Brain Injury (epidural / subdural)
diseases.management: Epidural hematoma (EDH): typically from middle meningeal artery tear; "lucid interval" classic but not universal. Surgical evacuation (craniotomy) indicated for EDH >30 mL, thickness >15 mm, or midline shift >5 mm regardless of GCS. Acute subdural hematoma (SDH): most prevalent focal TBI lesion; high mortality. BTF guidelines recommend evacuation for SDH >10 mm thickness or >5 mm midline shift regardless of GCS, or with GCS drop ≥2 points, or ICP >20 mmHg. Large craniotomy (~12 × 15 cm) for optimal visualization and decompression. Medical management: elevate HOB 30°, maintain CPP 60–70 mmHg, osmotherapy (mannitol 20% 0.25–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline 3% 250 mL or 23.4% 30 mL) for elevated ICP, seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam or phenytoin × 7 days), normothermia, avoid hypotension (SBP 90) and hypoxia (SpO₂ 90%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Hypotension (SBP 90 — doubles mortality). Hypoxia (SpO₂ 90). Steroids (CRASH trial: increased mortality in TBI). Burr hole drainage of solid acute SDH clots (ineffective). | Hypotension (SBP 90 — doubles mortality). Hypoxia. Steroids (CRASH trial: increased mortality). Prophylactic anticonvulsants beyond 7 days.

treatments.duration: for large EDH/SDH meeting surgical criteria
treatments.notes: large bone flap for SDH
monitor serum osmolality (hold if >320)
no osmolality ceiling; preferred in hypovolemia
early post-traumatic seizure prophylaxis

diseases.name: Hemothorax / Tension Pneumothorax
diseases.specialty: Trauma Surgery / Emergency Medicine / Critical Care
diseases.management: Tension pneumothorax is immediately life-threatening: one-way valve mechanism causing mediastinal shift, decreased venous return, cardiovascular collapse. Diagnosis is clinical (do not delay for imaging). Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS midaxillary) or finger thoracostomy, followed by chest tube. Simple pneumothorax: chest tube (small bore 8.5–24 Fr acceptable); small occult PTX (3.5 cm apical rim) may be observed ≥24 hr. Hemothorax ≥500 mL: drain with large-bore chest tube (28–40 Fr). Massive hemothorax (>1500 mL initial or >200 mL/hr × 3 hr): emergent thoracotomy. Small HTX (300 mL) in stable patient may be observed.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in decompression for tension PTX. CXR before treatment of clinical tension PTX. Small-bore tubes for large hemothorax (risk of retained HTX/empyema). | CXR before treatment of clinical tension PTX. Small-bore tubes for large hemothorax.

treatments.dosage: 14-gauge needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS midaxillary
treatments.notes: temporizing only

treatments.dosage: 28–40 Fr for HTX; 8.5–24 Fr for PTX
treatments.notes: connect to water seal or suction

treatments.route: for >1500 mL initial output or >200 mL/hr × 3 hr

diseases.name: Compartment Syndrome
diseases.specialty: Orthopedic Surgery / Trauma Surgery / Emergency Medicine
diseases.management: Time-sensitive surgical emergency from increased pressure within a closed fascial compartment, most commonly after fractures or crush injuries. Clinical diagnosis: pain out of proportion (earliest finding), pain with passive stretch, tense compartment, paresthesias, motor/sensory deficits, decreased pulses (late). Intracompartmental pressure measurement: absolute pressure >30 mmHg or delta pressure (diastolic BP minus compartment pressure) 30 mmHg indicates fasciotomy. Treatment: emergent fasciotomy (all compartments of affected extremity). Tissue necrosis occurs within 6–12 hr. Post-fasciotomy: monitor for rhabdomyolysis, manage wounds (delayed primary closure or skin grafting at 48–72 hr).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in fasciotomy (irreversible necrosis after 6–12 hr). Elevation above heart level (may worsen ischemia). Circumferential dressings/casts (remove immediately if suspected). Regional anesthesia may mask symptoms — maintain high clinical suspicion. | Delay in fasciotomy (irreversible necrosis after 6–12 hr). Elevation above heart level. Circumferential dressings/casts.

treatments.notes: delay worsens outcomes
monitor UOP, CK, electrolytes

diseases.name: Pelvic / Open Fracture Management
diseases.management: Pelvic fractures: hemodynamically unstable pelvic fractures require pelvic binder or sheet for temporary stabilization, massive transfusion protocol, and angioembolization or preperitoneal packing for hemorrhage control. CT angiography identifies arterial bleeding.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in pelvic stabilization. Nonselective embolization when selective is possible (higher complication rate). Definitive internal fixation before physiological stability achieved.

treatments.notes: use when hemodynamically unstable pelvic fracture

treatments.route: when angiography unavailable or hemodynamically unstable
for unstable fractures in physiologically compromised patients

treatments.notes: high VTE risk

diseases.name: Intracerebral Hemorrhage
diseases.management: ICH management follows a "bundle of care" approach: simultaneous anticoagulation reversal, blood pressure management, and stroke unit protocols within 6 hours of onset. BP target: SBP 130–140 mmHg (avoid 130 mmHg — potentially harmful); initiate treatment ASAP with smooth, sustained control. Anticoagulation reversal: 4-factor PCC + IV vitamin K for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors (note: andexanet alfa was withdrawn from US market). Platelet transfusion is potentially harmful outside of emergency surgery. Cerebellar hemorrhage >15 mL or with neurological deterioration/brainstem compression/hydrocephalus → immediate surgical evacuation ± EVD. Minimally invasive approaches for supratentorial ICH reduce mortality but functional outcome data are neutral. DVT prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression; pharmacologic prophylaxis can start 24–48 hr after hemorrhage stability.
diseases.notes: Avoid: SBP 130 mmHg (potentially harmful). Platelet transfusion (unless emergency surgery or severe thrombocytopenia — worsens outcomes). Prophylactic antiseizure medications (no benefit for long-term seizure control). Corticosteroids (no benefit). Continuous hyperosmolar therapy (no benefit).

treatments.dosage: 5 mg/hr, titrate by 2.5 mg/hr q5–15 min (max 15 mg/hr)
treatments.notes: smooth, sustained control

treatments.dosage: 10–20 mg IV bolus q10–20 min or infusion 2–8 mg/min

treatments.notes: preferred over FFP; give with IV vitamin K 10 mg
sustains reversal

diseases.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
diseases.management: Aneurysmal SAH is a neurosurgical emergency with ~50% mortality. Diagnosis: non-contrast CT (sensitivity ~98% within 6 hr); if negative and >6 hr from onset, LP or MRI. Early aneurysm treatment (within 24 hr) via endovascular coiling (preferred when feasible) or surgical clipping to prevent rebleeding. SBP 140 mmHg until aneurysm secured. Nimodipine 60 mg q4h × 21 days for prevention of delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI). Maintain euvolemia (avoid hypovolemia and hypervolemia). Monitor for hydrocephalus (EVD if needed), vasospasm (transcranial Doppler), and DCI. Seizure prophylaxis is controversial; short-term use may be considered.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Hypovolemia (worsens DCI). Hypotension. Delay in aneurysm treatment (rebleeding risk highest in first 24 hr). Long-term antiepileptic prophylaxis (no proven benefit).

treatments.notes: only proven agent to improve outcomes in SAH

diseases.name: Delirium
diseases.management: Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention and cognition. Diagnosis: CAM (Confusion Assessment Method), 4AT, or 3D-CAM. Primary prevention with multicomponent nonpharmacologic approaches is the cornerstone: reorientation, early mobilization, therapeutic activities, hydration, nutrition, sleep hygiene, hearing/vision adaptations, medication review. Treatment priorities: (1) identify and treat underlying cause (infection, metabolic derangement, medications, pain, urinary retention, constipation), (2) nonpharmacologic interventions, (3) pharmacologic therapy only for severe agitation posing safety risk. No FDA-approved drugs for delirium. Antipsychotics (haloperidol, quetiapine) lack strong efficacy evidence and carry risk of adverse events; use only when nonpharmacologic measures fail and patient is a danger to self/others. Benzodiazepines reserved for alcohol/sedative withdrawal delirium only — worsen delirium from other causes.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines (except alcohol/sedative withdrawal — worsen delirium). Anticholinergic medications (precipitate/worsen delirium). Physical restraints (worsen agitation, increase injury). Cholinesterase inhibitors (no benefit, potential harm).

treatments.dosage: reorientation, mobilization, sleep hygiene, sensory aids, hydration, medication review
treatments.notes: cornerstone of prevention and treatment
use only when nonpharmacologic measures fail and safety risk
less EPS than haloperidol; more sedating

diseases.name: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
diseases.management: Immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy; ~100,000 new cases/year worldwide. Preceded by infection (most commonly Campylobacter jejuni). Progressive ascending weakness over up to 4 weeks. Diagnosis: clinical + nerve conduction studies (polyradiculoneuropathy) + CSF (albuminocytological dissociation, though both may be normal early). Close monitoring for respiratory failure (serial FVC — intubate if FVC 30%), bulbar weakness, and autonomic dysfunction. IVIG and plasma exchange are equally effective; initiate within 2 weeks of onset in patients unable to walk independently. IVIG preferred in autonomic instability and children. Supportive care: DVT prophylaxis, enteral nutrition if bulbar weakness, early physiotherapy, pain management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroids alone (not effective as monotherapy). Combining IVIG + PE sequentially (no added benefit over either alone). Delay in immunotherapy beyond 2 weeks of onset.

treatments.notes: preferred over PE in autonomic instability; equally effective

treatments.dosage: 5 sessions (50 mL/kg per session) over 2 weeks
treatments.notes: avoid in autonomic instability (fluid shifts)

diseases.name: Spinal Cord Compression / Cauda Equina
diseases.management: Spinal cord compression: emergent MRI (or CT myelogram if MRI unavailable). Metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC): dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus then 16 mg/day in divided doses; radiotherapy (most common treatment); surgery for spinal instability, rapidly progressing deficits, or single-level disease with good performance status and >3 months expected survival. Cauda equina syndrome (CES): presents with bilateral sciatica, saddle anesthesia, urinary retention (most specific), decreased anal tone. Emergent MRI → urgent surgical decompression. Timing of surgery debated but earlier intervention (ideally 24–48 hr) portends greater chance of neurologic recovery.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in MRI when CES suspected. Delay in surgical decompression for CES. Steroids in non-metastatic CES (no proven benefit).

treatments.dosage: 10 mg IV bolus then 4 mg q6h (16 mg/day)
treatments.notes: reduces edema and cord compression

treatments.dosage: various fractionation schemes (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions)
treatments.notes: most common treatment for MSCC
earlier intervention improves neurologic recovery

treatments.frequency: for spinal instability, single-level disease, good performance status

diseases.name: Migraine
diseases.management: Migraine therapy consists of acute treatment to relieve attacks and preventive treatment to reduce frequency/severity. Acute treatment: triptans and NSAIDs are first-line for moderate-severe attacks (high SOE); combination of triptan + NSAID (e.g., sumatriptan + naproxen) has greatest net benefit. Acetaminophen for mild/non-incapacitating attacks. Newer options: gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant, zavegepant) and ditans (lasmiditan) — nonvasoconstrictive, useful when triptans contraindicated. Preventive therapy indicated when ≥6 headache days/month, ≥4 with impairment, or ≥3 with severe impairment. Oral preventives: beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol), topiramate, valproate, amitriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan. Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) for patients failing ≥2 oral preventives. OnabotulinumtoxinA for chronic migraine only.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids and barbiturate combinations (dependency, medication overuse). Triptans/ergots in cardiovascular disease (vasoconstriction). Medication overuse (triptans >10 days/month, NSAIDs >15 days/month).

treatments.dosage: 50–100 mg oral; 6 mg SC; 20 mg nasal
treatments.notes: most effective when taken early; avoid in cardiovascular disease
can combine with triptan for greatest benefit

treatments.frequency: at onset, may repeat ×1 after 2 hr

diseases.name: Dementia (Alzheimer type)
diseases.management: Alzheimer disease (AD) is treated with cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) for mild-to-severe stages and memantine (NMDA receptor antagonist) for moderate-to-severe stages. Combination donepezil + memantine shows largest benefit (effect size 0.76) for moderate-to-severe AD. Cholinesterase inhibitors should not be stopped just because dementia severity increases. Nonpharmacologic interventions: cognitive stimulation, physical exercise, caregiver support, structured activities. Neuropsychiatric symptoms: SSRIs for depression/anxiety; antipsychotics with caution (increased stroke/death risk in elderly with dementia — use lowest dose, shortest duration). Discontinue anticholinergic and sedating medications.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticholinergic medications (worsen cognition — tricyclics, oxybutynin, diphenhydramine). Benzodiazepines and Z-drugs (sedation, falls, worsen cognition). Chronic antipsychotics (increased stroke/death risk — use lowest dose, shortest duration). Cholinesterase inhibitors in MCI (not effective).

treatments.dosage: 5 mg daily × 6 weeks then 10 mg daily (23 mg for moderate-severe)
treatments.notes: most commonly used; GI side effects

treatments.dosage: patch 4.6 mg/24h → 9.5–13.3 mg/24h; or capsule 1.5 mg BID → 6 mg BID
treatments.notes: patch preferred for adherence and fewer GI effects
most effective at ≥24 mg

treatments.dosage: 5 mg daily → 10 mg BID (or ER 28 mg daily)
treatments.notes: can combine with donepezil
largest benefit in moderate-severe AD

diseases.name: Hyponatremia
diseases.management: Affects ~5% of adults and ~35% of hospitalized patients. Management depends on symptoms, chronicity, and volume status. Severe symptoms (seizures, somnolence, coma): emergency treatment with 3% hypertonic saline boluses (100–150 mL over 10–20 min, repeat 2–3× until symptoms improve); goal increase 4–6 mEq/L within 1–2 hr. Correction limits: ≤10 mEq/L in first 24 hr, ≤18 mEq/L in 48 hr; for high-risk patients (malnutrition, alcoholism, hypokalemia, Na 105), limit to ≤8 mEq/L per 24 hr. Overcorrection → risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS). If overcorrected: stop hypertonic saline, give free water (D5W 3 mL/kg/hr) or desmopressin 2 mcg IV q8h. Chronic management by etiology: hypovolemic → NS; euvolemic (SIADH) → fluid restriction, urea, or vaptans; hypervolemic → fluid/sodium restriction, diuretics.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Overcorrection >10 mEq/L in 24 hr (ODS risk). Tolvaptan in liver disease (hepatotoxicity). NS in SIADH (may worsen hyponatremia). Rapid correction in chronic hyponatremia.

treatments.notes: goal ↑Na 4–6 mEq/L in 1–2 hr
first-line for euvolemic hyponatremia
use when fluid restriction fails
use when Na correction exceeds safe limits
use when fluid restriction fails or tolvaptan contraindicated

diseases.name: Hypernatremia
diseases.management: Hypernatremia (>145 mEq/L) occurs in 1–2% of ED patients without CKD and is associated with increased mortality. Most commonly results from decreased water intake or excess water loss. First-line treatment: address underlying cause and correct free water deficit using the free water deficit formula. Administer oral or enteral free water whenever possible; if IV needed, use hypotonic fluids (D5W, 0.45% NaCl, or 0.2% NaCl). In adults, correction rate of 12 mEq/L per 24 hours is advised; failure to correct within 72 hours is associated with worsening mortality. In infants, correct at 0.5 mEq/L/hr (12 mEq/L in 24 hr) to avoid seizures. Isotonic saline should be avoided as it can worsen hypernatremia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Isotonic saline (can worsen hypernatremia). Overcorrection >12 mEq/L/24 hr (risk of cerebral edema, especially in pediatric patients). Delay in correction (associated with increased mortality).

treatments.dosage: calculated free water deficit administered over 24–48 hr
treatments.notes: preferred route

treatments.dosage: rate based on free water deficit calculation
treatments.notes: use when oral route not feasible

treatments.dosage: rate based on free water deficit
treatments.notes: less hypotonic than D5W

treatments.dosage: 1–2 mcg IV/SC q12h or 10–20 mcg intranasal BID
treatments.notes: use when central DI is the cause

diseases.name: Hypomagnesemia
diseases.management: Hypomagnesemia (serum Mg 0.7 mmol/L or 1.7 mg/dL) is common in hospitalized patients. Common causes: alcoholism, diuretics (thiazides, loops), PPIs, cisplatin, amphotericin B, GI losses. Mild cases: oral magnesium supplements (organic salts — citrate, glycinate, gluconate — are better absorbed than oxide). Symptomatic or severe hypomagnesemia (tetany, seizures, arrhythmias, refractory hypokalemia/hypocalcemia): IV magnesium sulfate 8–12 g in first 24 hr, then 4–6 g/day for 3–4 days to replete stores; max infusion rate 2 g/hr. Renal insufficiency: reduce dose by 25–50%. Refractory cases: amiloride (potassium-sparing diuretic that enhances Mg reabsorption), SGLT2 inhibitors.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Magnesium oxide (poorly absorbed). Rapid IV infusion >2 g/hr (risk of hypotension, flushing). Failure to replete Mg before correcting refractory hypokalemia or hypocalcemia.

treatments.dosage: 8–12 g in first 24 hr (1–2 g/hr initially), then 4–6 g/day × 3–4 days
treatments.notes: max rate 2 g/hr; reduce dose 25–50% in renal impairment

treatments.dosage: 200–400 mg elemental Mg daily in divided doses
treatments.notes: diarrhea is common side effect; organic salts better absorbed
use when renal Mg wasting (e.g., diuretic-induced, Gitelman)

diseases.name: Hypophosphatemia
diseases.specialty: Nephrology / Hospital Medicine / Critical Care / Endocrinology
diseases.management: Hypophosphatemia is classified as mild (2–2.5 mg/dL), moderate (1–1.9 mg/dL), or severe (1 mg/dL). Common in refeeding syndrome, alcoholism, DKA, post-hepatectomy, and ICU patients. Severe symptomatic hypophosphatemia: IV sodium phosphate or potassium phosphate (0.08–0.64 mmol/kg over 6–12 hr depending on severity). Mild/moderate asymptomatic: oral phosphate supplementation (Neutra-Phos or K-Phos 250–500 mg elemental phosphorus 2–3× daily) with active vitamin D for chronic cases. Monitor calcium (IV phosphate can cause hypocalcemia) and potassium (if using potassium phosphate).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Rapid IV phosphate infusion (hypocalcemia, soft tissue calcification). Calcium-containing phosphate binders in acute setting. Failure to identify underlying cause (refeeding, DKA, etc.).

treatments.medication: IV sodium phosphate or potassium phosphate
treatments.notes: monitor calcium and potassium; risk of hypocalcemia
diarrhea is dose-limiting side effect
use when chronic renal phosphate wasting or vitamin D-dependent causes

diseases.name: Hypoglycemia
diseases.management: Hypoglycemia is defined as blood glucose 70 mg/dL (alert value) with clinically important hypoglycemia at 54 mg/dL. Conscious patients: 15 g fast-acting oral glucose (glucose tablets preferred), recheck in 15 min, repeat if still 70 mg/dL ("rule of 15"). Patients on acarbose should use pure glucose only (acarbose inhibits sucrose breakdown). Unconscious/unable to swallow: glucagon (SC, IM, or intranasal) or IV dextrose (25 g D50). Preparations not requiring reconstitution are preferred (nasal glucagon, dasiglucagon auto-injector). After recovery, give 20 g long-acting carbohydrate. Reevaluate treatment plan after any level 2 or 3 hypoglycemia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Foods high in fat/protein for initial treatment (delay glucose absorption). Sucrose-containing foods in patients on acarbose. Failure to reevaluate treatment plan after level 2/3 hypoglycemia.

treatments.notes: pure glucose preferred; avoid fat/protein initially

treatments.dosage: 25 g (50 mL of D50) IV push
treatments.notes: for healthcare settings

treatments.dosage: 3 mg one spray in one nostril
treatments.notes: no reconstitution needed; preferred for lay rescuers
traditional formulation requires reconstitution
stable liquid formulation; no reconstitution

diseases.name: Neutropenic Fever
diseases.management: Febrile neutropenia is defined as single oral temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) or ≥38.0°C sustained over 1 hour with ANC 500/μL. Assessment should occur within 15 minutes of triage; first dose of empiric antibiotics within 1 hour. Risk stratification using MASCC score (≥21 = low risk) or CISNE guides inpatient vs. outpatient management. Empiric monotherapy with antipseudomonal beta-lactam is recommended (Category 1): cefepime, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam. Vancomycin is NOT part of standard initial regimen — add only for specific indications (catheter infection, skin/soft tissue, pneumonia, hemodynamic instability, MRSA risk). Low-risk patients may receive oral outpatient therapy: ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate (not if on fluoroquinolone prophylaxis). Antifungal therapy for persistent fever >4–7 days in high-risk patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vancomycin as standard initial empiric therapy (not recommended). Delay in first antibiotic dose beyond 1 hour. Oral regimen if on fluoroquinolone prophylaxis.

treatments.duration: until ANC recovery and afebrile ≥48 hr
treatments.notes: Category 1; adjust for renal function
Category 1; preferred for suspected ESBL
Category 1

treatments.dosage: cipro 750 mg q12h + amox-clav 875 mg q12h
treatments.notes: Category 1; not if on FQ prophylaxis
add for catheter infection, skin/soft tissue, MRSA risk, hemodynamic instability

diseases.name: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
diseases.management: DIC is always secondary to an underlying condition (sepsis, malignancy, trauma, obstetric emergencies). Diagnosis via ISTH DIC scoring system (platelet count, fibrin markers, PT, fibrinogen). Cornerstone of treatment: treat the underlying cause. Transfusion of platelets and plasma reserved for patients who are actively bleeding (not prophylactically in non-bleeding patients). Targets: platelets >50,000/μL, PT/aPTT 150 mg/dL (>200 mg/dL in obstetric DIC). Prophylactic-dose heparin (UFH or LMWH) recommended in critically ill non-bleeding patients for VTE prevention. Therapeutic heparin considered when thrombosis predominates. Antifibrinolytics (TXA) generally avoided except in severe hyperfibrinolytic states (leukemia, trauma).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine platelet/plasma transfusion in non-bleeding patients. Antifibrinolytics (except hyperfibrinolytic states). Therapeutic heparin in patients with high bleeding risk.

treatments.notes: not for non-bleeding patients
for active bleeding with prolonged coagulation times
preferred fibrinogen source
moderate-to-high quality evidence

diseases.name: Tumor Lysis Syndrome
diseases.management: TLS is an oncologic emergency from rapid tumor cell lysis releasing K⁺, PO₄, uric acid, and causing hypocalcemia. Laboratory TLS: ≥2 metabolic abnormalities in 24 hr. Clinical TLS: lab TLS + renal failure, seizures, arrhythmias, or death. Prevention is key: risk-stratify patients (high risk: BL, high WBC, LDH >2× ULN, bulky disease, renal impairment). Cornerstone: aggressive IV hydration (1.5–2× maintenance without K⁺), frequent electrolyte monitoring (q4–6h). Hyperuricemia prevention: low risk → allopurinol 300 mg/day (start 2–3 days before chemo); high risk → rasburicase 3–6 mg fixed dose (or 0.1–0.2 mg/kg) IV before chemo. Rasburicase contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (hemolysis, methemoglobinemia). Urine alkalinization no longer recommended. Hyperkalemia: standard algorithms. Hyperphosphatemia: phosphate binders (sevelamer; avoid calcium carbonate). Hypocalcemia: treat only if symptomatic. Hemodialysis/CRRT for refractory electrolyte abnormalities or worsening renal function.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Urine alkalinization (risk of calcium phosphate precipitation). Calcium carbonate as phosphate binder (worsens calcium phosphate deposition). Calcium supplementation for asymptomatic hypocalcemia. Rasburicase in G6PD deficiency.

treatments.notes: contraindicated in G6PD deficiency; place blood samples on ice

treatments.duration: start 2–3 days before chemo, continue 10–14 days
treatments.notes: switch to rasburicase if TLS develops
target UOP ≥2 mL/kg/hr
do not use calcium carbonate (risk of calcium phosphate precipitation)

diseases.name: Transfusion Reactions
diseases.management: Universal initial management for all transfusion reactions: stop the transfusion, keep IV line open with NS, recheck blood product labeling and patient ID, send blood product and patient samples to blood bank, supportive care. Type-specific management: (1) FNHTR: antipyretics, supportive care; may restart if symptoms resolve. (2) Allergic (mild): antihistamine ± corticosteroid; may restart if urticaria resolves. (3) Anaphylactic: IM epinephrine, antihistamines (H1+H2), bronchodilator, corticosteroid; do NOT restart. (4) Acute hemolytic: vigorous IV NS hydration (UOP >0.5–1 mL/kg/hr), monitor for DIC, renal failure; ICU admission. (5) TRALI: supportive with O₂, mechanical ventilation if needed, restrictive fluids. (6) TACO: diuretics, O₂, slow future transfusions. (7) Septic: broad-spectrum antibiotics, blood cultures from patient and product.
