diseases.notes: Avoid: Restarting transfusion after anaphylactic or acute hemolytic reaction. Mannitol in acute hemolytic reaction (not evidence-based; caution in anemia). Diuretics in TRALI (restrictive fluid strategy preferred).

treatments.dosage: stop blood product, maintain IV access with NS

treatments.notes: for anaphylaxis only
for urticaria/mild allergic reactions

treatments.dosage: vigorous hydration to maintain UOP >0.5–1 mL/kg/hr
treatments.notes: ICU admission; monitor for DIC
slow future transfusions

diseases.name: B12 / Folate Deficiency Anemia
diseases.management: Megaloblastic anemia from B12 or folate deficiency presents with macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL), hypersegmented neutrophils, and pancytopenia in severe cases. B12 deficiency may cause neurologic manifestations (subacute combined degeneration). If dual deficiency, prioritize B12 repletion first to prevent precipitating/worsening neurologic damage. B12 replacement: IM hydroxocobalamin 1 mg × 5–6 doses over 3 weeks, then 1 mg IM every 3 months for life (for pernicious anemia or irreversible causes); high-dose oral B12 (1000–2000 mcg/day) is effective for patients with intact absorption. Folate replacement: folic acid 5 mg daily × 4 months.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Folic acid alone in B12 deficiency — may correct anemia but worsen/precipitate subacute combined degeneration of the cord. IV cyanocobalamin (most excreted in urine).

treatments.dosage: 1 mg IM × 5–6 doses over 3 weeks, then 1 mg IM every 3 months
treatments.notes: check reticulocyte response at 7–10 days
effective when absorption intact; not for pernicious anemia unless high-dose
treat underlying cause

diseases.name: Hemolytic Anemia
diseases.management: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is the most common acquired hemolytic anemia. Warm AIHA (60–70% of cases): IgG-mediated, reacts at body temperature. Cold agglutinin disease (20–25%): IgM-mediated, complement activation. Warm AIHA first-line: glucocorticoids (prednisone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day × 1–3 weeks then taper); ~75% respond within 3 weeks but relapse is common (15–40%). Second-line: rituximab (preferred over splenectomy in current practice). Splenectomy reserved for later lines. Refractory: cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate, cyclosporine, or newer agents (fostamatinib, rilzabrutinib). Cold agglutinin disease: treat underlying B-cell clone (rituximab-based); avoid cold exposure; complement inhibitors emerging. Supportive: folic acid, transfusion when clinically significant anemia, prophylactic anticoagulation for severe hemolysis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Transfusion avoidance is NOT appropriate when anemia is clinically significant (transfuse "least incompatible" units if needed). Cold exposure in cold agglutinin disease.

treatments.notes: ~75% response; relapse common
preferred over splenectomy
response 60–75%; risk of infection, thrombosis
compensate for increased erythropoiesis

diseases.name: Osteoarthritis
diseases.management: OA management follows a stepwise, multimodal approach. Core treatments (strong recommendations per ACR/AF 2019): exercise, weight loss (if overweight/obese), self-efficacy/self-management programs. Pharmacologic: topical NSAIDs (strong recommendation for knee OA, first-line pharmacotherapy), oral NSAIDs (strong recommendation for hand/knee/hip), intra-articular glucocorticoid injections (strong for knee). Conditional recommendations: acetaminophen, duloxetine, tramadol, topical capsaicin (knee), acupuncture. Surgical: total joint arthroplasty for end-stage disease refractory to conservative management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid (strongly recommended against by OARSI for hip; inconsistent for knee). Arthroscopy for OA (consistently recommended against). Opioids (high risk, limited benefit).

treatments.notes: strong recommendation; supervised programs enhance effectiveness
strong recommendation for knee/hip OA

treatments.dosage: 1% gel applied QID or 1.5% solution BID
treatments.notes: strong recommendation for knee OA; fewer systemic effects than oral NSAIDs
strong recommendation; use gastroprotection; avoid in CKD, CVD risk
strong recommendation for knee; conditional for hip
conditional recommendation; useful for central sensitization component

diseases.name: Pseudogout (CPPD)
diseases.management: CPPD disease is caused by calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition. No disease-modifying therapy exists (unlike gout). Acute CPP crystal arthritis: intra-articular glucocorticoids are first-line for amenable joints; oral colchicine (0.6–1.2 mg/day, optional 1.2 mg loading) or NSAIDs with gastroprotection as alternatives; systemic glucocorticoids for elderly or those with contraindications to colchicine/NSAIDs. Chronic CPP arthritis: trial-and-error approach — daily low-dose colchicine (0.6–1.2 mg) for flare prophylaxis, NSAIDs, low-dose prednisone (5–10 mg/day), hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate (5–15 mg/week). IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) for refractory acute flares; tocilizumab for refractory chronic disease. Screen for underlying metabolic conditions (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, hypophosphatasia) especially if age 60.
diseases.notes: Avoid: No disease-modifying therapy exists for CPPD (unlike urate-lowering therapy in gout). Failure to screen for metabolic causes in patients 60 years.

treatments.notes: first-line for amenable joints

treatments.dosage: acute: 1.2 mg load then 0.6 mg 1 hr later; prophylaxis: 0.6–1.2 mg daily
treatments.notes: avoid in significant renal/hepatic impairment
use with gastroprotection

treatments.dosage: acute: 30–40 mg/day taper over 7–10 days; chronic: 5–10 mg/day
treatments.notes: for elderly or NSAID/colchicine contraindications
some data support use
for refractory acute flares

diseases.name: Giant Cell Arteritis / Polymyalgia Rheumatica
diseases.management: GCA is a large-vessel vasculitis affecting patients >50 years; PMR frequently coexists. GCA requires urgent treatment to prevent irreversible vision loss. High-dose glucocorticoids are the cornerstone: prednisone 40–60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA; IV methylprednisolone 250–1000 mg/day × 3 days for visual symptoms or impending vision loss, followed by oral prednisone. Slow taper over 12–18 months aiming for 0–5 mg/day by 12 months. Tocilizumab (SC 162 mg weekly or IV 8 mg/kg monthly) is FDA-approved as a glucocorticoid-sparing agent for GCA — sustained remission at 52 weeks: 56% with tocilizumab vs. 18% with placebo. Upadacitinib (JAK inhibitor) is also now FDA-approved for GCA. Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week is an alternative steroid-sparing agent when tocilizumab is unavailable or contraindicated. PMR alone: prednisone 12.5–25 mg/day with slow taper over 12–18 months; tocilizumab for relapsing PMR.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in glucocorticoid initiation for suspected GCA (risk of permanent vision loss). Waiting for temporal artery biopsy before starting treatment. Rapid steroid taper (high relapse rate).

treatments.notes: initiate immediately if GCA suspected; do not wait for biopsy
for impending or established vision loss

treatments.dosage: 162 mg SC weekly or 8 mg/kg IV monthly
treatments.notes: FDA-approved; sustained remission 56% vs. 18% placebo at 52 weeks
when tocilizumab unavailable or contraindicated

diseases.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
diseases.management: AAV (GPA, MPA, EGPA) requires remission induction followed by maintenance therapy. Induction: glucocorticoids + rituximab (preferred) or cyclophosphamide. Rituximab is preferred for relapsing disease and PR3-ANCA; cyclophosphamide preferred for severe renal failure. Reduced-dose glucocorticoid regimens are now supported (LoVAS trial). Avacopan (oral C5a receptor antagonist, 30 mg BID) was FDA-approved as adjunctive therapy to reduce glucocorticoid exposure; however, the FDA has proposed withdrawal of approval due to data integrity concerns with the ADVOCATE trial, and the NEJM retracted the pivotal paper in June 2026. Maintenance: rituximab (preferred over azathioprine per MAINRITSAN trial — relapse-free survival 100% vs. 63% at 28 months). Duration of maintenance: guided by relapse risk factors (PR3-ANCA, GPA diagnosis, prior relapse favor prolonged therapy).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prolonged high-dose glucocorticoids (toxicity). Cyclophosphamide for maintenance (toxicity). Premature discontinuation of maintenance in high-risk patients.

treatments.dosage: 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 doses or 1000 mg × 2 doses 2 weeks apart
treatments.notes: preferred for relapsing disease and PR3-ANCA

treatments.dosage: IV 15 mg/kg q2–3 weeks × 3–6 months or oral 2 mg/kg/day × 3–4 months
treatments.notes: preferred for severe renal failure

treatments.dosage: 1 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg), taper to ≤7.5 mg by 5 months

treatments.notes: superior to azathioprine (MAINRITSAN)
when rituximab unavailable
NOTE: FDA has proposed withdrawal of approval due to data integrity concerns; NEJM retracted ADVOCATE paper June 2026

diseases.name: Digoxin Toxicity
diseases.management: Cardiac glycoside poisoning causes GI symptoms, confusion, hyperkalemia, and cardiac conduction abnormalities (AV block, VT, VF, asystole). Serum digoxin levels >2 mcg/L with hyperkalemia or arrhythmias indicate high risk of death. Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Fab) are the definitive treatment — clinical response rates 50–90% within 30–45 minutes. Each 40 mg vial of digoxin Fab binds ~0.5 mg of digoxin; dosing based on estimated amount ingested or empiric 10–20 vials for life-threatening toxicity. Atropine for symptomatic bradycardia as temporizing measure. Correct hyperkalemia (avoid calcium — may worsen toxicity). Dialysis is NOT effective for digoxin removal (large volume of distribution).
diseases.notes: Avoid: IV calcium for hyperkalemia (may worsen cardiac toxicity — "stone heart" concern). Dialysis (digoxin not dialyzable). Electrical cardioversion (may precipitate refractory VF — use only as last resort).

treatments.dosage: dose based on estimated digoxin body load (each 40 mg vial binds 0.5 mg digoxin); empiric 10–20 vials for life-threatening toxicity
treatments.notes: response in 30–45 min; monitor potassium
variable effectiveness in digoxin toxicity
if no contraindications

diseases.name: Lithium Toxicity
diseases.management: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (therapeutic 0.8–1.2 mEq/L; toxic ≥1.5 mEq/L). At levels >3 mEq/L, patients may progress to seizures, coma, and irreversible brain damage. No specific antidote exists. Management: discontinue lithium, discontinue drugs impairing lithium excretion (NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides), aggressive IV NS hydration to restore volume and enhance renal clearance. Hemodialysis is the treatment of choice for severe toxicity — lithium is highly dialyzable. EXTRIP workgroup recommends HD when: Li >4.0 mEq/L with impaired renal function, decreased consciousness, seizures, or life-threatening dysrhythmias (regardless of level); or Li >5.0 mEq/L; or expected time to reduce Li 1.0 mEq/L exceeds 36 hours. Continue HD for ≥6 hours or until Li 1.0 mEq/L and clinical improvement. Monitor for rebound in serum lithium after HD (redistribution from tissues).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Activated charcoal (does not adsorb lithium). NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics (impair lithium excretion). Premature discontinuation of HD (rebound lithium levels from tissue redistribution).

treatments.notes: restore volume; enhance renal clearance

treatments.dosage: intermittent HD preferred; CRRT acceptable alternative
treatments.frequency: continue ≥6 hr or until Li 4.0 with renal impairment, altered consciousness, seizures, dysrhythmias; or Li >5.0

treatments.duration: for large ingestions of sustained-release formulations
treatments.notes: activated charcoal does NOT adsorb lithium

diseases.name: Serotonin Syndrome
diseases.management: Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening toxidrome from excess serotonergic activity. Diagnosis is clinical using the Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria: requires a serotonergic agent + one of: spontaneous clonus; inducible clonus with agitation or diaphoresis; ocular clonus with agitation or diaphoresis; tremor and hyperreflexia; or hypertonia + temperature >38°C + ocular or inducible clonus. Management: (1) discontinue all serotonergic agents, (2) supportive care, (3) benzodiazepines for agitation (essential regardless of severity), (4) cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist) for moderate-severe cases, (5) active cooling for hyperthermia >41.1°C with neuromuscular paralysis and intubation. Most cases resolve within 24 hours after drug discontinuation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Physical restraints (worsen lactic acidosis and hyperthermia). Antipyretics alone (hyperthermia is from muscular activity, not hypothalamic dysregulation). Bromocriptine (may worsen serotonin syndrome). Dantrolene (not effective — mechanism is muscular hyperactivity, not malignant hyperthermia).

treatments.dosage: diazepam 5–10 mg IV or lorazepam 1–2 mg IV
treatments.notes: improves survival in animal models

treatments.dosage: 12 mg initial dose, then 2 mg q2h until response (max 32 mg/24 hr)
treatments.notes: binds 85–95% of serotonin receptors at 12–32 mg/day

diseases.name: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
diseases.management: NMS is a rare but life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to dopamine-blocking agents (antipsychotics). Characterized by hyperthermia, muscular rigidity ("lead pipe"), autonomic instability, and altered mental status. Mortality ~10% despite advances. CK often >10,000 U/L. Management: (1) immediately discontinue offending agent, (2) aggressive supportive care (IV fluids targeting UOP 200–300 mL/hr for rhabdomyolysis, cooling, electrolyte correction), (3) lorazepam for mild rigidity, (4) dantrolene for persistent/worsening rigidity and hyperthermia, (5) bromocriptine or amantadine as dopamine agonist alternatives. ECT may be considered for refractory cases. Evidence for pharmacologic interventions is limited — no RCTs exist; treatment is based on expert consensus.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Restarting the offending antipsychotic (wait ≥2 weeks after resolution). Succinylcholine (hyperkalemia risk from rhabdomyolysis). Antipyretics alone (ineffective — hyperthermia is from muscular rigidity).

treatments.dosage: 1–2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat q5–10 min PRN (max 10 mg/kg/day)
treatments.notes: monitor LFTs; risk of hepatotoxicity
displaces antipsychotic from dopamine receptors
fewer short-term side effects

treatments.dosage: for NMS unresponsive to pharmacologic therapy

diseases.name: Toxic Alcohols (methanol / ethylene glycol)
diseases.management: Methanol and ethylene glycol are metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to toxic metabolites (formic acid and glycolic/oxalic acid, respectively). Toxicity causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis, osmolal gap, and end-organ damage (methanol → optic nerve/CNS; ethylene glycol → renal failure). Treatment: (1) fomepizole (ADH inhibitor, affinity 8000× that of ethanol) is the preferred antidote — FDA-approved for both; (2) IV sodium bicarbonate for acidosis; (3) hemodialysis for severe acidosis, levels >50 mg/dL, deteriorating vital signs, visual symptoms (methanol), or AKI (ethylene glycol). Fomepizole dosing: 15 mg/kg loading, then 10 mg/kg q12h × 4 doses, then 15 mg/kg q12h; increase to q4h during HD. IV ethanol (target 100 mg/dL) is an alternative when fomepizole unavailable. Folate 50 mg IV q4h for methanol (enhances formate metabolism); thiamine + pyridoxine for ethylene glycol.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in antidotal therapy. Activated charcoal (poorly adsorbs toxic alcohols). Reliance on osmolal gap alone (may be normal if alcohol already metabolized).

treatments.dosage: 15 mg/kg IV load, then 10 mg/kg q12h × 4 doses, then 15 mg/kg q12h; q4h during HD
treatments.duration: until levels 20 mg/dL and asymptomatic with normal pH
treatments.notes: preferred over ethanol

treatments.frequency: until levels 50 mg/dL, severe acidosis, visual symptoms, AKI, deteriorating vitals

treatments.notes: increases ionization and urinary excretion of formate
requires ICU monitoring; blunts sensorium

diseases.name: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
diseases.management: CO binds hemoglobin with 200–250× greater affinity than oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). Symptoms are nonspecific: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, loss of consciousness. Diagnosis: COHb level (note: pulse oximetry is unreliable). Treatment: 100% high-flow oxygen via NRB mask is the most important initial treatment — reduces COHb half-life from ~5 hr (room air) to ~60–90 min. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO₂) at 2.5–3.0 ATA further reduces half-life to ~20–30 min. ACEP 2025 clinical policy: HBO₂ may reduce delayed neurological sequelae (DNS) but evidence remains heterogeneous; no definitive mortality benefit demonstrated. HBO₂ should be critically considered and ideally initiated within 6 hours for: loss of consciousness, neurologic deficits, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy, COHb >25% (>15% in pregnancy). Obtain ECG and cardiac biomarkers in severe cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Reliance on pulse oximetry (falsely normal in CO poisoning). Delay in high-flow O₂. Premature discontinuation of O₂ based on COHb alone (tissue injury persists after COHb normalizes).

treatments.notes: most important initial treatment

treatments.duration: for LOC, neurologic deficits, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy, COHb >25%
treatments.notes: may reduce delayed neurological sequelae

diseases.name: Diabetic Foot Infection
diseases.specialty: Infectious Disease / Podiatry / Vascular Surgery / Endocrinology
diseases.management: Per IWGDF/IDSA 2023 guidelines, ~50% of diabetic foot ulcers are not infected — do not treat uninfected ulcers with antibiotics. Classify severity: mild (superficial, limited cellulitis 2 cm), severe (systemic signs, sepsis). Mild DFI: empiric oral antibiotics targeting gram-positive cocci only (dicloxacillin, cephalexin); add MRSA coverage based on risk factors. Moderate-severe: broader coverage including gram-negatives ± anaerobes; options include beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations, carbapenems, or fluoroquinolones. Duration: 1–2 weeks for soft tissue; extend to 3–4 weeks if slowly resolving; 6 weeks for osteomyelitis without resection, 3 weeks after minor amputation with positive bone margins. Wound care, offloading, vascular assessment, and glycemic control are essential adjuncts.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics for uninfected ulcers (no benefit; increases resistance). Empiric Pseudomonas coverage in temperate climates (uncommon). Tigecycline (inferior outcomes).

treatments.dosage: cephalexin 500 mg QID or dicloxacillin 500 mg QID
treatments.notes: targets gram-positive cocci

treatments.dosage: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID
treatments.notes: for MRSA risk factors
covers gram-positives + gram-negatives + anaerobes
broad-spectrum; add vancomycin if MRSA risk
group 1 carbapenem; no Pseudomonas coverage

diseases.name: Tetanus
diseases.specialty: Infectious Disease / Critical Care / Emergency Medicine
diseases.management: Tetanus is a medical emergency requiring ICU admission. Caused by Clostridium tetani neurotoxin (tetanospasmin). Management: (1) human tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) 500 IU IM to neutralize unbound toxin, (2) metronidazole 500 mg IV q6h × 10 days (preferred over penicillin — lower mortality), (3) wound debridement, (4) tetanus vaccination (full series regardless of prior status), (5) benzodiazepines for spasm control (diazepam or midazolam), (6) magnesium sulfate or labetalol for autonomic dysfunction, (7) mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Penicillin as sole antibiotic (GABA antagonist properties may worsen spasms; higher mortality than metronidazole). Loud noises, bright lights, physical contact (trigger spasms).

treatments.notes: neutralizes unbound toxin; equine antitoxin if unavailable
preferred over penicillin (lower mortality: 7% vs. 24%)

treatments.duration: full series regardless of prior immunization status

treatments.notes: sedation and muscle relaxation

treatments.dosage: IV infusion titrated to clinical effect
treatments.notes: also has muscle relaxant properties
for refractory spasms

diseases.name: Trichomoniasis
diseases.management: Trichomonas vaginalis is the most common non-viral STI. Diagnosis: NAATs are preferred (most sensitive); wet mount sensitivity only 51–65%. Per CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines: women should receive metronidazole 500 mg oral BID × 7 days (superior to single-dose 2 g in women — reduces positive test-of-cure by half); men should receive metronidazole 2 g oral single dose. Alternative: tinidazole 2 g oral single dose (longer half-life, higher tissue levels, fewer GI side effects). Persistent infections: higher doses (metronidazole or tinidazole 2 g daily × 7 days); send for CDC nitroimidazole resistance testing if repeated failure. Concurrent partner treatment recommended. Rescreening at 3 months. Annual screening recommended for women with HIV.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Metronidazole gel (does not reach therapeutic levels in urethra/perivaginal glands; less efficacious than oral). Alcohol during treatment and 24 hr after metronidazole / 72 hr after tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction).

treatments.notes: superior to single dose in women
preferred regimen for men
longer half-life; fewer GI side effects; more expensive
for treatment failure after initial regimen

diseases.name: Cryptococcal Meningitis / Invasive Fungal (HIV)
diseases.management: Cryptococcal meningitis is a leading cause of death in advanced HIV (CD4 100). Diagnosis: serum/CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg), CSF India ink, fungal culture. Treatment follows a three-phase approach: induction → consolidation → maintenance. In resource-available settings: induction with liposomal amphotericin B 3–4 mg/kg/day IV + flucytosine 25 mg/kg QID × 2 weeks (standard of care). Alternative (WHO-recommended for resource-limited settings): single dose liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg + flucytosine + fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 14 days (AMBITION-cm regimen). Consolidation: fluconazole 800 mg/day × 8 weeks. Maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg/day until immune reconstitution (CD4 >200 for ≥12 months on ART). Manage elevated ICP aggressively with serial therapeutic LPs (opening pressure >25 cm H₂O → drain to 20 cm H₂O or 50% reduction). Delay ART initiation 4–6 weeks after antifungal induction to reduce IRIS risk.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fluconazole monotherapy for induction (~50% mortality at 10 weeks). Immediate ART initiation (delay 4–6 weeks to reduce IRIS). Amphotericin B + fluconazole without flucytosine (inferior to amphotericin B + flucytosine).

treatments.dosage: L-AmB 3–4 mg/kg/day IV + flucytosine 25 mg/kg QID
treatments.notes: monitor renal function, electrolytes, CBC

treatments.medication: Single-dose liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine + fluconazole
treatments.dosage: L-AmB 10 mg/kg single dose + flucytosine 25 mg/kg QID + fluconazole 1200 mg/day
treatments.notes: AMBITION-cm regimen; WHO-recommended
after induction

treatments.duration: until CD4 >200 for ≥12 months on ART
treatments.notes: secondary prophylaxis

treatments.dosage: drain CSF to opening pressure 20 cm H₂O or 50% reduction
treatments.notes: critical for survival

diseases.name: HELLP Syndrome
diseases.management: HELLP (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of preeclampsia occurring in ~20% of preeclampsia with severe features. Diagnosis: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, platelets 2× ULN. Delivery is the definitive treatment — recommended at time of diagnosis after maternal stabilization, regardless of gestational age. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (4–6 g IV loading dose, then 1–2 g/hr maintenance). Antihypertensive therapy for BP ≥160/110 mmHg (IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or oral nifedipine). Antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity if 34 weeks. Platelet transfusion: for platelets 50,000 if cesarean section; 30,000 for vaginal delivery
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in delivery once diagnosis confirmed. Thrombopoietin receptor agonists (extreme caution — hypercoagulable state of pregnancy). Expectant management beyond 34 weeks.

treatments.duration: do not delay for corticosteroids in late preterm period

treatments.dosage: 4–6 g IV loading over 5 min, then 1–2 g/hr maintenance

treatments.duration: 34 weeks for fetal maturity; may improve platelet count but no mortality benefit

treatments.dosage: 20 mg IV bolus, then 40–80 mg q10 min (max 300 mg)

treatments.duration: less effective due to accelerated destruction

diseases.name: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
diseases.management: Hyperemesis gravidarum (HG) is severe protracted NVP with >5% weight loss, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalance; affects 0.3–3.6% of pregnancies. GDF15 hormone genetic variants implicated in pathogenesis. Stepwise pharmacologic approach per ACOG/AGA: (1) First-line: pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 10–25 mg q6–8h ± doxylamine 12.5 mg q6–8h (Level A evidence). (2) Second-line: metoclopramide or ondansetron (ondansetron case-by-case before 10 weeks due to possible cardiac defect risk). (3) Third-line: methylprednisolone 16 mg IV q8h × 3 days then taper over 2 weeks (last resort; avoid before 10 weeks — possible cleft palate risk). Thiamine 100 mg daily for ≥7 days to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy — must be given BEFORE dextrose infusion. IV hydration with electrolyte correction for patients unable to tolerate oral intake. Enteral nutrition (NG/ND tube) preferred over parenteral if medical therapy fails.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dextrose infusion before thiamine replacement (Wernicke encephalopathy risk). PICC lines routinely (significant complications). Antithyroid drugs for gestational transient thyrotoxicosis associated with HG.

treatments.dosage: pyridoxine 10–25 mg q6–8h ± doxylamine 12.5 mg q6–8h
treatments.notes: Level A evidence; safe and effective
case-by-case before 10 weeks; no increased risk of stillbirth or major birth defects
fewer adverse effects than promethazine; monitor for EPS

treatments.dosage: 16 mg IV q8h × 3 days then taper over 2 weeks
treatments.notes: reduces rehospitalization; avoid before 10 weeks

treatments.dosage: 100 mg daily × ≥7 days, then 50 mg daily maintenance
treatments.notes: MUST give before dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy

diseases.name: Ovarian Torsion
diseases.management: Ovarian torsion is the fifth most common gynecologic emergency. Presents with sudden-onset abdominal pain (intermittent, nonradiating) with nausea and vomiting. Diagnosis is surgical — no clinical or imaging criteria are sufficient to confirm preoperatively; Doppler flow alone should NOT guide clinical decision-making (present in ~50% of torsed ovaries). Pelvic ultrasound is first-line imaging: findings include ovarian enlargement, absent/decreased blood flow, whirlpool sign, and follicular ring sign. If torsion suspected, timely intervention with diagnostic laparoscopy is indicated to preserve ovarian function and fertility. Minimally invasive surgical approach with detorsion and preservation of adnexal structures regardless of ovarian appearance — do NOT remove a torsed ovary unless oophorectomy is unavoidable (severely necrotic ovary falls apart). Intraoperative visual diagnosis of ovarian death is highly inaccurate (only 18–20% truly necrotic on pathology). Cystectomy does not need to be performed at time of detorsion; consider incision and drainage for large cysts, with ultrasound follow-up at 6–12 weeks.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Reliance on Doppler flow alone to exclude torsion (present in ~50% of torsed ovaries). Oophorectomy based on intraoperative appearance alone (highly inaccurate — only 18–20% truly necrotic). Delay in surgical intervention (tissue ischemia progresses).

treatments.notes: standard of care

treatments.dosage: only when ovary is severely necrotic and falls apart
not required at time of detorsion; may cause additional trauma

diseases.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
diseases.management: PCOS is diagnosed using Rotterdam criteria (≥2 of: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology on US). Per 2023 International Evidence-Based Guideline, management is lifelong and multimodal. Healthy lifestyle (diet + exercise) is strongly recommended as first-line for all features — similar efficacy to metformin for metabolic outcomes and cycle regulation. Combined oral contraceptive pills (COCPs) are first-line pharmacotherapy for menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism; prefer lower ethinyl estradiol dose preparations. Metformin is recommended primarily for metabolic features (insulin resistance, glucose, lipids), especially BMI ≥25; suggested max dose 2.5 g/day in adults, 2 g/day in adolescents. COCPs preferred over metformin for hirsutism; metformin preferred over COCPs for metabolic indications. Anti-androgens (spironolactone 25–100 mg/day) for hirsutism inadequately responsive to 6 months of COCPs. Anti-obesity medications (GLP-1 RAs, orlistat) may be considered per general population guidelines; ensure concurrent contraception with GLP-1 RAs. Fertility: letrozole is preferred first-line; clomiphene ± metformin second-line; gonadotropins or ovarian surgery second-line; IVF third-line.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Metformin routinely in pregnant women with PCOS (not recommended). Inositol as substitute for metformin (limited clinical benefits; metformin has greater efficacy). Failure to screen for depression/anxiety (significantly increased in PCOS).

treatments.notes: no specific preparation recommended; prefer lower EE dose

treatments.dosage: start 500 mg, increase by 500 mg q1–2 weeks to max 2.5 g/day (2 g in adolescents)
treatments.notes: extended-release minimizes GI side effects; monitor B12
after 6 months inadequate response to COCPs; requires contraception (teratogenic)
preferred over clomiphene for ovulation induction

treatments.dosage: clomiphene 50–150 mg daily on cycle days 3–7

diseases.name: Intussusception (Pediatric)
diseases.specialty: Pediatric Surgery / Pediatric Emergency Medicine / Radiology
diseases.management: Intussusception is the most common cause of acute intestinal obstruction in children 6 years (incidence ~56/100,000/year). Ileocolic intussusception involves invagination of ileum through ileocecal valve into cecum. Transabdominal ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice (sensitivity ~98%). Emergent enema reduction is the standard of care to prevent complications. Both air enema (fluoroscopy-guided) and hydrostatic enema (ultrasound-guided) are effective; meta-analysis of 49 studies (9,391 patients) showed ultrasound-guided hydrostatic enema reduction (UGHR) had higher success rate (RR 1.13), shorter reduction time, shorter hospital stay, and lower perforation rate compared to fluoroscopy-guided air enema. Pneumatic reduction success rate ~97.7%. Perforation risk during reduction is 1%. Dexamethasone may reduce recurrence of intussusception regardless of enema type. Surgical management (laparotomy with manual reduction or laparoscopy) for failed enema reduction, peritonitis, perforation, hemodynamic instability, or pathologic lead point.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Enema reduction in peritonitis, perforation, or hemodynamic instability (proceed directly to surgery). Delay in treatment (risk of bowel necrosis, perforation, shock).

treatments.frequency: single procedure, may repeat up to 3 attempts
treatments.notes: success rate 90–98%; UGHR may be preferred (higher success, no radiation, lower perforation)

treatments.dosage: for failed enema reduction, peritonitis, perforation, hemodynamic instability, or pathologic lead point

diseases.name: Pyloric Stenosis (Pediatric)
diseases.management: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (IHPS) typically presents at 3–10 weeks of age with progressive, projectile, non-bilious vomiting leading to dehydration and hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Incidence 4–5 per 1,000 live births with strong male predilection. Diagnosis: abdominal ultrasound (pyloric muscle thickness >3 mm, channel length >15 mm). Preoperative management: correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities (especially chloride and potassium) before surgery — this is critical and should not be rushed. Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure) is the definitive treatment and has been the standard for >110 years. Both open and laparoscopic approaches are effective; laparoscopic pyloromyotomy may offer earlier return to full feeding, shorter hospitalization, better cosmesis, and lower cost, but has a slightly higher rate of incomplete pyloromyotomy. G-POEM (gastric peroral endoscopic pyloromyotomy) is an emerging minimally invasive alternative with promising early results.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Surgery before correction of metabolic alkalosis and dehydration (anesthetic risk). Rushing to OR without adequate resuscitation.

treatments.duration: until chloride >100 mEq/L, potassium >3.5 mEq/L, bicarbonate 30 mEq/L
treatments.notes: critical before anesthesia
earlier feeding, shorter stay, better cosmesis; slightly higher incomplete myotomy rate

treatments.dosage: Ramstedt procedure via RUQ or periumbilical incision
treatments.notes: gold standard; lower incomplete myotomy rate

diseases.name: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression
diseases.management: MSCC is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention to preserve neurologic function. Per NCCN CNS Guidelines (v2.2026), initiate steroids immediately upon diagnosis: recommended minimum dexamethasone 4 mg IV q6h (16 mg/day), though doses may vary from 10–100 mg depending on severity. Recent literature generally suggests 12–32 mg/day of dexamethasone; high-dose steroids (96 mg/day) associated with significantly more serious adverse effects (14% vs. 0% with 16 mg/day). Treatment options: (1) Surgery (decompressive resection ± stabilization) followed by RT (Category 1 for solitary epidural compression by non-radiosensitive tumor, life expectancy ≥3 months, not paraplegic >24 hr). (2) Primary RT alone (most common treatment). (3) Primary systemic therapy with close monitoring for chemosensitive tumors (lymphoma, myeloma, germ cell, SCLC). RT dosing: conventional EBRT 8 Gy/1 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx, or 30 Gy/10 fx; SBRT (16–24 Gy/1 fx) preferred for life expectancy ≥3 months and radioresistant tumors.
diseases.notes: Avoid: High-dose steroids (96 mg/day dexamethasone) routinely — significantly more adverse effects without clear benefit over moderate doses. Laminectomy alone (no benefit over RT alone; may cause instability). Delay in steroid initiation.

treatments.dosage: minimum 4 mg IV q6h (16 mg/day); higher doses (10–100 mg loading) for profound/rapidly progressive deficits
treatments.duration: taper over days to weeks during/after RT
treatments.notes: initiate immediately; do not wait for imaging

treatments.medication: Surgery (decompressive resection ± stabilization) followed by RT
treatments.dosage: for solitary epidural compression, non-radiosensitive tumor, life expectancy ≥3 months, not paraplegic >24 hr
8 Gy/1 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx, 30 Gy/10 fx (conventional); 16–24 Gy/1 fx (SBRT)
treatments.notes: SBRT preferred for life expectancy ≥3 months

treatments.route: for lymphoma, myeloma, germ cell, SCLC

diseases.name: Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Syndrome
diseases.specialty: Interventional Radiology / Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology
diseases.management: SVC syndrome is caused by obstruction/occlusion of the SVC; malignancy accounts for ~70% of cases (lung cancer, lymphoma). SVC syndrome is typically NOT acutely life-threatening and is no longer considered a true emergency — prompt expedited care is warranted but histologic diagnosis should be obtained before treatment. Initial management: head elevation, consider loop diuretics for severe cerebral edema. Steroids are commonly used but lack efficacy data. Treatment is etiology-specific: SCLC → chemotherapy (Grade 1C); NSCLC → RT and/or stent insertion (Grade 1C). Endovascular stenting has become first-line for most patients, providing rapid symptom relief (>90%), high technical success (80–98%), and low complication rates. For life-threatening symptoms (cerebral/laryngeal edema, postural syncope): immediate stabilization (ABCs) → urgent venography and stent placement ± catheter-directed thrombolysis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Treatment before histologic diagnosis (unless life-threatening). Steroids before biopsy in suspected lymphoma (may obscure diagnosis). Routine corticosteroids (lack efficacy data; meta-analysis showed no benefit).

treatments.notes: technical success 80–98%; consider anticoagulation
Grade 1C; can combine with stent

diseases.name: Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
diseases.management: Per Endocrine Society 2023 Clinical Practice Guideline, management is stepwise based on severity. (1) IV isotonic saline: 1–2 L bolus then 200–500 mL/hr targeting UOP 100–150 mL/hr; lowers Ca by 1–1.5 mg/dL over 24 hr. (2) Calcitonin: 4–8 IU/kg SC/IM q6–12h × 48–72 hr for rapid lowering (onset 4–6 hr, lowers Ca 1–2 mg/dL); tachyphylaxis after 48–72 hr; consider for Ca >15 mg/dL or altered consciousness. (3) Zoledronic acid: 3–4 mg IV over 15–30 min (preferred bisphosphonate); normalizes Ca in 80–90% over 48–72 hr; median duration 30–40 days; dose-adjust for GFR 60. (4) Denosumab: 120 mg SC (repeat at weeks 1, 2, 4, then monthly); FDA-approved for HCM refractory to bisphosphonates; normalizes Ca in ≥70%; median duration 104 days; preferred in renal insufficiency. (5) Glucocorticoids: for lymphoma or elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (prednisone 60 mg/day × 10 days or hydrocortisone 200–400 mg IV/day × 3–5 days). Loop diuretics only after volume repletion.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Loop diuretics before volume repletion (worsens hypercalcemia). Bisphosphonates in severe renal impairment (GFR 30–35 — nephrotoxicity). Delay in IV hydration.

treatments.notes: lowers Ca 1–1.5 mg/dL in 24 hr; assess for volume overload
onset 4–6 hr; tachyphylaxis after 48–72 hr; for Ca >15 or altered consciousness

treatments.frequency: single dose, repeat in 7 days if needed, then q3–4 weeks
treatments.duration: normalizes Ca in 80–90%; median duration 30–40 days
treatments.notes: dose-adjust for GFR 60; avoid if GFR 30–35

treatments.dosage: 120 mg SC on days 1, 8, 15, 29, then monthly
treatments.duration: normalizes Ca in ≥70%; median duration 104 days
treatments.notes: FDA-approved for bisphosphonate-refractory HCM; preferred in renal insufficiency

treatments.dosage: prednisone 60 mg/day × 10 days or hydrocortisone 200–400 mg IV/day × 3–5 days

treatments.notes: lowers Ca 0.5–1.0 mg/dL; risk of worsening hypercalcemia if given before volume repletion

diseases.name: Malignant Bowel Obstruction
diseases.specialty: Surgical Oncology / Palliative Care / Medical Oncology
diseases.management: Malignant bowel obstruction (MBO) occurs most commonly in ovarian and colorectal cancers. Management depends on prognosis, performance status, and goals of care. Surgical intervention (resection, bypass, or stoma) is considered for patients with good PS, limited disease, and life expectancy >2 months; however, postoperative morbidity is high (30–40%) and reobstruction common. For patients not candidates for surgery: medical management with IV fluids, NG decompression (short-term), and pharmacologic symptom control. Octreotide (100–300 mcg SC TID or 10–30 mcg/hr continuous) reduces GI secretions and is more effective than hyoscine butylbromide. Dexamethasone (8–16 mg/day IV) may resolve partial obstruction and reduce peritumoral edema. Haloperidol or ondansetron for nausea. Venting gastrostomy for refractory symptoms when NG tube is not a long-term option. Endoscopic stenting for single-site large bowel obstruction as bridge to surgery or palliation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Surgery in patients with poor PS, diffuse carcinomatosis, or life expectancy 2 months (high morbidity, poor outcomes). Prolonged NG tube use (discomfort, aspiration risk).

treatments.dosage: 100–300 mcg SC TID or 10–30 mcg/hr continuous
treatments.notes: more effective than hyoscine butylbromide
may resolve partial obstruction; taper if no response
preferred antiemetic in MBO

treatments.dosage: for good PS, limited disease, life expectancy >2 months

treatments.frequency: for refractory symptoms when surgery not feasible

diseases.name: Leukostasis / Hyperleukocytosis
diseases.management: Hyperleukocytosis is defined as WBC >100,000/μL; leukostasis is the symptomatic manifestation (pulmonary and/or CNS symptoms from microvascular sludging). Most common in AML (especially monocytic subtypes) and ALL. Leukostasis is a medical emergency with mortality up to 20–40% within 1 week if untreated. Management: (1) Cytoreduction with hydroxyurea (50–100 mg/kg/day in divided doses) is the cornerstone — begin immediately. (2) Leukapheresis for symptomatic leukostasis (temporizing measure — reduces WBC by 30–60% per session but no proven survival benefit; NCCN recommends for symptomatic patients). (3) Initiate definitive chemotherapy as soon as possible. (4) Aggressive IV hydration and TLS prophylaxis (rasburicase preferred). (5) Avoid RBC transfusion until WBC reduced (increases viscosity). (6) Avoid G-CSF.
diseases.notes: Avoid: RBC transfusion before WBC reduction (increases viscosity and worsens leukostasis). G-CSF (increases WBC). Delay in cytoreduction.

treatments.notes: begin immediately; cornerstone of initial management
reduces WBC 30–60% per session; no proven survival benefit; for symptomatic patients
ultimate treatment
contraindicated in G6PD deficiency

diseases.name: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Toxicity (irAEs)
diseases.management: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) can affect any organ system; most common: dermatologic, GI (colitis), hepatic, endocrine (thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency), and pulmonary (pneumonitis). Management is graded by CTCAE severity. Grade 1: continue ICI with close monitoring. Grade 2: hold ICI; start prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day; resume ICI when ≤Grade 1. Grade 3: hold ICI; prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day (or IV methylprednisolone equivalent); if no improvement in 48–72 hr, add infliximab 5 mg/kg (for colitis) or mycophenolate (for hepatitis — infliximab contraindicated in hepatitis). Grade 4: permanently discontinue ICI; IV methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day; add infliximab or other immunosuppressant if refractory. Endocrine irAEs (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, hypophysitis): hormone replacement rather than immunosuppression; ICI can often continue. Steroid taper over ≥4 weeks to prevent flare. Screen for infections before immunosuppression.

treatments.notes: hold ICI until resolution

treatments.duration: until improvement then switch to oral pr

diseases.name: Breast Cancer
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Surgical Oncology / Radiation Oncology
diseases.management: Breast cancer is categorized into 3 subtypes: HR+/HER2− (70%), HER2+ (15–20%), and triple-negative (TNBC, 15%). Treatment is multimodal and subtype-dependent. Local therapy: breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) + RT or mastectomy ± reconstruction; sentinel lymph node biopsy for staging. Systemic therapy is subtype-driven:
diseases.notes: Avoid: Chemotherapy for low-risk HR+/HER2− with low recurrence score (RS 26 postmenopausal). Anthracyclines in HER2+ early breast cancer when non-anthracycline regimens are effective. Routine oophorectomy without discussion of alternatives.

treatments.notes: CDK4/6 inhibitor; see NCCN eligibility criteria
Category 1
Category 1

diseases.name: Lung Cancer — Non-Small-Cell (NSCLC)
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Thoracic Surgery / Radiation Oncology / Pulmonology
diseases.management: Treatment depends on stage, histology (squamous vs. nonsquamous), and biomarker status (PD-L1, EGFR, ALK, ROS1, RET, BRAF, KRAS G12C, MET, NTRK, HER2). Biomarker testing is essential before treatment initiation for stages IB–IV.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ICI monotherapy in patients with EGFR/ALK aberrations (limited benefit, increased toxicity). Empiric treatment without biomarker testing in advanced disease. Single-agent chemotherapy as first-line for good PS patients.

treatments.medication: Neoadjuvant nivolumab or durvalumab + platinum doublet → surgery → adjuvant ICI

treatments.notes: Category 1
Category 1

diseases.name: Colorectal Cancer
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Surgical Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Gastroenterology
diseases.management: Treatment depends on stage, MMR/MSI status, and molecular profiling (RAS, BRAF, HER2, NTRK). All stage II–III tumors should undergo molecular profiling including somatic PI3K pathway alterations (aspirin 100–162 mg daily × 3 years if positive).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oxaliplatin-based adjuvant therapy for low-risk stage II pMMR/MSS colon cancer (no survival benefit demonstrated). Adjuvant chemotherapy for dMMR/MSI-H stage II colon cancer (good prognosis without chemo). Anti-EGFR therapy in RAS-mutant or right-sided tumors (limited benefit).

treatments.medication: Colectomy with en bloc lymph node removal
Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (nivolumab + ipilimumab or pembrolizumab)

treatments.notes: start after recovery from surgery

diseases.name: Prostate Cancer
diseases.management: Treatment is risk-stratified based on PSA, Gleason Grade Group, and clinical T stage per NCCN risk groups.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Immediate treatment for low-risk prostate cancer (overtreatment). ADT monotherapy for localized disease (no survival benefit). PSA-only monitoring without biopsy/MRI on active surveillance.

treatments.dosage: PSA q3–6 mo, MRI, biopsies per protocol

treatments.route: with pelvic lymph node dissection for high risk

treatments.dosage: RT + ADT 4–36 mo depending on risk

treatments.notes: Category 1

diseases.name: Pancreatic Cancer
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Surgical Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Gastroenterology
diseases.management: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is staged as resectable (10–15%), borderline resectable, locally advanced (30–35%), or metastatic (50–55%). Multidisciplinary management and comprehensive genomic profiling (including BRCA1/2, PALB2, KRAS, MSI, NTRK, RET, NRG1, HER2) are recommended for all patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: FOLFIRINOX in ECOG ≥2 (high toxicity). Routine preoperative biliary drainage (increases morbidity without mortality benefit). Radiation therapy in progressing metastatic disease (except palliation).

treatments.notes: Category 1; mOS 54.4 mo
Category 1
Category 1
improves PFS

diseases.name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
diseases.specialty: Hepatology / Medical Oncology / Surgical Oncology / Interventional Radiology / Transplant Surgery
diseases.management: HCC management follows the BCLC staging system and depends on tumor burden, liver function (Child-Pugh class), and performance status. Multidisciplinary evaluation is essential.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bevacizumab-containing regimens without prior EGD/variceal management (bleeding risk). TACE in CTP-C or bilirubin >3 (hepatotoxicity). Combination locoregional + systemic therapy for BCLC-B outside clinical trials (AASLD advises against).

treatments.useWhen: BCLC 0–A, tumors ≤3 cm, not surgical candidates

treatments.route: single dose treme then durva q4w

diseases.name: Lung Cancer — Small-Cell (SCLC)
diseases.management: SCLC is staged as limited-stage (LS, ~1/3) or extensive-stage (ES, ~2/3). LS-SCLC: concurrent chemoradiation (Category 1) with cisplatin/etoposide × 4 cycles + thoracic RT (45 Gy BID over 3 weeks preferred, or 60–70 Gy daily). RT should start early with cycle 1–2 (Category 1). Consolidation durvalumab for up to 24 months after chemoRT without progression (Category 1). PCI or MRI brain surveillance for responders. ES-SCLC: platinum/etoposide + ICI (atezolizumab or durvalumab) × 4 cycles → maintenance ICI (Category 1 for all preferred regimens). Atezolizumab + lurbinectedin maintenance is a new Category 1 option. Stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0): consider surgical resection + adjuvant chemo ± RT.
diseases.notes: Avoid: G-CSF during concurrent chemoRT (Category 1 for not using GM-CSF). Chemotherapy alone without ICI for ES-SCLC (inferior). Delay in RT initiation for LS-SCLC.

treatments.dosage: cisplatin 75 mg/m² D1 + etoposide 100 mg/m² D1–3 q21d × 4 cycles
treatments.notes: RT start with cycle 1–2

treatments.dosage: 1500 mg D1 q28d for up to 24 months
treatments.notes: after chemoRT without progression

treatments.dosage: carbo AUC 5 D1 + etop 100 mg/m² D1–3 + atezo 1200 mg D1 q21d × 4 → atezo q21d or q28d
lurbi 3.2 mg/m² + atezo 1200 mg D1 q21d
treatments.notes: new option

diseases.name: Gastric Cancer
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Surgical Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Gastroenterology
diseases.management: Treatment depends on stage, HER2 status, PD-L1 CPS, CLDN18.2 status, and MMR/MSI status. Early (cTis–T1a): endoscopic resection. Localized (≥cT2 or N+): perioperative FLOT chemotherapy (Category 1); consider neoadjuvant/perioperative ICI if MSI-H/dMMR. Metastatic: biomarker-driven first-line — HER2+ → trastuzumab + pembrolizumab + chemo; HER2−/PD-L1 CPS ≥1 → nivolumab or pembrolizumab + chemo; CLDN18.2+ → zolbetuximab + chemo (FDA-approved 2025); dMMR/MSI-H → consider ICI-based therapy. Second-line: ramucirumab + paclitaxel; HER2+ → T-DXd.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fluoropyrimidines without DPYD testing (risk of severe toxicity). Anti-PD-1 monotherapy for PD-L1 CPS 0 gastric adenocarcinoma.

treatments.dosage: docetaxel 50 mg/m² + oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² + leucovorin 200 mg/m² + 5-FU 2600 mg/m² (24-h infusion), all day 1 of a 14-day cycle
treatments.notes: 4 pre-op cycles → surgery (4–8 wks after last pre-op dose) → 4 post-op cycles (start 4–6 wks after discharge)

diseases.name: Esophageal Cancer
diseases.specialty: Medical Oncology / Thoracic Surgery / Radiation Oncology / Gastroenterology
diseases.management: Treatment depends on histology (adenocarcinoma vs. SCC), stage, and biomarkers. Adenocarcinoma: perioperative FLOT (Category 1, preferred) or preoperative CROSS chemoradiation → surgery. ESOPEC trial showed FLOT superior to CROSS for adenocarcinoma. Adjuvant nivolumab for residual disease after neoadjuvant chemoRT (CheckMate 577). Consider neoadjuvant/perioperative ICI if MSI-H/dMMR. SCC: definitive chemoradiation (cisplatin/5-FU + RT) or neoadjuvant chemoRT → surgery. Metastatic: biomarker-driven — SCC with PD-L1 TPS ≥1 → nivolumab + chemo or nivolumab + ipilimumab; adenocarcinoma with PD-L1 CPS ≥5 → nivolumab + chemo.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine preoperative chemoRT for adenocarcinoma when perioperative FLOT is available (ESOPEC showed FLOT superior).

treatments.dosage: docetaxel 50 mg/m² + oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² + leucovorin 200 mg/m² + 5-FU 2600 mg/m² (24-h infusion), all day 1 of a 14-day cycle
treatments.duration: 4 pre-op cycles → surgery (4–8 wks after last pre-op dose) → 4 post-op cycles (start 4–6 wks after discharge)

treatments.dosage: 240 mg q2w or 480 mg q4w × 1 year

diseases.name: Renal Cell Carcinoma
diseases.management: Stage I (T1a): partial nephrectomy (preferred), percutaneous ablation, SBRT, or active surveillance. Stage I (T1b): partial or radical nephrectomy. Stage II–III: radical nephrectomy; adjuvant pembrolizumab (Category 1 for clear cell, grade 4 or stage III). Adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab is a new option. Stage IV (metastatic, clear cell): first-line ICI-TKI combinations preferred (Category 1) — axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab (intermediate/poor risk). Cabozantinib monotherapy preferred for poor/intermediate risk. Subsequent-line: switch class (TKI after ICI or vice versa), cabozantinib, belzutifan.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sunitinib as first-line when ICI-TKI combinations are available (inferior outcomes). Cytoreductive nephrectomy in poor-risk patients with large-volume metastases (systemic therapy first).

treatments.dosage: 200 mg q3w or 400 mg q6w × 1 year
treatments.useWhen: clear cell, stage II grade 4 or stage III, Category 1

treatments.medication: Axitinib + pembrolizumab or lenvatinib + pembrolizumab or cabozantinib + nivolumab or ipilimumab + nivolumab

diseases.name: Bladder / Urothelial Cancer
diseases.management: NMIBC: TURBT → risk-stratified intravesical therapy. Low-risk: TURBT + single-dose intravesical gemcitabine (Category 1, preferred) or mitomycin. High-risk: BCG induction + maintenance (Category 1). BCG-unresponsive: cystectomy, BCG + nogapendekin, gemcitabine intravesical system, nadofaragene, or pembrolizumab. MIBC (cT2–T4a, N0–1): neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemo (DDMVAC preferred) or perioperative cisplatin/gemcitabine + durvalumab (Category 1) → radical cystectomy. Cisplatin-ineligible: perioperative enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab. Bladder preservation with concurrent chemoRT + TURBT (Category 1) is an alternative. Adjuvant: nivolumab (Category 1) or pembrolizumab; ctDNA-MRD guided atezolizumab (Category 1). Metastatic first-line: enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab (preferred) or cisplatin/gemcitabine + nivolumab → avelumab maintenance.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemo in cisplatin-ineligible patients (use enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab instead). Intravesical chemo post-TURBT if concern for bladder perforation.

treatments.medication: Neoadjuvant DDMVAC or cisplatin/gemcitabine + durvalumab → cystectomy

diseases.name: Ovarian Cancer
diseases.management: Surgery: primary cytoreductive surgery (PCS) with goal of no residual disease, or neoadjuvant chemo → interval debulking for patients not candidates for PCS. First-line chemo: carboplatin/paclitaxel q3w × 6 cycles (preferred). Bevacizumab may be added. Maintenance therapy is biomarker-driven: BRCA1/2 mutated → olaparib (Category 1) or niraparib (Category 1); BRCA WT/HRD+ → olaparib + bevacizumab (Category 1); HRP/unknown → observe (if CR) or bevacizumab. Recurrent platinum-sensitive: platinum-based doublet ± bevacizumab → PARPi maintenance (BRCA-mutated only in recurrent setting). Platinum-resistant: single-agent chemo ± bevacizumab; mirvetuximab soravtansine for FRα+ (new standard).
diseases.notes: Avoid: PARPi maintenance in HRP tumors without BRCA mutation in recurrent setting (limited benefit). Prolonged PARPi >2 years in recurrent setting (increased MDS/AML risk). Fluconazole monotherapy for induction of cryptococcal meningitis.

treatments.dosage: carbo AUC 5–6 + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² q3w × 6 cycles
olaparib 300 mg BID or niraparib 200–300 mg daily
treatments.notes: Category 1

treatments.useWhen: stage II–IV, HRD+, after chemo + bev

treatments.dosage: 6 mg/kg adjusted ideal body weight q3w

diseases.name: Cervical Cancer
diseases.specialty: Gynecologic Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Medical Oncology
diseases.management: Early stage (IA1): conization or simple hysterectomy. IA2–IB2: radical hysterectomy + lymph node evaluation (open approach preferred per LACC trial). IB3–IVA (locally advanced): primary chemoradiation (cisplatin + EBRT + brachytherapy) completed within 56 days. Stage III–IVA PD-L1+: add concurrent and maintenance pembrolizumab (Category 1 for FIGO 2014 stage IIIA–IVA per KEYNOTE-A18). Induction carboplatin/paclitaxel → chemoRT is acceptable per INTERLACE. Metastatic/recurrent: platinum/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab; PD-L1 CPS ≥1 → add pembrolizumab.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Minimally invasive radical hysterectomy (inferior outcomes per LACC trial — open preferred). SBRT as substitute for brachytherapy. Treatment beyond 56 days (worse outcomes).

diseases.name: Endometrial (Uterine) Cancer
diseases.specialty: Gynecologic Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Medical Oncology
diseases.management: Primary treatment: TH/BSO + surgical staging (MIS preferred). Stage IA G1–2: observation preferred; consider vaginal brachytherapy if LVSI or age ≥60. Stage IB G3: RT ± systemic therapy. Stage III–IV: systemic therapy is now biomarker-driven — carboplatin/paclitaxel + pembrolizumab (Category 1, except carcinosarcoma), or + dostarlimab (Category 1), or + durvalumab (Category 1 for dMMR). HER2+ serous: add trastuzumab. Recurrent/advanced pMMR: lenvatinib + pembrolizumab. Recurrent dMMR: dostarlimab or pembrolizumab monotherapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Omitting molecular classification (MMR/MSI, p53, POLE testing now essential for treatment selection). Adjuvant chemotherapy alone for stage III–IV without ICI (inferior to chemo + ICI).

diseases.name: Head & Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
diseases.specialty: Head & Neck Surgery / Radiation Oncology / Medical Oncology
diseases.management: Treatment depends on subsite, stage, HPV/p16 status, and resectability. Early stage (I–II): surgery or definitive RT (similar survival ~70–90% at 5 years); surgery preferred for oral cavity. Locally advanced (III–IVA): surgery → adjuvant RT ± cisplatin (Category 1 for extranodal extension/positive margins) OR definitive concurrent chemoRT (high-dose cisplatin 100 mg/m² q3w, Category 1). Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (oral cavity, p16-negative oropharynx) is a new option. HPV+ oropharynx: concurrent chemoRT or surgery ± adjuvant therapy; de-escalation to 50 Gy may be considered in select low-risk patients (Category 2B). Recurrent/metastatic: pembrolizumab + platinum/5-FU (PD-L1 CPS ≥1) or pembrolizumab monotherapy (CPS ≥20).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cetuximab as radiosensitizer when cisplatin is feasible (inferior outcomes). Induction chemotherapy routinely (Category 3 at most sites). MIS for oral cavity cancer (open preferred).

treatments.frequency: Category 1 for chemoRT if ENE/positive margins

diseases.name: Melanoma
diseases.management: Localized (stage IA–IIA): wide local excision with margins based on depth (0.5–2 cm). SLNB for tumors ≥0.8 mm or ulcerated. Stage IIB–IIC: adjuvant nivolumab (Category 1) or pembrolizumab (Category 1). Stage III (resected): adjuvant nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF V600+) × 12 months; observation is an option especially for stage IIIA. Unresectable/metastatic: first-line combination checkpoint blockade preferred — ipilimumab + nivolumab (Category 1) or nivolumab + relatlimab (Category 1); anti-PD-1 monotherapy (Category 1); BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600+ (Category 1, other recommended). Second-line: switch class; lifileucel (TIL therapy) for anti-PD-1 refractory disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: BRAF/MEK inhibitors as first-line over ICI (ICI preferred due to OS benefit). PD-L1 testing to guide treatment decisions (not recommended). Adjuvant therapy for stage IIIA with 1 mm nodal burden (observation preferred).

treatments.medication: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab or dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF V600+)

treatments.frequency: mOS 51 mo vs. 34 mo nivo alone
for good PS patients after ICI and BRAF/MEK

diseases.name: Glioblastoma / Primary Brain Tumors
diseases.management: Glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype) is the most common malignant primary brain tumor; median survival ~14–17 months. Maximal safe resection is recommended (5-ALA fluorescence-guided). Age ≤70, KPS ≥60: RT (60 Gy/30 fx) + concurrent temozolomide → adjuvant temozolomide × 6 cycles ± tumor treating fields (TTF) (Category 1; TTF preferred). MGMT promoter methylation predicts temozolomide benefit (mOS 21.7 vs. 15.3 mo). Age >70 or KPS 60: hypofractionated RT ± temozolomide; temozolomide alone if MGMT methylated. Recurrent: clinical trial preferred; bevacizumab, temozolomide, lomustine, carmustine; reirradiation in select patients. Immunotherapy has not shown benefit in GBM to date.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Temozolomide beyond 6 cycles (no clear benefit). Immunotherapy outside clinical trials (no benefit demonstrated). Alternating electric field therapy for infratentorial tumors (supratentorial only).

treatments.medication: RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide ± TTF
treatments.dosage: TMZ 75 mg/m² daily during RT → 150–200 mg/m² D1–5 q28d × 6 cycles
treatments.notes: TTF preferred

treatments.duration: for patients unable to tolerate RT

diseases.name: Thyroid Cancer
diseases.specialty: Endocrine Surgery / Endocrinology / Medical Oncology / Nuclear Medicine
diseases.management: Thyroid cancer subtypes: papillary (84%), follicular (4%), oncocytic (2%), poorly differentiated (5%), anaplastic (1%), medullary (4%). Five-year relative survival ~98.5%. Surgery is curative in most well-differentiated thyroid cancer (WDTC). Microcarcinomas (≤1 cm): active surveillance can be considered. Tumors >1 cm: surgery ± RAI. Lobectomy preferred for 1–4 cm tumors without high-risk features; total thyroidectomy for ETE, lateral LN metastases, distant metastases, or tumors >4 cm. RAI therapy for intermediate/high-risk WDTC after total thyroidectomy. TSH suppression with levothyroxine based on risk stratification. RAI-refractory disease: lenvatinib (Category 1) or sorafenib (Category 1) preferred; cabozantinib after lenvatinib/sorafenib (Category 1 for papillary). Targeted therapies for actionable mutations: selpercatinib/pralsetinib (RET fusion), entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib (NTRK fusion), dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF V600E). Medullary thyroid cancer: total thyroidectomy + central LN dissection; vandetanib or cabozantinib for advanced; selpercatinib for RET-mutant MTC. Anaplastic: dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600E+.
diseases.notes: Avoid: RAI for medullary or anaplastic thyroid cancer (not RAI-avid). Routine RAI for low-risk papillary microcarcinoma (no benefit). TKI therapy for stable, indolent RAI-refractory disease (may not be appropriate).

treatments.useWhen: ETE, LN mets, distant mets, >4 cm

diseases.name: Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (BCC / SCC)
diseases.management: Keratinocyte carcinomas (BCC + cSCC) are the most common cancers in the US (~5.4 million/year). Treatment is risk-stratified. Low-risk BCC: C&E, shave removal, standard excision with 4 mm margins, or nonsurgical modalities for superficial BCC (topical imiquimod [preferred], topical fluorouracil, cryotherapy, photodynamic therapy). Low-risk cSCC: C&E, shave removal, standard excision with 4–6 mm margins, or Mohs/PDEMA. High-risk BCC/cSCC: Mohs micrographic surgery or PDEMA preferred (highest cure rates, maximum tissue conservation). Adjuvant RT for extensive perineural or large-nerve involvement. RT for nonsurgical candidates. Advanced/metastatic BCC: hedgehog pathway inhibitors (vismodegib, sonidegib); cemiplimab if refractory. Advanced/metastatic cSCC: cemiplimab or pembrolizumab (PD-L1 CPS ≥1). Recurrence after excision: ~3% BCC, ~5% cSCC; after Mohs: ~4% BCC, ~3% cSCC. 40% risk of additional keratinocyte carcinoma within 5 years.
diseases.notes: Avoid: C&E or shave removal if tumor extends beyond dermis (excision preferred). RT in patients 55 years (cosmesis worsens over time). Topical therapies for invasive cSCC (off-label, higher recurrence).

treatments.medication: Standard excision (4 mm BCC, 4–6 mm cSCC margins)

treatments.frequency: may have lower cure rate than excision

diseases.name: Soft-Tissue & Bone Sarcoma
diseases.specialty: Surgical Oncology / Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Orthopedic Oncology
diseases.management: Sarcomas are rare, heterogeneous tumors (>80 subtypes). Multidisciplinary management at experienced centers is essential. Localized extremity/trunk STS: wide surgical resection with oncologically appropriate margins + neoadjuvant RT (Category 1 for stage II–III) or adjuvant RT (Category 1). Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (AIM: doxorubicin/ifosfamide/mesna) for high-risk, chemosensitive subtypes. Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + RT for UPS/MFS/ddLPS (new option). Retroperitoneal sarcoma: surgery ± neoadjuvant RT for high local recurrence risk. Metastatic STS first-line: anthracycline-based regimens preferred (doxorubicin, AIM, epirubicin/ifosfamide); NTRK fusion+ → larotrectinib/entrectinib/repotrectinib. Subsequent-line: pazopanib, eribulin (Category 1 for liposarcoma), trabectedin (Category 1 for liposarcoma/LMS), docetaxel/gemcitabine. Subtype-specific: desmoid tumors → nirogacestat (Category 1) or sorafenib (Category 1); GIST → imatinib; ASPS → atezolizumab; TGCT → pexidartinib or vimseltinib (Category 1). Bone sarcoma (osteosarcoma): MAP chemotherapy (methotrexate/doxorubicin/cisplatin) + surgery.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine adjuvant chemotherapy for low-grade STS (no benefit). Chemotherapy for WDLPS without dedifferentiation. Surgery at non-specialized centers (higher recurrence rates).

treatments.medication: Wide surgical resection + neoadjuvant or adjuvant RT

diseases.name: Testicular Cancer
diseases.management: Testicular cancer is the most common malignancy in males 15–40 years; >90% cure rate. Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard for diagnosis and initial management. Sperm banking should be discussed before treatment. Management is histology- and stage-dependent.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Trans-scrotal orchiectomy (higher local recurrence, altered metastatic pathways). FDG-PET/CT for post-chemo assessment in nonseminoma (no role). Treatment based on elevated LDH alone.

diseases.name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)
diseases.management: AML treatment is risk-stratified by ELN 2022 criteria (favorable, intermediate, adverse) and fitness for intensive therapy. Rapid cytogenetic and molecular characterization (FLT3, NPM1, IDH1/2, TP53, CEBPA) is essential before treatment initiation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Intensive chemotherapy for TP53-mutated AML (poor outcomes; clinical trial preferred). Delay in molecular testing (impacts treatment selection). Venetoclax without dose adjustment for CYP3A4 interactions.

diseases.name: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)
diseases.management: Adult ALL treatment is subtype-driven: Ph-negative B-ALL, Ph-positive B-ALL, and T-ALL. Pediatric-inspired regimens have improved outcomes in AYA patients. Blinatumomab and inotuzumab ozogamicin have transformed the treatment landscape. MRD negativity by NGS is the primary treatment goal.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Chemoimmunotherapy alone for Ph+ ALL when TKI + blinatumomab is available (inferior outcomes). Omitting MRD assessment (critical for treatment decisions). Delay in CNS prophylaxis.

diseases.name: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
diseases.management: CML-CP treatment is TKI-based with near-normal life expectancy for most patients. Treatment selection considers risk score (ELTS), treatment goals (survival vs. TFR), comorbidities, toxicity profile, and cost. Low-risk: imatinib (1G TKI, Category 1) or 2G TKI (bosutinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, all Category 1) or asciminib (allosteric TKI, Category 1) — all preferred. Intermediate/high-risk: 2G TKI or asciminib preferred; imatinib is other recommended. Response milestones: BCR::ABL1 IS ≤10% at 3 mo, ≤1% at 6 mo, ≤0.1% at 12 mo (optimal). Failure to meet milestones → switch TKI based on BCR::ABL1 mutation profile. T315I mutation: ponatinib (preferred for AP/BP) or asciminib. TFR: consider TKI discontinuation in patients with sustained DMR (MR4.0) for ≥2 years. AP-CML: 2G or 3G TKI. BP-CML: ALL- or AML-type induction + TKI → allogeneic HCT.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Asciminib in patients lacking ABL1 exon 2 (no activity). TKI therapy during first trimester of pregnancy (teratogenic). Imatinib for intermediate/high-risk when 2G TKIs are available (slower molecular responses).

treatments.useWhen: all risk, Category 1, preferred for intermediate/high-risk

treatments.frequency: allosteric TKI; contraindicated if lacking ABL1 exon 2

treatments.useWhen: T315I mutation or resistance to ≥2 TKIs

diseases.name: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL)
diseases.management: CLL treatment is indicated only for symptomatic/progressive disease (Rai III–IV, Binet C, or symptomatic). Asymptomatic patients: observation regardless of risk. Treatment has shifted from chemoimmunotherapy to targeted agents. First-line without del(17p)/TP53: preferred — venetoclax/acalabrutinib ± obinutuzumab (fixed duration, Category 1), venetoclax + obinutuzumab (fixed duration, Category 1), acalabrutinib ± obinutuzumab (continuous, Category 1), zanubrutinib (continuous, Category 1). Other recommended: venetoclax/ibrutinib (fixed duration or MRD-guided, Category 1). First-line with del(17p)/TP53: venetoclax + obinutuzumab, venetoclax/acalabrutinib + obinutuzumab (MRD-guided), venetoclax/zanubrutinib (MRD-guided), acalabrutinib ± obinutuzumab, zanubrutinib — all preferred. Second-line: switch class (BTKi after BCL2i or vice versa); pirtobrutinib (ncBTKi, Category 1) for cBTKi-resistant disease. After both cBTKi and BCL2i: CAR-T (lisocabtagene maraleucel), pirtobrutinib, PI3Ki (idelalisib, duvelisib).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Chemoimmunotherapy as first-line when targeted agents are available (inferior PFS/OS). Treatment of asymptomatic early-stage CLL (no benefit). Ibrutinib in patients with significant cardiac comorbidities (atrial fibrillation risk).

diseases.name: Multiple Myeloma
diseases.management: Treatment is risk-stratified (R-ISS) and transplant eligibility-dependent. High-risk: t(4;14), t(14;16), t(14;20), del(17p), TP53 mutation, gain(1q).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Thalidomide in first-line when lenalidomide is available (higher neuropathy, VTE risk). Avoid nephrotoxic agents without dose adjustment. Limit alkylating agent exposure prior to stem cell collection. Avoid live vaccines during active therapy. | Nephrotoxic agents (NSAIDs, IV contrast without hydration) in patients with renal impairment. Avoid lenalidomide without dose adjustment in renal impairment (CrCl 60 mL/min). Avoid melphalan before stem cell collection (impairs harvest). Avoid live vaccines during active treatment. Avoid concurrent strong CYP3A4 inducers with bortezomib/ixazomib.

treatments.dosage: Dara SC 1800 mg + hyaluronidase or IV 16 mg/kg per schedule; Bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC; Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–14; Dex 40 mg PO weekly
treatments.duration: 4–6 induction cycles then ASCT then maintenance
treatments.notes: use when transplant-eligible, age 80, not frail

treatments.dosage: Dara per schedule; Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–21 of 28-day cycles; Dex 40 mg weekly (20 mg if >75 yr)
treatments.notes: use when transplant-ineligible

treatments.dosage: Bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC weekly (preferred) or twice weekly; Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–14; Dex 40 mg weekly
treatments.notes: use when anti-CD38 not available or not tolerated
use when post-ASCT or post-induction; two-drug maintenance for high-risk
use when transplant-eligible or ineligible non-frail

treatments.dosage: Dara 16 mg/kg IV (or SC) per protocol + Bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC days 1,4,8,11 + Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–14 + Dexamethasone 20–40 mg per protocol
treatments.notes: transplant-eligible newly diagnosed MM
transplant-eligible newly diagnosed MM

treatments.dosage: Bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC days 1,4,8,11 + Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–14 + Dexamethasone 40 mg days 1,2,4,5,8,9,11,12
treatments.notes: transplant-eligible, when anti-CD38 not available/tolerated

treatments.dosage: Dara per protocol + Lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–21 + Dexamethasone 40 mg weekly
treatments.notes: transplant-ineligible newly diagnosed MM

treatments.dosage: Bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC every 2 weeks ± Lenalidomide
treatments.useWhen: high-risk cytogenetics (del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16), 1q+) | two-drug maintenance recommended for high-risk MM

treatments.notes: newly diagnosed MM, transplant-eligible; category 1
newly diagnosed MM, transplant-eligible; category 1

treatments.dosage: bortezomib 1.3 mg/m² SC days 1,4,8,11 + lenalidomide 25 mg PO days 1–14 + dexamethasone 20 mg PO
treatments.useWhen: when quadruplet not feasible; remains standard triplet backbone

treatments.notes: transplant-eligible patients after adequate induction response

treatments.dosage: 10–15 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle
treatments.notes: post-HCT or post-induction; increased risk of secondary malignancies should be discussed
transplant-ineligible; category 1

treatments.dosage: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV Q4 weeks or denosumab 120 mg SC Q4 weeks
treatments.notes: all patients receiving primary myeloma therapy; denosumab preferred in renal insufficiency

treatments.useWhen: ≥4 prior lines (ide-cel) or ≥1 prior line (cilta-cel); durable responses

diseases.name: Hodgkin Lymphoma
diseases.management: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma (CHL) is highly curable (>85% overall). Treatment is stage- and risk-adapted. Favorable early-stage (IA/IIA by GHSG/EORTC): ABVD × 2 cycles → interim PET → if Deauville 1–3: ISRT 20 Gy (preferred) or ABVD × 2 more (chemo alone) (Category 1). Unfavorable early-stage (I/II with B symptoms, bulky, ≥4 nodal sites, ESR ≥50): Nivolumab-AVD × 4 + ISRT (adapted from NIVAHL) or BV-AVD + G-CSF × 4 + ISRT (adapted from BREACH) or BrECADD + G-CSF (stage IIB bulky). Stage III–IV (age 18–60): Preferred — Nivolumab-AVD × 6 cycles (Category 1) or BrECADD + G-CSF (Category 1); useful in certain circumstances — BV-AVD + G-CSF (Category 1) or ABVD (Category 1). Age >60: Nivolumab-AVD × 6 (Category 1, preferred for stage III–IV); A(B)VD with bleomycin caution (not beyond 2 cycles). NLPHL: ISRT alone for stage IA (preferred); chemo + rituximab ± ISRT for advanced. Relapsed/refractory: salvage chemo → auto-HCT; BV consolidation post-auto-HCT; pembrolizumab or nivolumab for relapse after auto-HCT.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bleomycin beyond 2 cycles in patients >60 years (pulmonary toxicity). G-CSF with ABVD (not recommended). eBEACOPP as standard (higher toxicity without OS benefit vs. newer regimens).

treatments.useWhen: stage III–IV, Category 1, useful in certain circumstances

diseases.name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (DLBCL, Follicular)
diseases.management: DLBCL: Most common NHL (~40%); curable in ~65%. First-line: Stage I–II nonbulky, smIPI 0–1 → R-CHOP × 3 → PET-adapted. Stage II bulky or III–IV: Preferred — R-CHOP (Category 1) or Pola-R-CHP (IPI ≥2, Category 1); other recommended — DA-EPOCH-R. R-CHOP × 6 cycles q21d remains standard. Pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab vedotin replacing vincristine) showed superior PFS in POLARIX for IPI ≥2. Poor LV function: DA-EPOCH-R, CDOP-R, CEOP-R, GCVP-R. Relapsed 12 months or primary refractory: CAR-T preferred (axicabtagene ciloleucel or lisocabtagene maraleucel, both Category 1); non-CAR-T candidates → bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab) + GEMOX or polatuzumab-based regimens. Relapsed >12 months: salvage chemo → auto-HCT.
diseases.notes: Avoid: R-CHOP for double-hit lymphoma (MYC + BCL2) — use DA-EPOCH-R instead. Routine treatment of asymptomatic early-stage FL (observation preferred). Auto-HCT for DLBCL relapsed 12 months when CAR-T is available (CAR-T superior).

diseases.name: Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS)
diseases.management: MDS treatment is risk-stratified by IPSS-R (very low, low, intermediate, high, very high). Intermediate risk: managed as lower risk if score ≤3.5, higher risk if >3.5.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Lenalidomide in del(5q) with monosomy 7 (treat as higher risk). HMA discontinuation before 4–6 cycles (may not have had adequate trial). Venetoclax without careful monitoring for cytopenias.

diseases.name: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (PV / ET / Myelofibrosis)
diseases.management: Polycythemia vera (PV): All patients: aspirin 81–100 mg/day + phlebotomy (hematocrit 45%). Low-risk (age ≤60, no prior thrombosis): phlebotomy + aspirin; cytoreductive therapy if symptomatic or frequent phlebotomy. High-risk (age >60 or prior thrombosis): cytoreductive therapy — preferred: hydroxyurea or ropeginterferon alfa-2b; other recommended: peginterferon alfa-2a; useful in certain circumstances: ruxolitinib. Second-line: ruxolitinib (Category 1 for HU resistance/intolerance).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cytoreductive therapy as initial treatment for low-risk PV (phlebotomy + aspirin first). Aspirin in patients with acquired VWS and extreme thrombocytosis (bleeding risk). Routine G-CSF in MF (may worsen splenomegaly).

diseases.name: Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea & Vomiting (CINV)
diseases.management: Antiemetic prophylaxis is based on emetogenic risk of the chemotherapy regimen. Prevention is the primary goal.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Ondansetron IV >16 mg single dose (QT prolongation). Routine prophylaxis for minimal-risk agents. Combining multiple dopamine-receptor antagonists (extrapyramidal risk). Corticosteroid premedication with cellular therapies.

diseases.name: Cancer Pain Management
diseases.specialty: Palliative Care / Medical Oncology / Pain Medicine
diseases.management: Cancer pain management follows a multimodal, mechanism-based approach. Universal screening at every contact. Mild pain: non-opioids first (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, adjuvants). Moderate/severe pain: short-acting opioids + non-opioids/adjuvants. Severe pain/crisis: consider hospitalization for rapid titration. Opioid-naïve starting doses: morphine 5–7.5 mg PO, oxycodone IR 2.5–5 mg, hydromorphone 2 mg PO, or hydrocodone 5 mg q3–4h PRN. If multiple daily doses needed, add long-acting opioid. Neuropathic pain: adjuvant analgesics — SNRIs (duloxetine preferred), gabapentin/pregabalin, TCAs, topical lidocaine. Bone pain: NSAIDs/steroids, bone-modifying agents (bisphosphonates, denosumab), palliative RT, vertebral augmentation. Interventional strategies for refractory pain. Buprenorphine is an option for patients sensitive to full agonist side effects.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs with thrombocytopenia or renal impairment. Meperidine for cancer pain (neurotoxic metabolite). Acetaminophen exceeding 4 g/day or with combination opioid-acetaminophen products (hepatotoxicity). Tramadol with serotonergic medications (serotonin syndrome risk).

treatments.dosage: ibuprofen 400–800 mg QID or naproxen 220–500 mg BID-TID
morphine 5 mg or oxycodone 2.5–5 mg q3–4h PRN

diseases.name: Cancer-Associated VTE
diseases.management: DOACs are preferred for most patients without gastric/GE lesions. LMWH preferred for patients with gastric/GE lesions. Duration: at least 3 months; indefinite while cancer is active or under treatment. After 6 months of apixaban 5 mg BID, consider dose reduction to 2.5 mg BID for long-term secondary prophylaxis (Category 1). Warfarin is inferior to LMWH and DOACs and should only be used if others are inaccessible.
diseases.notes: Avoid: DOACs in patients with intact intraluminal GI/GU tumors (increased bleeding). Warfarin as first-line (inferior to LMWH/DOACs). Stopping anticoagulation at 3 months if cancer is still active.

treatments.dosage: 10 mg BID × 7 days → 5 mg BID; after 6 mo consider 2.5 mg BID
treatments.useWhen: DVT/PE, Category 1, preferred if no GI/GU lesion

treatments.dosage: 15 mg BID × 21 days → 20 mg daily with food
treatments.useWhen: DVT/PE, preferred if no GI/GU lesion

treatments.dosage: 200 U/kg SC daily × 30 days → 150 U/kg daily

diseases.name: Chemotherapy-Induced Mucositis
diseases.management: Oral mucositis is a common dose-limiting toxicity of chemotherapy and radiation. Prevention is key. Dental evaluation before initiating therapy. Basic oral care: soft toothbrush, bland rinses (0.9% saline, sodium bicarbonate) twice daily and after meals. Cryotherapy (ice chips) during rapid infusions of agents like 5-FU and melphalan (evidence-based prevention). Photobiomodulation (low-level laser therapy) is recommended by MASCC/ISOO for prevention. Palifermin (keratinocyte growth factor) for patients receiving myeloablative conditioning with TBI for auto-HCT. Chlorhexidine mouthwash for bactericidal effect. Zinc supplementation (≥150 mg/day oral) may reduce severe mucositis in mid-to-late treatment phases.
