Management: topical anesthetics (lidocaine-based rinses), systemic analgesics (opioids for severe pain), gabapentin for mucositis pain, maintain hydration/nutrition, HSV/candida prophylaxis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Alcohol-containing mouthwashes (irritating). Chlorhexidine for treatment of established mucositis (no evidence of benefit for treatment, only prevention). "Magic mouthwash" as sole therapy (insufficient evidence).

treatments.dosage: hold in mouth 5 min before through 30 min after infusion
60 mcg/kg/day IV × 3 days before and 3 days after conditioning

diseases.name: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CAR-T Therapy)
diseases.management: CRS is graded per ASTCT consensus criteria based on fever, hypotension, and hypoxia. Prompt intervention is required. Rule out infection. Grade 1 (fever ≥38°C): consider tocilizumab for persistent/refractory fever; consider dexamethasone 10 mg IV q24h for early-onset CRS (72 h post-infusion); sepsis workup + empiric antibiotics if neutropenic. Grade 2 (hypotension not requiring vasopressors and/or low-flow O₂): tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV (max 800 mg) + dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12–24h; may repeat tocilizumab q8h (max 3 doses/24h, 4 total). Grade 3 (vasopressor-requiring hypotension and/or high-flow O₂): ICU care + tocilizumab + dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6–12h; echocardiogram + hemodynamic monitoring. Grade 4 (multiple vasopressors and/or positive pressure ventilation): ICU + tocilizumab + dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h; if refractory → methylprednisolone 1–2 g/day × 3 days → rapid taper. Refractory CRS: anakinra (first choice), siltuximab, ruxolitinib, or other agents with limited evidence. GM-CSF is NOT recommended with CAR-T therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: GM-CSF in the setting of CAR-T therapy (not recommended). Corticosteroid premedication before CAR-T infusion without indication (may increase risk of prolonged neurotoxicity). Delay in tocilizumab administration for worsening CRS.

treatments.frequency: repeat q8h PRN, max 3 doses/24h, 4 total

treatments.dosage: 10 mg IV q6–24h depending on grade

treatments.useWhen: severe CRS refractory to anti-IL-6 + steroids

diseases.name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
diseases.management: HCM management is guided by the presence or absence of LVOT obstruction (gradient ≥30 mmHg). Asymptomatic patients require monitoring only (echo every 3–5 years in adults, 1–2 years in children). Symptomatic obstructive HCM is treated stepwise: beta-blockers first-line → nondihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil) if intolerant → add disopyramide (with AV nodal blocker) or cardiac myosin inhibitor (mavacamten) if persistent symptoms → septal reduction therapy (surgical myectomy preferred, or alcohol septal ablation) at experienced centers if refractory. Non-obstructive HCM with HFrEF (EF 50%) is treated per standard HF guidelines. SCD risk stratification with ICD consideration is essential. Patients should maintain hydration, avoid vasodilators, and avoid dehydration. All patients should be managed in conjunction with an HCM center.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vasodilators (nifedipine, amlodipine, ACEi/ARBs in obstructive HCM), nitroglycerin, digoxin, dobutamine/dopamine, dihydropyridine CCBs, high-dose diuretics (reduce preload and worsen obstruction), stimulant medications (ADHD drugs). Do not combine beta-blockers with verapamil (excessive bradycardia/hypotension).

treatments.dosage: metoprolol 25–200 mg BID or nadolol 40–160 mg daily

treatments.useWhen: beta-blocker intolerant; avoid if severe obstruction or HF
persistent symptoms despite beta-blocker/CCB; must combine with AV nodal blocker

treatments.dosage: 5 mg PO daily, titrate to 2.5–15 mg based on LVOT gradient and LVEF
treatments.useWhen: symptomatic NYHA II-III obstructive HCM despite beta-blocker/CCB; adults only; monitor LVEF (discontinue if 50%)

treatments.notes: obstructive HCM with symptoms
persistent symptoms despite beta-blocker/CCB in obstructive HCM
persistent symptoms despite beta-blocker/CCB
preferred over alcohol ablation at experienced centers; operative mortality 1% at high-volume centers

diseases.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
diseases.management: DCM management focuses on identifying and treating reversible causes (alcohol, thyroid disease, tachycardia-mediated, peripartum, myocarditis) and applying guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF. Standard GDMT includes the four pillars: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor, titrated to target doses. Diuretics for volume management. ICD for primary prevention of SCD if LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months of optimal medical therapy (allow time for recovery — some DCM is reversible). CRT if LVEF ≤35% with LBBB and QRS ≥150 ms. Anticoagulation for AF. Genetic testing and family screening recommended. Advanced therapies (LVAD, transplant) for refractory end-stage disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (worsen HF, fluid retention), nondihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil/diltiazem — negative inotropy), thiazolidinediones, most antiarrhythmics except amiodarone.

treatments.dosage: 24/26 mg BID, titrate to 97/103 mg BID
treatments.notes: HFrEF with LVEF ≤40%

treatments.dosage: 3.125 mg BID, titrate to 25 mg BID (50 mg BID if >85 kg)
treatments.notes: stable HFrEF
LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II-IV
HFrEF regardless of diabetes status
HFrEF (EF ≤40%)
HFrEF
HFrEF with EF ≤35%
HFrEF regardless of diabetes
delay in new-onset DCM to allow recovery; LMNA carriers may warrant earlier ICD

diseases.name: Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM)
diseases.management: RCM management is primarily supportive and directed at the underlying etiology (amyloidosis, hemochromatosis, sarcoidosis, radiation, storage diseases). Judicious diuretic use is essential — overdiuresis in a nondistensible ventricle causes hypotension and low-output state. Sodium restriction (2–4 g/day) and fluid restriction (2 L/day). Rhythm control for AF is preferred over rate control (atrial contraction is critical for diastolic filling). Beta-blockers and ACEi/ARBs are often poorly tolerated due to hypotension. Anticoagulation for AF or intracardiac thrombus. No pharmacological therapy has been shown to improve diastolic filling or prolong survival except disease-specific treatments and heart transplantation. ICD for arrhythmia risk in selected patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Digoxin (binds to amyloid fibrils — toxicity at therapeutic levels in amyloidosis), CCBs (negative inotropy), beta-blockers (often poorly tolerated), overdiuresis.

treatments.useWhen: volume overload; use cautiously to avoid overdiuresis

treatments.notes: transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM)
ATTR cardiac amyloidosis
AF or intracardiac thrombus

diseases.name: Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR)
diseases.management: No disease-modifying medical therapy exists for TR (no Class I medical recommendations). Management focuses on treating the underlying cause of secondary TR (LV dysfunction → GDMT for HFrEF; pulmonary hypertension → pulmonary vasodilators; AF → rhythm control). Diuretics (loop ± MRA) for systemic congestion (Class IIa). Surgical repair is recommended when TR is severe and the patient is undergoing left-sided valve surgery. Isolated TV surgery or transcatheter intervention (TEER, TVIVO) may be considered for severe symptomatic TR refractory to medical therapy at experienced centers.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Overdiuresis (can worsen low-output state), excessive volume depletion.

treatments.dosage: furosemide 40–160 mg daily or bumetanide 1–4 mg daily

treatments.notes: right-sided congestion

treatments.duration: severe TR at time of left-sided surgery, or isolated severe symptomatic TR
treatments.notes: repair preferred over replacement when feasible

diseases.name: Pericardial Effusion
diseases.management: Management depends on size, hemodynamic significance, and etiology. Cardiac tamponade requires emergent pericardiocentesis. Non-hemodynamically significant effusions are managed by treating the underlying cause (viral/idiopathic → NSAIDs + colchicine; uremic → dialysis; malignant → drainage ± sclerotherapy or pericardial window; hypothyroid → thyroid replacement; autoimmune → immunosuppression). Moderate-large effusions without tamponade may be monitored with serial echo if stable. Recurrent effusions may require pericardial window or pericardiectomy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroids as first-line (increase recurrence risk); anticoagulants if hemorrhagic effusion.

treatments.notes: idiopathic/viral pericarditis with effusion

treatments.dosage: 0.5 mg BID (0.5 mg daily if 70 kg)

treatments.useWhen: cardiac tamponade or large symptomatic effusion

treatments.medication: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600–800 mg TID or aspirin 650–1000 mg TID)
treatments.notes: inflammatory/idiopathic effusion

treatments.duration: recurrent effusion or loculated effusion not amenable to percutaneous drainage

diseases.name: Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF)
diseases.management: Goals are eradication of inciting GAS infection, symptomatic management of arthritis/carditis/chorea, and initiation of secondary prophylaxis. Diagnosis via revised Jones criteria. Hospitalization recommended for initial management. Penicillin (single IM dose of benzathine penicillin G preferred) to eradicate GAS. NSAIDs (naproxen preferred over aspirin) for arthritis — rapid response expected; taper after 1–2 weeks. Corticosteroids (prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day, max 80 mg) for severe carditis with heart failure, though evidence for preventing long-term valve damage is lacking. Secondary penicillin prophylaxis is the cornerstone of preventing recurrence and RHD progression — duration depends on severity of cardiac involvement.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aspirin in children (Reye syndrome risk — naproxen preferred). Corticosteroids as routine (no proven benefit for preventing RHD).

treatments.dosage: 1.2 million units IM × 1 dose (600,000 units if 27 kg)
treatments.notes: acute GAS eradication

treatments.dosage: adults 250–500 mg BID; children 10–20 mg/kg/day divided BID (max 1000 mg/day)
treatments.notes: arthritis/arthralgia

treatments.useWhen: severe carditis with heart failure; does not prevent RHD

treatments.notes: eradicate GAS pharyngitis
if adherence concern
arthritis and fever
severe carditis with heart failure

diseases.name: Long QT Syndrome (LQTS)
diseases.management: Management includes lifestyle modification (avoid QT-prolonging drugs per CredibleMeds.org, maintain adequate potassium levels, avoid competitive sports in most), beta-blocker therapy (cornerstone — nadolol or propranolol preferred; metoprolol less effective), and genotype-guided adjunctive therapy. For LQT3, mexiletine (sodium channel blocker) may shorten QT. Left cardiac sympathetic denervation (LCSD) for patients with recurrent events despite beta-blockers. ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or recurrent syncope despite medical therapy. Genetic counseling and family screening recommended.
diseases.notes: Avoid: QT-prolonging drugs (see CredibleMeds.org), metoprolol (less effective than nadolol), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, sudden loud noises (LQT2 trigger), strenuous swimming (LQT1 trigger).

treatments.notes: LQT3 genotype; shortens QT interval

treatments.useWhen: cardiac arrest survivor, recurrent syncope despite beta-blocker

treatments.notes: all symptomatic LQTS and most asymptomatic
LQT3 specifically

treatments.duration: recurrent events despite beta-blocker + ICD, or ICD contraindicated

diseases.name: Brugada Syndrome
diseases.management: Brugada syndrome is a sodium channelopathy (SCN5A mutations in ~20–30%) with characteristic coved ST elevation in V1-V3 and risk of VF/SCD. No pharmacological therapy has been proven to prevent SCD. ICD is the only proven therapy for secondary prevention (cardiac arrest survivors) and is considered for high-risk patients (spontaneous type 1 pattern with syncope). Quinidine (Ito blocker) may be used to reduce arrhythmia burden in patients with recurrent ICD shocks or as alternative when ICD is refused/contraindicated. Isoproterenol for electrical storm. Catheter ablation of epicardial substrate in refractory cases. Avoid fever (unmasks/worsens pattern — treat aggressively with antipyretics), avoid sodium channel blockers, avoid excessive alcohol.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone, ajmaline — worsen/unmask Brugada), fever (treat aggressively), excessive alcohol, cocaine, large meals, vagotonic states. Class IC antiarrhythmics are contraindicated.

treatments.notes: cardiac arrest survivor or sustained VT

treatments.useWhen: recurrent ICD shocks, arrhythmia storm, or ICD refused/contraindicated

treatments.notes: VF storm, recurrent ICD shocks, ICD refused/contraindicated, or asymptomatic with spontaneous Type 1
targets abnormal epicardial substrate in RVOT; eliminates Type 1 pattern in >75%

diseases.name: Cor Pulmonale
diseases.management: Cor pulmonale is right heart failure secondary to pulmonary hypertension from lung disease. Treatment targets the underlying pulmonary condition (COPD → bronchodilators, oxygen; ILD → antifibrotics; OSA → CPAP; PE → anticoagulation). Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO₂ >90% (reduces hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction). Diuretics for volume overload (cautious — RV is preload-dependent). Pulmonary vasodilators (PDE5 inhibitors, ERA, prostanoids) only for WHO Group 1 PAH — generally NOT indicated for Group 3 (lung disease-related PH) outside of clinical trials. Digoxin may be considered for rate control in AF. Transplant referral for advanced disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Excessive diuresis (RV is preload-dependent), nondihydropyridine CCBs (negative inotropy), pulmonary vasodilators in Group 3 PH (may worsen V/Q mismatch).

treatments.notes: resting PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg or ≤59 mmHg with cor pulmonale/polycythemia
volume overload, hepatic congestion
bronchodilators, ICS, pulmonary rehab, smoking cessation for COPD

diseases.name: Bronchiectasis (Non-CF)
diseases.management: Management includes identifying and treating underlying causes (immunodeficiency → Ig replacement; ABPA → steroids + antifungals; NTM → targeted therapy), airway clearance techniques (taught by physiotherapist — active cycle breathing, oscillatory PEP devices, chest physiotherapy), and mucoactive agents (nebulized hypertonic saline 7% or mannitol) for patients with difficulty expectorating. Pulmonary rehabilitation for impaired exercise capacity. For patients at high risk of exacerbations: long-term macrolide therapy (azithromycin 250 mg daily or 500 mg 3×/week — strong recommendation per 2025 ERS guidelines). Inhaled antibiotics for chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection. Acute exacerbations treated with 14 days of antibiotics guided by prior sputum cultures. Surgical resection for localized disease refractory to medical therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Inhaled corticosteroids (unless concurrent asthma/COPD — may promote airway infections including NTM), routine oral corticosteroids.

treatments.notes: ≥3 exacerbations/year or high disease activity; check baseline ECG, audiogram, NTM cultures

Pseudomonas aeruginosa exacerbation

diseases.name: Cystic Fibrosis (CF)
diseases.management: CF management is multidisciplinary (pulmonologist, dietitian, respiratory therapist, social worker). CFTR modulator therapy has transformed outcomes — elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (Trikafta) is indicated for ~90% of patients ≥2 years with at least one F508del allele. Pulmonary therapies: mucolytics (dornase alfa 2.5 mg nebulized daily), hypertonic saline (7% nebulized daily), airway clearance techniques, anti-inflammatory therapy (azithromycin 3×/week), and inhaled antibiotics for chronic Pseudomonas (tobramycin 28-day on/off cycles). Pancreatic enzyme replacement for exocrine insufficiency. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K). CF-related diabetes screening and management. Nutritional optimization (high-calorie, high-fat diet). Lung transplant for end-stage disease. Median survival now >53 years.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aminoglycosides without monitoring (nephro/ototoxicity), prolonged systemic corticosteroids (growth suppression, diabetes), inhaled corticosteroids without indication.

treatments.notes: all CF patients ≥6 years

diseases.name: Pertussis (Whooping Cough)
diseases.management: Three phases: catarrhal (1–2 weeks, URI-like), paroxysmal (2–8 weeks, spasmodic cough with whoop/posttussive vomiting), convalescent (weeks-months). Diagnosis by PCR nasopharyngeal swab (preferred over culture). Antibiotics do NOT shorten disease course but reduce transmission — treat within 21 days of cough onset. Azithromycin is preferred. Supportive care for cough (no proven symptomatic treatment). Postexposure prophylaxis for household contacts and high-risk individuals (infants, pregnant women). Prevention: Tdap vaccination (all adolescents/adults ≥11 years; all pregnant women at 27–36 weeks each pregnancy).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Erythromycin in infants 21 days after cough onset (no benefit), cough suppressants (ineffective).

treatments.dosage: adults: 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2–5; children: 10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2–5
treatments.useWhen: confirmed or suspected pertussis within 21 days of cough onset; also for postexposure prophylaxis

treatments.dosage: adults: 500 mg BID; children: 7.5 mg/kg BID
adults: 160/800 mg BID; children: 8/40 mg/kg/day divided BID

diseases.name: Acute Bronchitis
diseases.management: Acute bronchitis is a self-limited viral infection of the bronchi lasting 1–3 weeks. Diagnosis is clinical (cough ± sputum production, no pneumonia on exam). Chest X-ray only if pneumonia suspected. Treatment is supportive: analgesics/antipyretics, adequate hydration, honey for cough (adults/children >1 year). Antibiotics are NOT indicated (viral etiology in >90%). Bronchodilators may provide modest benefit if wheezing present. Antitussives (dextromethorphan) have limited evidence but may be tried for severe cough disrupting sleep.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antibiotics (no benefit, promotes resistance), routine corticosteroids.

treatments.notes: cough suppression
fever, myalgia, discomfort

diseases.name: Lung Abscess
diseases.management: Most lung abscesses are caused by aspiration of oropharyngeal anaerobes. Prolonged antibiotic therapy is the mainstay — most resolve with antibiotics alone (>80%). CT chest for diagnosis. Clindamycin or ampicillin-sulbactam are first-line. Duration typically 4–6 weeks or until radiographic resolution. Percutaneous or bronchoscopic drainage for abscesses >6 cm or failing to respond after 7–14 days of antibiotics. Surgical resection rarely needed.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Metronidazole monotherapy (poor activity against microaerophilic streptococci often co-present).

treatments.dosage: 600 mg IV TID → 300 mg PO QID

treatments.duration: initial, then transition to amoxicillin-clavulanate PO; total 4–6+ weeks
treatments.notes: primary lung abscess

treatments.dosage: 600 mg IV every 8 hours, then 300–450 mg PO QID
treatments.notes: penicillin allergy

treatments.dosage: 500 mg TID + penicillin G 2 MU every 4 hours
treatments.notes: anaerobic coverage

treatments.duration: abscess >6 cm or failing antibiotics after 7–14 days
treatments.notes: CT-guided; risk of bronchopleural fistula and empyema

diseases.name: Pleurisy (Pleuritis)
diseases.management: Pleurisy is inflammation of the pleura causing sharp, localized chest pain worsened by breathing/coughing. Most commonly viral or idiopathic. Evaluate for underlying cause (PE, pneumonia, TB, autoimmune, malignancy). Treatment is directed at the underlying cause plus symptomatic pain management with NSAIDs. Colchicine may be added if recurrent or associated with pericarditis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids as first-line (respiratory depression, mask symptoms).

treatments.notes: autoimmune pleurisy refractory to NSAIDs

diseases.name: Massive Hemoptysis
diseases.management: Massive hemoptysis (>500 mL/24 hr or >100 mL/hr, or any amount causing hemodynamic instability or respiratory compromise) is a life-threatening emergency. Priorities: (1) Protect airway — intubate with large ETT (≥8.0), position bleeding lung dependent. (2) Stabilize hemodynamics — IV access, blood products. (3) Localize bleeding — urgent bronchoscopy (rigid preferred for massive bleeding). (4) Control bleeding — bronchial artery embolization (BAE) is the first-line definitive intervention (>90% immediate success). (5) Surgery (lobectomy/pneumonectomy) for BAE failure or recurrence. Treat underlying cause (TB, bronchiectasis, malignancy, AVM). IV tranexamic acid (1 g) and nebulized tranexamic acid (500 mg) may be used as temporizing measures.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticoagulants (hold/reverse), delay in airway protection.

treatments.useWhen: localized bleeding source on CT angiography
temporizing measure while arranging definitive therapy

treatments.notes: position bleeding lung dependent; selective mainstem intubation if needed
success 70–90%; recurrence 10–30% at 1 year

treatments.duration: failed BAE, recurrent hemoptysis, localized disease
treatments.notes: lobectomy preferred; high mortality in emergency setting

diseases.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)
diseases.management: PBC is an autoimmune cholestatic liver disease characterized by destruction of small intrahepatic bile ducts, predominantly affecting women. Diagnosis: cholestatic LFTs (elevated ALP) + positive antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA, >95% sensitivity). UDCA (13–15 mg/kg/day) is first-line for all patients — improves transplant-free survival (NNT 11 at 5 years). Assess biochemical response at 12 months (ALP 1.67× ULN, ≥15% ALP reduction, normal bilirubin). For inadequate responders: add elafibranor or seladelpar (PPAR agonists, FDA-approved 2024). Obeticholic acid was withdrawn from US market (2025) due to safety concerns. Manage pruritus (cholestyramine first-line, rifampin second-line, naltrexone, sertraline). DEXA every 2 years for bone density. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation if cholestasis is severe. Liver transplant for decompensated disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Obeticholic acid (withdrawn from US market 2025). Avoid cholestyramine within 4 hr of UDCA or other medications.

treatments.useWhen: all PBC patients with elevated ALP
inadequate response to UDCA or UDCA intolerant; also improves pruritus; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis

treatments.notes: pruritus; give 4–6 hr apart from other medications

diseases.name: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
diseases.management: PSC is a chronic cholestatic liver disease characterized by inflammation and fibrosis of intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts, strongly associated with IBD (60–80% have UC). No medical therapy has been proven to alter disease progression. UDCA (13–15 mg/kg/day) is now recommended as first-line treatment per recent guidelines, though high-dose UDCA (28–30 mg/kg/day) should be avoided (associated with worse outcomes). ERCP with balloon dilation for dominant strictures causing cholestasis. Screen for cholangiocarcinoma (annual MRI/MRCP + CA 19-9), gallbladder cancer (annual ultrasound), and colorectal cancer (annual colonoscopy if concurrent IBD). Liver transplant is the only definitive treatment for advanced disease — excellent outcomes (5-year survival >80%), but recurrence occurs in ~20%.
diseases.notes: Avoid: High-dose UDCA (28–30 mg/kg/day — associated with worse outcomes in PSC). No proven disease-modifying pharmacotherapy beyond standard-dose UDCA.

treatments.useWhen: all PSC patients; do NOT use high-dose (28–30 mg/kg/day)
dominant stricture with cholestasis or cholangitis

treatments.notes: dominant strictures (>1.5 mm CBD or >1 mm intrahepatic); short-term stenting if needed; brush cytology to rule out CCA

diseases.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis (AIH)
diseases.management: AIH is a chronic inflammatory liver disease characterized by elevated transaminases, hypergammaglobulinemia (elevated IgG), positive autoantibodies (ANA, SMA, anti-LKM1), and interface hepatitis on biopsy. Treatment is indicated when AST/ALT >2× ULN with elevated IgG, or any bridging/multilobular necrosis on biopsy. Standard induction: prednisone (or prednisolone) 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) ± azathioprine 50 mg/day (AASLD) or azathioprine started 2 weeks later (EASL). Taper steroids over 4–8 weeks to lowest effective dose. Azathioprine maintenance: 1–2 mg/kg/day. Remission achieved in ~85% (normal ALT, IgG, and histology). Budesonide 9 mg/day + azathioprine is an alternative for non-cirrhotic patients to reduce steroid side effects. Second-line for azathioprine intolerance: mycophenolate mofetil 1–1.5 g BID. Check TPMT before starting azathioprine. Attempt treatment withdrawal only after ≥2 years of complete biochemical and histologic remission — relapse rate is 50–90%.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Abrupt steroid withdrawal (high relapse risk), azathioprine without TPMT testing, budesonide in cirrhosis (first-pass metabolism lost).

treatments.dosage: prednisone 30–60 mg/day (taper to 5–10 mg) + azathioprine 50–150 mg/day (1–2 mg/kg)
budesonide 9 mg/day (taper to 3–6 mg) + azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day

diseases.name: Achalasia
diseases.management: Achalasia is an esophageal motility disorder characterized by absent peristalsis and impaired LES relaxation. Diagnosis by high-resolution manometry (Chicago Classification v4.0 — Types I, II, III). Definitive treatment options: pneumatic dilation (PD), laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) with partial fundoplication, or peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM). Type II achalasia responds best to all treatments. Type III (spastic) responds best to POEM (allows extended myotomy). PD and LHM have similar long-term efficacy (~90% at 2 years). POEM has excellent short-term results but higher rates of GERD (40–60%). Botulinum toxin injection into LES is a temporizing option for patients unfit for surgery/dilation (effect lasts 6–12 months). Pharmacologic therapy (nifedipine, isosorbide dinitrate) is least effective — reserved for patients who refuse or cannot undergo other interventions.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term pharmacologic therapy as sole treatment (inferior outcomes).

treatments.useWhen: Type I or II achalasia; perforation risk 1–3%

treatments.dosage: surgical myotomy with Dor or Toupet fundoplication
treatments.useWhen: Type I or II achalasia; preferred in younger patients

diseases.name: Gastroparesis
diseases.management: Gastroparesis is delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction. Most common etiologies: diabetic (30%), postsurgical (13%), idiopathic (36%). Diagnosis by 4-hr gastric emptying scintigraphy (>10% retention at 4 hr). Management: (1) Dietary — small particle diet, small frequent meals, low fat/fiber. (2) Glycemic control in diabetics. (3) Prokinetics — metoclopramide is the only FDA-approved drug (black box warning for tardive dyskinesia — limit to 12 weeks if possible). Domperidone available via FDA compassionate use. 5-HT4 agonists (prucalopride) may improve gastric emptying. (4) Antiemetics for symptom control. (5) Refractory cases: G-POEM (gastric peroral endoscopic myotomy) or gastric electrical stimulation (GES, humanitarian device). Intrapyloric botulinum toxin is NOT recommended (negative RCTs).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Intrapyloric botulinum toxin (not effective per RCTs), opioids (worsen gastroparesis), GLP-1 receptor agonists (delay gastric emptying), anticholinergics.

treatments.dosage: 5–10 mg PO/IV 30 min before meals and at bedtime
treatments.duration: shortest effective duration (black box: tardive dyskinesia)

diseases.name: Mallory-Weiss Tear
diseases.management: Mallory-Weiss tears are mucosal lacerations at the GEJ, typically from forceful vomiting/retching. Most (80–90%) stop bleeding spontaneously. Supportive care with IV fluids, antiemetics, and PPI. EGD for persistent or significant bleeding — endoscopic hemostasis (epinephrine injection, hemoclips, electrocautery). Angiographic embolization or surgery for refractory bleeding (rare).
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs, anticoagulants during active bleeding.

treatments.dosage: pantoprazole 40 mg IV BID or omeprazole 40 mg PO daily

treatments.useWhen: active or persistent bleeding on EGD

treatments.medication: PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hour infusion)

diseases.name: Gastric Outlet Obstruction (GOO)
diseases.management: GOO can be benign (PUD, caustic ingestion, Crohn) or malignant (pancreatic, gastric, duodenal cancer). Initial management: NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, correct electrolytes (hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis). PPI for PUD-related GOO. EGD for diagnosis and endoscopic balloon dilation (benign strictures — 12–15 mm, serial dilations). Malignant GOO: endoscopic stenting (SEMS) for palliation or surgical bypass (gastrojejunostomy). Endoscopic ultrasound-guided gastroenterostomy (EUS-GE) is emerging as an alternative.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oral intake before decompression, delay in identifying malignant etiology.

treatments.notes: PUD-related GOO

treatments.medication: NG decompression + IV fluids + electrolyte correction

diseases.name: Short Bowel Syndrome (SBS)
diseases.management: SBS results from extensive small bowel resection (200 cm remaining) causing malabsorption. Management phases: (1) Acute (weeks 1–4): IV fluids, TPN, correct electrolytes, PPI (gastric hypersecretion). (2) Adaptation (months 1–24): gradual enteral feeding advancement, oral rehydration solutions, dietary optimization (complex carbs, moderate fat, small frequent meals). (3) Maintenance: wean TPN as tolerated. Antidiarrheal agents (loperamide, codeine). Teduglutide (GLP-2 analog, FDA-approved) promotes intestinal adaptation and reduces TPN dependence. Cholestyramine for bile acid diarrhea (only if colon in continuity). Monitor for complications: D-lactic acidosis, oxalate kidney stones, hepatobiliary disease, catheter-related infections.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Hypotonic fluids (worsen secretory losses — use ORS), high-oxalate foods (if colon in continuity), abrupt TPN discontinuation.

treatments.notes: TPN-dependent SBS; monitor for intestinal polyps (colonoscopy before and during treatment)

treatments.dosage: 2–4 mg before meals and at bedtime

treatments.useWhen: gastric hypersecretion in early post-resection period

treatments.notes: all SBS-IF patients

diseases.name: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease)
diseases.management: IgA nephropathy is the most common immune-mediated GN worldwide. Per KDIGO 2025 guidelines, treatment targets proteinuria 0.5 g/day (ideally 0.3 g/day) and eGFR decline 1 mL/min/year. All patients: lifestyle modifications (sodium 2 g/day, smoking cessation, weight control, exercise), BP target ≤120/70 mmHg. Pharmacologic: (1) RAS blockade (ACEi/ARB at max tolerated dose) or sparsentan (dual endothelin-angiotensin receptor antagonist — alternative to RASi). (2) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) in combination with RASi/sparsentan. (3) Targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon/Tarpeyo 16 mg/day × 9 months) to reduce pathogenic IgA production — for patients with proteinuria ≥0.75 g/day despite ≥3 months of optimized supportive care. (4) Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day × 2 months then taper) as alternative if Nefecon unavailable. (5) Iptacopan (complement factor B inhibitor, FDA-approved 2025) for proteinuria reduction.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dual ACEi + ARB (hyperkalemia, no additional benefit per STOP-IgAN), fish oil (insufficient evidence per KDIGO 2025), tonsillectomy (no proven benefit outside Japan), mycophenolate in non-Chinese populations (limited evidence)

treatments.dosage: ramipril 10 mg daily or losartan 100 mg daily
maximally tolerated dose (e.g., ramipril 10 mg daily, losartan 100 mg daily)
treatments.notes: all patients with proteinuria ≥0.5 g/day

diseases.name: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD)
diseases.management: ADPKD (PKD1/PKD2 mutations) is the most common inherited kidney disease. Management focuses on slowing cyst growth and preserving renal function. Tolvaptan (V2 receptor antagonist) is the only FDA-approved disease-modifying therapy — indicated for patients at risk of rapid progression (total kidney volume >750 mL, eGFR decline >5 mL/min/year, Mayo class 1C-1E). BP target 110/75 mmHg (HALT-PKD trial) with ACEi/ARB preferred. Adequate hydration (>3 L/day) to suppress vasopressin. Sodium restriction (2 g/day). Avoid caffeine (stimulates cyst growth via cAMP). Screen for intracranial aneurysms (MRA) if family history of SAH/aneurysm. Monitor for complications: cyst infection (fluoroquinolones penetrate cysts), cyst hemorrhage, nephrolithiasis, liver cysts. Renal replacement therapy when ESRD develops.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (nephrotoxic), caffeine, metformin in advanced CKD, IV contrast without hydration.

treatments.dosage: 45/15 mg AM/PM, titrate to 60/30 then 90/30 mg
treatments.notes: rapidly progressive ADPKD; REMS program; monitor LFTs monthly × 18 months then Q3 months; hepatotoxicity risk
all ADPKD patients; BP target 110/75 mmHg

diseases.name: Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA)
diseases.management: RTA is classified into three major types. Type 1 (distal): impaired acid excretion → non-anion gap metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, urine pH >5.5, nephrocalcinosis/nephrolithiasis. Treatment: alkali therapy (sodium bicarbonate or potassium citrate 1–3 mEq/kg/day) to maintain serum bicarb 23–28 mEq/L. Type 2 (proximal): impaired bicarbonate reabsorption → requires higher alkali doses (10–15 mEq/kg/day), often with thiazide diuretic to enhance proximal reabsorption. Type 4 (hyperkalemic): aldosterone deficiency/resistance → hyperkalemia with mild acidosis. Treatment: correct hyperkalemia (dietary K restriction, fludrocortisone 0.1–0.2 mg/day if hypoaldosteronism, potassium binders), low-dose alkali if needed.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Potassium-sparing diuretics in Type 4 RTA, acetazolamide in Type 1 RTA (worsens acidosis).

treatments.useWhen: distal RTA; provides alkali + potassium replacement; reduces nephrolithiasis
proximal RTA; higher doses needed due to urinary bicarbonate wasting

diseases.name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)
diseases.management: BPH management is guided by symptom severity (IPSS/AUA-SI score). Mild symptoms (IPSS ≤7): watchful waiting with lifestyle modifications (fluid management, bladder training, reduce caffeine/alcohol). Moderate symptoms (IPSS 8–19): pharmacotherapy — alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, silodosin) are first-line for rapid symptom relief; 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) for prostate >30–40 g to reduce prostate volume and prevent progression. Combination therapy (alpha-blocker + 5-ARI) for moderate-severe symptoms with enlarged prostate. PDE5 inhibitors (tadalafil 5 mg daily) for concurrent LUTS and ED. Severe/refractory symptoms: surgical intervention — TURP remains gold standard; alternatives include HoLEP, UroLift, Rezūm, aquablation, prostatic artery embolization.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticholinergics in patients with significant PVR (>250 mL), alpha-blockers before cataract surgery (intraoperative floppy iris syndrome — inform ophthalmologist).

treatments.notes: prostate >30–40 g; reduces prostate volume 20–30%
IPSS ≥8 with prostate >30–40 g
prostate ≥30 cc
large prostate with moderate-severe LUTS

treatments.duration: refractory symptoms, urinary retention, CKD, bladder stones
treatments.notes: TURP: gold standard; HoLEP: preferred for large prostates (>80 cc); IPSS improvement 10–15 points

diseases.name: Testicular Torsion
diseases.management: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. The spermatic cord twists, causing venous congestion and arterial ischemia. Peak incidence in adolescence. Diagnosis is clinical (acute severe unilateral scrotal pain, high-riding testicle, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign). TWIST score guides management. High clinical suspicion → immediate surgical exploration without imaging. Intermediate suspicion → color Doppler ultrasound (sensitivity ~100%, specificity ~98%). Surgery within 6 hours → ~90% salvage rate; >12 hours → salvage drops to 24 hours → near 0%. Manual detorsion ("open the book" — lateral rotation) may be attempted as a temporizing measure while awaiting surgery. Bilateral orchiopexy is performed (contralateral fixation to prevent future torsion).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay (every hour matters), reliance on imaging when clinical suspicion is high.

treatments.useWhen: all suspected testicular torsion; fix both testes

treatments.notes: bilateral orchiopexy (both testes fixed); salvage rate ~90% if 6 hours; orchiectomy if nonviable
improves salvage; confirm with Doppler US after; does NOT replace surgery; all patients still need bilateral orchiopexy

diseases.name: Priapism
diseases.management: Ischemic (low-flow) priapism is a urologic emergency — painful, rigid erection >4 hours with no blood flow. Most common in sickle cell disease, PDE5 inhibitor use, intracavernosal injections. Treatment is time-sensitive: aspiration of corporal blood (dark, deoxygenated) + injection of phenylephrine (alpha-agonist) into corpus cavernosum. If phenylephrine fails → surgical shunt (distal first: Winter, T-shunt; then proximal if needed). Non-ischemic (high-flow) priapism is typically post-traumatic, painless, partially rigid — observation is appropriate; angiographic embolization if persistent.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Terbutaline (insufficient evidence), delay >24–36 hr (irreversible fibrosis/ED).

treatments.dosage: aspirate 20–30 mL blood, then inject phenylephrine 100–500 mcg every 3–5 min (max 1 mg in 1 hr)
treatments.useWhen: ischemic priapism >4 hr; monitor BP and HR

diseases.name: Erectile Dysfunction (ED)
diseases.management: ED evaluation should include cardiovascular risk assessment (ED is an independent predictor of CVD). Identify and treat reversible causes (medications, hypogonadism, depression, diabetes, lifestyle factors). First-line: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil, avanafil). Second-line: intracavernosal injection (alprostadil, trimix), vacuum erection device, intraurethral alprostadil. Third-line: penile prosthesis implantation. Testosterone replacement only if documented hypogonadism (total T 300 ng/dL on two morning samples).
diseases.notes: Avoid: PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates (severe hypotension — absolute contraindication), testosterone without documented hypogonadism.

treatments.dosage: 10–20 mg as needed or 2.5–5 mg daily
treatments.notes: ED ± BPH/LUTS

diseases.name: Urinary Incontinence
diseases.management: Classify type: stress (leakage with cough/exertion), urgency (OAB — sudden urge with leakage), mixed, or overflow. Stress UI: first-line is pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises × 3 months). Pessary for women. Surgical options: midurethral sling (gold standard for women), bulking agents. Urgency UI/OAB: behavioral therapy (bladder training, timed voiding) first-line. Pharmacotherapy: antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin) or beta-3 agonist (mirabegron, vibegron — preferred in elderly due to lower anticholinergic burden). Third-line: onabotulinumtoxinA 100 units intravesical, sacral neuromodulation, PTNS.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antimuscarinics in elderly with cognitive impairment (Beers criteria), antimuscarinics with narrow-angle glaucoma.

treatments.notes: OAB/urgency UI; avoid in elderly (anticholinergic burden, cognitive impairment)

diseases.name: Interstitial Cystitis / Bladder Pain Syndrome (IC/BPS)
diseases.management: IC/BPS presents with chronic pelvic pain, pressure, or discomfort perceived to be related to the bladder, with urinary frequency/urgency, lasting >6 weeks without infection. AUA guidelines recommend stepwise approach: First-line: patient education, dietary modification (avoid triggers: caffeine, alcohol, acidic foods, artificial sweeteners), stress management, pelvic floor physical therapy. Second-line: oral medications (amitriptyline 25–75 mg nightly, hydroxyzine 25–75 mg nightly, cimetidine 400 mg BID, pentosan polysulfate 100 mg TID — note FDA warning for pigmentary maculopathy with long-term PPS use). Third-line: cystoscopy with hydrodistension, intravesical instillations (DMSO, heparin/lidocaine cocktails). Fourth-line: sacral neuromodulation, intravesical botulinum toxin. Fifth-line: cyclosporine. Sixth-line: urinary diversion (last resort).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term pentosan polysulfate without ophthalmologic monitoring, opioids for chronic pain management.

treatments.useWhen: IC/BPS; FDA warning for pigmentary maculopathy — ophthalmologic monitoring recommended

treatments.dosage: 50 mL of 50% DMSO instilled for 15 min

diseases.name: Acromegaly
diseases.management: Acromegaly results from excess GH secretion, usually from a pituitary somatotroph adenoma. Goals: normalize IGF-1, control tumor mass, manage comorbidities. First-line: transsphenoidal surgery (TSS) — curative in ~55% overall (78% microadenomas, 53% macroadenomas). Medical therapy for persistent/recurrent disease or when surgery is not feasible: somatostatin receptor ligands (SRLs — octreotide LAR, lanreotide depot) are first-line medical therapy. Pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist) for SRL-resistant disease. Cabergoline for mildly elevated IGF-1 (2× ULN). Oral octreotide and paltusotine (oral SSTR2 agonist, FDA-approved 2025) for patients controlled on injectable SRLs who prefer oral therapy. Combination therapy for inadequate monotherapy response. Radiation therapy (stereotactic radiosurgery) for residual/recurrent disease. Manage comorbidities: diabetes, HTN, cardiomyopathy, sleep apnea, arthropathy, colonoscopy screening.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in treatment (irreversible skeletal/joint changes), high-dose cabergoline without cardiac monitoring (valvulopathy risk at doses used in Parkinson disease — lower risk at acromegaly doses).

treatments.useWhen: SRL-resistant acromegaly; normalizes IGF-1 in ~90% but does not shrink tumor
adults with inadequate response to surgery or inoperable; oral alternative to injectable SRLs

treatments.notes: curative in 73% micro, 61% macro; experienced pituitary surgeon essential; complications: hypopituitarism (5–10%), CSF leak, DI

diseases.name: Prolactinoma
diseases.management: Prolactinomas are the most common functioning pituitary adenomas. Dopamine agonists are first-line treatment (not surgery) — normalize prolactin in ~80–90% and reduce tumor size in ~70%. Cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine (more effective, better tolerated, twice-weekly dosing). Surgery (TSS) is reserved for dopamine agonist resistance/intolerance, cystic tumors, or patient preference (especially microprolactinomas with high surgical cure rate ~76%). Medication withdrawal can be attempted after ≥2 years of treatment if prolactin is normal and tumor has significantly shrunk (>50% reduction). Monitor MRI and prolactin levels. Women with microprolactinomas not desiring fertility may be treated with oral contraceptives instead.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine agonists in patients with impulse control disorders or psychosis. High-dose cabergoline (>2 mg/week) without echocardiographic monitoring (cardiac valvulopathy risk).

treatments.dosage: 0.25 mg twice weekly, titrate up to 1–2 mg/week based on prolactin
treatments.useWhen: all prolactinomas; more effective and better tolerated than bromocriptine

diseases.name: Hypopituitarism
diseases.management: Hypopituitarism is deficiency of one or more pituitary hormones. Causes: pituitary adenoma/surgery/radiation, Sheehan syndrome, traumatic brain injury, infiltrative disease. Treatment is hormone replacement of each deficient axis. Cortisol axis is most critical — adrenal crisis can be fatal. Replace in order of clinical priority: (1) Glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone) — MUST be replaced before thyroid hormone (thyroid replacement without cortisol can precipitate adrenal crisis). (2) Thyroid hormone (levothyroxine). (3) Sex steroids (testosterone or estrogen/progesterone). (4) GH replacement (if symptomatic and confirmed deficiency). (5) Desmopressin for central DI. Stress-dose steroids for illness/surgery.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Starting levothyroxine before glucocorticoid replacement (risk of adrenal crisis).

treatments.dosage: 15–25 mg/day divided BID-TID (10 mg AM, 5 mg PM typical)
treatments.notes: secondary adrenal insufficiency; stress dosing for illness

diseases.name: Carcinoid Syndrome
diseases.management: Carcinoid syndrome results from serotonin and other vasoactive substances secreted by neuroendocrine tumors (usually midgut NETs with hepatic metastases). Symptoms: flushing, diarrhea, wheezing, right-sided valvular heart disease (carcinoid heart disease). Diagnosis: 24-hr urine 5-HIAA, chromogranin A. Treatment: somatostatin analogs (octreotide LAR, lanreotide) are first-line for symptom control AND antiproliferative effect (PROMID, CLARINET trials). Telotristat ethyl (tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor) for refractory diarrhea despite SSA. Surgical debulking/hepatic-directed therapy for liver metastases. Echocardiography screening for carcinoid heart disease. Carcinoid crisis prevention: IV octreotide 250–500 mcg before surgery/procedures.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Triggering agents (alcohol, catecholamines, anesthesia without octreotide prophylaxis).

treatments.notes: equivalent to octreotide LAR

diseases.name: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN) Syndromes
diseases.management: MEN1 (3 P's: Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreatic NETs — menin gene): Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common, >95%) → subtotal parathyroidectomy. Pancreatic NETs (gastrinoma, insulinoma) → surgical resection if >2 cm or symptomatic. Pituitary adenomas → per standard management. MEN2A (RET proto-oncogene): Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC, ~100%) → prophylactic thyroidectomy based on RET mutation risk category. Pheochromocytoma (~50%) → adrenalectomy (alpha-blockade first). Primary hyperparathyroidism (~20–30%). MEN2B: MTC (aggressive, early) → thyroidectomy in infancy. Pheochromocytoma. Mucosal neuromas, marfanoid habitus. Genetic testing and family screening are essential for all MEN syndromes.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in genetic testing of at-risk family members, adrenalectomy without prior alpha-blockade in pheochromocytoma.

treatments.dosage: total thyroidectomy ± central lymph node dissection
treatments.notes: timing per ATA risk category: MEN2B within 6 months; high-risk RET mutations by age 5; moderate-risk may delay with calcitonin monitoring

diseases.name: Gynecomastia
diseases.management: Gynecomastia is benign enlargement of male breast tissue due to estrogen-androgen imbalance. Evaluate for underlying cause: medications (spironolactone, ketoconazole, cimetidine, marijuana, anabolic steroids), hypogonadism, liver disease, hyperthyroidism, testicular tumors (hCG-secreting). Workup: testosterone, estradiol, LH, FSH, hCG, liver/thyroid/renal function. Physiologic gynecomastia (neonatal, pubertal, senescent) is self-limited. Treatment: discontinue offending medications. If persistent and symptomatic (>12 months, painful): tamoxifen 10–20 mg daily × 3–6 months (most effective medical therapy). Surgical excision for cosmetic concerns or failed medical therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aromatase inhibitors (less effective than tamoxifen for established gynecomastia), testosterone without addressing underlying cause.

treatments.useWhen: painful or persistent gynecomastia >12 months; off-label
cosmetic concern or failed medical therapy

treatments.notes: physiologic or pubertal gynecomastia

diseases.name: Hirsutism
diseases.management: Hirsutism is excess terminal hair growth in androgen-dependent areas in women. Most common cause: PCOS. Evaluate: total/free testosterone, DHEA-S, 17-hydroxyprogesterone (rule out non-classic CAH). Treatment: combined oral contraceptives (first-line — suppress ovarian androgens) + antiandrogen (spironolactone 50–200 mg/day — most commonly used). Topical eflornithine for facial hirsutism. Laser hair removal/electrolysis for cosmetic management. Allow 6–12 months for pharmacologic effect. Finasteride and flutamide are alternatives but less commonly used due to teratogenicity/hepatotoxicity.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antiandrogens without contraception (teratogenic), finasteride in women of childbearing potential.

treatments.useWhen: add to COC for additional antiandrogen effect; must use reliable contraception (teratogenic)

treatments.dosage: apply BID to affected facial areas

treatments.notes: moderate-severe hirsutism, especially with PCOS

diseases.name: Bell Palsy
diseases.management: Bell palsy is acute idiopathic unilateral facial nerve (CN VII) paralysis involving the forehead (distinguishes from central/UMN lesion). Prognosis is good — >2/3 have complete spontaneous recovery. Treatment should begin within 72 hours of onset. Oral corticosteroids are first-line (NNT = 10 for complete recovery). Combination with antivirals may reduce synkinesis (NNT = 12). Eye protection is critical (artificial tears, lubricating ointment, taping at night) to prevent exposure keratopathy. Physical therapy for severe paralysis. No role for surgical decompression based on current evidence.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antivirals alone (ineffective without corticosteroids), delay beyond 72 hr (reduced benefit).

treatments.useWhen: combined with corticosteroids; may reduce synkinesis

treatments.dosage: artificial tears Q1–2H while awake, lubricating ointment at night, tape eyelid closed

diseases.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
diseases.management: Trigeminal neuralgia presents with unilateral, brief, electric shock-like paroxysms in the distribution of CN V, often triggered by innocuous stimuli (chewing, talking, wind). MRI brain with and without contrast is recommended in all cases to distinguish classic (neurovascular compression) from secondary (MS, tumor). Carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine are first-line pharmacotherapy — meaningful pain control in ~90% of patients. Titrate slowly to minimize side effects (dizziness, diplopia, ataxia, hyponatremia with oxcarbazepine). Second-line: lamotrigine, gabapentin, baclofen, botulinum toxin type A. Surgical options for refractory cases: microvascular decompression (MVD — most durable, ~80% long-term pain-free), percutaneous procedures (balloon compression, glycerol rhizolysis, radiofrequency thermocoagulation), or stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Abrupt withdrawal of carbamazepine (rebound pain), 40–80% cross-reactivity between carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine for allergic reactions.

treatments.useWhen: may have fewer side effects than carbamazepine; watch for hyponatremia

diseases.name: Cluster Headache
diseases.management: Cluster headache presents with severe unilateral periorbital pain lasting 15–180 min with ipsilateral autonomic features (tearing, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis). 3:1 male predominance. Treatment has three components: acute, transitional (bridge), and preventive. Acute: 100% oxygen at 12–15 L/min via non-rebreather mask AND subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg — both are first-line. Intranasal triptans (sumatriptan 20 mg, zolmitriptan 5 mg) are second-line. Preventive: verapamil is first-line (requires ECG monitoring for PR prolongation). Lithium and galcanezumab (300 mg SC monthly — FDA-approved for episodic cluster headache) are second-line. Transitional/bridge therapy: short course of prednisone (60–80 mg/day × 5 days then taper over 2 weeks) or greater occipital nerve block with corticosteroid.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oral triptans (too slow onset), opioids (ineffective), alcohol during cluster bouts (triggers attacks).

treatments.useWhen: all cluster attacks; 74% pain-free within 15 min

diseases.name: Essential Tremor
diseases.management: Essential tremor is the most common movement disorder — bilateral upper extremity action tremor present ≥3 years. Propranolol and primidone are first-line (AAN Level A evidence). Topiramate (>200 mg/day) is also clinically useful. Second-line: alprazolam, gabapentin, botulinum toxin A (especially for head/voice tremor). Surgical options for severe, medication-refractory tremor: deep brain stimulation (DBS) of ventral intermediate nucleus (VIM) of thalamus, MRI-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) thalamotomy (unilateral, FDA-approved).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Valproate (tremorgenic), lithium (tremorgenic), caffeine (worsens tremor).

treatments.useWhen: medication-refractory; ideal for patients with surgical comorbidities

treatments.notes: tremor interfering with daily activities

diseases.name: Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS) / Willis-Ekbom Disease
diseases.management: Diagnose clinically (urge to move legs, worse at rest, relieved by movement, worse in evening). Check and correct iron deficiency (ferritin 75 μg/L → oral or IV iron supplementation). First-line pharmacotherapy: alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin, gabapentin enacarbil) — preferred over dopamine agonists due to lower augmentation risk. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine patch) are second-line — effective but carry significant augmentation risk (up to 70% at 10 years). Low-dose opioids (oxycodone) for refractory cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine agonists as first-line (augmentation risk), antihistamines, SSRIs/SNRIs (worsen RLS), metoclopramide.

treatments.dosage: ferrous sulfate 325 mg + vitamin C 100 mg on empty stomach, or IV iron if oral intolerant

treatments.useWhen: alpha-2-delta ligand failure; monitor for augmentation and impulse control disorders

diseases.name: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH)
diseases.management: Classic triad: gait apraxia (magnetic gait — most prominent and first to improve), urinary incontinence, dementia. Diagnosis: ventriculomegaly on imaging (Evans index >0.3) disproportionate to sulcal enlargement. Supplementary tests: CSF tap test (removal of 30–50 mL CSF with gait assessment before and after — improvement supports diagnosis), extended lumbar drainage, or ICP monitoring. Treatment: ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt placement — 60–80% of patients improve, especially gait. Programmable valves preferred. Gait improves most reliably; cognitive improvement is variable. No effective medical therapy exists.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in referral (longer symptom duration = worse surgical outcomes), lumbar puncture without gait assessment (missed diagnostic opportunity).

treatments.useWhen: clinical triad + ventriculomegaly + positive CSF tap test

diseases.name: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)
diseases.management: ALS is a progressive, fatal neurodegenerative disease of upper and lower motor neurons. Mean survival 3–5 years from diagnosis. No curative therapy exists. Three FDA-approved disease-modifying therapies: riluzole (oral, modest survival benefit of 2–4 months), edaravone (oral suspension — Radicava ORS; IV formulation discontinued 2025), and tofersen (intrathecal, for SOD1 gene variants only). Multidisciplinary care (neurologist, PT, OT, speech therapy, respiratory therapy, dietitian, social work) improves survival by 4–7 months and quality of life. NIV for respiratory support. PEG tube for dysphagia/nutritional support. Symptom management: dextromethorphan-quinidine for pseudobulbar affect, baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity, anticholinergics for sialorrhea.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in multidisciplinary referral, delay in NIV initiation (start when FVC 50% or symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation).

treatments.useWhen: all ALS patients; monitor LFTs; modest survival benefit

treatments.dosage: 105 mg PO daily for 10–14 days per 28-day cycle

treatments.useWhen: SOD1 pathogenic variants only (~20% of familial ALS)

diseases.name: Peripheral Neuropathy
diseases.management: Identify and treat underlying cause (diabetes — most common, B12 deficiency, alcohol, hypothyroidism, medications, autoimmune, paraproteinemia). Workup: CBC, CMP, HbA1c, B12, TSH, SPEP/UPEP, ESR/CRP. EMG/NCS to characterize (axonal vs. demyelinating, sensory vs. motor). Symptomatic treatment of neuropathic pain: first-line agents include gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, and TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline). Topical capsaicin 8% patch or lidocaine 5% patch for localized pain. Combination therapy if monotherapy inadequate.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids as first-line, neurotoxic medications if possible (metformin → check B12, statins, chemotherapy).

diseases.name: Brain Abscess
diseases.management: Brain abscess is a focal suppurative infection. Sources: contiguous spread (sinusitis, otitis, dental), hematogenous (endocarditis, lung abscess, IV drug use), post-neurosurgical, or cryptogenic. Common organisms: Streptococcus spp., Staphylococcus aureus, anaerobes, Enterobacteriaceae. Immunocompromised: Toxoplasma, Nocardia, fungi. Treatment: empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole ± vancomycin) for 6–8 weeks. Stereotactic aspiration for abscesses >2.5 cm or diagnostic uncertainty. Surgical excision for multiloculated, posterior fossa, or fungal abscesses. Corticosteroids only for significant mass effect (may impair antibiotic penetration and encapsulation). Serial imaging to monitor response.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Lumbar puncture (risk of herniation), corticosteroids unless significant mass effect.

treatments.useWhen: abscess >2.5 cm, diagnostic uncertainty, or poor response to antibiotics

diseases.name: Narcolepsy
diseases.management: Type 1 (with cataplexy, low CSF hypocretin) and Type 2 (without cataplexy). Diagnosis: polysomnography + MSLT (mean sleep latency ≤8 min with ≥2 SOREMPs). Treatment: excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) — modafinil/armodafinil (first-line), solriamfetol, pitolisant, methylphenidate/amphetamines. Cataplexy — sodium oxybate (Xyrem/Xywav — treats both EDS and cataplexy), SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine), TCAs. Scheduled naps as adjunct.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Abrupt withdrawal of anticataplectic medications (rebound cataplexy), sedating medications, alcohol.

diseases.name: Concussion (Mild Traumatic Brain Injury)
diseases.management: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis — CT head only if red flags (GCS 15, focal deficits, seizure, vomiting, anticoagulation — use Canadian CT Head Rule). Initial management: relative rest for 24–48 hours (not strict bed rest), then gradual return to activity guided by symptoms. Graduated return-to-play protocol (6 steps, minimum 24 hr per step). Graduated return-to-learn for students. Symptom management: headache (acetaminophen, NSAIDs — avoid opioids), sleep disturbance (melatonin, sleep hygiene), vestibular symptoms (vestibular rehabilitation). Most symptoms resolve within 2–4 weeks. Persistent post-concussive symptoms (>4 weeks) warrant multidisciplinary management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Complete rest beyond 48 hr (delays recovery), return to contact sports before symptom resolution, repeat head injury before recovery (second impact syndrome).

treatments.medication: Relative rest then graded return to activity

diseases.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis / Axial Spondyloarthritis
diseases.management: NSAIDs are first-line (continuous use preferred if needed). If inadequate response to ≥2 NSAIDs over 4 weeks with persistently high disease activity (ASDAS ≥2.1): biologic DMARDs. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept, golimumab, certolizumab) are first-line biologics. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) are alternatives — preferred if concurrent psoriasis or TNFi contraindicated (heart failure, demyelinating disease). JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) are options for TNFi/IL-17i failure. Conventional DMARDs (sulfasalazine, methotrexate) are NOT effective for axial disease but may help peripheral arthritis. Physical therapy and regular exercise are essential throughout.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic corticosteroids for axial disease, conventional DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine) for axial symptoms, IL-17 inhibitors in active IBD.

diseases.name: Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA)
diseases.management: PsA is a heterogeneous disease with multiple domains (peripheral arthritis, axial disease, enthesitis, dactylitis, skin/nail psoriasis). Treatment is domain-based. Peripheral arthritis: NSAIDs → conventional DMARDs (methotrexate first-line) → biologic DMARDs (TNFi, IL-17i, IL-23i) or JAKi. Axial disease: NSAIDs → biologics (TNFi or IL-17i — no role for conventional DMARDs). Enthesitis/dactylitis: NSAIDs → biologics. Skin-predominant: IL-17i, IL-23i, or TNFi. Apremilast (PDE4 inhibitor) for mild-moderate disease when DMARDs are inappropriate.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic corticosteroids (psoriasis flare on withdrawal), IL-17i in active IBD.

diseases.name: Reactive Arthritis
diseases.management: Reactive arthritis is an asymmetric oligoarthritis occurring 1–4 weeks after GI (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia) or GU (Chlamydia) infection. Classic triad (formerly Reiter syndrome): arthritis, urethritis, conjunctivitis — present in minority. NSAIDs are first-line. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections for monoarthritis. If persistent (>3–6 months): sulfasalazine or methotrexate. Treat underlying Chlamydia infection if present (azithromycin or doxycycline). TNFi for refractory cases. Most cases self-limited (3–12 months), but ~15–30% develop chronic arthritis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic corticosteroids (limited role), antibiotics for enteric-triggered reactive arthritis (no benefit).

diseases.name: Primary Sjögren Syndrome
diseases.management: Sjögren syndrome is a chronic autoimmune disease targeting exocrine glands (salivary, lacrimal). Diagnosis: anti-SSA/Ro antibodies (most sensitive), minor salivary gland biopsy (focus score ≥1). Treatment is primarily symptomatic. Sicca symptoms: artificial tears, ocular lubricants, topical cyclosporine 0.05% eyedrops for moderate-severe dry eye. Oral dryness: muscarinic agonists (pilocarpine, cevimeline) — stimulate residual salivary gland function. Systemic manifestations: hydroxychloroquine for arthralgias/fatigue (limited trial evidence but widely used). Severe organ involvement (interstitial nephritis, vasculitis, neuropathy): corticosteroids ± immunosuppressants (rituximab, azathioprine, mycophenolate). 15–20× increased risk of B-cell lymphoma — monitor for parotid swelling, lymphadenopathy, declining complement.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Ocular glucocorticoid drops (not effective, risk of corneal damage and increased IOP), amitriptyline for neuropathic pain (worsens dryness — prefer gabapentin/pregabalin/duloxetine), medications that worsen sicca (anticholinergics, antihistamines, diuretics)

diseases.name: Porphyria (Acute Hepatic Porphyrias)
diseases.management: Acute hepatic porphyrias (AHP) include acute intermittent porphyria (most common), hereditary coproporphyria, variegate porphyria, and ALA dehydratase deficiency. Acute attacks present with severe abdominal pain without peritoneal signs, neuropsychiatric symptoms, autonomic dysfunction (tachycardia, hypertension), and hyponatremia. Diagnosis: elevated random urine porphobilinogen (PBG) and delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA). Acute attack management per AGA 2023 guidelines: discontinue porphyrinogenic drugs, IV dextrose (10% dextrose ~300 g/day as initial measure), IV hemin (Panhematin 3–4 mg/kg/day × 4 days — definitive treatment, rapidly downregulates ALAS1). Symptom relief typically occurs within 48–72 hours. Pain management with opioids (safe), antiemetics. Safe anticonvulsants: magnesium sulfate, benzodiazepines, levetiracetam. Recurrent attacks (≥4/year): prophylactic IV hemin or givosiran (siRNA targeting ALAS1, FDA-approved for AHP — reduced annualized attack rate by 74% vs. placebo in ENVISION trial). Liver transplantation is curative for intractable cases. Long-term monitoring: hepatocellular carcinoma screening (annual liver imaging starting age 50), renal function, blood pressure.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Porphyrinogenic drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides, carbamazepine, valproic acid, phenytoin, rifampin, griseofulvin, ergotamines, progesterone), fasting, alcohol, smoking. Safe drugs list available at porphyriafoundation.com.

diseases.name: Methemoglobinemia
diseases.management: Acquired methemoglobinemia occurs from oxidant exposure (dapsone, benzocaine, nitrites, phenazopyridine, primaquine). Methemoglobin (MetHb) >20% typically symptomatic (cyanosis, dyspnea, fatigue); >50% can cause seizures, coma, death. Diagnosis: co-oximetry on ABG (pulse oximetry unreliable — plateaus ~85%). Characteristic finding: chocolate-brown blood that does not turn red with oxygen exposure. Treatment per AHA 2025 guidelines: methylene blue is first-line antidote — acts as cofactor to reduce methemoglobin via NADPH-methemoglobin reductase pathway. Exchange transfusion for methylene blue failure (especially G6PD deficiency). Hyperbaric oxygen as adjunct. Ascorbic acid is too slow for acute management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Methylene blue in G6PD deficiency (worsens hemolysis), reliance on pulse oximetry for monitoring (use co-oximetry), dapsone rechallenge without cimetidine prophylaxis.

treatments.dosage: 1–2 mg/kg IV over 5 min; may repeat × 1 in 30–60 min if no response

diseases.name: Epiglottitis (Supraglottitis)
diseases.management: Epiglottitis is a life-threatening airway emergency. In the post-Hib vaccine era, it predominantly affects adults, with increasing incidence in older adults. Common pathogens: Streptococcus spp., Staphylococcus aureus, H. influenzae (non-typeable). Adults present with severe odynophagia (100%), dysphagia (85%), voice change (75%), and drooling — more subacute than pediatric presentation. Diagnosis: direct visualization via flexible laryngoscopy (preferred) or lateral neck radiograph (thumbprint sign). Airway assessment is paramount — stridor, respiratory distress, tachycardia, and rapid symptom onset predict need for airway intervention (~21% require intubation). Management: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam ± vancomycin for MRSA coverage) AND IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone). Early corticosteroids associated with 52% lower risk of intubation and shorter ICU/hospital stay. ICU admission for airway monitoring. Have difficult airway equipment ready.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Blind oropharyngeal examination in children (may precipitate complete obstruction), delay in airway assessment, discharge without period of observation.

treatments.duration: 7–10 days (step down to PO when improving)
