diseases.name: Peritonsillar Abscess (Quinsy)
diseases.management: Most common deep space infection of the head and neck. Typically polymicrobial (Group A Strep, Fusobacterium, oral anaerobes). Presents with severe sore throat, trismus, "hot potato" voice, uvular deviation, unilateral tonsillar swelling. Treatment: drainage (needle aspiration or incision and drainage — equivalent efficacy) + antibiotics. Needle aspiration is preferred initial approach (less painful, can be done in ED). Amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin for outpatient management. IV ampicillin-sulbactam for admitted patients. Corticosteroids (single dose dexamethasone) reduce pain and time to oral intake. Tonsillectomy for recurrent peritonsillar abscess (≥2 episodes).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in drainage, CT scan when diagnosis is clinically obvious (reserve for uncertain cases or suspected parapharyngeal extension).

treatments.useWhen: all peritonsillar abscesses; can repeat if reaccumulates
reduces pain and improves oral intake

treatments.notes: equally effective as needle aspiration; may have lower recurrence in some studies
severe cases, unable to swallow
covers GAS and anaerobes; also effective IV 600–900 mg Q8h

diseases.name: Retropharyngeal Abscess
diseases.management: More common in children 6 years (retropharyngeal lymph nodes atrophy by age 6). In adults, often from penetrating trauma, foreign body, or dental infection. Presents with fever, neck stiffness, dysphagia, drooling, muffled voice, neck swelling. CT neck with contrast is diagnostic (ring-enhancing fluid collection in retropharyngeal space). Danger: airway compromise, mediastinitis (descending infection), internal jugular vein thrombosis, carotid artery erosion. Treatment: IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin + ceftriaxone) ± surgical drainage. Small abscesses (2 cm) in stable patients may respond to IV antibiotics alone. Larger abscesses or clinical deterioration require surgical drainage (transoral or transcervical approach).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in imaging, blind attempts at drainage without imaging guidance, discharge before confirmed clinical improvement.

treatments.dosage: 3 g IV Q6H (adults); 200 mg/kg/day divided Q6H (peds)
transoral or transcervical incision and drainage
treatments.notes: abscess >2 cm, airway compromise, failed medical therapy

treatments.dosage: 600–900 mg Q8h (adult) or 30–40 mg/kg/day divided Q8h (pediatric)
treatments.notes: penicillin allergy or MRSA concern

treatments.duration: abscess >2 cm, airway compromise, failure to improve on IV antibiotics within 48 hours
treatments.notes: send cultures; transoral preferred for midline abscesses; transcervical for lateral or multiloculated

diseases.name: Otitis Externa (Swimmer's Ear)
diseases.management: Acute otitis externa is infection of the external auditory canal, most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. AAO-HNS guidelines: topical therapy is first-line (avoid systemic antibiotics for uncomplicated cases). Topical options: fluoroquinolone drops (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin), neomycin-polymyxin B-hydrocortisone, or acetic acid. If TM perforation suspected, use non-ototoxic drops (fluoroquinolones only — avoid aminoglycosides). Wick placement if canal is edematous and drops cannot penetrate. Keep ear dry. Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa: suspect in diabetic/immunocompromised patients with severe pain, granulation tissue at bone-cartilage junction — requires IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (ciprofloxacin PO or IV anti-pseudomonal) and CT/MRI imaging.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic antibiotics for uncomplicated otitis externa, aminoglycoside-containing drops with TM perforation, cotton swabs/ear instrumentation.

treatments.useWhen: uncomplicated otitis externa; safe with TM perforation
mild cases; restores acidic canal pH

treatments.notes: safe with TM perforation; fluoroquinolone + steroid; no ototoxicity risk
safe with TM perforation; no steroid component
effective and inexpensive; do NOT use if TM perforated (ototoxicity risk); contact sensitization possible
inexpensive; may be less effective; also used for prophylaxis

treatments.dosage: insert wick, apply drops to wick
treatments.notes: significantly edematous canal preventing drop delivery

diseases.name: Conjunctivitis
diseases.management: Classify: viral (most common — adenovirus, watery discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy), bacterial (purulent discharge, crusting), allergic (bilateral itching, watery discharge, chemosis). Viral: self-limited (1–2 weeks), supportive care (cold compresses, artificial tears), highly contagious — hand hygiene. Bacterial: topical antibiotics shorten duration — erythromycin ointment, trimethoprim-polymyxin B drops, or fluoroquinolone drops (reserved for contact lens wearers or severe cases). Allergic: topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizers (olopatadine, ketotifen), cool compresses, avoid allergens. Red flags requiring ophthalmology referral: vision loss, severe pain, photophobia, corneal opacity, contact lens wearer with red eye, herpes keratitis (dendritic ulcer), hyperacute purulent discharge (gonococcal — requires systemic ceftriaxone).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical corticosteroids without ophthalmology consultation (risk of herpes keratitis worsening, glaucoma), patching, contact lens wear until resolved.

treatments.dosage: apply to lower conjunctival sac QID × 5–7 days
treatments.notes: bacterial conjunctivitis
bacterial conjunctivitis
allergic conjunctivitis
supportive; strict hand hygiene; contagious × 10–14 days
broad-spectrum; well tolerated
reserve for contact lens wearers (Pseudomonas coverage) or severe cases
dual antihistamine + mast cell stabilizer; OTC (ketotifen); most effective class for allergic conjunctivitis
copious purulent discharge, rapid onset

diseases.name: Keratitis
diseases.management: Keratitis is corneal inflammation/infection — an ophthalmologic emergency. Bacterial keratitis: most common in contact lens wearers (Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus). Presents with pain, photophobia, tearing, corneal infiltrate/ulcer. Treatment: intensive topical fortified antibiotics (fluoroquinolone monotherapy — moxifloxacin or gatifloxacin Q1H around the clock for 48 hr, then taper; OR fortified tobramycin + fortified cefazolin alternating Q30min for severe ulcers). Corneal cultures before starting antibiotics for central/large ulcers. Herpes simplex keratitis (dendritic ulcer on fluorescein staining): topical ganciclovir 0.15% gel 5×/day or oral valacyclovir 500 mg TID. Fungal keratitis (suspect with vegetative trauma, tropical climate): topical natamycin 5% (first-line for filamentous fungi) or topical voriconazole. Acanthamoeba keratitis (contact lens wearers): topical polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) + propamidine (Brolene) for months.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical corticosteroids in infectious keratitis (without ophthalmology guidance), contact lens wear, patching (promotes bacterial growth).

treatments.dosage: 1 drop Q1H × 48 hr, then Q2H × 48 hr, then QID
treatments.notes: bacterial keratitis; corneal cultures first for central/large ulcers
herpes simplex keratitis
filamentous fungal keratitis

diseases.name: Endophthalmitis
diseases.management: Endophthalmitis is intraocular infection — an ophthalmologic emergency. Most common: post-cataract surgery (acute, within 6 weeks — coagulase-negative Staphylococcus most common). Also post-intravitreal injection, post-traumatic, or endogenous (hematogenous spread). Presents with pain, decreased vision, hypopyon, vitritis. Treatment per Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study (EVS): intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin 1 mg + ceftazidime 2.25 mg) are the mainstay. Vitrectomy: recommended if presenting visual acuity is light perception only (EVS showed benefit in this subgroup). Vitreous tap/biopsy for culture before antibiotics. Systemic antibiotics for endogenous endophthalmitis. Topical fortified antibiotics and cycloplegics as adjuncts.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in intravitreal antibiotics, intravitreal aminoglycosides (retinal toxicity — use ceftazidime instead), systemic antibiotics alone for exogenous endophthalmitis (poor intraocular penetration).

treatments.dosage: vancomycin 1 mg/0.1 mL + ceftazidime 2.25 mg/0.1 mL
treatments.frequency: may repeat in 48–72 hr if no improvement
treatments.useWhen: all endophthalmitis; obtain vitreous cultures first

treatments.notes: presenting VA = light perception only (EVS); also for endogenous or post-traumatic

diseases.name: Epididymitis / Orchitis
diseases.management: Acute epididymitis: in men 35 years, most commonly caused by Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae (STI-related). In men >35 years: E. coli and other Enterobacteriaceae (urinary pathogens). Orchitis: most commonly viral (mumps) or extension from epididymitis. Diagnosis: clinical (gradual onset scrotal pain, positive Prehn sign, positive cremasteric reflex — distinguishes from testicular torsion). Ultrasound with Doppler if torsion cannot be excluded. Treatment per CDC STI guidelines: STI-related — ceftriaxone 500 mg IM × 1 + doxycycline 100 mg BID × 10 days. Non-STI/enteric — levofloxacin 500 mg daily × 10 days. Supportive: scrotal elevation, NSAIDs, ice.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Missing testicular torsion (get ultrasound if any doubt), fluoroquinolones for STI-related epididymitis (inadequate gonococcal coverage).

treatments.dosage: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM × 1 + doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 10 days
treatments.notes: men 35 or STI risk factors
men >35, recent urinary instrumentation, no STI risk

diseases.name: Mastitis (Lactational)
diseases.management: Lactational mastitis affects ~10% of breastfeeding women, most commonly in the first 6 weeks postpartum. Caused by Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA). Initial management: continued breastfeeding/milk expression (essential — do not stop), warm compresses, NSAIDs, adequate hydration. Antibiotics if symptoms persist >24 hours or systemic symptoms present: dicloxacillin or cephalexin (first-line). If MRSA suspected or penicillin allergy: TMP-SMX or clindamycin (TMP-SMX compatible with breastfeeding). Breast abscess (fluctuant mass, failed antibiotics): ultrasound-guided needle aspiration or incision and drainage.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Stopping breastfeeding (worsens engorgement and abscess risk), amoxicillin-clavulanate as first-line (unnecessary broad coverage).

treatments.useWhen: lactational mastitis with systemic symptoms or no improvement after 24 hr of conservative measures

treatments.notes: MRSA suspected or penicillin allergy; compatible with breastfeeding

diseases.name: Septic Bursitis
diseases.management: Most commonly affects olecranon and prepatellar bursae. Staphylococcus aureus is the causative organism in ~80% of cases. Distinguish from non-septic (gout, trauma, overuse) by bursal fluid aspiration: WBC >2000/μL, positive Gram stain/culture, glucose 50% serum. Treatment: aspiration + oral antibiotics for mild-moderate cases (no systemic toxicity). Cephalexin or dicloxacillin first-line. TMP-SMX or doxycycline if MRSA risk. Serial aspirations every 1–3 days until fluid reaccumulation ceases. IV antibiotics for severe infection, immunocompromised, or failed oral therapy. Surgical bursectomy for refractory cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroid injection into infected bursa, missing septic arthritis (bursal fluid analysis is key).

treatments.notes: MRSA risk

treatments.useWhen: all septic bursitis; send fluid for culture, cell count, crystals

diseases.name: Dacryocystitis
diseases.management: Infection of the lacrimal sac due to nasolacrimal duct obstruction. Acute: tender, erythematous swelling at medial canthus, purulent discharge expressible from punctum. Most common organisms: S. aureus, Streptococcus spp., H. influenzae. Treatment: warm compresses, oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin). IV antibiotics if periorbital cellulitis or systemic toxicity. Abscess: incision and drainage. Definitive treatment for recurrent dacryocystitis: dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) — surgical bypass of obstructed nasolacrimal duct (success rate >90%). Congenital dacryocystitis (neonates): conservative management (lacrimal sac massage, warm compresses) — 90% resolve by age 12 months; probing if persistent.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in treatment (risk of orbital cellulitis, lacrimal sac fistula), nasolacrimal probing during acute infection.

diseases.name: Rosacea
diseases.management: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory facial dermatosis with four subtypes: erythematotelangiectatic (ETR), papulopustular, phymatous, and ocular. Treatment is phenotype-based. ETR: topical brimonidine 0.33% gel (alpha-2 agonist, reduces erythema within 30 min) or oxymetazoline 1% cream. Papulopustular: topical metronidazole 0.75–1%, topical ivermectin 1% cream (superior to metronidazole), or topical azelaic acid 15%. Moderate-severe papulopustular: oral doxycycline 40 mg modified-release daily (anti-inflammatory dose — avoids antibiotic resistance). Severe/refractory: oral isotretinoin (low-dose 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/day). Phymatous: isotretinoin for early disease, surgical/laser for established rhinophyma. Ocular: lid hygiene, topical cyclosporine 0.05%, oral doxycycline. All patients: gentle skincare, broad-spectrum sunscreen, avoid triggers (alcohol, spicy food, heat, topical steroids).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical corticosteroids (cause steroid rosacea/rebound), alcohol-based skincare products, triggers.

treatments.notes: papulopustular rosacea; superior to metronidazole
persistent facial erythema; onset within 30 min
moderate papulopustular rosacea; anti-inflammatory dose
FDA-approved for persistent facial erythema; alpha-1 agonist
well-established efficacy; less effective than ivermectin
anti-inflammatory sub-antimicrobial dose; does not promote antibiotic resistance
for severe papulopustular or early phymatous; teratogenic; iPLEDGE required

diseases.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
diseases.management: Chronic relapsing dermatitis affecting sebaceous gland-rich areas (scalp, face, chest). Associated with Malassezia yeast overgrowth. Scalp: antifungal shampoos (ketoconazole 2%, selenium sulfide 2.5%, zinc pyrithione, ciclopirox) used 2–3×/week. Face/body: topical ketoconazole 2% cream, topical calcineurin inhibitors (pimecrolimus, tacrolimus — preferred for facial use to avoid steroid atrophy), low-potency topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1% — short courses only). Severe/refractory: oral itraconazole pulse therapy (200 mg/day × 7 days/month). In HIV/AIDS: seborrheic dermatitis is often severe and widespread — treat underlying HIV.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Potent topical corticosteroids on face (atrophy, telangiectasia, steroid rosacea), prolonged topical steroid use.

treatments.notes: scalp seborrheic dermatitis
facial/body seborrheic dermatitis
facial seborrheic dermatitis; avoids steroid atrophy
low-potency steroid; short courses for acute flares; do not use long-term on face
for severe/refractory; monitor LFTs; drug interactions (CYP3A4)

diseases.name: Hidradenitis Suppurativa (HS)
diseases.management: HS is a chronic inflammatory disease of hair follicles in intertriginous areas (axillae, groin, inframammary). Severity staged by Hurley classification (I: abscesses without tracts; II: recurrent abscesses with sinus tracts; III: diffuse interconnected tracts). Treatment is stepwise. Hurley I (mild): topical clindamycin 1% BID, antiseptic washes. Hurley I–II (moderate): oral tetracyclines (doxycycline 100 mg BID) for 12 weeks. Refractory moderate: oral clindamycin 300 mg BID + rifampin 300 mg BID × 12 weeks. Hurley II–III (moderate-severe): adalimumab (only FDA-approved biologic for HS — 160 mg week 0, 80 mg week 2, then 40 mg weekly). Secukinumab (IL-17A inhibitor) FDA-approved 2023 for moderate-severe HS. Infliximab as alternative. Surgical: incision and drainage for acute abscesses (
diseases.notes: Avoid: Incision and drainage as sole treatment (high recurrence), systemic antibiotics long-term without plan, isotretinoin (ineffective for HS despite acne efficacy).

treatments.notes: Hurley I
Hurley I–II

treatments.dosage: clindamycin 300 mg BID + rifampin 300 mg BID
treatments.useWhen: refractory moderate HS; monitor for drug interactions with rifampin

diseases.name: Lichen Planus
diseases.management: Lichen planus is a T-cell-mediated inflammatory disorder affecting skin, mucous membranes, nails, and scalp. Cutaneous LP may resolve spontaneously within 1–2 years. Oral LP (OLP) is chronic and carries ~1.4% risk of malignant transformation. Cutaneous LP: potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol, betamethasone) are first-line. Oral retinoids (acitretin 30 mg/day) for widespread/refractory disease. Phototherapy (NB-UVB, PUVA) as alternative. Oral LP: topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone acetonide in orabase, fluocinonide gel) are first-line. Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1%) are second-line, especially for erosive OLP. Systemic therapy for severe/refractory: systemic corticosteroids (short course), methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Topical retinoids on skin (irritation), missing malignancy surveillance in erosive OLP, hepatitis C screening should be performed (association).

treatments.notes: localized cutaneous lichen planus
symptomatic oral lichen planus
erosive OLP refractory to topical steroids; no atrophy risk
widespread cutaneous LP or refractory OLP; teratogenic — contraception mandatory

diseases.name: Erythema Multiforme (EM)
diseases.management: EM is an acute, self-limited, immune-mediated condition most commonly triggered by HSV infection (~70% of cases) or medications. Characterized by target lesions (three-zone pattern) on extremities. EM minor: skin only. EM major: mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital). Distinguish from SJS/TEN (which involves epidermal detachment). Treatment: mild EM — supportive care (analgesics, topical corticosteroids, antiseptic mouthwashes for oral lesions). Severe EM major — short course systemic corticosteroids (debated but used in practice: prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day × 10–15 days with rapid taper). HSV-associated recurrent EM: antiviral prophylaxis with acyclovir or valacyclovir (continuous suppressive therapy). Refractory recurrent EM: azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, dapsone.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Confusing EM with SJS/TEN (different pathology and management), rechallenge with causative drug.

treatments.notes: EM minor
recurrent EM triggered by HSV (≥3 episodes/year)
severe mucosal involvement; evidence debated
most cases resolve spontaneously; lesions fixed for ≥7 days (distinguishes from urticaria)

treatments.dosage: acyclovir 400 mg BID or valacyclovir 500 mg BID
treatments.notes: continuous antiviral; first-line even for idiopathic recurrent EM; if dose inadequate, increase or switch antiviral

diseases.name: Erythema Nodosum
diseases.management: Erythema nodosum is the most common panniculitis — tender erythematous nodules on pretibial surfaces. Usually self-limited (3–6 weeks). Identify and treat underlying cause (streptococcal infection, sarcoidosis, IBD, medications, TB, pregnancy). Workup: CBC, CMP, ESR/CRP, ASO titer, chest X-ray, throat culture, PPD/IGRA. Treatment is supportive: bed rest, leg elevation, compression stockings, NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen). Potassium iodide (SSKI 300–900 mg/day) is effective for refractory cases. Colchicine as alternative. Systemic corticosteroids only after infection is excluded and for severe/refractory cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic corticosteroids before excluding infection (especially TB, streptococcal), biopsy rarely needed (clinical diagnosis).

treatments.dosage: ibuprofen 400–600 mg TID or naproxen 500 mg BID

treatments.useWhen: refractory to NSAIDs; contraindicated in pregnancy

treatments.notes: only after infection excluded; risk of rebound/recurrence on taper

diseases.name: Pemphigus Vulgaris
diseases.management: Pemphigus vulgaris is a life-threatening autoimmune blistering disease caused by IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein 3 (± desmoglein 1). Rituximab has become first-line therapy based on the Ritux 3 trial (89% complete remission off therapy at 24 months with rituximab + short-course prednisone vs. 34% with prednisone alone) and the PEMPHIX trial (rituximab superior to mycophenolate mofetil — 40% vs. 10% sustained complete remission at 52 weeks). Current approach: rituximab (1 g IV days 0 and 14) + low-dose prednisone (0.5 mg/kg/day, taper over 3–6 months), with maintenance rituximab infusions at 6 and 12 months. For patients unable to receive rituximab: high-dose prednisone (1–1.5 mg/kg/day) + steroid-sparing agent (mycophenolate mofetil or azathioprine). IVIG for acute flares or refractory disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in rituximab initiation (early use associated with higher remission rates), prolonged high-dose corticosteroids without steroid-sparing agent.

treatments.dosage: rituximab 1 g IV days 0 and 14 + prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day (taper over 3–6 months); maintenance rituximab 500 mg at months 6 and 12

treatments.useWhen: acute flares, refractory disease, or as bridge therapy

treatments.notes: for patients unable to receive rituximab; MMF takes 6–8 weeks for full effect
check TPMT before starting AZA; alternative to MMF

diseases.name: Bullous Pemphigoid
diseases.management: Bullous pemphigoid is the most common autoimmune blistering disease, predominantly affecting elderly patients (>70 years). Autoantibodies target BP180 and BP230 at the dermal-epidermal junction. Topical clobetasol propionate (whole-body application, 40 g/day tapered over 12 months) is first-line — a landmark NEJM trial showed it was as effective as oral prednisone with significantly lower mortality in extensive disease. Oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day is an alternative when whole-body topical application is impractical. Doxycycline 200 mg/day is a safer alternative to prednisolone with acceptable short-term blister control and superior long-term safety (BLISTER trial). Steroid-sparing agents: azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, methotrexate, dapsone. Rituximab for refractory cases. Dupilumab showing promise in recent trials.
diseases.notes: Avoid: High-dose oral corticosteroids (>0.75 mg/kg/day — increased mortality in elderly), prolonged systemic steroids without steroid-sparing plan.

treatments.dosage: 10–40 g/day applied to entire body, taper over 4–12 months
treatments.useWhen: all bullous pemphigoid; superior safety to oral steroids
when whole-body topical application impractical; safer than prednisolone long-term
when topical therapy impractical; higher adverse event rate in elderly

treatments.notes: higher dose (0.75–1 mg/kg/day) not superior and causes more adverse events

treatments.dosage: 300 mg SC every 2 weeks (after 600 mg loading)
treatments.notes: FDA-approved for BP (2024); anti-IL-4Rα; targets type 2 inflammation; well tolerated

diseases.name: Pyoderma Gangrenosum
diseases.management: Pyoderma gangrenosum is a rare neutrophilic dermatosis causing painful ulcerations. Associated with IBD (~30%), hematologic disorders, and rheumatologic diseases. Exhibits pathergy (worsening with surgical debridement/trauma). Diagnosis is clinical (validated scoring systems now available — not a diagnosis of exclusion). Treatment: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) and/or cyclosporine (3–5 mg/kg/day) are first-line with the most evidence (STOP GAP trial showed equivalent efficacy). TNF-alpha inhibitors (infliximab — RCT showed 46% response vs. 6% placebo at 2 weeks) are first-line for patients with concurrent IBD. Wound care: moist wound healing, avoid debridement (pathergy). Pain management is critical.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Surgical debridement (pathergy — worsens disease), skin grafting during active disease, missing underlying associated conditions.

treatments.useWhen: equivalent to prednisone per STOP GAP trial; preferred if steroid contraindications

treatments.dosage: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks
treatments.notes: concurrent IBD; RCT evidence of superiority over placebo

treatments.useWhen: mild/localized disease or adjunct to systemic therapy

treatments.notes: RCT: 46% response vs 6% placebo at 2 weeks; preferred with concurrent IBD
avoid surgical debridement (pathergy); pain management critical

diseases.name: Vitiligo
diseases.management: Vitiligo is an autoimmune disorder causing melanocyte destruction. Treatment aims to halt progression and promote repigmentation. Topical ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor) is the first FDA-approved treatment for nonsegmental vitiligo (2022). Topical corticosteroids (potent — betamethasone, mometasone) are first-line for localized disease (not face). Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1%, pimecrolimus 1%) are preferred for face/neck (no atrophy risk). NB-UVB phototherapy is first-line for widespread disease (2–3×/week for ≥6–12 months). Combination of topical therapy + phototherapy enhances repigmentation. Surgical options (autologous melanocyte transplant, suction blister grafting) for stable, localized, refractory vitiligo.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Potent topical steroids on face (atrophy), expecting rapid results (repigmentation takes months), sun exposure without protection of depigmented areas (burn risk).

treatments.useWhen: nonsegmental vitiligo; FDA-approved; face responds best

treatments.notes: facial/neck vitiligo; no atrophy risk

treatments.dosage: apply daily (intermittent — 2 weeks on, 1 week off to avoid atrophy)
treatments.notes: localized body vitiligo
widespread vitiligo (>20% BSA); combine with topical therapy
for limited vitiligo (10% BSA); best on trunk/extremities; monitor for atrophy

diseases.name: Alopecia Areata
diseases.management: Alopecia areata is an autoimmune, nonscarring hair loss disorder. Treatment depends on severity and extent. Limited patchy disease (50% scalp): intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (5–10 mg/mL, injected every 4–6 weeks) is first-line. Topical corticosteroids (clobetasol) and topical minoxidil as adjuncts. Severe/extensive disease (≥50% scalp — SALT ≥50): oral JAK inhibitors are now first-line. Baricitinib (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, FDA-approved 2022): 4 mg daily — BRAVE-AA1 and BRAVE-AA2 trials showed 38.8% and 35.9% achieved SALT ≤20 at 36 weeks vs. 6.2% and 3.3% with placebo. Ritlecitinib (JAK3/TEC inhibitor, FDA-approved 2023): 50 mg daily. Deuruxolitinib (JAK1/JAK2, FDA-approved 2025): 8 mg BID. Contact immunotherapy (DPCP) remains an option for extensive disease. Relapse is common after discontinuation of any therapy.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic corticosteroids long-term (relapse on discontinuation, side effects), expecting permanent cure (relapse common with all therapies).

treatments.dosage: 5–10 mg/mL, 0.1 mL per site, every 4–6 weeks
treatments.notes: limited patchy alopecia areata (50% scalp)
severe alopecia areata (SALT ≥50); FDA-approved; monitor CBC, lipids, LFTs
severe alopecia areata; FDA-approved 2023
severe AA; FDA-approved for ≥12 years; JAK3/TEC inhibitor; similar efficacy to baricitinib per indirect comparison

diseases.name: Pressure Injury (Pressure Ulcer)
diseases.management: Pressure injuries are staged per NPIAP: Stage 1 (intact skin, nonblanchable erythema), Stage 2 (partial-thickness skin loss), Stage 3 (full-thickness skin loss), Stage 4 (full-thickness with exposed bone/tendon/muscle), Unstageable (obscured by slough/eschar), Deep Tissue Injury (intact skin with deep damage). Management pillars: pressure offloading (repositioning every 2 hours, appropriate support surfaces — air-fluidized beds for Stage 3–4), nutritional optimization (protein 1.25–1.5 g/kg/day, caloric supplementation, correct deficiencies), wound care (cleansing with saline, debridement of necrotic tissue — sharp, enzymatic, or autolytic), and appropriate dressing selection. Stage 1–2: hydrocolloid or foam dressings. Stage 3–4: foam, alginate, or hydrofiber dressings based on exudate. Negative-pressure wound therapy for large Stage 3–4 wounds. Surgical consultation for Stage 3–4 wounds not responding to conservative management (flap closure). Monitor for infection and osteomyelitis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Donut-shaped cushions (increase pressure at edges), massage over bony prominences, dry gauze dressings (adhere to wound bed), cytotoxic wound cleansers (hydrogen peroxide, povidone-iodine on open wounds).

treatments.useWhen: all pressure injuries and at-risk patients
all pressure injuries; protein/amino acid supplementation improves healing

treatments.notes: superior to gauze for reducing wound size; maintain moist wound environment
large Stage 3–4 wounds

diseases.name: Warts (Verruca Vulgaris / Plantar Warts)
diseases.management: Common warts are caused by HPV (types 1, 2, 4, 27, 57). Many resolve spontaneously (65% within 2 years). Treatment is indicated for symptomatic, persistent, or spreading warts. Salicylic acid (17–40% topical) is first-line — modest but consistent efficacy over placebo (Cochrane review: 389 vs. 250 per 1000 cured). Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen (every 2–3 weeks × 3–4 sessions) is equally effective but more painful. Combination of cryotherapy + salicylic acid is superior to either alone for hand warts. Refractory warts: intralesional candida antigen immunotherapy, intralesional bleomycin, topical 5-fluorouracil, contact immunotherapy (DPCP/DNCB), or topical imiquimod 5%. Surgical excision/electrodesiccation for isolated refractory warts.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aggressive surgical excision of plantar warts (painful scarring), expectation of single-treatment cure.

treatments.dosage: apply daily after soaking and paring
treatments.useWhen: all common and plantar warts; OTC available
all warts; more aggressive freezing more effective but more painful
refractory or multiple warts; stimulates cell-mediated immunity

treatments.notes: superior to either alone for hand warts (832 vs 674 per 1000)

diseases.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)
diseases.management: OCD affects 2–3% of the population. First-line treatments are CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP) and SSRIs — both have strong evidence and are often used together. CBT/ERP is the most effective single treatment when performed by experienced practitioners. SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy (NNT = 5 vs. placebo). Higher SSRI doses are modestly more effective than lower doses. Allow 8–12 weeks at adequate dose before determining nonresponse. Continue SSRIs ≥6–12 months after response. For SSRI-refractory OCD: augmentation with low-dose antipsychotic (aripiprazole 2–10 mg, risperidone 0.5–2 mg) — especially effective in patients with comorbid tic disorder. Clomipramine (SRI, not SSRI) is an alternative but has more side effects. TMS (FDA-approved for OCD) and DBS for severe refractory cases.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines as monotherapy (no evidence for OCD), abrupt SSRI discontinuation, SNRIs (less evidence than SSRIs for OCD).

treatments.dosage: fluoxetine 20–80 mg, fluvoxamine 100–300 mg, sertraline 50–200 mg daily
treatments.useWhen: all OCD; use maximum tolerated dose; NNT = 5
all OCD; most effective single treatment
SSRI-refractory OCD; especially with comorbid tics
SSRI-refractory; more side effects (anticholinergic, cardiac — ECG monitoring)

diseases.name: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
diseases.management: Trauma-focused psychotherapy is first-line for all PTSD patients (APA 2025 guidelines). Effective modalities: prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT), and EMDR. Pharmacotherapy when psychotherapy is unavailable, refused, or insufficient: SSRIs are first-line (sertraline and paroxetine are FDA-approved for PTSD; fluoxetine also effective). Venlafaxine (SNRI) is an alternative. Continue medication ≥6–12 months. Sleep disturbances: prazosin (1–15 mg at bedtime) for trauma-related nightmares. Trazodone or hydroxyzine for insomnia. Screen for and treat comorbid conditions (depression, substance use, OSA). Do not delay PTSD treatment for comorbidities.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines (worsen PTSD outcomes, impair extinction learning), atypical antipsychotics as monotherapy (limited evidence, significant side effects).

treatments.useWhen: PTSD when psychotherapy unavailable or insufficient; FDA-approved

treatments.dosage: 1–15 mg PO at bedtime (start 1 mg, titrate slowly)
treatments.notes: trauma-related nightmares; monitor for orthostatic hypotension

diseases.name: Panic Disorder
diseases.management: SSRIs and SNRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy for panic disorder (NNT ~10 for remission at 2–6 months). CBT is equally effective and can be used alone or combined with pharmacotherapy (combination likely most effective). Among SSRIs, escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, and citalopram are effective. Venlafaxine (SNRI) is an alternative. Start at low doses (half the usual starting dose) and titrate slowly — patients with panic disorder are sensitive to medication side effects. Continue ≥6–12 months to minimize relapse (50% relapse if discontinued before 1 year). Benzodiazepines are NOT first-line (comparable efficacy to antidepressants but risk of tolerance, dependence, withdrawal, and increased mortality). Beta-blockers have no evidence of benefit despite common use.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines as first-line (dependence, withdrawal, mortality risk), beta-blockers (no evidence), antipsychotics (limited evidence, poor tolerability).

treatments.dosage: sertraline 25–200 mg or escitalopram 5–20 mg daily (start low)
weekly sessions × 12–16 weeks (cognitive restructuring + interoceptive exposure + in vivo exposure)
treatments.useWhen: all panic disorder; as effective as pharmacotherapy

diseases.name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)
diseases.management: Stimulant medications (methylphenidate and amphetamines) are the most effective pharmacotherapy for adult ADHD (~70% immediate improvement, moderate-to-large effect sizes). Extended-release formulations preferred over immediate-release (once-daily dosing, smoother effect). No recommendation to start with methylphenidate vs. amphetamine — try alternative if first is ineffective or poorly tolerated. Non-stimulant options: atomoxetine (selective NE reuptake inhibitor — first-line non-stimulant), extended-release guanfacine, extended-release clonidine. Bupropion and modafinil are third-line. CBT as adjunct to medication (not monotherapy). Multimodal approach recommended.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Stimulants in uncontrolled hypertension, psychosis, or active substance use disorder; bupropion in seizure disorder or bulimia.

treatments.dosage: start 18–36 mg daily, titrate to max 72 mg/day
treatments.useWhen: adult ADHD; titrate to optimal dose

treatments.dosage: 30 mg daily, titrate to max 70 mg/day
treatments.useWhen: adult ADHD; prodrug with lower abuse potential

treatments.dosage: 40 mg daily × 3 days, then 80 mg daily (target 1.2 mg/kg/day)
treatments.useWhen: stimulant contraindicated, substance use disorder, anxiety comorbidity, patient preference

diseases.name: Anorexia Nervosa
diseases.management: Weight restoration is the primary treatment goal. Initial caloric targets: 1500–2000 kcal/day with incremental increases to 3000–4000 kcal/day. Target weight gain: 0.9–1.8 kg/week (inpatient) or 0.5–0.9 kg/week (outpatient). Target BMI typically ≥20 for adults. Monitor for refeeding syndrome (hypophosphatemia is hallmark — check phosphate, potassium, magnesium daily during initial refeeding; supplement prophylactically). Psychotherapy: family-based treatment (FBT/Maudsley approach) is first-line for adolescents. CBT-E (enhanced) for adults. No medications reliably promote weight gain — SSRIs may help comorbid depression/anxiety but do not improve weight. Olanzapine may have modest benefit but limited by side effects. Nasogastric feeding if oral intake inadequate. APA guidelines recommend against TPN.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bupropion (seizure risk in malnourished/purging patients), medications that prolong QTc (risk of arrhythmia in electrolyte-depleted patients), reliance on medications for weight gain (no medications reliably promote weight gain), TPN (APA recommends against).

treatments.dosage: start 1500–2000 kcal/day, increase to 3000–4000 kcal/day; target weight gain 0.9–1.8 kg/week (inpatient) or 0.5–0.9 kg/week (outpatient)
treatments.useWhen: all anorexia nervosa; target BMI ≥20 for adults

treatments.dosage: check phosphate, potassium, magnesium daily during initial refeeding; supplement phosphate prophylactically
treatments.useWhen: all patients undergoing nutritional rehabilitation; hypophosphatemia is hallmark

treatments.notes: adolescents with involved caregiver; strongest evidence in this age group
adult anorexia nervosa

diseases.name: Bulimia Nervosa
diseases.management: CBT focused on eating disorders is first-line psychotherapy. APA 2023 guidelines recommend combining CBT with an SSRI, either initially or if minimal response to psychotherapy alone by 6 weeks. Fluoxetine 60 mg daily has the most evidence and is the only FDA-approved medication for bulimia nervosa. The therapeutic dose (60 mg) is higher than typical antidepressant doses. Guided self-help CBT (manual-based or internet-delivered) is an acceptable alternative when therapist-delivered CBT is unavailable. Family-based therapy for adolescents with involved caregivers. Reestablishing regular eating patterns is a central goal — eating regularly throughout the day minimizes the restriction-binge-purge cycle.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Bupropion (seizure risk in purging patients — contraindicated), citalopram (QTc prolongation risk in electrolyte-depleted patients), IPO monotherapy (less evidence than CBT for bulimia).

treatments.useWhen: all bulimia nervosa; FDA-approved; combine with CBT

diseases.name: Binge Eating Disorder (BED)
diseases.management: BED is the most prevalent eating disorder. APA 2023 guidelines recommend CBT or interpersonal therapy (IPT) as first-line, in individual or group formats. For patients who prefer medication or have not responded to psychotherapy: lisdexamfetamine (only FDA-approved medication for BED — reduced binge-eating frequency and binge-eating days vs. placebo) or second-generation antidepressants (SSRIs). Topiramate reduces binge eating and weight but has significant side effects. Combination of behavioral weight loss therapy + naltrexone-bupropion showed 57% binge-eating remission vs. 18% with placebo in an RCT. Weight loss is not the primary treatment goal — focus on reducing binge eating and associated psychopathology.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Focusing solely on weight loss without addressing binge eating, stimulant medications in patients with active substance use disorder.

treatments.useWhen: BED; FDA-approved; Schedule II controlled substance
BED with obesity; reduces binge eating and weight; cognitive side effects limit use

diseases.name: Insomnia (Chronic Insomnia Disorder)
diseases.management: CBT for insomnia (CBT-I) is first-line treatment per all major guidelines (ACP, AASM, NICE). CBT-I includes sleep restriction, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training, and sleep hygiene education. Can be delivered in-person, via telehealth, or through digital platforms. Produces sustained improvement even after treatment ends — superior to medications long-term. Pharmacotherapy when CBT-I is unavailable, ineffective, or as short-term adjunct: dual orexin receptor antagonists (DORAs — suvorexant, lemborexant, daridorexant) are newer options with favorable safety profiles. Low-dose doxepin (3–6 mg) is FDA-approved for sleep maintenance insomnia. Benzodiazepine receptor agonists (zolpidem, eszopiclone, zaleplon) are effective but carry risks of dependence, falls, and complex sleep behaviors. Ramelteon (melatonin receptor agonist) for sleep onset insomnia. Long-term medication use (>3 months) is discouraged. AASM 2026 guidelines address combination treatment (CBT-I + medication) — may be beneficial for some patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Benzodiazepines and Z-drugs long-term (dependence, falls, cognitive impairment — especially in elderly), diphenhydramine/antihistamines (anticholinergic effects, tolerance), trazodone (insufficient evidence per AASM), melatonin as monotherapy (insufficient evidence for chronic insomnia).

treatments.useWhen: all chronic insomnia; sustained benefit after treatment ends
sleep onset and/or maintenance insomnia; DORA
sleep onset and/or maintenance insomnia; DORA
sleep maintenance insomnia; FDA-approved at this dose

treatments.dosage: 5–10 mg PO at bedtime (5 mg in women, elderly)
treatments.useWhen: sleep onset insomnia; risk of complex sleep behaviors

diseases.name: Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD)
diseases.management: Psychotherapy is first-line and the only treatment with consistent evidence for BPD. No medications are FDA-approved for BPD, and pharmacotherapy does not improve core BPD symptoms. Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) is the most studied — reduces self-harm (RR 0.27 for suicidal behavior), symptom severity, and improves psychosocial functioning. Mentalization-based therapy (MBT) has comparable evidence. Other evidence-based options: schema-focused therapy, transference-focused psychotherapy, good psychiatric management. No significant differences in efficacy between these approaches. Pharmacotherapy is limited to targeted treatment of discrete comorbid disorders (e.g., SSRIs for comorbid major depression) and short-term crisis management (≤1 week — low-potency antipsychotics like quetiapine or sedative antihistamines like promethazine preferred over benzodiazepines).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Polypharmacy (common but unsupported), benzodiazepines (disinhibition, dependence, overdose risk), medications as primary treatment for BPD (no evidence for core symptoms), prolonged crisis medication use (limit to ≤1 week).

treatments.dosage: individual therapy + group skills training + phone coaching, ~1 year
treatments.useWhen: all BPD; most evidence for reducing self-harm and suicidality
comorbid major depression only; does not treat core BPD symptoms

diseases.name: Cocaine / Stimulant Use Disorder
diseases.management: No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy exists for stimulant use disorder. Contingency management is the only behavioral intervention with strong evidence — reduces stimulant use days and cravings. CBT and community reinforcement approach are also effective psychosocial treatments. Pharmacotherapy options (all off-label): for methamphetamine use disorder, extended-release naltrexone + bupropion showed higher treatment response (≥75% negative urine samples) vs. placebo in a multisite RCT. Mirtazapine has shown benefit in MSM populations. Bupropion alone may benefit lighter methamphetamine users. For cocaine use disorder: topiramate + extended-release mixed amphetamine salts (MAS-ER) in combination showed positive effects on cocaine abstinence. Modafinil may benefit cocaine use disorder without co-occurring alcohol use disorder. ASAM/AAAP 2024 guidelines provide conditional recommendations for these agents.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Prescribing psychostimulants for stimulant use disorder without addiction medicine expertise, benzodiazepines for stimulant withdrawal.

treatments.dosage: incentive-based reinforcement for negative drug screens
treatments.useWhen: all stimulant use disorders; strongest evidence
all stimulant use disorders; combine with contingency management

treatments.dosage: naltrexone 380 mg IM monthly + bupropion XL 450 mg PO daily
treatments.notes: methamphetamine use disorder

treatments.dosage: topiramate titrated to 200–300 mg/day + MAS-ER 60 mg/day
treatments.notes: cocaine use disorder; consider especially with co-occurring ADHD or AUD

diseases.name: Somatic Symptom Disorder (SSD)
diseases.management: SSD is characterized by excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to somatic symptoms causing significant distress or functional impairment. The key management principles: schedule regular appointments (not symptom-driven), establish a strong therapeutic alliance, acknowledge and legitimize symptoms, and limit unnecessary diagnostic testing and subspecialty referrals. CBT is the best-studied psychotherapy — small-to-moderate effect sizes on somatic symptom severity. Short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy and mindfulness-based therapies also show benefit. Pharmacotherapy: SSRIs or TCAs (low-dose) can alleviate symptoms. SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) may be particularly useful when pain is prominent. Referral to mental health professional when primary care management is insufficient.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Excessive diagnostic testing, multiple subspecialty referrals, opioids, benzodiazepines, dismissing symptoms as "not real."

treatments.dosage: brief, regular appointments (e.g., monthly) regardless of symptoms
treatments.useWhen: all SSD; reduces unnecessary ED visits and testing
SSD with significant distress or comorbid depression/anxiety

diseases.name: Uterine Fibroids (Leiomyoma)
diseases.management: Uterine fibroids are the most common female pelvic tumors (>60% of women aged 30–44). Asymptomatic fibroids can be managed expectantly. Symptomatic management depends on desire for future fertility and symptom type (bleeding vs. bulk). Medical therapy for AUB-L: oral GnRH antagonist combinations (relugolix + estradiol/norethindrone acetate [Myfembree], elagolix + estradiol/norethindrone acetate [Oriahnn]) are FDA-approved for up to 24 months. LNG-IUD (52 mg) reduces heavy menstrual bleeding. Tranexamic acid for acute heavy bleeding. GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for short-term preoperative use (shrink fibroids, correct anemia). Procedural options: uterine artery embolization (UAE) for uterine preservation (limited fertility data), radiofrequency ablation, MRI-guided focused ultrasound. Surgical: myomectomy (fertility-preserving — minimally invasive when feasible), hysterectomy (definitive — vaginal approach preferred when feasible). Iron replacement for associated anemia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Morcellation of fibroids without containment (risk of disseminating occult leiomyosarcoma), GnRH agonists >6 months without add-back (bone loss), expectation that fibroids resolve without menopause.

treatments.useWhen: AUB-L; may be less effective with large submucosal fibroids

treatments.dosage: 3.75 mg IM monthly or 11.25 mg IM Q3 months
treatments.useWhen: preoperative to shrink fibroids and correct anemia; add-back therapy to mitigate bone loss

treatments.notes: symptomatic fibroids with desire for future pregnancy; risk of recurrence

treatments.useWhen: completed childbearing, failed other treatments; vaginal approach preferred

diseases.name: Menopause (Vasomotor Symptoms and Genitourinary Syndrome)
diseases.management: Hormone therapy (HT) remains the most effective treatment for vasomotor symptoms (VMS) — reduces hot flash frequency by ~75% vs. placebo. Per NAMS 2022 position statement: for women 60 years or within 10 years of menopause onset without contraindications, the benefit-risk ratio is favorable. Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration, with periodic reevaluation. Women with a uterus require progestogen (or bazedoxifene) with estrogen to prevent endometrial hyperplasia. Transdermal estradiol may have lower VTE risk than oral. Non-hormonal options: fezolinetant (NK3 receptor antagonist, FDA-approved 2023 for moderate-severe VMS), SSRIs/SNRIs (paroxetine 7.5 mg is FDA-approved for VMS), gabapentin, clonidine, oxybutynin. Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM): low-dose vaginal estrogen (cream, tablet, ring) is first-line for vaginal symptoms only — minimal systemic absorption. Ospemifene (oral SERM) for dyspareunia. Vaginal DHEA (prasterone) as alternative.
diseases.notes: Avoid: HT initiation >10 years after menopause or age >60 (unfavorable risk-benefit), HT for chronic disease prevention (USPSTF recommends against), compounded bioidentical hormones (unregulated, no safety advantage).

treatments.dosage: lowest effective dose (e.g., estradiol 0.5 mg PO or 0.025–0.05 mg/day patch + micronized progesterone 100–200 mg if uterus intact)
treatments.notes: moderate-severe VMS in women 60 or within 10 years of menopause; no contraindications

treatments.useWhen: VMS; only FDA-approved non-hormonal option for VMS

treatments.dosage: estradiol cream 0.5 g 2×/week or vaginal tablet 10 mcg 2×/week
treatments.notes: vaginal dryness, dyspareunia; minimal systemic absorption

diseases.name: Preterm Labor
diseases.management: Preterm labor is defined as regular contractions with cervical change between 20 0/7 and 36 6/7 weeks. Goals: delay delivery to administer antenatal corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection. Tocolytics buy 48 hours (not proven to improve neonatal outcomes beyond allowing steroid administration). First-line tocolytic: nifedipine (oral, 20 mg loading then 10–20 mg Q4–6H) or indomethacin (32 weeks, limit to 48 hours — risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure). Antenatal corticosteroids: betamethasone 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hours apart (24 0/7 to 33 6/7 weeks — reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death). Single rescue course if ≥14 days since prior course and 34 weeks. Magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection (32 weeks — reduces cerebral palsy). GBS prophylaxis if status unknown or positive. Antibiotics for preterm PROM (ampicillin + erythromycin × 7 days — prolongs latency).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tocolysis >48 hours (no proven benefit), indomethacin ≥32 weeks (premature ductal closure), terbutaline (FDA black box warning against prolonged use), tocolysis with chorioamnionitis.

treatments.duration: one course (rescue course if ≥14 days later and 34 weeks)
treatments.notes: 24 0/7 to 33 6/7 weeks with threatened preterm delivery

treatments.dosage: 20 mg PO loading, then 10–20 mg Q4–6H
treatments.useWhen: buy time for corticosteroids; avoid in hypotension

treatments.dosage: 4–6 g IV loading over 20–30 min, then 1–2 g/hr
treatments.useWhen: 32 weeks for fetal neuroprotection (reduces cerebral palsy)

treatments.dosage: 50–100 mg PO/PR loading, then 25–50 mg Q6H

diseases.name: Placenta Previa
diseases.management: Placenta previa is an absolute indication for cesarean delivery. Transvaginal ultrasound is the diagnostic gold standard (safe in previa). Antepartum bleeding occurs in 40–60%. Management depends on bleeding status and gestational age. Asymptomatic: expectant management with pelvic rest, serial ultrasound. Planned cesarean delivery at 36 0/7 to 37 6/7 weeks for uncomplicated placenta previa without bleeding. Earlier delivery for anterior previa (higher hemorrhage risk) or recurrent bleeding. Antenatal corticosteroids if delivery anticipated 37 weeks. Active bleeding: hospitalization, IV access, type and screen, blood products available. Maternal/fetal instability → emergent cesarean regardless of gestational age. Multidisciplinary "placenta team" approach (obstetrics, anesthesia, interventional radiology) for suspected placenta accreta spectrum.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Vaginal delivery (absolute contraindication with complete previa), digital cervical exam, delay in delivery with maternal/fetal instability.

treatments.dosage: betamethasone 12 mg IM × 2 doses, 24 hr apart
pelvic rest, serial ultrasound, avoid intercourse/cervical exams
treatments.notes: asymptomatic previa

diseases.name: Placental Abruption
diseases.management: Placental abruption complicates ~1% of pregnancies. Clinical diagnosis (painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, contractions, fetal distress) — ultrasound has limited sensitivity (fresh clot is isoechoic to placenta). Risk factors: prior abruption, hypertension/preeclampsia, cocaine use, trauma, smoking. Management is individualized by severity and gestational age. Severe abruption with fetal/maternal compromise: emergent cesarean delivery. Fetal demise: vaginal delivery preferred (avoid cesarean unless maternal indication). Mild abruption at term with reassuring status: may attempt vaginal delivery with close monitoring. Preterm mild abruption with stable mother/fetus: expectant management with hospitalization, continuous monitoring, antenatal corticosteroids, and readiness for emergent delivery. Aggressive management of DIC (cryoprecipitate, FFP, platelets). Massive transfusion protocol if hemorrhagic shock. Concealed hemorrhage may be much greater than visible bleeding.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Underestimating blood loss (concealed hemorrhage), delay in delivery with fetal/maternal compromise, tocolysis in significant abruption.

treatments.notes: fetal distress, maternal hemodynamic instability, severe abruption
fetal demise; avoid unnecessary cesarean

treatments.dosage: hospitalization, continuous fetal monitoring, antenatal corticosteroids
treatments.notes: mild abruption, preterm, stable mother and fetus

treatments.dosage: PRBCs, FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets per protocol

diseases.name: Chorioamnionitis (Intraamniotic Infection)
diseases.management: Suspected when maternal temperature ≥39.0°C, or 38.0–38.9°C with one additional risk factor (maternal/fetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, purulent amniotic fluid, leukocytosis). Intrapartum antibiotics reduce neonatal sepsis, pneumonia, and bacteremia. First-line: ampicillin 2 g IV Q6H + gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV Q24H (or 1.5 mg/kg Q8H). Add clindamycin 900 mg IV Q8H at cord clamping if cesarean delivery. Post-delivery antibiotics: one additional dose may be given but extended courses are unnecessary. Antipyretics (acetaminophen) should be administered. Chorioamnionitis alone is NOT an indication for cesarean delivery — vaginal delivery is preferred. Ensure adequate labor progress (may need oxytocin augmentation — chorioamnionitis associated with dysfunctional labor). Communicate with neonatal team for neonatal evaluation. Antenatal corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate (if 32 weeks) should still be given but delivery should not be delayed to complete the course.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cesarean delivery solely for chorioamnionitis (not an indication), delaying delivery to complete corticosteroid course, withholding corticosteroids/magnesium sulfate because of infection.

treatments.dosage: ampicillin 2 g IV Q6H + gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV Q24H
900 mg IV Q8H at cord clamping
treatments.notes: cesarean delivery to reduce endometritis

diseases.name: Gestational Hypertension
diseases.management: Gestational hypertension is new-onset BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks without proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction. ~25% progress to preeclampsia. ACOG recommends antihypertensive treatment for BP ≥140/90 (updated from prior threshold of ≥160/110 based on CHAP trial — treatment at ≥140/90 reduced adverse pregnancy outcomes by 18% without increasing SGA). First-line: labetalol 200–800 mg PO BID-TID or nifedipine XL 30–120 mg PO daily. Methyldopa is an alternative. Monitor for progression to preeclampsia (weekly labs, symptom assessment). Delivery at 37 0/7 weeks if well-controlled without severe features. Low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily starting 12–16 weeks for preeclampsia prevention in high-risk patients.
diseases.notes: Avoid: ACE inhibitors and ARBs (teratogenic), atenolol (fetal growth restriction), diuretics (volume depletion).

treatments.dosage: 200 mg PO BID, titrate to max 800 mg TID
30 mg PO daily, titrate to max 120 mg/day
treatments.useWhen: gestational hypertension; avoid in aortic stenosis

diseases.name: Cervical Insufficiency
diseases.management: Cervical insufficiency is painless cervical dilation leading to recurrent second-trimester pregnancy loss. History-indicated cerclage: placed at 12–14 weeks in women with ≥3 prior second-trimester losses or prior cerclage. Ultrasound-indicated cerclage: placed when cervical length 25 mm on transvaginal ultrasound at 16–24 weeks in women with prior spontaneous preterm birth. Vaginal progesterone (200 mg nightly) is an alternative for short cervix without prior preterm birth. Rescue/emergency cerclage: placed with advanced dilation (bulging membranes) — success rates 40–60%. McDonald technique (purse-string suture) is most common. Shirodkar technique for recurrent failures. Cerclage removal at 36–37 weeks. Contraindications: active preterm labor, chorioamnionitis, PPROM, fetal anomaly incompatible with life.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cerclage with PPROM, active labor, or chorioamnionitis; cerclage in multiple gestations (no proven benefit).

treatments.dosage: McDonald or Shirodkar cerclage at 12–14 weeks
treatments.notes: ≥3 prior second-trimester losses or prior cerclage

treatments.dosage: cerclage when CL 25 mm at 16–24 weeks
treatments.notes: short cervix on TVU + prior spontaneous preterm birth

treatments.useWhen: short cervix (25 mm) without prior preterm birth

diseases.name: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma)
diseases.management: SSc is classified as limited cutaneous (lcSSc — skin distal to elbows/knees, anti-centromere Ab, risk of PAH) or diffuse cutaneous (dcSSc — proximal skin, anti-Scl-70/anti-RNA polymerase III, risk of ILD and renal crisis). Treatment is organ-based. Skin fibrosis: mycophenolate mofetil (first-line), methotrexate, cyclophosphamide, rituximab, tocilizumab. Rapidly progressive dcSSc: autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) in select patients. ILD: mycophenolate mofetil (first-line), nintedanib (FDA-approved for SSc-ILD), tocilizumab (FDA-approved for SSc-ILD). PAH: combination therapy with PDE5 inhibitor + endothelin receptor antagonist (ERA), add prostacyclin if needed. Raynaud/digital ulcers: nifedipine (first-line), PDE5 inhibitors, IV iloprost, bosentan (prevents new digital ulcers). Scleroderma renal crisis: ACE inhibitors (captopril — first-line, even if normotensive). GI dysmotility: PPI for GERD, prokinetics for gastroparesis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroids >15 mg/day prednisone equivalent (precipitate scleroderma renal crisis in dcSSc), prophylactic ACE inhibitors (paradoxically associated with worse renal crisis outcomes), cyclosporine (nephrotoxic).

treatments.notes: dcSSc skin disease and/or SSc-ILD
SSc-ILD; FDA-approved; can combine with mycophenolate
SSc-ILD; FDA-approved for slowing pulmonary function decline
Raynaud phenomenon
scleroderma renal crisis; do NOT use prophylactically (may worsen outcomes)
SSc-ILD with progressive fibrosis
early SSc-ILD with active systemic inflammation
refractory dcSSc or SSc-ILD

treatments.dosage: 1–2 mg/kg/day PO or 500–750 mg/m² IV monthly
treatments.notes: severe dcSSc or SSc-ILD

treatments.duration: rapidly progressive early dcSSc failing immunosuppression
treatments.notes: greater mRSS improvement and survival vs cyclophosphamide (ASTIS, SCOT trials); treatment-related mortality ~10%; careful patient selection essential
scleroderma renal crisis
prevention of new digital ulcers

diseases.name: Dermatomyositis / Polymyositis (Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies)
diseases.management: High-dose glucocorticoids are the mainstay of initial treatment (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, max 100 mg). For rapidly worsening disease: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days. A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant should be started concurrently or early: methotrexate (preferred for dermatomyositis per current recommendations) or azathioprine. Mycophenolate mofetil and tacrolimus are used especially when ILD is present. IVIG (2 g/kg over 2–5 days) is the only therapy with phase 3 RCT evidence in dermatomyositis — significantly reduces disease activity. Rituximab for refractory disease. Rapidly progressive ILD (especially anti-MDA5+): intensive triple therapy (glucocorticoids + cyclophosphamide + calcineurin inhibitor). Screen for malignancy (dermatomyositis has ~15–25% cancer association, especially within 3 years of diagnosis). Skin management: sun protection, topical glucocorticoids/calcineurin inhibitors.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in malignancy screening (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, age-appropriate cancer screening), hydroxychloroquine monotherapy (insufficient for muscle disease — only adjunct for skin).

treatments.useWhen: dermatomyositis; preferred over azathioprine per current recommendations
polymyositis or methotrexate intolerant; check TPMT

treatments.dosage: 2 g/kg divided over 2–5 days every 4 weeks

treatments.notes: refractory to glucocorticoids + steroid-sparing agents
anti-MDA5+ rapidly progressive ILD

diseases.name: Behçet Disease (Behçet Syndrome)
diseases.management: Behçet disease is a systemic vasculitis with recurrent oral/genital ulcers, uveitis, skin lesions, and vascular/neurologic involvement. Treatment is organ-based per EULAR 2025 recommendations. Mucocutaneous/joint: colchicine is first-line for prevention of recurrences. Apremilast (FDA-approved for oral ulcers in Behçet) is second-line. Refractory: azathioprine, TNF inhibitors, thalidomide, interferon alfa. Ocular (posterior uveitis/retinal vasculitis): azathioprine + systemic corticosteroids; monoclonal anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab) for severe/refractory disease. Vascular (DVT, arterial aneurysm): immunosuppression (azathioprine, cyclophosphamide) ± anticoagulation (controversial — risk of aneurysm rupture). Neuro-Behçet: IV methylprednisolone + azathioprine; infliximab for refractory. GI: 5-ASA, corticosteroids, azathioprine, anti-TNF.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Anticoagulation alone for vascular Behçet without immunosuppression (does not address underlying vasculitis), cyclosporine in neuro-Behçet (neurotoxic).

treatments.notes: oral/genital ulcers, erythema nodosum, arthritis
refractory oral ulcers; FDA-approved for Behçet oral ulcers
posterior uveitis, vascular involvement, neuro-Behçet

treatments.dosage: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks
treatments.notes: severe/refractory uveitis, neuro-Behçet, vascular Behçet

diseases.name: Fibromyalgia
diseases.management: Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization syndrome characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction. Non-pharmacologic interventions are first-line: aerobic exercise (strongest evidence), CBT, patient education, sleep hygiene. Pharmacologic therapy targets specific symptom domains. Three FDA-approved medications: duloxetine (pain + depression), pregabalin (pain + sleep), milnacipran (pain + fatigue). Amitriptyline (10–50 mg nightly) and cyclobenzaprine (5–10 mg nightly) are widely used off-label with moderate evidence. Multimodal approach combining exercise + pharmacotherapy + psychological support is most effective.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids (ineffective, risk of hyperalgesia and dependence), NSAIDs as monotherapy (ineffective for central pain), corticosteroids (no benefit).

treatments.useWhen: pain with comorbid depression/anxiety; FDA-approved for fibromyalgia

diseases.name: Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)
diseases.management: PAN is a necrotizing vasculitis of medium-sized arteries. Exclude HBV-associated PAN (requires antiviral therapy). Severity guides treatment using the Five Factor Score (FFS: GI involvement, renal insufficiency, proteinuria >1 g/day, CNS involvement, cardiomyopathy). Nonsevere (FFS = 0): glucocorticoids alone (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, taper over 12–18 months) — ~50% achieve remission. Severe (FFS ≥1): glucocorticoids + cyclophosphamide (IV pulse preferred: 0.6 g/m² monthly × 6 months, then switch to azathioprine or methotrexate for maintenance). Refractory: TNF inhibitors (infliximab), tocilizumab. HBV-associated PAN: short-course corticosteroids (2 weeks) + plasma exchange + antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Long-term immunosuppression in HBV-PAN (worsens viral replication), delay in cyclophosphamide for severe disease.

treatments.notes: FFS = 0

diseases.name: Raynaud Phenomenon
diseases.management: Primary Raynaud (idiopathic, no underlying disease) vs. secondary (associated with SSc, SLE, etc.). All patients: cold avoidance, smoking cessation, avoid vasoconstrictive drugs. Pharmacotherapy for moderate-severe symptoms: dihydropyridine CCBs (nifedipine) are first-line. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) are second-line. IV iloprost (prostacyclin analog) for severe/refractory cases with digital ischemia. Bosentan (ERA) to prevent new digital ulcers in SSc. Topical nitroglycerin for localized symptoms. Digital sympathectomy for critical ischemia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Beta-blockers (worsen vasospasm), ergotamines, sympathomimetics, smoking.

treatments.notes: moderate-severe RP

diseases.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)
diseases.management: APS is characterized by thrombosis and/or pregnancy morbidity with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2GPI). Risk stratification by aPL profile (triple positivity = highest risk). Primary thromboprophylaxis: low-dose aspirin for asymptomatic high-risk aPL carriers. Thrombotic APS: vitamin K antagonists (warfarin) are the mainstay — target INR 2–3 for venous thrombosis, INR 2–3 or 3–4 for arterial thrombosis. DOACs should NOT be used in triple-positive APS (TRAPS trial showed increased thrombotic events with rivaroxaban vs. warfarin). Obstetric APS: low-dose aspirin + prophylactic LMWH during pregnancy. Refractory obstetric APS: therapeutic LMWH, add hydroxychloroquine, low-dose prednisone in first trimester. Catastrophic APS: triple therapy (anticoagulation + corticosteroids + plasma exchange or IVIG) ± rituximab.
diseases.notes: Avoid: DOACs in triple-positive APS (TRAPS trial), rivaroxaban in arterial APS, abrupt anticoagulation discontinuation.

treatments.notes: APS with thrombosis; do NOT switch to DOACs in triple-positive APS

treatments.dosage: ASA 81 mg daily + enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily
treatments.notes: obstetric APS

treatments.useWhen: SLE-associated APS, refractory obstetric APS, thrombotic recurrence

treatments.duration: lifelong (for unprovoked thrombosis with persistent aPL)
treatments.notes: cornerstone of APS management; bridge with LMWH/UFH initially

diseases.name: Adult-Onset Still Disease (AOSD)
diseases.management: AOSD presents with quotidian (daily) spiking fevers, evanescent salmon-colored rash, arthritis, serositis, lymphadenopathy, markedly elevated ferritin (often >1000 ng/mL with glycosylated fraction 20%), leukocytosis, and liver enzyme elevation. NSAIDs and glucocorticoids are initial therapy. Steroid-sparing: methotrexate for arthritis-predominant disease. IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra, canakinumab — FDA-approved for AOSD) are recommended early for patients with poor prognostic factors (systemic features, high ferritin, liver involvement). IL-6 inhibitor (tocilizumab) for refractory disease, especially with prominent joint involvement. TNF inhibitors are third-line. Monitor for macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) — a life-threatening complication.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in IL-1 blockade for severe systemic disease, missing MAS (treat with high-dose corticosteroids ± cyclosporine ± anakinra).

treatments.useWhen: steroid-dependent or poor prognostic factors; rapid onset of action
FDA-approved for AOSD; longer dosing interval than anakinra

treatments.useWhen: refractory to IL-1 inhibitors, especially with prominent arthritis

treatments.notes: systemic AOSD (fever, rash, serositis predominant)

diseases.name: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII Deficiency) / Hemophilia B (Factor IX Deficiency)
diseases.management: Hemophilia severity: mild (5–40% factor activity), moderate (1–5%), severe (1%). Treatment has evolved beyond factor replacement. Hemophilia A: emicizumab (bispecific antibody mimicking FVIII, SC, FDA-approved for hemophilia A ± inhibitors) has become standard prophylaxis — maintains trough FVIII-equivalent activity of 15–20 IU/dL. Factor VIII concentrates (standard or extended half-life) remain options. Hemophilia B: factor IX concentrates (standard or extended half-life, e.g., eftrenonacog alfa). Non-factor therapies approved for both hemophilia A and B: concizumab (anti-TFPI), fitusiran (anti-antithrombin siRNA), marstacimab (anti-TFPI) — all SC, all for prophylaxis ± inhibitors. Gene therapy: valoctocogene roxaparvovec (hemophilia A) and etranacogene dezaparvovec (hemophilia B) are FDA-approved. Acute bleeds: factor replacement (or bypassing agents if inhibitors present — rFVIIa, aPCC). Desmopressin for mild hemophilia A.
diseases.notes: Avoid: aPCC (FEIBA) concurrently with emicizumab (thrombotic microangiopathy risk — use rFVIIa for breakthrough bleeds instead), IM injections, aspirin/NSAIDs.

treatments.dosage: 3 mg/kg SC weekly × 4 weeks (loading), then 1.5 mg/kg weekly or 3 mg/kg Q2W or 6 mg/kg Q4W
treatments.notes: hemophilia A ± inhibitors; does not treat acute bleeds

treatments.dosage: dose per bleed severity and target level

diseases.name: Von Willebrand Disease (VWD)
diseases.management: VWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. Type 1 (partial quantitative deficiency, ~70–80%), Type 2 (qualitative defects — 2A, 2B, 2M, 2N), Type 3 (complete deficiency, rare, severe). Treatment depends on type and clinical scenario. Desmopressin (DDAVP): first-line for Type 1 (and some Type 2A) — releases stored VWF, raises VWF/FVIII 3–5×. Perform desmopressin challenge test after diagnosis. VWF-containing concentrates (plasma-derived VWF/FVIII or recombinant VWF): for Type 2B, Type 3, desmopressin non-responders, major surgery. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid, aminocaproic acid): adjunct for mucosal bleeding, dental procedures, heavy menstrual bleeding. Hormonal therapy (COCs, levonorgestrel IUD) for menorrhagia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Desmopressin in Type 2B (paradoxical thrombocytopenia), aspirin/NSAIDs, desmopressin >3 consecutive days (tachyphylaxis + hyponatremia risk).

treatments.notes: Type 1 VWD with confirmed desmopressin response; avoid in Type 2B (worsens thrombocytopenia)

treatments.notes: desmopressin non-responders, Type 2B, Type 3, major surgery

diseases.name: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) Deficiency
diseases.management: G6PD deficiency is the most common human enzymopathy (X-linked). The cornerstone of management is prevention — avoiding oxidative triggers. Acute hemolytic episodes are usually self-limited (reticulocytes replace older, more deficient RBCs). Supportive care: hydration, folic acid supplementation, transfusion for severe anemia (Hb 7 g/dL or hemodynamic instability). Neonatal jaundice: phototherapy, exchange transfusion if severe. Chronic nonspherocytic hemolytic anemia (rare, severe variants): folic acid, monitor for gallstones, splenectomy rarely helpful, iron chelation if transfusion-dependent.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Fava beans, primaquine, tafenoquine, dapsone, rasburicase, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, methylene blue, high-dose vitamin C, naphthalene (mothballs).

treatments.dosage: avoid fava beans, primaquine, dapsone, rasburicase, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, methylene blue

treatments.useWhen: severe hemolytic anemia (Hb 7 g/dL or symptomatic)

treatments.notes: neonatal hyperbilirubinemia in G6PD-deficient neonates

diseases.name: Thalassemia (Alpha and Beta)
diseases.management: Beta-thalassemia major (transfusion-dependent, TDT): regular RBC transfusions every 2–4 weeks to maintain pre-transfusion Hb 9.5–10.5 g/dL. Iron chelation is essential (started after 10–20 transfusions or ferritin >1000 ng/mL): deferasirox (oral, first-line), deferoxamine (SC/IV infusion), deferiprone (oral, preferred for cardiac iron overload). Luspatercept (activin receptor ligand trap, FDA-approved) reduces transfusion burden in TDT. Allogeneic HSCT is curative (best outcomes in young patients with HLA-matched sibling donor). Gene therapy: betibeglogene autotemcel (Zynteglo, FDA-approved for TDT) and exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy, CRISPR-based, FDA-approved). Beta-thalassemia intermedia (non-transfusion-dependent, NTDT): monitor, transfuse for symptomatic anemia, hydroxyurea to increase HbF, luspatercept. Alpha-thalassemia: silent carrier and trait require no treatment; HbH disease may need intermittent transfusions; Hb Bart hydrops fetalis is usually fatal in utero.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Iron supplementation (unless documented iron deficiency), oxidative stress in concurrent G6PD deficiency.

treatments.useWhen: iron overload from chronic transfusions; monitor renal/hepatic function

diseases.name: Aplastic Anemia
diseases.management: Aplastic anemia is bone marrow failure with pancytopenia and hypocellular marrow. Severity: severe (ANC 500, platelets 20K, reticulocytes 20K) and very severe (ANC 200). Treatment depends on age and donor availability. Young patients (90%). Older patients or no matched donor: immunosuppressive therapy (IST) with horse ATG + cyclosporine + eltrombopag (RACE trial showed superior response with triple therapy — overall response 94% vs. 66% with ATG + cyclosporine alone). Eltrombopag (thrombo
diseases.notes: Avoid: Rabbit ATG as first-line (inferior to horse ATG for initial therapy), delay in HSCT referral (outcomes worsen with prolonged transfusion burden and alloimmunization), non-irradiated blood products in transplant candidates (transfusion-associated GVHD risk)

diseases.name: Postpartum Depression (PPD)
diseases.management: PPD affects ~10–15% of postpartum women. Screen with Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS ≥10 warrants further evaluation). SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy (sertraline preferred due to low breast milk transfer). Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) is effective alone or combined with medication. Zuranolone (Zurzuvae) is the first oral medication FDA-approved specifically for PPD — a 14-day course of 50 mg nightly showed rapid improvement (by day 3) with sustained antidepressant effects through day 45 in phase 3 trials. Brexanolone (IV infusion over 60 hours) was previously available but has been withdrawn from the market. Zuranolone may serve as a bridge to maintenance SSRI therapy. For severe PPD with suicidal ideation or psychotic features: psychiatric hospitalization, ensure infant safety. Breastfeeding-compatible medications: sertraline, paroxetine, nortriptyline. Screen for bipolar disorder before starting antidepressants (risk of mania).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Brexanolone (withdrawn from market Dec 2024), fluoxetine in breastfeeding (longer half-life, higher infant exposure), antidepressants without screening for bipolar disorder.

treatments.useWhen: PPD; low breast milk transfer; compatible with breastfeeding
all PPD; can be used alone for mild-moderate or combined with medication

treatments.dosage: 50 mg PO nightly × 14 days (take with fat-containing food)
treatments.useWhen: PPD; rapid onset (day 3); may bridge to SSRI; avoid driving/hazardous activities for 12 hr after dose

diseases.name: Adenomyosis
diseases.management: Adenomyosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, causing dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and subfertility. Diagnosis: transvaginal ultrasound (heterogeneous myometrium, asymmetric uterine wall thickening) or MRI for confirmation. No drug is specifically labeled for adenomyosis — treatment is extrapolated from endometriosis and fibroid data. LNG-IUD (52 mg) is the most effective long-term medical option for both bleeding and pain. Dienogest 2 mg daily and other progestins reduce pain symptoms. Combined oral contraceptives for younger patients desiring contraception. GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for short-term preoperative use. GnRH antagonist combinations (relugolix-based, elagolix-based) are emerging options. NSAIDs and tranexamic acid for symptomatic relief. Surgical: hysterectomy remains definitive treatment for completed childbearing. Uterine-sparing options (adenomyomectomy, uterine artery embolization, high-intensity focused ultrasound) for fertility preservation, though recurrence rates are significant.
diseases.notes: Avoid: GnRH agonists >6 months without add-back therapy (bone loss), expectation of cure with medical therapy alone (regression but not eradication).

treatments.useWhen: AUB and dysmenorrhea from adenomyosis; most effective long-term medical option
preoperative shrinkage or short-term symptom control

diseases.name: Pelvic Organ Prolapse (POP)
diseases.management: POP affects ~40% of women >40 years. Asymptomatic prolapse: observation. Symptomatic management: pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT/Kegel exercises) as first-line conservative therapy. Vaginal pessary is first-line nonsurgical treatment — up to 92% of women can be successfully fitted. Ring pessary is tried first; Gellhorn pessary if ring fails. Pessary + PFMT is superior to PFMT alone (NNT = 3 for symptom improvement at 12 months). Follow-up every 3–4 months if patient cannot self-manage pessary; annually if self-managing. Topical vaginal estrogen as adjunct (reduces erosion risk). Surgery when conservative measures fail or patient preference: native tissue repair (colporrhaphy, uterosacral ligament suspension, sacrospinous fixation) or mesh-augmented repair (sacrocolpopexy — laparoscopic/robotic preferred). Sacrocolpopexy has higher anatomic success but mesh-related complications. Obliterative procedures (colpocleisis) for women who do not desire future vaginal intercourse — high satisfaction, low morbidity.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Transvaginal mesh for anterior compartment repair (FDA removed from market 2019), surgery without trial of conservative management, neglecting pessary follow-up (erosion risk).

treatments.dosage: ring pessary first, Gellhorn if ring fails
treatments.useWhen: symptomatic POP; alternative to surgery; 92% successful fitting rate

treatments.dosage: anterior/posterior colporrhaphy, uterosacral or sacrospinous fixation
treatments.useWhen: failed conservative management; avoids mesh complications

treatments.notes: apical prolapse; higher anatomic success; mesh erosion risk 2–10%

diseases.name: Neonatal Hyperbilirubinemia
diseases.management: AAP 2022 revised guidelines: universal bilirubin screening (transcutaneous or serum) before discharge. Phototherapy thresholds are based on gestational age, age in hours, and presence of neurotoxicity risk factors (GA 38 weeks, albumin 3.0 g/dL, isoimmune hemolytic disease, G6PD deficiency, sepsis, clinical instability). Thresholds are higher than 2004 guidelines based on evidence that neurotoxicity does not occur until well above prior exchange transfusion thresholds. Intensive phototherapy: narrow-spectrum LED blue light (≥30 µW/cm² per nm at ~475 nm) to maximum skin surface area. Home phototherapy is an option for low-risk infants (≥38 weeks, ≥48 hr old, clinically well, no risk factors, TSB ≤1 mg/dL above threshold). Exchange transfusion for TSB approaching exchange threshold or signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy. Evaluate for hemolytic disease if TSB at/above phototherapy threshold at 24 hours of age. Ensure adequate feeding (breastfeeding support, supplementation if needed).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sunlight as therapeutic phototherapy (impractical, burn risk), subtracting direct/conjugated bilirubin from TSB when using thresholds, delaying evaluation for hemolysis when TSB elevated at 24 hours.

treatments.dosage: LED blue light ≥30 µW/cm² per nm, maximize skin exposure
treatments.useWhen: TSB at or above hour-specific phototherapy threshold per AAP 2022 nomograms
low-risk infants (≥38 wk, ≥48 hr, no risk factors, TSB ≤1 mg/dL above threshold)
