diseases.name: Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC)
diseases.management: NEC is a devastating intestinal inflammatory disease of preterm infants. Staged by modified Bell criteria (I: suspected; II: definite — pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas; III: advanced — perforation, shock). Medical management is first-line: NPO (bowel rest), orogastric decompression, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole for anaerobic coverage), parenteral nutrition, cardiovascular/respiratory support, serial abdominal radiographs (every 6–8 hours in acute phase). Bell stage I: antibiotics × 3 days with close monitoring. Bell stage II: antibiotics × 7–14 days. Bell stage III: surgical consultation. Pneumoperitoneum (intestinal perforation) is the only absolute indication for surgery. Relative indications: clinical deterioration despite maximal medical therapy, fixed dilated loop, severe thrombocytopenia/acidosis. Surgical options: exploratory laparotomy with resection of necrotic bowel (preferred — better neurodevelopmental outcomes) vs. peritoneal drainage (temporizing in extremely low birth weight infants). Prevention: exclusive human milk feeding, standardized feeding protocols, probiotics (emerging evidence).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgical consultation for Bell stage III, enteral feeding during active NEC, reliance on peritoneal drainage as definitive treatment.

treatments.duration: Bell I: 3 days; Bell II: 7–14 days

diseases.name: Hirschsprung Disease
diseases.management: Hirschsprung disease is congenital absence of ganglion cells in the distal bowel (most commonly rectosigmoid ~80%). Presents in neonates with delayed meconium passage (>24 hours), abdominal distension, vomiting. Diagnosis: contrast enema (transition zone), confirmed by rectal suction biopsy (absence of ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve trunks). Pre-surgical management: rectal irrigations for bowel decompression (effective for rectosigmoid disease). Total colonic disease requires diverting ileostomy. Treat Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) with rectal irrigations + IV antibiotics — most feared complication (mortality 1–10%). Definitive surgery: pull-through procedure (Swenson, Soave, or Duhamel technique) — removes aganglionic segment and anastomoses normally innervated bowel to anus. Single-stage transanal pull-through is increasingly performed in neonates. Laparoscopic-assisted approaches offer excellent outcomes. Post-operative complications: obstructive symptoms (6–40%), enterocolitis, soiling. Long-term follow-up with pediatric GI essential.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in diagnosis (risk of HAEC, perforation), barium enema without rectal irrigations in obstructed neonate, missing transition zone on biopsy.

treatments.dosage: resection of aganglionic bowel + pull-through anastomosis

diseases.name: Biliary Atresia
diseases.management: Biliary atresia is a progressive fibroinflammatory cholangiopathy — universally fatal if untreated. Presents in first weeks of life with prolonged conjugated jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly. Diagnosis: ultrasound (absent/abnormal gallbladder, triangular cord sign), hepatobiliary scintigraphy (no excretion into bowel), confirmed by intraoperative cholangiogram. Kasai hepatic portoenterostomy (HPE) is the initial surgical treatment — resection of fibrotic extrahepatic biliary tree and Roux-en-Y anastomosis to porta hepatis. Timing is critical: perform by 8–10 weeks of age for optimal transplant-free survival. Post-Kasai, ~70% achieve some bile flow; ~50% clear jaundice by 6 months (TSB 2 mg/dL within 3 months predicts prolonged transplant-free survival). Post-operative management: ursodeoxycholic acid, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K), cholangitis prophylaxis (TMP-SMX), nutritional optimization. Despite Kasai, 60–75% ultimately require liver transplantation (5-year native liver survival 37–40%). Liver transplant outcomes are excellent (10-year survival ~90%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in Kasai beyond 10 weeks (worse outcomes), missing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in neonates (always fractionate bilirubin if jaundice persists >2 weeks).

treatments.dosage: resection of extrahepatic biliary tree + Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy

prevents cholangitis; most common post-operative complication

treatments.useWhen: failed Kasai or progressive liver disease
treatments.notes: 10-year survival ~90%; living-donor transplant common in pediatrics
cholestasis impairs fat absorption; optimize nutrition pre-transplant

diseases.name: Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia (BPD)
diseases.management: BPD is defined as need for supplemental oxygen at 36 weeks' postmenstrual age in preterm infants. Prevention is the primary strategy. Evidence-based prevention (high certainty): delivery room CPAP over routine intubation (RR 0.80), early selective surfactant over delayed (RR 0.83), less invasive surfactant administration (LISA), volume-targeted ventilation over pressure-limited (RR 0.73), early inhaled corticosteroids (RR 0.86), early systemic hydrocortisone in high-risk neonates (RR 0.90). Caffeine citrate (standard of care for apnea of prematurity) also reduces BPD. Treatment of established BPD: supplemental oxygen to target SpO₂ 90–95%, diuretics (furosemide, chlorothiazide + spironolactone) for pulmonary edema, inhaled bronchodilators PRN, systemic corticosteroids (dexamethasone — late use for ventilator-dependent infants, balancing BPD reduction against neurodevelopmental risk). Nutritional optimization (enhanced caloric intake 120–150 kcal/kg/day). Post-discharge: RSV prophylaxis (palivizumab or nirsevimab), influenza vaccination, pulmonary follow-up.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Early systemic dexamethasone in first week (increased cerebral palsy risk), prolonged intubation when noninvasive support feasible, oxygen saturation targets 95% (increased ROP risk).

treatments.medication: Delivery room CPAP + early selective surfactant via LISA
treatments.useWhen: all preterm infants at risk; avoids intubation
all preterm infants 32 weeks for apnea prevention and BPD reduction

treatments.notes: ventilator-dependent infants >7 days; balance BPD reduction vs. neurodevelopmental risk

treatments.dosage: furosemide 1–2 mg/kg/dose or chlorothiazide 10–20 mg/kg/day + spironolactone 1–3 mg/kg/day

treatments.notes: reduces BPD by ~37%; also reduces need for ventilation; start early (within first 10 days)

treatments.dosage: CPAP at birth; surfactant via thin catheter if needed
treatments.notes: avoids intubation; RR 0.80–0.90 for BPD/mortality

treatments.dosage: 0.5 mg/kg BID × 7 days then 0.5 mg/kg daily × 3 days
treatments.notes: RR 0.90 for BPD/mortality; for high-risk infants (28 weeks); GI perforation risk if combined with indomethacin

treatments.dosage: various regimens (DART: 0.15 mg/kg/day × 3 days, taper over 10 days)
treatments.notes: reduces BPD/mortality; reserve for infants at highest risk; concerns about cerebral palsy with high-dose/prolonged courses

treatments.dosage: furosemide 1 mg/kg/dose or chlorothiazide 10–20 mg/kg/day + spironolactone 1–3 mg/kg/day
treatments.notes: fluid overload, pulmonary edema; monitor electrolytes; no proven long-term benefit on BPD outcomes

diseases.name: Failure to Thrive / Faltering Weight
diseases.management: AAP/NASPGHAN 2026 guidelines recommend replacing "failure to thrive" with "faltering weight." Diagnosis uses anthropometric z-scores rather than percentiles. Most common cause is inadequate caloric intake — identified by detailed feeding history and physical examination. Mild-moderate malnutrition without signs of specific illness: nutrition interventions in primary care. First-line: behavioral interventions + increased caloric intake (estimate daily calories needed vs. current intake). For breastfed infants: optimize breastfeeding technique, consider supplementation. For formula-fed: concentrate formula or add caloric supplements. Older children: calorie-dense foods, scheduled meals/snacks. If initial interventions fail: laboratory workup (CBC, CMP, celiac screening, lead level, iron studies, urinalysis, ESR). Referral to multidisciplinary team (dietitian, feeding therapist, social worker) for refractory cases. Hospitalization for severe malnutrition (z-score ≤ −3), dehydration, or concern for neglect. Monitor for refeeding syndrome in severely malnourished children. Screen for underlying conditions (celiac disease, CF, congenital heart disease, GERD) and psychosocial factors.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Extensive laboratory workup before trial of nutritional intervention (low yield in mild cases), force-feeding (worsens feeding aversion), overlooking psychosocial factors.

treatments.dosage: estimate caloric needs, increase intake by 50% above maintenance
treatments.useWhen: all faltering weight; caloric deficit correction is usually sufficient

treatments.dosage: increase formula caloric density (e.g., 24–30 kcal/oz) or add modular supplements
treatments.notes: formula-fed infants not gaining weight on standard formula

treatments.dosage: dietitian, feeding therapist, social worker, pediatric GI
treatments.useWhen: failed initial interventions, severe malnutrition, suspected organic cause

treatments.dosage: structured mealtimes (20–30 min), limit distractions, positive reinforcement, avoid force-feeding
treatments.notes: most effective when combined with caloric optimization

treatments.dosage: oral motor therapy, swallow evaluation if aspiration concern
treatments.notes: for oral motor dysfunction, food aversion, swallowing difficulties

treatments.dosage: iron, zinc, vitamin D as indicated
treatments.notes: treat documented deficiencies; vitamin D for all breastfed infants

diseases.name: Phenylketonuria (PKU)
diseases.management: PKU is an autosomal recessive disorder of phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) deficiency. Detected by newborn screening. Dietary treatment must begin within first 20 days of life to prevent intellectual disability. Mainstay: lifelong phenylalanine-restricted diet — exclude high-protein foods (meat, fish, eggs, dairy, nuts), allow measured low-Phe foods (some fruits, vegetables, grains), supplement with Phe-free amino acid formula (provides essential amino acids, vitamins, minerals). Target blood Phe: 120–360 µmol/L (2–6 mg/dL) per European guidelines. Classical PKU tolerates ~200–500 mg Phe/day. Sapropterin (Kuvan, BH4 cofactor) is FDA-approved for BH4-responsive PKU — allows liberalization of diet in ~25–50% of patients (more effective in mild/moderate PKU). Pegvaliase (Palynziq, PEGylated phenylalanine ammonia lyase) is FDA-approved for adults with uncontrolled Phe >600 µmol/L — SC injection, requires REMS due to anaphylaxis risk. Maternal PKU: strict Phe control before conception and throughout pregnancy (target Phe 120–360 µmol/L) to prevent fetal damage (microcephaly, congenital heart disease, intellectual disability).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aspartame (contains phenylalanine), over-restriction of Phe (causes malnutrition, osteoporosis), discontinuation of diet (cognitive decline even in adults), pregnancy without strict Phe control.

treatments.dosage: restrict Phe to 200–500 mg/day (classical PKU), Phe-free amino acid formula daily
treatments.useWhen: all PKU; begin within first 20 days of life

treatments.dosage: 2.5 mg SC daily, titrate to 20–40 mg daily
treatments.notes: adults with uncontrolled Phe >600 µmol/L; REMS program (anaphylaxis risk — carry epinephrine)

treatments.dosage: restrict dietary Phe to 200–500 mg/day (classic PKU); Phe-free amino acid formula to meet protein needs
treatments.notes: begin within first 20 days of life; target blood Phe 120–360 µmol/L (children); regular monitoring
~25–50% of PKU patients respond; allows increased Phe tolerance; FDA-approved

treatments.dosage: 2.5 mg SC, titrate to 20–40 mg daily
treatments.notes: FDA-approved for adults with blood Phe >600 µmol/L on existing management; reduces Phe to 120 µmol/L in ~60%; REMS program (anaphylaxis risk); injection site reactions

treatments.dosage: strict Phe control (target 120–360 µmol/L) before conception and throughout pregnancy
treatments.notes: prevents fetal PKU syndrome (microcephaly, CHD, IUGR)

diseases.name: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
diseases.management: CP is a group of permanent movement and posture disorders due to non-progressive brain injury. Classified by motor type (spastic 80%, dyskinetic, ataxic) and topography (hemiplegia, diplegia, quadriplegia). Functional classification by GMFCS (levels I–V). Management is multidisciplinary: physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, orthotic devices. Spasticity management is stepwise: (1) Physical therapy + bracing/casting (first-line). (2) Botulinum toxin A injections for localized/segmental spasticity (Level A evidence — most effective when combined with PT and orthoses; repeat no sooner than every 6 months). (3) Oral medications for generalized spasticity: baclofen (most widely used), diazepam (short-term, Level B), tizanidine (Level C). (4) Intrathecal baclofen pump for severe generalized spasticity (GMFCS IV–V). (5) Selective dorsal rhizotomy for ambulatory children with spastic diplegia (GMFCS II–III) — permanent spasticity reduction. Orthopedic surgery for fixed contractures and bony deformities (hip surveillance programs, multilevel surgery). Seizure management, nutritional support (gastrostomy if needed), communication devices.
diseases.notes: Avoid: BoNT-A as stand-alone therapy (must combine with PT/orthoses), excessive BoNT-A reinjection frequency (6 months — muscle atrophy/fibrosis), long-term benzodiazepines (tolerance, sedation).

treatments.dosage: 5–10 mg TID (titrate; max 80 mg/day in adults)
treatments.notes: generalized spasticity; sedation is dose-limiting
severe generalized spasticity (GMFCS IV–V); requires surgical implantation

treatments.useWhen: ambulatory spastic diplegia (GMFCS II–III); permanent spasticity reduction

treatments.dosage: goal-directed, task-specific, child-initiated movement training; environmental enrichment
treatments.notes: begin immediately upon diagnosis; 90% of gross motor potential reached by age 5

treatments.dosage: 3–8 U/kg total per session (lower limb ≤8 U/kg, upper limb ≤6 U/kg); per-muscle 1–6 U/kg within the session cap
treatments.notes: max 8 U/kg (300 U) in cycle 1, 10 U/kg (340 U) in subsequent cycles; focal spasticity interfering with function; most evidence-based focal treatment; combine with therapy
generalized spasticity; sedation, withdrawal risk with abrupt discontinuation
GMFCS IV–V; trial dose before pump placement; complications: catheter malfunction, infection, withdrawal

treatments.dosage: selective sectioning of dorsal rootlets L1–S2
treatments.duration: ambulatory children (GMFCS II–III) with pure spasticity; permanent spasticity reduction; requires intensive post-op rehab

treatments.dosage: AP pelvic radiograph every 6–12 months from age 12 months
treatments.notes: hip displacement in 1 in 3; early detection allows preventive intervention

diseases.name: Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)
diseases.management: DDH is the most common joint condition in infants (4–23 per 1000 live births). Risk factors: breech presentation, female sex, family history. Screening: Ortolani and Barlow maneuvers at every well-child visit up to 3 months. Ultrasound is preferred imaging 4 months; radiography after 4 months. For infants with risk factors but normal exam: repeat hip exams at every well-child visit + consider ultrasound at 6 weeks. Pavlik harness is first-line treatment for infants 90% success rate when initiated early. Wear 23 hours/day for 6–8 weeks minimum. Monitor with ultrasound every 4 weeks during bracing. If Pavlik harness fails: rigid abduction brace or closed reduction under anesthesia + spica cast. Infants >6 months or failed closed reduction: open reduction ± pelvic/femoral osteotomy. Complications of bracing: avascular necrosis of femoral head, femoral nerve palsy, skin irritation.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Restrictive swaddling with hips extended/adducted (increases DDH risk), forceful reduction, delay in referral for confirmed DDH.

treatments.useWhen: infants 90% success; monitor with serial ultrasound

treatments.dosage: under general anesthesia, hip arthrogram, spica cast × 12 weeks
treatments.useWhen: failed Pavlik harness or diagnosis at 6–18 months

treatments.dosage: open reduction with possible pelvic/femoral osteotomy
treatments.useWhen: failed closed reduction or diagnosis >18 months
90% success for Barlow-positive hips; examine every 2–4 weeks; ultrasound every 4 weeks; discontinue if no improvement by 3–4 weeks (risk of AVN

treatments.notes: Pavlik harness failure; may obviate need for surgery

treatments.dosage: reduction under GA + hip spica cast
treatments.notes: arthrogram to confirm reduction; cast × 3–6 months; AVN risk

diseases.name: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE)
diseases.management: SCFE is the most common hip disorder in adolescents (10.8 per 100,000). Associated with obesity, growth spurts, endocrine abnormalities. Classified as stable (can bear weight) or unstable (cannot bear weight — higher complication rate). Presents with limp, hip/groin/thigh/knee pain, obligatory external rotation with hip flexion (Drehmann sign), limited internal rotation. Diagnosis: AP pelvis + frog-leg lateral radiographs (stable) or cross-table lateral (unstable). Immediate management: non-weight-bearing status, urgent orthopedic referral. Standard surgical treatment for stable SCFE: in situ fixation with a single cannulated screw — prevents further slippage. Unstable SCFE: surgery within 24 hours (if not possible, delay >72 hours may reduce AVN risk); in situ fixation ± open reduction or modified Dunn procedure. Endocrine workup for atypical presentations (age 16, height/weight 10th percentile). Contralateral prophylactic pinning is controversial.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Forceful reduction (restricts blood supply → AVN), weight-bearing before surgical fixation, missing SCFE in patients presenting with knee pain only (referred pain).

treatments.notes: all stable SCFE; prevents further slippage

treatments.medication: Urgent in situ fixation ± open reduction
treatments.dosage: surgery within 24 hours; modified Dunn procedure for severe slips
treatments.notes: unstable SCFE; AVN rate up to 50%

treatments.useWhen: all SCFE upon diagnosis; prevent further slippage

diseases.name: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA)
diseases.management: JIA is the most common chronic rheumatic disease in children. ACR 2021 guidelines: treatment is stepwise based on subtype. Oligoarticular JIA: NSAIDs ± intra-articular glucocorticoid injections (triamcinolone hexacetonide preferred) → if inadequate response, methotrexate (first-line csDMARD) → if inadequate response, biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitors — adalimumab, etanercept — are most commonly used; no preferred bDMARD). Polyarticular JIA: methotrexate first-line csDMARD → bDMARDs preferred over combining/switching csDMARDs. Systemic JIA (sJIA): IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra, canakinumab) or IL-6 inhibitors (tocilizumab) are conditionally recommended as initial monotherapy — csD
diseases.notes: Avoid: csDMARDs as initial monotherapy for sJIA (strongly recommended against), long-term glucocorticoids without steroid-sparing agent, missing uveitis screening.

treatments.dosage: ibuprofen 30–40 mg/kg/day divided TID-QID or naproxen 10–20 mg/kg/day divided BID
treatments.notes: initial therapy for all JIA subtypes
oligoarticular JIA; triamcinolone hexacetonide preferred over acetonide

treatments.useWhen: inadequate response to NSAIDs ± intra-articular steroids; first-line for polyarticular JIA

treatments.dosage: adalimumab 20–40 mg SC every 2 weeks or etanercept 0.8 mg/kg SC weekly
treatments.notes: inadequate response to methotrexate in polyarticular JIA

treatments.medication: IL-1 inhibitor (anakinra or canakinumab) or IL-6 inhibitor (tocilizumab)
treatments.dosage: anakinra 1–2 mg/kg/day SC, canakinumab 4 mg/kg SC Q4 weeks, or tocilizumab 8–12 mg/kg IV Q2 weeks
treatments.notes: systemic JIA; conditionally recommended as initial monotherapy per ACR 2021

diseases.name: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura / IgA Vasculitis (IgAV)
diseases.management: IgAV is the most common systemic vasculitis in children (peak age 4–6 years). Classic tetrad: palpable purpura (lower extremities/buttocks), arthritis/arthralgia, abdominal pain, and renal involvement. Most cases are self-limited (resolving within 4–6 weeks). Supportive care is the mainstay: rest, hydration, NSAIDs for joint pain (ibuprofen — avoid if GI bleeding suspected), acetaminophen for pain. Glucocorticoids: no evidence that steroids prevent nephritis in children without renal involvement. Steroids may shorten duration of abdominal pain and joint symptoms but do not alter overall disease course. Renal monitoring is critical: urinalysis at least monthly for ≥6 months from onset (majority who develop nephritis do so within 3 months). IgAV nephritis (IgAVN): ACEi or ARB for persistent proteinuria >3 months. Oral prednisone/prednisolone for 3–6 months or pulsed IV methylprednisolone for nephrotic-range proteinuria or RPGN with histologic risk. Additional immunosuppressants (calcineurin inhibitors, cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate mofetil, rituximab) for steroid-refractory or severe nephritis per KDIGO 2025. Kidney biopsy for nephrotic-range proteinuria or impaired GFR. Intussusception is the most common surgical complication (~2–5%).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Glucocorticoids to prevent nephritis (no evidence of benefit), NSAIDs if GI bleeding suspected, missing renal monitoring (urinalysis monthly × ≥6 months).

treatments.notes: severe abdominal pain, joint symptoms; does NOT prevent nephritis

treatments.dosage: enalapril or losartan at standard pediatric doses
treatments.notes: IgAVN with persistent proteinuria >3 months

treatments.dosage: oral prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day × 3–6 months or IV methylprednisolone pulses
treatments.notes: nephrotic-range proteinuria or RPGN with histologic risk per KDIGO 2025

diseases.name: Glaucoma (Open-Angle and Angle-Closure)
diseases.management: Open-angle glaucoma (OAG): IOP lowering is the only proven treatment. First-line: prostaglandin analogs (latanoprost, bimatoprost, travoprost) — reduce IOP by 25–33%, once-daily dosing, minimal systemic side effects. Second-line: beta-blockers (timolol), alpha-agonists (brimonidine), carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide), Rho kinase inhibitors (netarsudil). Combination drops for inadequate monotherapy. Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) is an effective first-line alternative to drops (LiGHT trial: SLT achieved target IOP without drops in 74.2% at 3 years). Surgical options for refractory OAG: trabeculectomy, glaucoma drainage devices, minimally invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS). Acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG): ophthalmologic emergency. Immediate IOP lowering: IV acetazolamide 500 mg, topical timolol 0.5%, topical apraclonidine 1%, topical pilocarpine 1–2% (use once IOP 40 mmHg — ineffective at higher pressures due to iris sphincter ischemia), IV mannitol 1–2 g/kg if needed. Definitive treatment: laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) after IOP controlled. Prophylactic LPI in contralateral eye. Cataract extraction is a viable alternative if visually significant cataract present.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Pilocarpine at IOP >40 mmHg (ineffective — iris sphincter ischemia), topical vasoconstrictors in patients with cardiovascular disease, delay in AACG treatment (irreversible blindness).

treatments.notes: open-angle glaucoma; reduces IOP 25–33%
OAG; LiGHT trial showed 74.2% achieved target IOP without drops at 3 years

treatments.medication: acetazolamide + topical timolol + topical apraclonidine + topical pilocarpine
treatments.dosage: acetazolamide 500 mg IV, timolol 0.5% 1 drop, apraclonidine 1% 1 drop, pilocarpine 1–2% once IOP 40
treatments.notes: acute angle-closure crisis

treatments.dosage: after IOP controlled and corneal edema cleared
treatments.notes: all angle-closure; prophylactic in contralateral eye

diseases.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD)
diseases.management: AMD is classified as early (medium drusen), intermediate (large drusen/pigmentary changes), or late (geographic atrophy "dry" or neovascular "wet"). Early AMD: lifestyle modification (smoking cessation, healthy diet, exercise). Intermediate AMD: AREDS2 supplementation (vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, zinc oxide 80 mg, cupric oxide 2 mg daily) — reduces progression to late AMD by ~25%. Neovascular (wet) AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF injections are first-line — ranibizumab, aflibercept (2 mg and 8 mg), bevacizumab (off-label), faricimab, brolucizumab. Most require injections every 4–8 weeks initially; faricimab and aflibercept 8 mg allow dosing up to every 16 weeks. Geographic atrophy: complement inhibitors (pegcetacoplan, avacincaptad pegol) FDA-approved 2023 to slow progression, but risk-benefit profile remains uncertain. Amsler grid for home monitoring. Regular ophthalmic follow-up.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Beta-carotene in AREDS formula (replaced by lutein/zeaxanthin — beta-carotene increases lung cancer risk in smokers), delay in anti-VEGF initiation for wet AMD.

treatments.dosage: vitamin C 500 mg + vitamin E 400 IU + lutein 10 mg + zeaxanthin 2 mg + zinc 80 mg + copper 2 mg daily
treatments.notes: intermediate or late AMD in at least one eye; reduces progression ~25%

treatments.dosage: intravitreal injection every 4–16 weeks depending on agent
treatments.notes: neovascular AMD; initiate promptly to prevent severe vision loss

treatments.dosage: intravitreal injection monthly or every other month
treatments.notes: geographic atrophy; slows progression but risk-benefit uncertain

diseases.name: Cataract
diseases.management: Cataracts are the most common cause of blindness worldwide. Risk factors: age (two-thirds of population >80 years), corticosteroids, diabetes, UV exposure, trauma. Symptoms: painless progressive blurring, glare, reduced contrast sensitivity. Nonsurgical management of early cataracts: updated spectacle/contact lens prescription, magnification, increased illumination. No pharmacologic agents are proven to prevent or reverse cataracts. Surgery (phacoemulsification with IOL implantation) is indicated when visual impairment impedes activities of daily living. Cataract surgery is associated with >30% reduction in falls and 20–30% reduction in dementia risk. Performed under topical anesthesia — no preoperative bloodwork or ECG required, no need to discontinue anticoagulants. Intracameral antibiotics (moxifloxacin or cefuroxime) reduce endophthalmitis from 0.07% to 0.02%. Advanced technology IOLs (multifocal, toric) can reduce spectacle dependence but are not covered by insurance. Tamsulosin (alpha-1 blocker for BPH) increases risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome — some ophthalmologists temporarily discontinue preoperatively.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Unnecessary preoperative testing (no bloodwork/ECG needed), discontinuing anticoagulants for surgery (not required), nutritional supplements for cataract prevention (no proven benefit).

treatments.useWhen: visually significant cataract impairing ADLs; only proven treatment

diseases.name: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR)
diseases.management: DR is the leading cause of incident blindness in US adults aged 20–74. Prevention: optimize glycemic control (every 1% HbA1c reduction decreases DR risk by ~35%), blood pressure control, lipid management. Screening: annual dilated eye exam (type 2 DM at diagnosis, type 1 DM within 5 years of diagnosis). Mild-moderate NPDR: observation with annual screening. Severe NPDR/PDR: panretinal laser photocoagulation (PRP) reduces risk of severe vision loss by 50%. Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (aflibercept, ranibizumab, faricimab) are a reasonable alternative to PRP for PDR — noninferior or superior visual acuity outcomes with less peripheral visual field loss, but require more frequent visits. Center-involved diabetic macular edema (DME): anti-VEGF is first-line (aflibercept superior to bevacizumab when vision is 20/50 or worse). Faricimab and aflibercept 8 mg allow dosing up to every 16 weeks. Persistent DME despite anti-VEGF: macular laser or intravitreal corticosteroids. Aspirin is NOT contraindicated (does not increase retinal hemorrhage risk).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in referral for moderate+ NPDR or any DME, withholding aspirin for cardioprotection (no increased retinal hemorrhage risk).

treatments.dosage: aflibercept 2 mg or 8 mg, ranibizumab, faricimab, or bevacizumab (off-label) per protocol
treatments.notes: center-involved DME impairing visual acuity; aflibercept preferred if vision ≤20/50
high-risk PDR, severe NPDR; reduces severe vision loss by 50%
PDR; noninferior to PRP with less visual field loss; requires more visits

diseases.name: Retinal Detachment (Rhegmatogenous)
diseases.management: Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD) is a surgical emergency. Symptoms: flashing lights, floaters, curtain/shadow over visual field. Immediate ophthalmology referral. Three surgical approaches: (1) Pneumatic retinopexy (PR): gas bubble injection + cryotherapy/laser — outpatient, best for single superior break ≤1 clock hour. (2) Scleral buckle (SB): silicone band sutured to sclera to indent eye wall — gold standard for >60 years. (3) Pars plana vitrectomy (PPV): vitreous removal + gas/silicone oil tamponade — most commonly used for complex detachments. Surgery successful in >95% of cases. Best visual outcomes when macula is still attached at time of repair (20/40 or better in ~75% vs. ~40% with detached macula). Positioning requirements after gas tamponade. Regular follow-up — risk of detachment in contralateral eye.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in surgical repair (worse visual outcomes), missing symptoms (flashes, floaters, visual field defect warrant urgent evaluation).

treatments.dosage: intravitreal gas injection + cryotherapy or laser
treatments.notes: single superior retinal break ≤1 clock hour; outpatient procedure

treatments.dosage: silicone band sutured to sclera + cryotherapy
vitreous removal + gas or silicone oil tamponade + endolaser
treatments.useWhen: complex RRD, posterior breaks, PVR; most commonly used approach

diseases.name: Uveitis
diseases.management: Uveitis is classified by location: anterior (41–60%), intermediate (9–15%), posterior (17–23%), panuveitis (7–32%). Rule out infectious causes (toxoplasmosis, herpes, TB, HIV — 11–21% in high-income countries). Anterior uveitis: topical corticosteroid drops (prednisolone acetate 1%) are first-line + cycloplegic (cyclopentolate or atropine) for pain and synechiae prevention. Taper over weeks. Recurrent/chronic anterior uveitis: consider steroid-sparing agents. Intermediate/posterior/panuveitis: high risk of sight-threatening complications — requires systemic and/or intravitreal corticosteroids + immunosuppressive agents. First-line DMARDs: methotrexate (remission in ~52%), mycophenolate mofetil (inflammation control in ~71%). Second-line: adalimumab (extended time to treatment failure to 24 weeks vs. 13 weeks with placebo; reduced treatment failure from 78.5% to 54.5%). Intravitreal corticosteroid implants (dexamethasone, fluocinolone acetonide) for chronic noninfectious uveitis. Treat underlying systemic disease (e.g., spondyloarthritis, sarcoidosis, Behçet).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Missing infectious etiology before immunosuppression, low-dose oral prednisone alone for posterior uveitis (inadequate), prolonged topical steroids without monitoring IOP (steroid-induced glaucoma).

treatments.dosage: prednisolone 1 drop Q1–2H initially, taper over weeks; cyclopentolate 1% TID
treatments.notes: acute anterior uveitis
noninfectious intermediate/posterior/panuveitis; remission ~52%

treatments.useWhen: refractory to first-line DMARDs; reduced treatment failure from 78.5% to 54.5%
chronic noninfectious uveitis with macular edema

diseases.name: Optic Neuritis
diseases.management: Optic neuritis presents with painful monocular vision loss, dyschromatopsia, and relative afferent pupillary defect. Most commonly associated with MS (~50% lifetime prevalence in MS). Also associated with NMOSD (anti-AQP4) and MOGAD (anti-MOG). Acute treatment: high-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days (ONTT showed faster visual recovery but no effect on final visual outcome at 1 year for MS-associated ON). High-dose oral corticosteroids (1250 mg prednisone × 3 days) are bioequivalent to IV methylprednisolone per RCT. Low-dose oral prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) is contraindicated — increased recurrence risk (ONTT). Plasma exchange for steroid-refractory cases. IVIG as alternative if PLEX unavailable. MRI brain to assess MS risk — early disease-modifying therapy if high-risk MRI. NMOSD-associated ON: poorer visual outcomes; requires immediate maintenance therapy (eculizumab, satralizumab, inebilizumab). MOGAD-associated ON: responsive to steroids but relapse-prone; IVIG for maintenance.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Low-dose oral prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) as monotherapy (increased recurrence per ONTT), delay in MRI for MS risk stratification, delay in maintenance therapy for NMOSD.

treatments.useWhen: acute optic neuritis; accelerates visual recovery
bioequivalent to IV methylprednisolone per RCT; when IV access impractical

diseases.name: Ménière Disease
diseases.management: Ménière disease is characterized by episodic vertigo (≥20 minutes), fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. Management is stepwise from least to most aggressive. Lifestyle: low-sodium diet (1500–2300 mg/day), limit caffeine and alcohol. Acute vertigo attacks: vestibular suppressants (meclizine 25 mg, diazepam 5 mg) and antiemetics (ondansetron, promethazine). Maintenance: betahistine (most commonly prescribed worldwide — 24–48 mg TID; not FDA-approved in US) and/or diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide, triamterene). Vestibular rehabilitation. Hearing aids for hearing loss. Refractory disease: intratympanic dexamethasone (steroid injection through tympanic membrane — preserves hearing). Intratympanic gentamicin (chemical labyrinthectomy — effective for vertigo control but risk of further hearing loss). Surgery: endolymphatic sac decompression (hearing-sparing), vestibular nerve section (hearing-sparing, highly effective), labyrinthectomy (hearing-ablative, last resort).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Meclizine as maintenance therapy (vestibular suppressants only for acute attacks), delay in hearing aid fitting, aggressive surgical intervention before exhausting medical options.

treatments.useWhen: vertigo prevention; most commonly used worldwide; not FDA-approved in US

treatments.dosage: HCTZ 25 mg daily or triamterene 37.5–75 mg daily
treatments.useWhen: vertigo prevention; commonly used in US
refractory vertigo; risk of further hearing loss (chemical labyrinthectomy)

diseases.name: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV)
diseases.management: BPPV is the most common cause of peripheral vertigo, caused by displaced otoconia in the semicircular canals (posterior canal in 60–90%). Diagnosis: Dix-Hallpike test (triggered transient upbeat-torsional nystagmus). Treatment: Epley canalith repositioning maneuver is first-line — NNT of 3 for symptom resolution at 1 week. At 1 month, 88–98% of patients have complete resolution. Semont maneuver is equally effective (no difference in head-to-head comparison). Repeat maneuver if nystagmus persists after first attempt. Vestibular rehabilitation as adjunct for residual dizziness. Vitamin D supplementation in deficient patients reduces recurrence. Meclizine is NOT effective for BPPV and may cause unnecessary sedation. Horizontal canal BPPV: diagnosed by supine roll test, treated with Lempert (BBQ roll) maneuver. Refractory BPPV (rare): posterior canal plugging surgery.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Meclizine (not effective for BPPV, causes sedation), benzodiazepines (interfere with central compensation, increase fall risk), unnecessary imaging for classic BPPV presentation.

treatments.dosage: canalith repositioning (hold each position ≥20 seconds, head ≥20° below horizontal)
treatments.useWhen: posterior canal BPPV; NNT = 3; 88–98% resolution at 1 month
posterior canal BPPV; equally effective to Epley

treatments.notes: horizontal canal BPPV

treatments.useWhen: residual dizziness after repositioning; best outcomes with combination approach

diseases.name: Tinnitus
diseases.management: Tinnitus affects ~10–15% of adults. Evaluate for underlying causes: hearing loss (most common association), cerumen impaction, otosclerosis, Ménière disease, acoustic neuroma, medications (aspirin, aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, cisplatin). Pulsatile tinnitus warrants vascular imaging (CTA/MRA) to exclude vascular malformation, paraganglioma, or IIH. No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for tinnitus. AAO-HNS 2014 guidelines: hearing aids for patients with hearing loss (amplification reduces tinnitus perception). Sound therapy (white noise generators, environmental enrichment). CBT is the most effective behavioral intervention (reduces tinnitus distress — moderate-quality evidence). Tinnitus retraining therapy (TRT) combines counseling with sound generators. Avoid prescribing antidepressants, anticonvulsants, or benzodiazepines solely for tinnitus (insufficient evidence per guidelines). Address comorbid anxiety, depression, insomnia.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antidepressants/anticonvulsants/benzodiazepines solely for tinnitus (insufficient evidence), Ginkgo biloba (no benefit), missing pulsatile tinnitus workup (vascular imaging needed).

treatments.notes: tinnitus with coexisting hearing loss; reduces tinnitus perception

treatments.useWhen: bothersome tinnitus; most effective intervention for reducing distress

treatments.dosage: white noise generators, environmental sound enrichment
treatments.useWhen: all bothersome tinnitus; reduces contrast between tinnitus and silence

diseases.name: Epistaxis
diseases.management: Epistaxis has a 60% lifetime prevalence; ~6% seek medical attention. Most bleeds are anterior (Kiesselbach plexus). Systematic escalating approach: (1) Digital compression to lower third of nose × 15–20 minutes while leaning forward. (2) Anterior rhinoscopy — if bleeding site identified: topical vasoconstrictor (oxymetazoline) and/or silver nitrate cautery. (3) If bleeding continues or site not identified: anterior nasal packing (resorbable preferred — more comfortable, eliminates rebleeding risk on removal; nonresorbable if resorbable fails). (4) Continued bleeding despite anterior packing: posterior packing (Foley catheter or posterior pack), arterial ligation, or embolization — requires ENT consultation and hospital admission. Tranexamic acid (topical or oral) is effective adjunct. For patients on anticoagulants: resorbable packing preferred; consider temporary anticoagulant discontinuation in consultation with prescribing physician. Preventive measures: nasal saline spray, humidification, petroleum jelly to nasal mucosa.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Cauterizing both sides of septum simultaneously (perforation risk), topical vasoconst

treatments.dosage: pinch lower third of nose × 15–20 minutes, lean forward
oxymetazoline spray then cautery to bleeding site
treatments.useWhen: anterior bleeding site identified; avoid cauterizing both sides of septum (perforation risk)

treatments.dosage: resorbable (gelatin, oxidized cellulose) or nonresorbable (Merocel, Rapid Rhino)
treatments.useWhen: continued bleeding despite cautery; resorbable preferred

treatments.dosage: Foley catheter, sphenopalatine artery ligation, or angiographic embolization
treatments.useWhen: posterior epistaxis refractory to anterior packing; requires ENT and hospital admission

diseases.name: Sialadenitis (Acute Suppurative)
diseases.management: Acute suppurative sialadenitis most commonly affects the parotid gland. Risk factors: dehydration, poor oral hygiene, medications causing xerostomia, ductal obstruction (sialolithiasis). Typical flora: S. aureus, viridans streptococci, anaerobes. Conservative management is first-line: hydration, warm compresses, gland massage (milking toward duct orifice), sialagogues (lemon drops, vitamin C lozenges — contraindicated in the acute inflammatory phase per some sources), and oral hygiene. Antibiotics when infection suspected (fever, erythema, purulence at duct orifice): amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin as first-line (covers gram-positives and anaerobes). Cephalosporins and fluoroquinolones achieve highest salivary concentrations. MRSA coverage (vancomycin, linezolid) if risk factors present. Abscess formation requires surgical drainage. Sialolithiasis (most common cause of recurrent sialadenitis): stones 5 mm may pass with conservative measures; larger stones require sialendoscopy or surgical removal. Recurrent parotitis of childhood is typically self-limited.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sialagogues during acute inflammatory phase (may worsen symptoms), delay in drainage of abscess, unnecessary gland excision for first episode.

treatments.dosage: hydration, warm compresses, gland massage, sialagogues, oral hygiene

treatments.useWhen: penicillin allergy; covers gram-positives and anaerobes

treatments.dosage: endoscopic stone extraction or open excision

diseases.name: Low Back Pain / Sciatica
diseases.management: ACP 2017 and Lancet 2018 guidelines: most acute LBP improves within 4–6 weeks regardless of treatment. First-line for acute LBP: education, reassurance, advice to remain active (avoid bed rest), superficial heat, and NSAIDs or skeletal muscle relaxants if pharmacotherapy desired. Nonpharmacologic options: massage, acupuncture, spinal manipulation (second-line/adjunctive). Acetaminophen is NOT recommended (no benefit over placebo). Chronic LBP (>12 weeks): exercise therapy and CBT are first-line. SNRIs (duloxetine) have evidence for chronic LBP. Opioids: limited use with caution only. Imaging not indicated in first 4–6 weeks unless red flags (cancer, infection, cauda equina, fracture, progressive neurologic deficit). Sciatica/radiculopathy: NSAIDs, maintain activity, exercise-based PT. Oral corticosteroids have some evidence but risk-benefit is debated. Epidural steroid injections: short-term benefit only. Surgery (discectomy) for herniated disc with persistent radiculopathy >6 weeks — faster leg pain relief but 1-year outcomes similar to conservative care. Spinal stenosis: decompressive laminectomy for moderate-severe symptoms failing conservative management.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Acetaminophen (no benefit), systemic glucocorticoids for nonspecific LBP (not recommended), routine imaging 4–6 weeks without red flags, opioids as first-line.

treatments.dosage: reassurance, avoid bed rest, heat wraps
treatments.notes: all acute LBP

treatments.dosage: ibuprofen 400–600 mg TID or naproxen 250–500 mg BID
treatments.useWhen: acute or chronic LBP; GI/renal risk assessment

treatments.dosage: structured exercise program + cognitive behavioral therapy
treatments.notes: chronic LBP >12 weeks
chronic LBP; SNRI with evidence of efficacy
herniated disc with persistent radiculopathy >6 weeks failing conservative care; faster relief but 1-year outcomes similar

diseases.name: Rotator Cuff Disease (Tendinopathy / Tear)
diseases.management: Rotator cuff disease encompasses tendinopathy, partial tears, and full-thickness tears. Most patients improve with conservative management. First-line: activity modification, PT (progressive strengthening of rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers — 6–12 weeks), NSAIDs for pain. Subacromial corticosteroid injection for persistent pain (short-term benefit; limit to 2–3 injections — repeated injections may weaken tendon). Partial tears: conservative management as above. Full-thickness tears: surgical repair (arthroscopic) indicated for acute traumatic tears in active patients, failed conservative management after 3–6 months, or progressive weakness/functional limitation. Small-to-medium tears have better surgical outcomes than massive tears. Asymptomatic full-thickness tears can be observed with serial imaging. Post-operative rehabilitation: sling immobilization 4–6 weeks, then progressive ROM and strengthening over 4–6 months.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Repeated corticosteroid injections (tendon weakening), early surgical referral without trial of PT, MRI for initial evaluation of uncomplicated shoulder pain.

treatments.useWhen: all rotator cuff disease; first-line regardless of tear size

treatments.dosage: methylprednisolone 40 mg or triamcinolone 40 mg + lidocaine
treatments.useWhen: persistent pain despite PT; limit to 2–3 injections
acute traumatic tear, failed conservative management 3–6 months, progressive weakness

diseases.name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
diseases.management: Adhesive capsulitis progresses through freezing (painful), frozen (stiff), and thawing phases over 1–3 years. Often self-limited but ~40% report persistent symptoms at 4 years. Diagnosis is clinical: global restriction of passive glenohumeral motion (especially external rotation). Imaging reserved to exclude other pathology. First-line: PT (stretching, ROM exercises, manual therapy) + oral NSAIDs. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides meaningful short-term benefit (especially in freezing phase) — lower doses (10 mg) may be as effective as higher doses (40 mg). Hydrodilatation (distension arthrography) with corticosteroid ± hyaluronic acid shows benefit. UK FROST trial: no superiority among PT, manipulation under anesthesia (MUA), or arthroscopic capsular release at 12 months — PT was accessed more quickly and was lower cost. MUA was most cost-effective. Arthroscopic capsular release reserved for refractory cases (failed 9–12 months of conservative management). Diabetes is a major risk factor and associated with more refractory disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aggressive stretching in freezing phase (worsens pain), expectation of rapid recovery (natural history 1–3 years), neglecting diabetes screening.

treatments.useWhen: all adhesive capsulitis; accessed most quickly, lowest cost
failed conservative management; most cost-effective surgical option
refractory cases after 9–12 months; fewer patients require further treatment

diseases.name: Plantar Fasciitis
diseases.management: Plantar fasciitis is the most common cause of heel pain. Typically self-limiting but mean symptom duration is ~2 years. APTA 2024 guidelines: first-line includes stretching (plantar fascia-specific and gastrocnemius-soleus), strengthening exercises (foot/ankle musculature), and short-term antipronation taping. Prefabricated foot orthoses provide modest benefit (not as sole therapy per APTA). Night splints for recalcitrant symptoms (>6 months). Corticosteroid injections: short-term pain relief (1 month) but no benefit beyond 1–6 months. Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT) demonstrates broad efficacy across all timepoints. PRP injections support long-term improvement. Oral NSAIDs have limited evidence (celecoxib not superior to placebo). Surgical plantar fasciotomy reserved for refractory cases (>6–12 months of failed conservative treatment).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Oral NSAIDs as primary treatment (limited evidence), repeated corticosteroid injections (fat pad atrophy, fascia rupture), orthotics as sole therapy.

treatments.dosage: plantar fascia stretching, gastrocnemius-soleus stretching, foot/ankle resistance training
treatments.useWhen: all plantar fasciitis; supervised exercise more beneficial than home exercise alone
adjunct to stretching/strengthening; not as sole therapy; custom orthoses not superior

treatments.notes: symptoms >6 months; 73% satisfaction

treatments.useWhen: severe pain; benefit 1 month only; risk of fat pad atrophy/plantar fascia rupture
recalcitrant plantar fasciitis; broad efficacy across timepoints

diseases.name: Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow)
diseases.management: Lateral epicondylitis affects 1–3% of adults. Self-limiting in ~80% within 1 year. First-line: activity modification, counterforce bracing (forearm strap), eccentric strengthening exercises (wrist extensors). PT with eccentric exercises is the most effective conservative treatment. Topical NSAIDs preferred over oral (fewer systemic side effects). Corticosteroid injection: short-term benefit (6 weeks) but worse long-term outcomes than placebo at 6–12 months — associated with higher recurrence. PRP injections: emerging evidence of benefit for chronic cases (>3 months), but data are mixed. Autologous blood injection: similar to PRP. Nitroglycerin patches: some evidence of benefit. ESWT: conflicting evidence. Surgery (open or arthroscopic debridement of ECRB origin) reserved for refractory cases (>6–12 months).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Corticosteroid injection (short-term benefit but worse long-term outcomes and higher recurrence), prolonged immobilization, early surgical referral.

treatments.dosage: wrist extensor eccentric strengthening, counterforce brace

treatments.useWhen: pain relief; fewer systemic side effects than oral NSAIDs

treatments.notes: chronic lateral epicondylitis >3 months; emerging evidence

diseases.name: Meniscal Tear / ACL Injury
diseases.management: Meniscal tears: evidence-based guidelines recommend PT-based rehabilitation (≥3 months) as first-line for most traumatic and degenerative tears. No evidence that surgery is superior to rehabilitation for most tears. Exceptions requiring surgery: locked knee (displaced bucket-handle tear), tears with concurrent ACL rupture. Meniscal repair preferred over meniscectomy when tear involves peripheral vascularized zone (outer 10–30%). Degenerative tears: exercise therapy is first-line with few additional benefits from surgery. ACL injury: management depends on activity level, instability, and associated injuries. Nonoperative management (PT-based rehabilitation focusing on quadriceps/hamstring strengthening, neuromuscular training) is appropriate for low-demand patients without instability. ACL reconstruction indicated for: active patients with recurrent instability, high-demand athletes, concurrent repairable meniscal tears. Reconstruction with autograft (bone-patellar tendon-bone or hamstring) preferred. Return to sport: 9–12 months post-reconstruction with functional testing criteria met.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy for degenerative tears (no benefit over PT), early return to sport after ACL reconstruction (9 months — higher reinjury risk), neglecting concurrent meniscal pathology during ACL reconstruction.

treatments.notes: most traumatic and degenerative meniscal tears; no evidence surgery is superior
peripheral vascularized zone tears; preferred over meniscectomy for long-term joint preservation

treatments.useWhen: active patients with recurrent instability, high-demand athletes, concurrent repairable meniscal tears; return to sport 9–12 months

treatments.notes: low-demand patients without instability

diseases.name: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
diseases.management: Lumbar spinal stenosis affects ~11% of US adults; prevalence increases with age. Diagnosis: clinical history of neurogenic claudication (back/leg pain provoked by lumbar extension, relieved by flexion) confirmed by MRI. First-line: education, NSAIDs if tolerated, PT (flexion-based exercises, stationary cycling). Natural history: ~1/3 improve, ~50% stable, ~10–20% worsen over 3 years without surgery. Epidural steroid injections: short-term benefit only; long-term benefit not demonstrated. Decompressive laminectomy for moderate-severe symptoms failing conservative management — improves symptoms more than nonoperative therapy. Fusion added only for concurrent instability (spondylolisthesis). MILD procedure (minimally invasive lumbar decompression) and interspinous spacers are emerging options.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Routine imaging without red flags, opioids (not recommended per Danish guidelines), acetaminophen (no benefit), expectation that epidural injections provide long-term relief.

treatments.useWhen: all lumbar spinal stenosis; can be prescribed without imaging
short-term pain relief; long-term benefit not demonstrated
moderate-severe symptoms failing conservative management; add fusion only for instability

diseases.name: Lumbar Disc Herniation
diseases.management: Most herniated discs improve with conservative therapy within 6 weeks. Reassure patients regarding favorable prognosis — avoid alarming terminology ("ruptured disc"). First-line: NSAIDs, exercise-based PT, maintain activity. Imaging (MRI) not indicated unless symptoms persist >4–6 weeks and patient is a candidate for injection or surgery. Epidural glucocorticoid injections may offer temporary relief for severe pain. Surgery (microdiscectomy) for persistent radiculopathy >6 weeks with congruent symptoms, exam findings, and MRI — faster leg pain relief but 1-year outcomes generally do not differ from prolonged conservative therapy. Cauda equina syndrome (urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness) requires emergent surgical decompression.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Early imaging (4–6 weeks without red flags), alarming terminology, surgery without trial of conservative care (unless cauda equina syndrome).

treatments.useWhen: all lumbar disc herniation without severe neurologic deficit
persistent radiculopathy >6 weeks with congruent clinical-MRI findings; faster relief but 1-year outcomes similar to conservative care

diseases.name: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Osteonecrosis)
diseases.management: AVN affects ~20,000 patients/year in the US, predominantly young adults (3rd–5th decade). Risk factors: corticosteroids, alcohol, SLE, sickle cell disease, trauma. Staged by ARCO classification (I–IV). Pre-collapse disease (ARCO I–II): joint preservation is the goal. Core decompression is the surgical mainstay for pre-collapse disease — reduces intraosseous pressure and promotes revascularization. Augmentation with bone marrow aspirate concentrate (BMAC), mesenchymal stem cells, or bone grafting improves outcomes. Hip arthroscopy combined with core decompression shows promising results. Pharmacologic adjuncts: bisphosphonates and denosumab may slow progression but remain adjunctive. Protected weight-bearing during treatment. Post-collapse disease (ARCO III–IV): total hip arthroplasty (THA) — excellent long-term outcomes even in younger patients. Eliminate modifiable risk factors (reduce corticosteroid dose, alcohol cessation).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in diagnosis (progression to collapse is irreversible), continued corticosteroid use without reassessment, expectation that pharmacotherapy alone prevents collapse.

treatments.dosage: core decompression + BMAC or bone grafting
treatments.notes: ARCO I–II; best outcomes in early-stage disease
ARCO III–IV with femoral head collapse; excellent long-term outcomes

diseases.name: Stress Fracture (Bone Stress Injury)
diseases.management: Stress fractures are classified by anatomic risk: low-risk (medial tibia, metatarsal shaft, fibula) vs. high-risk (femoral neck tension side, anterior tibial cortex, navicular, 5th metatarsal base, sesamoids). Low-risk sites: activity modification (no activity causing pain >3/10), acetaminophen for pain, reassess every 1–3 weeks with provocative testing. Immobilization (CAM boot, air cast) if walking causes pain. Gradual return to activity once pain-free. High-risk sites: immobilization, non-weight-bearing, urgent referral to sports medicine or orthopedics — may require prophylactic surgical fixation even for lower-grade injuries. Femoral neck tension-side fractures require operative fixation. Evaluate for RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport), vitamin D deficiency, and calcium intake. MRI is the reference standard for early detection and severity grading. NSAIDs are controversial (reports of increased nonunion risk).
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (controversial — possible increased nonunion risk), return to activity before pain-free, missing RED-S evaluation in athletes.

treatments.dosage: avoid pain-provoking activity, CAM boot if needed
treatments.notes: low-risk stress fractures; gradual return when pain-free
high-risk sites (femoral neck tension side, anterior tibia, navicular, 5th metatarsal base)

treatments.dosage: prophylactic screw fixation or intramedullary nailing
treatments.notes: femoral neck tension-side fractures, displaced high-risk fractures

diseases.name: Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis (AIS)
diseases.management: AIS is the most common form of scoliosis. Treatment is guided by Cobb angle and skeletal maturity. Curves 20°: observation with scoliosis-specific PT and serial monitoring until skeletal maturity. Curves 20°–45°: bracing (TLSO brace worn ≥12–18 hours/day) + scoliosis-specific PT. BrAIST trial demonstrated bracing reduces progression to ≥50° (72% success vs. 48% observation). Nighttime bracing (8–12 hours) is an effective alternative for patients who reject full-time bracing. Curves >45–50°: surgical consultation — posterior spinal fusion with instrumentation. Sports participation is permitted as tolerated at all stages. USPSTF 2018: insufficient evidence to recommend for or against routine screening (I statement). SOSORT guidelines: observation 46–50°.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Unnecessary imaging in mild curves without progression, restricting sports participation, neglecting brace compliance monitoring.

treatments.dosage: serial exams and radiographs, scoliosis-specific exercises
treatments.notes: curves 20°; monitor for progression
curves 20°–45° with remaining growth; BrAIST: 72% success vs. 48% observation

diseases.name: Heat Stroke
diseases.management: Heat stroke is defined as core temperature >40°C with CNS dysfunction (altered mental status, seizures, coma). Rapid cooling is the cornerstone — delays worsen outcomes. Target: reduce core temperature to 38–39°C within 30 minutes. Cold-water or ice-water immersion is first-line (cooling rate 0.20–0.35°C/min). If immersion not feasible: pour water over body and fan (0.10°C/min), cold IV fluids, ice packs to neck/groin/axillae. Continuous core temperature monitoring (rectal probe) is imperative. Begin cooling before and during transport. Benzodiazepines for agitation, shivering, and seizures. After initial cooling, monitor in ICU for progression to hematologic-enzymatic and hepatic-renal phases. Classic heat stroke (elderly, comorbid): conductive/evaporative cooling may be better tolerated than immersion.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Antipyretics (ineffective, aggravate coagulopathy and liver injury), dantrolene (no evidence of benefit), delay in cooling for any reason other than CPR.

treatments.dosage: immerse in cold/ice water; cooling rate 0.20–0.35°C/min
treatments.useWhen: all heat stroke; initiate in field before transport

treatments.dosage: pour water + fan, cold IV fluids, ice packs to neck/groin/axillae
treatments.useWhen: when immersion not feasible; classic heat stroke in elderly

treatments.dosage: diazepam 5–10 mg IV or midazolam 2–5 mg IV/IM

diseases.name: Hypothermia
diseases.management: Hypothermia classified by core temperature: mild (32–35°C), moderate (28–32°C), severe (28°C). Mild: passive external rewarming (remove wet clothing, warm blankets, warm environment). Moderate: active external rewarming (forced warm air blankets — Bair Hugger, warm IV fluids 40–42°C). Severe: active core rewarming (warm humidified oxygen, warm IV fluids, warm bladder/peritoneal lavage, ECMO/cardiopulmonary bypass for cardiac arrest or hemodynamic instability). Handle patients gently (risk of VF with rough handling). Cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia: continue CPR, limit defibrillation attempts to 1 until core temp >30°C, withhold IV medications until >30°C (per AHA). ECMO is the gold standard for rewarming in cardiac arrest — survival rates up to 50% with good neurologic outcomes. "No one is dead until warm and dead" — resuscitation should continue until core temperature ≥32°C.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Rough handling (VF risk), repeated defibrillation at core temp 30°C, IV medications at core temp 30°C, premature termination of resuscitation.

treatments.dosage: remove wet clothing, warm blankets, warm environment
treatments.notes: mild hypothermia (32–35°C)

treatments.dosage: forced warm air blankets + warm IV fluids (40–42°C)
treatments.notes: moderate hypothermia (28–32°C)
severe hypothermia with cardiac arrest; survival up to 50%

diseases.name: Drowning
diseases.management: Drowning is a leading cause of unintentional death worldwide. Rescue from water → immediate assessment of breathing and pulse. Begin CPR immediately if apneic/pulseless — prioritize rescue breaths (hypoxia is the primary mechanism of death). Remove wet clothing, prevent further heat loss. All submersion victims with any respiratory symptoms require observation (minimum 4–8 hours — delayed pulmonary edema possible). Supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia. Intubation and mechanical ventilation with lung-protective strategy (low tidal volume, PEEP) for respiratory failure/ARDS. Targeted temperature management for comatose survivors. Cervical spine precautions only if mechanism suggests injury (diving, high-impact). Prognostication is difficult — cold water submersion may be neuroprotective (especially in children). Heimlich maneuver is NOT recommended (does not remove water from lungs, delays ventilation).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Heimlich maneuver (does not remove water, delays ventilation), routine cervical spine immobilization without mechanism, premature prognostication (cold water may be neuroprotective).

treatments.dosage: standard BLS/ACLS with emphasis on ventilation
treatments.useWhen: apneic/pulseless drowning victim; prioritize rescue breaths

treatments.notes: all symptomatic submersion victims; delayed pulmonary edema possible

treatments.dosage: low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW), PEEP

diseases.name: Electrical Injury
diseases.management: Electrical injuries range from minor to fatal. High-voltage (>1000 V) injuries cause deep tissue damage along current pathway. Low-voltage (1000 V) injuries more commonly cause cardiac arrhythmias. Lightning strikes cause immediate cardiac arrest (asystole or VF). Immediate management: ensure scene safety, remove from electrical source, standard ACLS for cardiac arrest. Cardiac monitoring: all patients with high-voltage exposure, loss of consciousness, abnormal ECG, or cardiac symptoms require ≥24 hours of continuous monitoring. Asymptomatic patients with normal ECG after low-voltage exposure can be discharged. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for
diseases.notes: Avoid: Relying on cutaneous burn size to estimate injury severity (deep tissue damage far exceeds surface burns), premature wound closure (tissue demarcation takes days), succinylcholine for intubation (prolonged paralysis if cholinesterase inhibited).

treatments.useWhen: high-voltage exposure, LOC, abnormal ECG, cardiac symptoms; low-voltage with normal ECG and asymptomatic can be discharged

treatments.notes: high-voltage injuries; Parkland formula underestimates needs

treatments.dosage: debridement at 24–48 hours, repeat as needed, flap coverage when demarcated
treatments.useWhen: all high-voltage burns with deep tissue injury

diseases.name: High-Altitude Illness (AMS / HACE / HAPE)
diseases.management: Wilderness Medical Society 2024 guidelines. Acute mountain sickness (AMS): onset within 1–2 days above 2500 m. Mild AMS: stop ascent, rest at current altitude, NSAIDs/acetaminophen for headache, antiemetics for nausea. Moderate-severe AMS: dexamethasone 4 mg every 6 hours + consider descent. Acetazolamide 250 mg BID may be added as adjunct. Prevention: gradual ascent (≤500 m/day above 2500 m), acetazolamide 125 mg BID or dexamethasone 2 mg Q6H for moderate/high-risk profiles. HACE: global encephalopathy with ataxia and altered mental status — immediate descent, supplemental O₂ to SpO₂ >90%, dexamethasone 8 mg initial then 4 mg Q6H. Portable hyperbaric chamber (Gamow bag) if descent not feasible. HAPE: onset 2–4 days after ascent above 3000 m. Treatment: descent, supplemental O₂, bed rest. Nifedipine SR 30 mg Q12H if O₂ unavailable and descent not feasible. Prevention in those with prior HAPE: nifedipine SR 30 mg Q12H. No role for diuretics in HAPE.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Further ascent with ongoing symptoms, diuretics for HAPE (intravascular volume depletion), low-dose oral prednisone (not studied for altitude illness), reliance on acetazolamide alone for HACE treatment.

treatments.dosage: AMS: 4 mg PO/IM Q6H; HACE: 8 mg initial then 4 mg Q6H
treatments.duration: until descent achieved or symptoms resolved
treatments.notes: moderate-severe AMS or HACE

treatments.dosage: prevention: 125 mg PO BID; treatment: 250 mg PO BID
treatments.useWhen: prevention of AMS; adjunct to dexamethasone for treatment; facilitates acclimatization

treatments.notes: HAPE when O₂ unavailable and descent not feasible; prevention in those with prior HAPE
all moderate-severe altitude illness; lifesaving for HACE and HAPE

diseases.name: Snakebite Envenomation
diseases.management: ~7,000–8,000 venomous snakebites/year in the US (mostly pit vipers — rattlesnakes, copperheads, cottonmouths). Antivenom is the definitive treatment. Crotalidae polyvalent immune Fab (CroFab) and Crotalidae immune F(ab')₂ (Anavip) are FDA-approved for pit viper envenomation in the US. Wilderness Medical Society 2026 guidelines classify severity: dry bite (observation 12 hours), minor (nonprogressive — antivenom), moderate-severe (progressive symptoms/systemic signs — antivenom + supportive care, admit). Initial labs: CBC, PT/INR, fibrinogen, CMP, CK, type and screen. Repeat labs every 1 hour after antivenom until initial control achieved. Elevate bitten extremity during antivenom infusion. Wound care: clean, tetanus prophylaxis. First aid: immobilize limb, remove constricting jewelry, transport to hospital. Contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for antivenom guidance.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tourniquets (worsen tissue injury), suction devices (ineffective), ice/cryotherapy (tissue injury), incision and drainage, prophylactic antibiotics (no benefit unless necrosis), fasciotomy (antivenom + elevation preferred — fasciotomy not shown to improve outcomes over antivenom alone).

treatments.dosage: CroFab: 4–6 vials IV initially, repeat until initial control; Anavip: 10 vials IV initially
treatments.useWhen: all pit viper envenomation with progressive local or systemic effects

treatments.dosage: IV fluids, pain management (opioids PRN), wound care, tetanus prophylaxis

diseases.name: Organophosphate / Carbamate Poisoning
diseases.management: Organophosphates inhibit acetylcholinesterase → muscarinic excess (DUMBELS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation) + nicotinic excess (fasciculations → paralysis) + CNS effects (seizures, altered mental status). AHA 2025 guidelines: cornerstones of treatment are decontamination, atropine, benzodiazepines, and oximes. Decontamination: remove clothing, copious soap and water irrigation (rescuers must wear PPE). Atropine is the primary antidote — blocks muscarinic effects. Initial dose: adults 2–4 mg IV, children 0.05 mg/kg IV. Double dose every 5 minutes until atropinization achieved (clear lungs on auscultation, HR >80, SBP >80). Maintenance atropine infusion. Very large cumulative doses may be required. Pralidoxime (2-PAM): reactivates acetylcholinesterase (reverses nicotinic effects/paralysis). Adults: 30 mg/kg IV bolus then 8–10 mg/kg/hr infusion. Must be given early before "aging" occurs. High-dose pralidoxime favored over low-dose. Benzodiazepines: diazepam (first-line) or midazolam for seizures and agitation. Early intubation improves outcomes. Avoid succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis due to pseudocholinesterase inhibition), delay in atropine administration, inadequate atropine dosing (relative tolerance in OP poisoning), withholding pralidoxime when class of poison unknown.

treatments.dosage: adults 2–4 mg IV, double Q5 min until atropinized; children 0.05 mg/kg IV
treatments.useWhen: all symptomatic OP/carbamate poisoning; blocks muscarinic effects only

treatments.dosage: adults 30 mg/kg IV bolus then 8–10 mg/kg/hr infusion
treatments.useWhen: significant OP poisoning (fasciculations, weakness, paralysis); give early before aging; high-dose preferred

treatments.dosage: diazepam 5–10 mg IV or midazolam 5 mg IV/IM
treatments.useWhen: seizures, agitation; diazepam is first-line per AHA

diseases.name: Cyanide Poisoning
diseases.management: Cyanide inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase → arrest of cellular respiration → lactic acidosis, cardiovascular collapse, seizures, death within minutes. Sources: structure fires (most common), industrial exposure, ingestion of cyanide salts. AHA 2025 guidelines: hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12a) is the primary recommended antidote — scavenges cyanide to form nontoxic cyanocobalamin. Does not cause methemoglobinemia or hypotension, making it safe in concurrent CO poisoning. Adults: 5 g IV over 15 minutes; children: 70 mg/kg IV (max 5 g). Alternative: sodium nitrite 300 mg IV (adults) or 0.2 mL/kg (children, max 10 mL) — induces methemoglobinemia to bind cyanide. Avoid sodium nitrite in concurrent CO poisoning (worsens oxygen-carrying capacity). Sodium thiosulfate 12.5 g IV (adults) or 1 mL/kg (children, max 50 mL) enhances cyanide elimination — too slow for monotherapy but synergistic with hydroxocobalamin or sodium nitrite. Administer 100% O₂. Empiric treatment if clinical suspicion is high (confirmatory testing not rapidly available).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Sodium nitrite in concurrent CO poisoning (worsens methemoglobinemia and oxygen-carrying capacity), delay in empiric treatment while awaiting confirmatory labs (not rapidly available), mouth-to-mouth resuscitation in cyanide gas exposure (rescuer risk).

treatments.dosage: adults 5 g IV over 15 min; children 70 mg/kg IV (max 5 g)
treatments.useWhen: suspected cyanide poisoning; safe in concurrent CO poisoning; 72% survival in fire-related cases

treatments.dosage: sodium nitrite 300 mg IV (adults) then sodium thiosulfate 12.5 g IV immediately after
treatments.useWhen: cyanide poisoning when hydroxocobalamin unavailable and CO poisoning not a concern
enhances cyanide elimination; synergistic with hydroxocobalamin or sodium nitrite; too slow for monotherapy

treatments.dosage: high-flow O₂ via NRB or ventilator
treatments.useWhen: all suspected cyanide poisoning; animal studies suggest benefit with antidotes

diseases.name: Anticholinergic Toxidrome
diseases.management: Anticholinergic toxidrome results from blockade of muscarinic receptors. Common causes: antihistamines (diphenhydramine), TCAs, antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine), atropine, scopolamine, jimsonweed. Classic presentation mnemonic — "Hot as a hare (hyperthermia), Blind as a bat (mydriasis), Dry as a bone (anhidrosis, dry mucous membranes), Red as a beet (flushed skin), Mad as a hatter (delirium/agitation), Full as a flask (urinary retention)." Also: tachycardia, decreased bowel sounds, seizures. Key distinguishing feature from sympathomimetic toxidrome: DRY skin and absent bowel sounds (sympathomimetics cause diaphoresis). Treatment: supportive care is the mainstay — IV fluids, cooling for hyperthermia, benzodiazepines for agitation/seizures, bladder catheterization for urinary retention. Physostigmine (tertiary acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that crosses BBB) is the specific antidote for severe anticholinergic toxicity (delirium, agitation, seizures, tachyarrhythmias). Dose: 0.5–2 mg IV slow push over 5 minutes; may repeat in 20–30 minutes. Contraindicated in TCA overdose (risk of asystole) and with conduction delays on ECG.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Physostigmine in TCA overdose (risk of asystole) or with QRS prolongation, flumazenil (may precipitate seizures if co-ingestion), restraints without sedation (worsens hyperthermia/rhabdomyolysis).

treatments.dosage: IV fluids, cooling, lorazepam 1–2 mg IV for agitation/seizures
0.5–2 mg IV slow push over 5 min; may repeat in 20–30 min
treatments.notes: severe anticholinergic delirium, agitation, tachyarrhythmias; contraindicated in TCA overdose or conduction delays

diseases.name: Sympathomimetic Toxidrome
diseases.management: Sympathomimetic toxidrome results from excessive catecholamine activity. Common causes: amphetamines, methamphetamine, cocaine, cathinones ("bath salts"), MDMA, synthetic cannabinoids. AHA 2025 guidelines: clinical manifestations include tachycardia, hypertension, agitation, seizures, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, and metabolic acidosis. Can cause VF/VT, myocardial infarction (vasospasm even with normal coronaries), takotsubo cardiomyopathy. Key distinguishing feature from anticholinergic toxidrome: DIAPHORESIS (wet skin). Treatment: benzodiazepines are the cornerstone — treat agitation, seizures, hypertension, and tachycardia. Aggressive cooling for hyperthermia (>40°C is rapidly life-threatening). IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis. Avoid beta-blockers (unopposed alpha stimulation → worsened hypertension). For refractory hypertension: phentolamine, nitroprusside, or nicardipine. Cocaine-specific: avoid beta-blockers; benzodiazepines + nitroglycerin for chest pain; phentolamine for refractory hypertension.

treatments.dosage: diazepam 5–10 mg IV or midazolam 5 mg IV/IM; repeat as needed
treatments.useWhen: all sympathomimetic toxicity; treats agitation, seizures, hypertension, tachycardia

treatments.dosage: ice water immersion, evaporative cooling, cold IV fluids
phentolamine 5 mg IV or nicardipine 5–15 mg/hr IV
treatments.notes: hypertension refractory to benzodiazepines

diseases.name: Mitral Stenosis
diseases.management: Mitral stenosis (MS) is most commonly caused by rheumatic heart disease (globally); other causes include mitral annular calcification (elderly), congenital, carcinoid, and radiation. The stenotic valve creates a diastolic pressure gradient between LA and LV → elevated LA pressure → pulmonary congestion, pulmonary hypertension, AF, and right heart failure. Severity grading: mild (MVA >1.5 cm²), moderate (1.0–1.5 cm²), severe (1.0 cm²). Medical management includes rate control (beta-blockers or nondihydropyridine CCBs to prolong diastolic filling time), diuretics for congestion, and anticoagulation for AF or prior embolism. Intervention is indicated for symptomatic severe MS: percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy (PMBC) is preferred if valve morphology is favorable (Wilkins score ≤8, no significant MR, no LA thrombus). Surgical mitral valve replacement for unfavorable anatomy or failed PMBC. Secondary prevention of rheumatic fever with penicillin prophylaxis if rheumatic etiology.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tachycardia (shortens diastolic filling time → worsens symptoms), excessive diuresis (preload-dependent), anticoagulation with DOACs in rheumatic MS with AF (warfarin preferred — DOACs not validated)

treatments.notes: rate control to prolong diastolic filling
rate control if beta-blocker intolerant
pulmonary congestion
AF, prior embolism, or LA thrombus

treatments.duration: symptomatic severe MS with favorable valve morphology (Wilkins ≤8)
treatments.notes: contraindicated if moderate-severe MR or LA thrombus

treatments.medication: Penicillin V or benzathine penicillin G

diseases.name: Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome
diseases.management: OHS is defined by the triad of obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²), daytime hypercapnia (PaCO₂ ≥45 mmHg), and sleep-disordered breathing, in the absence of other causes of hypoventilation (neuromuscular disease, severe COPD, chest wall deformity). ~90% of OHS patients have concurrent OSA. Pathophysiology involves reduced respiratory system compliance, increased work of breathing, leptin resistance, and blunted central chemoreceptor response to CO₂. Management: (1) positive airway pressure (PAP) therapy — CPAP is first-line if concurrent OSA (most patients); bilevel PAP (BiPAP) with backup rate for those with persistent hypercapnia on CPAP or predominant hypoventilation without OSA; (2) weight loss (target BMI 35; bariatric surgery if BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities — most effective long-term intervention); (3) supplemental oxygen if persistent hypoxemia despite PAP; (4) avoid respiratory depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines, sedatives). Acute decompensation (acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure) treated with NIV (BiPAP) in the ICU.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Opioids, benzodiazepines, sedatives (worsen hypoventilation), supplemental O₂ without PAP (may suppress respiratory drive and worsen hypercapnia)

treatments.notes: OHS with concurrent OSA (~90%)
OHS without OSA, or persistent hypercapnia on CPAP

diseases.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis
diseases.management: HH is an autosomal recessive iron overload disorder, most commonly caused by homozygous C282Y mutation in the HFE gene (>90% of cases in Northern European descent). Excess iron deposits in the liver (cirrhosis), heart (cardiomyopathy), pancreas (diabetes), joints (arthropathy), skin (bronze discoloration), and pituitary (hypogonadism). Diagnosis: elevated transferrin saturation (>45%) and ferritin, confirmed by HFE genotyping. Liver biopsy or MRI for iron quantification if ferritin >1000 or abnormal LFTs. Management: therapeutic phlebotomy is the mainstay — weekly removal of 500 mL blood (each unit removes ~250 mg iron) until target ferritin 50–100 μg/L, then maintenance phlebotomy every 2–4 months. Phlebotomy reduces fatigue, improves cognition, reverses liver fibrosis (including cirrhosis regression in up to 23%), but does NOT reliably improve established arthropathy, diabetes, or hypogonadism. Iron chelation (deferasirox) only for patients intolerant of phlebotomy or with contraindications (severe anemia, heart failure). Screen first-degree relatives. Avoid alcohol (synergistic hepatotoxicity), raw shellfish (Vibrio vulnificus risk), and vitamin C supplements (increases iron absorption).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Alcohol (accelerates liver fibrosis), raw shellfish (Vibrio vulnificus — increased susceptibility), vitamin C supplements >500 mg/day (increases iron absorption), iron supplements

treatments.duration: until ferritin 50–100 μg/L, then maintenance every 2–4 months
treatments.notes: all patients with elevated ferritin and confirmed iron overload
intolerant/refractory to phlebotomy, or contraindication (anemia, heart failure)
