diseases.name: Wilson Disease
diseases.management: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism (ATP7B gene mutation) causing impaired biliary copper excretion → copper accumulation in liver, brain (basal ganglia), cornea (Kayser-Fleischer rings), and kidneys. Presents with hepatic disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis, acute liver failure), neuropsychiatric disease (tremor, dysarthria, dystonia, parkinsonism, psychiatric symptoms), or both. Diagnosis: low ceruloplasmin (40 μg/day; >100 μg/day highly suggestive), Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp exam, elevated hepatic copper on biopsy (>250 μg/g dry weight), Leipzig scoring system. Treatment is lifelong. Symptomatic patients: start with chelation therapy (D-penicillamine or trientine) — start low and increase gradually over 2–4 weeks to avoid paradoxical neurological worsening (10–20% with penicillamine, 10–15% with trientine). Trientine is preferred by many due to fewer side effects. Zinc salts for maintenance therapy or asymptomatic patients without organ damage. Combination therapy (chelator + zinc, temporally separated) for decompensated cirrhosis. Liver transplantation for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis failing medical therapy. Avoid copper-rich foods (shellfish, liver, chocolate, nuts, mushrooms).
diseases.notes: Avoid: Copper-rich foods (shellfish, liver, organ meats, chocolate, nuts, mushrooms), simultaneous administration of chelator and zinc (zinc chelated → neither works), iron supplements with trientine (toxic complex)

treatments.dosage: initial 15–20 mg/kg/day (max 2000 mg), maintenance 10–15 mg/kg/day (~750–1000 mg/day)
treatments.notes: symptomatic WD
maintenance after chelation, or asymptomatic patients without organ damage

treatments.duration: acute liver failure (Wilson score ≥11), decompensated cirrhosis failing medical therapy
treatments.notes: curative; corrects metabolic defect; excellent outcomes (>85% 5-year survival)

diseases.name: Hepatorenal Syndrome
diseases.management: HRS-AKI (formerly type 1 HRS) is a functional renal failure in patients with advanced cirrhosis and ascites, caused by extreme splanchnic vasodilation → reduced effective arterial blood volume → renal vasoconstriction. Diagnosis requires: (1) cirrhosis with ascites, (2) AKI (increase in creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours or ≥50% within 7 days), (3) no improvement after 2 days of diuretic withdrawal and albumin challenge (1 g/kg/day × 2 days, max 100 g/day), (4) absence of shock, nephrotoxic drugs, or structural kidney disease. Treatment: vasoconstrictors + albumin as bridge to liver transplantation. Terlipressin is the preferred vasoconstrictor (FDA-approved 2022 for HRS-AKI in the US based on CONFIRM trial — verified HRS reversal in 32% vs 17% placebo). Norepinephrine is equally effective but requires ICU. Midodrine + octreotide is inferior but used when terlipressin and norepinephrine are unavailable. All patients should be evaluated urgently for liver transplantation (definitive treatment). Vasoconstrictors do not improve survival — they are a bridge to transplant.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs (nephrotoxic), aminoglycosides, IV contrast (if possible), delay in transplant evaluation, excessive albumin without volume assessment (pulmonary edema risk)

treatments.dosage: 0.5–1 mg IV bolus every 4–6 hours (or 2 mg/day continuous infusion, increase every 24–48 hours up to 12 mg/day)
treatments.duration: until creatinine ≤1.5 mg/dL or max 14 days
treatments.notes: HRS-AKI

diseases.name: Microscopic Colitis (Collagenous / Lymphocytic)
diseases.management: Microscopic colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease characterized by chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhea with grossly normal-appearing colonic mucosa on colonoscopy — diagnosis requires colonic biopsies showing either thickened subepithelial collagen band (collagenous colitis, ≥10 μm) or increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (lymphocytic colitis, ≥20 per 100 epithelial cells). Predominantly affects older women. Risk factors/triggers: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs, smoking. Management: (1) discontinue offending medications; (2) mild symptoms — loperamide, bismuth subsalicylate, or cholestyramine; (3) moderate-severe symptoms — budesonide 9 mg/day × 6–8 weeks (first-line per AGA guidelines; clinical remission in ~80% vs ~30% placebo). Relapse occurs in up to 70% after budesonide discontinuation — many patients require low-dose maintenance (3 mg/day or every other day). Mesalamine is NOT recommended (inferior to budesonide; may be inferior to placebo). Refractory cases: immunomodulators (azathioprine, methotrexate) or biologics (anti-TNF agents) — limited evidence. Surgery (diverting ileostomy or colectomy) is last resort.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Mesalamine (inferior to budesonide; may be inferior to placebo per AGA), NSAIDs and PPIs (may trigger or worsen MC), systemic corticosteroids (budesonide preferred due to lower systemic side effects)

treatments.duration: 6–8 weeks induction, then taper to 3 mg/day maintenance

diseases.name: Glomerulonephritis (Post-Streptococcal / RPGN)
diseases.management: Glomerulonephritis (GN) encompasses a spectrum of immune-mediated glomerular diseases. Post-streptococcal GN (PSGN) is the most common cause of acute GN in children, occurring 1–3 weeks after GAS pharyngitis or 3–6 weeks after skin infection. Presents with acute nephritic syndrome: hematuria (cola-colored urine), proteinuria, edema, hypertension, and oliguria. Diagnosis: low C3 (normalizes by 6–8 weeks), positive ASO/anti-DNase B titers, urinalysis with dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts. Biopsy rarely needed in classic pediatric presentation. Treatment is supportive: fluid/salt restriction, loop diuretics for volume overload, antihypertensives (CCBs or hydralazine acutely). Prognosis is excellent in children (>95% full recovery); adults have higher risk of persistent renal impairment.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in biopsy/treatment for RPGN (irreversible renal damage within days-weeks), immunosuppression in PSGN without crescents (no benefit; may worsen outcomes), nephrotoxic agents

treatments.dosage: fluid/salt restriction, furosemide 1–2 mg/kg/day, amlodipine for HTN
treatments.notes: classic pediatric PSGN

treatments.dosage: 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 days, then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day taper
treatments.notes: all RPGN types

diseases.name: Primary Hyperaldosteronism
diseases.management: PA is the most common cause of secondary hypertension, present in ~5–10% of all hypertensives and ~20% of resistant hypertension. Caused by autonomous aldosterone production — most commonly bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (BAH, ~60%) or aldosterone-producing adenoma (APA/Conn syndrome, ~35%). Screening: aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) — positive if ARR >30 with aldosterone >15 ng/dL. Confirmatory testing: oral sodium loading, saline infusion test, or fludrocortisone suppression test. Subtype differentiation: adrenal CT + adrenal vein sampling (AVS) — AVS is the gold standard to distinguish unilateral from bilateral disease (CT alone misclassifies ~38%). Treatment: unilateral disease (APA/UAH) — laparoscopic adrenalectomy (curative; BP cure in ~35–50%, improvement in ~80%); bilateral disease (BAH) — mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA). Per Endocrine Society 2025 guidelines, PA-specific therapy (medical or surgical) is recommended over nonspecific antihypertensive therapy. Spironolactone is first-line MRA (start 12.5–25 mg/day, titrate to 25–100 mg/day; max 400 mg/day); eplerenone is alternative (fewer antiandrogenic side effects but less potent, requires BID dosing). Amiloride (ENaC blocker) is an option if MRAs are not tolerated. Finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) is emerging but not yet standard for PA.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in subtype differentiation (AVS before surgery), CT alone for lateralization (misclassifies ~38%), potassium supplementation without addressing underlying aldosterone excess, ACEi/ARBs as sole therapy (do not block aldosterone adequately in PA)

treatments.duration: confirmed unilateral PA (APA or UAH) by AVS
treatments.notes: BP cure ~35–50%, improvement ~80%; biochemical cure ~95%; preop MRA for 4–6 weeks to correct hypokalemia

diseases.name: Tension-Type Headache
diseases.management: TTH is the most common primary headache disorder (prevalence ~40%). Characterized by bilateral, non-pulsating, pressing/tightening quality, mild-to-moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine physical activity, without nausea/vomiting (photophobia OR phonophobia may be present, but not both). Episodic TTH (15 days/month) vs chronic TTH (≥15 days/month for ≥3 months). Acute treatment: simple analgesics — ibuprofen 400 mg or acetaminophen 1000 mg are first-line (lower doses of acetaminophen are not effective). Combination analgesics with caffeine may add benefit. Triptans are NOT effective for TTH. Limit acute medication use to 10 days/month to prevent medication overuse headache. Preventive therapy for frequent episodic (≥10 days/month) or chronic TTH: amitriptyline 10–75 mg at bedtime is first-line preventive (reduces frequency and need for abortive medications; benefit seen after ~3 months). Second-line: mirtazapine 15–30 mg at bedtime, venlafaxine up to 150 mg/day. OnabotulinumtoxinA is NOT effective for TTH (unlike chronic migraine). Nonpharmacologic: physical therapy/manual therapy, biofeedback, CBT, stress management, regular exercise.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Triptans for TTH (ineffective), opioids/barbiturates (dependence, medication overuse headache), OnabotulinumtoxinA for TTH (not effective), acute medication >10 days/month (medication overuse headache)

treatments.notes: episodic TTH
episodic TTH

diseases.name: Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS)
diseases.management: RLS affects ~3% of US adults to a clinically significant degree. Characterized by an overwhelming urge to move the legs, often with unpleasant sensations (aching, tingling, crawling), provoked by rest/immobility, relieved by movement, and worse in the evening/night. Diagnosis is clinical (no polysomnography needed). Associated conditions: iron deficiency (most important modifiable factor), pregnancy, CKD/ESRD, peripheral neuropathy, MS, Parkinson disease. Exacerbating medications: serotonergic antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs), dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), centrally acting antihistamines (diphenhydramine). Management: (1) check ferritin and transferrin saturation — supplement iron if ferritin ≤100 ng/mL or TSAT 20% (ferrous sulfate 325 mg with vitamin C every other day, or IV iron 1000 mg if oral intolerant/ineffective); (2) discontinue exacerbating medications if possible; (3) gabapentinoids are now first-line pharmacotherapy (paradigm shift from dopamine agonists) — gabapentin 300–2400 mg, gabapentin enacarbil 600 mg, or pregabalin 150–450 mg; ~70% achieve much/very much improved symptoms; (4) dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine) are no longer first-line due to augmentation risk (7–10% annual incidence) — use only if gabapentinoids fail; (5) low-dose opioids (methadone 5–10 mg, oxycodone) for refractory/augmented RLS.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Dopamine agonists as first-line (augmentation risk), SSRIs/SNRIs if possible (worsen RLS), diphenhydramine (worsens RLS), abrupt DA withdrawal in augmented patients (severe rebound — cross-taper)

treatments.dosage: ferrous sulfate 325 mg with vitamin C 100 mg every other day (oral) or ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg IV (if oral fails)
treatments.notes: ferritin ≤100 ng/mL or TSAT 20%

diseases.name: Idiopathic Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (iNPH)
diseases.management: iNPH is characterized by the classic triad of gait disturbance (magnetic gait — wide-based, shuffling, difficulty initiating), urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment (subcortical dementia pattern — psychomotor slowing, executive dysfunction). Gait is typically the earliest and most responsive symptom. Incidence ~5.5/100,000. Diagnosis: clinical triad + ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy on imaging (Evans index >0.3 on CT/MRI), with normal opening pressure on LP (≤25 cm H₂O). Key imaging features: disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid space hydrocephalus (DESH pattern), callosal angle 90°, periventricular white matter changes. Predictive testing for shunt response: large-volume CSF tap test (30–50 mL removed, assess gait velocity before and after — improvement of ≥0.1 m/sec is positive; high specificity but sensitivity only 26–61%), extended lumbar drainage (72 hours; higher sensitivity 50–100%), and CSF outflow resistance (Ro) measurement (elevated Ro predicts shunt response — Level B per AAN). CSF shunt surgery is the only established effective treatment. A 2025 multicenter RCT confirmed that 75% of patients with open shunts had improved gait velocity at 3 months (0.23 m/sec improvement vs 0.03 m/sec placebo). Gait velocity increased by 54% from baseline to 8–12 months post-surgery in one large cohort. Ventriculoperitoneal shunt with programmable valve is most common. Complications: subdural hematoma (12% in open-shunt group), infection, shunt malfunction; reoperation rate ~3% at 30 days. Acetazolamide was evaluated in the DRAIN trial (2026) and showed no symptomatic benefit with poor tolerability — not recommended. Age alone does not decrease chance of shunt success.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Acetazolamide (DRAIN trial 2026: no benefit, poorly tolerated), delay in shunt referral (progressive deterioration without treatment), reliance on tap test alone to exclude iNPH (low sensitivity — negative tap does not rule out shunt-responsive iNPH)

treatments.notes: 75% improved gait at 3 months; gait velocity +54% at 8–12 months; programmable valve allows post-op adjustment; subdural hematoma risk ~12%; reoperation ~3–10%
assess gait velocity before and 1–4 hours after; positive if ≥0.1 m/sec improvement; high specificity but low sensitivity (26–61%)
higher sensitivity (50–100%) and PPV (80–100%); requires hospitalization

diseases.name: Huntington Disease
diseases.management: HD is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion (≥36 repeats) in the HTT gene on chromosome 4. Onset typically age 30–50. Characterized by progressive motor dysfunction (chorea → rigidity/bradykinesia in later stages), cognitive decline (executive dysfunction → dementia), and psychiatric symptoms (depression, irritability, apathy, psychosis). Diagnosis: genetic testing (≥40 repeats = full penetrance; 36–39 = reduced penetrance). No disease-modifying therapy exists. Management is symptomatic and multidisciplinary. Chorea: VMAT2 inhibitors are the only evidence-based pharmacotherapy — tetrabenazine (FDA-approved 2008; most effective but highest adverse event rate — depression, parkinsonism, akathisia), deutetrabenazine (FDA-approved 2017; better tolerated, fewer neuropsychiatric side effects, BID dosing), and valbenazine (FDA-approved 2024 for HD chorea; intermediate tolerability). MDS evidence-based review supports VMAT2 inhibitors for chorea. CYP2D6 genotyping recommended before tetrabenazine/deutetrabenazine (poor metabolizers need dose reduction). Psychiatric symptoms: SSRIs for depression/anxiety/OCD (citalopram, sertraline), atypical antipsychotics for psychosis/severe irritability/chorea (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone — also reduce chorea), benzodiazepines for anxiety. Cognitive decline: no proven pharmacotherapy (cholinesterase inhibitors not effective). Supportive care: PT/OT/speech therapy, nutritional support (high caloric needs due to hyperkinesia), advance care planning, genetic counseling for family members.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Tetrabenazine in patients with active suicidality or untreated depression (black box warning), dopamine-blocking agents in patients with significant parkinsonism/bradykinesia (worsen motor function), cholinesterase inhibitors for cognitive decline (no benefit in HD)

treatments.dosage: 6–48 mg/day (start 6 mg daily, titrate by 6 mg/week)
treatments.notes: HD-associated chorea
HD-associated chorea
HD-associated chorea

diseases.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
diseases.management: CTS is the most common entrapment neuropathy, caused by compression of the median nerve at the wrist. Prevalence 0.6–5.8%, more common in women and after age 45. Risk factors: repetitive hand use, pregnancy, diabetes, hypothyroidism, obesity, rheumatoid arthritis. Presents with pain, paresthesia, and numbness in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger), worse at night. Thenar atrophy is a late finding, highly specific. Diagnosis: clinical (Phalen test, Tinel sign — varying sensitivity/specificity); electrodiagnostic studies (NCS) for atypical cases, severity assessment, and surgical prognosis — abnormal NCS rules in CTS but normal NCS does not exclude it. Ultrasound (cross-sectional area >10 mm² at carpal tunnel inlet) is increasingly used. Management is stepwise: (1) mild-moderate CTS — nonsurgical: night splinting in neutral wrist position (as effective as continuous wear), local corticosteroid injection (provides greater improvement than splinting at 6 weeks, similar at 6 months; no dose-response relationship); (2) the DISTRICTS trial (2025) compared starting with surgery vs starting with injection — both strategies effective, with injection-first being a reasonable initial approach; (3) severe CTS (objective weakness, sensory deficits, thenar atrophy, or failed conservative therapy) — surgical decompression: open or endoscopic carpal tunnel release (equally effective). OTC analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) have not shown benefit for CTS.
diseases.notes: Avoid: NSAIDs/acetaminophen for CTS (no demonstrated benefit), prolonged conservative management in severe CTS with denervation (delays definitive treatment, may result in irreversible nerve damage), oral corticosteroids (short-term benefit only, systemic side effects)

treatments.notes: mild-moderate CTS
mild-moderate CTS

treatments.duration: severe CTS (thenar atrophy, weakness, sensory loss) or failed conservative therapy
treatments.notes: open and endoscopic equally effective; endoscopic may have faster return to work; success rate >90%

diseases.name: Transverse Myelitis
diseases.management: TM is an inflammatory disorder of the spinal cord causing acute or subacute motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction. Presents with bilateral (though often asymmetric) weakness, sensory level, and bowel/bladder dysfunction evolving over hours to days. Etiologies: idiopathic, MS (partial myelitis, 2 vertebral segments), NMOSD (longitudinally extensive TM ≥3 segments, AQP4-IgG+), MOGAD, post-infectious, systemic autoimmune (SLE, sarcoidosis), paraneoplastic. Workup: urgent spinal cord MRI with gadolinium, brain MRI (assess for MS/NMOSD), CSF analysis (pleocytosis, elevated protein, oligoclonal bands), serum AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG, comprehensive autoimmune and infectious panel. Treatment: (1) First-line: high-dose IV methylprednisolone 1000 mg/day × 3–5 days — standard empiric therapy; ~50–70% have partial or complete recovery. (2) Second-line (steroid-refractory): plasma exchange (PLEX) — 5–7 sessions over 10–14 days; a sham-controlled trial showed 42% moderate-to-marked improvement vs 5.9% sham; particularly effective in NMOSD. IVIG (2 g/kg over 2–5 days) is an alternative, especially in pediatric patients. (3) Long-term immunotherapy for relapsing etiologies: MS → disease-modifying therapy; NMOSD → rituximab, eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab; MOGAD → chronic immunosuppression if relapsing. Rehabilitation (PT/OT) is essential for recovery of function. Prognosis varies: ~33% good recovery, ~33% moderate disability, ~33% severe disability.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in IV corticosteroids (even if infectious etiology not fully excluded — benefit outweighs risk), delay in PLEX for severe/steroid-refractory cases (outcomes worsen with delay), missing NMOSD diagnosis (requires specific long-term immunotherapy different from MS)

treatments.notes: all suspected TM
steroid-refractory TM, especially NMOSD
steroid-refractory, especially pediatric or when PLEX not available
MS: DMTs; NMOSD: rituximab, eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab; MOGAD: rituximab, IVIG, mycophenolate

diseases.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis (Axial Spondyloarthritis)
diseases.management: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a chronic inflammatory disease primarily affecting the sacroiliac joints and spine, leading to progressive stiffness and potential ankylosis. Part of the axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) spectrum. Affects ~0.5% of the population, with strong HLA-B27 association (~90%). Extraarticular manifestations include anterior uveitis, inflammatory bowel disease, and psoriasis. Per ACR/SAA/SPARTAN 2019 guidelines and ASAS-EULAR recommendations: exercise and physical therapy are recommended throughout the disease course. NSAIDs at maximum tolerated dose are first-line pharmacotherapy — continuous use preferred for persistently active disease. No particular NSAID is superior. If inadequate response to ≥2 NSAIDs over ≥4 weeks total, escalate to biologic or targeted synthetic DMARDs. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab) are conditionally recommended as first biologic over IL-17 inhibitors. ~60% of patients achieve adequate sustained response to TNFi. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) are recommended if TNFi contraindicated or primary non-response to TNFi. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) are options after TNFi/IL-17i failure. Conventional DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine) are NOT effective for axial disease but sulfasalazine may help peripheral arthritis. Systemic glucocorticoids are strongly recommended against.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Systemic glucocorticoids for axial disease (strongly recommended against per ACR), methotrexate/sulfasalazine for axial symptoms (ineffective), etanercept if concurrent IBD or uveitis (monoclonal TNF antibodies preferred), IL-17i in active IBD (may worsen)

treatments.dosage: per drug (e.g., naproxen 500 mg BID, celecoxib 200 mg BID)
treatments.notes: all active AS
active AS despite NSAIDs
TNFi contraindicated or primary non-response
TNFi contraindicated or primary non-response
inadequate response to TNFi
inadequate response to TNFi

diseases.name: Hemophilia A / B
diseases.management: Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) and B (factor IX deficiency) are X-linked recessive bleeding disorders. Severity classified by factor level: severe (1%), moderate (1–5%), mild (5–40%). Severe phenotype presents with spontaneous hemarthroses, muscle bleeds, and life-threatening hemorrhage. Prophylaxis is recommended for all severe bleeding phenotypes to prevent arthropathy. Treatment paradigm has shifted dramatically with non-factor therapies. Factor replacement: standard half-life (SHL) and extended half-life (EHL) recombinant factor VIII or IX concentrates — given IV for prophylaxis or on-demand for bleeds. Emicizumab (bispecific antibody bridging FIXa and FX, mimicking FVIII function) is FDA-approved for hemophilia A prophylaxis with or without inhibitors — given SC every 1, 2, or 4 weeks; transforms severe hemophilia A to mild phenotype (trough FVIII-equivalent activity ~15–20 IU/dL). Non-factor therapies for hemophilia A and B: concizumab (anti-TFPI monoclonal antibody — FDA-approved for hemophilia A/B with or without inhibitors), fitusiran (anti-antithrombin siRNA — FDA-approved for hemophilia A/B with or without inhibitors), marstacimab (anti-TFPI — FDA-approved for hemophilia A/B without inhibitors). All non-factor therapies are SC. Inhibitor development (neutralizing antibodies to factor) is the most serious treatment complication (~30% in severe hemophilia A, ~3–5% in hemophilia B). Inhibitor management: immune tolerance induction (ITI) with high-dose factor, bypassing agents (rFVIIa, aPCC) for acute bleeds. Gene therapy: valoctocogene roxaparvovec (FDA-approved for severe hemophilia A) and etranacogene dezaparvovec (FDA-approved for hemophilia B).
diseases.notes: Avoid: aPCC >100 U/kg/day in patients on emicizumab (TMA risk), delay in inhibitor detection (check inhibitor titers regularly), delay in prophylaxis initiation in severe hemophilia (prevents arthropathy)

treatments.dosage: 3 mg/kg/week × 4 weeks loading, then 1.5 mg/kg/week or 3 mg/kg/Q2W or 6 mg/kg/Q4W
treatments.notes: hemophilia A with or without inhibitors
hemophilia A without inhibitors
hemophilia B without inhibitors
hemophilia A or B with or without inhibitors
hemophilia A or B with or without inhibitors
severe hemophilia A or B without inhibitors

diseases.name: Hereditary Spherocytosis
diseases.management: HS is the most common inherited hemolytic anemia in Northern European populations (~1 in 2000). Caused by mutations in RBC membrane/cytoskeletal proteins (ankyrin, band 3, spectrin, protein 4.2) leading to loss of membrane surface area → spherocytes → splenic trapping and destruction. Autosomal dominant in ~75%. Severity ranges from asymptomatic carrier to severe transfusion-dependent anemia. Presents with anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly, gallstones (pigmented). Diagnosis: clinical + labs (spherocytes on smear, elevated MCHC, reticulocytosis, elevated indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin) + confirmatory testing (EMA binding test — most sensitive/specific; osmotic fragility test). Management: (1) folic acid 1 mg/day (all patients with chronic hemolysis); (2) transfusion for aplastic crises (parvovirus B19) or severe anemia; (3) splenectomy is curative — eliminates the primary site of RBC destruction, normalizes Hb, reduces reticulocyte count and bilirubin. Indications: severe HS (Hb 10%), moderate HS with significant symptoms, rec
diseases.notes: Avoid: Splenectomy before age 3 (very high OPSI risk), delay in parvovirus B19 aplastic crisis recognition (may need urgent transfusion), iron supplementation without documented iron deficiency

treatments.notes: all patients with chronic hemolysis

treatments.duration: severe HS (Hb 8), moderate HS with significant symptoms, symptomatic gallstones
treatments.notes: normalizes Hb; Hb increases ~3.6 g/dL post-op; spherocytes persist but lifespan normalizes; OPSI risk lifelong — vaccinate pre-op, prophylactic penicillin post-op

treatments.duration: moderate HS in young children (preserve immune function)
treatments.notes: Hb increases ~2.2 g/dL; preserves splenic phagocytic function; ~25% require completion splenectomy due to regrowth

treatments.dosage: pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23), Hib, meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB)

treatments.notes: prevents OPSI; educate about fever management and medical alert identification

diseases.name: Empyema
diseases.management: Empyema is infected pleural fluid, typically complicating pneumonia (parapneumonic effusion). Stages: exudative (simple parapneumonic — free-flowing), fibrinopurulent (complicated — septations, loculations, pH 1000, positive Gram stain/culture), and organizing (trapped lung, pleural peel). Community-acquired organisms: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus anginosus group, anaerobes, Staphylococcus aureus. Hospital-acquired: MRSA, Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae. Antibiotic choice BRANCHES on community- vs hospital-acquired (AATS 2017 / ERS-ESTS 2023): community-acquired (low MRSA/Pseudomonas risk) = ceftriaxone + metronidazole OR ampicillin-sulbactam; hospital-acquired/postprocedural = vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 g q6h, Pseudomonas-active dosing) OR vancomycin + cefepime + metronidazole; add meropenem instead if ESBL suspected. Maintain anaerobic coverage empirically even when anaerobic cultures are negative — anaerobes are common but hard to culture. MRSA coverage only for hospital-acquired/postprocedural or MRSA risk factors, NOT routinely for community-acquired. Duration ≥2–3 weeks from drainage and defervescence; IV for the first 5–7 days then oral step-down if stable and the organism is susceptible. Management: (1) systemic antibiotics per the branch above; (2) drainage — small-bore chest tube (≥14 Fr; tubes <12 Fr fail more often in empyema); (3) intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg BID × 3 days — MIST-2; neither agent works alone) for loculated collections; (4) VATS decortication for failed tube ± fibrinolytics or organized (stage II/III) empyema — success 82–92%, ideally within 10 days; (5) thoracotomy with decortication for VATS failure/conversion (14%) or extensive stage III disease.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Aminoglycosides (inactivated in empyema fluid — do not use), intrapleural antibiotics (no benefit over systemic), delay in drainage (increases mortality)

treatments.duration: 2–3 weeks total (IV 5–7 days then PO step-down)
treatments.notes: add metronidazole for anaerobic coverage; narrow based on cultures
continue empirically even if anaerobic cultures negative

treatments.duration: 2–3 weeks total (IV 5–7 days then PO step-down)
treatments.notes: inherent anaerobic coverage; alternative to ceftriaxone + metronidazole

treatments.notes: alternative to piperacillin-tazobactam when paired with vancomycin

treatments.notes: reserve for suspected ESBL-producing organisms

treatments.notes: image-guided placement; tubes <12 Fr have higher failure rate in empyema

diseases.name: Boerhaave Syndrome
diseases.management: Boerhaave syndrome is a transmural perforation of the esophagus, typically of the left posterolateral distal esophagus, caused by a sudden increase in intraesophageal pressure (forceful vomiting, retching, straining). Presents with Mackler triad: vomiting, lower chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema. Diagnosis: CT chest with oral water-soluble contrast (sensitivity >90%) or esophagogram. This is a surgical emergency — mortality is 20–40% if treatment is delayed >24 hours. Empiric antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h is preferred (ACS Gastrointestinal Surgical Emergencies 2021); ampicillin-sulbactam or meropenem are acceptable alternatives; 7–14 days minimum, extended to 3–4 weeks for established mediastinitis or inadequate source control. Antifungal (fluconazole) is NOT routine — add for chronic PPI use, chronic steroids/immunosuppression, known esophageal candidiasis, or HIV (invasive candidiasis with dissemination has been reported, including a fatal case). Antibiotic CHOICE does not fundamentally change with time-to-presentation, but intensity and duration escalate: early (<24 h) contained perforation = standard pip-tazo, 7–10 days; delayed (>24 h) or established mediastinitis = same broad coverage but longer duration, more aggressive source control, and a lower threshold for antifungals; escalate to meropenem ± vancomycin if septic shock or resistant-organism risk. Management: (1) immediate resuscitation, NPO, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; (2) drainage of pleural/mediastinal collections (chest tube); (3) definitive repair — primary surgical repair (within 24 hours — best outcomes; lowest mortality 13.3% in meta-analysis), endoscopic management (stenting, endoscopic vacuum therapy, clips — increasingly used), or combined. A multinational study (2025) showed similar in-hospital mortality (~12%) across endoscopic, surgical, and combined approaches. Conservative management (antibiotics + drainage without repair) may be considered for contained perforations without sepsis.
diseases.notes: Avoid: Delay in diagnosis/treatment (mortality doubles after 24 hours), barium contrast for initial imaging (use water-soluble contrast — barium causes mediastinitis), oral intake before confirmed seal

treatments.duration: 7–14 days (extend to 3–4 weeks if established mediastinitis or inadequate source control)
treatments.notes: preferred empiric per ACS textbook; covers aerobes + anaerobes
acceptable alternative per ACS textbook

treatments.useWhen: Boerhaave syndrome, especially if ESBL risk or beta-lactam allergy precluding pip-tazo
treatments.notes: acceptable alternative per ACS textbook; consider for delayed presentation with established mediastinitis

treatments.useWhen: Boerhaave syndrome with risk factors (chronic PPI, steroids/immunosuppression, known esophageal candidiasis, HIV)

treatments.notes: remains cornerstone of management; lowest mortality (13.3%) in meta-analysis; includes debridement, repair over buttress, and mediastinal/pleural drainage

treatments.notes: comparable survival to surgery; higher re-intervention rate (OR 4.55 vs. surgery); stent migration in ~24%

medications.mechanism: Binds tubulin dimers → inhibits microtubule formation → inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis, NLRP3 inflammasome activation, IL-1β production; does NOT lower uric acid
medications.onset: PO — pain relief within 24 hrs; peak plasma 1–2 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4; P-glycoprotein substrate; enterohepatic recirculation
medications.monitoring: CBC; renal/hepatic function; GI symptoms; drug interactions (CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors)
medications.maxDose: ACUTE GOUT FLARE: 1.2 mg at first sign, then 0.6 mg 1 hr later (max 1.8 mg over 1 hr); then 0.6 mg once or twice daily starting day 2 for 7–10 days. PROPHYLAXIS: 0.6 mg once or twice daily (max 1.2 mg/day). FMF: 1.2–2.4 mg/day (adults). Do NOT repeat acute treatment course more than once every 2 weeks (severe renal/hepatic impairment)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Mild-moderate renal/hepatic impairment: no dose adjustment but monitor closely. Severe renal impairment (CrCl 30): prophylaxis start 0.3 mg/day; acute flare — no dose adjustment but do not repeat course more than q2 weeks. Dialysis: prophylaxis 0.3 mg twice weekly; acute flare 0.6 mg × 1 dose, do not repeat more than q2 weeks. Severe hepatic impairment: dose reduction may be needed for prophylaxis; acute flare — no dose adjustment but do not repeat more than q2 weeks. Concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole, protease inhibitors): reduce dose significantly or AVOID; concomitant P-gp inhibitors (cyclosporine): same precaution
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; crosses placenta; limited data
medications.sideEffects: GI (most common and dose-limiting — diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain); bone marrow suppression (chronic use — pancytopenia, aplastic anemia); myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (especially with statins or CYP3A4 inhibitors in renal impairment); peripheral neuropathy; alopecia; TOXICITY (overdose): multi-organ failure (GI → bone marrow → cardiovascular collapse; no antidote; mortality high with ingestion >0.5 mg/kg)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant strong CYP3A4 AND P-gp inhibitors in patients with renal or hepatic impairment (FATAL toxicity reported); hepatic cirrhosis with severe CKD (eGFR 20–30); hypersensitivity. NOT an analgesic — does not treat pain from other causes. Also used for pericarditis (COPE, CORP, CORP-2 trials) and FMF.

medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥55: full dose; CrCl 35–54: full dose q8h; CrCl 11–34: half dose q12h; CrCl ≤10: half dose q18–24h
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible; most commonly used surgical prophylaxis antibiotic
medications.sideEffects: Phlebitis, diarrhea, rash, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; broader gram-negative spectrum than 1st-gen (adds H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis); retains gram-positive activity; beta-lactamase stable
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.excretion: Renal (~90% unchanged via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion)
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC
medications.maxDose: PO: 500 mg q12h; IV: 1.5 g q8h (up to 9 g/day)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 10–20: standard dose q12h; CrCl 10: standard dose q24h; hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, rash, C. difficile, Jarisch-Herxheimer (Lyme disease)
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity. PO: take with food (increases absorption).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; unique among 2nd-gen for excellent anaerobic coverage (Bacteroides fragilis); also covers gram-negatives
medications.metabolism: Minimal
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC
medications.maxDose: 12 g/day (2 g q4h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–50: 1–2 g q8–12h; CrCl 10–29: 1–2 g q12–24h; CrCl 5–9: 0.5–1 g q12–24h; CrCl 5: 0.5–1 g q24–48h; hemodialyzable
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, phlebitis, rash, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity. Key use: surgical prophylaxis for colorectal surgery (covers anaerobes).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; excellent gram-negative coverage (including N. meningitidis, N. gonorrhoeae, H. influenzae, Enterobacteriaceae); good gram-positive (streptococci, not MSSA); crosses inflamed meninges
medications.metabolism: Minimal
medications.monitoring: CBC, hepatic function; signs of biliary sludging
medications.maxDose: 4 g/day (adults); 100 mg/kg/day (pediatric meningitis)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Generally NO dose adjustment needed due to dual elimination; consider dose reduction in combined severe hepatic + renal impairment; NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, rash, biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis (especially in children), C. difficile, eosinophilia
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity; neonates with hyperbilirubinemia (displaces bilirubin); do NOT co-administer with IV calcium-containing solutions in neonates (fatal precipitates)

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; broad spectrum — gram-positive (streptococci, MSSA) + gram-negative (Enterobacteriaceae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa); stable against many AmpC beta-lactamases; crosses inflamed meninges
medications.metabolism: Minimal (to N-methylpyrrolidine)
medications.monitoring: Renal function (neurotoxicity risk with accumulation); CBC; signs of neurotoxicity (altered mental status, myoclonus, seizures)
medications.maxDose: 6 g/day (2 g q8h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–60: reduce dose; CrCl 11–29: further reduce; CrCl ≤10: 250 mg–1 g q24h; hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis; MONITOR for neurotoxicity in renal impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Rash, diarrhea, nausea, NEUROTOXICITY (encephalopathy, myoclonus, seizures — especially in renal impairment), C. difficile, positive Coombs test
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; excellent gram-negative coverage including Pseudomonas aeruginosa; weaker gram-positive activity than other 3rd-gen cephalosporins
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC
medications.maxDose: 6 g/day (2 g q8h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 31–50: 1 g q12h; CrCl 16–30: 1 g q24h; CrCl 6–15: 500 mg q24h; CrCl 5: 500 mg q48h; hemodialysis: 1 g after each session
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, rash, phlebitis, C. difficile, eosinophilia
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity. Key use: Pseudomonas infections, febrile neutropenia, nosocomial pneumonia.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; good gram-positive (streptococci, MSSA) and gram-negative (H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis) coverage; does NOT cover Pseudomonas
medications.metabolism: Not appreciably metabolized
medications.excretion: Renal (~18% unchanged); remainder eliminated in feces
medications.monitoring: Renal function
medications.maxDose: 600 mg/day (300 mg q12h or 600 mg q24h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30: 300 mg q24h; hemodialysis: 300 mg every other day (give dose after dialysis)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, rash, red-tinged stools (iron interaction — not blood), vaginal candidiasis, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity. Note: iron supplements and antacids decrease absorption — separate by 2 hrs.

medications.brandNames: Spiriva HandiHaler (DPI — 18 mcg), Spiriva Respimat (SMI — 1.25 or 2.5 mcg/actuation)
medications.mechanism: Binds M1–M5 muscarinic receptors with similar affinity; dissociates SLOWLY from M1 and M3 receptors (bronchodilation >24 hrs) but RAPIDLY from M2 receptors (avoids paradoxical acetylcholine release that occurs with ipratropium); inhibits M3-mediated smooth muscle contraction → sustained bronchodilation; site-specific action in airways; also reduces mucus hypersecretion
medications.halfLife: Terminal ~25 hrs (inhaled); ~5–6 days (IV — reflects slow dissociation from receptors)
medications.metabolism: Minimal; ~25% metabolized via CYP2D6 and CYP3A4; remainder excreted unchanged
medications.excretion: Renal (~74% unchanged after IV; ~14% of inhaled dose in urine)
medications.monitoring: Symptom response; FEV1; exacerbation frequency; signs of anticholinergic effects
medications.maxDose: HandiHaler: 18 mcg (1 capsule) once daily; Respimat COPD: 5 mcg (2 puffs of 2.5 mcg) once daily; Respimat Asthma: 2.5 mcg (2 puffs of 1.25 mcg) once daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 50: use with caution (plasma levels may increase); no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Dry mouth (most common — 4–16%), pharyngitis, sinusitis, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, tachycardia (rare), paradoxical bronchospasm (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to tiotropium or ipratropium (cross-reactivity); NOT for acute rescue. Caution in narrow-angle glaucoma, prostatic hyperplasia, bladder-neck obstruction. HandiHaler and Respimat are NOT interchangeable (different doses). TIOSPIR trial confirmed safety equivalence of Respimat vs HandiHaler. First-line maintenance bronchodilator for COPD (GOLD guidelines). Also FDA-approved as add-on for uncontrolled asthma (steps 4–5).

medications.mechanism: Potent glucocorticoid with ~200× higher affinity for glucocorticoid receptor than cortisol and ~1000× higher topical anti-inflammatory potency; inhibits multiple cell types (mast cells, eosinophils, neutrophils, macrophages, lymphocytes) and mediators (histamine, eicosanoids, leukotrienes, cytokines) involved in airway inflammation; unique property of intracellular esterification in airway tissue → acts as sustained-release mechanism → prolonged local anti-inflammatory effect; moderate lipophilicity (more water-soluble than fluticasone) → faster dissolution and onset; extensive first-pass hepatic degradation (85–95%) limits systemic bioavailability
medications.onset: Inhaled — clinical improvement within 24 hrs; maximal benefit 1–2 weeks
medications.durationOfAction: 12–24 hrs (BID dosing typical; some patients maintained on once daily)
medications.halfLife: Plasma ~2–3 hrs; intracellular esterification prolongs airway residence time
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (extensive first-pass — 85–95%); metabolites have 1% glucocorticoid activity
medications.monitoring: Symptom control; peak flow/FEV1; growth velocity (pediatric); oral candidiasis; bone density (chronic high-dose)
medications.maxDose: Adults: 720 mcg BID (Flexhaler); Pediatric nebulization: 0.5 mg BID (max 1 mg/day); varies by formulation
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: increased systemic exposure (CYP3A4 metabolism) — use lowest effective dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B (preferred ICS in pregnancy — most safety data); NAEPP recommends budesonide as preferred ICS in pregnancy
medications.sideEffects: Oral candidiasis (thrush — rinse mouth after use), dysphonia (hoarseness), pharyngeal irritation, cough; systemic effects at high doses: adrenal suppression, growth retardation (pediatric — ~1 cm in first year, effect attenuates), osteoporosis, cataracts, glaucoma, skin thinning, easy bruising
medications.contraindications: Primary treatment of status asthmaticus or acute bronchospasm (NOT a rescue inhaler); hypersensitivity. Rinse mouth with water after each use (reduces candidiasis risk). Most versatile ICS — available as DPI, nebulization, and in combination with formoterol (Symbicort) for MART strategy.

medications.brandNames: Flovent HFA (MDI), Flovent Diskus (DPI), ArmonAir RespiClick/Digihaler; Fluticasone Furoate: Arnuity Ellipta
medications.mechanism: Synthetic trifluorinated corticosteroid; glucocorticoid receptor binding affinity 18× dexamethasone, ~2× beclomethasone, ~3× budesonide; same anti-inflammatory mechanism as budesonide but higher lipophilicity → longer airway residence time but slower dissolution; negligible oral bioavailability (1% for propionate) due to extensive first-pass metabolism
medications.onset: Inhaled — clinical improvement within 24 hrs; maximal benefit 1–2 weeks
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 to inactive 17β-carboxylic acid metabolite; oral bioavailability 1% (propionate); ~15% (furoate)
medications.monitoring: Same as budesonide
medications.maxDose: Propionate HFA: 440 mcg BID (adults); Diskus: 500 mcg BID; Furoate: 200 mcg once daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: increased systemic exposure — use lowest effective dose; strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole) significantly increase systemic exposure → Cushing syndrome/adrenal suppression reported
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C (less safety data than budesonide in pregnancy)
medications.sideEffects: Same as budesonide; potentially greater systemic effects at equivalent doses due to higher potency and lipophilicity (debated); higher risk of adrenal suppression with CYP3A4 inhibitors
medications.contraindications: Same as budesonide. Higher potency than budesonide — approximately 2:1 dose ratio (fluticasone 250 mcg ≈ budesonide 400 mcg). Ritonavir interaction: AVOID concurrent use (dramatic increase in systemic fluticasone → iatrogenic Cushing syndrome).

medications.brandNames: Advair Diskus (DPI), Advair HFA (MDI), AirDuo RespiClick/Digihaler, Wixela Inhub (generic)
medications.mechanism: Fluticasone: ICS (as above). Salmeterol: selective LABA — ≥50× more selective for beta-2 receptors than albuterol; long lipophilic side chain anchors in cell membrane adjacent to beta-2 receptor → prolonged receptor stimulation; stimulates adenylyl cyclase → increased cAMP → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation; also inhibits mast cell mediator release; complementary mechanisms — ICS reduces inflammation and LABA provides sustained bronchodilation; ICS upregulates beta-2 receptor expression (prevents tachyphylaxis); LABA enhances nuclear translocation of glucocorticoid receptor (synergy)
medications.onset: Salmeterol — 10–20 min (NOT rapid-acting); peak 3–4 hrs; Fluticasone — hours to days
medications.metabolism: Salmeterol: hepatic via CYP3A4 to inactive metabolite (alpha-hydroxysalmeterol); Fluticasone: as above
medications.excretion: Salmeterol: fecal (60%), renal (25%); Fluticasone: as above
medications.monitoring: Symptom control; FEV1; exacerbation frequency; heart rate; growth (pediatric); oral candidiasis; bone density (chronic high-dose)
medications.maxDose: Advair Diskus: 500/50 mcg BID; Advair HFA: 230/21 mcg BID; AirDuo: 232/14 mcg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: increased exposure to both components — use lowest effective dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: ICS effects (candidiasis, dysphonia) + LABA effects (tremor, palpitations, tachycardia, headache, muscle cramps); upper respiratory infection; pharyngitis; hypokalemia (rare); hyperglycemia (rare); paradoxical bronchospasm (rare); severe milk protein allergy: avoid DPI formulations (contain lactose)
medications.contraindications: Primary treatment of status asthmaticus; hypersensitivity; severe milk protein allergy (DPI formulations). Salmeterol is NOT for acute rescue (slow onset). Do NOT use additional LABA. FDA BOXED WARNING (historical — removed 2017 after AUSTRI/VESTRI trials confirmed safety of ICS/LABA combinations): LABA monotherapy increases asthma-related death; ALWAYS use with ICS. Step-up therapy for asthma not controlled on low-dose ICS alone. Also indicated for COPD maintenance.

medications.mechanism: Selectively and competitively blocks cysteinyl leukotriene type 1 (CysLT1) receptor with high affinity; no agonist activity; CysLTs (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) are products of arachidonic acid metabolism released from mast cells and eosinophils → cause airway edema, smooth muscle contraction, mucus secretion, eosinophil recruitment; montelukast blocks these effects; inhibits both early- and late-phase bronchoconstriction (75% and 57% respectively); also reduces peripheral blood eosinophils by 9–15%
medications.onset: PO — 2 hrs (peak plasma); clinical effect within 1 day; EIB protection within 2 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary), CYP2C8, CYP2C9; extensive first-pass metabolism
medications.monitoring: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (FDA boxed warning); symptom response; no routine labs needed
medications.maxDose: Adults/adolescents ≥15 yrs: 10 mg daily; 6–14 yrs: 5 mg chewable daily; 2–5 yrs: 4 mg chewable/granules daily; 12–23 months: 4 mg granules daily (asthma only)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; mild-moderate hepatic impairment: no adjustment; severe hepatic impairment: not studied
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; headache, abdominal pain, pharyngitis, cough, influenza; NEUROPSYCHIATRIC EVENTS (FDA BOXED WARNING — 2020): agitation, aggression, anxiousness, depression, disorientation, dream abnormalities, hallucinations, insomnia, irritability, restlessness, somnambulism, suicidal thinking and behavior, tremor; Churg-Strauss syndrome (rare — may unmask when corticosteroids are tapered); elevated LFTs (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNING: serious neuropsychiatric events — healthcare providers should consider risks vs benefits; avoid for mild symptoms (e.g., allergic rhinitis) when alternatives are available; counsel patients/caregivers about neuropsychiatric symptoms. NOT for acute bronchospasm. Take in the evening (asthma). Less effective than ICS for asthma control — alternative (not preferred) controller. Useful for exercise-induced bronchospasm and aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD).

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits presynaptic serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) → increases serotonin concentration in synaptic cleft; minimal effect on norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake (weak dopamine reuptake inhibition — unique among SSRIs); no significant affinity for muscarinic, histaminergic, or adrenergic receptors → fewer anticholinergic, sedative, and cardiovascular effects than TCAs
medications.onset: PO — clinical antidepressant effect 2–4 weeks; anxiolytic effect may be earlier
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via N-demethylation (CYP2B6, CYP2C19, CYP2C9, CYP3A4, CYP2D6); extensive first-pass; N-desmethylsertraline is substantially less active; linear pharmacokinetics
medications.monitoring: Suicidality (especially in young adults 25 — FDA boxed warning); mood; signs of serotonin syndrome; sodium (hyponatremia risk); bleeding signs
medications.maxDose: MDD: 200 mg/day; OCD: 200 mg/day; Panic/PTSD/SAD: 200 mg/day; PMDD: 150 mg/day (continuous) or 100 mg/day (luteal)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; mild hepatic impairment: half the dose; moderate-severe: not recommended
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; most studied SSRI in pregnancy; generally considered relatively safe; neonatal adaptation syndrome possible
medications.sideEffects: Nausea (most common), diarrhea (more than other SSRIs), headache, insomnia/somnolence, sexual dysfunction (ejaculatory delay, decreased libido — higher rate than some SSRIs), tremor, diaphoresis, dry mouth, dizziness
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); concomitant pimozide; concomitant disulfiram (oral solution contains alcohol); hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNING: suicidality in children, adolescents, and young adults. Lowest CYP450 drug interaction potential among SSRIs (mild CYP2D6 inhibition only at higher doses). Preferred SSRI in cardiovascular disease (SADHART trial). Oral solution available.

medications.mechanism: Same as sertraline — selectively inhibits SERT; also has weak norepinephrine reuptake inhibition; active metabolite norfluoxetine is also a potent SSRI
medications.onset: PO — clinical effect 2–4 weeks; full effect may take 4–6 weeks
medications.durationOfAction: Very long-acting due to active metabolite
medications.halfLife: Fluoxetine ~1–4 days; Norfluoxetine (active metabolite) ~7–15 days — LONGEST half-life of any SSRI
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary) to norfluoxetine (active); also CYP2C9, CYP3A4; nonlinear pharmacokinetics (inhibits own metabolism via CYP2D6); potent CYP2D6 inhibitor
medications.monitoring: Same as sertraline; weight (may cause initial weight loss); activation/agitation (more activating than other SSRIs)
medications.maxDose: MDD: 80 mg/day; OCD: 80 mg/day; Bulimia: 60 mg/day; PMDD: 20 mg/day; Panic: 60 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: lower dose or less frequent dosing (compensated cirrhosis: half-life doubled)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; extensive human data; generally considered relatively safe; associated with slight increase in cardiac malformations in some studies (debated)
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, insomnia, anxiety/nervousness (more activating), headache, anorexia/weight loss (initially), sexual dysfunction, tremor, diarrhea, rash
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout — PLUS 5-week washout when switching FROM fluoxetine to MAOI due to long half-life); concomitant pimozide or thioridazine. Long half-life advantages: lowest risk of discontinuation syndrome; missed doses less problematic; weekly formulation available. Long half-life disadvantages: prolonged washout needed; drug interactions persist weeks after stopping. Potent CYP2D6 inhibitor — significant drug interactions (codeine, tamoxifen, TCAs, antipsychotics).

medications.mechanism: S-enantiomer of citalopram; most selective SSRI — inhibits SERT with minimal effect on other receptors or transporters; ~100× more potent than R-enantiomer at SERT; no significant affinity for muscarinic, histaminergic, adrenergic, opioid, or benzodiazepine receptors
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 to S-demethylcitalopram and S-didemethylcitalopram (both minimally active); linear pharmacokinetics
medications.monitoring: Same as sertraline
medications.maxDose: MDD: 20 mg/day; GAD: 20 mg/day; Elderly: 10 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Mild-moderate renal impairment: no adjustment; severe renal impairment: use with caution; hepatic impairment: 10 mg/day max
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Generally best tolerated SSRI — lower risk of headache, dizziness, sedation, and GI side effects than other SSRIs; nausea, insomnia, ejaculatory disorder, somnolence, increased sweating, fatigue, decreased libido
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); concomitant pimozide; hypersensitivity. QT prolongation: less risk than citalopram (no dose-dependent QT effect at therapeutic doses, unlike citalopram which has max 40 mg due to QT risk). Low drug interaction potential (mild CYP2D6 inhibition). Often considered first-line SSRI due to tolerability and clean pharmacology.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin (primary at low doses) and norepinephrine (at higher doses — 30:1 serotonin:NE binding ratio); weak dopamine reuptake inhibition; NO significant affinity for muscarinic, histaminergic, or alpha-1 adrenergic receptors; dose-dependent mechanism: ≤150 mg acts primarily as SSRI; >150 mg adds NE reuptake inhibition; active metabolite O-desmethylvenlafaxine (ODV/desvenlafaxine) has similar activity
medications.onset: PO — clinical effect 2–4 weeks; possibly faster onset than SSRIs (debated)
medications.halfLife: Venlafaxine ~5 hrs; ODV ~12 hrs; low protein binding (27%)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary) to ODV (active); CYP3A4 (minor); subject to CYP2D6 polymorphism variability
medications.excretion: Renal (~87% within 48 hrs — 5% unchanged venlafaxine, 29% unconjugated ODV, 26% conjugated ODV)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure (dose-dependent hypertension — check at baseline and periodically); heart rate; suicidality; sodium; cholesterol (may increase); signs of serotonin syndrome
medications.maxDose: IR: 375 mg/day (divided TID); XR: 225 mg/day (MDD/GAD/SAD); higher doses used off-label
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Mild-moderate renal impairment: reduce dose 25%; hemodialysis: reduce dose 50%; hepatic impairment: reduce dose 50%; severe hepatic impairment: reduce dose >50%
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; neonatal adaptation syndrome; possible increased risk of persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn
medications.sideEffects: Nausea (most common — 31–58%), headache, insomnia, somnolence, dry mouth, dizziness, diaphoresis, sexual dysfunction, constipation; dose-dependent hypertension (sustained — especially >300 mg/day; ~3–13%); tachycardia; discontinuation syndrome (SEVERE — short half-life; must taper slowly over weeks; "brain zaps," dizziness, nausea, irritability); cholesterol elevation; mydriasis (risk of angle-closure glaucoma); serotonin syndrome (with MAOIs)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); hypersensitivity. Ascending dose-response curve — may be more effective at higher doses. Risk of death from overdose greater than SSRIs. XR formulation preferred (once daily, better tolerated). Low protein binding → fewer protein-binding drug interactions.

medications.mechanism: More balanced and potent SNRI than venlafaxine — ~10:1 serotonin:NE binding ratio (vs 30:1 for venlafaxine); inhibits neuronal reuptake of both serotonin and norepinephrine; no significant affinity for muscarinic, histaminergic, dopaminergic, or adrenergic receptors; NE reuptake inhibition in descending pain pathways contributes to analgesic effect
medications.onset: PO — clinical effect 1–4 weeks; pain relief may begin within 1 week
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP1A2 (primary) and CYP2D6; extensive first-pass metabolism; moderate CYP2D6 inhibitor
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; hepatic function (LFTs — hepatotoxicity risk); suicidality; sodium; signs of serotonin syndrome
medications.maxDose: MDD: 120 mg/day (60 mg once or twice daily); GAD: 120 mg/day; Neuropathic pain: 60 mg/day; Fibromyalgia: 60 mg/day; Chronic MSK pain: 60 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30 or ESRD: AVOID (2× higher AUC); hepatic impairment: AVOID (5× higher AUC in moderate impairment); do NOT use in chronic liver disease or cirrhosis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; neonatal adaptation syndrome
medications.sideEffects: Nausea (most common — 20–25%), dry mouth, somnolence, fatigue, constipation, decreased appetite, diaphoresis, sexual dysfunction; hepatotoxicity (rare — cases of hepatic failure; avoid in liver disease); orthostatic hypotension; hypertension (less than venlafaxine); discontinuation syndrome (moderate — taper over 2+ weeks); urinary hesitancy/retention
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma; hepatic impairment; CrCl 30; concomitant strong CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin); heavy alcohol use (hepatotoxicity risk). Enteric-coated pellets — do NOT crush or chew (degrades in acidic environment). Broader indications than venlafaxine: MDD, GAD, diabetic neuropathy, fibromyalgia, chronic MSK pain. Moderate CYP2D6 inhibitor — interactions with CYP2D6 substrates.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin (primary) and norepinephrine; also blocks muscarinic (M1), histamine (H1), and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors → anticholinergic, sedative, and hypotensive effects; sodium channel blockade (quinidine-like) → cardiac conduction slowing; demethylated to nortriptyline (active metabolite — secondary amine with more NE selectivity)
medications.onset: PO — sedation within hours; antidepressant effect 2–4 weeks; pain relief 1–3 weeks
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C19 (demethylation to nortriptyline) and CYP2D6 (hydroxylation); extensive first-pass metabolism; subject to CYP2C19 and CYP2D6 polymorphisms
medications.monitoring: ECG (baseline — QTc, PR interval; contraindicated if heart block); blood levels (therapeutic range for depression: amitriptyline + nortriptyline 80–200 ng/mL); suicidality; anticholinergic effects; weight
medications.maxDose: Depression: 300 mg/day (inpatient); 150 mg/day (outpatient); Neuropathic pain: 25–150 mg/day; Migraine prophylaxis: 10–150 mg/day; Elderly: start 10–25 mg at bedtime
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose (extensive hepatic metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; crosses placenta; neonatal withdrawal symptoms reported
medications.sideEffects: Anticholinergic (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision — most common), sedation/drowsiness (H1 blockade), weight gain (H1 blockade), orthostatic hypotension (alpha-1 blockade), cardiac conduction abnormalities (QRS/QTc prolongation — most dangerous), tachycardia, sexual dysfunction, seizures (lowers threshold), SIADH/hyponatremia, delirium (elderly)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); acute recovery phase post-MI; heart block; hypersensitivity. LETHAL IN OVERDOSE — narrow therapeutic index; as few as 10× therapeutic dose can be fatal (cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, coma); avoid in suicidal patients. Avoid in elderly (Beers criteria — anticholinergic burden, falls, delirium). Most sedating TCA — useful for insomnia/pain but limits daytime use. First-line for neuropathic pain (off-label).

medications.mechanism: Active metabolite of amitriptyline; preferentially inhibits norepinephrine reuptake (more NE-selective than amitriptyline); less anticholinergic, less sedating, less orthostatic hypotension than tertiary amine TCAs; still blocks muscarinic, H1, alpha-1, and sodium channels but to lesser degree
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary — hydroxylation); subject to CYP2D6 polymorphism
medications.monitoring: Same as amitriptyline; therapeutic drug monitoring recommended (therapeutic window: 50–150 ng/mL — one of few antidepressants
medications.maxDose: 150 mg/day; Elderly/adolescents: 30–50 mg/day; Neuropathic pain: 25–150 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D
medications.sideEffects: Same as amitriptyline but LESS sedation, LESS anticholinergic effects, LESS orthostatic hypotension, LESS weight gain — best-tolerated TCA; still carries cardiac conduction risk in overdose
medications.contraindications: Same as amitriptyline. Preferred TCA for elderly (if TCA needed) due to better side-effect profile. CYP2D6 poor metabolizers: reduce dose to 40% of normal; ultrarapid metabolizers: increase to 170% of normal (DPWG guidelines). Pharmacogenetics-guided dosing achieves therapeutic levels ~6 days faster than standard dosing.

medications.mechanism: Dual mechanism — (1) inhibits serotonin reuptake (SERT; Ki = 367 nM — weaker than SSRIs) and (2) potent 5-HT2A receptor antagonist (Ki = 35.6 nM — stronger than SERT inhibition); also 5-HT2B/2C antagonist, partial 5-HT1A agonist, alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist (→ orthostatic hypotension, priapism), and weak H1 antagonist (→ sedation); at LOW doses (25–100 mg), 5-HT2A antagonism and H1 blockade predominate → sedation (used as hypnotic); at HIGHER doses (150–400 mg), SERT inhibition becomes clinically significant → antidepressant effect
medications.onset: PO — sedation within 30–60 min; antidepressant effect 2–4 weeks; peak plasma ~1 hr (fasting), ~2 hrs (with food)
medications.durationOfAction: 6–8 hrs (IR — sedation); antidepressant effect requires sustained dosing
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to active metabolite m-chlorophenylpiperazine (mCPP — 5-HT2C agonist; may cause anxiety/dysphoria); extensively metabolized; 1% excreted unchanged; 89–95% protein bound
medications.monitoring: Suicidality; signs of priapism; orthostatic blood pressure; serotonin syndrome risk
medications.maxDose: Outpatient: 400 mg/day (divided); Inpatient: 600 mg/day; Insomnia (off-label): 25–100 mg at bedtime
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; use with caution in hepatic impairment (CYP3A4 metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/drowsiness (most common — desired for insomnia use), orthostatic hypotension (alpha-1 blockade), dizziness, dry mouth, headache, nausea, blurred vision; PRIAPISM (rare but serious — 1:6,000–1:8,000; surgical emergency; counsel male patients); cardiac arrhythmias (rare — QT prolongation); minimal sexual dysfunction (advantage over SSRIs); minimal weight gain
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); hypersensitivity. Most commonly prescribed as low-dose hypnotic (25–100 mg at bedtime) rather than antidepressant. Take with food (reduces dizziness, increases absorption). No abuse potential. Minimal sexual dysfunction — useful adjunct or alternative when SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction is problematic.

medications.brandNames: Wellbutrin (IR), Wellbutrin SR, Wellbutrin XL, Zyban (smoking cessation), Contrave (with naltrexone — weight loss)
medications.mechanism: Inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine (NOT serotonin — unique among antidepressants); also blocks nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChR — mechanism for smoking cessation); structurally related to cathinone (amphetamine-like); three active metabolites: hydroxybupropion (primary — via CYP2B6; reaches 10× parent drug levels), threohydrobupropion, erythrohydrobupropion
medications.onset: PO — clinical effect 1–4 weeks; SR/XL peak 3 hrs
medications.durationOfAction: IR 6–8 hrs (TID); SR 12 hrs (BID); XL 24 hrs (once daily)
medications.halfLife: Bupropion ~21 hrs; Hydroxybupropion ~20 hrs; Threohydrobupropion ~37 hrs; Erythrohydrobupropion ~33 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2B6 (primary — to hydroxybupropion); also carbonyl reduction to threo/erythro metabolites; potent CYP2D6 inhibitor (clinically significant — affects codeine, tamoxifen, TCAs)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; seizure risk assessment; mood/suicidality; weight
medications.maxDose: IR: 450 mg/day (150 mg TID); SR: 400 mg/day (200 mg BID); XL: 450 mg/day (once daily); single dose max: IR 150 mg, SR 200 mg
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Mild hepatic impairment: consider reduced frequency; moderate (Child-Pugh B): max 150 mg/day (IR) or 100 mg/day (SR) or 150 mg every other day (XL); severe (Child-Pugh C): max 75 mg/day (IR) or 100 mg every other day (SR/XL); eGFR 90: consider reduced dose (metabolite accumulation)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Insomnia (most common), dry mouth, headache, nausea, agitation/anxiety, tachycardia, constipation, weight LOSS (unique — only antidepressant associated with weight loss); SEIZURES (dose-dependent — 0.4% at ≤450 mg/day; risk increases sharply above 450 mg/day; contraindicated in seizure disorders); NO sexual dysfunction (major advantage); NO weight gain; hypertension (dose-related)
medications.contraindications: Seizure disorder; current/prior bulimia or anorexia nervosa (increased seizure risk); abrupt discontinuation of alcohol, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, or antiepileptics; concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); concomitant other bupropion products. Activating antidepressant — useful for fatigue, low motivation, ADHD symptoms. Avoid single doses >150 mg (IR) or >200 mg (SR) to minimize seizure risk.

medications.drugClass: Noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant (NaSSA) / tetracyclic
medications.mechanism: Unique pharmacology — (1) potent central alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptor and heteroreceptor antagonist → increases NE and serotonin release; (2) potent 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, and 5-HT3 antagonist → enhanced 5-HT1A-mediated neurotransmission (antidepressant effect) while blocking 5-HT2/3 (reduces sexual dysfunction, nausea, anxiety); (3) potent H1 antagonist (→ sedation, appetite stimulation, weight gain); (4) NO monoamine reuptake inhibition (unlike SSRIs/SNRIs). Paradoxical dose-response for sedation: at LOW doses (15 mg), H1 blockade predominates → MORE sedation; at HIGHER doses (30–45 mg), noradrenergic activation counteracts H1 effects → LESS sedation
medications.onset: PO — sedation within 30 min; antidepressant effect 1–4 weeks; peak plasma ~2 hrs
medications.halfLife: ~20–40 hrs (mean ~26 hrs); longer in females (~37 hrs) than males (~26 hrs)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary), CYP3A4, CYP1A2; demethylation and hydroxylation followed by glucuronide conjugation
medications.monitoring: Weight; metabolic parameters (lipids, glucose); CBC (agranulocytosis risk — rare); suicidality
medications.maxDose: 45 mg/day (once daily at bedtime)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 40: clearance reduced ~30%; moderate-severe hepatic impairment: clearance reduced ~30%; use with caution and consider lower doses in both
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Somnolence (54% — most common), increased appetite (17%), weight gain (≥7% body weight in 7.5%), dry mouth, dizziness, elevated cholesterol/triglycerides; agranulocytosis/neutropenia (rare — 0.1%; FDA warning; discontinue if sore throat/fever with low WBC); minimal sexual dysfunction (major advantage); minimal nausea (5-HT3 blockade); QT prolongation (rare)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); hypersensitivity. Advantages: promotes sleep and appetite (useful in depressed patients with insomnia and weight loss/anorexia); minimal sexual dysfunction; minimal nausea; rapid onset of sleep effect. ODT formulation dissolves on tongue without water. Often used as augmentation strategy with SSRIs ("California rocket fuel" = mirtazapine + venlafaxine).

medications.mechanism: Binds to benzodiazepine site on GABA-A receptor → allosteric modulation → increases frequency of chloride channel opening in response to GABA → enhanced inhibitory neurotransmission; anxiolytic, sedative, hypnotic, anticonvulsant, amnestic, muscle relaxant effects; does NOT directly activate GABA-A receptor (requires GABA)
medications.onset: PO — 20–30 min; IV — 1–5 min; IM — 15–30 min (well absorbed IM — unique among benzodiazepines)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via direct glucuronide conjugation (Phase II ONLY — does NOT undergo Phase I oxidative metabolism via CYP450); this is the KEY pharmacokinetic advantage — metabolism unaffected by age, liver disease, or CYP450 inhibitors
medications.monitoring: Respiratory status; level of sedation; signs of dependence/withdrawal
medications.maxDose: Anxiety: 10 mg/day (divided BID-TID); Status epilepticus: 4 mg IV (may repeat once); Procedural sedation: 4 mg IV; ICU sedation: variable (intermittent or continuous)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment needed for hepatic impairment (glucuronide conjugation preserved — PREFERRED benzodiazepine in liver disease); no specific renal adjustment; use with caution in elderly (start low)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; neonatal withdrawal syndrome; avoid in 1st trimester (possible teratogenicity)
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/drowsiness, cognitive impairment, anterograde amnesia, respiratory depression (especially with opioids), paradoxical agitation (rare — more common in elderly), dependence/withdrawal (with chronic use), ataxia, hypotension (IV)
medications.contraindications: Acute narrow-angle glaucoma; hypersensitivity; severe respiratory insufficiency. IV lorazepam contains propylene glycol — monitor for toxicity with high doses/prolonged infusions (metabolic acidosis, AKI, osmol gap). Preferred benzodiazepine for: status epilepticus (IV — longer duration of antiseizure effect than diazepam), liver disease, elderly (no CYP450 metabolism, no active metabolites), alcohol withdrawal.

medications.mechanism: Same as lorazepam — GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulator
medications.onset: PO — 15–30 min (rapid — high lipophilicity); IV — 1–3 min (fastest onset of all benzodiazepines); IM — erratic absorption (AVOID IM — poor/unpredictable absorption unlike lorazepam); Rectal — 10–15 min
medications.durationOfAction: Single dose: short (rapid redistribution from brain to fat despite long half-life); Repeated doses: very long (accumulation)
medications.halfLife: Diazepam ~20–100 hrs (mean ~48 hrs); Active metabolite desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam) ~30–200 hrs; increases significantly with age and liver disease
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C19 (primary — to desmethyldiazepam/nordiazepam, active) and CYP3A4 (to temazepam, active); both active metabolites further metabolized to oxazepam (also active) then glucuronidated; extensive Phase I oxidative metabolism → highly susceptible to CYP450 interactions, age, and liver disease
medications.monitoring: Same as lorazepam; prolonged sedation risk due to active metabolites
medications.maxDose: Anxiety: 40 mg/day; Status epilepticus: 10–20 mg IV (may repeat); Alcohol withdrawal: variable (symptom-triggered); Muscle spasm: 2–10 mg TID-QID; Rectal (seizure rescue): 0.2–0.5 mg/kg
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Hepatic impairment: AVOID or reduce dose significantly (oxidative metabolism impaired; half-life dramatically prolonged); elderly: reduce dose by 50% (decreased clearance, increased sensitivity); no specific renal adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; crosses placenta freely; "floppy infant syndrome"
medications.sideEffects: Same as lorazepam; prolonged sedation (active metabolites accumulate); greater risk of falls in elderly; paradoxical reactions; dependence
medications.contraindications: Same as lorazepam; myasthenia gravis (relative); severe hepatic insufficiency; sleep apnea. Advantages: fastest IV onset; rectal formulation for seizure rescue (Diastat); excellent muscle relaxant. Disadvantages: erratic IM absorption; active metabolites accumulate; problematic in elderly and liver disease.

medications.mechanism: Same as lorazepam — GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulator; unique water-soluble imidazobenzodiazepine — ring opens at physiologic pH to become lipophilic (rapid CNS penetration); most potent benzodiazepine for amnesia
medications.onset: IV — 1–5 min; IM — 5–15 min (well absorbed — 90% bioavailability); Intranasal — 5–10 min; PO — 10–20 min
medications.durationOfAction: IV — 30–60 min (single dose); IM — 1–2 hrs; much shorter than lorazepam or diazepam
medications.halfLife: ~1.5–2.5 hrs (shortest of common benzodiazepines); prolonged in elderly (up to 4 hrs), obesity, hepatic impairment, critical illness (up to 12+ hrs with prolonged infusion — context-sensitive half-time increases dramatically)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to alpha-hydroxymidazolam (active — ~50% potency; accumulates in renal failure); highly susceptible to CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, protease inhibitors → dramatically increased sedation)
medications.excretion: Renal (~60–80% as glucuronide conjugate of alpha-hydroxymidazolam)
medications.monitoring: Continuous respiratory monitoring (procedural sedation); SpO2; blood pressure; level of sedation
medications.maxDose: Procedural sedation: 2.5–5 mg IV (titrate); ICU sedation: continuous infusion 0.02–0.1 mg/kg/hr; Seizure rescue (intranasal): 5 mg; Pediatric sedation: 0.05–0.1 mg/kg IV
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Hepatic impairment: reduce dose (CYP3A4 metabolism); renal impairment: active metabolite accumulates → prolonged sedation; elderly: reduce dose by 30–50%
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D
medications.sideEffects: Respiratory depression (most concerning — especially with opioids; FDA boxed warning for benzodiazepine-opioid combination), hypotension, paradoxical agitation (more common in children and elderly), hiccups, nausea, profound anterograde amnesia
medications.contraindications: Same as lorazepam; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole — dramatically increased effect). Preferred for: procedural sedation (short duration, potent amnesia), ICU sedation (titratable infusion — but propofol/dexmedetomidine now preferred), IM sedation for acute agitation (reliable IM absorption), intranasal seizure rescue. Flumazenil reverses all benzodiazepine effects.

medications.brandNames: Keppra, Keppra XR, Spritam (3D-printed ODT), Roweepra
medications.mechanism: Binds to synaptic vesicle glycoprotein 2A (SV2A) in presynaptic terminals; modulates neurotransmitter release (exact mechanism unclear); does NOT interact with sodium channels, GABA receptors, or glutamate receptors; unique mechanism among antiepileptics
medications.onset: PO — rapid; peak 1 hr (IR); IV — immediate; clinical effect within days
medications.halfLife: ~6–8 hrs (adults); ~5 hrs (children); prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism — NOT CYP450-dependent; primary metabolism via enzymatic hydrolysis of acetamide group in blood (not liver); 66% excreted unchanged
medications.monitoring: Mood/behavior (irritability, aggression — common); renal function (dose adjustment); seizure frequency; no routine drug levels needed (but available)
medications.maxDose: Adults: 3,000 mg/day (1,500 mg BID); Pediatric: 60 mg/kg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 50–80: 500–1,000 mg BID; CrCl 30–49: 250–750 mg BID; CrCl 30: 250–500 mg BID; ESRD on dialysis: 500–1,000 mg daily + 250–500 mg supplemental dose after dialysis; NO hepatic dose adjustment needed
medications.pregnancyLactation: Low teratogenic risk — reasonable first-line choice in women of childbearing potential
medications.sideEffects: Somnolence/fatigue (most common), behavioral/psychiatric effects (irritability, aggression, anxiety, depression — up to 13%; "Keppra rage"), dizziness, headache, infection; NO rash; NO hepatotoxicity; NO drug interactions
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. KEY ADVANTAGES: no drug interactions (ideal for polypharmacy patients); no hepatic metabolism; rapid titration to therapeutic dose; IV formulation available; broad-spectrum efficacy (focal and generalized seizures); low teratogenicity. Disadvantage: behavioral side effects limit use in some patients.

medications.brandNames: Depakene (valproic acid), Depakote (divalproex — enteric-coated), Depakote ER, Depacon (IV)
medications.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms — (1) increases GABA concentration (inhibits GABA transaminase, enhances GABA synthesis); (2) blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (use-dependent); (3) blocks T-type calcium channels (absence seizures); (4) modulates glutamate/NMDA signaling; broadest-spectrum antiepileptic — effective for focal, generalized, absence, myoclonic seizures
