medications.onset: PO — peak 1–4 hrs (IR); 4–17 hrs (ER); IV — immediate; clinical effect days to weeks
medications.halfLife: ~9–16 hrs (adults); shorter in children; nonlinear pharmacokinetics (protein binding saturable → free fraction increases at higher doses)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic — extensive; glucuronidation (major), beta-oxidation, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2A6; enzyme INHIBITOR (increases levels of lamotrigine, phenobarbital, carbamazepine epoxide, warfarin)
medications.monitoring: Serum levels (therapeutic range 50–100 mcg/mL; free levels if albumin low or high total levels); LFTs (baseline and periodically — hepatotoxicity risk); CBC with platelets (thrombocytopenia); ammonia (if encephalopathy suspected); lipase/amylase (pancreatitis risk)
medications.maxDose: Epilepsy: 60 mg/kg/day; Mania: 60 mg/kg/day; Migraine prophylaxis: 1,000 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment (not renally eliminated); CONTRAINDICATED in significant hepatic disease or dysfunction; children 2 years at highest risk of fatal hepatotoxicity
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED in women of childbearing potential unless no alternative — FDA BOXED WARNING: (1) major congenital malformations (spina bifida up to 10%); (2) neurodevelopmental delay (reduced IQ — up to 40% of exposed children); (3) hepatotoxicity (fatal); (4) pancreatitis (life-threatening). Effective contraception mandatory
medications.sideEffects: GI upset (nausea, vomiting — less with divalproex), tremor, weight gain, hair loss/thinning, thrombocytopenia (dose-dependent), hepatotoxicity (FDA boxed warning — fatal; highest risk in children 2 on polytherapy), pancreatitis (FDA boxed warning), hyperammonemia (with or without encephalopathy — can occur without LFT elevation), PCOS/menstrual irregularities, sedation
medications.contraindications: Hepatic disease; urea cycle disorders (fatal hyperammonemic encephalopathy); mitochondrial disease caused by POLG mutations (Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome — fatal hepatotoxicity); pregnancy (unless no alternative); hypersensitivity. First-line for generalized epilepsy (except in women of childbearing potential). Also used for bipolar mania and migraine prophylaxis.

medications.brandNames: Dilantin (capsules, suspension), Phenytek (ER capsules); Fosphenytoin (Cerebyx — IV prodrug)
medications.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in a use-dependent (frequency-dependent) manner — preferentially binds to inactivated state of Na⁺ channels → stabilizes neuronal membranes → prevents repetitive firing of action potentials; does NOT affect normal single action potentials; also modulates calcium channels and neurotransmitter release
medications.onset: PO — peak 4–12 hrs (highly variable); IV phenytoin — 10–30 min; IV fosphenytoin — faster (converted to phenytoin in ~15 min)
medications.halfLife: ~22 hrs (range 7–42 hrs); NONLINEAR (zero-order/Michaelis-Menten) pharmacokinetics — small dose increases can cause disproportionately large increases in serum levels → toxicity risk
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 (primary — 90%) and CYP2C19 (10%); saturable metabolism → nonlinear kinetics; potent CYP450 INDUCER (increases metabolism of many drugs)
medications.monitoring: Serum levels ESSENTIAL (therapeutic range: total 10–20 mcg/mL; free 1–2 mcg/mL); correct for albumin in hypoalbuminemia (adjusted level = measured / (0.2 × albumin + 0.1)); CBC; LFTs; folate; vitamin D; bone density (chronic use); signs of toxicity (nystagmus → ataxia → lethargy → coma)
medications.maxDose: Oral: 600 mg/day (usually); Loading dose: 15–20 mg/kg IV (phenytoin at max 50 mg/min; fosphenytoin at max 150 mg PE/min)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Renal impairment: free fraction increases (decreased protein binding) — monitor free levels; hepatic impairment: reduced metabolism — use with caution, monitor levels; hypoalbuminemia: correct total levels or use free levels
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; fetal hydantoin syndrome (craniofacial abnormalities, nail/digit hypoplasia, growth restriction, cognitive impairment); deplete folate → neural tube defects; supplement folate
medications.sideEffects: Dose-related: nystagmus (first sign of toxicity — at levels >20), ataxia, diplopia, slurred speech, sedation, cognitive impairment. Chronic: gingival hyperplasia (50%), hirsutism, coarsening of facial features, peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, osteomalacia (vitamin D depletion — CYP induction), folate deficiency/megaloblastic anemia. Idiosyncratic: rash (5–10%), SJS/TEN (rare — HLA-B*15:02 in Southeast Asian descent), DRESS, hepatotoxicity, lupus-like syndrome, lymphadenopathy (pseudolymphoma). IV: purple glove syndrome (extravasation), cardiac arrhythmias (infuse ≤50 mg/min), hypotension
medications.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia, SA block, 2nd/3rd degree AV block, Adams-Stokes syndrome (IV); hypersensitivity; concomitant delavirdine. HLA-B*15:02 testing recommended before use in patients of Southeast Asian ancestry (SJS/TEN risk). Fosphenytoin preferred for IV loading (water-soluble prodrug; faster infusion rate; no purple glove syndrome; less hypotension).

medications.brandNames: Tegretol, Tegretol XR, Carbatrol, Epitol, Equetro (bipolar)
medications.mechanism: Same primary mechanism as phenytoin — use-dependent blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels; also modulates calcium channels; structurally related to TCAs (iminostilbene backbone); active metabolite carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide also has antiepileptic activity
medications.onset: PO — peak 4–8 hrs (IR); 12–26 hrs (XR); clinical effect days
medications.halfLife: Initial ~25–65 hrs; at steady state ~12–17 hrs (AUTOINDUCTION — carbamazepine induces its own metabolism via CYP3A4; half-life decreases by ~50% over 3–5 weeks; requires dose adjustment after initiation)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide (active; further metabolized by epoxide hydrolase); potent CYP450 INDUCER (CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2) — EXTENSIVE drug interactions; also induces UGT
medications.monitoring: Serum levels (therapeutic range 4–12 mcg/mL); CBC with differential (aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis — FDA boxed warning); LFTs; sodium (SIADH/hyponatremia); HLA-B15:02 before initiation in patients of Southeast Asian ancestry (SJS/TEN risk — FDA boxed warning)
medications.maxDose: Epilepsy: 1,200 mg/day (adults); 1,000 mg/day (children 12–15 yrs); Trigeminal neuralgia: 1,200 mg/day; Bipolar: 1,600 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: use with caution (extensive hepatic metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; neural tube defects (~1%), craniofacial defects, growth restriction; supplement folate
medications.sideEffects: Dose-related: dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia, diplopia, nausea, blurred vision. Idiosyncratic: rash (10%), SJS/TEN (FDA BOXED WARNING — HLA-B15:02 in Southeast Asian descent), DRESS, aplastic anemia/agranulocytosis (FDA BOXED WARNING — 5–8× higher risk than general population), hepatotoxicity, SIADH/hyponatremia (especially elderly). Chronic: osteomalacia (enzyme induction → vitamin D depletion)
medications.contraindications: Bone marrow depression; hypersensitivity to TCAs; concomitant MAOIs (14-day washout); concomitant nefazodone; HLA-B15:02 positive. TWO FDA BOXED WARNINGS: (1) aplastic anemia/agranulocytosis; (2) SJS/TEN with HLA-B15:02. Most drug interactions of any antiepileptic — reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, many other drugs. First-line for trigeminal neuralgia. Also used for focal epilepsy and bipolar disorder.

medications.mechanism: Stabilizes voltage-dependent sodium channels (preferentially binds to slow inactivated state); inhibits release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, aspartate); also weak inhibition of 5-HT3 receptors and voltage-gated calcium channels; broad-spectrum efficacy for both focal and generalized seizures
medications.onset: PO — peak 1.4–4.8 hrs (IR); clinical effect requires slow titration over 4–12 weeks
medications.halfLife: ~25 hrs (monotherapy); ~12–14 hrs (with enzyme inducers — carbamazepine, phenytoin); ~60 hrs (with valproate — CRITICAL interaction)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via UGT1A4 glucuronidation (primary — NOT CYP450); does NOT induce or inhibit hepatic enzymes → minimal effect on other drug metabolism; however, its OWN metabolism is dramatically affected by enzyme inducers and inhibitors
medications.monitoring: Rash (especially first 8 weeks); serum levels (reference range 3–14 mcg/mL; useful but not mandatory); mood (bipolar); seizure frequency; levels in pregnancy (decrease significantly — monitor monthly)
medications.maxDose: Monotherapy: 500 mg/day (divided BID); With valproate: 200 mg/day; With enzyme inducers: 700 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B): reduce by 25%; severe (Child-Pugh C): reduce by 50%; significant renal impairment: reduce dose (glucuronide metabolite accumulates)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Low teratogenic risk — preferred antiepileptic in women of childbearing potential (along with levetiracetam); dose-related low risk of major malformations; earlier concern about cleft palate not confirmed in subsequent studies; serum levels decrease significantly in pregnancy → monitor and increase dose as needed
medications.sideEffects: Dose-related: headache, dizziness, diplopia, ataxia, nausea, insomnia, somnolence. Idiosyncratic: RASH (~3.5% adults, higher in children; risk factors: rapid titration, concomitant valproate, young age); SJS/TEN (1% adults; higher in children — especially with valproate); aseptic meningitis (rare); hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (rare). Minimal cognitive effects, minimal weight gain, minimal sedation — well tolerated overall
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. CRITICAL DOSING RULES: (1) MUST titrate slowly to reduce rash/SJS risk; (2) with valproate: start at HALF the usual dose (valproate doubles lamotrigine half-life); (3) with enzyme inducers: need HIGHER doses; (4) hormonal contraceptives lower lamotrigine levels (uncertain mechanism). Broad-spectrum, well-tolerated, low teratogenicity — first-line for many epilepsy types and bipolar maintenance (prevents depressive episodes more than manic).

medications.mechanism: Potent dopamine D2 receptor antagonist (highest D2 affinity among common antipsychotics); also blocks alpha-1 adrenergic receptors; minimal anticholinergic, histaminergic, or serotonergic activity; high D2 potency → effective for positive symptoms but high EPS risk; D2 blockade in nigrostriatal pathway → EPS; in tuberoinfundibular pathway → hyperprolactinemia
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; IM — 10–20 min; IV — immediate; Decanoate — 3–9 days (peak ~6 days)
medications.halfLife: PO ~12–36 hrs; IV ~14–26 hrs; Decanoate ~3 weeks
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary), CYP2D6, and glucuronidation; reduced haloperidol (active metabolite — back-converted to haloperidol)
medications.monitoring: ECG (QTc — especially IV route); EPS/tardive dyskinesia; metabolic parameters; prolactin; temperature (NMS risk)
medications.maxDose: Acute psychosis: 20 mg/day (PO); Acute agitation: 5–10 mg IM q30–60min PRN; Decanoate: 450 mg/month (max); ICU delirium: 0.5–2 mg IV q4h PRN (off-label)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: EPS (most common — acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia); hyperprolactinemia (galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction); QTc prolongation (especially IV — torsades de pointes risk); NMS (rare but life-threatening); sedation (less than low-potency antipsychotics); minimal weight gain; minimal metabolic effects (advantage over atypicals). Tardive dyskinesia risk increases with duration
medications.contraindications: Parkinson disease (worsens motor symptoms); dementia-related psychosis (FDA boxed warning — increased mortality in elderly); QTc prolongation; comatose states; hypersensitivity. IV haloperidol: higher QTc risk — continuous cardiac monitoring recommended. Preferred for: acute agitation (IM — rapid onset, reliable absorption), ICU delirium (low dose), Tourette syndrome. Low-potency alternative: chlorpromazine.

medications.brandNames: Zyprexa, Zyprexa Zydis (ODT), Zyprexa Relprevv (ER IM — long-acting)
medications.mechanism: Multi-receptor antagonist with strong affinity for dopamine D1–D5, serotonin 5-HT2A/2B/2C, histamine H1, muscarinic M1–M5, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors; 5-HT2A/D2 ratio >1 (atypical profile → lower EPS than haloperidol); high H1 and 5-HT2C affinity → weight gain and metabolic effects; high muscarinic M3 affinity → dysregulates glucose/insulin metabolism
medications.onset: PO — peak 5–8 hrs; IM (short-acting) — 15–45 min; ODT — same as PO (absorbed GI, not sublingual)
medications.halfLife: ~21–54 hrs (mean ~30 hrs); longer in elderly, females, non-smokers
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via direct glucuronidation (40%), CYP1A2 (primary oxidative — 30%), CYP2D6 (minor); smoking induces CYP1A2 → smokers need higher doses; no active metabolites
medications.monitoring: Fasting glucose and HbA1c; lipid panel; weight/BMI (at baseline, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, 12 weeks, then quarterly); waist circumference; blood pressure; EPS
medications.maxDose: Schizophrenia: 20 mg/day; Bipolar mania: 20 mg/day; Acute agitation IM: 30 mg/day (10 mg × 3); Relprevv: 405 mg q4 weeks
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: consider lower starting dose; CYP1A2 interactions: smoking cessation → dose reduction needed (levels increase ~50%)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; neonatal EPS and withdrawal symptoms reported
medications.sideEffects: METABOLIC EFFECTS (most significant): weight gain (mean 2.6 kg at 6 weeks; ≥7% body weight in 22–29% — WORST among antipsychotics), hyperglycemia/new-onset diabetes, dyslipidemia (↑triglycerides, ↑LDL), metabolic syndrome; sedation/somnolence (dose-dependent); orthostatic hypotension; anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation); EPS (lower than haloperidol but higher than quetiapine); hyperprolactinemia (less than risperidone); QTc prolongation (rare)
medications.contraindications: Dementia-related psychosis (FDA boxed warning — increased mortality); hypersensitivity. Relprevv (ER IM): post-injection delirium/sedation syndrome (PDSS — 0.07% of injections; requires 3-hr observation in certified facility). IM olanzapine: do NOT combine with IM benzodiazepines (cardiorespiratory depression risk). Most effective antipsychotic for some measures but worst metabolic profile — reserve for patients who fail other agents or weigh risks vs benefits.

medications.mechanism: Multi-receptor antagonist; moderate affinity for dopamine D2 (LOWEST D2 affinity among atypicals — "loosely bound" → rapid dissociation → very low EPS), serotonin 5-HT2A, histamine H1 (very high — sedation), alpha-1 adrenergic (orthostatic hypotension); active metabolite norquetiapine: norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (antidepressant effect) + potent H1 antagonist + moderate 5-HT2C antagonist; NO significant muscarinic affinity (despite anticholinergic-like effects from norquetiapine's H1 blockade)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to norquetiapine (active) and other inactive metabolites; also sulfoxidation; susceptible to CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers
medications.monitoring: Same metabolic monitoring as olanzapine; fasting glucose, lipids, weight; blood pressure (orthostatic hypotension); cataracts (lens exam recommended at baseline and q6 months — preclinical data showed cataracts in dogs); thyroid function (dose-dependent TSH decrease)
medications.maxDose: Schizophrenia: 800 mg/day; Bipolar mania: 800 mg/day; Bipolar depression: 300 mg/day; MDD adjunct (XR): 300 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: start 25 mg/day, titrate slowly (reduced clearance by 30%)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/somnolence (most common — 18–57%; dose-dependent; H1 blockade), orthostatic hypotension (especially during titration — alpha-1 blockade), weight gain (moderate — less than olanzapine but more than risperidone), metabolic effects (hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia — cholesterol increase more than risperidone), dry mouth, constipation, dizziness; LOWEST EPS risk among atypicals (advantage); low prolactin elevation (may actually decrease prolactin); QTc prolongation (rare); cataracts (theoretical — based on animal data)
medications.contraindications: Dementia-related psychosis (FDA boxed warning); hypersensitivity. Broadest indications among atypicals: schizophrenia, bipolar mania, bipolar depression, MDD adjunct. Low-dose off-label use for insomnia is common but controversial (metabolic risks at any dose). Must titrate slowly due to orthostatic hypotension risk. Norquetiapine's NE reuptake inhibition explains unique antidepressant efficacy.

medications.brandNames: Risperdal, Risperdal M-Tab (ODT), Risperdal Consta (long-acting IM q2 weeks), Perseris (SC monthly)
medications.mechanism: Potent 5-HT2A and D2 receptor antagonist (balanced serotonin-dopamine antagonism — "SDA"); also blocks alpha-1 adrenergic (orthostatic hypotension) and H1 (mild sedation) receptors; 5-HT2A/D2 ratio ~20:1 (highest among atypicals → atypical profile at low doses); at higher doses (>6 mg), D2 occupancy increases → more EPS (dose-dependent); active metabolite 9-hydroxyrisperidone (paliperidone) has similar receptor profile
medications.onset: PO — peak 1–2 hrs; Consta IM — 3 weeks (requires oral supplementation for first 3 weeks)
medications.halfLife: Risperidone ~3 hrs (extensive CYP2D6 metabolizers) or ~20 hrs (poor metabolizers); 9-OH-risperidone ~21 hrs; "active moiety" (risperidone + 9-OH-risperidone) ~20 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary) to 9-hydroxyrisperidone/paliperidone (active — equipotent); CYP3A4 (minor); CYP2D6 polymorphism affects parent:metabolite ratio but NOT total active moiety (clinical effect similar across genotypes)
medications.excretion: Renal (~70% — primarily as active moiety); fecal (~14%)
medications.monitoring: Prolactin (HIGHEST prolactin elevation among atypicals); metabolic parameters (glucose, lipids, weight); EPS; blood pressure
medications.maxDose: Schizophrenia: 16 mg/day (usual 4–8 mg/day); Bipolar mania: 6 mg/day; Irritability in autism: 3 mg/day (pediatric); Consta: 50 mg q2 weeks
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30: start 0.5 mg BID, titrate slowly (max 3 mg/day); hepatic impairment: start 0.5 mg BID, titrate slowly
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; neonatal EPS and withdrawal
medications.sideEffects: Hyperprolactinemia (MOST COMMON and HIGHEST among atypicals — galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction, osteoporosis with chronic use); EPS (dose-dependent — low at ≤4 mg/day, increases significantly >6 mg/day; more than olanzapine/quetiapine but less than haloperidol); weight gain (moderate — less than olanzapine); orthostatic hypotension; sedation (less than quetiapine/olanzapine); metabolic effects (moderate); QTc prolongation (mild)
medications.contraindications: Dementia-related psychosis (FDA boxed warning); hypersensitivity. Most potent D2 antagonist among atypicals — good efficacy for positive symptoms. Dose-dependent EPS profile: "atypical" at low doses, increasingly "typical" at high doses. Paliperidone (active metabolite) available as separate drug (Invega) with less CYP450 metabolism.

medications.brandNames: Sinemet (IR — 10/100, 25/100, 25/250), Sinemet CR (controlled-release — 25/100, 50/200), Rytary (ER capsules), Duopa/Duodopa (enteral suspension), Inbrija (inhaled levodopa — rescue)
medications.mechanism: Levodopa: metabolic precursor of dopamine; crosses blood-brain barrier (unlike dopamine itself) → converted to dopamine by aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC/DOPA decarboxylase) in remaining nigrostriatal neurons → replenishes striatal dopamine. Carbidopa: peripheral AADC inhibitor; does NOT cross BBB; blocks peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine → (1) increases levodopa bioavailability to brain by ~4×; (2) reduces peripheral dopaminergic side effects (nausea, vomiting, orthostatic hypotension); minimum 75 mg/day carbidopa needed for adequate peripheral inhibition
medications.onset: IR — 30–60 min; CR — 1–2 hrs (less predictable); Inhaled — 10–20 min (rescue for OFF episodes)
medications.durationOfAction: IR — 3–5 hrs (shortens with disease progression — "wearing off"); CR — 4–6 hrs; Rytary — 4–5 hrs
medications.halfLife: Levodopa ~1.5 hrs (IR); ~2 hrs (CR); Carbidopa ~2 hrs; short half-life is the fundamental problem → pulsatile dopamine stimulation → motor complications
medications.metabolism: Levodopa: peripheral AADC (blocked by carbidopa), COMT (to 3-O-methyldopa), MAO; central AADC (to dopamine — therapeutic effect); Carbidopa: not extensively metabolized
medications.monitoring: Motor response (ON/OFF time diary); dyskinesias; orthostatic blood pressure; psychiatric symptoms (hallucinations, impulse control disorders); melanoma screening (theoretical risk — debated)
medications.maxDose: No absolute maximum; typical range 300–1,600 mg/day levodopa (divided 3–6 doses); dyskinesia risk increases >400 mg/day; ensure ≥75 mg/day carbidopa
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; use with caution in severe renal/hepatic/cardiovascular disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; contraindicated in nursing (inhibits lactation)
medications.sideEffects: Early: nausea (most common — take with food initially; less with adequate carbidopa), orthostatic hypotension, somnolence/sleep attacks, dizziness. Motor complications (develop in 30–50% within 4–6 years; 100% by 20 years): wearing off (end-of-dose deterioration), ON-OFF fluctuations (unpredictable), peak-dose dyskinesias (choreiform movements), early-morning dystonia. Psychiatric: vivid dreams, hallucinations (especially visual — dose-related), impulse control disorders (less than dopamine agonists), psychosis. Other: darkening of urine/sweat (melanin metabolites — harmless)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant non-selective MAOIs (hypertensive crisis — stop MAOI 14 days before; selective MAO-B inhibitors like selegiline/rasagiline are safe); narrow-angle glaucoma (relative); suspicious undiagnosed skin lesions (melanoma concern — debated). FIRST-LINE therapy for motor symptoms in Parkinson disease. IR formulation preferred for initiation (more predictable absorption than CR). Take on empty stomach for best absorption in advanced disease (protein competes for transport). "Drug holiday" is DANGEROUS — do NOT abruptly discontinue (NMS-like syndrome).

medications.brandNames: Imitrex (PO, nasal, SC), Onzetra Xsail (nasal), Zembrace SymTouch (SC autoinjector), Tosymra (nasal), Treximet (with naproxen)
medications.mechanism: Agonist at 5-HT1B receptors on intracranial blood vessels → cranial vasoconstriction (reverses pathologic vasodilation during migraine); agonist at 5-HT1D receptors on trigeminal nerve terminals → inhibits release of pro-inflammatory neuropeptides (CGRP, substance P, neurokinin A) → reduces neurogenic inflammation and pain transmission; may also inhibit central trigeminal nucleus caudalis transmission
medications.onset: SC — 10–15 min (fastest); Intranasal — 15–30 min; PO — 30–60 min; most effective when taken early in attack while pain is still mild
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via MAO-A (primary) to inactive indole acetic acid analogue; also renal MAO-A; low oral bioavailability (~14% PO; ~97% SC; ~16% intranasal)
medications.monitoring: Cardiovascular risk assessment before first dose (chest symptoms — usually non-cardiac "triptan sensations"); blood pressure
medications.maxDose: PO: 200 mg/24 hrs (100 mg × 2; separate by ≥2 hrs); SC: 12 mg/24 hrs (6 mg × 2; separate by ≥1 hr); Intranasal: 40 mg/24 hrs (20 mg × 2)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal adjustment; mild-moderate hepatic impairment: max single PO dose 50 mg; severe hepatic impairment: contraindicated (PO)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; limited data; generally avoided but may be used if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Injection site reactions (SC — 59%), tingling/paresthesia, warm/hot sensation, flushing, chest/throat/neck tightness/pressure/heaviness ("triptan sensations" — non-cardiac in most cases; ~4%), dizziness, drowsiness, nausea; medication overuse headache (with frequent use — limit to ≤10 days/month); transient blood pressure elevation; serotonin syndrome (rare — with SSRIs/SNRIs; theoretical concern, low actual risk)
medications.contraindications: Ischemic heart disease, coronary vasospasm (Prinzmetal angina), uncontrol

medications.brandNames: Neurontin, Gralise (ER — PHN only), Horizant (enacarbil prodrug — RLS, PHN)
medications.mechanism: Binds with high affinity to the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels → reduces calcium-dependent release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, norepinephrine, substance P) at presynaptic nerve terminals; structurally related to GABA but has NO effect on GABA binding, uptake, or degradation; does NOT bind GABA-A, GABA-B, or benzodiazepine receptors; does NOT block sodium channels or inhibit COX
medications.onset: PO — peak 2–4 hrs; clinical effect for pain may take 1–2 weeks; for seizures, days
medications.halfLife: ~5–7 hrs; not affected by dose or multiple dosing
medications.metabolism: NOT metabolized — excreted entirely unchanged by kidneys; no CYP450 involvement; no drug interactions via metabolism
medications.excretion: Renal (100% unchanged); proportional to creatinine clearance
medications.monitoring: Renal function (dose adjustment); sedation; signs of misuse/abuse (Schedule V in some states); suicidality (FDA warning for all antiepileptics); respiratory depression (with opioids — FDA warning)
medications.maxDose: PHN: 1,800 mg/day (600 mg TID); Epilepsy: 3,600 mg/day (1,200 mg TID); doses above 1,800 mg/day have DECREASING bioavailability (saturable L-amino acid transporter absorption — unique pharmacokinetic limitation)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥60: 300–1,200 mg TID; CrCl 30–59: 200–700 mg BID; CrCl 15–29: 200–700 mg daily; CrCl 15: 100–300 mg daily; Hemodialysis: supplemental dose after each session; NO hepatic adjustment needed
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Somnolence/drowsiness (most common — 19%), dizziness (17%), ataxia, fatigue, peripheral edema, weight gain, nystagmus, tremor; respiratory depression (FDA warning — especially with opioids, CNS depressants, elderly, COPD); abuse potential (euphoria at high doses — Schedule V in some states); suicidality (FDA class warning for antiepileptics)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. KEY PHARMACOKINETIC LIMITATION: dose-dependent decrease in bioavailability (60% at 900 mg/day → 27% at 4,800 mg/day) due to saturable intestinal absorption via L-amino acid transporter → ceiling effect at higher doses; this is why pregabalin (linear absorption) may be preferred at higher doses. No drug interactions. Widely used off-label for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, anxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal.

medications.drugClass: Gabapentinoid — alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligand (Schedule V controlled substance)
medications.mechanism: Same target as gabapentin — binds α2δ-1 subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels with 3–6× HIGHER affinity than gabapentin → reduces calcium influx → decreased release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, norepinephrine, substance P); also modulates descending noradrenergic and serotonergic pain pathways; does NOT bind GABA receptors, sodium channels, or COX enzymes; may increase GABA transporter density with prolonged use
medications.onset: PO — peak ~1.5 hrs; clinical effect for pain 1–2 weeks; for anxiety within 1 week
medications.metabolism: Negligible (2%); NOT metabolized by CYP450; excreted unchanged
medications.excretion: Renal (~98% unchanged); proportional to creatinine clearance
medications.monitoring: Same as gabapentin; also monitor for edema, weight gain, visual changes (blurred vision)
medications.maxDose: DPN: 300 mg/day (100 mg TID); PHN: 600 mg/day (200 mg TID or 300 mg BID); Epilepsy: 600 mg/day; Fibromyalgia: 450 mg/day (225 mg BID); SCI pain: 600 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥60: full dose; CrCl 30–60: 50% of dose; CrCl 15–30: 25% of dose; CrCl 15: 12.5% of dose; supplemental dose after hemodialysis; NO hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Dizziness (most common — 29%), somnolence (22%), peripheral edema, weight gain (up to 7% body weight in 12%), dry mouth, blurred vision, difficulty concentrating; respiratory depression (FDA warning — with opioids/CNS depressants); abuse potential (euphoria — Schedule V; higher abuse potential than gabapentin); angioedema (rare); rhabdomyolysis (rare); suicidality (FDA class warning)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. KEY ADVANTAGES over gabapentin: (1) LINEAR pharmacokinetics (no saturable absorption — predictable dose-response); (2) higher potency (3–6× more potent); (3) BID dosing (vs TID); (4) FDA-approved for fibromyalgia and GAD (Europe). Schedule V controlled substance (gabapentin is not federally scheduled). More expensive than gabapentin.

medications.mechanism: Not fully elucidated; centrally acting — (1) inhibits COX enzymes in the CNS (particularly COX-3 variant, debated) → reduces prostaglandin synthesis centrally → antipyretic and analgesic effects; (2) activates descending serotonergic inhibitory pain pathways; (3) modulates endocannabinoid system (metabolite AM404 inhibits anandamide reuptake); (4) NO significant peripheral anti-inflammatory activity (unlike NSAIDs) — does NOT inhibit platelet function, does NOT cause GI ulceration, does NOT affect renal prostaglandins at therapeutic doses
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; IV — 5–10 min; Rectal — 1–2 hrs
medications.halfLife: ~2–3 hrs (adults); prolonged in neonates (~7 hrs), liver disease, and overdose
medications.metabolism: Hepatic — primarily Phase II conjugation: glucuronidation (~40–67%) and sulfation (~20–46%); minor pathway via CYP2E1 (and CYP1A2, CYP3A4) → NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinoneimine — highly reactive toxic metabolite); NAPQI normally detoxified by glutathione conjugation → mercapturic acid (nontoxic); in OVERDOSE: glucuronidation/sulfation pathways saturated → increased CYP2E1 shunting → excess NAPQI → glutathione depletion → NAPQI binds hepatocyte proteins → centrilobular hepatic necrosis
medications.monitoring: LFTs if chronic use or risk factors; serum acetaminophen level in overdose (Rumack-Matthew nomogram); total daily dose from ALL sources (many combination products contain acetaminophen)
medications.maxDose: Adults: 4,000 mg/day (some guidelines recommend ≤3,000 mg/day in elderly or chronic use); 2,000 mg/day with chronic alcohol use or liver disease; Pediatric: 75 mg/kg/day (max 4,000 mg/day); IV: 4,000 mg/day (adults >50 kg); single dose max 1,000 mg PO or 15 mg/kg IV
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Mild-moderate hepatic impairment: reduce dose, limit to 2,000 mg/day; severe hepatic impairment: AVOID; renal impairment: extend dosing interval (CrCl 30: q8h instead of q4-6h)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Generally considered safe in pregnancy (preferred analgesic/antipyretic); crosses placenta
medications.sideEffects: Remarkably safe at therapeutic doses; rare: rash, hypersensitivity, thrombocytopenia. HEPATOTOXICITY in overdose: leading cause of acute liver failure in US/UK; dose-dependent; single ingestion >150 mg/kg (or >7.5–10 g in adults) potentially toxic; risk factors: chronic alcohol use, fasting/malnutrition (glutathione depletion), CYP2E1 inducers (isoniazid, chronic alcohol); clinical stages: Stage 1 (0–24 hrs) — asymptomatic or nausea; Stage 2 (24–72 hrs) — RUQ pain, rising AST/ALT; Stage 3 (72–96 hrs) — peak hepatotoxicity (AST/ALT >1,000, coagulopathy, possible ALF); Stage 4 — recovery or death. ANTIDOTE: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — replenishes glutathione; most effective within 8–10 hrs of ingestion but beneficial even late; IV regimen: 300 mg/kg over 20–21 hrs; oral regimen: 140 mg/kg loading then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses
medications.contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment; severe active liver disease; hypersensitivity. Warn patients about acetaminophen content in combination products (hydrocodone/acetaminophen, cold medications). FDA max single OTC dose: 650 mg (extra strength).

medications.drugClass: NSAID — propionic acid derivative (non-selective COX inhibitor)
medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 (relatively balanced COX-1/COX-2 ratio) → decreases prostaglandin synthesis → anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects; COX-1 inhibition → decreased thromboxane A2 (antiplatelet effect — reversible, unlike aspirin) and decreased protective GI prostaglandins (GI toxicity); COX-2 inhibition → decreased inflammatory prostaglandins at site of injury/inflammation; also acts centrally in spinal cord and CNS
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; IV — 10 min; peak 1–2 hrs
medications.halfLife: ~2 hrs (adults); short half-life but anti-inflammatory effect persists longer in synovial fluid
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 (primary) to inactive metabolites; also CYP2C8; >99% protein bound
medications.monitoring: Renal function; blood pressure; signs of GI bleeding; CBC (if prolonged use); LFTs
medications.maxDose: OTC: 1,200 mg/day; Prescription: 3,200 mg/day (800 mg QID); Pediatric: 40 mg/kg/day (divided q6-8h); IV: 3,200 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl 30); avoid in advanced liver disease; use lowest effective dose for shortest duration in mild-moderate impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C (1st/2nd trimester); Category D (3rd trimester — premature closure of ductus arteriosus); AVOID after 20 weeks gestation (FDA warning — oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment)
medications.sideEffects: GI (most common — dyspepsia 3–9%, nausea, abdominal pain, GI ulceration/bleeding/perforation); cardiovascular (FDA boxed warning — increased risk of MI and stroke, dose- and duration-dependent; naproxen may have lower CV risk); renal (AKI, fluid retention, edema, hyperkalemia — especially in volume-depleted, elderly, CKD, ACEi/ARB/diuretic use); hypertension; platelet dysfunction (reversible); hypersensitivity (bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive asthma — cross-reactivity); SJS/TEN (rare)
medications.contraindications: Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer; history of NSAID-induced asthma/urticaria/angioedema; perioperative use in CABG surgery (FDA boxed warning); severe renal impairment; 3rd trimester pregnancy; hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNINGS: (1) cardiovascular thrombotic events; (2) GI bleeding. Ibuprofen may interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet effect (competes for COX-1 binding site) — take aspirin ≥30 min before ibuprofen or ≥8 hrs after.

medications.brandNames: Aleve (OTC — naproxen sodium 220 mg), Naprosyn (Rx — naproxen 250/375/500 mg), Anaprox (naproxen sodium), EC-Naprosyn
medications.mechanism: Same as ibuprofen — non-selective COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor; slightly more COX-1 selective than ibuprofen
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; peak 2–4 hrs (naproxen); 1–2 hrs (naproxen sodium — faster absorption)
medications.halfLife: ~12–17 hrs (longest half-life among common OTC NSAIDs — allows BID dosing)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 to 6-O-desmethylnaproxen (inactive); >99% protein bound
medications.monitoring: Same as ibuprofen
medications.maxDose: OTC: 660 mg/day (220 mg TID); Rx: 1,500 mg/day (initial), then 1,000 mg/day; Gout flare: 750 mg initially, then 250 mg q8h; Pediatric: 10 mg/kg/day (divided BID)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as ibuprofen
medications.pregnancyLactation: Same as ibuprofen
medications.sideEffects: Same class effects as ibuprofen; may have LOWER cardiovascular risk than other NSAIDs (PRECISION trial — non-inferior to celecoxib; some meta-analyses suggest naproxen is associated with lowest CV risk among NSAIDs); higher GI risk than celecoxib
medications.contraindications: Same as ibuprofen. Advantages over ibuprofen: longer duration (BID vs QID), possibly lower CV risk. Disadvantage: higher GI risk than COX-2 selective agents. Does NOT interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet effect as much as ibuprofen (longer half-life allows temporal separation).

medications.brandNames: Toradol (discontinued brand; generic widely available), Sprix (intranasal)
medications.drugClass: NSAID — pyrrolizine carboxylic acid derivative (potent COX-1 inhibitor)
medications.mechanism: Same COX inhibition as other NSAIDs but with MUCH greater analgesic potency relative to anti-inflammatory effect; more COX-1 selective than ibuprofen → higher GI bleeding risk; analgesic potency comparable to moderate-dose opioids
medications.onset: IM — 10 min; IV — immediate; PO — 30–60 min; peak 1–2 hrs (IM), 30–60 min (IV)
medications.halfLife: ~5–6 hrs (adults); prolonged in elderly (~7 hrs) and renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via conjugation (glucuronidation) and p-hydroxylation; 50% metabolized
medications.monitoring: Renal function; signs of GI bleeding; platelet function; blood pressure
medications.maxDose: STRICT 5-DAY MAXIMUM DURATION (FDA boxed warning). Single-dose IM: 60 mg (65 yrs), 30 mg (≥65 yrs/renal impairment/50 kg). Single-dose IV: 30 mg (65 yrs), 15 mg (≥65 yrs/renal impairment/50 kg). Multiple-dose IM/IV: 30 mg q6h (max 120 mg/day) for 65 yrs; 15 mg q6h (max 60 mg/day) for ≥65 yrs/renal impairment/50 kg. PO: 20 mg then 10 mg q4-6h (max 40 mg/day) — continuation therapy ONLY
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Advanced renal impairment: CONTRAINDICATED; moderate impairment: use lower dose; hepatic impairment: use with caution
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C (1st/2nd trimester); AVOID 3rd trimester
medications.sideEffects: GI bleeding/ulceration/perforation (HIGHEST risk among NSAIDs — dose- and duration-dependent); renal impairment; postoperative wound bleeding; edema; headache; nausea; drowsiness
medications.contraindications: Active or history of peptic ulcer/GI bleeding; advanced renal impairment or risk of renal failure; labor and delivery; nursing mothers; cerebrovascular bleeding; hemorrhagic diathesis; concomitant aspirin or other NSAIDs; concomitant probenecid or pentoxifylline; prophylactic use before major surgery; perioperative CABG. FDA BOXED WARNINGS: (1) GI risk; (2) CV thrombotic risk; (3) renal risk; (4) bleeding risk; (5) 5-day maximum duration; (6) NOT for minor or chronic pain. Most potent parenteral NSAID — opioid-sparing in acute/postoperative pain.

medications.brandNames: MS Contin (ER tablets), Kadian (ER capsules), Roxanol (oral solution), Duramorph (epidural/intrathecal), Infumorph (epidural/intrathecal), Arymo ER, MorphaBond ER
medications.mechanism: Full agonist at mu-opioid receptors (MOR — primary; analgesia, euphoria, respiratory depression, miosis, constipation, physical dependence); also activates kappa and delta opioid receptors; MOR activation → inhibits adenylyl cyclase → decreases cAMP → opens K⁺ channels and closes Ca²⁺ channels → hyperpolarizes neurons → inhibits neurotransmitter release → analgesia; acts at both spinal (dorsal horn — presynaptic inhibition of substance P release) and supraspinal (periaqueductal gray, rostral ventromedial medulla) levels
medications.onset: PO IR — 30 min; IV — 5–10 min; IM/SC — 15–30 min; peak PO 60 min, IV 20 min
medications.halfLife: ~2–4 hrs (adults); prolonged in renal/hepatic impairment and neonates
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via UGT2B7 glucuronidation (primary — NOT CYP450-dependent) to morphine-3-glucuronide (M3G — ~60%; inactive, possibly neuroexcitatory — myoclonus, hyperalgesia) and morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G — ~10%; ACTIVE — analgesic potency 2–4× morphine; accumulates in renal failure → prolonged sedation/respiratory depression)
medications.excretion: Renal (~90% as glucuronide metabolites; ~10% unchanged); minor biliary/fecal
medications.monitoring: Pain scores; respiratory rate and sedation (especially first 24–72 hrs); bowel function; signs of dependence/misuse; renal function (M6G accumulation)
medications.maxDose: No absolute ceiling dose for cancer pain (titrate to effect); typical starting doses: PO 15–30 mg q4h (opioid-naïve); IV 2–4 mg q3-4h; PCA: 1–2 mg demand dose, 6–10 min lockout
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Renal impairment: REDUCE dose 25–50% (M3G and M6G accumulate — risk of prolonged sedation, respiratory depression, neuroexcitation); consider alternative opioid (hydromorphone, fentanyl) in severe renal impairment; hepatic impairment (cirrhosis): reduce dose (decreased clearance, increased half-life)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome (NOWS) with chronic use
medications.sideEffects: Constipation (most common — does NOT develop tolerance; prophylactic bowel regimen recommended), nausea/vomiting (tolerance develops), sedation/drowsiness, respiratory depression (dose-limiting toxicity), pruritus (especially neuraxial — histamine release), urinary retention, miosis, hypotension (histamine release → vasodilation), physical dependence and tolerance (with chronic use), hyperalgesia (paradoxical — with chronic high-dose use)
medications.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression; acute or severe bronchial asthma (unmonitored); known or suspected GI obstruction (including paralytic ileus); hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNINGS: (1) addiction, abuse, misuse; (2) life-threatening respiratory depression; (3) neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome; (4) risks with concomitant benzodiazepines or CNS depressants; (5) ER/LA formulations: risk of fatal overdose with improper use. Prototype opioid — all others compared to morphine for equianalgesic dosing.

medications.brandNames: Dilaudid (IR tablets, oral liquid, injection), Exalgo (ER tablets)
medications.mechanism: Same as morphine — full mu-opioid receptor agonist; semi-synthetic derivative of morphine (6-oxo modification); more lipophilic → faster onset; ~5–7× more potent than morphine (IV equianalgesic ratio morphine:hydromorphone ≈ 5:1)
medications.onset: PO — 15–30 min; IV — 5 min; SC — 15 min; peak PO 30–60 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via UGT2B7 glucuronidation to hydromorphone-3-glucuronide (H3G — inactive but potentially neuroexcitatory; accumulates in renal failure); also 6-hydroxy reduction metabolites; NO active analgesic metabolites (unlike morphine's M6G) — advantage in renal impairment
medications.monitoring: Same as morphine
medications.maxDose: No absolute ceiling for cancer pain; typical starting: PO 2–4 mg q4-6h; IV 0.2–1 mg q2-3h; PCA: 0.2 mg demand dose
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Moderate renal impairment: reduce dose 50%; severe renal impairment: reduce dose 75% (H3G accumulates but less clinically significant than M6G); preferred over morphine in renal impairment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; NOWS with chronic use
medications.sideEffects: Same as morphine; possibly less nausea and pruritus than morphine (less histamine release); H3G accumulation in renal failure may cause neuroexcitation (myoclonus, agitation, seizures)
medications.contraindications: Same as morphine. Advantages over morphine: (1) no active analgesic metabolite → safer in renal impairment; (2) higher potency → smaller volume for injection; (3) higher water solubility → suitable for high-concentration SC infusions; (4) possibly better clinical utility profile. Equianalgesic: morphine 30 mg PO ≈ hydromorphone 6 mg PO; morphine 10 mg IV ≈ hydromorphone 1.5–2 mg IV.

medications.brandNames: OxyContin (ER — abuse-deterrent), Roxicodone (IR), OxyIR, Percocet (with acetaminophen), Percodan (with aspirin), Xtampza ER
medications.mechanism: Full mu-opioid receptor agonist; also has kappa-opioid receptor activity (may contribute to analgesic effect); semi-synthetic derivative of thebaine; ~1.5× more potent than morphine PO (equianalgesic: morphine 30 mg PO ≈ oxycodone 20 mg PO); higher oral bioavailability than morphine (~60–87% vs ~30%)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary — to noroxycodone, weakly active) and CYP2D6 (minor — to oxymorphone, active but low concentrations); CYP3A4 inhibitors increase oxycodone levels significantly; CYP2D6 polymorphism has minimal clinical impact (unlike codeine)
medications.monitoring: Same as morphine; CYP3A4 drug interactions
medications.maxDose: No absolute ceiling for cancer pain; typical starting: IR 5–15 mg q4-6h; ER 10 mg q12h (opioid-naïve)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Hepatic impairment: reduce starting dose by 33–50% (CYP3A4 metabolism); renal impairment: reduce dose (AUC increased ~50% in CrCl 60)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; NOWS with chronic use
medications.sideEffects: Same as morphine; constipation may be slightly more common
medications.contraindications: Same as morphine; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors require dose reduction. OxyContin abuse-deterrent formulation: resists crushing, dissolving, and extraction (reduces but does not eliminate abuse). Higher oral bioavailability than morphine makes it preferred for oral dosing in some settings.

medications.brandNames: Duragesic (transdermal patch), Sublimaze (IV), Actiq (transmucosal lozenge), Fentora (buccal tablet), Abstral (sublingual), Lazanda (nasal spray), Subsys (sublingual spray), Ionsys (iontophoretic transdermal)
medications.mechanism: Full mu-opi
medications.onset: IV — immediate (1–2 min); IM — 7–8 min; Transdermal — 6–12 hrs (lag time); Transmucosal — 5–15 min; Intranasal — 5–10 min
medications.durationOfAction: IV single dose — 30–60 min (rapid redistribution); Transdermal — 72 hrs (continuous release); Transmucosal — 1–2 hrs
medications.halfLife: IV — ~3.7 hrs (terminal; 219 min); context-sensitive half-time increases dramatically with prolonged infusion (accumulation in fat/muscle); Transdermal — ~17 hrs (apparent — due to continued absorption from skin depot after patch removal)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to norfentanyl (inactive); NO active metabolites (major advantage over morphine in renal failure); oral bioavailability ~30% (extensive first-pass); transmucosal bioavailability 50–90%; intranasal ~89%
medications.monitoring: Respiratory rate and sedation (continuous monitoring for IV); pain scores; signs of misuse; patch adhesion and heat exposure (transdermal)
medications.maxDose: Highly variable by formulation and indication. IV anesthesia: 2–50 mcg/kg; IV analgesia: 25–100 mcg q1-2h; Transdermal: start 12–25 mcg/hr (opioid-tolerant ONLY); TIRF (transmucosal immediate-release): individually titrated for breakthrough cancer pain in opioid-tolerant patients ONLY
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Preferred opioid in renal failure (no active metabolites); hepatic impairment: reduce dose (CYP3A4 metabolism); CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, protease inhibitors, grapefruit juice) → dramatically increased fentanyl levels → fatal respiratory depression
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; NOWS with chronic use
medications.sideEffects: Same as morphine; LESS histamine release (less pruritus, less hypotension than morphine); chest wall rigidity ("wooden chest syndrome" — with rapid high-dose IV bolus; treat with naloxone or neuromuscular blocker); bradycardia (vagal stimulation); respiratory depression (dose-limiting); constipation (less than morphine with transdermal); transdermal-specific: skin irritation, accidental exposure risk (especially children — fatal), heat increases absorption (fever, heating pads, hot baths → overdose)
medications.contraindications: Opioid-naïve patients (transdermal and TIRF formulations — FDA boxed warning; fatal respiratory depression); significant respiratory depression; acute/intermittent pain; postoperative pain (transdermal); hypersensitivity. FDA REMS for TIRF products (Transmucosal Immediate-Release Fentanyl). Transdermal patches: cut/damaged patches can cause fatal overdose; dispose properly (fold adhesive sides together). Illicit fentanyl is the leading cause of opioid overdose deaths in the US.

medications.brandNames: Ultram (IR), Ultram ER, ConZip (ER), Qdolo (oral solution)
medications.drugClass: Centrally acting analgesic — atypical opioid / dual-mechanism (Schedule IV)
medications.mechanism: DUAL mechanism — (1) weak mu-opioid receptor agonist (parent drug has low MOR affinity; active metabolite O-desmethyltramadol/M1 has ~200× higher MOR affinity — CYP2D6-dependent); (2) inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake (SNRI-like activity — parent drug); analgesic effect is ~50% opioid and ~50% monoaminergic; overall potency ~1/10th morphine
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (to M1/O-desmethyltramadol — active, primary analgesic metabolite) and CYP3A4 (to N-desmethyltramadol — inactive); CYP2D6 polymorphism critically affects efficacy: poor metabolizers (~7% Caucasians) → minimal M1 → reduced analgesia; ultrarapid metabolizers → excess M1 → increased opioid effects/toxicity
medications.monitoring: Pain scores; seizure risk; serotonin syndrome signs; respiratory depression (especially with other CNS depressants); signs of misuse
medications.maxDose: IR: 400 mg/day (100 mg QID); ER: 300 mg/day; Age >75: 300 mg/day; CrCl 30: 200 mg/day (IR only; ER contraindicated); Severe hepatic impairment: 50 mg q12h
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30: max 200 mg/day (IR), extend interval to q12h; ER contraindicated; Cirrhosis: 50 mg q12h; dialysis: poorly removed
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; NOWS with chronic use; FDA warning: avoid in children 12 yrs and breastfeeding mothers (CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers → fatal respiratory depression in nursing infants)
medications.sideEffects: Nausea/vomiting (most common — 24%), dizziness, constipation (less than typical opioids), headache, somnolence; SEIZURES (dose-dependent — 0.5–1%; risk increases with SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, antipsychotics; lower seizure threshold); serotonin syndrome (with SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, TCAs, triptans — higher risk than other opioids due to SNRI activity); respiratory depression (less than typical opioids but NOT absent — especially with other CNS depressants); dependence/withdrawal (both opioid-type AND SNRI-type withdrawal — unique dual withdrawal syndrome)
medications.contraindications: Children 12 yrs; post-tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy in children 18 yrs; concomitant MAOIs (within 14 days); significant respiratory depression; acute/severe asthma; known CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers; hypersensitivity. Lower abuse potential than typical opioids (Schedule IV vs II) but abuse does occur. NOT recommended as first-line opioid — limited efficacy advantage over NSAIDs with added risks.

medications.brandNames: Narcan (nasal spray — OTC since 2023), Kloxxado (8 mg nasal spray), Zimhi (5 mg IM autoinjector), Evzio (discontinued autoinjector)
medications.drugClass: Opioid antagonist — pure competitive mu-opioid receptor antagonist
medications.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at mu, kappa, and delta opioid receptors with HIGHEST affinity for mu receptors; displaces opioid agonists from receptors → rapidly reverses respiratory depression, sedation, and hypotension of opioid overdose; does NOT produce analgesia, euphoria, or respiratory depression when given alone (no intrinsic activity); does NOT reverse effects of non-opioid CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol, barbiturates)
medications.onset: IV — 1–2 min (fastest); IM — 2–5 min; SC — 2–5 min; Intranasal — 8–13 min (Tmax 15–30 min); endotracheal — 2–3 min
medications.durationOfAction: 30–90 min (SHORTER than most opioids → re-sedation/re-narcotization risk — must observe for recurrence)
medications.halfLife: ~30–90 min (mean ~60–90 min); shorter than virtually all opioid agonists
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via glucuronide conjugation to naloxone-3-glucuronide (inactive); extensive first-pass metabolism (oral bioavailability ~2–5% — essentially inactive orally; this is why oral naloxone in combination products like Suboxone acts only if injected)
medications.monitoring: Respiratory rate and effort (primary endpoint — goal is respiratory rate >10/min, NOT full consciousness); oxygen saturation; recurrence of respiratory depression (observe ≥2 hrs after last dose; longer for long-acting opioids or transdermal fentanyl); withdrawal symptoms
medications.maxDose: No absolute maximum; titrate to respiratory function. Initial: IV 0.04–0.4 mg (titrate in 0.04 mg increments in opioid-dependent patients to avoid precipitating severe withdrawal); IM 0.4–2 mg; Intranasal 4 mg (one spray); may repeat q2-3 min as needed. AHA guidelines: 2–4 mg IN or 0.2–2 mg IV/IM, repeat q3 min PRN. Fentanyl overdoses may require higher/repeated doses
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment needed; extremely safe medication
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; may precipitate withdrawal in opioid-dependent mother → fetal distress; use only if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Opioid withdrawal syndrome (if given to opioid-dependent patient — agitation, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, piloerection, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis); rare: pulmonary edema, ventricular arrhythmias, seizures (very rare — possibly related to catecholamine surge from abrupt withdrawal); higher doses (8 mg IN) associated with more frequent/severe withdrawal than 4 mg (37.6% vs 19.4% vomiting)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. No abuse potential; not a controlled substance. Available OTC (3 mg and 4 mg nasal spray) since 2023. Goal of administration: restore adequate RESPIRATION, not full consciousness (titrate to avoid precipitating withdrawal). After reversal, patient MUST be monitored for re-narcotization (opioid outlasts naloxone). For suspected fentanyl overdose, may need repeated doses due to fentanyl's high receptor affinity.

medications.mechanism: (1) ACETAMINOPHEN ANTIDOTE: replenishes hepatic glutathione stores (NAC is a precursor to cysteine → cysteine is rate-limiting substrate for glutathione synthesis); glutathione conjugates and detoxifies NAPQI (toxic acetaminophen metabolite); also serves as direct substitute for glutathione in NAPQI conjugation; may also enhance sulfation pathway and have direct antioxidant/anti-inflammatory effects; (2) MUCOLYTIC: free sulfhydryl group opens disulfide bonds in mucus glycoproteins → reduces mucus viscosity
medications.onset: IV — immediate (glutathione replenishment begins within hours); Oral — peak 1–2 hrs; most effective when given within 8–10 hrs of acetaminophen ingestion but beneficial even late (up to 24+ hrs)
medications.durationOfAction: Treatment course-dependent (21 hrs IV; 72 hrs oral)
medications.metabolism: Deacetylated to cysteine; further metabolized to glutathione and other disulfides; also forms N,N-diacetylcysteine
medications.monitoring: Acetaminophen level and Rumack-Matthew nomogram; INR (endpoint: INR 1.5); AST/ALT (endpoint: declining and 50% of peak or 1,000 IU/L); signs of anaphylactoid reaction (IV — common); clinical status
medications.maxDose: IV protocol (FDA-approved 3-bag, 21-hr): total 300 mg/kg — Loading: 150 mg/kg over 60 min; 2nd infusion: 50 mg/kg over 4 hrs; 3rd infusion: 100 mg/kg over 16 hrs. Modified 2-bag (20-hr): 200 mg/kg over 4 hrs then 100 mg/kg over 16 hrs (fewer anaphylactoid reactions). Oral (72-hr Prescott protocol): 140 mg/kg loading then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 additional doses. Mucolytic: 3–5 mL of 20% solution nebulized TID-QID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; use in hepatic failure is the primary indication
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; should be given if indicated — acetaminophen toxicity is more dangerous than NAC
medications.sideEffects: IV: anaphylactoid reactions (up to 10–20% — flushing, urticaria, pruritus, bronchospasm, hypotension; NOT true IgE-mediated; rate-related — most common during loading dose; slow infusion rate and give antihistamines; rarely requires discontinuation); nausea/vomiting. Oral: nausea/vomiting (very common — unpleasant taste/smell of sulfur; mix with cola or juice); diarrhea
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity (rare true allergy). Virtually always give NAC if acetaminophen toxicity is suspected — benefit far outweighs risk. If started within 8 hrs of ingestion, hepatotoxicity is virtually prevented. Continue NAC until INR 1.5 and clinical improvement, even if this exceeds the standard protocol duration.

medications.brandNames: EpiPen (0.3 mg autoinjector), EpiPen Jr (0.15 mg), Auvi-Q (autoinjector with voice instructions), Adrenalin (injection — 1 mg/mL), various generic autoinjectors
medications.drugClass: Catecholamine — direct-acting sympathomimetic (non-selective alpha and beta adrenergic agonist)
medications.mechanism: Acts on ALL adrenergic receptors: Alpha-1 → vasoconstriction (increases SVR, reverses hypotension and mucosal edema in anaphylaxis); Alpha-2 → vasoconstriction; Beta-1 → positive inotropy and chronotropy (increases cardiac output); Beta-2 → bronchodilation (reverses bronchospasm), stabilizes mast cell membranes (inhibits further histamine/mediator release — unique among vasopressors), increases glycogenolysis. In ANAPHYLAXIS: alpha-1 effects reverse vasodilation and reduce vascular permeability/angioedema; beta-1 effects support cardiac output; beta-2 effects reverse bronchospasm and inhibit mediator release — epinephrine is the ONLY drug that addresses ALL pathophysiologic mechanisms of anaphylaxis
medications.onset: IM (anterolateral thigh) — 5–10 min (peak 8–10 min); IV — immediate (1–2 min); SC — slower and less reliable than IM; Inhaled — 3–5 min
medications.durationOfAction: IM — 15–20 min (short — may need repeat dose); IV bolus — 5–10 min
medications.metabolism: Rapidly metabolized by COMT (catechol-O-methyltransferase) and MAO (monoamine oxidase) in liver, kidneys, and other tissues to metanephrine and vanillylmandelic acid (VMA)
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; ECG (arrhythmia risk); respiratory status; clinical response; repeat dosing need
medications.maxDose: ANAPHYLAXIS: 0.3–0.5 mg IM (1:1,000 = 1 mg/mL) q5-15 min PRN (anterolateral thigh); Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg IM (max 0.3 mg per dose); Autoinjectors: ≥30 kg: 0.3 mg; 15–30 kg: 0.15 mg. CARDIAC ARREST (ACLS): 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000 = 0.1 mg/mL) q3-5 min. ANAPHYLAXIS IV (refractory): 0.1 mg IV slowly (dilute to 1:100,000) or infusion 1–10 mcg/min. CROUP: 0.5 mL/kg racemic epinephrine (max 5 mL) nebulized
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment; use in emergencies regardless of comorbidities
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use in anaphylaxis regardless — maternal death is greater risk; may decrease uterine blood flow
medications.sideEffects: Tachycardia, palpitations, anxiety/tremor, headache, hypertension, nausea; arrhythmias (ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation — especially IV); myocardial ischemia (especially in elderly/CAD); tissue necrosis (extravasation of IV; digital ischemia — rare with IM autoinjector); hyperglycemia; hypokalemia (beta-2 mediated)
medications.contraindications: NO absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis — epinephrine should ALWAYS be given for anaphylaxis regardless of age, comorbidities, or medications. Relative: severe hypertension, tachyarrhythmias, CAD (but still give for anaphylaxis). Beta-blocker use may reduce epinephrine effectiveness → may need glucagon (1–5 mg IV) as adjunct. IM route preferred over SC (faster, more reliable absorption). NEVER give IV push for anaphylaxis unless cardiac arrest or refractory shock with monitoring.

medications.brandNames: Benadryl (OTC), various generics; injectable formulation available
medications.mechanism: Competitive antagonist (inverse agonist) at H1 histamine receptors; also significant anticholinergic (muscarinic) activity → drying effects, sedation; also blocks sodium channels (local anesthetic effect); crosses blood-brain barrier readily (lipophilic, non-zwitterionic) → CNS effects (sedation, antiemetic, anti-motion sickness); also has serotonin reuptake inhibition at high concentrations
medications.halfLife: ~4–8 hrs (adults); ~9–15 hrs in elderly and liver disease
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary); extensive first-pass metabolism; oral bioavailability ~40–60%; metabolized to nordiphenhydramine and dinordiphenhydramine
medications.excretion: Renal (as metabolites; little unchanged drug within 24 hrs)
medications.monitoring: Sedation level; anticholinergic effects (especially elderly — delirium risk); urinary retention
medications.maxDose: PO: 300 mg/day (25–50 mg q4-6h); IV/IM: 400 mg/day (10–50 mg per dose; IV rate ≤25 mg/min); Sleep aid: 50 mg at bedtime; Pediatric: 5 mg/kg/day or 150 mg/m²/day (divided q6-8h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; hepatic impairment: use with caution (hepatic metabolism); elderly: AVOID if possible (Beers Criteria — high anticholinergic burden → delirium, cognitive impairment, falls, urinary retention, constipation)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; generally considered safe; avoid near delivery (neonatal effects)
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/drowsiness (MOST COMMON — desired for sleep aid, undesired otherwise), anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, tachycardia), dizziness, impaired coordination, thickened bronchial secretions, paradoxical excitation (children), cognitive impairment (especially elderly); OVERDOSE: anticholinergic toxidrome (hyperthermia, tachycardia, mydriasis, delirium, seizures, urinary retention — "hot as a hare, blind as a bat, dry as a bone, red as a beet, mad as a hatter"); QT prolongation and torsades (massive overdose); rhabdomyolysis
medications.contraindications: Neonates/premature infants; nursing mothers; use as local anesthetic (injectable); hypersensitivity. Beers Criteria: AVOID in elderly (strongly anticholinergic — increased risk of delirium, falls, cognitive decline). Adjunct in anaphylaxis (NOT first-line — epinephrine is first-line; diphenhydramine is second-line for urticaria/pruritus). Useful for acute dystonia (50 mg IV/IM) and drug-induced EPS.

medications.drugClass: Second-generation H1 antihistamine — piperazine derivative (active metabolite of hydroxyzine)
medications.mechanism: Selective peripheral H1 receptor inverse agonist; zwitterionic structure → LOW CNS penetration (does not readily cross BBB — less sedating than first-generation antihistamines); does NOT have significant anticholinergic, antiserotonergic, or anti-alpha-adrenergic activity; also has anti-inflammatory properties (inhibits eosinophil chemotaxis, reduces ICAM-1 expression)
medications.onset: PO — 20 min (50% of subjects); 1 hr (95% of subjects); peak plasma ~1 hr
medications.halfLife: ~8.3 hrs (adults); ~6.2 hrs (children 7–12 yrs); ~12.5 hrs (elderly — decreased renal clearance)
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism — limited oxidative O-dealkylation to inactive metabolite; specific CYP enzyme NOT identified; negligible CYP450 interaction (major advantage — no significant drug interactions); ~50% excreted unchanged in urine
medications.excretion: Renal (~70%); fecal (~10%); ~50% as unchanged parent drug
medications.monitoring: Minimal monitoring needed; sedation (more sedating than loratadine/fexofenadine among 2nd-gen antihistamines)
medications.maxDose: Adults/children ≥12 yrs: 10 mg/day; Children 6–11 yrs: 5–10 mg/day; Children 2–5 yrs: 2.5–5 mg/day; Children 6 mo–2 yrs: 2.5 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 11–31: 5 mg/day; Hemodialysis: 5 mg/day (not significantly removed by dialysis); Hepatic impairment: 5 mg/day; Elderly: 5 mg/day (decreased clearance)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Somnolence/drowsiness (most common — 14% at 10 mg; MORE sedating than loratadine or fexofenadine but LESS than first-generation antihistamines; dose-dependent), headache, dry mouth, fatigue, pharyngitis; minimal anticholinergic effects; no cardiotoxicity (no QT prolongation — unlike terfenadine/astemizole); no significant weight gain
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cetirizine, hydroxyzine, or levocetirizine; severe renal impairment (CrCl 10) without dose adjustment. Most sedating of the commonly used 2nd-generation antihistamines (cetirizine > loratadine > fexofenadine for sedation). Levocetirizine (Xyzal) is the active R-enantiomer — 2× more potent, dosed at 5 mg/day. Advantage over diphenhydramine: once-daily dosing, less sedation, no anticholinergic effects, longer duration.

medications.mechanism: Structural analogue of hypoxanthine; inhibits xanthine oxidase (XO) — the enzyme that converts hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid; both allopurinol and its active metabolite oxypurinol inhibit XO; reduces uric acid PRODUCTION (uricostatic — does NOT increase uric acid excretion); lowers serum and urinary uric acid levels
medications.onset: PO — uric acid reduction begins within 24–48 hrs; peak uric acid lowering 1–3 weeks; full clinical benefit (gout flare reduction) may take 3–6 months of sustained therapy
medications.halfLife: Allopurinol ~1–2 hrs; Oxypurinol (active metabolite) ~15–28 hrs (prolonged in renal impairment — up to 40+ hrs)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via xanthine oxidase to oxypurinol (active — responsible for most of the pharmacologic effect); allopurinol is a substrate AND inhibitor of XO
medications.excretion: Renal (~80% as oxypurinol; ~10% as allopurinol unchanged); oxypurinol clearance is proportional to GFR
medications.monitoring: Serum uric acid (target 6.0 mg/dL; 5.0 mg/dL for tophaceous gout); renal function (dose adjustment); LFTs; CBC; HLA-B58:01 genotyping BEFORE initiation (in high-risk populations — Southeast Asian, African American, Korean); signs of hypersensitivity (rash, fever, eosinophilia)
medications.maxDose: 800 mg/day (FDA-approved); most patients achieve target at 300–600 mg/day; start LOW: 100 mg/day (normal renal function) or 50 mg/day (eGFR 60); titrate by 100 mg increments q2-5 weeks (or 50 mg increments if eGFR 60) to serum urate target
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Traditional dose capping by CrCl is NO LONGER recommended by ACR 2020 guidelines — dose should be titrated to serum urate target regardless of renal function (start low, go slow); however, starting dose should be reduced: eGFR 60: start 50 mg/day; oxypurinol accumulates in renal impairment → monitor closely; hepatic impairment: use with caution
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; limited data
medications.sideEffects: Rash (1–2% — maculopapular; requires cessation); ALLOPURINOL HYPERSENSITIVITY SYNDROME (AHS — rare but potentially fatal: SJS/TEN, DRESS, hepatitis, AKI, eosinophilia; mortality 20–25%; risk factors: HLA-B58:01, CKD, diuretic use, high starting dose); gout flare exacerbation (during initiation — urate mobilization; use prophylactic colchicine or NSAID for 3–6 months); GI upset; elevated LFTs; bone marrow suppression (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; HLA-B58:01 positive (use febuxostat instead). DRUG INTERACTIONS: azathioprine and 6-mercaptopurine (allopurinol inhibits their metabolism via XO → dramatically increased levels → severe myelosuppression; reduce azathioprine/6-MP dose by 67–75% or AVOID combination); ampicillin/amoxicillin (increased rash risk). First-line urate-lowering therapy for gout. Treat-to-target approach: start low, titrate to serum urate 6.0 mg/dL. Can be started DURING a gout flare (with anti-inflammatory prophylaxis).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding PBPs; bactericidal; broadest-spectrum beta-lactam — covers gram-positives (not MRSA), gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), and anaerobes; stable against most beta-lactamases including ESBLs and AmpC
medications.metabolism: Minimal; one microbiologically inactive metabolite
medications.excretion: Renal (~70% unchanged via filtration and tubular secretion); ~28% as inactive metabolite
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC; signs of seizure (lower seizure risk than imipenem)
medications.maxDose: 6 g/day (2 g q8h for meningitis); 3 g/day (1 g q8h for standard indications)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 26–50: recommended dose q12h; CrCl 10–25: half dose q12h; CrCl 10: half dose q24h; hemodialyzable — give dose after dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, rash, headache, C. difficile, seizures (lower risk than imipenem), elevated LFTs
medications.contraindications: Carbapenem/beta-lactam hypersensitivity; anaphylaxis to beta-lactams. Does NOT require cilastatin (unlike imipenem). Lower seizure threshold than imipenem — preferred carbapenem for CNS infections.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; broad spectrum against gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes; stable against ESBLs and AmpC beta-lactamases; does NOT cover Pseudomonas or Acinetobacter (key difference from meropenem/imipenem)
medications.metabolism: Minimal; hydrolyzed to ring-opened metabolite
medications.monitoring: Renal function; signs of seizure
medications.maxDose: 1 g/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥31: no adjustment; CrCl ≤30 or hemodialysis: 500 mg daily; give supplemental 150 mg dose after hemodialysis if given within 6 hrs before dialysis; hepatic impairment: no data but minimal hepatic metabolism
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, headache, phlebitis, C. difficile, seizures (rare)
medications.contraindications: Carbapenem/beta-lactam hypersensitivity; IM formulation contains lidocaine — contraindicated in lidocaine allergy. Key advantage: once-daily dosing; IM option for outpatient parenteral therapy.

medications.mechanism: Imipenem inhibits cell wall synthesis; broadest-spectrum beta-lactam — covers gram-positives (not MRSA), gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), and anaerobes. Cilastatin inhibits renal dehydropeptidase-I, preventing renal metabolism of imipenem and increasing urinary recovery
medications.metabolism: Imipenem metabolized by renal dehydropeptidase (blocked by cilastatin); cilastatin metabolized renally
medications.excretion: Renal (~70% imipenem, ~75% cilastatin unchanged when co-administered)
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC; seizure monitoring (HIGHEST seizure risk among carbapenems — especially CrCl 30)
medications.maxDose: 4 g/day (1 g q6h); reduce for renal impairment
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Extensive dose adjustments required — CrCl 41–70: reduce dose/extend interval; CrCl 21–40: further reduce; CrCl 6–20: 250 mg q12h; CrCl ≤5 (not on HD): do not use unless HD initiated within 48 hrs; hemodialyzable — give dose after dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use only if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Seizures (highest among carbapenems — 1.5% overall, higher with renal impairment/CNS disease), nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, C. difficile, rash, phlebitis
medications.contraindications: Carbapenem/beta-lactam hypersensitivity; severe CNS disorders with seizure risk (relative). Avoid in meningitis (use meropenem instead).

medications.mechanism: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis; bacteriostatic (bactericidal at high concentrations against some organisms); concentrates intracellularly — excellent for atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella)
medications.durationOfAction: Prolonged tissue activity (days after last dose due to tissue accumulation)
medications.halfLife: 68 hrs (terminal — due to extensive tissue uptake and slow release)
medications.metabolism: Minimal; not significantly metabolized by CYP450 (weak CYP3A4 inhibitor — less drug interaction risk than erythromycin/clarithromycin)
medications.excretion: Biliary (primary route — predominantly unchanged); renal (~6% unchanged in urine)
medications.monitoring: QTc interval (risk of QT prolongation/torsades); hepatic function; hearing (rare ototoxicity at high doses)
medications.maxDose: 500 mg/day PO (standard); 2 g single dose (gonorrhea); 600 mg/day (MAC)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment (GFR 10–80: minimal PK change); caution in ESRD (61% increase in Cmax); caution in hepatic impairment (not studied); avoid in severe hepatic disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible with pregnancy and lactation
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, QT prolongation, hepatotoxicity (rare cholestatic hepatitis), ototoxicity (reversible, high doses), C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Macrolide hypersensitivity; history of cholestatic jaundice/hepatic dysfunction with prior azithromycin use; concomitant use with drugs that prolong QT.

medications.mechanism: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis; bacteriostatic; active metabolite (14-OH clarithromycin) has synergistic activity against H. influenzae
medications.halfLife: 3–4 hrs (parent); 5–6 hrs (active metabolite); increases with renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (STRONG CYP3A4 inhibitor — significant drug interactions); active metabolite 14-OH clarithromycin
medications.monitoring: Hepatic function; QTc interval; drug interactions (CYP3A4); renal function
medications.maxDose: 1 g/day (500 mg BID)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30: reduce dose by 50% (250 mg BID or 500 mg daily); no adjustment for hepatic impairment if renal function normal
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; avoid in pregnancy (animal teratogenicity); avoid in lactation
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, metallic taste (dysgeusia), headache, QT prolongation, hepatotoxicity, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Macrolide hypersensitivity; concomitant use with colchicine in renal/hepatic impairment; concomitant cisapride, pimozide, ergotamine (CYP3A4 interactions); history of QT prolongation. Key use: H. pylori triple therapy, MAC prophylaxis/treatment.

medications.mechanism: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis; bacteriostatic; also a motilin receptor agonist (prokinetic effect)
medications.onset: PO — 1–4 hrs (variable by formulation); IV — immediate
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (STRONG CYP3A4 inhibitor — many drug interactions); N-demethylation
medications.monitoring: QTc interval; hepatic function; drug interactions (CYP3A4); hearing (high-dose IV)
medications.maxDose: 4 g/day PO; 4 g/day IV
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment (hepatically metabolized); NOT removed by hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible with pregnancy; excreted in breast milk
medications.sideEffects: GI upset (most common — nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps — due to motilin agonism), QT prolongation (especially IV), cholestatic hepatitis (estolate salt), ototoxicity (high-dose IV), phlebitis (IV), C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Macrolide hypersensitivity; concomitant terfenadine, astemizole, cisapride, pimozide, ergotamine; history of cholestatic jaundice with prior erythromycin. Note: prokinetic use at low doses (250 mg IV q6h or 50–100 mg PO) for gastroparesis.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV (type II topoisomerases), preventing DNA replication, transcription, repair, and recombination; bactericidal; concentration-dependent killing
medications.halfLife: 4 hrs (normal renal function); prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (~15%); partially via CYP1A2 (moderate CYP1A2 inhibitor — increases theophylline, tizanidine, caffeine levels)
medications.monitoring: Renal function; tendon symptoms; QTc; blood glucose (hypo/hyperglycemia); signs of peripheral neuropathy; C. difficile
medications.maxDose: 1500 mg/day PO (750 mg BID); 1200 mg/day IV (400 mg q8h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–50: 250–500 mg q12h; CrCl 5–29: 250–500 mg q18h; hemodialysis/CAPD: 250–500 mg q24h (give after dialysis); no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Generally avoided (animal arthropathy); use only if benefit outweighs risk; excreted in breast milk
medications.sideEffects: GI upset, headache, dizziness, tendinitis/tendon rupture (FDA boxed warning), peripheral neuropathy, QT prolongation, C. difficile, photosensitivity, aortic aneurysm/dissection, hypo/hyperglycemia, CNS effects (seizures, psychosis)
medications.contraindications: Fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity; concomitant tizanidine. FDA BOXED WARNING: tendinitis/rupture, peripheral neuropathy, CNS effects — reserve for infections without alternative treatment options.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV; bactericidal; broader gram-positive coverage than ciprofloxacin (S. pneumoniae — "respiratory fluoroquinolone"); also covers atypicals and gram-negatives
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism (5%); not significantly affected by CYP450
medications.excretion: Renal (~87% unchanged); NOT removed by hemodialysis or CAPD
medications.monitoring: Renal function; tendon symptoms; QTc; blood glucose; peripheral neuropathy; C. difficile
medications.maxDose: 750 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 20–49: 750 mg q48h or 500 mg initial then 250 mg q24h (dose-dependent); CrCl 10–19: 750 mg initial then 500 mg q48h or 500 mg initial then 250 mg q48h; hemodialysis/CAPD: supplemental doses not required; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Generally avoided; use only if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Same FDA boxed warnings as ciprofloxacin — tendinitis/rupture, peripheral neuropathy, CNS effects; GI upset, headache, insomnia, QT prolongation, C. difficile, photosensitivity, aortic aneurysm/dissection
medications.contraindications: Fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity. Key advantage over ciprofloxacin: better pneumococcal coverage; once-daily dosing.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV; bactericidal; broadest gram-positive coverage among fluoroquinolones (excellent S. pneumoniae); also covers atypicals and anaerobes
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via glucuronide and sulfate conjugation (NOT CYP450-dependent — fewer drug interactions)
medications.excretion: Fecal (~25% unchanged); renal (~20% unchanged); glucuronide/sulfate metabolites in urine and feces
medications.monitoring: QTc interval (HIGHEST QT prolongation risk among fluoroquinolones); hepatic function; tendon symptoms; blood glucose
medications.maxDose: 400 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: NO dose adjustment for renal impairment (primarily non-renal elimination); caution in hepatic impairment (limited data); NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Generally avoided
medications.sideEffects: Same FDA boxed warnings; QT prolongation (greatest risk among FQs), nausea, diarrhea, dizziness, hepatotoxicity (rare fulminant), C. difficile
