medications.contraindications: Fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity; known QT prolongation; concomitant Class IA/III antiarrhythmics. Key differences: covers anaerobes (unlike cipro/levo); NO renal dose adjustment needed; NOT effective for UTI (low urinary concentration).

medications.mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis by blocking aminoacyl-tRNA binding; bacteriostatic; broad spectrum — gram-positives, gram-negatives, atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Rickettsia), spirochetes, some protozoa
medications.metabolism: Partially inactivated in GI tract by chelation; NOT significantly metabolized hepatically
medications.excretion: Renal (~40% in 72 hrs with normal function; as low as 1–5% in severe renal failure); fecal (via bile and direct intestinal secretion)
medications.monitoring: Signs of photosensitivity; esophageal irritation; intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri with concurrent isotretinoin)
medications.maxDose: 200 mg/day (100 mg BID); 300 mg/day for severe infections
medications.renalHepaticDosing: NO dose adjustment for renal impairment (unique among tetracyclines — serum half-life unchanged in renal failure); NOT removed by hemodialysis; caution in hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester) and children 8 years (permanent tooth discoloration, enamel hypoplasia); excreted in breast milk
medications.sideEffects: Photosensitivity, GI upset (nausea, esophagitis — take upright with water), C. difficile, vaginal candidiasis, tooth discoloration (children), pseudotumor cerebri (rare)
medications.contraindications: Tetracycline hypersensitivity; pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester); children 8 years (except for life-threatening infections). Take with food (reduces GI upset without significantly affecting absorption — unlike other tetracyclines).

medications.mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis; bacteriostatic; most lipophilic tetracycline — excellent CNS and tissue penetration; active against some MRSA strains
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (partial); more extensively metabolized than doxycycline
medications.monitoring: Vestibular symptoms (dizziness, vertigo — unique to minocycline); hepatic function; signs of autoimmune reactions (drug-induced lupus); skin pigmentation
medications.maxDose: 200 mg/day (100 mg BID)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment for renal impairment; max 200 mg/day regardless; caution in hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy and children 8 years (same as doxycycline)
medications.sideEffects: Vestibular toxicity (dizziness, vertigo — most common among tetracyclines), blue-gray skin/mucosal pigmentation (prolonged use), photosensitivity (less than doxycycline), GI upset, drug-induced lupus (rare), pseudotumor cerebri
medications.contraindications: Tetracycline hypersensitivity; pregnancy; children 8 years. Key differences from doxycycline: more vestibular side effects; skin pigmentation; better MRSA activity; less photosensitivity.

medications.mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit irreversibly, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis; bactericidal; concentration-dependent killing with prolonged post-antibiotic effect; covers aerobic gram-negatives (Enterobacteriaceae, Pseudomonas); synergistic with beta-lactams against gram-positives (enterococci, staphylococci)
medications.halfLife: 2–3 hrs (normal renal function); markedly prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.monitoring: Serum levels (peak and trough for conventional dosing; trough or random level for extended-interval); renal function (BUN, creatinine); audiometry for prolonged courses; vestibular function
medications.maxDose: 7 mg/kg/day (extended-interval); 5 mg/kg/day (conventional divided q8h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Dose interval = serum creatinine × 8 (hrs) for conventional dosing; extended-interval: CrCl 40–59: q36h; CrCl 20–39: q48h; CrCl 20: use conventional dosing with levels; hemodialysis: redose based on levels post-dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; AVOID — crosses placenta; risk of fetal ototoxicity
medications.sideEffects: Nephrotoxicity (usually reversible), ototoxicity (vestibular > auditory; may be irreversible), neuromuscular blockade (rare — avoid in myasthenia gravis)
medications.contraindications: Aminoglycoside hypersensitivity; myasthenia gravis (relative). Target trough 1 mg/L (extended-interval) or 2 mg/L (conventional).

medications.mechanism: Same as gentamicin — binds 30S ribosomal subunit; bactericidal; concentration-dependent killing; slightly more active against Pseudomonas aeruginosa than gentamicin; slightly less nephrotoxic than gentamicin
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.monitoring: Same as gentamicin — serum levels, renal function, audiometry
medications.maxDose: 5 mg/kg/day (conventional); 7 mg/kg/day (extended-interval); inhaled: 300 mg BID (cystic fibrosis)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same adjustments as gentamicin; dose based on levels; hemodialyzable
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; AVOID
medications.sideEffects: Nephrotoxicity (slightly less than gentamicin), ototoxicity (vestibular and auditory), neuromuscular blockade
medications.contraindications: Aminoglycoside hypersensitivity. Key use: Pseudomonas infections; inhaled tobramycin for cystic fibrosis.

medications.mechanism: Same as gentamicin — binds 30S ribosomal subunit; bactericidal; resistant to most aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes — broadest spectrum among aminoglycosides; active against many gentamicin/tobramycin-resistant organisms
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.monitoring: Serum levels (peak 20–35 mcg/mL; trough 5 mcg/mL for conventional; trough 4 mcg/mL for extended-interval); renal function; audiometry
medications.maxDose: 15 mg/kg/day (conventional or extended-interval)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Adjust interval based on renal function and levels; hemodialyzable — redose after dialysis based on levels
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; AVOID
medications.sideEffects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (auditory > vestibular — opposite of gentamicin), neuromuscular blockade
medications.contraindications: Aminoglycoside hypersensitivity. Key advantage: active against many gentamicin-resistant gram-negatives; used for MDR TB.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis by binding D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, preventing cross-linking; bactericidal (slowly); active against gram-positives only — MRSA, MSSA, streptococci, enterococci (E. faecalis; E. faecium may be VRE), C. difficile (oral only)
medications.onset: IV — infusion over ≥60 min; oral — not absorbed systemically (local GI effect only)
medications.halfLife: 4–6 hrs (normal renal function); up to 7–10 days in anuria
medications.metabolism: Not significantly metabolized
medications.excretion: Renal (~75% unchanged via glomerular filtration); oral vancomycin excreted in feces
medications.monitoring: AUC/MIC-guided dosing (target AUC₂₄ 400–600 mg·h/L per 2020 ASHP/IDSA/SIDP guidelines — replaces trough-only monitoring); renal function (BUN, creatinine); CBC; audiometry for prolonged courses
medications.maxDose: 2 g/dose; typically 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h (actual body weight); loading dose 25–30 mg/kg for serious infections
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Daily dose (mg) ≈ 15 × GFR (mL/min); CrCl 100: ~1545 mg/day; CrCl 50: ~770 mg/day; CrCl 10: ~155 mg/day; anuria: 1 g q7–10 days; hemodialyzable — redose based on levels post-dialysis; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C (IV); B (oral); use with caution — potential ototoxicity to fetus
medications.sideEffects: Red man syndrome (histamine-mediated — slow infusion rate), nephrotoxicity (AKI — risk increases with higher AUC), ototoxicity (usually with concurrent aminoglycosides), thrombocytopenia, phlebitis, C. difficile (IV use)
medications.contraindications: Vancomycin hypersensitivity. Oral vancomycin NOT absorbed — only for C. difficile colitis. IV vancomycin NOT effective for C. difficile.

medications.mechanism: Binds 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit at a unique site, preventing formation of the 70S initiation complex; bacteriostatic against enterococci and staphylococci; bactericidal against streptococci; active against MRSA, VRE, penicillin-resistant pneumococci
medications.onset: PO/IV — rapid; 100% oral bioavailability (IV-to-PO switch seamless)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic oxidation to two inactive metabolites (PNU-142586 and PNU-142300); NOT CYP450-dependent
medications.excretion: Renal (~30% unchanged; ~50% as metabolites); metabolites accumulate in renal impairment
medications.monitoring: CBC weekly (thrombocytopenia — most common dose-limiting toxicity, especially after >2 weeks or in renal impairment); lactic acid (lactic acidosis with prolonged use); visual acuity (optic neuropathy); serotonergic drug interactions
medications.maxDose: 1200 mg/day (600 mg q12h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No FDA-recommended dose adjustment for renal impairment; however, metabolites accumulate and thrombocytopenia risk increases — consider dose reduction (e.g., 600 mg daily or 300 mg q12h) in CrCl 30 with TDM; give after hemodialysis (~30% removed); no hepatic adjustment (mild-moderate)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use only if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Thrombocytopenia (most common — dose/duration-dependent), anemia, lactic acidosis (prolonged use), peripheral/optic neuropathy (prolonged use >28 days), serotonin syndrome (weak reversible MAO inhibitor — avoid with SSRIs, SNRIs, meperidine, serotonergic agents), nausea, diarrhea, headache
medications.contraindications: Concomitant MAOIs or within 2 weeks of MAOI use; uncontrolled hypertension/pheochromocytoma/carcinoid (MAO inhibition). Limit duration to ≤28 days when possible. Avoid tyramine-rich foods (MAO inhibition).

medications.mechanism: Calcium-dependent binding to bacterial cell membrane, causing rapid depolarization and loss of membrane potential; bactericidal (concentration-dependent); active against gram-positives only — MRSA, VRE, streptococci; NOT active against gram-negatives
medications.halfLife: ~8 hrs (normal renal function); prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Minimal; not CYP450-dependent
medications.monitoring: CPK weekly (rhabdomyolysis risk); renal function; signs of eosinophilic pneumonia
medications.maxDose: 6 mg/kg/day (bacteremia)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥30: no adjustment; CrCl 30 (including HD/CAPD): 4 mg/kg or 6 mg/kg q48h; give after hemodialysis on dialysis days; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; use only if clearly needed
medications.sideEffects: CPK elevation/rhabdomyolysis (monitor weekly), eosinophilic pneumonia (discontinue immediately), peripheral neuropathy, diarrhea, injection site reactions
medications.contraindications: Daptomycin hypersensitivity. Do NOT use for pneumonia — inactivated by pulmonary surfactant. Avoid concurrent statins if possible (additive myopathy risk).

medications.mechanism: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis; bacteriostatic (bactericidal at high concentrations); covers gram-positives (MSSA, streptococci, some community-acquired MRSA) and anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium except C. difficile); also inhibits toxin production (useful in necrotizing fasciitis, toxic shock)
medications.halfLife: 2.4 hrs (PO); ~3 hrs (IV); increased slightly in renal/hepatic impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primarily) to clindamycin sulfoxide (active) and N-desmethylclindamycin
medications.excretion: Renal (~10% bioactive); fecal (~3.6%); remainder as inactive metabolites
medications.monitoring: Signs of C. difficile colitis (most important); hepatic function with prolonged use
medications.maxDose: PO: 450 mg q6h (1.8 g/day); IV: 600–900 mg q8h (up to 4.8 g/day for life-threatening infections)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: NO dose adjustment for renal impairment; NOT removed by hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis; no specific hepatic adjustment but monitor in severe liver disease (prolonged half-life)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible; used for GBS prophylaxis in penicillin-allergic patients
medications.sideEffects: C. difficile colitis (FDA boxed warning — most serious), diarrhea (up to 20%), nausea, rash, elevated LFTs, esophagitis (take with water, upright)
medications.contraindications: Clindamycin/lincomycin hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNING: C. difficile-associated diarrhea — reserve for infections where less toxic alternatives are inappropriate.

medications.drugClass: Folate antagonist combination (dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor + sulfonamide)
medications.mechanism: Sequential blockade of folate synthesis — sulfamethoxazole inhibits dihydropteroate synthase; trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase; synergistic bactericidal effect; broad spectrum against gram-positives (MRSA, S. pneumoniae), gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus), and Pneumocystis jirovecii
medications.halfLife: TMP 8–10 hrs; SMX ~10 hrs; both prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: SMX hepatic acetylation and glucuronidation; TMP hepatic metabolism (~20%); N-acetyl SMX metabolite accumulates in renal impairment
medications.excretion: Renal (TMP ~50–60% unchanged; SMX ~20% unchanged; higher as metabolites); urine concentrations much higher than serum
medications.monitoring: CBC (megaloblastic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia); renal function; potassium (hyperkalemia — TMP blocks ENaC); hepatic function; rash (SJS/TEN risk)
medications.maxDose: DS (160/800 mg) q12h for standard infections; PJP treatment: 15–20 mg TMP/kg/day divided q6–8h
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl >30: standard dose; CrCl 15–30: half the usual dose; CrCl 15: NOT recommended; hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D (near term); avoid in 1st trimester (neural tube defects) and near term (kernicterus); avoid in G6PD deficiency (hemolytic anemia)
medications.sideEffects: GI upset, rash (including SJS/TEN — especially in HIV), hyperkalemia, megaloblastic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity, photosensitivity, crystalluria, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Sulfonamide hypersensitivity; megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency; pregnancy at term; infants 2 months; severe hepatic/renal impairment (CrCl 15); G6PD deficiency (relative).

medications.mechanism: Enters anaerobic/microaerophilic organisms where nitro group is reduced to cytotoxic intermediates that damage DNA and inhibit nucleic acid synthesis; bactericidal; active against anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium, Fusobacterium) and protozoa (Giardia, Entamoeba, Trichomonas); NO aerobic activity
medications.halfLife: ~8 hrs (range 6–14 hrs); increased to ~18 hrs in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic oxidation and glucuronidation; hydroxy-metronidazole (active metabolite)
medications.monitoring: CBC with prolonged use; neurologic symptoms (peripheral neuropathy); hepatic function
medications.maxDose: 4 g/day (500 mg q6h or 7.5 mg/kg q6h)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment or ESRD/CAPD; hemodialysis removes significant amounts — supplement after dialysis; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): reduce dose by 50%; mild-moderate hepatic impairment: no adjustment but monitor
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; historically avoided in 1st trimester but no proven teratogenicity; compatible with lactation (AAP)
medications.sideEffects: Metallic taste (most common), nausea, diarrhea, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use — may be irreversible), seizures (high doses), disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol, dark urine, C. difficile (paradoxically)
medications.contraindications: Nitroimidazole hypersensitivity; concurrent alcohol (disulfiram reaction) or disulfiram use (psychotic reactions); 1st trimester pregnancy (relative). Avoid prolonged courses >4 weeks (neuropathy risk).

medications.mechanism: Reduced by bacterial flavoproteins to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, RNA, proteins, and cell wall synthesis; bactericidal in urine; multiple mechanisms make resistance uncommon; active against E. coli, Enterococcus, S. saprophyticus, Klebsiella; NOT active against Proteus, Pseudomonas, Serratia
medications.onset: PO — rapidly absorbed; peak urine levels in 30 min
medications.halfLife: 20–60 min (very short — rapidly excreted in urine)
medications.metabolism: ~60% metabolized in tissues; remainder excreted unchanged in urine
medications.excretion: Renal (~40% unchanged in urine); achieves therapeutic concentrations ONLY in urine — NOT effective for systemic infections
medications.monitoring: Pulmonary function (chronic use — pulmonary fibrosis); hepatic function; CBC; renal function
medications.maxDose: 100 mg q6h (macrocrystals); 100 mg q12h (Macrobid); prophylaxis: 50–100 mg at bedtime
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 60: CONTRAINDICATED (inadequate urine drug levels + increased toxicity risk); NOT effective in renal impairment; dialyzable
medications.pregnancyLactation: Compatible in 2nd/3rd trimester; CONTRAINDICATED at term (38–42 weeks) and during labor (neonatal hemolytic anemia); avoid in 1st trimester if alternatives available
medications.sideEffects: GI upset (less with macrocrystals/Macrobid), pulmonary toxicity (acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis or chronic pulmonary fibrosis — especially with prolonged use in elderly), hepatotoxicity (cholestatic/chronic active hepatitis), peripheral neuropathy, hemolytic anemia (G6PD deficiency)
medications.contraindications: CrCl 60 mL/min; anuria/oliguria; pregnancy at term; neonates 1 month; G6PD deficiency; history of cholestatic jaundice/hepatic dysfunction with prior nitrofurantoin. Use ONLY for lower UTI (cystitis) — NOT for pyelonephritis (no tissue penetration).

medications.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits MurA (phosphoenolpyruvate transferase), blocking the first committed step of peptidoglycan cell wall synthesis; bactericidal; broad spectrum against gram-positives and gram-negatives including ESBL-producing E. coli and VRE; unique mechanism — no cross-resistance with other antibiotic classes
medications.onset: PO — 2–4 hrs (peak serum); urine concentrations maintained ≥26 hrs
medications.durationOfAction: Oral — urinary concentrations maintained >100 mcg/mL for ~26 hrs after single dose
medications.halfLife: PO: 5.7 hrs (±2.8); IV: 2.8 hrs; markedly prolonged in renal impairment (up to 50 hrs in anuria)
medications.metabolism: Not metabolized
medications.excretion: Renal (~38–80% unchanged in urine depending on formulation); oral bioavailability ~37%
medications.monitoring: Renal function (IV formulation); signs of C. difficile
medications.maxDose: Oral: 3 g single dose (uncomplicated UTI); IV: 6 g q8h (cUTI)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Oral (Monurol): no specific adjustment but efficacy decreases with lower CrCl; IV (Contepo): CrCl >50: 6 g q8h; CrCl 26–50: 4 g q8h; CrCl 10–25: 2 g q12h; hemodialysis: 2 g after each session
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; limited data but generally considered safe
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea (most common), nausea, headache, vaginitis, C. difficile
medications.contraindications: Fosfomycin hypersensitivity. Oral: single-dose convenience for uncomplicated UTI; do NOT use multiple oral doses for systemic infections (poor bioavailability). Sachet must be dissolved in water (not hot).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, blocking RNA transcription; bactericidal; active against M. tuberculosis, many gram-positives (MSSA, streptococci), and some gram-negatives; also used for meningococcal prophylaxis and prosthetic joint infections (biofilm penetration)
medications.halfLife: 3.35 hrs initially; decreases to 2–3 hrs with repeated dosing (autoinduction of metabolism); increases in renal impairment at doses >600 mg
medications.metabolism: Hepatic deacetylation to 25-desacetyl rifampin (active metabolite); undergoes enterohepatic circulation; POTENT inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, P-glycoprotein — causes EXTENSIVE drug interactions
medications.monitoring: LFTs (baseline and monthly during intensive phase); CBC; drug interactions (critical — reduces levels of warfarin, OCP, calcineurin inhibitors, HIV antiretrovirals, azole antifungals, methadone, many others)
medications.maxDose: 600 mg/day (adults); 10–20 mg/kg/day (pediatric, max 600 mg)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment at doses ≤600 mg/day (primarily hepatic/biliary elimination); NOT removed by hemodialysis; AVOID or use with extreme caution in hepatic impairment (hepatotoxic)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; teratogenic in animals at high doses; use if benefit outweighs risk; give vitamin K to mother and neonate (risk of postnatal hemorrhage)
medications.sideEffects: Hepatotoxicity (most serious — monitor LFTs), orange-red discoloration of body fluids (urine, tears, sweat — warn patients; stains contact lenses), GI upset, flu-like syndrome (intermittent dosing), thrombocytopenia, interstitial nephritis (rare), drug interactions (most clinically significant)
medications.contraindications: Rifamycin hypersensitivity; concomitant use with many drugs (check interactions before prescribing). Take on empty stomach (food reduces absorption by 30%). Orange discoloration is harmless but alarming — counsel patients.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug activated by mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase (KatG); inhibits InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase), blocking mycolic acid synthesis in the mycobacterial cell wall; bactericidal against actively growing M. tuberculosis; bacteriostatic against resting bacilli
medications.halfLife: Fast acetylators: 0.5–1.6 hrs; Slow acetylators: 2–5 hrs (NAT2 polymorphism determines acetylation rate)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic acetylation by NAT2 (genetically polymorphic — fast vs slow acetylators); also hydrolysis; metabolites include acetylisoniazid, isonicotinic acid, acetylhydrazine (hepatotoxic)
medications.excretion: Renal (75–95% within 24 hrs as metabolites and unchanged drug)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (baseline and monthly — hepatotoxicity risk increases with age, alcohol use, concurrent hepatotoxic drugs); symptoms of peripheral neuropathy; pyridoxine (B6) supplementation
medications.maxDose: 300 mg/day (daily); 900 mg/dose (intermittent 2–3×/week)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Minimal dose adjustment for renal impairment; give after hemodialysis; AVOID or use with extreme caution in active hepatic disease or history of INH-associated hepatitis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use for active TB (benefit outweighs risk); give pyridoxine 25–50 mg/day; compatible with lactation
medications.sideEffects: Hepatotoxicity (most serious — risk increases with age: ~0.3% age 20–34, ~2.3% age >50; fatal hepatitis reported), peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine 25–50 mg/day — especially in pregnancy, HIV, diabetes, malnutrition, alcoholism, renal failure), SLE-like syndrome, seizures (overdose), optic neuritis, drug interactions (inhibits CYP2C19, CYP3A4 — increases phenytoin, carbamazepine levels)
medications.contraindications: Previous INH-associated hepatitis; acute hepatic disease; hypersensitivity. Always co-prescribe pyridoxine (B6) 25–50 mg/day for at-risk patients.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug converted to pyrazinoic acid by mycobacterial pyrazinamidase (PncA); disrupts membrane energetics and fatty acid synthesis; bactericidal against semi-dormant M. tuberculosis in acidic environments (intracellular, caseous lesions); critical sterilizing activity that allows shortening of TB treatment from 9 to 6 months
medications.metabolism: Hepatic hydrolysis to pyrazinoic acid (active); then hydroxylation to 5-hydroxypyrazinoic acid
medications.monitoring: LFTs (hepatotoxicity); uric acid (hyperuricemia — nearly universal); joint symptoms (gout flares); blood glucose
medications.maxDose: Weight-based: 55 kg: 1000 mg/day; 56–75 kg: 1500 mg/day; 76–90 kg: 2000 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30 or hemodialysis: 25–35 mg/kg 3×/week (post-dialysis); avoid in severe hepatic disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; WHO considers safe in pregnancy; CDC/ATS recommend use when benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Hepatotoxicity (dose-related — most hepatotoxic of first-line TB drugs), hyperuricemia (nearly universal — usually asymptomatic; may precipitate gout), arthralgias, GI upset, rash, photosensitivity
medications.contraindications: Severe hepatic disease; acute gout; hypersensitivity. Used ONLY in first 2 months of TB treatment (intensive phase). Hyperuricemia alone is NOT a reason to stop — only stop if symptomatic gout or hepatotoxicity.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits arabinosyl transferase (EmbB), blocking arabinogalactan synthesis in the mycobacterial cell wall; bacteriostatic; active against M. tuberculosis and M. avium complex
medications.halfLife: 3–4 hrs (normal renal function); prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic oxidation (~15%) to inactive metabolites
medications.monitoring: Visual acuity and color vision (baseline and monthly — optic neuritis is dose-dependent and usually reversible if caught early); renal function; uric acid
medications.maxDose: 15–25 mg/kg/day (max 2.5 g/day for daily dosing); 50 mg/kg for twice-weekly dosing
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 10–50: 15–25 mg/kg q24–36h; CrCl 10: 15–25 mg/kg q48h; hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use if benefit outweighs risk (no proven teratogenicity in humans); compatible with lactation
medications.sideEffects: Optic neuritis (most important — dose-related; retrobulbar neuritis causing decreased visual acuity, red-green color blindness; usually reversible if stopped promptly; risk increases at doses >15 mg/kg/day and with renal impairment), hyperuricemia, peripheral neuropathy (rare), GI upset, rash
medications.contraindications: Known optic neuritis; inability to report visual changes (young children — use with caution in children too young for visual acuity testing); hypersensitivity. Counsel patients to report any visual changes immediately.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits influenza neuraminidase, preventing release of newly formed viral particles from infected cells; active against influenza A and B
medications.metabolism: Prodrug; hepatic ester hydrolysis to oseltamivir carboxylate (active); NOT CYP450-dependent
medications.excretion: Renal (>99% of active metabolite via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion)
medications.monitoring: Renal function; neuropsychiatric symptoms (rare, mainly pediatric)
medications.maxDose: Treatment: 75 mg BID × 5 days; Prophylaxis: 75 mg daily × 7–10 days
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–60: 30 mg BID (treatment) or 30 mg daily (prophylaxis); CrCl 10–30: 30 mg daily (treatment) or 30 mg every other day (prophylaxis); ESRD on HD: 30 mg after each HD session; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; CDC recommends use in pregnant women (benefit outweighs risk); compatible with lactation
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, vomiting (most common — take with food), headache, neuropsychiatric events (rare — delirium, self-injury, mainly in pediatric patients in Japan)
medications.contraindications: Oseltamivir hypersensitivity. Most effective when started within 48 hrs of symptom onset; still recommended in hospitalized patients regardless of timing.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug; selectively phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase (TK) in infected cells → triphosphate form inhibits viral DNA polymerase and incorporates into viral DNA causing chain termination; active against HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV; less active against EBV, CMV
medications.halfLife: 2.5–3.3 hrs (normal renal function); up to 20 hrs in anuria
medications.metabolism: Minimal; ~15% converted to 9-carboxymethoxymethylguanine
medications.excretion: Renal (~62–91% unchanged via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion)
medications.monitoring: Renal function (crystalline nephropathy — ensure adequate hydration); CBC
medications.maxDose: IV: 10–15 mg/kg q8h (HSV encephalitis, neonatal HSV); PO: 800 mg 5×/day (VZV)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Extensive renal dose adjustments required — CrCl 25–50: standard dose q12h (IV); CrCl 10–25: standard dose q24h (IV); CrCl 10: 50% dose q24h (IV); hemodialysis: give dose after each session; PO adjustments also required; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; widely used in pregnancy (suppressive therapy for genital herpes from 36 weeks); compatible with lactation
medications.sideEffects: Crystalline nephropathy/AKI (IV — prevent with adequate hydration and slow infusion), nausea, headache, phlebitis (IV), neurotoxicity (tremor, confusion — especially in renal impairment), thrombocytopenia (rare)
medications.contraindications: Acyclovir/valacyclovir hypersensitivity. IV: infuse over ≥1 hr with adequate hydration to prevent crystalluria.

medications.drugClass: Nucleoside analogue prodrug (L-valyl ester of acyclovir)
medications.mechanism: Rapidly converted to acyclovir after oral absorption; same mechanism as acyclovir (viral TK activation → DNA polymerase inhibition); 3–5× higher oral bioavailability than acyclovir
medications.metabolism: First-pass intestinal/hepatic hydrolysis to acyclovir + L-valine; then acyclovir metabolism as above
medications.monitoring: Renal function; CBC; signs of TTP/HUS (high doses in immunocompromised)
medications.maxDose: 1 g TID (herpes zoster); 1 g BID (genital herpes initial episode)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–49: reduce dose (indication-specific); CrCl 10–29: further reduce; CrCl 10: 500 mg q24h (genital herpes); hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; widely used; compatible with lactation
medications.sideEffects: Same as acyclovir — nausea, headache, nephrotoxicity; TTP/HUS (rare — high doses in immunocompromised, especially transplant patients)
medications.contraindications: Acyclovir/valacyclovir hypersensitivity. Advantage over acyclovir: better bioavailability allows less frequent dosing.

medications.mechanism: Phosphorylated by CMV UL97 kinase → triphosphate inhibits CMV DNA polymerase; active primarily against CMV; also HSV, VZV, HHV-6
medications.halfLife: 2.5–3.6 hrs (normal renal function); up to 28 hrs in severe renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Minimal; not significantly metabolized
medications.excretion: Renal (~91% unchanged via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion)
medications.monitoring: CBC twice weekly (myelosuppression — dose-limiting); renal function; ophthalmologic exams (CMV retinitis)
medications.maxDose: Induction: 5 mg/kg q12h × 14–21 days; Maintenance: 5 mg/kg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 50–69: 2.5 mg/kg q12h (induction); CrCl 25–49: 2.5 mg/kg q24h; CrCl 10–24: 1.25 mg/kg q24h; CrCl 10: 1.25 mg/kg 3×/week after HD; hemodialyzable
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category X (teratogenic, embryotoxic); CONTRAINDICATED; effective contraception required during and 90 days after treatment (males and females)
medications.sideEffects: Neutropenia (most common dose-limiting — 40%), thrombocytopenia, anemia, nephrotoxicity, CNS effects (headache, confusion, seizures), fever
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; ANC 500; platelets 25,000; pregnancy. Avoid concurrent zidovudine (additive myelosuppression). Valganciclovir (Valcyte) is the oral prodrug with better bioavailability.

medications.mechanism: Metabolized intracellularly to active nucleoside triphosphate; inhibits RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) of SARS-CoV-2, causing premature chain termination; active against SARS-CoV-2 and other RNA viruses
medications.metabolism: Intracellular metabolism to active triphosphate; plasma hydrolysis
medications.monitoring: LFTs (baseline and during treatment — hepatotoxicity reported); renal function; eGFR; signs of hypersensitivity
medications.maxDose: 200 mg loading dose Day 1, then 100 mg daily; hospitalized: 5–10 days; non-hospitalized high-risk: 3 days
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for any degree of renal impairment including dialysis (per updated FDA label based on Phase I/III data); no dose adjustment for mild/moderate/severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B/C); monitor LFTs
medications.pregnancyLactation: No adequate data; use if benefit outweighs risk
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, elevated transaminases, hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis (including infusion-related reactions), bradycardia (sinus), rash
medications.contraindications: Clinically significant hypersensitivity to remdesivir. FDA-approved for hospitalized COVID-19 and non-hospitalized high-risk patients.

medications.drugClass: Nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) + nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI)
medications.mechanism: TDF is a prodrug of tenofovir (acyclic nucleotide analogue of adenosine monophosphate); emtricitabine is a cytidine analogue. Both are phosphorylated intracellularly to active diphosphate/triphosphate forms that inhibit HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with natural substrates and causing DNA chain termination; also active against HBV
medications.onset: PO — TDF peak 1 hr; FTC peak 1–2 hrs
medications.halfLife: Tenofovir ~17 hrs; Emtricitabine ~10 hrs; intracellular half-lives much longer (tenofovir-DP ~150 hrs; FTC-TP ~39 hrs)
medications.metabolism: TDF hydrolyzed to tenofovir (not CYP450-dependent); FTC minimally metabolized (3′-sulfoxide diastereomers and glucuronic acid conjugate)
medications.excretion: Both primarily renal — tenofovir ~70–80% unchanged (glomerular filtration + tubular secretion); FTC ~86% unchanged
medications.monitoring: HIV status (confirm negative before PrEP); renal function (creatinine, eGFR — at baseline, 3 months, then q6 months); HBV serology (risk of HBV flare on discontinuation); bone density (long-term use); urinalysis (proximal tubulopathy)
medications.maxDose: 1 tablet (200/300 mg) daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–49: 1 tablet q48h (treatment); NOT recommended for PrEP if CrCl 60; CrCl 30: not recommended; hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis (q7 days for treatment); no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; compatible with pregnancy and lactation; used for PrEP and PMTCT
medications.sideEffects: Nephrotoxicity (proximal renal tubulopathy, Fanconi syndrome — TDF), decreased bone mineral density (TDF), nausea, headache, diarrhea, lactic acidosis/hepatomegaly with steatosis (rare, class effect), HBV flare on discontinuation, skin hyperpigmentation (FTC)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; unknown or positive HIV status (for PrEP — risk of resistance); CrCl 60 for PrEP use. BOXED WARNING: Risk of HBV exacerbation upon discontinuation; risk of HIV resistance if used for PrEP in undiagnosed HIV infection.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450 enzyme lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol; depletes ergosterol in fungal cell membrane, increasing permeability; fungistatic against most Candida species; active against C. albicans, C. parapsilosis, C. tropicalis, Cryptococcus neoformans; limited activity against C. glabrata, C. krusei (intrinsically resistant)
medications.onset: PO — 1–2 hrs; IV — immediate; PO bioavailability >90% (IV-to-PO switch seamless)
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism (~11%); weak CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitor (fewer drug interactions than itraconazole/voriconazole but still clinically significant — increases warfarin, phenytoin, cyclosporine levels)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (hepatotoxicity); renal function; drug interactions (warfarin, phenytoin, cyclosporine, sulfonylureas)
medications.maxDose: 400 mg/day (standard); up to 800–1200 mg/day for cryptococcal meningitis (off-label high-dose)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl >50: no adjustment; CrCl ≤50: 50% of recommended dose; hemodialysis: full dose after each session; no single-dose adjustment needed (e.g., VVC 150 mg); no specific hepatic adjustment but avoid in severe liver disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED for chronic/high-dose use (teratogenic — craniofacial, skeletal, cardiac defects); single 150 mg dose for VVC: risk debated but generally avoided in 1st trimester; excreted in breast milk at concentrations similar to plasma
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, headache, rash, elevated LFTs, hepatotoxicity (rare but serious — cholestasis, hepatic necrosis), QT prolongation (dose-dependent), alopecia (prolonged use), adrenal insufficiency (rare)
medications.contraindications: Azole hypersensitivity; concomitant terfenadine (at doses ≥400 mg/day), cisapride, erythromycin, pimozide, quinidine (QT prolongation risk); chronic/high-dose use in pregnancy.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits fungal CYP51 (lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase), blocking ergosterol synthesis; fungistatic; broad spectrum — Aspergillus, Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Coccidioides, Candida, dermatophytes; active metabolite hydroxy-itraconazole also has antifungal activity
medications.onset: PO — 2–5 hrs (peak); steady state in ~15 days without loading dose
medications.halfLife: ~21 hrs (single dose); ~64 hrs at steady state (saturable metabolism)
medications.metabolism: Extensively hepatic via CYP3A4 to hydroxy-itraconazole (active) and many inactive metabolites; POTENT CYP3A4 inhibitor and P-glycoprotein/BCRP inhibitor — EXTENSIVE drug interactions
medications.excretion: Fecal (~54% — 3–18% unchanged); renal (~35% as inactive metabolites; 1 mcg/mL for serious infections); signs of CHF
medications.maxDose: Sporanox: 400 mg/day; Tolsura: 260 mg/day (different bioavailability — NOT interchangeable)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment (minimally renally excreted); NOT removed by hemodialysis; caution in hepatic impairment (extensively hepatically metabolized); IV formulation (cyclodextrin vehicle) contraindicated in CrCl 30 (cyclodextrin accumulation)
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED (teratogenic in animals); effective contraception required during treatment and for 2 months after; excreted in breast milk — avoid
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, rash, headache, hepatotoxicity (FDA boxed warning — including fatal hepatic failure), CHF/negative inotropic effect (FDA boxed warning — do not use in CHF), hypokalemia, peripheral neuropathy, QT prolongation
medications.contraindications: Azole hypersensitivity; CHF (current or history — negative inotropic effect); pregnancy; concomitant use with many CYP3A4 substrates (methadone, ergot alkaloids, lovastatin, simvastatin, triazolam, oral midazolam, felodipine, nisoldipine, ivabradine, naloxegol, many others). FDA BOXED WARNINGS: (1) CHF — negative inotropic effect; (2) Drug interactions — potentially fatal; (3) Hepatotoxicity. Capsules require gastric acid for absorption — take with food; solution take on empty stomach. Sporanox and Tolsura are NOT interchangeable.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits fungal CYP51 (lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase); fungicidal against Aspergillus (unique among azoles); broadest-spectrum triazole — Aspergillus, Candida (including some fluconazole-resistant), Scedosporium, Fusarium, Cryptococcus; first-line for invasive aspergillosis
medications.onset: PO — 1–2 hrs; IV — immediate; PO bioavailability ~96%
medications.halfLife: ~6 hrs (dose-dependent; nonlinear pharmacokinetics in adults due to saturable metabolism)
medications.metabolism: Extensively hepatic via CYP2C19 (primary), CYP2C9, CYP3A4; major metabolite is N-oxide (72% of circulating metabolites; minimal antifungal activity); CYP2C19 genetic polymorphism causes wide interpatient variability (poor metabolizers have ~4× higher exposure than extensive metabolizers); moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor
medications.excretion: Renal (~80% as metabolites; 1 for efficacy, 5.5 to minimize toxicity); LFTs; visual acuity; CYP2C19 genotype (if available); renal function (IV formulation); drug interactions
medications.maxDose: IV: 6 mg/kg q12h (loading), 4 mg/kg q12h (maintenance); PO: 400 mg q12h (loading), 200–300 mg q12h (maintenance)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: PO: no renal adjustment needed; IV: avoid if CrCl 50 (SBECD accumulation — use oral instead); mild-moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): use standard loading dose, halve maintenance dose; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): use only if benefit outweighs risk
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; CONTRAINDICATED (teratogenic in animals); effective contraception required
medications.sideEffects: Visual disturbances (most common — ~30%; photopsia, color changes, blurred vision; usually transient and reversible), hepatotoxicity (elevated LFTs, hepatitis, hepatic failure), rash/photosensitivity (risk of squamous cell carcinoma with prolonged use — photoprotection essential), QT prolongation, periostitis/fluorosis (prolonged use >6 months), hallucinations, peripheral neuropathy
medications.contraindications: Azole hypersensitivity; concomitant rifampin, carbamazepine, long-acting barbiturates, efavirenz (at standard doses), ritonavir (at standard doses), ergot alkaloids, sirolimus, St. John's wort; pregnancy. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers: consider 50% dose reduction; ultrarapid metabolizers: may need 100–300% dose increase.

medications.mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, forming pores that increase membrane permeability and cause leakage of intracellular contents → cell death; also forms extramembranous aggregates that extract ergosterol from lipid bilayers; fungicidal; broadest-spectrum antifungal — Candida, Aspergillus (except A. terreus), Mucorales, Cryptococcus, Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Coccidioides, dimorphic fungi
medications.halfLife: Biphasic — initial 24 hrs; terminal up to 15 days (tissue accumulation)
medications.metabolism: No metabolites identified; primary route of elimination unknown
medications.excretion: Renal (~40% over 7 days; ~2–5% unchanged in urine); biliary; NOT affected by hepatic or renal dysfunction; poorly dialyzed
medications.monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine — before each dose or daily); electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺ — wasting is common); CBC; LFTs; vital signs during infusion (infusion reactions)
medications.maxDose: Deoxycholate: 1.5 mg/kg/day; ABLC: 5 mg/kg/day; L-AMB: 3–5 mg/kg/day (up to 10 mg/kg for mucormycosis)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; if nephrotoxicity develops with deoxycholate: reduce dose by 50%, use alternate-day dosing, or switch to lipid formulation; lipid formulations have significantly less nephrotoxicity; NOT removed by dialysis; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; use if benefit outweighs risk (limited data but long clinical experience)
medications.sideEffects: NEPHROTOXICITY (most common serious — AKI in up to 50% with deoxycholate; renal tubular acidosis; K⁺/Mg²⁺ wasting), infusion reactions (fever, rigors, chills, nausea — premedicate with acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, meperidine for rigors; saline loading reduces nephrotoxicity), anemia (normochromic normocytic), phlebitis, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity (unless life-threatening infection with no alternative). Lipid formulations preferred over deoxycholate when available (less nephrotoxicity, fewer infusion reactions). Deoxycholate, ABLC, and L-AMB are NOT interchangeable — different dosing and toxicity profiles. NEVER mix with saline (precipitates — use D5W only for deoxycholate).

medications.mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, forming pores that increase permeability and cause leakage of intracellular contents; fungistatic and fungicidal; active against Candida albicans and other Candida species; NOT absorbed systemically from intact skin, mucous membranes, or GI tract
medications.onset: Topical/oral — local effect only; no systemic absorption
medications.metabolism: Not applicable (not absorbed); oral nystatin passes through GI tract unchanged
medications.excretion: Fecal (oral — passed unchanged in stool); not renally excreted
medications.monitoring: Clinical response; signs of local irritation
medications.maxDose: Oral suspension: 400,000–600,000 units QID (thrush); Oral tablets: 500,000–1,000,000 units TID (GI candidiasis); Topical: apply BID-TID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No adjustment needed (not systemically absorbed); note: in severe renal insufficiency, oral nystatin may occasionally achieve detectable plasma levels
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category A (topical); C (oral); compatible with pregnancy and lactation (not absorbed)
medications.sideEffects: Oral: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (large doses); Topical: local irritation, contact dermatitis (rare)
medications.contraindications: Nystatin hypersensitivity. Oral suspension: swish and swallow (or swish and spit); retain in mouth as long as possible before swallowing. NOT effective for systemic fungal infections (no absorption). Resistance uncommon in C. albicans but can develop in other Candida species.

medications.mechanism: Binds selectively to glutamate-gated chloride ion channels in invertebrate nerve and muscle cells, increasing membrane permeability to chloride ions → hyperpolarization → paralysis and death of parasite; may also interact with GABA-gated chloride channels; active against endoparasites (Onchocerca, Strongyloides, Ascaris, Wuchereria, Enterobius) and ectoparasites (Sarcoptes scabiei, Pediculus)
medications.onset: PO — ~4 hrs (peak); lipophilic — accumulates in fat and liver
medications.durationOfAction: Variable by indication (single dose often sufficient)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primarily); CYP2D6 and CYP2E1 (minor); does not significantly inhibit CYP enzymes at therapeutic doses
medications.monitoring: Clinical response; in onchocerciasis — monitor for Mazzotti reaction (inflammatory response to dying microfilariae)
medications.maxDose: 200 mcg/kg single dose (strongyloidiasis); 150 mcg/kg single dose (onchocerciasis); scabies: 200 mcg/kg, may repeat in 7–14 days
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment for renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment (hepatically metabolized); not studied in severe hepatic disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; avoid unless benefit outweighs risk (limited human data); excreted in breast milk at low concentrations
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses; dizziness, nausea, diarrhea, pruritus; Mazzotti reaction in onchocerciasis (pruritus, urticaria, fever, myalgia, hypotension — correlates with microfilarial load); neurotoxicity at very high doses or in patients with BBB disruption (e.g., Loa loa co-infection)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; caution in Loa loa co-infection (risk of fatal encephalopathy with high microfilarial loads). Take on empty stomach with water (high-fat meal increases bioavailability 2.5-fold — may cause unpredictable absorption).

medications.mechanism: Atovaquone: selectively inhibits mitochondrial electron transport at cytochrome bc1 complex (Complex III), collapsing mitochondrial membrane potential; Proguanil: prodrug metabolized to cycloguanil, which inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR); synergistic combination — proguanil also enhances atovaquone's effect on mitochondrial membrane potential independent of DHFR inhibition; active against P. falciparum (blood and liver stages — causal prophylaxis)
medications.onset: PO — atovaquone peak 1–8 hrs; proguanil peak 1–4 hrs
medications.halfLife: Atovaquone ~2–3 days; Proguanil ~12–21 hrs; Cycloguanil ~14 hrs
medications.metabolism: Atovaquone: NOT significantly metabolized; highly protein-bound (>99%); Proguanil: hepatic via CYP2C19 to cycloguanil (active)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (hepatotoxicity reported including hepatic failure); parasitemia (treatment); CBC
medications.maxDose: Prophylaxis: 1 adult tablet (250/100 mg) daily; Treatment: 4 adult tablets daily × 3 days
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Prophylaxis/treatment: NOT recommended in severe renal impairment (CrCl 30) — proguanil/cycloguanil accumulate and risk of pancytopenia; no specific hepatic adjustment but limited data
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED (insufficient data; animal studies show fetal toxicity); avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding if infant 5 kg unless no alternatives
medications.sideEffects: Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting (up to 19% in pediatric treatment doses), headache, diarrhea, elevated LFTs, photosensitivity, mouth ulcers, vivid dreams; rare: hepatotoxicity (including hepatic failure requiring transplant), SJS, neutropenia, anemia, seizures
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; severe renal impairment (CrCl 30); pregnancy (unless no alternatives); prophylaxis in infants 5 kg. Advantages: causal prophylaxis (kills liver-stage parasites — can stop 7 days after leaving endemic area vs. 4 weeks for mefloquine/doxycycline); well-tolerated. Does NOT prevent P. vivax/P. ovale relapse (no hypnozoite activity — need primaquine/tafenoquine).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II; reduces aldosterone secretion; increases bradykinin (contributes to vasodilation and cough)
medications.metabolism: NOT metabolized; excreted unchanged
medications.monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine); potassium; blood pressure
medications.maxDose: 40 mg/day (HTN, HF); 10 mg/day (acute MI)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–80: no adjustment; CrCl 10–30: reduce initial dose to 50% (5 mg); hemodialysis: removable — dose on dialysis days; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED (all trimesters — fetal renal injury, oligohydramnios, death)
medications.sideEffects: Cough (up to 20%), hyperkalemia, hypotension, dizziness, AKI, angioedema (rare but serious — 2–4× more common in Black patients)
medications.contraindications: ACEi hypersensitivity; history of angioedema with ACEi; concomitant aliskiren in diabetes; pregnancy. Do NOT combine with ARBs.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug hydrolyzed to enalaprilat (active); inhibits ACE; same mechanism as lisinopril
medications.metabolism: Hepatic hydrolysis of enalapril (prodrug) to enalaprilat (active)
medications.monitoring: Same as lisinopril
medications.maxDose: 40 mg/day (divided BID)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–80: no adjustment; CrCl 10–30: reduce dose by 50%; hemodialysis: dose on dialysis days only; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Same as lisinopril
medications.contraindications: Same as lisinopril. Available as IV enalaprilat for hypertensive emergencies (1.25 mg IV q6h).

medications.mechanism: Prodrug converted to ramiprilat (active); inhibits ACE
medications.metabolism: Hepatic hydrolysis to ramiprilat (active)
medications.monitoring: Same as lisinopril
medications.maxDose: 20 mg/day (HTN); 10 mg/day (CV risk reduction — HOPE trial)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 30–60: no adjustment; CrCl 10–30: reduce dose by 50%; hemodialysis: dose on dialysis days; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Same as lisinopril
medications.contraindications: Same as lisinopril. HOPE trial demonstrated CV mortality reduction in high-risk patients.

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptors; prevents vasoconstriction, aldosterone release, and sympathetic activation; does NOT affect bradykinin (no cough)
medications.onset: PO — 1 hr; peak 3–4 hrs (active metabolite 6 hrs)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 to active metabolite E-3174 (10–40× more potent than parent); ~14% of dose converted
medications.monitoring: Renal function; potassium; blood pressure
medications.maxDose: 100 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; no experience in eGFR 10 or dialysis; hepatic impairment: reduce starting dose to 25 mg (increased bioavailability)
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Dizziness, hyperkalemia, hypotension, AKI (less cough/angioedema than ACEi)
medications.contraindications: ARB hypersensitivity; concomitant aliskiren in diabetes; pregnancy. Do NOT combine with ACEi. Also has uricosuric effect (unique among ARBs).

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks AT1 receptors; same class mechanism as losartan
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism (~20%); NOT a CYP450 substrate (fewer drug interactions than losartan)
medications.monitoring: Same as losartan
medications.maxDose: 320 mg/day (HTN); 160 mg BID (HF)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR >10: no adjustment; no experience in eGFR 10 or dialysis; NOT removed by hemodialysis; caution in severe hepatic impairment (biliary excretion)
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Same as losartan
medications.contraindications: Same as losartan.

medications.mechanism: Sacubitril (prodrug → LBQ657) inhibits neprilysin, preventing degradation of natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP, CNP) → vasodilation, natriuresis, reduced fibrosis; Valsartan blocks AT1 receptors; dual mechanism provides superior neurohormonal modulation vs ACEi/ARB alone
medications.onset: PO — sacubitril peak 0.5 hrs; valsartan peak 1.5 hrs; LBQ657 peak 2 hrs
medications.halfLife: Sacubitril ~1.4 hrs; LBQ657 ~11.5 hrs; Valsartan ~9.9 hrs
medications.metabolism: Sacubitril converted to LBQ657 by esterases; valsartan minimally metabolized
medications.monitoring: Renal function; potassium; blood pressure; BNP (NOT reliable for monitoring HF — neprilysin inhibition raises BNP; use NT-proBNP instead)
medications.maxDose: 97/103 mg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR ≥30: no adjustment; eGFR 30: reduce starting dose to 24/26 mg BID; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): not recommended; moderate (Child-Pugh B): reduce starting dose
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Hypotension (most common), hyperkalemia, AKI, cough (less than ACEi), angioedema (risk — must have 36-hr washout from ACEi before starting), dizziness
medications.contraindications: ACEi use within 36 hrs (angioedema risk); history of angioedema with ACEi/ARB; concomitant aliskiren in diabetes; pregnancy. PARADIGM-HF: 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril.

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors (cardioselective at low-moderate doses); reduces heart rate, contractility, and AV conduction; reduces renin release; selectivity diminishes at high doses
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (extensive first-pass ~50% bioavailability); CYP2D6 polymorphism causes wide variability — poor metabolizers (~8% Caucasians) have several-fold higher plasma levels
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; signs of HF decompensation; blood glucose (may mask hypoglycemia symptoms)
medications.maxDose: Tartrate: 450 mg/day; Succinate: 400 mg/day; HF: 200 mg/day (succinate only — MERIT-HF)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No formal renal dose adjustment (hepatically metabolized); lower doses may be needed in reduced GFR; hepatic impairment: reduce dose (increased bioavailability)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; use if benefit outweighs risk; may cause fetal bradycardia
medications.sideEffects: Bradycardia, hypotension, fatigue, dizziness, bronchospasm (less than non-selective), depression, sexual dysfunction, masking of hypoglycemia, rebound hypertension/tachycardia if abruptly discontinued
medications.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia; heart block >1st degree (without pacemaker); cardiogenic shock; decompensated HF; sick sinus syndrome. Tartrate and succinate are NOT interchangeable. Only succinate (XL) is FDA-approved for HF.

medications.mechanism: Blocks beta-1, beta-2, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors; provides vasodilation (alpha-1 block) in addition to heart rate/contractility reduction; also has antioxidant properties
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (primary), CYP2C9, CYP3A4; extensive first-pass metabolism; stereoselective
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure (orthostatic); signs of HF decompensation; hepatic function
medications.maxDose: HTN: 25 mg BID; HF: 25 mg BID (85 kg) or 50 mg BID (≥85 kg)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (fecal elimination); CONTRAINDICATED in severe hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Hypotension (more than metoprolol — alpha-1 block), dizziness, fatigue, bradycardia, weight gain, diarrhea, hyperglycemia (less than other beta-blockers), bronchospasm
medications.contraindications: Same as metoprolol plus severe hepatic impairment; reactive airway disease (non-selective). Take with food (slows absorption, reduces orthostatic hypotension). FDA-approved for HFrEF (COPERNICUS, COMET trials).

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta-1 receptors; cardioselective; hydrophilic — minimal CNS penetration
medications.metabolism: NOT metabolized (hydrophilic — no hepatic first-pass)
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; renal function
medications.maxDose: 100 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 15–35: 50 mg/day; CrCl 15: 25 mg/day; hemodialysis: 25–50 mg after each session; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; associated with fetal growth restriction
medications.sideEffects: Bradycardia, hypotension, fatigue, cold extremities, less CNS effects than lipophilic beta-blockers
medications.contraindications: Same as metoprolol. NOT recommended as first-line for HTN (inferior stroke prevention vs other antihypertensives). NOT FDA-approved for HF.

medications.mechanism: Blocks beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors; lipophilic — crosses BBB extensively; also has membrane-stabilizing activity at high doses
medications.halfLife: IR 3–6 hrs; ER 8–20 hrs; IV 2.4 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6, CYP1A2, CYP2C19; extensive first-pass (~25% bioavailability); active metabolite 4-hydroxypropranolol
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; blood glucose
medications.maxDose: HTN: 640 mg/day; migraine prophylaxis: 240 mg/day; essential tremor: 320 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (hepatically metabolized); hepatic impairment: reduce dose (increased bioavailability); NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm (non-selective — avoid in asthma/COPD), fatigue, vivid dreams/insomnia (lipophilic — CNS penetration), cold extremities, masking of hypoglycemia, sexual dysfunction
medications.contraindications: Asthma/reactive airway disease (absolute — non-selective); same cardiac contraindications as metoprolol. Multiple indications: HTN, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor, performance anxiety, thyrotoxicosis, portal HTN (variceal bleeding prophylaxis).

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta-1 receptors; ultra-short acting due to rapid ester hydrolysis; ideal for acute rate control and perioperative use
medications.metabolism: Rapid hydrolysis by red blood cell esterases (NOT plasma cholinesterase or CYP450); acid metabolite has minimal activity
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure (continuous); ECG
medications.maxDose: Loading 500 mcg/kg over 1 min; infusion 50–300 mcg/kg/min
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal or hepatic impairment (RBC esterase metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Hypotension (most common), bradycardia, injection site reactions, diaphoresis, nausea, dizziness
medications.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia; heart block >1st degree; cardiogenic shock; decompensated HF; IV administration of CCBs and esmolol in close proximity (risk of severe hypotension). Key advantage: ultra-short duration allows rapid titration and quick offset if adverse effects occur.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle; causes peripheral arterial vasodilation; reduces afterload; NO significant effect on cardiac conduction or contractility (vascular selective)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (~90% converted to inactive metabolites via CYP3A4)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; heart rate; peripheral edema
medications.maxDose: 10 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (PK not significantly affected by renal impairment); hepatic impairment/elderly: start 2.5 mg (decreased clearance, 40–60% increase in AUC)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Peripheral edema (dose-dependent — most common), headache, flushing, dizziness, palpitations, fatigue; does NOT cause reflex tachycardia (gradual onset)
medications.contraindications: DHP CCB hypersensitivity. Safe in HFrEF (PRAISE trial — no increase in mortality). Simvastatin max 20 mg and lovastatin max 20 mg when combined with amlodipine.

medications.brandNames: Cardizem, Cardizem CD, Tiazac, Cartia XT
medications.mechanism: Inhibits L-type calcium channels in both vascular smooth muscle AND cardiac tissue; reduces heart rate (SA node), slows AV conduction, reduces contractility (negative inotrope/chronotrope/dromotrope); also causes coronary and peripheral vasodilation
medications.onset: PO IR — 30–60 min; IV — 3 min; peak IV 7 min
medications.halfLife: IR 3–4.5 hrs; ER 4–9.5 hrs; IV 3–5 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (extensive first-pass; bioavailability ~40%); active metabolite desacetyldiltiazem (~25–50% activity); moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; ECG (PR interval); signs of HF; drug interactions (CYP3A4)
medications.maxDose: IR: 360 mg/day; ER: 480 mg/day; IV: 15 mg/hr infusion
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose (increased bioavailability, prolonged half-life); NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Bradycardia, hypotension, AV block, constipation, peripheral edema (less than DHPs), dizziness, headache, flushing
medications.contraindications: Sick sinus syndrome/2nd-3rd degree AV block (without pacemaker); severe hypotension; acute MI with pulmonary congestion; HFrEF (negative inotrope — AVOID); concomitant IV beta-blockers; WPW with AF. Simvastatin max 10 mg and lovastatin max 20 mg when combined.

medications.mechanism: Same as diltiazem but MORE potent negative inotrope and AV nodal blockade; less peripheral vasodilation than diltiazem
medications.halfLife: IR 2–7 hrs; SR 12–17 hrs; IV 6–8 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (extensive first-pass; bioavailability 10–30%); active metabolite norverapamil (~20% activity); moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor; also inhibits P-glycoprotein
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; ECG (PR interval); digoxin levels (increases digoxin by 50–75%); signs of HF; constipation
medications.maxDose: IR: 480 mg/day; SR: 480 mg/day; IV: 10 mg
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose by 70–80% (markedly increased bioavailability); NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Constipation (most common — up to 42%), bradycardia, AV block, hypotension, HF exacerbation, peripheral edema, dizziness, headache
medications.contraindications: Same as diltiazem plus severe LV dysfunction; concomitant beta-blockers (IV — risk of asystole); WPW with AF. Simvastatin max 10 mg and lovastatin max 20 mg when combined. Most constipating CCB.

medications.mechanism: Same as amlodipine — vascular-selective L-type calcium channel blocker; potent peripheral vasodilator; NO significant cardiac conduction effects
medications.halfLife: 2–5 hrs (IR); ~7 hrs (XL — apparent due to slow absorption)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (extensive first-pass; bioavailability 40–50%)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; heart rate (reflex tachycardia with IR); peripheral edema
medications.maxDose: IR: 120 mg/day (rarely used); XL: 120 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (PK not significantly affected); hepatic impairment: reduce dose; NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; used off-label for tocolysis and preeclampsia
medications.sideEffects: Peripheral edema, headache, flushing, dizziness, reflex tachycardia (IR — avoid in ACS), gingival hyperplasia
medications.contraindications: DHP CCB hypersensitivity; cardiogenic shock; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin). AVOID short-acting (IR) nifedipine — associated with increased ischemic events and mortality (precipitous BP drop, reflex tachycardia). Use ONLY extended-release formulations.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2) in thick ascending limb of loop of Henle; blocks reabsorption of ~25% of filtered sodium; also increases renal prostaglandin synthesis (contributes to renal vasodilation); most potent diuretic class
medications.halfLife: 0.5–1 hr (normal); prolonged in renal failure and neonates
medications.metabolism: ~50% excreted unchanged; remainder glucuronidated (primarily in kidney)
medications.monitoring: Electrolytes (K⁺, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺); renal function; volume status; blood pressure; uric acid; blood glucose
medications.maxDose: 600 mg/day (HF); 80 mg/dose IV (single); higher doses in renal failure
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Higher doses needed in renal impairment to achieve same diuretic effect (reduced tubular secretion); no specific dose cap but monitor closely; hepatic impairment: use with caution (risk of hepatic encephalopathy)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, ototoxicity (high-dose IV bolus), dehydration, AKI (prerenal), hypotension
medications.contraindications: Anuria; sulfonamide hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity debated); hepatic coma. Erratic oral bioavailability (10–100%) — consider IV or switch to torsemide/bumetanide for more predictable absorption.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC) in distal convoluted tubule; blocks reabsorption of ~5% of filtered sodium; also reduces calcium excretion (useful in nephrolithiasis); long-term antihypertensive effect partly from reduced peripheral vascular resistance
medications.metabolism: NOT significantly metabolized
medications.monitoring: Electrolytes (K⁺, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺); renal function; uric acid; blood glucose; lipids
medications.maxDose: 50 mg/day (HTN — higher doses increase side effects without much additional BP lowering); 200 mg/day (edema)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Ineffective when CrCl 30 (reduced delivery to tubular site of action); avoid in severe renal impairment; hepatic impairment: use with caution (electrolyte shifts may precipitate hepatic encephalopathy)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; avoid in 1st trimester if possible
medications.sideEffects: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia (especially elderly women), hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, hypercalcemia, hyperlipidemia, photosensitivity, erectile dysfunction
medications.contraindications: Anuria; sulfonamide hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity debated). Less potent than chlorthalidone for BP reduction and CV outcomes.

medications.mechanism: Same site of action as HCTZ (NCC in DCT) but also inhibits carbonic anhydrase in RBCs; longer-acting and more potent than HCTZ
medications.halfLife: 45–60 hrs (longest among thiazides — due to extensive RBC partitioning creating a tissue reservoir)
medications.metabolism: Minimal
medications.monitoring: Same as HCTZ; more vigilant electrolyte monitoring due to longer duration
medications.maxDose: 100 mg/day (edema); 25–50 mg/day (HTN)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as HCTZ — ineffective when CrCl 30; avoid in severe renal impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Same as HCTZ but higher rates of hypokalemia and hyponatremia (longer duration of action)
medications.contraindications: Same as HCTZ. Preferred over HCTZ by many guidelines — stronger evidence base for CV outcomes (ALLHAT, SHEP trials); more potent 24-hr BP reduction.

medications.mechanism: Competitively blocks aldosterone at mineralocorticoid receptors in DCT and collecting duct; reduces sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion; also has anti-androgenic effects (blocks androgen receptors, inhibits testosterone synthesis)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (extensive) to multiple active metabolites including canrenone and 7-alpha-thiomethylspironolactone
medications.monitoring: Potassium (hyperkalemia — most dangerous); renal function; blood pressure; gynecomastia symptoms
medications.maxDose: HF: 50 mg/day; HTN/edema: 200 mg/day; ascites: 400 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Contraindicated in "severe renal failure" and eGFR 30 (per some guidelines); use with extreme caution if eGFR 30–45 with close K⁺ monitoring; no hepatic adjustment but active metabolites may accumulate
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D (feminization of male fetus — anti-androgenic)
medications.sideEffects: Hyperkalemia (most dangerous), gynecomastia (up to 10% — anti-androgenic), menstrual irregularities, sexual dysfunction, GI upset, dizziness
medications.contraindications: Anuria; eGFR 30; K⁺ ≥5.0; Addison disease; concomitant eplerenone. RALES trial: 30% mortality reduction in severe HFrEF.

medications.mechanism: Same as spironolactone but 50% less potent; highly selective for mineralocorticoid receptor — NO anti-androgenic or progesterone agonist effects (no gynecomastia)
medications.onset: PO — peak 1.5 hrs; full effect days
medications.durationOfAction: 24 hrs (shorter than spironolactone — may need BID for HTN)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 to inactive metabolites; does NOT inhibit or induce CYP enzymes
medications.monitoring: Same as spironolactone — K⁺ and renal function
medications.maxDose: HF: 50 mg/day; HTN: 50 mg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR ≥50: standard dosing; eGFR 30–49: start 25 mg every other day; eGFR 30: contraindicated; no hepatic adjustment for mild-moderate; avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole — contraindicated)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Hyperkalemia (most significant — similar rate to spironolactone), dizziness, diarrhea, cough, fatigue; gynecomastia only 0.5% (vs ~10% spironolactone)
medications.contraindications: K⁺ >5.5; eGFR 30; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors; concomitant K⁺ supplements or K⁺-sparing diuretics. EPHESUS and EMPHASIS-HF trials demonstrated mortality benefit. Preferred over spironolactone in males due to lack of anti-androgenic effects.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug denitrified by mitochondrial aldehyde dehydrogenase to release nitric oxide (NO); NO activates guanylyl cyclase → increased cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation; predominantly venodilator (reduces preload); at higher doses also dilates coronary arteries and peripheral arteries (reduces afterload)
medications.onset: SL — 1–3 min; IV — seconds; Patch — 30 min; Ointment — 20–60 min
medications.durationOfAction: SL — 25 min; IV — 3–5 min after stopping; Patch — 10–12 hrs; Ointment — 4–8 hrs
medications.metabolism: Extensive hepatic and intravascular metabolism; active dinitrate metabolites (t½ ~40 min)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; heart rate; headache; signs of tolerance
medications.maxDose: SL: 0.4 mg q5min × 3 doses; IV: 5–400 mcg/min; Patch: 0.8 mg/hr
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustments; use with caution in hepatic impairment (extensive hepatic metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Headache (most common), flushing, hypotension, reflex tachycardia, dizziness, syncope, methemoglobinemia (rare, high doses)
medications.contraindications: Concurrent PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil — severe hypotension; wait 24 hrs for sildenafil/vardenafil, 48 hrs for tadalafil); severe hypotension; hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM); right ventricular infarction; increased ICP. MUST maintain 10–14 hr nitrate-free interval to prevent tolerance (typically remove patch at bedtime).

medications.brandNames: Isordil (ISDN), BiDil (ISDN/hydralazine), Imdur (ISMN ER), Monoket (ISMN IR)
medications.mechanism: Same as nitroglycerin — NO release → cGMP → vasodilation
medications.onset: ISDN PO — 60 min; ISMN PO — 30–45 min
medications.durationOfAction: ISDN IR — 8 hrs; ISDN SR — 12 hrs; ISMN IR — 6 hrs; ISMN ER — 12–24 hrs
medications.halfLife: ISDN — ~40 min (parent); active metabolites isosorbide-5-mononitrate (t½ ~4–5 hrs); ISMN — 4–5 hrs
medications.metabolism: ISDN: extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism (bioavailability 10–90%, average ~25%); ISMN: 100% bioavailable (no first-pass metabolism — more predictable)
medications.monitoring: Same as nitroglycerin
medications.maxDose: ISDN: 40 mg TID; ISMN IR: 20 mg BID (7 hrs apart); ISMN ER: 240 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific adjustments
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Same as nitroglycerin — headache, hypotension, dizziness, flushing
medications.contraindications: Same as nitroglycerin. ISDN must be dosed asymmetrically (e.g., 8 AM and 1 PM) to maintain nitrate-free interval. ISMN ER given once daily in AM provides built-in nitrate-free interval. BiDil (ISDN 20 mg/hydralazine 37.5 mg) — FDA-approved for HFrEF in self-identified Black patients (A-HeFT trial).

medications.drugClass: Class III antiarrhythmic (also has Class I, II, IV properties)
medications.mechanism: Predominantly blocks potassium channels (IKr, IKs, IK1) prolonging repolarization and refractoriness; also blocks sodium channels (Class I), calcium channels (Class IV), and has non-competitive beta-blocking activity (Class II); contains 37% iodine by weight — affects thyroid function
medications.onset: PO — days to weeks (extremely long loading); IV — hours
medications.durationOfAction: Weeks to months after discontinuation (due to massive tissue accumulation)
medications.halfLife: 40–55 days (range 9–36 days initially; terminal up to 142 days); active metabolite desethylamiodarone (DEA) t½ ~61 days
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 and CYP2C8 to DEA (active); inhibits CYP2D6, CYP2C9, CYP3A4, P-glycoprotein — EXTENSIVE drug interactions
medications.excretion: Biliary/fecal (primary); renal (negligible); NOT removed by dialysis
medications.monitoring: TSH q6 months (thyroid dysfunction 2–24%); LFTs q6 months; PFTs/CXR at baseline and if symptomatic (pulmonary toxicity 1–2%, fatal in ~10%); ECG (QTc); ophthalmologic exam if visual symptoms; K⁺/Mg²⁺
medications.maxDose: Loading: 800–1600 mg/day × 1–3 weeks; Maintenance: 200–400 mg/day; IV: 150 mg bolus then 1 mg/min × 6 hrs then 0.5 mg/min
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (negligible renal excretion); avoid in severe hepatic disease (hepatotoxic); NOT removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D (fetal thyroid dysfunction, IUGR, prematurity)
medications.sideEffects: Thyroid dysfunction (hypo- or hyperthyroidism — 2–24%), pulmonary toxicity (interstitial pneumonitis/fibrosis — 1–2%, fatal in 10%), hepatotoxicity (elevated LFTs common; hepatitis rare), corneal microdeposits (nearly universal — rarely symptomatic), blue-gray skin discoloration (prolonged use), photosensitivity, peripheral neuropathy, tremor, ataxia, QT prolongation (TdP risk ~0.7% — lower than other Class III agents), bradycardia
medications.contraindications: Cardiogenic shock; sick sinus syndrome/2nd-3rd degree AV block (without pacemaker); severe sinus node dysfunction. FDA-approved only for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias, but widely used off-label for AF. Most effective antiarrhythmic for maintaining sinus rhythm but limited by toxicity profile.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → increases intracellular Na⁺ → increases intracellular Ca²⁺ via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger → positive inotrope; also increases vagal tone (parasympathomimetic) → slows AV conduction and heart rate; neurohormonal modulation (reduces sympathetic activity, reduces renin)
medications.halfLife: 36–48 hrs (normal renal function); up to 4–6 days in anuria
medications.metabolism: ~16% hepatic; ~80% excreted unchanged
medications.excretion: Renal (~60–80% unchanged via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion); NOT significantly removed by hemodialysis
medications.monitoring: Serum digoxin level (target 0.5–0.9 ng/mL for HF; 2.0 ng/mL to avoid toxicity; draw ≥6 hrs post-dose); electrolytes (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypercalcemia increase toxicity risk); renal function; heart rate; ECG
medications.maxDose: 0.25 mg/day (most patients); 0.125 mg daily or every other day in elderly, renal impairment, low body weight
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Dose reduction required in renal impairment (primary renal excretion); CrCl 50: reduce dose; CrCl 10: reduce dose by 50–75%; NOT removed by hemodialysis; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX — toxicity common. GI: nausea, vomiting, anorexia (earliest signs). Cardiac: any arrhythmia (classically bidirectional VT, atrial tachycardia with block, accelerated junctional rhythm, PVCs). Neuro: visual disturbances (yellow-green halos — xanthopsia), confusion, weakness. Hypokalemia potentiates toxicity
medications.contraindications: Ventricular fibrillation; hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (relative). Toxicity treatment: digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind/DigiFab). Drug interactions: amiodarone, verapamil, quinidine increase digoxin levels (reduce digoxin dose by 50% when adding these).

medications.mechanism: Activates A1 adenosine receptors on AV nodal cells → opens K⁺ channels (IKAdo) → hyperpolarization → slows AV conduction and interrupts re-entrant circuits through AV node; also inhibits cAMP-mediated Ca²⁺ influx; ultra-short acting
medications.halfLife: 10 seconds (rapidly taken up by RBCs and endothelial cells via nucleoside transporters; metabolized to inosine and AMP)
medications.metabolism: Intracellular phosphorylation and deamination; NOT hepatic CYP450
medications.monitoring: Continuous ECG; blood pressure; have resuscitation equipment available
medications.maxDose: 1st dose: 6 mg rapid IV push; 2nd dose: 12 mg (may repeat 12 mg × 1); Pediatric: 0.1 mg/kg (max 6 mg), then 0.2 mg/kg (max 12 mg)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No adjustment needed (not renally/hepatically eliminated)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; has been used safely
medications.sideEffects: Transient chest tightness/pain, dyspnea, flushing, headache, dizziness, nausea; transient asystole (expected — warn patient); bronchospasm (in asthmatics)
