medications.contraindications: 2nd/3rd degree AV block or sick sinus syndrome (without pacemaker); asthma/severe COPD (bronchospasm); known hypersensitivity. Must give as RAPID IV push followed by immediate 20 mL NS flush (use stopcock closest to patient). Dipyridamole potentiates effect (reduce dose to 3 mg). Caffeine/theophylline antagonize effect (may need higher doses). Carbamazepine increases risk of AV block.

medications.mechanism: Potent sodium channel blocker (slow binding kinetics); markedly slows conduction in His-Purkinje system and ventricular myocardium; prolongs QRS duration; also blocks IKr (potassium channel); minimal effect on repolarization
medications.halfLife: 12–27 hrs (average ~20 hrs); prolonged in HF and renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2D6 (~70%); CYP2D6 poor metabolizers have significantly higher levels
medications.monitoring: ECG (QRS duration — should not increase >25% from baseline; PR interval); drug levels (target 0.2–1.0 mcg/mL); renal function; hepatic function
medications.maxDose: PSVT/PAF: 300 mg/day; Sustained VT: 400 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 35: reduce dose by 50% or more; start 50 mg BID; hepatic impairment: reduce dose; monitor levels closely
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Dizziness, visual disturbances (blurred vision), dyspnea, headache, nausea, tremor, proarrhythmia (most serious — can organize AF into atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction; ventricular tachycardia)
medications.contraindications: Structural heart disease (ischemic heart disease, HFrEF — CAST trial showed increased mortality); 2nd/3rd degree AV block; bundle branch block with bifascicular block (without pacemaker); cardiogenic shock. CAST TRIAL: increased mortality in post-MI patients — AVOID in structural heart disease. "Pill-in-the-pocket" approach: single oral loading dose (200–300 mg based on weight) for self-termination of paroxysmal AF in selected patients without structural heart disease.

medications.mechanism: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis; upregulates hepatic LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance; also reduces VLDL, TG; increases HDL; pleiotropic effects (anti-inflammatory, endothelial function, plaque stabilization)
medications.onset: PO — 2 weeks (lipid changes); maximal effect 4–6 weeks
medications.durationOfAction: 24 hrs (long-acting — can be taken any time of day)
medications.halfLife: ~14 hrs; active metabolites extend inhibitory activity to 20–30 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4; active ortho- and para-hydroxylated metabolites (~70% of HMG-CoA reductase inhibitory activity); lipophilic
medications.monitoring: Lipid panel (baseline, 4–12 weeks after initiation, then annually); LFTs (baseline; repeat if symptoms); CPK (if muscle symptoms); blood glucose (may increase)
medications.maxDose: 80 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (minimal renal excretion); NOT removed by hemodialysis; CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease or unexplained persistent LFT elevations
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Myalgia (most common — 5–10%), elevated LFTs, rhabdomyolysis (rare), new-onset diabetes (dose-dependent), GI upset, headache, cognitive effects (rare, reversible)
medications.contraindications: Active liver disease; pregnancy/lactation; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors at high statin doses (clarithromycin, itraconazole, HIV protease inhibitors). Max 20 mg with amlodipine; max 40 mg with diltiazem. High-intensity: 40–80 mg (≥50% LDL reduction); Moderate-intensity: 10–20 mg (30–49% LDL reduction).

medications.mechanism: Same as atorvastatin; most potent statin — ~3× more potent than atorvastatin for LDL reduction
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism via CYP2C9 (minor); hydrophilic — less susceptible to CYP450 interactions than atorvastatin/simvastatin; NOT a CYP3A4 substrate
medications.monitoring: Same as atorvastatin
medications.maxDose: 40 mg/day (20 mg in Asian patients — higher plasma levels)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR ≥30: no adjustment; eGFR 30 (not on dialysis): start 5 mg, max 10 mg; hemodialysis: limited data; CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED
medications.sideEffects: Same as atorvastatin; proteinuria (dose-dependent, usually tubular — not clinically significant)
medications.contraindications: Same as atorvastatin. Fewer CYP3A4 interactions than atorvastatin (advantage). High-intensity: 20–40 mg; Moderate-intensity: 5–10 mg. Asian patients: start 5 mg (2× higher exposure).

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits Niemann-Pick C1-Like 1 (NPC1L1) protein at the intestinal brush border, blocking absorption of dietary and biliary cholesterol; reduces cholesterol delivery to liver → upregulates hepatic LDL receptors; complementary mechanism to statins
medications.onset: PO — within 2 weeks; maximal effect 4 weeks
medications.metabolism: Hepatic and intestinal glucuronidation (UGT1A1, UGT1A3) to active glucuronide conjugate; undergoes enterohepatic recirculation; NOT significantly CYP450-dependent
medications.monitoring: Lipid panel; LFTs (when combined with statin)
medications.maxDose: 10 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; NOT recommended in moderate-severe hepatic impairment (increased exposure)
medications.pregnancyLactation: CONTRAINDICATED (when combined with statin)
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; diarrhea, arthralgia, upper respiratory infection, sinusitis; myalgia (when combined with statin); elevated LFTs (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; moderate-severe hepatic impairment (monotherapy); active liver disease (when combined with statin). IMPROVE-IT trial: adding ezetimibe to simvastatin reduced CV events vs simvastatin alone in post-ACS patients. Reduces LDL by ~18% as monotherapy; ~25% additional reduction when added to statin.

medications.mechanism: Binds antithrombin III (AT-III), accelerating its inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa by ~1000-fold; also inhibits factors IXa, XIa, XIIa; requires AT-III as cofactor
medications.halfLife: Dose-dependent (30 min at 25 U/kg to 150 min at 400 U/kg); average ~60–90 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (reticuloendothelial system); partially depolymerized
medications.monitoring: aPTT (target 1.5–2.5× control, typically 60–80 sec); anti-Xa levels (target 0.3–0.7 IU/mL — more reliable in obese, critically ill); platelet count (HIT screening — baseline, then q2–3 days for 4–14 days)
medications.maxDose: Weight-based protocols; VTE: 80 U/kg bolus then 18 U/kg/hr; ACS: 60 U/kg bolus (max 4000 U) then 12 U/kg/hr (max 1000 U/hr)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; monitor closely in renal/hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Does NOT cross placenta — safe in pregnancy (preferred anticoagulant)
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding (most common), HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia — Type II: immune-mediated, 1–5%, onset day 5–14; paradoxical thrombosis), osteoporosis (long-term use), hyperkalemia (aldosterone suppression), alopecia
medications.watchFor: Antidote: protamine sulfate (1 mg neutralizes ~100 U heparin). HIT: suspect if platelet count drops >50% from baseline or to 150,000 — discontinue all heparin immediately and start alternative anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin). Rapid-onset HIT can occur within hours if prior heparin exposure within 30 days. Anti-Xa monitoring preferred over aPTT in critically ill patients (less affected by acute-phase reactants, lupus anticoagulant, factor deficiencies). Nonlinear pharmacokinetics — half-life increases with dose. Pregnancy: does not cross placenta — preferred anticoagulant in pregnancy.
medications.contraindications: Active major bleeding; severe thrombocytopenia; HIT history; inability to monitor. Reversal: protamine sulfate (1 mg per 100 U heparin given in last 2–3 hrs).
medications.indications: FDA: prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism (DVT, PE), treatment of PE, prevention of clotting in arterial and cardiac surgery, anticoagulation during extracorporeal circulation (dialysis, cardiopulmonary bypass), DIC (low-dose), ACS (UA/NSTEMI, STEMI). Off-label: bridging anticoagulation, catheter patency.
medications.dosing:
- VTE treatment: 80 U/kg IV bolus, then 18 U/kg/hour continuous infusion; adjust per aPTT or anti-Xa nomogram
- VTE prophylaxis: 5000 U SC every 8–12 hours
- ACS: 60 U/kg IV bolus (max 4000 U), then 12 U/kg/hour (max 1000 U/hour)
- Cardiopulmonary bypass: 300–400 U/kg IV (ACT-guided)
Route: IV (bolus and continuous infusion), SC (prophylaxis)

medications.mechanism: Preferentially inhibits factor Xa (anti-Xa:anti-IIa ratio ~3.8:1) via AT-III; more predictable pharmacokinetics than UFH; less protein binding
medications.metabolism: Hepatic desulfation and depolymerization
medications.monitoring: Routine monitoring NOT required (predictable PK); anti-Xa levels in obesity (>150 kg), renal impairment, pregnancy (target: treatment 0.6–1.0 IU/mL at 4 hrs post-dose; prophylaxis 0.2–0.5 IU/mL); platelet count (HIT risk lower than UFH — ~0.5%)
medications.maxDose: Treatment: 1 mg/kg SC q12h or 1.5 mg/kg SC daily; Prophylaxis: 40 mg SC daily or 30 mg SC q12h
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl ≥30: standard dosing; CrCl 30: 1 mg/kg SC daily (treatment) or 30 mg SC daily (prophylaxis); NOT recommended in severe renal impairment by some guidelines; partially removed by hemodialysis
medications.pregnancyLactation: Does NOT cross placenta — preferred anticoagulant in pregnancy
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding, injection site hematoma/pain, HIT (lower risk than UFH), thrombocytopenia, elevated LFTs, osteoporosis (less than UFH), hyperkalemia
medications.contraindications: Active major bleeding; HIT; hypersensitivity to heparin/pork products; epidural/spinal anesthesia (timing restrictions). Reversal: protamine (60–75% reversal of anti-Xa activity; 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin if within 8 hrs).

medications.mechanism: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), blocking recycling of vitamin K; depletes functional vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) and proteins C and S; onset delayed because existing clotting factors must be cleared (factor VII t½ ~6 hrs; factor II t½ ~60 hrs)
medications.onset: PO — anticoagulant effect in 24–72 hrs; full therapeutic effect 5–7 days
medications.halfLife: 36–42 hrs (R-warfarin ~45 hrs; S-warfarin ~29 hrs; S-isomer is 3–5× more potent)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic — S-warfarin via CYP2C9 (primary); R-warfarin via CYP1A2, CYP2C19, CYP3A4; VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms significantly affect dose requirements; EXTENSIVE drug and food interactions
medications.monitoring: INR (target 2.0–3.0 for most indications; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valve); check INR frequently during initiation (daily × 3–5 days, then weekly, then monthly when stable); genotyping (CYP2C9, VKORC1) may guide initial dosing
medications.maxDose: Individualized by INR; typical maintenance 2–10 mg/day; wide interpatient variability
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal dose adjustment (hepatically metabolized); hepatic impairment: increased sensitivity (reduced clotting factor synthesis); use lower doses
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category X (teratogenic — warfarin embryopathy: nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses in 1st trimester; CNS abnormalities in 2nd/3rd trimester)
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding (most common — major bleeding 1–3%/year), skin necrosis (rare — protein C deficiency; days 3–8 of therapy), purple toe syndrome, calciphylaxis, cholesterol microembolization, teratogenicity
medications.contraindications: Pregnancy; active bleeding; hemorrhagic tendencies; recent CNS/eye surgery; unsupervised patients with high fall risk. Reversal: vitamin K (PO/IV), 4-factor PCC (Kcentra) for urgent reversal, FFP. Numerous drug interactions — always check.

medications.drugClass: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) — Factor Xa inhibitor
medications.mechanism: Directly and reversibly inhibits free and clot-bound factor Xa; does NOT require AT-III as cofactor; predictable pharmacokinetics
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary); also CYP1A2, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2J2; P-glycoprotein substrate
medications.excretion: Renal (~27%); biliary/intestinal (~73%) — LEAST renal dependence among DOACs
medications.monitoring: Routine monitoring NOT required; anti-Xa levels (calibrated to apixaban) if needed (overdose, emergent surgery, extremes of weight); renal function periodically
medications.maxDose: AF: 5 mg BID; VTE treatment: 10 mg BID × 7 days then 5 mg BID; VTE prophylaxis (hip/knee): 2.5 mg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: AF dose reduction to 2.5 mg BID if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, SCr ≥1.5 mg/dL; can be used at any CrCl including ESRD/hemodialysis (FDA-approved); only 6.7% cleared by hemodialysis; mild-moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): no adjustment; severe (Child-Pugh C): not recommended
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended (limited data)
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding (most common — major bleeding ~2.1%/yr in ARISTOTLE), bruising, anemia, nausea, epistaxis
medications.contraindications: Active pathological bleeding; severe hypersensitivity; prosthetic heart valves (not studied). Reversal: andexanet alfa (Andexxa) — FDA-approved specific reversal agent; 4-factor PCC as alternative. Reduce to 2.5 mg BID with strong dual CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir). ARISTOTLE trial: superior to warfarin for stroke prevention with less bleeding.

medications.mechanism: Directly and reversibly inhibits free and clot-bound factor Xa; highly selective; predictable PK
medications.durationOfAction: 24 hrs (once-daily for AF; BID for VTE treatment initial phase)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4/5 (primary), CYP2J2; P-glycoprotein substrate
medications.excretion: Renal (~66% — 36% unchanged); fecal (~28%) — MORE renal dependence than apixaban
medications.monitoring: Routine monitoring NOT required; anti-Xa levels (calibrated to rivaroxaban) if needed; renal function periodically
medications.maxDose: AF: 20 mg daily with evening meal; VTE treatment: 15 mg BID × 21 days then 20 mg daily; VTE prophylaxis: 10 mg daily; CAD/PAD (with aspirin): 2.5 mg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: AF: CrCl >50: 20 mg daily; CrCl 15–50: 15 mg daily; CrCl 15 or dialysis: FDA label allows 15 mg daily for AF but limited data; VTE: avoid if CrCl 30; hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C with coagulopathy): avoid
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding, bruising, back pain, epistaxis, GI bleeding (higher rate than warfarin in ROCKET-AF)
medications.contraindications: Active bleeding; severe hypersensitivity; prosthetic heart valves. MUST take 15 mg and 20 mg doses WITH FOOD (increases bioavailability by 39%). Reversal: andexanet alfa; 4-factor PCC. Avoid with strong dual CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors. ROCKET-AF: noninferior to warfarin for stroke prevention.

medications.mechanism: Directly and reversibly inhibits free and clot-bound thrombin; also inhibits thrombin-induced platelet aggregation; prodrug (dabigatran etexilate) converted to active dabigatran by esterases
medications.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism; NOT a CYP450 substrate; P-glycoprotein substrate (important for drug interactions)
medications.excretion: Renal (~80% unchanged) — MOST renal dependence among DOACs
medications.monitoring: Routine monitoring NOT required; aPTT provides qualitative assessment; ecarin clotting time (ECT) or dilute thrombin time (dTT) for quantitative assessment; renal function (critical — 80% renal excretion)
medications.maxDose: AF: 150 mg BID; VTE treatment: 150 mg BID (after 5–10 days parenteral anticoagulation); VTE prophylaxis (hip): 110 mg × 1 then 220 mg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: AF: CrCl >30: 150 mg BID; CrCl 15–30: 75 mg BID (FDA-approved; not studied in RE-LY); CrCl 15 or dialysis: CONTRAINDICATED; CrCl 30–50 with concomitant dronedarone/ketoconazole: 75 mg BID; hepatic impairment: no adjustment (not hepatically metabolized)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding, dyspepsia/GI symptoms (most common — ~11%; due to tartaric acid core), GI bleeding (higher rate than warfarin), gastritis
medications.contraindications: Active bleeding; prosthetic heart valves (RE-ALIGN trial — increased thromboembolism and bleeding); CrCl 15 or dialysis; concomitant P-gp inhibitors with CrCl 30. Reversal: idarucizumab (Praxbind) — specific monoclonal antibody fragment; FDA-approved. Capsules must be stored in original packaging (moisture-sensitive — degrades if removed). RE-LY trial: 150 mg BID superior to warfarin for stroke prevention; 110 mg BID noninferior with less bleeding.

medications.mechanism: Irreversibly acetylates COX-1 (and COX-2 at higher doses); blocks thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis in platelets → inhibits platelet aggregation for the lifespan of the platelet (~7–10 days); at higher doses also inhibits prostaglandin synthesis (anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antipyretic)
medications.durationOfAction: Antiplatelet effect lasts 7–10 days (platelet lifespan); analgesic/antipyretic 4–6 hrs
medications.halfLife: Aspirin ~15–20 min (rapidly hydrolyzed to salicylic acid); salicylic acid ~2–3 hrs (low dose) to 15–30 hrs (high dose — saturable metabolism)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic hydrolysis to salicylic acid; further conjugation (glycine, glucuronic acid); saturable at high doses (zero-order kinetics)
medications.excretion: Renal (as salicylic acid and metabolites; pH-dependent — alkaline urine increases excretion)
medications.monitoring: Signs of bleeding; GI symptoms; tinnitus (salicylism at high doses)
medications.maxDose: Antiplatelet: 81–325 mg daily (81 mg preferred for long-term use); Analgesic: 4 g/day; ACS loading: 162–325 mg (chewed, non-enteric)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Avoid in severe renal impairment (salicylate accumulation); avoid in severe hepatic impairment (coagulopathy); no specific dose adjustment for mild-moderate
medications.pregnancyLactation: Avoid in 3rd trimester (premature closure of ductus arteriosus); low-dose (81 mg) used for preeclampsia prevention
medications.sideEffects: GI bleeding/ulceration (most common serious), dyspepsia, tinnitus, Reye syndrome (children with viral illness — CONTRAINDICATED 19 yrs for viral illness), bruising, allergic reactions (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease — AERD)
medications.contraindications: Aspirin allergy/NSAID hypersensitivity; active GI bleeding; children/adolescents with viral illness (Reye syndrome); severe hepatic impairment. Low-dose aspirin (81 mg) preferred over higher doses for CV prevention (better safety profile with similar efficacy per PLATO, CURRENT-OASIS 7).

medications.mechanism: Prodrug requiring 2-step hepatic activation via CYP2C19 (primary), CYP3A4, CYP1A2, CYP2B6; active metabolite irreversibly binds P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets → inhibits ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation for platelet lifespan (~7–10 days)
medications.onset: PO — 2–6 hrs (loading dose accelerates); peak platelet inhibition 3–7 days without loading
medications.metabolism: Hepatic — 85% hydrolyzed by esterases to inactive metabolite; only ~15% undergoes 2-step CYP-mediated activation; CYP2C19 is rate-limiting — CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles (\2, *3) cause reduced activation ("poor metabolizers" — ~2% Caucasians, ~15% Chinese)
medications.monitoring: Platelet function testing NOT routinely recommended; CYP2C19 genotyping may guide therapy (FDA boxed warning); signs of bleeding
medications.maxDose: Loading: 300–600 mg; Maintenance: 75 mg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment (limited experience; bleeding diathesis)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding, bruising, GI symptoms, rash, TTP (rare — much less than ticlopidine), neutropenia (rare)
medications.contraindications: Active bleeding; hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNING: CYP2C19 poor metabolizers have reduced effectiveness — consider alternative P2Y12 inhibitor (prasugrel, ticagrelor). Omeprazole/esomeprazole reduce clopidogrel activation (CYP2C19 inhibition) — use pantoprazole instead. Discontinue 5 days before elective surgery.

medications.mechanism: NOT a prodrug — directly and reversibly binds P2Y12 ADP receptor at allosteric site (noncompetitive antagonist); does NOT require hepatic activation; faster onset and offset than clopidogrel; also inhibits adenosine reuptake via equilibrative nucleoside transporter 1 (ENT1) → increases local adenosine levels (may contribute to dyspnea and bradycardia side effects)
medications.onset: PO — 30 min; peak platelet inhibition ~2 hrs
medications.durationOfAction: 3–5 days (reversible binding — faster offset than clopidogrel)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary) to active metabolite AR-C124910XX (~30–40% activity of parent); P-glycoprotein substrate
medications.monitoring: Signs of bleeding; dyspnea; heart rate; uric acid (may increase)
medications.maxDose: ACS: 90 mg BID (after 180 mg loading); Long-term post-MI (>1 yr): 60 mg BID; Stroke/TIA: 90 mg BID × 30 days
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment (minimal renal excretion of parent); NOT studied in dialysis; mild hepatic impairment: no adjustment; moderate: use with caution; severe (Child-Pugh C): AVOID (not studied, likely increased exposure)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended
medications.sideEffects: Dyspnea (up to 14% — adenosine-mediated; usually mild, transient; rarely requires discontinuation), bleeding (more non-CABG-related bleeding than clopidogrel), bradycardia (ventricular pauses — adenosine effect), hyperuricemia, elevated creatinine (~11%), headache, nausea
medications.contraindications: Active bleeding; history of intracranial hemorrhage; severe hepatic impairment; concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir) or inducers (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine). MUST use aspirin ≤100 mg daily (higher aspirin doses reduce ticagrelor efficacy — PLATO finding). Discontinue 3–5 days before elective surgery. PLATO trial: superior to clopidogrel in ACS (reduced CV death, MI, stroke). NOT affected by CYP2C19 polymorphisms (advantage over clopidogrel).

medications.mechanism: Directly relaxes vascular smooth muscle (arterioles >> veins) by interfering with calcium movements; reduces peripheral vascular resistance and afterload; causes reflex tachycardia and increased cardiac output; increases renin activity; maintains renal and cerebral blood flow
medications.onset: PO — 20–30 min; IV — 10–20 min; IM — 10–30 min
medications.halfLife: 2–8 hrs (bimodal — fast vs slow acetylators; slow acetylators have higher drug levels and more lupus risk)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via N-acetyltransferase (NAT2 — genetic polymorphism: fast vs slow acetylators); extensive first-pass metabolism (bioavailability 26–50%)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; heart rate (reflex tachycardia); ANA titers (if prolonged use >6 months or doses >200 mg/day); CBC
medications.maxDose: PO: 300 mg/day (divided TID-QID); IV: 20–40 mg/dose; BiDil: 2 tablets TID (ISDN 20 mg/hydralazine 37.5 mg per tablet)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Reduce dose in renal impairment (accumulation of active metabolites); reduce dose in hepatic impairment (extensive hepatic metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; commonly used in pregnancy for hypertension (long safety record); no longer considered first-line (labetalol, nifedipine preferred)
medications.sideEffects: Reflex tachycardia, headache, flushing, palpitations, sodium/water retention (use with diuretic), drug-induced lupus-like syndrome (dose-dependent — risk increases >200 mg/day; more common in slow acetylators; presents with arthralgias, myalgias, fever, positive ANA; anti-histone antibodies; reversible on discontinuation), peripheral neuropathy (pyridoxine-responsive)
medications.contraindications: Mitral valve rheumatic heart disease; coronary artery disease (reflex tachycardia may worsen ischemia — use with beta-blocker); dissecting aortic aneurysm. Must use with beta-blocker (reflex tachycardia) and diuretic (sodium retention). BiDil FDA-approved for HFrEF in self-identified Black patients (A-HeFT trial).

medications.mechanism: Stimulates alpha-2 adrenergic receptors in brainstem (nucleus tractus solitarius) → reduces sympathetic outflow from CNS → decreases heart rate, peripheral vascular resistance, and blood pressure; also stimulates imidazoline receptors; reduces norepinephrine release
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; Patch — 2–3 days to steady state
medications.halfLife: 12–16 hrs (range 6–24 hrs); prolonged in renal impairment
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (~50%); ~50% excreted unchanged
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure; heart rate; signs of sedation; rebound hypertension on discontinuation
medications.maxDose: PO: 0.8 mg/day (divided BID); Patch: 0.3 mg/day (weekly); ADHD (Kapvay): 0.4 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment but prolonged half-life in renal impairment — use lower doses; NOT significantly removed by hemodialysis; caution in hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; crosses placenta; used in pregnancy when other agents fail
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/drowsiness (most common — up to 35%), dry mouth (40%), constipation, dizziness, bradycardia, REBOUND HYPERTENSION (most dangerous — if abruptly discontinued; can cause hypertensive crisis; must taper over 2–4 days), depression, contact dermatitis (patch — up to 50%), sexual dysfunction
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. CRITICAL: Never abruptly discontinue — must taper to avoid rebound hypertensive crisis (especially if on concurrent beta-blocker — beta-blocker should be discontinued several days before clonidine taper). Generally reserved as last-line antihypertensive due to CNS side effects. Also used off-label for: opioid withdrawal, ADHD, menopausal hot flashes, Tourette syndrome.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits SGLT2 in proximal renal tubule → blocks reabsorption of ~90% of filtered glucose → glycosuria (up to 70–80 g/day glucose excretion); also promotes natriuresis and osmotic diuresis; reduces preload and afterload; activates tubuloglomerular feedback → reduces intraglomerular pressure (renoprotective); reduces body weight (~2–3 kg) and BP (~3–5/1–2 mmHg)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation via UGT2B7, UGT1A3, UGT1A8, UGT1A9 to inactive metabolites; NOT significantly CYP450-dependent
medications.monitoring: Renal function; blood pressure; signs of volume depletion; ketones if symptoms of DKA; genital infections; blood glucose (if on insulin/sulfonylureas — reduce doses to avoid hypoglycemia)
medications.maxDose: T2DM: 25 mg daily; HF: 10 mg daily; CKD: 10 mg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: T2DM glycemic control: do not initiate if eGFR 30 (reduced glycemic efficacy); HF/CKD: can initiate down to eGFR 20; can continue if already on therapy even if eGFR falls below 20; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended (2nd/3rd trimester — fetal renal effects)
medications.sideEffects: Genital mycotic infections (most common — 6–12%), UTI, increased urination, volume depletion/hypotension, euglycemic DKA (rare but serious — hold before surgery), AKI (dehydration-related), Fournier gangrene (rare), hypoglycemia (when combined with insulin/SU)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; dialysis. EMPA-REG OUTCOME: 38% reduction in CV death in T2DM with CVD. EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved: reduced HF hospitalization. EMPA-KIDNEY: 28% reduction in kidney disease progression.

medications.mechanism: Same as empagliflozin — selectively inhibits SGLT2 in proximal tubule
medications.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation via UGT1A9 (primary) to inactive 3-O-glucuronide metabolite; NOT significantly CYP450-dependent
medications.excretion: Renal (~75% — 2% unchanged parent; mostly as glucuronide metabolite); fecal (~21%)
medications.monitoring: Same as empagliflozin
medications.maxDose: T2DM: 10 mg daily; HF: 10 mg daily; CKD: 10 mg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as empagliflozin — T2DM glycemic control: do not initiate if eGFR 25; HF/CKD: can initiate down to eGFR 25 (DAPA-CKD); can continue if eGFR falls below initiation threshold; severe renal impairment: systemic exposure ~200% higher but no dose adjustment needed for HF/CKD indications; severe hepatic impairment: not studied
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended
medications.sideEffects: Same as empagliflozin
medications.contraindications: Same as empagliflozin. DAPA-HF: 26% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization in HFrEF. DELIVER: benefit in HFpEF. DAPA-CKD: 39% reduction in kidney disease progression (including non-diabetic CKD).

medications.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin analogue with asparagine→glycine substitution at A21 and two arginine additions at B30; forms microprecipitates at physiologic pH after SC injection → slow, prolonged absorption; binds insulin receptors → promotes glucose uptake, inhibits hepatic glucose production, suppresses lipolysis
medications.durationOfAction: U-100: ~24 hrs (peakless); U-300: up to 36 hrs (flatter profile, less hypoglycemia)
medications.halfLife: Not clinically relevant (absorption-rate limited); effective duration ~24 hrs
medications.metabolism: Metabolized to active metabolites M1 and M2 at injection site and systemically
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (fasting glucose for dose titration); HbA1c q3 months; signs of hypoglycemia; injection sites (lipodystrophy)
medications.maxDose: Individualized; no absolute maximum; typical starting dose 10 U or 0.1–0.2 U/kg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Reduce dose in renal impairment (reduced insulin clearance — increased hypoglycemia risk); reduce dose in hepatic impairment (reduced gluconeogenesis and insulin metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; insulin is preferred treatment for diabetes in pregnancy; human insulin preferred over analogues by some guidelines, but glargine has extensive safety data
medications.sideEffects: Hypoglycemia (most common serious), weight gain, injection site reactions, lipodystrophy (rotate sites), allergic reactions (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypoglycemia; hypersensitivity. Do NOT mix with other insulins (acidic pH disrupts microprecipitate formation). U-100 and U-300 are NOT interchangeable (different PK profiles). Administer once daily at same time each day.

medications.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin analogue with proline→lysine swap at B28-B29; reduced self-association → faster absorption from SC site; same receptor binding and glucose-lowering effect as regular insulin but faster onset and shorter duration
medications.metabolism: Hepatic and renal (same as endogenous insulin)
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (pre- and post-prandial); HbA1c; signs of hypoglycemia
medications.maxDose: Individualized; based on insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio and correction factor
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as glargine — reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; safe in pregnancy
medications.sideEffects: Hypoglycemia, weight gain, injection site reactions, lipodystrophy, hypokalemia
medications.contraindications: Hypoglycemia; hypersensitivity. Administer 15–20 min before meals (or immediately after in young children/unpredictable eaters). Can be mixed with NPH (draw lispro first). Lyumjev has even faster onset (~5 min earlier peak) due to citrate and treprostinil excipients.

medications.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin; hexameric form must dissociate to monomers for absorption → slower onset than rapid-acting analogues; same mechanism as endogenous insulin
medications.metabolism: Hepatic and renal
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose; electrolytes (K⁺ — insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly); HbA1c
medications.maxDose: Individualized; DKA: 0.1–0.14 U/kg/hr IV infusion; Hyperkalemia: 10 U IV with dextrose
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as other insulins — reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; preferred insulin in pregnancy by some guidelines (longest safety record)
medications.sideEffects: Hypoglycemia, weight gain, hypokalemia (IV use), injection site reactions
medications.contraindications: Hypoglycemia. ONLY insulin that can be given IV. Administer 30 min before meals (SC). Humulin R U-500 available for severe insulin resistance (NOT interchangeable with U-100). Can be mixed with NPH.

medications.mechanism: Regular insulin complexed with protamine and zinc → forms crystalline suspension that delays absorption; provides intermediate-duration basal coverage with a peak (unlike glargine/detemir)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic and renal
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose; HbA1c; signs of hypoglycemia (especially at peak — 4–8 hrs)
medications.maxDose: Individualized; typically given BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Same as other insulins
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; safe in pregnancy; long safety record
medications.sideEffects: Hypoglycemia (higher risk than glargine/detemir — especially nocturnal, due to peak action), weight gain, injection site reactions, lipodystrophy
medications.contraindications: Hypoglycemia. Must resuspend before injection (roll gently — do NOT shake). Has a PEAK (4–8 hrs) — higher risk of nocturnal hypoglycemia vs long-acting analogues. Can be mixed with regular insulin or lispro. Less expensive than analogues — important for cost-sensitive patients. Usually requires BID dosing.

medications.mechanism: Reduces hepatic glucose production (primary — inhibits mitochondrial complex I and activates AMPK); increases peripheral insulin sensitivity; increases intestinal glucose utilization; stimulates GLP-1 secretion; does NOT stimulate insulin secretion → no hypoglycemia as monotherapy
medications.onset: PO — IR peak 2.5 hrs; XR peak 7 hrs
medications.metabolism: NOT metabolized — excreted unchanged; no hepatic metabolism, no metabolites identified in humans
medications.excretion: Renal (~90% of absorbed dose within 24 hrs via tubular secretion and glomerular filtration); renal clearance ~3.5× creatinine clearance
medications.monitoring: Renal function (eGFR before initiation and at least annually); B12 levels (long-term use depletes B12 — check periodically); blood glucose; HbA1c
medications.maxDose: IR: 2,550 mg/day (divided BID-TID); XR: 2,000 mg/day; Pediatric (≥10 yrs): 2,000 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR ≥45: no adjustment; eGFR 30–44: do not initiate (may continue if already on it at reduced dose); eGFR 30: CONTRAINDICATED (risk of lactic acidosis from drug accumulation); hold before iodinated contrast if eGFR 30–60; avoid in hepatic impairment (impaired lactate clearance)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; generally discontinued in pregnancy (insulin preferred); some guidelines support use in gestational diabetes
medications.sideEffects: GI (most common — diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, metallic taste; up to 25%; dose-related; take with food; XR better tolerated); lactic acidosis (rare — 0–0.084/1,000 patient-years; almost always with contraindicated comorbidities); B12 deficiency (long-term — up to 30%); weight neutral or slight weight loss
medications.contraindications: eGFR 30; metabolic acidosis including DKA; conditions predisposing to lactic acidosis (severe hepatic disease, excessive alcohol, acute HF, sepsis, shock). First-line oral agent for T2DM per all major guidelines. HbA1c reduction ~1.0–1.5%.

medications.mechanism: Stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells by binding sulfonylurea receptor (SUR1) on beta-cell membrane → closes ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels → depolarization → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis; requires functioning beta cells
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (extensive); primary metabolites are inactive hydroxylation products and polar conjugates; no significant first-pass metabolism
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (fasting and postprandial); HbA1c; signs of hypoglycemia; renal function
medications.maxDose: IR: 40 mg/day (divided BID if >15 mg); XL: 20 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Use with caution in renal impairment (hypoglycemia risk from reduced clearance of active drug); start 2.5 mg in elderly/hepatic impairment; hepatic impairment: reduce dose (extensive hepatic metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; insulin preferred in pregnancy
medications.sideEffects: Hypoglycemia (most common serious — lower risk than glyburide), weight gain (~2 kg), GI upset, dizziness, headache, photosensitivity, SIADH (rare), hemolytic anemia (rare)
medications.contraindications: T1DM; DKA; sulfonamide hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity debated). Administer 30 min before meals (IR). Shorter half-life than glyburide — preferred in elderly and renal impairment. HbA1c reduction ~1.0–1.5%.

medications.mechanism: Same as glipizide — binds SUR1 on beta cells; may also have extrapancreatic insulin-sensitizing effects (debated); binds to a different subunit of SUR1 than glyburide → faster association/dissociation
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 to active metabolite (M1, ~33% activity) and inactive metabolite (M2)
medications.monitoring: Same as glipizide
medications.maxDose: 8 mg/day (US); 6 mg/day (UK); little additional benefit above 4 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Start 1 mg in renal impairment (active metabolite accumulation); use with caution; hepatic impairment: start 1 mg (CYP2C9 metabolism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Same as glipizide; may have lower hypoglycemia risk than glyburide; fewer cardiovascular effects than glyburide
medications.contraindications: Same as glipizide. Once-daily dosing (convenience). HbA1c reduction ~1.0–1.5%.

medications.mechanism: Selectively and reversibly inhibits DPP-4 enzyme, which normally degrades incretin hormones (GLP-1 and GIP); increases active incretin levels 2–3 fold → glucose-dependent insulin secretion ↑, glucagon secretion ↓; does NOT cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy (glucose-dependent mechanism)
medications.onset: PO — peak 1–4 hrs; DPP-4 inhibition within 1 hr
medications.metabolism: Minimal — ~79% excreted unchanged; minor metabolism via CYP3A4 and CYP2C8 (6 trace metabolites with no significant DPP-4 inhibitory activity)
medications.excretion: Renal (~87% — predominantly unchanged via active tubular secretion); fecal (~13%)
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose; HbA1c; renal function (dose adjustment needed); signs of pancreatitis
medications.maxDose: 100 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: eGFR ≥45: 100 mg daily; eGFR 30–44: 50 mg daily; eGFR 30 (including ESRD/dialysis): 25 mg daily; mild-moderate hepatic impairment: no adjustment; severe hepatic impairment: not studied
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; upper respiratory infection, nasopharyngitis, headache; pancreatitis (rare — FDA warning; discontinue if suspected); joint pain (arthralgia — FDA warning); bullous pemphigoid (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis, angioedema, SJS reported); history of pancreatitis (relative). Weight neutral. HbA1c reduction ~0.5–0.8%. TECOS trial: CV safety confirmed (noninferior to placebo).

medications.brandNames: Victoza (T2DM — up to 1.8 mg), Saxenda (obesity — up to 3.0 mg)
medications.mechanism: Acylated human GLP-1 analogue (97% homology to native GLP-1); fatty acid side chain enables albumin binding → prolonged half-life; activates GLP-1 receptor → glucose-dependent insulin secretion ↑, glucagon secretion ↓, delayed gastric emptying, CNS appetite suppression (hypothalamic satiety centers)
medications.metabolism: Endogenous peptide degradation (DPP-4, NEP); NOT dependent on specific organ for elimination; no single organ is major route of elimination
medications.excretion: Renal (~6% as intact peptide); fecal (~5%); majority metabolized endogenously
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose; HbA1c; signs of pancreatitis; thyroid nodules (medullary thyroid carcinoma — boxed warning); heart rate; renal function (dehydration risk from GI side effects)
medications.maxDose: T2DM: 1.8 mg SC daily; Obesity: 3.0 mg SC daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal impairment (not renally eliminated); use with caution in severe renal impairment (limited experience; dehydration from GI effects may worsen renal function); no hepatic dose adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Not recommended
medications.sideEffects: GI (most common — nausea 20–40%, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation; dose-related; usually transient with slow titration), injection site reactions, headache, pancreatitis (rare), gallbladder disease (cholelithiasis), increased heart rate (~2–3 bpm), hypoglycemia (when combined with insulin/SU)
medications.contraindications: Personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC); MEN2 syndrome; hypersensitivity. FDA BOXED WARNING: thyroid C-cell tumors in rodents — relevance to humans unknown. LEADER trial: 13% reduction in MACE in T2DM with CVD. Weight loss ~5–8% (Saxenda). HbA1c reduction ~1.0–1.5%.

medications.brandNames: Ozempic (SC — T2DM), Wegovy (SC — obesity/CV risk; also oral 25 mg for obesity), Rybelsus (oral — T2DM)
medications.mechanism: Same as liraglutide — GLP-1 analogue (94% homology to native GLP-1); structural modifications (amino acid substitution at position 8 + C-18 fatty diacid chain) provide enhanced albumin binding and DPP-4 resistance → much longer half-life than liraglutide; most potent GLP-1 RA for weight loss and HbA1c reduction
medications.onset: SC — peak 1–3 days; Oral — peak 1 hr (fasting)
medications.metabolism: Proteolytic degradation of peptide backbone and sequential beta-oxidation of fatty acid side chain; NOT dependent on CYP450
medications.monitoring: Same as liraglutide
medications.maxDose: SC T2DM: 2 mg weekly; SC obesity/CV: 2.4 mg weekly; Oral T2DM (Rybelsus R1): 14 mg daily; Oral obesity (Wegovy): 25 mg daily
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No dose adjustment for renal or hepatic impairment; use with caution in severe renal impairment (dehydration risk)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Discontinue ≥2 months before planned pregnancy (long half-life)
medications.sideEffects: Same as liraglutide but potentially more GI effects at higher doses; nausea (up to 44%), vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, pancreatitis (rare), gallbladder disease, diabetic retinopathy complications (rapid glucose improvement — SUSTAIN-6), injection site reactions
medications.contraindications: Same as liraglutide — MTC/MEN2; hypersensitivity. Oral formulation: must take on empty stomach with ≤4 oz water, 30 min before food/other meds (SNAC absorption enhancer). SELECT trial: 20% reduction in MACE in overweight/obese adults with CVD (without diabetes). FLOW trial: 24% reduction in kidney disease progression in T2DM with CKD. STEP trials: weight loss 15–17% (SC 2.4 mg). HbA1c reduction ~1.5–1.8%.

medications.brandNames: Synthroid, Levoxyl, Unithroid, Tirosint (gel cap), Tirosint-SOL (liquid)
medications.mechanism: Synthetic L-thyroxine (T4); converted to active T3 by peripheral deiodinases (type 1 and type 2); T3 binds nuclear thyroid receptors → activates gene transcription → regulates metabolism, growth, development; ~80% of circulating T3 is derived from T4 deiodination
medications.onset: PO — clinical effect 3–5 days; peak effect 4–6 weeks at given dose
medications.durationOfAction: Long-acting (once-daily); T4 half-life allows stable levels
medications.halfLife: ~6–7 days (euthyroid); shorter in hyperthyroidism, longer in hypothyroidism
medications.metabolism: Peripheral deiodination to T3 (active) and reverse T3 (inactive); also conjugated in liver (glucuronidation, sulfation) and excreted in bile; enterohepatic recirculation
medications.monitoring: TSH (primary — for primary hypothyroidism; check 6–8 weeks after dose change, then annually when stable); free T4 (for secondary/tertiary hypothyroidism or initial monitoring); biotin interferes with immunoassays — stop 2 days before testing
medications.maxDose: Individualized; typical full replacement ~1.6 mcg/kg/day; elderly/cardiac disease: start 12.5–25 mcg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; absorption may be affected by GI conditions
medications.pregnancyLactation: Safe and essential — dose requirements increase ~30% in pregnancy (add 2 extra doses/week when pregnancy confirmed); monitor TSH q4 weeks until mid-gestation
medications.sideEffects: Overreplacement mimics hyperthyroidism: tachycardia, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, diarrhea, heat intolerance, bone loss (especially postmenopausal women), atrial fibrillation (elderly). Underreplacement: persistent hypothyroid symptoms
medications.contraindications: Uncorrected adrenal insufficiency (must replace cortisol BEFORE thyroid hormone — risk of adrenal crisis); acute MI (relative — start low dose); thyrotoxicosis. Take on empty stomach 30–60 min before breakfast with water only. Separate from calcium, iron, PPIs by ≥4 hrs. Narrow therapeutic index — maintain consistent brand/formulation.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), blocking iodine organification and coupling of iodotyrosines → reduces thyroid hormone synthesis (T3 and T4); does NOT block release of preformed hormone (takes weeks to deplete stores); may also have immunomodulatory effects (reduces TRAb levels over time in Graves disease)
medications.onset: PO — clinical improvement 1–2 weeks; euthyroidism typically 4–8 weeks
medications.halfLife: 4–6 hrs; duration of action exceeds half-life (intrathyroidal concentration)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic
medications.monitoring: TFTs (free T4, T3, TSH) q4–8 weeks initially, then q2–4 months once euthyroid; CBC (baseline — agranulocytosis risk); LFTs (baseline); WBC if fever/sore throat develops
medications.maxDose: 40 mg/day (severe hyperthyroidism); maintenance 5–10 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal dose adjustment; use with caution in hepatic impairment (hepatotoxicity risk — cholestatic pattern)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Preferred in 2nd/3rd trimester (PTU preferred in 1st trimester due to methimazole teratogenicity); methimazole associated with aplasia cutis and choanal/esophageal atresia in 1st trimester
medications.sideEffects: Minor: rash (5%), arthralgia, GI upset, taste disturbance. Major: agranulocytosis (0.1–0.5% — sudden onset; NOT dose-related; instruct patients to stop drug and get CBC if fever/sore throat), hepatotoxicity (cholestatic — less severe than PTU), vasculitis (ANCA-positive — rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; nursing mothers (relative — low levels in breast milk, generally considered safe at doses ≤20 mg/day). First-line antithyroid drug (except 1st trimester pregnancy). Treatment duration 12–18 months for Graves disease; ~50% remission rate.

medications.mechanism: Same as methimazole — inhibits TPO; ADDITIONALLY blocks peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 (inhibits type 1 deiodinase) — unique advantage in thyroid storm; may also have immunomodulatory effects
medications.onset: PO — clinical improvement 1–2 weeks; euthyroidism 4–8 weeks
medications.durationOfAction: 8 hrs (requires TID dosing — less convenient than methimazole)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (extensive)
medications.monitoring: Same as methimazole; LFTs more important (hepatotoxicity risk higher)
medications.maxDose: 600 mg/day (divided TID); thyroid storm: up to 1,000–1,200 mg/day; maintenance: 50 mg BID-TID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; AVOID in hepatic impairment (FDA boxed warning for hepatotoxicity)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Preferred in 1st trimester (less teratogenic than methimazole); switch to methimazole in 2nd trimester if possible; dose ratio PTU:methimazole = 20:1
medications.sideEffects: Same minor effects as methimazole; agranulocytosis (similar rate); HEPATOTOXICITY (FDA BOXED WARNING — fulminant hepatic necrosis, liver failure, death; more severe than methimazole; hepatocellular pattern); vasculitis (ANCA-positive — more common than methimazole)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; hepatic impairment. FDA BOXED WARNING: severe liver injury including fatal hepatic necrosis. Reserved for: 1st trimester pregnancy, methimazole intolerance, thyroid storm (blocks T4→T3 conversion). NOT first-line due to hepatotoxicity risk.

medications.mechanism: Identical to endogenous cortisol; binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors → translocates to nucleus → modulates gene transcription; anti-inflammatory (inhibits phospholipase A2, COX-2, cytokines); immunosuppressive; metabolic effects (gluconeogenesis, protein catabolism, fat redistribution); also has significant mineralocorticoid activity (sodium retention, potassium excretion)
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (CYP3A4 and 11β-HSD)
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose; electrolytes; blood pressure; signs of adrenal suppression; bone density (chronic use); growth (pediatric)
medications.maxDose: Physiologic replacement: 15–25 mg/day (divided BID-TID; 2/3 AM, 1/3 PM); Stress dosing: 100 mg IV bolus then 200 mg/24 hrs continuous infusion (or 50 mg IV q6h); Anti-inflammatory: variable
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; use with caution
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; crosses placenta but largely inactivated by placental 11β-HSD2; preferred corticosteroid in pregnancy for adrenal insufficiency
medications.sideEffects: (Chronic use) Cushing syndrome, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, immunosuppression, GI ulceration, myopathy, skin thinning, mood changes, insomnia, hypertension, hypokalemia, weight gain, cataracts, glaucoma
medications.contraindications: Systemic fungal infections (relative); live vaccines during immunosuppressive doses. Glucocorticoid potency: 1× (reference). Mineralocorticoid potency: 1× (significant — provides some mineralocorticoid coverage in adrenal insufficiency). Equivalence: hydrocortisone 20 mg = prednisone 5 mg = dexamethasone 0.75 mg.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug — converted to prednisolone (active) by hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1; same mechanism as hydrocortisone but 4× more potent anti-inflammatory effect with less mineralocorticoid activity
medications.halfLife: Plasma ~3.5 hrs (prednisone); prednisolone ~2–4 hrs; biologic ~18–36 hrs
medications.metabolism: Hepatic conversion to prednisolone (active); further hepatic metabolism
medications.monitoring: Same as hydrocortisone; blood glucose (especially in diabetics — significant hyperglycemic effect)
medications.maxDose: Variable by indication; typical anti-inflammatory: 5–60 mg/day; severe disease: 1–2 mg/kg/day; pulse therapy: up to 1 g/day IV methylprednisolone (equivalent)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; severe hepatic impairment may impair conversion to prednisolone (consider using prednisolone directly)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; crosses placenta but largely inactivated by placental 11β-HSD2
medications.sideEffects: Same as hydrocortisone; more pronounced with chronic use
medications.contraindications: Same as hydrocortisone. Glucocorticoid potency: 4× hydrocortisone. Mineralocorticoid potency: 0.8× hydrocortisone (minimal at anti-inflammatory doses). Equivalence: prednisone 5 mg = hydrocortisone 20 mg = dexamethasone 0.75 mg. Taper required if >7–14 days of use (adrenal suppression risk).

medications.mechanism: Same as hydrocortisone but 25–30× more potent anti-inflammatory effect with virtually NO mineralocorticoid activity; longest biologic half-life among common glucocorticoids; potent HPA axis suppression
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4
medications.monitoring: Same as hydrocortisone; blood glucose (potent hyperglycemic effect)
medications.maxDose: Variable; cerebral edema: 10 mg IV then 4 mg IV q6h; croup: 0.6 mg/kg × 1 (max 16 mg); COVID-19: 6 mg daily × 10 days; anti-inflammatory: 0.75–9 mg/day; Cushing test: 1 mg at 11 PM
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; NOT inactivated by placental 11β-HSD2 (crosses placenta actively — used for fetal lung maturation: betamethasone preferred but dexamethasone is alternative)
medications.sideEffects: Same as hydrocortisone; more potent HPA suppression; more pronounced metabolic effects (hyperglycemia, myopathy)
medications.contraindications: Same as hydrocortisone. Glucocorticoid potency: 25–30× hydrocortisone. Mineralocorticoid potency: 0 (no sodium retention — advantage in cerebral edema, no need for mineralocorticoid replacement in adrenal insufficiency). Equivalence: dexamethasone 0.75 mg = prednisone 5 mg = hydrocortisone 20 mg. RECOVERY trial: reduced 28-day mortality in hospitalized COVID-19 patients requiring O₂.

medications.mechanism: Potent mineralocorticoid — binds mineralocorticoid receptors in distal nephron → increases sodium reabsorption and potassium/hydrogen excretion; also has glucocorticoid activity (~10× hydrocortisone) but used primarily for mineralocorticoid replacement
medications.onset: PO — peak effect hours; full effect days
medications.metabolism: Hepatic
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure (sitting and standing — orthostatic hypotension indicates underreplacement); electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺); signs of edema (overreplacement); plasma renin (optional — upper normal range suggests adequate dosing)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; use with caution in renal impairment (sodium/water retention)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Hypertension, edema, hypokalemia, headache, weight gain (overreplacement); salt craving, orthostatic hypotension (underreplacement)
medications.contraindications: Systemic fungal infections. Used ONLY for mineralocorticoid replacement in primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) and salt-losing congenital adrenal hyperplasia. NOT needed in secondary/tertiary adrenal insufficiency (aldosterone secretion preserved via RAAS). Advise patients not to restrict salt intake. Increase dose 50–100% in hot weather/excessive sweating.

medications.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (proton pump) on parietal cell secretory surface; blocks the final common pathway of gastric acid secretion; prodrug activated in acidic environment of parietal cell canaliculus; forms covalent disulfide bond with cysteine residues on proton pump
medications.onset: PO — acid suppression within 1 hr; maximal effect 2–4 days of daily dosing (cumulative pump inhibition)
medications.durationOfAction: 24 hrs (irreversible binding — new pump synthesis required for acid recovery; ~50% pump turnover in 24 hrs)
medications.halfLife: 0.5–1 hr (plasma); duration of action far exceeds plasma half-life due to irreversible binding
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C19 (primary) and CYP3A4; time-dependent CYP2C19 autoinhibition → nonlinear PK (AUC increases disproportionately with repeated dosing); CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (~3% Caucasians, ~15–20% Asians) have 3–5× higher exposure
medications.monitoring: Symptom response; consider H. pylori testing before long-term use; Mg²⁺ (long-term — hypomagnesemia); B12 (long-term); bone density (chronic use — hip fracture risk); renal function (AIN risk)
medications.maxDose: GERD: 20 mg daily; Erosive esophagitis: 40 mg daily; Zollinger-Ellison: 120 mg TID; H. pylori: 20 mg BID (with antibiotics)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; hepatic impairment: consider dose reduction (increased bioavailability); Asian patients may need lower doses (CYP2C19 polymorphism)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated; headache, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, flatulence. Long-term risks: C. difficile infection, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, bone fractures (hip/wrist/spine), acute interstitial nephritis, fundic gland polyps,
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity; concomitant rilpivirine. Take 30–60 min before first meal of the day. Highest drug interaction potential among PPIs due to CYP2C19 inhibition.

medications.mechanism: Same as omeprazole — irreversibly inhibits H⁺/K⁺-ATPase; binds preferentially to Cys813 and Cys822 (greater selectivity than omeprazole); activated at slightly lower pH than omeprazole
medications.onset: PO — acid suppression within 2.5 hrs; IV — rapid; maximal effect 2–4 days
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C19 and CYP3A4; also metabolized by sulphotransferase (unique among PPIs); LOWER affinity for CYP450 enzymes than omeprazole → fewer drug interactions
medications.monitoring: Same as omeprazole
medications.maxDose: PO: 40 mg daily (GERD); 40 mg BID (Zollinger-Ellison); IV: 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr continuous infusion (GI bleed)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; hepatic impairment: max 40 mg/day (delayed elimination); no dose adjustment for mild-moderate hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Same as omeprazole class effects
medications.contraindications: Same as omeprazole. KEY ADVANTAGE: lowest drug interaction potential among PPIs — preferred PPI when patient is on clopidogrel, warfarin, or other CYP2C19-metabolized drugs. Available IV (advantage for NPO patients, GI bleed). No significant interaction with clopidogrel.

medications.mechanism: Competitive, reversible inhibitor of histamine H2 receptors on parietal cells; inhibits basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion; suppresses both acid concentration and volume; does NOT affect pepsin secretion independently (proportional to volume)
medications.onset: PO — within 1 hr; peak 1–3 hrs; IV — within 30 min
medications.metabolism: Minimal first-pass metabolism; 25–30% of oral dose recovered unchanged in urine; only identified metabolite is S-oxide
medications.excretion: Renal (65–70% — significant unchanged drug via tubular excretion); metabolic (30–35%)
medications.monitoring: Symptom response; renal function (dose adjustment needed)
medications.maxDose: GERD: 20 mg BID; DU/GU: 40 mg daily; Zollinger-Ellison: 160 mg q6h; OTC: 20 mg BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 50: reduce dose by 50% or increase dosing interval to q36–48h; no hepatic dose adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Generally very well tolerated; headache (most common), dizziness, constipation, diarrhea; thrombocytopenia (rare); confusion in elderly/renal impairment (rare); tachyphylaxis with chronic use (tolerance develops within 2 weeks — acid secretion returns toward baseline)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Less potent acid suppression than PPIs — PPIs preferred for erosive esophagitis and H. pylori eradication. No significant CYP450 interactions (advantage). Tachyphylaxis limits long-term efficacy. Pediatric dosing: 0.5 mg/kg/dose (max 40 mg) once or twice daily.

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks serotonin 5-HT3 receptors both peripherally on vagal nerve terminals in the GI tract and centrally in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (area postrema); NOT a dopamine receptor antagonist (no extrapyramidal effects); cytotoxic chemotherapy and radiation cause serotonin release from enterochromaffin cells → stimulates vagal afferents via 5-HT3 receptors → vomiting reflex; ondansetron blocks this pathway
medications.onset: PO — 30 min; IV — immediate; ODT — 15–30 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (primary), CYP1A2, CYP2D6; extensive first-pass metabolism
medications.monitoring: ECG in patients at risk for QT prolongation; electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺); LFTs
medications.maxDose: PO: 24 mg (single dose for highly emetogenic chemo); IV: 16 mg (FDA removed 32 mg IV dose due to QT risk); Severe hepatic impairment: max 8 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal dose adjustment; severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): max 8 mg/day (reduced clearance)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; some data suggest possible association with cleft palate (debated); widely used in pregnancy for hyperemesis
medications.sideEffects: Headache (most common — 11–27%), constipation (dose-related — slows colonic transit), dizziness, fatigue, QT prolongation (dose-dependent — risk of torsades de pointes), transient LFT elevations, serotonin syndrome (when combined with serotonergic drugs)
medications.contraindications: Concomitant apomorphine (severe hypotension); hypersensitivity to other 5-HT3 antagonists. No extrapyramidal effects (major advantage over metoclopramide/promethazine). ODT formulation dissolves on tongue without water.

medications.drugClass: Dopamine D2 receptor antagonist / prokinetic / antiemetic
medications.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors centrally (chemoreceptor trigger zone — antiemetic) and peripherally (GI tract — prokinetic); also stimulates 5-HT4 receptors (enhances acetylcholine release → increases gastric motility, accelerates gastric emptying, increases LES tone); weak 5-HT3 antagonist at high doses
medications.onset: PO — 30–60 min; IV — 1–3 min; IM — 10–15 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (CYP2D6 — minor); ~20% excreted unchanged
medications.monitoring: Signs of tardive dyskinesia (irreversible — FDA boxed warning); extrapyramidal symptoms; mental status; prolactin levels if symptomatic
medications.maxDose: 10 mg QID (40 mg/day); FDA recommends treatment duration ≤12 weeks (tardive dyskinesia risk)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: CrCl 40: reduce dose by 50%; no specific hepatic adjustment but use with caution
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Drowsiness/fatigue (most common — 10%), restlessness, extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia — especially young women; akathisia), TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (FDA BOXED WARNING — irreversible; risk increases with duration and cumulative dose; more common in elderly women), hyperprolactinemia (galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia), depression, neuroleptic malignant syndrome (rare), QT prolongation
medications.contraindications: GI obstruction/perforation/hemorrhage; pheochromocytoma; seizure disorders; concomitant drugs causing EPS. FDA BOXED WARNING: tardive dyskinesia — avoid treatment >12 weeks except in rare cases. Prokinetic effect useful for gastroparesis and post-surgical ileus.

medications.mechanism: Competitive H1 receptor antagonist (antihistaminic); also has anticholinergic, sedative, antiemetic, and anti-motion sickness effects; weak dopamine D2 antagonist (~1/10 of chlorpromazine); antiemetic effect via H1 blockade and anticholinergic effects on vestibular system and CTZ
medications.onset: PO — 20 min; IV — 5 min; IM — 20 min; PR — 20 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic; sulfoxides of promethazine and N-desmethylpromethazine are predominant metabolites
medications.monitoring: Respiratory status (respiratory depression risk, especially with opioids); blood pressure; signs of tissue necrosis (IV administration)
medications.maxDose: 25 mg q4–6h (nausea); 50 mg (sedation); Obstetric: max 100 mg/24 hrs; Pediatric: 1.1 mg/kg (≥2 yrs only)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment; use with caution in hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; widely used in pregnancy for nausea (long safety record)
medications.sideEffects: Sedation/drowsiness (most common — desired effect in some settings), dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation (anticholinergic effects), respiratory depression (especially with opioids), hypotension, extrapyramidal symptoms (less than metoclopramide), photosensitivity, NMS (rare), lowered seizure threshold
medications.contraindications: Children 2 years (FDA BOXED WARNING — fatal respiratory depression); intra-arterial injection (SEVERE tissue necrosis, gangrene — FDA BOXED WARNING); SC injection (chemical irritation, tissue damage); comatose patients; concomitant CNS depressants in children. IV administration: must dilute, give slowly (max 25 mg/min), preferably via large-bore vein; IM preferred route. Deep IM injection preferred over IV due to tissue necrosis risk.

medications.mechanism: Activates mu-opioid receptors in intestinal wall → slows intestinal motility, increases intestinal transit time, increases water and electrolyte absorption, increases anal sphincter tone; does NOT cross blood-brain barrier at therapeutic doses (P-glycoprotein substrate — actively effluxed from CNS) → no central opioid effects at recommended doses
medications.durationOfAction: 4–6 hrs (initial); up to 16 hrs with repeated dosing
medications.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 and CYP2C8; extensive first-pass metabolism (systemic bioavailability ~0.3%)
medications.excretion: Fecal (primarily — as unchanged drug and metabolites); renal (2%)
medications.monitoring: Stool frequency; signs of ileus/toxic megacolon; cardiac monitoring if overdose suspected
medications.maxDose: Acute diarrhea: 4 mg initially, then 2 mg after each loose stool; max 16 mg/day (OTC) or 8 mg/day (self-treatment); Chronic diarrhea: 16 mg/day
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific renal adjustment; use with caution in hepatic impairment (reduced first-pass metabolism → increased systemic exposure)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C
medications.sideEffects: Constipation (most common), abdominal cramps, nausea, dizziness, dry mouth; at SUPRATHERAPEUTIC doses: QT prolongation, torsades de pointes, ventricular tachycardia, cardiac arrest (FDA safety alert 2018 — abuse potential as "poor man's methadone")
medications.contraindications: Acute dysentery (bloody diarrhea with fever); acute ulcerative colitis; bacterial enterocolitis (Salmonella, Shigella, C. difficile — may worsen by preventing toxin clearance); abdominal distension; ileus; children 2 years. Do NOT use in infectious diarrhea where organism eradication is needed. Safe and effective OTC antidiarrheal when used as directed.

medications.mechanism: Inert, non-absorbable, non-metabolized polymer that sequesters water in the intestinal lumen via osmotic effect → increases stool water content, softens stool, increases stool volume → stimulates peristalsis; NOT fermented by colonic bacteria (less gas/bloating than lactulose)
medications.onset: PO — 24–48 hrs (constipation); 1–4 hrs (bowel prep at high doses)
medications.durationOfAction: Variable (effect lasts as long as drug is present in colon)
medications.metabolism: NOT absorbed or metabolized
medications.monitoring: Stool frequency and consistency; electrolytes (prolonged use — rare electrolyte disturbances); hydration status
medications.maxDose: Constipation: 17 g (1 capful) daily dissolved in 4–8 oz liquid; no clear maximum dose; Bowel prep: 240 mL q10 min until 4 L consumed
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No adjustment needed (not absorbed); safe in renal impairment (unlike magnesium-based laxatives)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; limited data but considered relatively safe (not absorbed)
medications.sideEffects: Diarrhea (dose-related), bloating (less than lactulose), abdominal cramping, nausea, flatulence; electrolyte disturbances (rare — hypokalemia, hypernatremia with excessive diarrhea)
medications.contraindications: Known or suspected bowel obstruction; hypersensitivity. First-line osmotic laxative — superior to lactulose for chronic constipation (Cochrane review: better stool frequency, stool form, abdominal pain, and need for additional medications). OTC availability. Response shown to be durable over 6 months. Ensure adequate fluid intake.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic non-absorbable disaccharide (β-galactosido-fructose); NOT digested in small intestine; reaches colon intact where colonic bacteria metabolize it to lactic acid, acetic acid, and other short-chain organic acids → lowers colonic pH → osmotic effect draws water into lumen (laxative); for hepatic encephalopathy: acidic pH converts ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4⁺) which cannot be absorbed → trapped in colon and excreted; also promotes growth of ammonia-consuming bacteria (Lactobacillus)
medications.onset: PO — 24–48 hrs (constipation); 24–48 hrs (hepatic encephalopathy — may take 2–3 days for full effect)
medications.metabolism: NOT absorbed; metabolized by colonic bacteria to organic acids
medications.monitoring: Constipation: stool frequency. HE: mental status, serum ammonia (goal 2–3 soft stools/day), electrolytes (hyponatremia, hypokalemia with excessive diarrhea)
medications.maxDose: Constipation: 40 g (60 mL)/day; HE: 20–30 g (30–45 mL) q1–2h until initial bowel movement, then titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day (typical maintenance 20–30 g BID-TID); HE rectal enema: 200 g in 700 mL water/saline, retain 30–60 min
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No renal adjustment; safe in hepatic impairment (primary indication for HE)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; only osmotic laxative studied in pregnancy
medications.sideEffects: Bloating and flatulence (most common — dose-dependent; due to bacterial fermentation; limits use), abdominal cramping, diarrhea, nausea; electrolyte disturbances with excessive diarrhea (hyponatremia, hypokalemia); hyperglycemia (contains galactose and lactose — caution in diabetics)
medications.contraindications: Galactosemia; patients requiring low-galactose diet. Less effective and less well tolerated than PEG for constipation (more bloating/gas). Primary role: hepatic encephalopathy (first-line treatment and prophylaxis). For constipation: consider if PEG unavailable or in pregnancy.

medications.mechanism: Aluminum hydroxide salt of sulfated sucrose; in acidic environment (pH 4), polymerizes and cross-links to form viscous, sticky paste that selectively binds to ulcer crater (proteinaceous exudate at ulcer base); forms protective barrier against acid, pepsin, and bile salts; also stimulates prostaglandin synthesis, mucus and bicarbonate secretion, and epidermal growth factor binding; inhibits pepsin activity by ~32%; adsorbs bile salts; minimal acid-neutralizing capacity (~14–16 mEq/g)
medications.onset: PO — forms protective barrier within 1 hr
medications.metabolism: NOT significantly absorbed or metabolized
medications.excretion: Fecal (primarily); small amounts absorbed excreted renally
medications.monitoring: Symptom response; aluminum levels in renal impairment (chronic use)
medications.maxDose: Active DU: 1 g QID (1 hr before meals and at bedtime); Maintenance: 1 g BID
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Use with caution in renal impairment (aluminum accumulation risk — aluminum toxicity, encephalopathy, osteomalacia); avoid chronic use in dialysis patients; no hepatic adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Constipation (most common — 2%), dry mouth, nausea, GI discomfort, bezoar formation (rare); aluminum accumulation in renal failure
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Must take on EMPTY STOMACH (requires acidic pH to activate). Separates from many drugs by binding them in GI tract — give other medications 2 hrs before or after sucralfate (especially fluoroquinolones, phenytoin, digoxin, ketoconazole, levothyroxine, warfarin). Largely replaced by PPIs but still used for stress ulcer prophylaxis in ICU (no acid suppression → may reduce pneumonia risk vs PPIs), radiation proctitis, bile reflux gastritis.

medications.brandNames: Asacol HD (DR tablets), Delzicol (DR capsules), Pentasa (ER capsules), Lialda (DR tablets), Apriso (ER capsules), Rowasa (enema), Canasa (suppository)
medications.mechanism: Not fully understood; topical anti-inflammatory effect on colonic epithelial cells; inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase pathways → reduces prostaglandin and leukotriene production in colonic mucosa; may also scavenge free radicals, inhibit NF-κB, and modulate immune cell function; acts locally in GI tract (systemic absorption is NOT the goal)
medications.onset: PO — variable (depends on formulation and release site); Rectal — 3–21 days for clinical improvement
medications.halfLife: Mesalamine ~0.5–1.5 hrs (plasma); N-acetyl-5-ASA (major metabolite) ~5–10 hrs; plasma levels are NOT indicative of efficacy (local action)
medications.metabolism: Intestinal and hepatic N-acetylation to N-acetyl-5-ASA (inactive metabolite); ~20–30% of oral dose absorbed systemically
medications.excretion: Renal (absorbed portion — as N-acetyl-5-ASA); fecal (unabsorbed portion — active drug at site of action)
medications.monitoring: Renal function (baseline and periodically — nephrotoxicity risk); CBC; LFTs; symptom response; colonoscopy per guidelines
medications.maxDose: Formulation-dependent; Asacol HD: 4.8 g/day; Lialda: 4.8 g/day; Pentasa: 4 g/day; Delzicol: 2.4 g/day; Rowasa enema: 4 g/60 mL nightly; Canasa suppository: 1 g nightly
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Use with caution in renal impairment (nephrotoxicity — interstitial nephritis, nephrotic syndrome); avoid in severe renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B; generally considered safe in pregnancy (benefits outweigh risks in UC)
medications.sideEffects: Headache, abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea (paradoxical — may mimic UC flare), flatulence; nephrotoxicity (interstitial nephritis — rare but serious; monitor renal function); hepatotoxicity (rare); pancreatitis (rare); pericarditis/myocarditis (rare — hypersensitivity); blood dyscrasias (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to mesalamine or salicylates; severe renal impairment. Different formulations release drug at different GI sites: Pentasa (small bowel + colon), Asacol HD/Delzicol (terminal ileum + colon — pH-dependent release at pH ≥6), Lialda (colon — pH ≥7), Apriso (colon — pH ≥6). Rectal formulations (enema, suppository) for left-sided/distal UC. First-line for mild-moderate ulcerative colitis (induction and maintenance).

medications.brandNames: ProAir HFA/RespiClick/Digihaler, Ventolin HFA, Proventil HFA
medications.mechanism: Selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist; stimulates adenylyl cyclase → increases intracellular cAMP → relaxes bronchial smooth muscle; also inhibits mast cell mediator release, increases mucociliary clearance, decreases vascular permeability; preferential beta-2 selectivity (respiratory tract) over beta-1 (cardiac), though cardiac effects occur at higher doses
medications.onset: Inhaled — 5–15 min; Nebulized — 5–15 min; PO — 30 min
medications.metabolism: Hepatic (sulfate conjugation to inactive metabolite); GI tract (PO)
medications.excretion: Renal (~70% within 24 hrs — as unchanged drug and metabolites)
medications.monitoring: Heart rate; blood pressure; serum potassium (beta-agonists cause intracellular K⁺ shift → hypokalemia); blood glucose; symptom response; frequency of rescue use (>2 days/week suggests inadequate control)
medications.maxDose: MDI: 2 puffs q4–6h PRN (rescue); acute exacerbation: 4–8 puffs q20min × 3; Nebulizer: 2.5–5 mg q20min × 3 (acute); Continuous nebulization: 10–15 mg/hr
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category C; widely used in pregnancy (long safety record)
medications.sideEffects: Tremor (most common — beta-2 mediated skeletal muscle effect), tachycardia, palpitations, nervousness, headache, hypokalemia (dose-dependent), hyperglycemia, paradoxical bronchospasm (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Rescue inhaler — should NOT be used as maintenance therapy. Increasing use indicates worsening asthma control. Levalbuterol (Xopenex) is R-enantiomer — theoretically fewer cardiac side effects but clinical advantage debated.

medications.brandNames: Atrovent HFA (MDI), Atrovent (nasal spray); Combivent Respimat (with albuterol)
medications.mechanism: Competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic M1, M2, and M3 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle → bronchodilation; blocks vagally-mediated bronchoconstriction; also reduces mucus secretion; does NOT cross blood-brain barrier (quaternary ammonium compound); non-selective muscarinic blockade (blocks M2 feedback inhibitory receptors — less ideal than tiotropium)
medications.metabolism: Minimal; partially metabolized by ester hydrolysis
medications.monitoring: Symptom response; signs of anticholinergic effects
medications.maxDose: MDI: 2 puffs QID; Nebulizer: 0.5 mg q6–8h; Acute exacerbation: 0.5 mg q20min × 3 (with albuterol)
medications.renalHepaticDosing: No specific dose adjustment
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category B
medications.sideEffects: Dry mouth (most common), cough, headache, nausea, blurred vision (if sprayed in eyes — mydriasis, acute angle-closure glaucoma), urinary retention, constipation, paradoxical bronchospasm (rare)
medications.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to atropine or its derivatives; hypersensitivity to soy lecithin or related food products (some formulations). Caution in narrow-angle glaucoma, prostatic hypertrophy, bladder-neck obstruction. In COPD: used as rescue bronchodilator (often combined with albuterol — Combivent). In acute asthma exacerbation: added to albuterol for severe exacerbations (additive bronchodilation). NOT recommended as monotherapy for asthma maintenance.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase → blocks purine and thymidylate synthesis → antiproliferative and anti-inflammatory effects; at low doses, increases adenosine release (anti-inflammatory)
medications.indications: Rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, JIA, ectopic pregnancy, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, NHL, osteosarcoma, choriocarcinoma, many off-label autoimmune uses
medications.dosing:
- RA: start 7.5–15 mg PO once weekly; titrate to ≥15 mg/week within 4–6 weeks per ACR 2021; max 20–25 mg/week; switch to SC if oral not tolerated or inadequate response
- Psoriasis: 10–25 mg PO once weekly
- JIA: 10 mg/m² PO once weekly
- Oncology (high-dose): 12 g/m² IV with leucovorin rescue (osteosarcoma)
Route: PO, SC, IM, IV, intrathecal
medications.monitoring:
CBC with differential, LFTs, creatinine at baseline then every 2–4 weeks initially, then every 1–3 months; hepatitis B/C screening before initiation
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity (fibrosis/cirrhosis with cumulative dose), pneumonitis, mucositis, teratogenicity (category X), nephrotoxicity (high-dose)
medications.sideEffects: Myelosuppression (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia), hepatotoxicity (fibrosis, cirrhosis — risk increases with cumulative dose and alcohol), stomatitis/mucositis, nausea, pulmonary toxicity (interstitial pneumonitis), nephrotoxicity, teratogenicity (Category X)
medications.watchFor: Always co-prescribe folic acid 1 mg daily (or folinic acid weekly) to reduce adverse effects. Split dose over 24 hours or switch to SC if oral not tolerated. Contraindicated in pregnancy — discontinue ≥3 months before conception. Reduce dose in renal impairment. Drug interactions: NSAIDs, TMP-SMX, proton pump inhibitors (increase methotrexate levels)

medications.mechanism: Prodrug metabolized in liver to active alkylating metabolites → cross-links DNA → cytotoxic to resting and dividing lymphocytes; impairs humoral and cellular immunity
