medications.indications: Lupus nephritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis, severe autoimmune diseases, breast cancer, lymphoma, leukemia, ovarian cancer, conditioning for HCT
medications.dosing:
- IV pulse (lupus nephritis — Euro-Lupus): 500 mg IV every 2 weeks × 6 doses
- IV pulse (NIH/high-dose): 750–1000 mg/m² IV monthly × 6 months (max 1200 mg/dose)
- Oral: 1–2 mg/kg/day (autoimmune); 2 mg/kg/day (vasculitis)
- High-dose immunoablative: 50 mg/kg/day × 4 days (aplastic anemia, refractory autoimmune)
Route: IV, PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC with differential weekly × 4 weeks then monthly; urinalysis with each infusion; urine cytology if cumulative dose >36 g; pregnancy test before each infusion
KEY TOXICITIES: Hemorrhagic cystitis (acrolein metabolite), myelosuppression, gonadal toxicity (premature ovarian failure, azoospermia), increased malignancy risk (bladder cancer, lymphoma, leukemia), teratogenicity, infections
medications.sideEffects: Myelosuppression (neutropenia — dose-limiting), hemorrhagic cystitis (acrolein metabolite — prevent with MESNA and aggressive hydration), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, gonadal toxicity (premature ovarian failure, azoospermia), secondary malignancies (bladder cancer, leukemia, lymphoma — risk increases with cumulative dose >100 g), cardiotoxicity (high-dose), pulmonary fibrosis, teratogenicity
medications.watchFor: IV preferred over oral (lower cumulative dose, less bladder/malignancy toxicity). Hydration pre/post infusion. MESNA for high-dose regimens (not routinely needed for standard IV pulse per ACR 2025). Limit duration to 12 months when possible. Fertility counseling/preservation before treatment. PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX for all patients. Transition to maintenance agent (azathioprine, MMF) once remission achieved (3–6 months)

medications.mechanism: Prodrug converted to 6-mercaptopurine → inhibits purine synthesis → suppresses T-cell and B-cell proliferation
medications.indications: Renal transplant rejection prophylaxis, RA, SLE, IBD (off-label), autoimmune hepatitis, myasthenia gravis, vasculitis maintenance
medications.dosing:
- Transplant: 3–5 mg/kg/day initially, maintenance 1–3 mg/kg/day
- RA: start 1 mg/kg/day (50–100 mg), increase by 0.5 mg/kg/day every 4 weeks; max 2.5 mg/kg/day
- IBD/autoimmune: 2–2.5 mg/kg/day
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC weekly × 1 month, biweekly × 2 months, then monthly. LFTs periodically. TPMT and NUDT15 genotyping/phenotyping before initiation (homozygous deficiency → consider alternative therapy; heterozygous → dose reduce)
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting), hepatotoxicity, GI intolerance (nausea, vomiting — hypersensitivity reaction), pancreatitis, increased infection risk, increased malignancy risk (lymphoma, skin cancer)
medications.sideEffects: Myelosuppression (leukopenia — dose-limiting, especially in TPMT-deficient patients), hepatotoxicity, nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, pancreatitis, idiosyncratic flu-like reaction (15–20%, occurs within 2 weeks — requires discontinuation), increased infection risk, increased malignancy risk (lymphoma — dose/duration dependent)
medications.watchFor: Reduce dose by 67–75% if co-administered with allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor blocks azathioprine metabolism → severe myelosuppression). Safe in pregnancy (one of few immunosuppressants considered compatible). Therapeutic response takes 6–8 weeks; minimum 12-week trial before considering refractory

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits IMPDH → blocks de novo purine synthesis in lymphocytes (which lack salvage pathway) → suppresses T-cell and B-cell proliferation
medications.indications: Transplant rejection prophylaxis (kidney, heart, liver), lupus nephritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis maintenance, myasthenia gravis, pemphigus, other autoimmune diseases
medications.dosing:
- Kidney transplant: 1 g PO BID (adults); 600 mg/m² PO BID (pediatric, max 2 g/day)
- Heart/liver transplant: 1.5 g PO BID
- Lupus nephritis (induction): 2–3 g/day PO divided BID
- Lupus nephritis (maintenance): 1–2 g/day PO divided BID
- Myfortic (enteric-coated mycophenolic acid): 720 mg PO BID = 1 g MMF BID
Route: PO, IV
medications.monitoring:
CBC with differential weekly × 1 month, biweekly × 2 months, then monthly. LFTs periodically
KEY TOXICITIES: GI (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting — most common), myelosuppression (leukopenia, anemia), increased infection risk (CMV, BK virus), teratogenicity (category D — REMS program)
medications.sideEffects: GI effects (diarrhea — most common, nausea, vomiting), myelosuppression (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia), increased infection risk (CMV disease more common than with azathioprine), teratogenicity (Category D — REMS program required)
medications.watchFor: Teratogenic — contraindicated in pregnancy; requires negative pregnancy test and two forms of contraception. Take on empty stomach for optimal absorption (MMF). Do not crush tablets. Enteric-coated mycophenolic acid (Myfortic) may reduce GI side effects. Not interchangeable with MMF without physician supervision. Dose reduce for neutropenia (ANC 1300)

medications.mechanism: Binds CD20 on B lymphocytes → complement-dependent cytotoxicity, antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, and direct apoptosis → B-cell depletion
medications.indications: NHL (DLBCL, follicular), CLL, RA (after TNF inhibitor failure), GPA/MPA (ANCA vasculitis), pemphigus vulgaris, off-label for many autoimmune diseases (SLE, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, ITP, MS, myasthenia gravis)
medications.dosing:
- NHL: 375 mg/m² IV weekly × 4–8 doses (or with CHOP)
- CLL: 375 mg/m² cycle 1, then 500 mg/m² cycles 2–6 with FC
- RA: 1000 mg IV × 2 doses separated by 2 weeks; repeat every 24 weeks (not sooner than Q16 weeks); given with methotrexate
- GPA/MPA induction: 375 mg/m² IV weekly × 4 weeks with glucocorticoids
- GPA/MPA maintenance: 500 mg IV at months 0, 2 weeks, 6, 12, 18 (MAINRITSAN protocol)
- Pemphigus vulgaris: 1000 mg IV × 2 doses separated by 2 weeks; maintenance 500 mg IV at months 12 and 18
Route: IV (infusion)
medications.monitoring:
CBC before each course; HBsAg and anti-HBc screening before initiation (risk of HBV reactivation); quantitative immunoglobulins periodically
KEY TOXICITIES: Infusion-related reactions (premedicate with acetaminophen, antihistamine, methylprednisolone 100 mg IV), infections (HBV reactivation, PML — rare), late-onset neutropenia, hypogammaglobulinemia, mucocutaneous reactions (rare — Stevens-Johnson)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Fatal infusion-related reactions (80% with first infusion), severe mucocutaneous reactions (SJS/TEN), HBV reactivation (fulminant hepatitis, death), progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)
- Common: Infusion reactions (fever, chills, rigors, hypotension — 77% first infusion, decreasing with subsequent), infections (bacterial, viral, fungal), cytopenias (lymphopenia 40%, neutropenia 6%), hypogammaglobulinemia
- Serious: Late-onset neutropenia, cardiac events, bowel perforation, tumor lysis syndrome (in high tumor burden)
medications.watchFor: Premedicate before every infusion. Screen for HBV before initiation — antiviral prophylaxis if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+. PCP and herpes prophylaxis for CLL patients during and 12 months after treatment. Live vaccines contraindicated. B-cell recovery takes 6–12 months after last dose. Biosimilars available

medications.mechanism: Accumulates in lysosomes → raises pH → inhibits antigen processing and TLR signaling → reduces cytokine production; anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, antithrombotic, and metabolic effects
medications.indications: SLE (cornerstone — all patients unless contraindicated), RA, malaria prophylaxis/treatment, chronic discoid lupus
medications.dosing:
- SLE: 200–400 mg PO daily (max 5 mg/kg/day of actual body weight to minimize retinal toxicity)
- RA: initial 400–600 mg PO daily; maintenance 200–400 mg PO daily
- Malaria prophylaxis: 400 mg PO once weekly (start 2 weeks before travel)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Baseline ophthalmologic exam within first year, then annual screening after 5 years (or sooner if risk factors: renal impairment, tamoxifen use, high dose, >5 years use) per AAO 2016. OCT and visual field testing
KEY TOXICITIES: Retinal toxicity (bull's-eye maculopathy — irreversible; risk increases after 5 years and cumulative dose), QTc prolongation, cardiomyopathy (rare, with long-term use), GI symptoms, skin hyperpigmentation, myopathy
medications.sideEffects: Retinal toxicity (irreversible "bull's eye" maculopathy — risk increases after 5 years and cumulative dose >1000 g; dose >5 mg/kg/day is major risk factor), GI upset (nausea, diarrhea), skin hyperpigmentation, neuromyopathy (rare, long-term), QT prolongation, cardiomyopathy (rare but serious with long-term use)
medications.watchFor: Should NOT be discontinued in SLE even during remission (reduces flares, thrombosis, organ damage, and mortality). Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Dose should not exceed 5 mg/kg/day actual body weight. Retinal toxicity risk: ~1% at 5–7 years, ~2% at 10 years, rising to ~20% after 20 years at higher doses

medications.mechanism: Inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → blocks DNA synthesis → cytoreductive; in sickle cell disease: increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF), reduces sickling, decreases WBC and platelet counts, increases MCV
medications.indications: Sickle cell disease (FDA-approved — Droxia/Siklos), myeloproliferative neoplasms (PV, ET, CML), head and neck cancer (with radiation)
medications.dosing:
- Sickle cell disease: start 15 mg/kg/day PO; increase by 5 mg/kg/day every 8 weeks to max tolerated dose (up to 35 mg/kg/day); target: MCV rise, ANC >2000, platelets >80,000
- Myeloproliferative neoplasms: 15–20 mg/kg/day PO; adjust to maintain target counts
- CML: 20–30 mg/kg/day PO
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC with differential every 2 weeks during dose titration, then every 4–8 weeks; renal function; LFTs
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), GI symptoms, skin/nail hyperpigmentation, leg ulcers (long-term), macrocytosis (expected — not a reason to stop), teratogenicity
medications.sideEffects: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia — dose-limiting but reversible), macrocytosis (expected, marker of adherence), nausea, skin/nail hyperpigmentation, leg ulcers (rare, long-term), teratogenicity (avoid in pregnancy/lactation), theoretical concern for secondary malignancy (not substantiated in long-term SCD follow-up)
medications.watchFor: In sickle cell disease, reduces pain crises by ~50%, reduces ACS, reduces transfusion needs, and improves survival. Recommended for all patients with SCD ≥9 months of age per NHLBI 2014 and ASH 2019 guidelines. Macrocytosis (elevated MCV) is an expected pharmacologic effect and marker of adherence. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Dose-adjust for renal impairment

medications.mechanism: Binds soluble and membrane-bound IL-6 receptors → blocks IL-6 signaling → reduces inflammation, acute phase reactants, and autoimmune activity
medications.indications: RA (after DMARD failure), GCA, systemic JIA, polyarticular JIA, CRS (CAR-T therapy), SSc-ILD, COVID-19 (hospitalized, on systemic corticosteroids + supplemental O₂)
medications.dosing:
- RA: IV 4 mg/kg Q4 weeks, increase to 8 mg/kg (max 800 mg); SC 162 mg Q2 weeks (100 kg) or Q1 week (≥100 kg)
- GCA: IV 6 mg/kg Q4 weeks (max 600 mg); SC 162 mg weekly or Q2 weeks + glucocorticoid taper
- SSc-ILD: SC 162 mg weekly
- sJIA: IV 12 mg/kg Q2 weeks (30 kg) or 8 mg/kg Q2 weeks (≥30 kg); SC 162 mg Q2 weeks (30 kg) or weekly (≥30 kg)
- CRS: 8 mg/kg IV (≥30 kg) or 12 mg/kg IV (30 kg); max 800 mg; may repeat × 3 doses (≥8 hours apart)
Route: IV, SC
medications.monitoring:
LFTs, CBC with differential, lipids at baseline and every 4–8 weeks initially; CRP/ESR (note: tocilizumab suppresses CRP — cannot use CRP to monitor disease activity or detect infection)
KEY TOXICITIES: Infections (serious bacterial, TB reactivation, opportunistic), hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, hyperlipidemia, GI perforation (especially with concurrent corticosteroids/NSAIDs and diverticular disease), infusion reactions
medications.sideEffects: Infections (upper respiratory, UTI; serious infections including TB, opportunistic), GI perforation (especially in patients with diverticular disease — assess before starting), elevated transaminases, hyperlipidemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, infusion/injection site reactions, hypersensitivity
medications.watchFor: Screen for TB before initiation. CRP is unreliable as infection marker during treatment. Do not combine with other biologics. GI perforation risk — use caution in patients with diverticulitis history. Dose modifications for elevated LFTs, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia per label

medications.mechanism: Binds RANK ligand → inhibits osteoclast formation, function, and survival → decreases bone resorption
medications.indications:
- Prolia (60 mg): postmenopausal osteoporosis, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis, bone loss from ADT (prostate cancer) or AI therapy (breast cancer)
- Xgeva (120 mg): prevention of SREs in bone metastases/multiple myeloma, giant cell tumor of bone, hypercalcemia of malignancy refractory to bisphosphonates
medications.dosing:
- Osteoporosis (Prolia): 60 mg SC every 6 months
- Bone metastases/myeloma (Xgeva): 120 mg SC every 4 weeks
- Giant cell tumor: 120 mg SC Q4 weeks + loading doses on days 8 and 15
- Hypercalcemia of malignancy: 120 mg SC Q4 weeks + loading doses on days 8 and 15
Route: SC
medications.monitoring:
Serum calcium, vitamin D, creatinine before initiation; periodic calcium monitoring; dental exam at baseline (ONJ risk)
KEY TOXICITIES: Hypocalcemia (especially in renal impairment, vitamin D deficiency), osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ — rare), atypical femoral fracture (rare, with prolonged use), rebound bone loss and vertebral fractures after discontinuation
medications.sideEffects: Hypocalcemia (especially in CKD, vitamin D deficiency — can be severe/symptomatic), osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ — rare), atypical femoral fractures (rare), back/extremity pain, infections (cellulitis, UTI), skin reactions (dermatitis, eczema, rash)
medications.watchFor: Must continue indefinitely or transition to bisphosphonate upon discontinuation (rebound vertebral fractures). Ensure calcium and vitamin D sufficiency before and during treatment. Can be used in renal impairment (unlike bisphosphonates) but higher hypocalcemia risk. Dental exam before initiation. Not interchangeable between Prolia and Xgeva formulations

medications.mechanism: Binds hydroxyapatite in bone → internalized by osteoclasts → inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase (mevalonate pathway) → osteoclast apoptosis → decreased bone resorption
medications.indications:
- Reclast (5 mg): postmenopausal osteoporosis, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis, Paget disease, male osteoporosis
- Zometa (4 mg): hypercalcemia of malignancy, bone metastases (solid tumors), multiple myeloma
medications.dosing:
- Osteoporosis (Reclast): 5 mg IV once yearly; osteopenia: 5 mg IV every 2 years
- Paget disease: 5 mg IV single dose
- Bone metastases/myeloma (Zometa): 4 mg IV every 4–12 weeks (Q12 weeks noninferior to Q4 weeks for SRE prevention in CRPC)
- Hypercalcemia of malignancy: 4 mg IV single dose over ≥15 minutes
- Post-denosumab transition: 5 mg IV at 6–7 months after last denosumab dose; may need repeat dose at 3–6 months based on bone turnover markers
Route: IV (infusion over ≥15 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
Creatinine before each dose; calcium, phosphate, magnesium; dental exam at baseline
KEY TOXICITIES: Acute phase reaction (fever, myalgia, arthralgia — mitigate with acetaminophen pre/post infusion), nephrotoxicity (ensure adequate hydration, avoid if CrCl 3–5 years), ocular toxicities (uveitis, scleritis — rare), atrial fibrillation (signal from HORIZON trial)
medications.sideEffects: Acute phase reaction (fever, myalgia, arthralgia, flu-like symptoms — common after first dose, usually self-limited), nephrotoxicity (acute tubular necrosis — ensure adequate hydration, infuse over ≥15 min), hypocalcemia, osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ — rare, higher risk with oncologic dosing), atypical femoral fractures (rare, with prolonged use), ocular inflammation (uveitis, scleritis — rare)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated if CrCl 35 mL/min (use denosumab instead). Ensure vitamin D sufficiency and adequate hydration before infusion. Infuse over ≥15 minutes (rapid infusion increases renal toxicity). Drug holiday may be considered after 3–5 years in osteoporosis (fracture protection persists). More cost-effective than denosumab for osteoporosis. First-dose acute phase reaction occurs in ~30% (less with subsequent doses)

medications.mechanism: Pooled polyclonal IgG from thousands of donors. Multiple mechanisms: Fc receptor blockade on macrophages (reduces phagocytosis of antibody-coated cells), anti-idiotypic antibody neutralization, complement inhibition, modulation of cytokine production, suppression of pathogenic autoantibodies, passive antibody replacement in immunodeficiency.
medications.indications:
- Replacement: Primary immunodeficiency (XLA, CVID), secondary hypogammaglobulinemia (CLL, post-HSCT, post-rituximab)
- Immunomodulatory: ITP, Guillain-Barré syndrome, CIDP, Kawasaki disease, myasthenia gravis (crisis/exacerbation), dermatomyositis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, multifocal motor neuropathy
medications.dosing:
- Immunodeficiency replacement: 400–600 mg/kg IV every 3–4 weeks (target trough IgG >500 mg/dL)
- ITP: 1 g/kg IV × 1–2 days or 400 mg/kg/day × 5 days
- Guillain-Barré: 400 mg/kg/day IV × 5 days (total 2 g/kg)
- Kawasaki disease: 2 g/kg IV single dose over 10–12 hours + aspirin
- Myasthenia crisis: 2 g/kg IV divided over 2–5 days
- CIDP: 2 g/kg IV loading over 2–4 days, then 1 g/kg every 3 weeks maintenance
Route: IV (also available as subcutaneous immunoglobulin [SCIG] for replacement)
medications.monitoring: Renal function (creatinine before and after), vital signs during infusion, IgA level before first dose (IgA-deficient patients at risk for anaphylaxis with IgA-containing products)
medications.sideEffects:
- Common: Headache, fever, chills, myalgia, nausea, fatigue (rate-related — slow infusion rate)
- Serious: Anaphylaxis (especially in IgA deficiency), aseptic meningitis, renal failure (osmotic nephrosis — especially with sucrose-containing products; avoid in renal impairment), thromboembolic events (stroke, MI, DVT, PE — risk increases with high dose, rapid infusion, advanced age, immobility, hypercoagulable states), hemolytic anemia (anti-A/anti-B antibodies in product — monitor in non-O blood types), transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
medications.watchFor: Slow initial infusion rate and increase gradually. Pre-hydrate patients. Use IgA-depleted products for IgA-deficient patients. Sucrose-free products preferred in renal impairment. Wait ≥3 months after IVIG before administering live vaccines (passive antibodies interfere). SCIG is an alternative for replacement therapy — better tolerated, self-administered at home, steadier IgG levels. Cost is very high (~$5,000–$10,000+ per infusion depending on dose/weight).

medications.mechanism: Binds FKBP-12 → inhibits calcineurin → blocks IL-2 transcription → suppresses T-cell activation and proliferation
medications.indications: Transplant rejection prophylaxis (kidney, liver, heart), lupus nephritis, nephrotic syndrome (FSGS, membranous), atopic dermatitis (topical)
medications.dosing:
- Kidney transplant: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (with MMF: 0.1 mg/kg/day; with azathioprine: 0.2 mg/kg/day)
- Liver transplant: 0.1–0.15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
- Heart transplant: 0.075 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
- IV (if unable to take PO): 0.03–0.05 mg/kg/day continuous infusion
- Target trough levels: transplant 5–15 ng/mL (varies by time post-transplant and organ); glomerular disease 5–10 ng/mL
Route: PO, IV, topical
medications.monitoring:
Trough levels (12-hour post-dose); renal function (creatinine, BUN); electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺); glucose; blood pressure; LFTs
KEY TOXICITIES: Nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting), neurotoxicity (tremor, headache, seizures), hyperglycemia/new-onset diabetes, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypertension, GI symptoms, alopecia, increased infection risk, increased malignancy risk (lymphoma, skin cancer)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Increased susceptibility to infection and malignancy (lymphoma, skin cancer). IV formulation contains castor oil — risk of anaphylaxis.
- Common (≥15%): Nephrotoxicity (~40%), tremor, hypertension, hyperglycemia/new-onset diabetes after transplant (NODAT), hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, diarrhea, nausea, headache, insomnia, paresthesia, anemia, leukopenia, CMV infection, peripheral edema
- Serious: PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome), seizures, TMA/HUS, QT prolongation, myocardial hypertrophy, pure red cell aplasia
medications.watchFor: Narrow therapeutic index — requires therapeutic drug monitoring. Avoid grapefruit (CYP3A4 inhibitor increases levels). Many drug interactions (azole antifungals, macrolides increase levels; rifampin, phenytoin decrease levels). Extended-release formulation (Envarsus XR) dosed once daily. More potent immunosuppression than cyclosporine per transplant literature

medications.mechanism: Binds cyclophilin → inhibits calcineurin → blocks IL-2 transcription → suppresses T-cell activation
medications.indications: Transplant rejection prophylaxis, psoriasis, RA (severe), nephrotic syndrome, uveitis, dry eye (topical — Restasis)
medications.dosing:
- Transplant: 10–15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID initially, taper to 5–10 mg/kg/day; target trough 100–175 ng/mL (renal); 200–350 ng/mL early post-transplant
- RA: 3–5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
- Psoriasis: 2.5–5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
- Nephrotic syndrome: 3–5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; target trough 100–175 ng/mL
Route: PO, IV
medications.monitoring:
Trough levels; creatinine, BUN, electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺); blood pressure; lipids; LFTs; CBC
KEY TOXICITIES: Nephrotoxicity, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hirsutism, gingival hyperplasia, tremor, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyperuricemia/gout, increased infection and malignancy risk
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Increased susceptibility to infection and malignancy (lymphoma, skin cancer). Nephrotoxicity. Hypertension. Only physicians experienced in immunosuppressive therapy should prescribe.
- Common: Nephrotoxicity (dose-dependent, both acute and chronic — chronic nephrotoxicity with interstitial fibrosis is irreversible), hypertension (~50%), tremor, hirsutism/hypertrichosis, gingival hyperplasia, hyperlipidemia, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyperuricemia/gout, headache, paresthesia
- Serious: TMA/HUS, seizures (especially with hypomagnesemia), hepatotoxicity, skin malignancies (especially with UV exposure)
medications.watchFor: Neoral (microemulsion) and Sandimmune are NOT bioequivalent — do not interchange without monitoring. Narrow therapeutic index. Avoid grapefruit. Many CYP3A4 drug interactions. Less potent than tacrolimus but different side effect profile (more hirsutism/gingival hyperplasia, less diabetes/neurotoxicity). Reduce dose if creatinine rises >30% above baseline

medications.mechanism: Blocks IL-1α and IL-1β from binding IL-1 receptor type I → reduces inflammation, fever, and acute phase response
medications.indications: RA (after DMARD failure), NOMID/CAPS, DIRA; off-label: gout flares, adult-onset Still disease, pericarditis (recurrent), macrophage activation syndrome, familial Mediterranean fever
medications.dosing:
- RA: 100 mg SC once daily
- NOMID/CAPS: 1–2 mg/kg SC daily, adjustable up to 8 mg/kg/day
- DIRA: 1–2 mg/kg SC daily
- Gout flare (off-label): 100 mg SC daily × 3–5 days
- Recurrent pericarditis (off-label): 100 mg SC daily (2 mg/kg in children), taper after resolution
Route: SC
medications.monitoring:
Neutrophil count at baseline, monthly × 3 months, then quarterly × 1 year; renal function
KEY TOXICITIES: Injection site reactions (71% — most common, typically mild, resolve within 4 weeks), neutropenia, serious infections (2% vs 1% placebo), hepatotoxicity (rare), hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis (rare), DRESS (rare in autoinflammatory patients)
medications.sideEffects: Injection site reactions (71% — erythema, ecchymosis, inflammation; usually mild, resolve in 2–4 weeks), infections (upper respiratory), neutropenia (8%), headache, nausea, diarrhea, arthralgia. Rare: anaphylaxis, angioedema, DRESS syndrome.
medications.watchFor: Do NOT combine with TNF blockers (7% serious infection rate in combination vs 0% etanercept alone). Consider every-other-day dosing in severe renal insufficiency or ESRD. Short half-life (4–6 hours) — rapid offset is an advantage in acute settings. Live vaccines contraindicated. Rapid onset of action makes it useful for acute flares (gout, Still disease, pericarditis)

medications.brandNames: Remicade; biosimilars: Inflectra, Renflexis, Avsola, others
medications.mechanism: Binds soluble and transmembrane TNF-α → neutralizes TNF-mediated inflammation; induces apoptosis of TNF-expressing inflammatory cells; modulates downstream cytokine cascade
medications.indications: Crohn disease (adult and pediatric), ulcerative colitis (adult and pediatric), RA (with methotrexate), ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, plaque psoriasis
medications.dosing:
- Crohn disease / UC: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 (induction), then every 8 weeks (maintenance); may increase to 10 mg/kg or shorten interval to Q6 weeks for loss of response
- RA: 3 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks (with methotrexate); may increase to 10 mg/kg or shorten to Q4 weeks
- AS: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 6 weeks
- PsA / Psoriasis: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks
Route: IV (infusion over ≥2 hours; may shorten to 1 hour in selected patients after 3 initial infusions)
medications.monitoring:
TB screening (TST or IGRA) before initiation; HBV screening (HBsAg, anti-HBc); LFTs periodically; CBC
KEY TOXICITIES: BOXED WARNING — serious infections (TB, invasive fungal, bacterial sepsis) and malignancy (lymphoma, HSTCL in young males on combination with thiopurines). Infusion reactions (acute and delayed serum sickness-like), hepatotoxicity (including acute liver failure), heart failure exacerbation (contraindicated in moderate-severe CHF NYHA III/IV), demyelinating disease (new onset or exacerbation), lupus-like syndrome, cytopenias, paradoxical psoriasis
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Serious infections (TB, invasive fungal — histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, blastomycosis — bacterial, viral, opportunistic). Malignancy (lymphoma, HSTCL in young patients on combination with thiopurines).
- Common: Infusion reactions (20% — fever, chills, urticaria, dyspnea, hypotension), upper respiratory infections, headache, abdominal pain, nausea, elevated transaminases
- Serious: Hepatotoxicity (acute liver failure reported), heart failure exacerbation (contraindicated in moderate-severe CHF, NYHA III/IV), demyelinating disease (MS, GBS — new onset or exacerbation), lupus-like syndrome (ANA positivity in ~50%, clinical lupus rare), cytopenias, serum sickness-like reactions, SJS/TEN, reactivation of HBV
medications.watchFor: Screen for TB and HBV before initiation — antiviral prophylaxis if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+. Concomitant methotrexate or thiopurine reduces immunogenicity (anti-drug antibodies). Therapeutic drug monitoring (trough levels + anti-drug antibodies) recommended for loss of response. Contraindicated in moderate-severe heart failure. Live vaccines contraindicated. Multiple biosimilars available

medications.mechanism: Inhibits JAK1 and JAK3 → blocks signaling of multiple cytokines (IL-2, IL-4, IL-6, IL-7, IL-9, IL-15, IL-21, IFN-α/β/γ) → suppresses T-cell activation, differentiation, and function
medications.indications: RA (after TNF blocker failure), PsA (after TNF blocker failure), AS (after TNF blocker failure), UC (after TNF blocker failure), pcJIA (≥2 years, after TNF blocker failure)
medications.dosing:
- RA / PsA / AS: 5 mg PO BID or 11 mg XR PO daily
- UC induction: 10 mg PO BID × 8 weeks (may extend to 16 weeks if needed)
- UC maintenance: 5 mg PO BID (use lowest effective dose)
- pcJIA: 5 mg PO BID (≥40 kg); weight-based dosing (40 kg)
- Dose reduce to 5 mg daily with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or moderate CYP3A4 + strong CYP2C19 inhibitors; moderate-severe hepatic impairment; moderate-severe renal impairment
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC (lymphocytes, neutrophils, hemoglobin) at baseline, 4–8 weeks, then every 3 months; LFTs; lipids at 4–8 weeks; TB screening before initiation; viral hepatitis screening
KEY TOXICITIES: BOXED WARNING — serious infections, increased mortality, malignancy (lymphoma, lung cancer, NMSC), MACE (MI, stroke, CV death), thrombosis (PE, DVT, arterial). Herpes zoster (significantly increased risk — consider Shingrix before initiation). Neutropenia, lymphopenia, anemia, hepatotoxicity, hyperlipidemia, GI perforation
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Serious infections (TB, opportunistic, herpes zoster — notably higher HZ risk than biologics), increased mortality, malignancy and lymphoproliferative disorders (MACE), thrombosis (DVT, PE — especially at 10 mg BID dose)
- Common: Upper respiratory infections, nasopharyngitis, diarrhea, headache, herpes zoster
- Lab: Lymphopenia, neutropenia, anemia, elevated LDL/total cholesterol, elevated transaminases, elevated creatinine
- Serious: GI perforation (especially with concurrent corticosteroids/NSAIDs), VTE, MACE, NMSC
medications.watchFor: ORAL SURVEILLANCE trial (RA patients ≥50 years with ≥1 CV risk factor): tofacitinib 5 mg or 10 mg BID showed higher rates of MACE, malignancy, thrombosis, and all-cause mortality vs TNF blockers. FDA now requires boxed warning for all JAK inhibitors. Use only after TNF blocker failure per FDA label. 10 mg BID NOT recommended for RA/PsA/AS. Do not combine with biologic DMARDs or potent immunosuppressants. Live vaccines contraindicated

medications.mechanism: Binds free IgE at Cε3 domain → prevents IgE from binding high-affinity FcεRI on mast cells and basophils → reduces IgE-mediated degranulation, histamine release, and allergic inflammation; downregulates FcεRI expression over time
medications.indications: Moderate-severe persistent allergic asthma (≥6 years), chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (adults), IgE-mediated food allergy (≥1 year), chronic spontaneous urticaria (≥12 years)
medications.dosing:
- Asthma: 75–375 mg SC every 2 or 4 weeks (based on pretreatment serum IgE level and body weight per dosing table)
- CRSwNP: 75–600 mg SC every 2 or 4 weeks (based on IgE and weight)
- Food allergy: 75–600 mg SC every 2 or 4 weeks (based on IgE and weight); used in conjunction with food allergen avoidance
- CSU: 150 or 300 mg SC every 4 weeks (dose NOT based on IgE level)
Route: SC
medications.monitoring:
Observe in healthcare setting for anaphylaxis after each injection (especially first 3 doses); serum IgE levels before initiation (for dosing in asthma/CRSwNP/food allergy — NOT useful for monitoring response); reassess asthma control periodically
KEY TOXICITIES: BOXED WARNING — anaphylaxis (0.1–0.2%; ~60–70% within first 3 doses; can occur >1 year into treatment). Injection site reactions, malignancy signal (observed in clinical studies — causal relationship unclear), eosinophilic conditions (Churg-Strauss upon corticosteroid reduction), serum sickness-like reactions, thrombocytopenia (rare), alopecia (rare)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Anaphylaxis (0.1–0.2% — can occur after any dose, most within first 3 doses; prescribe epinephrine autoinjector)
- Common: Injection site reactions (45%), arthralgia, headache, fatigue, dizziness, upper respiratory infections, sinusitis, pharyngitis
- Serious: Serum sickness-like reactions, eosinophilic conditions (Churg-Strauss/EGPA — may unmask when steroids are tapered), malignancy (early signal not confirmed in larger analyses), thrombocytopenia (rare), alopecia (rare)
medications.watchFor: NOT for acute bronchospasm or status asthmaticus. Do not abruptly discontinue corticosteroids upon initiation. Patients with prior anaphylaxis history are at higher risk. Self-administration may be appropriate after initial doses in healthcare setting. Serum total IgE increases during treatment (bound to omalizumab) — do not recheck IgE to guide dosing adjustments. For food allergy: does NOT eliminate allergy, reduces severity of accidental exposure reactions

medications.mechanism: Blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle → arterial vasodilation → reduces SVR and blood pressure; minimal negative inotropy; cerebral and coronary vasodilation
medications.indications: Hypertensive emergency, perioperative hypertension, acute ischemic stroke (BP management), ICH (BP management), aortic dissection (adjunct to beta-blocker)
medications.dosing:
- IV infusion: start 5 mg/hour; increase by 2.5 mg/hour every 5–15 minutes; max 15 mg/hour
- Typical maintenance: 3–5 mg/hour after target BP achieved
- Onset: 5–15 minutes; duration of action: 30–60 minutes after stopping
Route: IV (continuous infusion only — requires dedicated line; peripheral or central)
medications.monitoring:
Continuous arterial blood pressure monitoring (arterial line preferred in ICU); heart rate; neurologic status (stroke/ICH)
KEY TOXICITIES: Hypotension (dose-dependent), reflex tachycardia (less than nitroprusside), headache, nausea, phlebitis at peripheral IV site (change site every 12 hours), flushing
medications.sideEffects: Hypotension, tachycardia (reflex), headache, nausea, flushing, peripheral edema, dizziness, infusion site phlebitis/thrombophlebitis (IV)
medications.watchFor: Preferred IV antihypertensive for acute ischemic stroke and ICH (AHA/ASA guidelines) — predictable dose-response, no cyanide toxicity, cerebral vasodilation. In aortic dissection, must use WITH beta-blocker first (to prevent reflex tachycardia and increased aortic shear stress). Peripheral IV compatible but causes phlebitis — change site Q12H or use central line. More predictable than nitroprusside. No dose adjustment for renal/hepatic impairment (but use caution)

medications.drugClass: Synthetic catecholamine (β1-selective agonist with β2 and mild α1 activity)
medications.mechanism: Primarily stimulates β1-adrenergic receptors → increases myocardial contractility (inotropy) and heart rate (chronotropy); β2 activity causes mild peripheral vasodilation; net effect: increased cardiac output with modest decrease in SVR
medications.indications: Acute decompensated heart failure (short-term hemodynamic support), cardiogenic shock (with vasopressor), low cardiac output syndrome post-cardiac surgery, pharmacologic stress testing (dobutamine stress echo)
medications.dosing:
- IV infusion: 2–20 mcg/kg/min; start at 2–5 mcg/kg/min and titrate to hemodynamic response
- Stress echo: start 5 mcg/kg/min, increase every 3 minutes to 10, 20, 30, 40 mcg/kg/min (with atropine if needed)
- Onset: 1–2 minutes; half-life: 2–3 minutes
- No loading dose required
Route: IV (continuous infusion — central line preferred)
medications.monitoring:
Continuous ECG (arrhythmias); blood pressure; heart rate; cardiac output (if PA catheter); urine output; lactate
KEY TOXICITIES: Tachycardia (dose-limiting), arrhythmias (PVCs, VT, AF), hypotension (β2-mediated vasodilation — especially if hypovolemic), myocardial ischemia (increased O₂ demand), tachyphylaxis (downregulation of β-receptors with prolonged use >72 hours), hypokalemia, headache, nausea
medications.sideEffects: Tachycardia, arrhythmias (PVCs, VT, AF), hypotension (at higher doses due to β2 vasodilation), hypertension, angina/myocardial ischemia, headache, nausea, hypokalemia. Tolerance develops with prolonged use (>72 hours — β-receptor downregulation).
medications.watchFor: Effectiveness reduced by concurrent β-blockers (competitive antagonism at β-receptors) — milrinone preferred in β-blocked patients. Tachyphylaxis develops with prolonged infusion (>72 hours) — consider milrinone for longer-term support. Not a vasopressor — may cause hypotension; often combined with norepinephrine in cardiogenic shock. DOREMI trial: milrinone and dobutamine showed similar clinical outcomes in cardiogenic shock. Short half-life allows rapid dose adjustment. Avoid in HOCM (increases outflow tract obstruction)

medications.drugClass: Endogenous catecholamine (potent α1 agonist with moderate β1 activity)
medications.mechanism: Potent α1-adrenergic receptor agonist → vasoconstriction → increases SVR and MAP; moderate β1 activity → mild increase in cardiac contractility; minimal β2 activity → no significant vasodilation or bronchodilation
medications.indications: Septic shock (first-line vasopressor), cardiogenic shock (with inotrope), distributive shock, vasodilatory shock, post-cardiac arrest
medications.dosing:
- IV infusion: start 0.01–0.05 mcg/kg/min (or 2–4 mcg/min); titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg
- Typical range: 0.1–0.5 mcg/kg/min (5–30 mcg/min)
- High-dose: >0.5 mcg/kg/min (consider adding vasopressin at this point)
- No max dose per se, but doses >1 mcg/kg/min associated with increased mortality and limb ischemia
Route: IV (continuous infusion — central line preferred; peripheral IV acceptable short-term with monitoring for extravasation)
medications.monitoring:
Continuous arterial BP (arterial line preferred); heart rate; urine output; lactate; peripheral perfusion (skin mottling, capillary refill); IV site (extravasation)
KEY TOXICITIES: Peripheral ischemia (digital, limb — especially at high doses or with peripheral IV extravasation), arrhythmias (less than dopamine or epinephrine), tissue necrosis with extravasation (treat with phentolamine 5–10 mg in 10 mL NS injected locally), reflex bradycardia (baroreceptor-mediated), mesenteric ischemia (high doses), hyperglycemia
medications.sideEffects: Peripheral ischemia (digits, skin necrosis), arrhythmias, reflex bradycardia, tissue necrosis from extravasation (treat with phentolamine 5–10 mg in 10 mL NS infiltrated locally), hypertension (excessive), organ ischemia (mesenteric, renal at very high doses)
medications.watchFor: First-line vasopressor for septic shock per SSC 2021 and 2026 guidelines — superior to dopamine (lower mortality, fewer arrhythmias). Target MAP ≥65 mmHg (60–65 mmHg may be acceptable per 65 Trial). Add vasopressin (0.03–0.04 U/min) as second-line when escalating norepinephrine doses. Can be given via well-secured peripheral IV short-term during resuscitation while central access is obtained. Extravasation antidote: phentolamine injection into affected area. Unlike dopamine, does NOT have significant chronotropic effect — less tachycardia. Transforms unstressed blood volume to stressed volume — augments preload in addition to afterload

medications.mechanism: Provides bicarbonate ions → buffers hydrogen ions → raises blood and urine pH; in hyperkalemia, shifts potassium intracellularly via pH-dependent mechanism
medications.indications: Severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.1), hyperkalemia (adjunct — temporizing), sodium channel blocker toxicity (TCA overdose, cocaine cardiotoxicity), urinary alkalinization (rhabdomyolysis, methotrexate clearance, salicylate poisoning), cardiac arrest (specific situations)
medications.dosing:
- Metabolic acidosis: 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus; may repeat based on ABG; or continuous infusion (150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 150–200 mL/hour)
- Hyperkalemia: 50 mEq IV over 5 minutes (most effective if concurrent acidosis)
- TCA overdose: 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus; target serum pH 7.45–7.55; may repeat or start infusion
- Urinary alkalinization (rhabdomyolysis): 150 mEq in 1 L D5W at 200 mL/hour; target urine pH >6.5
- Cardiac arrest: 1 mEq/kg IV; consider if prolonged arrest, pre-existing acidosis, hyperkalemia, or TCA overdose
- 8.4% solution = 1 mEq/mL; 4.2% solution for neonates
Route: IV (bolus or infusion)
medications.monitoring:
ABG/VBG (pH, pCO₂, bicarbonate); serum electrolytes (K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺); urine pH (if alkalinizing); ionized calcium
KEY TOXICITIES: Metabolic alkalosis (overshoot), hypernatremia (hyperosmolar — 8.4% solution is 2000 mOsm/L), hypokalemia (intracellular shift), hypocalcemia (alkalosis reduces ionized calcium → tetany, seizures), paradoxical intracellular acidosis (CO₂ diffuses into cells faster than bicarbonate), volume overload, tissue necrosis with extravasation
medications.sideEffects: Metabolic alkalosis (overshoot), hypernatremia, hypokalemia (shifts K⁺ intracellularly), hypocalcemia (alkalosis reduces ionized Ca²⁺ — can precipitate tetany/seizures), volume overload, paradoxical intracellular acidosis (CO₂ crosses cell membranes faster than HCO₃⁻), tissue necrosis from extravasation (hypertonic solution)
medications.watchFor: In TCA overdose, sodium bicarbonate is the antidote — sodium loading overcomes sodium channel blockade AND alkalinization reduces free drug. Target serum pH 7.45–7.55 (not higher). In hyperkalemia, bicarbonate is least effective temporizing measure — insulin/glucose and albuterol are preferred; bicarbonate most useful if concurrent metabolic acidosis. Routine use in cardiac arrest is NOT recommended (AHA) — only in specific situations. In rhabdomyolysis, urinary alkalinization prevents myoglobin precipitation in renal tubules. Incompatible with many IV drugs — flush line before/after. Avoid in respiratory acidosis (worsens CO₂ retention)

medications.mechanism: Essential cofactor for >300 enzymatic reactions; stabilizes cell membranes; blocks NMDA receptors (anticonvulsant); suppresses automaticity and triggered activity in cardiac tissue; relaxes smooth muscle (bronchodilation, tocolysis, vasodilation)
medications.indications: Hypomagnesemia, eclampsia/preeclampsia (seizure prophylaxis and treatment), torsades de pointes, refractory ventricular arrhythmias, severe acute asthma (refractory), preterm labor (tocolysis — short-term), digoxin toxicity (adjunct)
medications.dosing:
- Hypomagnesemia (mild-moderate): 1–2 g IV over 1 hour; may repeat
- Hypomagnesemia (severe/symptomatic): 2–4 g IV over 15–60 minutes, then 1 g/hour infusion
- Eclampsia/preeclampsia: loading 4–6 g IV over 15–30 minutes, then 1–2 g/hour continuous infusion (maintain serum Mg 4–7 mEq/L)
- Torsades de pointes: 1–2 g IV over 5–20 minutes (may push faster in pulseless); may repeat in 5–15 minutes
- Severe asthma: 2 g IV over 20 minutes
- Cardiac arrest (torsades): 1–2 g IV push
Route: IV, IM (painful — avoid if possible)
medications.monitoring:
Serum magnesium levels; deep tendon reflexes (loss of DTRs = first sign of toxicity at ~7–10 mEq/L); respiratory rate (respiratory depression at ~10–13 mEq/L); urine output (renal excretion); ECG; calcium at bedside (antidote)
KEY TOXICITIES: Dose-dependent toxicity spectrum: loss of DTRs (7–10 mEq/L) → respiratory depression (10–13 mEq/L) → cardiac arrest (>15 mEq/L). Hypotension, flushing, nausea, muscle weakness, sedation. Neonatal depression if given before delivery
medications.sideEffects: Flushing, sweating, hypotension (dose-dependent vasodilation), loss of deep tendon reflexes (Mg >4 mEq/L), respiratory depression/paralysis (Mg ~10 mEq/L), cardiac arrest (Mg >12 mEq/L), hypothermia, hypocalcemia (secondary)
medications.watchFor: Antidote for magnesium toxicity: calcium gluconate 1 g IV over 3 minutes (or calcium chloride via central line). Always check DTRs and respiratory rate before each dose in eclampsia management. Dose reduce or avoid in renal impairment (renally cleared). Replete potassium simultaneously — hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia (Mg is required for Na-K-ATPase function). Torsades de pointes — magnesium is first-line even if serum Mg is normal. In asthma, evidence is strongest for pediatric patients; adult benefit is less clear

medications.mechanism: Reversibly binds lysine-binding sites on plasminogen → prevents plasminogen activation to plasmin → inhibits fibrinolysis → stabilizes clot
medications.indications: Trauma-associated hemorrhage (CRASH-2), postpartum hemorrhage (WOMAN trial), perioperative bleeding (cardiac, orthopedic, spine surgery), heavy menstrual bleeding, hereditary bleeding disorders, epistaxis, GI bleeding (limited evidence)
medications.dosing:
- Trauma (CRASH-2): 1 g IV over 10 minutes within 3 hours of injury, then 1 g IV over 8 hours
- Postpartum hemorrhage: 1 g IV over 10 minutes; may repeat once after 30 minutes if bleeding continues
- Perioperative (orthopedic): 10–15 mg/kg IV before incision; may repeat or infuse 1 mg/kg/hour
- Cardiac surgery: 10–30 mg/kg IV loading, then 1–16 mg/kg/hour (avoid very high doses — seizure risk)
- Heavy menstrual bleeding: 1300 mg PO TID × 5 days (during menses)
- Epistaxis: topical (soak gauze in IV solution) or 1 g IV
Route: IV, PO, topical
medications.monitoring:
Renal function (dose reduce if CrCl 30); signs of thrombosis; seizure monitoring (high-dose cardiac surgery)
KEY TOXICITIES: GI (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain — most common with oral), seizures (dose-dependent — especially high-dose cardiac surgery; total dose >100 mg/kg), thromboembolic events (theoretical — NOT confirmed in large RCTs; CRASH-2 and WOMAN showed no increase in VTE), visual disturbances (rare — retinal changes with prolonged use), hypotension (rapid IV injection)
medications.sideEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, headache. Rare: seizures (dose-dependent — especially at high doses in cardiac surgery; max 100 mg/kg recommended), thromboembolic events (theoretical concern — NOT demonstrated in meta-analyses of RCTs), visual disturbances (retinal changes with prolonged use), hypersensitivity
medications.watchFor: CRASH-2 landmark trial: TXA within 3 hours of trauma reduced bleeding-related mortality by one-third; NO benefit if given >3 hours (may increase mortality). WOMAN trial: TXA reduced death from bleeding in postpartum hemorrhage when given within 3 hours. Time-dependent efficacy — give EARLY. No significant increase in thromboembolic events in major trials. Dose reduce in renal impairment. Seizure risk is dose-dependent — ISMICS recommends max total dose 100 mg/kg in cardiac surgery. Cheap, widely available, on WHO essential medicines list. Contraindicated in active intravascular clotting (DIC with predominant thrombosis) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (may reduce rebleeding but increases ischemic complications)

medications.mechanism: Same as nicardipine — L-type calcium channel blockade → arterial vasodilation; ultrashort half-life (1 minute) due to rapid ester hydrolysis by blood esterases
medications.indications: Perioperative hypertension, hypertensive emergency (alternative to nicardipine)
medications.dosing:
- IV infusion: start 1–2 mg/hour; double every 90 seconds until BP approaches target, then increase by less than double every 5–10 minutes; usual maintenance 4–6 mg/hour
- Max: most patients are treated at ≤16 mg/hour; up to 32 mg/hour has only limited short-term experience; 24-hour cap is 1000 mL (500 mg = an average of ≤21 mg/hour) because of lipid load — this cap, not the 32 mg/hour peak, is the binding constraint for sustained infusion
- Little experience with infusion durations beyond 72 hours at any dose
- Onset: 2–4 minutes; offset: 5–15 minutes after stopping
Route: IV (continuous infusion — lipid emulsion formulation)
medications.monitoring:
Continuous blood pressure monitoring; heart rate; triglyceride levels with prolonged use
KEY TOXICITIES: Hypotension, reflex tachycardia (less than nitroprusside), headache, nausea, atrial fibrillation (rare), rebound hypertension after discontinuation (transition to oral agents before stopping)
medications.sideEffects: Headache (6.3%), nausea (4.8%), vomiting (3.2%), reflex tachycardia, hypotension, atrial fibrillation (perioperative), acute renal failure (perioperative). Post-marketing: hypertriglyceridemia, ileus, hypersensitivity.
medications.watchFor: Lipid emulsion vehicle — contraindicated in soy/egg allergy, defective lipid metabolism (pathological hyperlipidemia, lipoid nephrosis, acute pancreatitis). Contributes to lipid intake (~2 g fat per hour at typical doses) — account in TPN/propofol calculations. Ultrashort half-life (~1 minute) allows very precise titration — advantage over nicardipine. No negative inotropic or chronotropic effects. Faster BP reduction than nicardipine in clinical trials. Preferred for perioperative hypertension, acute pulmonary edema, and acute ICH per 2025 AHA/ACC guidelines. Must transition to oral antihypertensives before discontinuation (rebound hypertension risk). Lipid load is the real dose ceiling: the FDA label caps infusion at 1000 mL (≈21 mg/hour averaged) per 24 hours even though 32 mg/hour has been used short-term — quoting 32 mg/hour alone overstates what is sustainable.

medications.drugClass: Combined alpha-1 and nonselective beta-adrenergic blocker (IV beta:alpha ratio ~7:1)
medications.mechanism: Blocks alpha-1 receptors (vasodilation) and beta-1/beta-2 receptors (reduces heart rate, contractility) → lowers BP without reflex tachycardia; IV beta:alpha ratio ~7:1; oral ratio ~3:1
medications.indications: Hypertensive emergency, perioperative hypertension, eclampsia/preeclampsia, aortic dissection (alternative to esmolol), hyperadrenergic states
medications.dosing:
- IV bolus: 0.3–1.0 mg/kg (max 20 mg) slow IV push over 2 minutes; may repeat or double dose every 10 minutes; max cumulative dose 300 mg/24 hours
- IV infusion: 0.4–1.0 mg/kg/hour, up to 3 mg/kg/hour
- Oral (transition): 100–400 mg PO BID
- Onset: 5 minutes (IV); duration: 3–6 hours (sometimes longer at high doses)
Route: IV (bolus or infusion), PO
medications.monitoring:
Continuous BP monitoring; heart rate; respiratory status (bronchospasm)
KEY TOXICITIES: Bradycardia, orthostatic hypotension, bronchospasm (beta-2 blockade), heart failure exacerbation, scalp tingling, nausea, fatigue, hepatotoxicity (rare — idiosyncratic)
medications.sideEffects: Orthostatic hypotension (significant — keep supine), dizziness, nausea, fatigue, scalp tingling, bronchospasm, bradycardia, heart block, hepatotoxicity (rare, idiosyncratic — can be fatal)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in reactive airway disease/asthma (beta-2 blockade), second/third-degree heart block, bradycardia, decompensated heart failure. Safe in pregnancy (eclampsia/preeclampsia — AHA/ACOG recommended). Especially useful in hyperadrenergic syndromes (cocaine toxicity — debated but used; pheochromocytoma — only after alpha blockade). Prolonged duration of action (3–6 hours) is a disadvantage vs. esmolol/clevidipine for precise titration. Caution in cocaine toxicity — some experts prefer phentolamine or nicardipine to avoid unopposed alpha stimulation

medications.mechanism: Inhibits PDE-3 → prevents cAMP breakdown → increases intracellular calcium in cardiac myocytes (positive inotropy and lusitropy) AND decreases calcium in vascular smooth muscle (vasodilation) → increased cardiac output with reduced preload and afterload
medications.indications: Acute decompensated heart failure (short-term), cardiogenic shock, right heart failure with pulmonary hypertension, low cardiac output post-cardiac surgery, bridge to transplant/LVAD
medications.dosing:
- Loading dose: 50 mcg/kg IV over 10 minutes (often omitted in hypotensive patients due to vasodilation)
- Maintenance: 0.375–0.75 mcg/kg/min continuous infusion
- Max: 1.13 mg/kg/day
- Half-life: 1–4 hours (significantly prolonged in renal impairment)
- Dose reduce for renal impairment (CrCl 5: 0.2 mcg/kg/min; CrCl 10: 0.23 mcg/kg/min; CrCl 20: 0.28 mcg/kg/min; CrCl 30: 0.33 mcg/kg/min; CrCl 40: 0.38 mcg/kg/min; CrCl 50: 0.43 mcg/kg/min)
Route: IV (continuous infusion — central line preferred)
medications.monitoring:
Continuous ECG; blood pressure; heart rate; renal function (dose adjust); electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺); platelet count (rare thrombocytopenia)
KEY TOXICITIES: Hypotension (dose-limiting — potent vasodilator; worse with loading dose; long half-life makes it difficult to reverse quickly), arrhythmias (ventricular — similar rate to dobutamine), thrombocytopenia (rare), headache, hypokalemia
medications.sideEffects: Ventricular arrhythmias (12.1% — VEA 8.5%, NSVT 2.8%, sustained VT 1%, VF 0.2%), hypotension (2.9%), headache (2.9%), hypokalemia, thrombocytopenia (0.4%), torsades de pointes (rare, post-marketing)
medications.watchFor: Advantage over dobutamine: effective in β-blocked patients (mechanism independent of β-receptors); greater reduction in pulmonary artery pressures (preferred in RV failure with pulmonary hypertension); less tachycardia; no tachyphylaxis. Disadvantage: longer half-life (1–4 hours vs. 2–3 minutes for dobutamine) — hypotension harder to reverse; must dose-reduce in renal impairment. Loading dose often omitted in hypotensive patients. OPTIME-CHF trial: milrinone did not improve outcomes and increased arrhythmias/hypotension in acute HF — reserve for refractory cases. DOREMI trial: similar outcomes to dobutamine in cardiogenic shock

medications.mechanism: Recombinant modified tPA → binds fibrin in thrombus → converts plasminogen to plasmin → dissolves fibrin clot; more fibrin-specific and longer half-life than alteplase → allows single IV bolus dosing
medications.indications: Acute STEMI (FDA-approved), acute ischemic stroke (FDA-approved 2025; increasingly used off-label prior), massive/submassive PE (off-label)
medications.dosing:
- STEMI: weight-based single IV bolus over 5 seconds:
- 60 kg: 30 mg
- 60–69 kg: 35 mg
- 70–79 kg: 40 mg
- 80–89 kg: 45 mg
- ≥90 kg: 50 mg
- Acute ischemic stroke: 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus (max 25 mg) — per AHA/ASA and recent trials (AcT, TASTE)
- PE (off-label): various doses used; commonly half-dose (25–50 mg IV bolus) or full weight-based STEMI dosing
Route: IV (single bolus — major advantage over alteplase which requires 1-hour infusion)
medications.monitoring:
Neurologic checks (stroke — every 15 minutes × 2 hours, then every 30 minutes × 6 hours); BP (maintain 180/105 post-thrombolysis for stroke); signs of bleeding; fibrinogen; CBC; coagulation studies
KEY TOXICITIES: Hemorrhage (major risk — intracranial hemorrhage 0.9–7% depending on indication; GI, GU, retroperitoneal bleeding), allergic reactions (rare), reperfusion arrhythmias (STEMI), cholesterol embolization (rare), angioedema (rare — more common with concurrent ACE inhibitor)
medications.sideEffects: Bleeding (most common — intracranial hemorrhage ~0.9% STEMI, ~2% stroke), reperfusion arrhythmias, hypersensitivity, cholesterol embolization, thromboembolism (paradoxical)
medications.watchFor: Single IV bolus is the major advantage over alteplase (1-hour infusion) — faster administration, simpler logistics, especially in stroke (door-to-needle time). For stroke: 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg) is the emerging standard dose based on AcT and TASTE trials — noninferior to alteplase 0.9 mg/kg. Contraindications same as all thrombolytics: active internal bleeding, recent (3 months) intracranial surgery/stroke/head trauma, intracranial neoplasm/AVM/aneurysm, known bleeding diathesis, severe uncontrolled hypertension. Administer with heparin for STEMI (per STEMI protocol). Do not give with other thrombolytics. Cryoprecipitate and TXA for life-threatening bleeding post-thrombolysis

medications.mechanism: Prodrug → hepatic conversion to active metabolite (faster and more complete than clopidogrel) → irreversibly binds P2Y12 receptor on platelets → inhibits ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation
medications.indications: ACS (UA/NSTEMI and STEMI) managed with PCI — in combination with aspirin (DAPT)
medications.dosing:
- Loading dose: 60 mg PO (at time of PCI)
- Maintenance: 10 mg PO daily (5 mg daily if 60 kg)
- Duration: typically 12 months post-PCI (may extend or shorten based on bleeding/ischemic risk)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Signs of bleeding; CBC; consider platelet function testing in select cases
KEY TOXICITIES: BOXED WARNING — significant/fatal bleeding (TIMI major bleeding 2.4% vs 1.8% clopidogrel in TRITON-TIMI 38). Increased bleeding risk in: age ≥75 (generally not recommended unless high-risk ischemic situation — diabetes, prior MI), body weight 60 kg (use 5 mg maintenance), prior TIA/stroke (CONTRAINDICATED — net clinical harm)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Significant, sometimes fatal, bleeding. Risk factors: age ≥75, weight 60 kg, propensity to bleed.
- Common: Bleeding (TIMI major non-CABG 2.4% vs. 1.8% clopidogrel), hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, headache, back pain, dyspnea, nausea, dizziness, cough, fatigue, non-cardiac chest pain
- Serious: Fatal bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, CABG-related bleeding (significantly higher), TTP (rare)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in prior TIA or stroke (increased ICH risk — net harm in TRITON-TIMI 38). More potent, faster onset, and less interpatient variability than clopidogrel (no CYP2C19 dependence). Generally not recommended in age ≥75 (excess bleeding without clear ischemic benefit — unless diabetes or prior MI). Use 5 mg maintenance in patients 60 kg. TRITON-TIMI 38: prasugrel superior to clopidogrel for reducing ischemic events in ACS-PCI but with more bleeding. Not studied in medically managed ACS (only PCI). Ticagrelor is the main alternative — reversible P2Y12 inhibitor with similar efficacy

medications.mechanism: Blocks ENaC in the collecting duct principal cells → reduces sodium reabsorption → decreases potassium and hydrogen ion secretion → mild natriuresis with potassium retention; independent of aldosterone
medications.indications: Hypertension (adjunct), heart failure (adjunct to loop/thiazide diuretics to prevent hypokalemia), Liddle syndrome (specific treatment), lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (blocks lithium entry via ENaC), hypokalemia prevention
medications.dosing:
- Hypertension/HF: 5–10 mg PO daily (max 20 mg/day)
- Liddle syndrome: 5–20 mg PO daily
- Lithium-induced NDI: 5–10 mg PO daily (reduces lithium reabsorption via ENaC)
- Usually combined with hydrochlorothiazide (Moduretic: amiloride 5 mg + HCTZ 50 mg)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Serum potassium (hyperkalemia risk); renal function; sodium
KEY TOXICITIES: Hyperkalemia (dose-limiting — especially with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, K⁺ supplements, renal impairment), hyponatremia, metabolic acidosis (reduced H⁺ secretion), GI (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain), headache, dizziness
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Hyperkalemia (potentially fatal) — risk increased in renal impairment, diabetes, elderly, and when used alone or with other K⁺-sparing agents/ACEi/ARBs/K⁺ supplements
- Common (>1%): Headache, nausea/anorexia, diarrhea, abdominal pain, flatulence, mild skin rash
- Rare: Aplastic anemia, neutropenia, hepatotoxicity, hyponatremia, metabolic acidosis
medications.watchFor: Weak diuretic alone — primarily used as adjunct to prevent hypokalemia from thiazides/loops. Specific treatment for Liddle syndrome (gain-of-function ENaC mutation — spironolactone is ineffective because Liddle is aldosterone-independent). Useful in lithium-induced NDI — blocks lithium entry through ENaC in collecting duct. Contraindicated in severe renal impairment (GFR 5.5 mEq/L. Unlike spironolactone, does NOT cause gynecomastia (no anti-androgen activity)

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks mineralocorticoid receptor with balanced cardiac and renal tissue distribution → anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic effects → reduces kidney disease progression and cardiovascular events; unlike spironolactone, does not have steroidal side effects (gynecomastia, menstrual irregularities) and has lower hyperkalemia risk
medications.indications: CKD associated with type 2 diabetes (reduce risk of eGFR decline, ESKD, CV death, MI, HF hospitalization); heart failure with LVEF ≥40% (FINEARTS-HF trial)
medications.dosing:
- eGFR ≥60: start 20 mg PO daily
- eGFR 25–59: start 10 mg PO daily; may increase to 20 mg after 4 weeks if K⁺ ≤4.8 mEq/L
- eGFR 25: not recommended (limited data)
- Serum K⁺ must be ≤5.0 mEq/L before initiation
- Avoid with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir) and grapefruit
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Serum potassium at 1 month after initiation and after dose changes, then periodically; eGFR; blood pressure
KEY TOXICITIES: Hyperkalemia (most important — 2.3% discontinuation rate in FIDELIO-DKD vs 0.9% placebo; lower risk than steroidal MRAs), hypotension, initial eGFR dip (expected — similar to SGLT2 inhibitors and ACE inhibitors; does not indicate kidney injury)
medications.sideEffects: Hyperkalemia (14.3% vs. 7.6% placebo in INFINITY pooled analysis; hospitalization for hyperkalemia 0.9% vs. 0.2%; no deaths from hyperkalemia), hypotension, hyponatremia
medications.watchFor: FIDELIO-DKD: 18% reduction in composite kidney outcome; FIGARO-DKD: 13% reduction in composite CV outcome (primarily HF hospitalization). FIDELITY pooled analysis confirmed benefits across eGFR 25–>60 and UACR 30–>300. FINEARTS-HF: reduced HF events in HFmrEF/HFpEF regardless of diabetes status. Use ON TOP of maximally tolerated ACE inhibitor/ARB ± SGLT2 inhibitor. KDIGO and ADA guidelines recommend finerenone for CKD with T2D and albuminuria.

medications.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, translocates to nucleus, modulates gene transcription. Suppresses inflammatory and immune responses by inhibiting NF-κB, reducing prostaglandin/leukotriene synthesis, decreasing cytokine production (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), reducing leukocyte migration, and inducing lymphocyte apoptosis. ~5× potency of hydrocortisone; minimal mineralocorticoid activity.
medications.indications: Acute exacerbations of MS, acute spinal cord injury (controversial), severe asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, organ transplant rejection (pulse therapy), autoimmune conditions (SLE flare, vasculitis, dermatomyositis), allergic reactions, CRS (CAR-T), irAEs (Grade 3–4), acute gout, ITP
medications.dosing:
- MS acute relapse: 1000 mg IV daily × 3–5 days (pulse therapy)
- Severe asthma/COPD: 40–125 mg IV q6h initially, then taper
- Transplant rejection (pulse): 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 days
- Anti-inflammatory: 10–40 mg IV/IM; may repeat
- Oral: 4–48 mg/day PO in divided doses (Medrol Dosepak: 4 mg tablets, 6-day taper)
- Intra-articular: 20–80 mg (Depo-Medrol)
- Equivalence: 4 mg methylprednisolone = 5 mg prednisone = 20 mg hydrocortisone = 0.75 mg dexamethasone
Route: IV, IM, PO, intra-articular
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (hyperglycemia), BP, electrolytes (K⁺), signs of infection, bone density (long-term), ophthalmologic exam (cataracts, glaucoma with prolonged use), adrenal function (if prolonged use — taper to avoid adrenal crisis)
medications.sideEffects: Short-term: hyperglycemia, insomnia, mood changes, fluid retention, hypertension, GI upset. Long-term: osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, adrenal suppression, Cushing syndrome, immunosuppression, cataracts, glaucoma, myopathy, skin thinning, poor wound healing, growth suppression (pediatric). IV push: arrhythmias, cardiac arrest (rare — infuse over ≥30 min for doses ≥500 mg)
medications.watchFor: IV formulation (Solu-Medrol) contains succinate ester — rapid onset. Do NOT give IV push for high doses (≥500 mg) — infuse over 30–60 min (risk of arrhythmia, hypotension, cardiac arrest). Taper required after >2 weeks of use to avoid adrenal crisis. PJP prophylaxis recommended if ≥20 mg prednisone equivalent daily for ≥4 weeks. Stress dose steroids needed during illness/surgery if on chronic therapy.

medications.mechanism: Active metabolite of prednisone (prednisone requires hepatic conversion to prednisolone). Same mechanism as other glucocorticoids — binds glucocorticoid receptors, modulates gene transcription, suppresses inflammation and immune responses. ~4× potency of hydrocortisone; minimal mineralocorticoid activity.
medications.indications: Same as prednisone — preferred over prednisone in hepatic impairment (no hepatic conversion needed) and in pediatrics (liquid formulation available). Asthma, allergic conditions, autoimmune diseases, inflammatory conditions, nephrotic syndrome (pediatric), croup, ophthalmic inflammation (Pred Forte)
medications.dosing:
- Adult anti-inflammatory: 5–60 mg/day PO in 1–4 divided doses
- Pediatric asthma exacerbation: 1–2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) × 3–5 days
- Pediatric nephrotic syndrome: 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) × 4–6 weeks, then 1.5 mg/kg every other day × 4–6 weeks taper
- Croup: 1–2 mg/kg PO single dose (or dexamethasone preferred)
- Ophthalmic (Pred Forte 1%): 1–2 drops q1h during day, q2h at night; taper as inflammation subsides
- Equivalence: 5 mg prednisolone = 5 mg prednisone = 4 mg methylprednisolone = 0.75 mg dexamethasone = 20 mg hydrocortisone
Route: PO (tablet, liquid — 15 mg/5 mL ODT), ophthalmic
medications.monitoring: Same as prednisone — blood glucose, BP, electrolytes, growth (pediatric), bone density (long-term), ophthalmologic exam, adrenal function if prolonged use
medications.sideEffects: Same as all glucocorticoids. Short-term: hyperglycemia, insomnia, mood changes, appetite increase, fluid retention. Long-term: osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, Cushing syndrome, growth suppression (pediatric), cataracts, glaucoma, immunosuppression, skin thinning, AVN
medications.watchFor: Preferred over prednisone in hepatic impairment (already active — no hepatic conversion needed). Preferred in pediatrics (liquid formulation available, better palatability). Bioequivalent to prednisone in patients with normal liver function. Same taper requirements as prednisone (>2 weeks of use). Orapred solution contains alcohol — consider in pediatric patients.

medications.mechanism: Binds to the β-subunit of bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, inhibiting RNA synthesis. Broad-spectrum activity against gram-positive and gram-negative aerobic and anaerobic enteric bacteria. 0.4% systemic absorption — acts locally in the GI tract. Modulates gut microbiota composition and reduces bacterial translocation and endotoxemia.
medications.indications: FDA-approved: (1) Hepatic encephalopathy — reduction in risk of recurrence of overt HE in adults with advanced liver disease (with lactulose). (2) IBS with diarrhea (IBS-D). (3) Traveler's diarrhea caused by non-invasive E. coli.
medications.dosing:
- Hepatic encephalopathy: 550 mg PO BID continuously (with lactulose)
- IBS-D: 550 mg PO TID × 14 days; may retreat up to 2 additional times if symptoms recur
- Traveler's diarrhea: 200 mg PO TID × 3 days
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Minimal monitoring needed due to negligible systemic absorption. Monitor for C. difficile if diarrhea worsens. LFTs in patients with severe hepatic impairment (increased systemic exposure).
medications.sideEffects: Generally very well tolerated — safety profile comparable to placebo. HE trials: peripheral edema, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, ascites (10–15%). IBS trials: nausea, abdominal pain, headache (10%). Rare: C. difficile infection, hypersensitivity.
medications.watchFor: Low risk of bacterial resistance due to minimal systemic absorption and limited cross-resistance with other antimicrobials. In HE prevention trial: 58% reduction in breakthrough HE episodes (HR 0.42) and 50% reduction in HE-related hospitalizations vs. placebo. In IBS-D: ~9% absolute benefit over placebo for global symptom relief. No dose adjustment needed for renal impairment. Systemic exposure increases in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C) — use with caution.

medications.mechanism: Selective competitive antagonist of arginine vasopressin (AVP) at the V2 receptor in the renal collecting duct. Blocks aquaporin-2 insertion into the apical membrane, promoting free water excretion (aquaresis) without significant electrolyte loss. In ADPKD: reduces cAMP-driven cyst growth.
medications.indications: FDA-approved: (1) Samsca: Clinically significant hypervolemic and euvolemic hyponatremia (SIADH, HF, cirrhosis) resistant to fluid restriction. (2) Jynarque: Slowing kidney function decline in adults at risk of rapidly progressive ADPKD.
medications.dosing:
- Hyponatremia (Samsca): Start 15 mg PO once daily; may increase to 30 mg after ≥24 hr, then to 60 mg max. Do NOT use >30 days. Initiate and re-initiate in hospital only.
- ADPKD (Jynarque): Start 45 mg AM + 15 mg PM (split-dose); titrate monthly to 60/30, then 90/30 mg as tolerated. Continuous use.
- Avoid fluid restriction in first 24 hr (risk of overcorrection)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Serum sodium q6h for first 24 hr, then frequently during titration. LFTs at baseline, 2 weeks, 4 weeks, then monthly for 18 months, then q3 months (ADPKD — hepatotoxicity risk). Fluid intake/output. Renal function.
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING (Samsca): Initiate and re-initiate in hospital — risk of overly rapid correction of serum sodium causing osmotic demyelination syndrome.
- BOXED WARNING (Jynarque): Risk of serious and potentially fatal liver injury — ALT/AST monitoring required.
- Common: Thirst (16%), dry mouth (13%), polyuria/pollakiuria (11%), constipation, asthenia, hyperglycemia
- Serious: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (overcorrection), hepatotoxicity (Jynarque — 4.4% ALT >3× ULN), dehydration, hypernatremia (~10%)
medications.watchFor: SALT-1/SALT-2 trials: 66.6% normalization of Na at day 30 vs. 26.8% placebo. TEMPO 3:4 (ADPKD): 45% reduction in kidney growth, 26% slower eGFR decline over 3 years. REPRISE: 35% slower eGFR decline in later-stage ADPKD. Contraindicated in liver disease (Samsca), hypovolemic hyponatremia, anuric patients, inability to sense/respond to thirst. CYP3A4 substrate — avoid strong inhibitors/inducers. Cost is very high.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic analog of arginine vasopressin with enhanced antidiuretic activity (V2 receptor) and minimal vasopressor activity (V1 receptor). Increases water reabsorption in renal collecting ducts via aquaporin-2 insertion. Also releases von Willebrand factor (vWF) and factor VIII from endothelial stores (Weibel-Palade bodies), enhancing hemostasis.
medications.indications: Central diabetes insipidus (first-line), primary nocturnal enuresis (≥6 years), hemophilia A (mild — factor VIII >5%), von Willebrand disease (type 1), uremic bleeding, prevention of overcorrection in hyponatremia treatment, nocturia
medications.dosing:
- Central DI (oral): Start 0.05 mg BID, titrate to 0.1–0.8 mg/day divided BID-TID
- Central DI (intranasal): 10–40 mcg/day divided 1–3 doses
- Central DI (IV/SC): 1–4 mcg/day divided BID
- Nocturnal enuresis: 0.2 mg PO at bedtime (max 0.6 mg); fluid restriction after evening dose
- Hemophilia A / vWD type 1 (hemostatic): 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 15–30 min (or intranasal 150 mcg per nostril for >50 kg, 150 mcg one nostril for 50 kg) — 30 min before procedure
- Overcorrection rescue (hyponatremia): 2 mcg IV q8h with D5W
Route: PO, intranasal, IV, SC
medications.monitoring: Serum sodium (risk of hyponatremia — especially in elderly, children, patients with excessive fluid intake). Fluid intake/output. Factor VIII and vWF levels (hemostatic use). Urine osmolality and volume (DI).
medications.sideEffects: Hyponatremia/water intoxication (most serious — can cause seizures, especially in children and elderly; restrict fluids with evening doses), headache, nausea, flushing, abdominal cramps, nasal congestion (intranasal). Rare: hypertension (transient), thrombotic events (theoretical — vWF release), tachyphylaxis with repeated hemostatic dosing (vWF stores deplete after 2–3 doses within 48 hr)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in type 2B vWD (may worsen thrombocytopenia) and type 3 vWD (no vWF stores to release). Tachyphylaxis occurs with repeated hemostatic doses — limit to 2–3 doses within 48 hr. Fluid restriction essential with nocturnal enuresis dosing. Test dose recommended before hemostatic use to confirm adequate factor VIII/vWF response. Intranasal absorption unreliable during URI — switch to parenteral.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic octapeptide analog of somatostatin with longer half-life (1.5 hr SC vs. 1–3 min for native somatostatin). Binds somatostatin receptors (primarily SSTR2 and SSTR5). Inhibits secretion of GH, IGF-1, insulin, glucagon, gastrin, VIP, secretin, motilin, and pancreatic polypeptide. Reduces splanchnic blood flow, portal pressure, and GI motility. Inhibits pancreatic exocrine secretion.
medications.indications: FDA-approved: Acromegaly, carcinoid tumors (diarrhea/flushing), VIPomas. Off-label (widely used): Acute variceal hemorrhage, hepatorenal syndrome (with midodrine + albumin), refractory diarrhea (short bowel syndrome, chemotherapy-induced, AIDS-related), sulfonylurea overdose (refractory hypoglycemia), pancreatic fistula, chylothorax, dumping syndrome
medications.dosing:
- Acromegaly: Start 50 mcg SC TID, titrate to 100–500 mcg SC TID; LAR depot: 20 mg IM q4 weeks
- Carcinoid: 100–600 mcg/day SC in 2–4 divided doses; LAR: 20–30 mg IM q4 weeks
- VIPoma: 200–300 mcg/day SC in 2–4 divided doses
- Variceal hemorrhage: 50 mcg IV bolus, then 25–50 mcg/hr continuous infusion × 2–5 days
- Sulfonylurea overdose: 50–100 mcg SC/IV q6–12h (or 25–50 mcg/hr infusion)
- Refractory diarrhea: 100–150 mcg SC TID
Route: SC, IV, IM (LAR depot only)
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (hypo- and hyperglycemia), thyroid function (TSH — suppression), gallbladder ultrasound (cholelithiasis with long-term use), LFTs, vitamin B12 (long-term), stool fat (steatorrhea)
medications.sideEffects: GI (nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea/steatorrhea, flatulence — 30–60%), cholelithiasis/biliary sludge (15–30% with long-term use — due to gallbladder stasis), injection site pain (7.7%), hyperglycemia (16% in acromegaly) or hypoglycemia (3%), hypothyroidism (12% in acromegaly), bradycardia, pancreatitis, fat malabsorption, vitamin B12 deficiency
medications.watchFor: For variceal bleeding, octreotide is used as adjunct to endoscopic therapy — reduces rebleeding risk. LAR (long-acting release) depot formulation allows monthly dosing but requires 2 weeks to reach therapeutic levels — bridge with SC during initiation. Tachyphylaxis may occur with prolonged use. Dose adjustment not needed for renal impairment. Hepatic impairment: half-life may increase in cirrhosis.

medications.mechanism: Recombinant form of urate oxidase (uricase), an enzyme absent in humans. Catalyzes enzymatic oxidation of uric acid to allantoin (5–10× more soluble than uric acid), which is readily excreted renally. Reduces uric acid levels much more rapidly than xanthine oxidase inhibitors (within 4 hr vs. days).
medications.indications: FDA-approved: Initial management of plasma uric acid levels in pediatric and adult patients with leukemia, lymphoma, and solid tumor malignancies receiving anticancer therapy expected to result in tumor lysis and elevated uric acid.
medications.dosing:
- FDA label: 0.2 mg/kg IV over 30 min daily for up to 5 days
- Practice (NCCN/evidence-based): Fixed dose 3–6 mg IV single dose (one dose is frequently adequate); re-dose only if uric acid rises again. Fixed dosing is very effective and reduces cost.
- Administer before chemotherapy initiation
Route: IV infusion over 30 min only (never bolus)
medications.monitoring: Uric acid levels — MUST collect blood in pre-chilled heparin tubes and immediately place on ice; assay within 4 hr (rasburicase degrades uric acid ex vivo at room temperature → spuriously low readings). Electrolytes (K⁺, PO₄, Ca²⁺). Renal function. G6PD status before first dose.
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: (1) Hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis (1% but can be fatal — immediately and permanently discontinue). (2) Hemolysis in G6PD deficiency (hydrogen peroxide is a byproduct — contraindicated). (3) Methemoglobinemia (1%). (4) Interference with uric acid measurements (special sample handling required).
- Common (≥20%, with concurrent chemo): Vomiting, nausea, fever, peripheral edema, anxiety, headache, abdominal pain, constipation, diarrhea, hypophosphatemia
- Serious: Anaphylaxis, hemolysis, methemoglobinemia, neutralizing antibodies (up to 18% with re-exposure)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency — screen patients at higher risk (African, Mediterranean ancestry) before use. Single course of treatment only (FDA label) — re-exposure increases risk of hypersensitivity and neutralizing antibodies. Cannot be used with allopurinol simultaneously (allopurinol blocks substrate for rasburicase). Dramatically more effective than allopurinol at lowering uric acid but no clear reduction in renal complications in prophylaxis setting. Very expensive — fixed dosing (3–6 mg) is cost-effective alternative to weight-based dosing.

medications.mechanism: Produced by controlled pyrolysis of carbonaceous material (wood, coconut shell) followed by activation (steam/chemical) to create a highly porous structure with enormous surface area (~1000 m²/g). Adsorbs toxins in the GI lumen via van der Waals forces, preventing systemic absorption. Does NOT absorb all substances — ineffective for small, charged, or inorganic molecules.
medications.indications: GI decontamination in acute oral poisoning/overdose. Effective for most drugs and organic toxins. Multiple-dose activated charcoal (MDAC) for enhanced elimination of specific toxins.
medications.dosing:
- Single-dose (adults): 50–100 g PO or via NG/OG tube
- Single-dose (children 1–12 yr): 25–50 g or 0.5–1 g/kg
- Single-dose (infants 1 yr): 10–25 g or 0.5–1 g/kg
- Optimal ratio: 10:1 (charcoal:toxin) by weight
- MDAC: Initial 50–100 g, then 25–50 g q4–6h (or ≥12.5 g/hr) — for carbamazepine, dapsone, phenobarbital, theophylline, cardiac glycosides, colchicine, phenytoin, thallium
- Administer as early as possible; traditionally within 1 hr, but 2026 guidelines now recommend up to 6 hr for many poisons, and beyond 6 hr if ongoing absorption suspected (pharmacobezoars, modified-release formulations)
Route: PO, NG/OG tube
medications.monitoring: Airway protection (aspiration risk). Bowel sounds (contraindicated in ileus/obstruction). Serial drug levels if available. Electrolytes with MDAC.
medications.sideEffects: Vomiting (most common — 12–20%), aspiration pneumonitis/pneumonia (most serious — especially with impaired consciousness), constipation, black stools, bowel obstruction (rare, with MDAC), corneal abrasion (if contact with eyes)
medications.watchFor: INEFFECTIVE for: alcohols (methanol, ethylene glycol, ethanol, isopropanol), iron, lithium, potassium, acids/alkalis, hydrocarbons, heavy metals (lead, mercury, arsenic), cyanide. Do NOT administer if: unprotected airway, impaired consciousness without intubation, bowel obstruction/perforation, caustic ingestion. Do NOT intubate solely to give charcoal unless life-threatening toxicity expected. Sorbitol-containing formulations should NOT be used for MDAC (dehydration, electrolyte imbalance). The "1-hour rule" is now considered obsolete — benefit extends well beyond 1 hr for many poisons.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic form of folate. Converted to tetrahydrofolate (THF), which serves as a one-carbon carrier in nucleotide synthesis (thymidylate and purine synthesis required for DNA replication), amino acid metabolism (homocysteine → methionine), and methylation reactions. Essential for rapidly dividing cells (hematopoietic, fetal neural tube).
