medications.indications: Megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency, neural tube defect prevention (periconceptional), adjunct with methotrexate (toxicity reduction), hemolytic anemias (increased folate demand), chronic hemolysis (SCD, thalassemia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia), alcoholism, malabsorption syndromes, dialysis patients
medications.dosing:
- Folate deficiency anemia: Up to 1 mg PO daily (therapeutic); maintenance 0.4 mg/day adults
- Neural tube defect prevention: 0.4–0.8 mg PO daily for all persons who could become pregnant (USPSTF Grade A); start ≥1 month before conception through first trimester
- High-risk NTD prevention (prior NTD-affected pregnancy): 4 mg PO daily
- Methotrexate adjunct: 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg weekly on non-MTX day)
- Chronic hemolysis: 1–5 mg PO daily
- Pediatric: 0.1 mg/day (infants), up to 0.3 mg/day (4 yr), 0.4 mg/day (≥4 yr)
- Pregnancy/lactation: 0.8 mg/day
Route: PO (preferred), IM, IV, SC
medications.monitoring: CBC with reticulocyte count (response). Serum folate and B12 levels. Rule out B12 deficiency before treating with folic acid alone (>0.1 mg/day can mask B12 deficiency).
medications.sideEffects: Very well tolerated. Rare: allergic reactions (rash, bronchospasm), GI upset (nausea, bloating — at high doses ≥15 mg). May decrease serum B12 levels with prolonged therapy. May antagonize anticonvulsant effect of phenytoin.
medications.watchFor: CRITICAL: Folic acid >0.1 mg/day can correct the hematologic manifestations of B12 deficiency while allowing neurologic damage (subacute combined degeneration) to progress — always rule out B12 deficiency first. Tolerable upper intake level: 1 mg/day from supplements (National Academy of Sciences). Drug interactions: methotrexate (antagonism — this is therapeutic), phenytoin (reduced anticonvulsant effect), sulfasalazine (impairs folate absorption).

medications.drugClass:
Blood products / Coagulation factor replacement
CRYOPRECIPITATE:
medications.mechanism: Contains all coagulation factors, fibrinogen (~2–4 mg/mL), albumin, and immunoglobulins. Each unit ~200–250 mL. Must be ABO-compatible.
medications.indications: Active bleeding with multiple coagulation factor deficiency (DIC, liver disease, massive transfusion), warfarin reversal (when PCC unavailable — PCC preferred), TTP (therapeutic plasma exchange), coagulopathy of liver disease with bleeding/pre-procedure
medications.dosing:
10–15 mL/kg IV (typically 4–6 units in adults). Target: PT/INR 1.5× control. Each unit raises factor levels by ~3–5%.
Route: IV (both)
medications.monitoring: Fibrinogen level (cryoprecipitate), PT/INR/aPTT (FFP), signs of transfusion reaction, volume status
medications.sideEffects: Transfusion reactions (febrile, allergic, anaphylactic), TRALI, TACO (volume overload — especially FFP at 10–15 mL/kg), infection transmission (not pathogen-reduced in most countries), citrate toxicity (hypocalcemia with rapid/massive transfusion), alloimmunization
medications.watchFor: Cryoprecipitate does NOT undergo pathogen inactivation — infection risk exists (withdrawn in some European countries in favor of fibrinogen concentrate). FFP requires thawing (~30 min) — delays availability. For warfarin reversal, 4-factor PCC is preferred over FFP (faster, smaller volume, more effective). Fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP) is an alternative to cryoprecipitate — standardized dosing, pathogen-reduced, but more expensive.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits fungal lanosterol demethylase (sterol 14α-demethylase / CYP51) → blocks ergosterol synthesis → disrupts fungal cell membrane integrity. Highly selective for fungal CYP51 over human CYP enzymes → minimal drug-drug interactions and reduced hepatotoxicity compared to azole antifungals
medications.indications: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) — to reduce incidence of recurrence in females with a history of RVVC who are NOT of reproductive potential
medications.dosing:
Two approved regimens:
- Oteseconazole-only regimen: 600 mg PO on Day 1, 450 mg PO on Day 2, then 150 mg PO weekly for 11 weeks (starting Day 14)
- Fluconazole/oteseconazole regimen: Fluconazole 150 mg PO on Days 1, 4, and 7; then oteseconazole 150 mg PO daily Days 14–20; then 150 mg PO weekly for 11 weeks starting Day 28
- Take with food
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Clinical response (recurrence of VVC episodes); pregnancy testing (contraindicated in pregnancy)
KEY TOXICITIES: Generally well tolerated. Headache (7.4%), nausea (3.6%). Adverse event rates similar to comparator groups in phase 3 trials. No significant hepatotoxicity signal (unlike other azoles)
medications.sideEffects: Generally well tolerated — adverse event rates similar to placebo. Headache (7.1%), nausea (3.7%), abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, vulvovaginal burning. No QT prolongation. No hepatotoxicity signal (unlike other azoles).
medications.watchFor: CONTRAINDICATED in females of reproductive potential — teratogenic risk (animal studies). Extremely long half-life (~138 days) — drug persists in body for months after discontinuation; effective contraception required during and for a prolonged period after treatment. Phase 3 trials: oteseconazole reduced RVVC recurrence to 3.9–6.7% vs 39–42% placebo through 48–50 weeks. Noninferior to fluconazole for acute VVC treatment. Active against fluconazole-resistant Candida species. Minimal CYP inhibition (unlike fluconazole) — fewer drug interactions. FDA-approved 2022. Ibrexafungerp (triterpenoid glucan synthase inhibitor) is an alternative for RVVC

medications.drugClass: Long-acting anti-RSV monoclonal antibody (recombinant human IgG1κ)
medications.mechanism: Binds to the prefusion conformation of RSV fusion (F) protein → prevents viral entry into host cells by blocking membrane fusion. YTE modification in Fc region extends serum half-life to ~71 days (vs ~21 days for palivizumab) → single dose provides season-long protection
medications.indications: Prevention of RSV-associated lower respiratory tract disease in neonates and infants born during or entering their first RSV season (all infants 8 months); children 8–19 months at increased risk for severe RSV disease entering their second RSV season
medications.dosing:
- Infants 5 kg: 50 mg IM single dose
- Infants ≥5 kg (first RSV season): 100 mg IM single dose
- Children 8–19 months (second RSV season, high-risk): 200 mg IM (two 100 mg injections at different sites)
- Administer before or during RSV season (October–March in most of continental US)
- Can be co-administered with routine childhood vaccines
Route: IM (single dose)
medications.monitoring:
Observe for hypersensitivity reactions post-injection; no routine lab monitoring required
KEY TOXICITIES: Generally very well tolerated. Injection site reactions (0.3%), rash (0.9%), fever. No increase in serious adverse events vs placebo in clinical trials
medications.sideEffects: Very well tolerated. Injection site reactions (0.3%), rash (0.9%), fever. Post-marketing: rare hypersensitivity reactions (urticaria, dyspnoea, cyanosis, hypotonia). No increase in serious adverse events vs. placebo in clinical trials.
medications.watchFor: ACIP recommends nirsevimab for ALL infants 8 months born during or entering first RSV season (August 2023). Efficacy: 79% reduction in medically attended RSV LRTI, 80% reduction in RSV hospitalization, 90% reduction in ICU admission. Real-world effectiveness (NVSN 2023–24): 90% against RSV hospitalization. NOT needed for most infants whose mother received RSVpreF vaccine ≥14 days before birth (but may be considered in high-risk situations). Replaces palivizumab for most indications (palivizumab required monthly dosing × 5 months, was limited to high-risk preterm infants). Clesrovimab (Enflonsia) is a newer alternative anti-RSV monoclonal antibody approved 2025. AAP recommends any licensed RSV immunization product without preference between nirsevimab and clesrovimab

medications.mechanism: Binds to presynaptic cholinergic nerve terminals → internalized via receptor-mediated endocytosis → light chain cleaves SNAP-25 (synaptosomal-associated protein 25 kDa) → prevents SNARE complex assembly → blocks acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junction → reversible chemical denervation. Also inhibits sensory neuropeptide release (substance P, CGRP) — basis for migraine and pain indications. Effect onset: 2–7 days; peak: 4–6 weeks; duration: 3–4 months (then new nerve sprouts restore transmission)
medications.dosing:
- Chronic migraine: 155–195 units IM across 31–39 injection sites (head/neck) every 12 weeks
- Cervical dystonia: 50–300 units IM divided among affected muscles; max 300 units per session; minimum 3 months between treatments
- Upper limb spasticity (adult): 10–60 units per muscle site; max 240 units per session
- Lower limb spasticity (adult): 25–75 units per muscle site; max 400 units per session
- Overactive bladder: 100 units intradetrusor (20 injection sites of 5 units each)
- Neurogenic detrusor overactivity: 200 units intradetrusor
- Axillary hyperhidrosis: 50 units per axilla (10–15 injection sites)
- Blepharospasm: 25 units per eye; max 100 units
- Glabellar lines: 20 units (5 injection sites of 4 units each)
- Pediatric spasticity (≥2 yrs): 3–8 U/kg TOTAL per session (lower limb ≤8 U/kg, upper limb ≤6 U/kg); max 8 U/kg (300 U) cycle 1, 10 U/kg (340 U) later cycles; per-muscle 1–6 U/kg within the session cap
Route: IM, intradetrusor (bladder)
medications.monitoring:
Clinical response; dysphagia assessment (cervical dystonia); post-void residual (bladder indications); respiratory function (patients with neuromuscular disorders)
KEY TOXICITIES: BOXED WARNING — distant spread of toxin effect (can cause botulism-like symptoms: generalized weakness, dysphagia, dysphonia, respiratory compromise, death — especially in children treated for spasticity and in patients with underlying neuromuscular disorders). Local: injection site pain, bruising, weakness of adjacent muscles. Cervical dystonia: dysphagia (19–33%), neck weakness, dry mouth. Bladder: UTI (18–49%), urinary retention (5–17% — may require catheterization). Migraine: neck pain, headache, eyelid ptosis. Cosmetic: ptosis, asymmetry, headache. Neutralizing antibody formation (2–5% — reduces efficacy with repeated treatments; minimize by using lowest effective dose and longest feasible intervals)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Distant spread of toxin effect — botulinum toxin may spread from injection site to produce symptoms consistent with botulism (generalized muscle weakness, diplopia, ptosis, dysphagia, dysphonia, dysarthria, urinary incontinence, respiratory compromise). Risk greatest with high doses and in patients with neuromuscular disorders. Deaths reported (mostly in pediatric cerebral palsy — unapproved use).
- Common by indication: Chronic migraine — neck pain (9%), headache (5%), eyelid ptosis (4%), muscular weakness (4%). Cervical dystonia — dysphagia (19%), neck pain, headache. Spasticity — focal weakness. OAB — UTI (18%), dysuria (9%), urinary retention (6%). Cosmetic — eyelid ptosis (3%), headache, injection site reactions.
- Serious: Anaphylaxis (rare), aspiration pneumonia (from dysphagia), respiratory failure, antibody formation with loss of response
medications.watchFor: Units are NOT interchangeable between Botox, Dysport, and Xeomin — different potency assays (roughly Botox 1 unit ≈ Dysport 2.5–3 units ≈ Xeomin 1 unit, but formal conversion ratios are not established). Contraindicated in infection at injection site and known hypersensitivity. Use with caution in myasthenia gravis, Lambert-Eaton, ALS (risk of severe systemic weakness). Aminoglycosides and other drugs that interfere with neuromuscular transmission may potentiate effect. For chronic migraine: must meet criteria (≥15 days/month, ≥4 hours/day, for >3 months); NOT indicated for episodic migraine. Immunogenicity (neutralizing antibodies) is a concern with long-term use — Xeomin (free of complexing proteins) may have lower immunogenicity
medications.indications:
Cervical dystonia, blepharospasm, strabismus, spasticity (upper and lower limb — adults and pediatric ≥2 years), chronic migraine prophylaxis (≥15 headache days/month), overactive bladder (refractory to anticholinergics), neurogenic detrusor overactivity, severe primary axillary hyperhidrosis, cosmetic (glabellar lines, crow's feet, forehead lines)
DOSING (units are NOT interchangeable between products):
- Chronic migraine (Botox): 155 units total, divided across 31 injection sites (5 units/site) in head/neck muscles every 12 weeks
- Cervical dystonia (Botox): 150–300 units divided among affected muscles; (Dysport): 500 units initial
- Upper limb spasticity (Botox): 150–400 units divided among affected muscles
- Overactive bladder (Botox): 100 units injected into detrusor muscle via cystoscopy
- Neurogenic detrusor overactivity (Botox): 200 units into detrusor
- Axillary hyperhidrosis (Botox): 50 units per axilla (intradermal)
- Glabellar lines (Botox): 20 units total (5 sites × 4 units); (Dysport): 50 units
- Re-treatment interval: minimum 12 weeks for all indications

medications.mechanism: Forms intrastrand and interstrand DNA crosslinks → inhibits DNA replication and transcription → triggers apoptosis; cell-cycle nonspecific
medications.indications: Testicular cancer (curative — BEP regimen), ovarian cancer, bladder cancer, HNSCC, NSCLC, cervical cancer, esophageal cancer, gastric cancer
medications.dosing:
- Testicular (BEP): 20 mg/m² IV daily × 5 days, every 21 days
- Ovarian: 75–100 mg/m² IV every 3–4 weeks
- Bladder: 50–70 mg/m² IV every 3–4 weeks
- HNSCC (with radiation): 100 mg/m² IV every 3 weeks × 3 cycles
- Requires aggressive pre- and post-hydration (≥1–2 L NS) + mannitol diuresis
Route: IV (slow infusion)
medications.monitoring:
Creatinine/BUN/electrolytes (Mg²⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) before each cycle; audiometry at baseline and periodically; CBC; LFTs
KEY TOXICITIES: Nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting — up to 30% AKI; requires aggressive hydration), ototoxicity (irreversible hearing loss in 40–60%; cumulative), severe nausea/vomiting (highly emetogenic — requires triple antiemetic: NK1 antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone), peripheral neuropathy (cumulative, sensory), myelosuppression, hypomagnesemia, SIADH, anaphylaxis
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: Nephrotoxicity (dose-related, cumulative — 20–30%), peripheral neuropathy (dose-related, may be irreversible), severe nausea/vomiting (highly emetogenic — requires triple antiemetic prophylaxis), myelosuppression (25–30%)
- Other: Ototoxicity (tinnitus, high-frequency hearing loss — cumulative, irreversible; 40–60% in pediatric patients), hypomagnesemia (requiring supplementation), SIADH, secondary AML, anaphylaxis, ocular toxicity
medications.watchFor: Most emetogenic chemotherapy agent — always requires prophylactic antiemetics. Hydration is mandatory (≥1 L NS pre and post). Contraindicated in pre-existing renal impairment or hearing loss (relative). Amifostine may reduce nephrotoxicity. Sodium thiosulfate approved for ototoxicity prevention in pediatric localized hepatoblastoma. Revolutionized testicular cancer (cure rate >95% with BEP)

medications.mechanism: Same as cisplatin (DNA crosslinks) but slower activation kinetics due to cyclobutanedicarboxylate ligand → less nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity
medications.indications: Ovarian cancer, NSCLC, SCLC, breast cancer (TNBC), endometrial cancer, head and neck cancer, many combination regimens
medications.dosing:
- Dosed by AUC (Calvert formula): Dose (mg) = target AUC × (GFR + 25)
- Common targets: AUC 5–6 every 3 weeks (most solid tumors); AUC 2 weekly
- High-dose (HCT conditioning): AUC 16–20
- GFR capped at 125 mL/min per FDA guidance to prevent overdosing in patients with high creatinine clearance
Route: IV (infusion over 15–60 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
CBC with differential (nadir day 21, recovery day 28); renal function; electrolytes
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — especially thrombocytopenia, more severe than cisplatin), nausea/vomiting (moderately emetogenic), hypersensitivity reactions (increasing risk after ≥6 cycles — up to 27% after 7+ cycles), nephrotoxicity (less than cisplatin but still present), neurotoxicity (less than cisplatin), ototoxicity (less than cisplatin)
medications.sideEffects:
- Myelosuppression (dose-limiting): Thrombocytopenia (25% grade 3–4 — more prominent than with cisplatin), neutropenia (16% grade 3–4), anemia. Nadir day 21.
- Nausea/vomiting: Moderately emetogenic (less than cisplatin)
- Peripheral neuropathy: 4–10% (less than cisplatin; increases with prior platinum exposure and age)
- Hypersensitivity: 2% (increased risk after ≥6 cycles or prior platinum exposure — can be severe/anaphylactic)
- Nephrotoxicity: Minimal at standard doses (major advantage over cisplatin)
- Ototoxicity: Rare at standard doses
- Hepatotoxicity: Transaminase elevation
medications.watchFor: Only chemotherapy agent dosed by target AUC rather than BSA. Calvert formula requires accurate GFR estimation. Less nephrotoxic and emetogenic than cisplatin — often substituted when cisplatin not tolerated. Hypersensitivity reactions increase with repeated exposure — desensitization protocols available. Does not require aggressive hydration like cisplatin

medications.mechanism: DACH-platinum complex forms DNA adducts that are not recognized by mismatch repair → effective in cisplatin-resistant tumors; cell-cycle nonspecific
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (FOLFOX, CAPOX — adjuvant and metastatic), gastric/GEJ cancer, pancreatic cancer (FOLFIRINOX), hepatobiliary cancers
medications.dosing:
- FOLFOX: 85 mg/m² IV over 2 hours on day 1, every 2 weeks
- CAPOX: 130 mg/m² IV over 2 hours on day 1, every 3 weeks
- FOLFIRINOX: 85 mg/m² IV on day 1, every 2 weeks
- Adjuvant CRC: 3 months (CAPOX) or 6 months (FOLFOX) per IDEA collaboration
Route: IV (infuse over 2 hours; do NOT use chloride-containing solutions — causes degradation; use D5W)
medications.monitoring:
CBC; renal function; hepatic function; neurologic assessment before each cycle
KEY TOXICITIES: Peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting — 70–90% any grade; acute cold-triggered dysesthesias within hours of infusion + chronic cumulative sensory neuropathy), myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), nausea/vomiting (moderately emetogenic), hepatotoxicity (sinusoidal obstruction syndrome with prolonged use), hypersensitivity (increasing after ≥6 cycles), laryngopharyngeal dysesthesia (acute, cold-triggered)
medications.sideEffects:
- Peripheral neuropathy (signature toxicity — two forms):
- Acute (56%): Cold-triggered paresthesias of hands, feet, perioral area, throat within hours; pharyngolaryngeal dysesthesia (1–2% grade 3–4); resolves within 14 days
- Chronic/persistent (48%): Cumulative sensory neuropathy — paresthesias, proprioception loss; may interfere with daily activities; partially reversible over months-years after discontinuation
- Myelosuppression: Neutropenia (41–44% grade 3–4), thrombocytopenia, anemia
- GI: Nausea/vomiting (moderately emetogenic), diarrhea, stomatitis
- Hepatotoxicity: Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD), transaminase elevation
- Hypersensitivity: 2–3% grade 3–4 (increased after ≥6 cycles)
- Other: QT prolongation, PRES, rhabdomyolysis (rare), pulmonary fibrosis (rare)
medications.watchFor: Acute neuropathy is cold-triggered — advise patients to avoid cold drinks/foods and cold air for 3–5 days after infusion. Chronic neuropathy is cumulative and may be irreversible — consider stop-and-go strategy (oxaliplatin holiday after 6–8 cycles, reintroduce if needed). Duloxetine is the only agent with evidence for treatment of established CIPN. Do NOT infuse in saline (chloride degrades oxaliplatin). 3-month adjuvant CAPOX is noninferior to 6-month FOLFOX for low-risk stage III CRC

medications.mechanism: Intercalates into DNA → inhibits topoisomerase II → generates free radicals → causes DNA strand breaks and oxidative damage; cell-cycle nonspecific
medications.indications: Breast cancer (AC, TAC), lymphomas (CHOP, ABVD), sarcomas, ovarian cancer, bladder cancer, thyroid cancer, multiple myeloma, many others. Liposomal: ovarian cancer, AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma, multiple myeloma
medications.dosing:
- Single agent: 60–75 mg/m² IV every 21 days
- AC (breast): doxorubicin 60 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m² every 21 days
- CHOP (lymphoma): doxorubicin 50 mg/m² IV day 1
- Liposomal (Doxil): 50 mg/m² IV every 28 days (ovarian); 20 mg/m² IV every 21 days (Kaposi)
- Lifetime cumulative dose limit: 450–550 mg/m² (cardiotoxicity threshold)
Route: IV (bolus or infusion; VESICANT — extravasation causes severe tissue necrosis)
medications.monitoring:
CBC; LFTs; LVEF (echocardiogram or MUGA) at baseline and periodically (every 3 months or before each cycle after cumulative dose >300 mg/m²)
KEY TOXICITIES: Cardiotoxicity (dose-limiting — acute: arrhythmias, pericarditis-myocarditis; chronic: dilated cardiomyopathy/CHF, cumulative dose-dependent, risk increases sharply >450 mg/m²), myelosuppression (neutropenia nadir day 10–14), alopecia (nearly universal), nausea/vomiting (highly emetogenic), mucositis/stomatitis, red-orange discoloration of urine, radiation recall dermatitis, secondary leukemia (AML/MDS — late)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: (1) Cardiomyopathy — cumulative, dose-dependent; incidence 1–2% at 300 mg/m², 5–8% at 450 mg/m², 6–20% at 500 mg/m²; may present as acute (arrhythmias) or delayed CHF (months to years after treatment). (2) Secondary AML/MDS. (3) Extravasation → severe tissue necrosis (vesicant — apply ice, consider dexrazoxane rescue). (4) Severe myelosuppression.
- Common: Alopecia (nearly universal), nausea/vomiting (moderately-highly emetogenic), mucositis/stomatitis, myelosuppression (nadir day 10–14), red-orange discoloration of urine
- Serious: Radiation recall dermatitis, tumor lysis syndrome, hepatotoxicity
medications.watchFor: Lifetime cumulative dose limit 450–550 mg/m² — monitor LVEF closely. Dexrazoxane (iron chelator) may be used as cardioprotectant when cumulative dose >300 mg/m² (controversial — may reduce antitumor efficacy). Paclitaxel increases doxorubicin AUC by ~30% when given concurrently (enhanced cardiotoxicity) — give doxorubicin first, then paclitaxel; this interaction does NOT occur with docetaxel. VESICANT — extravasation antidote is dexrazoxane (Totect) or topical DMSO. Liposomal formulation has less cardiotoxicity but causes palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome)

medications.mechanism: Binds β-tubulin → stabilizes microtubules → prevents depolymerization → arrests cell cycle in G2/M phase → apoptosis
medications.indications: Breast cancer, ovarian cancer, NSCLC, Kaposi sarcoma, pancreatic cancer (nab-paclitaxel), gastric cancer, HNSCC, bladder cancer
medications.dosing:
- Conventional paclitaxel: 175 mg/m² IV over 3 hours every 21 days; or 80 mg/m² IV weekly
- Nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane): 260 mg/m² IV every 21 days; or 100–125 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15 every 28 days
- Premedication required for conventional paclitaxel: dexamethasone 20 mg PO (12 and 6 hours before), diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, ranitidine/famotidine IV (30 minutes before) — to prevent hypersensitivity from Cremophor EL vehicle
- Nab-paclitaxel does NOT require premedication (no Cremophor)
Route: IV
medications.monitoring:
CBC (nadir day 8–11); peripheral neuropathy assessment; LFTs (dose reduce for hepatic impairment)
KEY TOXICITIES: Peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting — sensory > motor; cumulative; more frequent than docetaxel), myelosuppression (neutropenia), hypersensitivity reactions (Cremophor EL vehicle — conventional formulation; ~2% despite premedication), alopecia (universal), myalgia/arthralgia, cardiac conduction abnormalities (bradycardia), nausea/vomiting (low-moderate emetogenicity)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Anaphylaxis and severe hypersensitivity (2–4% despite premedication — due to cremophor EL vehicle; fatal reactions reported). Do not treat if ANC 1500 (solid tumors) or 1000 (KS).
- Common: Neutropenia (dose-limiting), alopecia (nearly universal), peripheral neuropathy (cumulative, sensory > motor), myalgia/arthralgia (24–72 hr post-infusion), nausea, diarrhea, mucositis
- Serious: Cardiac conduction abnormalities (bradycardia, heart block), severe neuropathy, pneumonitis
- Nab-paclitaxel: No cremophor → no premedication needed, no hypersensitivity; higher neuropathy rate
medications.watchFor: Conventional paclitaxel contains Cremophor EL — requires premedication and non-PVC infusion sets (DEHP leaching). Nab-paclitaxel eliminates Cremophor-related issues. Paclitaxel increases doxorubicin levels when co-administered — give doxorubicin FIRST. CYP2C8 and CYP3A4 substrate — many drug interactions. Weekly dosing may have better tolerability than Q3-week. Dose reduce for hepatic impairment (bilirubin >1.5 mg/dL)

medications.mechanism: Same as paclitaxel — binds β-tubulin, stabilizes microtubules, arrests mitosis; ~2× more potent than paclitaxel in vitro
medications.indications: Breast cancer (TAC, TC), NSCLC, castration-resistant prostate cancer (with prednisone), gastric/GEJ cancer, HNSCC (induction — TPF)
medications.dosing:
- Breast (single agent): 60–100 mg/m² IV every 21 days
- TAC (breast adjuvant): 75 mg/m² + doxorubicin 50 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 500 mg/m² every 21 days
- TC (breast): 75 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m² every 21 days
- Prostate (CRPC): 75 mg/m² IV every 21 days + prednisone 5 mg PO BID
- NSCLC: 75 mg/m² IV every 21 days
- HNSCC (TPF induction): 75 mg/m² + cisplatin 75 mg/m² + 5-FU 750 mg/m²/day × 5 days
- Premedication: dexamethasone 8 mg PO BID × 3 days starting day before infusion (reduces fluid retention and hypersensitivity)
Route: IV (infusion over 1 hour)
medications.monitoring:
CBC (nadir day 7–10); LFTs before each cycle (contraindicated if bilirubin >ULN or AST/ALT >1.5× ULN with ALP >2.5× ULN); fluid retention assessment
KEY TOXICITIES: Neutropenia (dose-limiting — grade 3/4 in 66% with TAC; febrile neutropenia 25%), fluid retention (cumulative — peripheral edema, pleural effusion, weight gain; reduced by dexamethasone premedication), hypersensitivity reactions, peripheral neuropathy (less than paclitaxel), skin/nail toxicity (onycholysis, skin rash), alopecia (universal), mucositis/stomatitis, asthenia, lacrimation (canalicular stenosis)
medications.sideEffects:
- Common: Neutropenia (dose-limiting — 75% grade 3–4; febrile neutropenia 11%), fluid retention syndrome (cumulative — peripheral edema, pleural effusion, ascites, weight gain; mitigated by dexamethasone premedication), alopecia, skin/nail changes (onycholysis, skin rash), fatigue, myalgia, diarrhea, stomatitis
- Less common: Peripheral neuropathy (less than paclitaxel), hypersensitivity (despite premedication), hepatotoxicity, lacrimation/epiphora (lacrimal duct stenosis)
- Serious: Severe neutropenia with sepsis, treatment-related mortality (~2% in prostate cancer trials)
medications.watchFor: Dexamethasone premedication is MANDATORY (reduces fluid retention and hypersensitivity). Does NOT enhance doxorubicin cardiotoxicity (unlike paclitaxel). Contraindicated with significant hepatic impairment. G-CSF prophylaxis recommended with TAC regimen (febrile neutropenia rate >20%). Fluid retention is cumulative — worsens after cumulative dose >400 mg/m²

medications.mechanism: Converted to active metabolites → inhibits thymidylate synthase (blocks DNA synthesis) and incorporates into RNA (disrupts RNA processing); S-phase specific
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (FOLFOX, FOLFIRI, FOLFOXIRI), gastric/GEJ cancer, pancreatic cancer, HNSCC, breast cancer (CMF), esophageal cancer, anal cancer
medications.dosing:
- Bolus: 400–500 mg/m² IV bolus (with leucovorin)
- Continuous infusion: 2400 mg/m² IV over 46 hours (FOLFOX/FOLFIRI)
- HNSCC (with cisplatin + radiation): 1000 mg/m²/day continuous infusion × 4 days
- Leucovorin enhances 5-FU activity (stabilizes ternary complex with thymidylate synthase)
Route: IV (bolus or continuous infusion)
medications.monitoring:
CBC; DPD deficiency testing before initiation (DPYD genotyping or uracil level — FDA recommends testing); mucositis assessment; hand-foot syndrome assessment
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), mucositis/stomatitis (dose-limiting for bolus), diarrhea (dose-limiting for infusional), hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia — more with infusional), cardiotoxicity (coronary vasospasm — chest pain, ST changes; 1–18% incidence), DPD deficiency toxicity (potentially fatal — severe mucositis, diarrhea, neutropenia, neurotoxicity)
medications.sideEffects:
- Infusion-related: Diarrhea (dose-limiting for infusional), mucositis/stomatitis (dose-limiting for bolus), myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia — more with infusional), nausea
- Cardiac: Coronary vasospasm/angina (1–18%), cardiomyopathy, arrhythmias, sudden death — more common with continuous infusion; may occur in patients without prior cardiac disease
- DPD deficiency: Patients with dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency have severely impaired 5-FU metabolism → life-threatening toxicity (severe mucositis, diarrhea, neutropenia, neurotoxicity). DPYD genotyping identifies ~3–8% of patients who are partial deficient and ~0.1–0.5% who are complete deficient.
medications.watchFor: DPD deficiency screening is now recommended before initiation — homozygous DPD deficiency is contraindication (fatal toxicity); heterozygous requires 50% dose reduction. Infusional 5-FU has different toxicity profile than bolus (more hand-foot syndrome, less myelosuppression). Capecitabine is the oral prodrug of 5-FU. Cardiotoxicity may mimic ACS — discontinue immediately if chest pain occurs. Leucovorin (folinic acid) is NOT an antidote — it enhances 5-FU activity

medications.mechanism: Oral prodrug → converted to 5-FU preferentially in tumor tissue via thymidine phosphorylase (higher concentration in tumor than normal tissue) → same mechanism as 5-FU
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (adjuvant and metastatic — CAPOX), breast cancer (metastatic), gastric/GEJ cancer, pancreatic cancer, hepatobiliary cancer
medications.dosing:
- Standard: 1000–1250 mg/m² PO BID days 1–14, every 21 days
- CAPOX: 1000 mg/m² PO BID days 1–14 + oxaliplatin 130 mg/m² day 1, every 21 days
- Metronomic (off-label): 1500 mg PO BID days 1–7 and 15–21, every 28 days
- Take within 30 minutes after meals with water
- Dose reduce for renal impairment (CrCl 30–50: reduce to 75%; CrCl 30: contraindicated)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC; renal function; DPD deficiency testing before initiation; hand-foot syndrome assessment
KEY TOXICITIES: Hand-foot syndrome (dose-limiting — more common than with IV 5-FU), diarrhea, nausea, myelosuppression (less than IV 5-FU), hyperbilirubinemia, cardiotoxicity (coronary vasospasm — same as 5-FU), DPD deficiency toxicity (same as 5-FU)
medications.sideEffects: Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia — 50–60%, dose-limiting), diarrhea (50%), nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression (less than IV 5-FU), hyperbilirubinemia, stomatitis, fatigue, cardiotoxicity (same as 5-FU — coronary vasospasm)
medications.watchFor: Same DPD deficiency precautions as 5-FU. Drug interaction with warfarin — increases INR significantly (monitor closely or avoid). Renal dose adjustment required. Take with food. Oral convenience — often preferred over IV 5-FU for outpatient regimens. 3-month adjuvant CAPOX is noninferior to 6-month FOLFOX for low-risk stage III CRC

medications.mechanism: Incorporated into DNA → chain termination; inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → depletes deoxynucleotide pools; S-phase specific with self-potentiating mechanism
medications.indications: Pancreatic cancer, NSCLC, bladder cancer, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, biliary tract cancer, soft tissue sarcoma
medications.dosing:
- Pancreatic (single agent): 1000 mg/m² IV over 30 minutes, days 1, 8, 15 every 28 days
- NSCLC (with cisplatin): 1000–1250 mg/m² IV days 1, 8 every 21 days
- Bladder (GC): 1000 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15 every 28 days
- Breast: 1250 mg/m² IV days 1, 8 every 21 days (with paclitaxel)
- Fixed-dose rate infusion (10 mg/m²/min) may improve efficacy but increases toxicity
Route: IV (standard 30-minute infusion)
medications.monitoring:
CBC before each dose (hold if ANC 1000 or platelets 100,000); renal function; LFTs; pulmonary symptoms
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), flu-like syndrome (fever, myalgia), hepatotoxicity (transaminase elevation), pulmonary toxicity (dyspnea, interstitial pneumonitis — rare but serious), hemolytic uremic syndrome (rare), peripheral edema, rash, nausea (low-moderate emetogenicity)
medications.sideEffects: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), nausea/vomiting (mild-moderate), flu-like symptoms (fever, myalgia), elevated transaminases (60–70%), peripheral edema, rash, dyspnea, proteinuria. Serious: Pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis — 0.1%), HUS/TMA (0.25%), hepatotoxicity (including VOD), capillary leak syndrome, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome
medications.watchFor: Infusion rate matters — 10 mg/m²/min (standard 30-min infusion) is optimal; fixed-dose-rate infusion (FDR) at 10 mg/m²/min regardless of dose increases intracellular activation but also increases myelosuppression. Radiation recall and radiation sensitization occur — use caution with concurrent or recent RT. Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel is current standard for metastatic pancreatic cancer (MPACT trial: median OS 8.5 vs. 6.7 months).

medications.mechanism: Converted to ara-CTP → incorporated into DNA during S-phase → chain termination; also inhibits DNA polymerase; S-phase specific
medications.indications: AML (induction and consolidation — backbone of "7+3"), ALL (consolidation), CNS lymphoma, intrathecal for leptomeningeal disease
medications.dosing:
- AML induction ("7+3"): 100–200 mg/m²/day continuous IV infusion × 7 days + daunorubicin or idarubicin × 3 days
- High-dose consolidation (HiDAC): 3 g/m² IV over 3 hours Q12H on days 1, 3, 5 (6 doses total) × 3–4 cycles
- Intrathecal: 50–75 mg preservative-free, 2–3 times per week
- Liposomal intrathecal (DepoCyt): 50 mg every 14 days (if available)
Route: IV (continuous infusion or high-dose intermittent), intrathecal, SC
medications.monitoring:
CBC daily during induction; LFTs; renal function; cerebellar function assessment (especially with high-dose — finger-to-nose, heel-to-shin before each dose)
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (severe — expected with induction; nadir day 7–10, recovery day 21–28), cerebellar toxicity (high-dose — dysarthria, ataxia, nystagmus; risk increases with age >60, renal impairment, cumulative dose; IRREVERSIBLE — must stop immediately), cytarabine syndrome (fever, myalgia, bone pain, rash — 6–12 hours post-dose; treat with corticosteroids), conjunctivitis (high-dose — prophylax with steroid eye drops), GI toxicity (mucositis, diarrhea), hepatotoxicity
medications.sideEffects:
- Myelosuppression (dose-limiting): Biphasic leukopenia (nadir days 7–9 and 15–24), thrombocytopenia (nadir days 12–15), anemia
- GI: Nausea/vomiting, mucositis, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity
- HiDAC-specific toxicity: Cerebellar toxicity (dysarthria, ataxia, nystagmus — 10–25% at doses ≥3 g/m²; may be irreversible; risk increases with age >60 and renal impairment), conjunctivitis/keratitis (prevented by steroid eye drops), skin rash/desquamation
- Cytarabine syndrome: Fever, myalgia, bone pain, maculopapular rash, conjunctivitis, malaise — 6–12 hr after administration; responds to corticosteroids
- Intrathecal: Arachnoiditis (nausea, vomiting, headache, fever), myelopathy (rare), leukoencephalopathy (rare, especially with concurrent cranial radiation)
medications.watchFor: Cerebellar toxicity with high-dose — perform neurologic exam before EACH dose; discontinue permanently if any cerebellar signs. Risk factors: age >60, renal insufficiency (reduce dose if CrCl 60), cumulative dose. Prophylactic steroid eye drops (dexamethasone 0.1% Q6H) during and 48 hours after high-dose to prevent chemical conjunctivitis. Tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis required. Intrathecal formulation must be preservative-free

medications.mechanism: Stabilizes topoisomerase II-DNA complex → prevents DNA religation → double-strand breaks → apoptosis; S and G2 phase specific
medications.indications: Testicular cancer (BEP), SCLC (EP — etoposide + cisplatin/carboplatin), AML, ALL, lymphomas, HCT conditioning
medications.dosing:
- Testicular (BEP): 100 mg/m² IV days 1–5, every 21 days
- SCLC (EP): 100 mg/m² IV days 1–3, every 21 days (with cisplatin or carboplatin)
- AML: 100–150 mg/m² IV days 1–3 or 1–5
- Oral: 50 mg/m² PO daily × 21 days (oral bioavailability ~50% — round to nearest 50 mg)
- HCT conditioning: 60 mg/kg IV single dose
Route: IV (infusion over 30–60 minutes — not faster, risk of hypotension), PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC (nadir day 7–14, recovery day 20); blood pressure during infusion; LFTs; renal function
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — leukopenia > thrombocytopenia), hypotension (infusion rate-related — infuse over ≥30 minutes), nausea/vomiting (moderate), alopecia, mucositis, secondary leukemia (treatment-related AML/MDS — 2–12% at 5–7 years; 11q23 translocations; shorter latency than alkylating agents), hypersensitivity reactions (rare — more with IV), hepatotoxicity
medications.sideEffects:
- Myelosuppression (dose-limiting): Leukopenia (60–91%), thrombocytopenia (22–41%), anemia. Nadir days 7–14 (granulocytes) and 9–16 (platelets). Recovery by day 20. No cumulative toxicity.
- GI: Nausea/vomiting (31–43%, mild-moderate), anorexia, diarrhea, mucositis
- Alopecia: 8–66%
- Hypotension: 1–2% (related to infusion rate — infuse slowly over ≥30 min)
- Hypersensitivity/anaphylactoid: 0.7–2% IV (chills, fever, bronchospasm, dyspnea, hypotension)
- Secondary malignancy: Treatment-related AML/MDS (risk 1–3%, typically with 11q23 translocations, latency 2–3 years — shorter than alkylating agent-related AML)
medications.watchFor: Infuse slowly (≥30 min for standard doses, ≥60 min for high doses) to prevent hypotension. Secondary AML risk — balanced translocations involving 11q23 (MLL gene); latency 2–3 years (shorter than alkylator-related AML). Oral bioavailability ~50% — oral dose is approximately 2× IV dose. Etoposide phosphate (Etopophos) is water-soluble prodrug — less hypotension, can be given as rapid infusion. Dose reduce for renal impairment (CrCl 15–50: reduce by 25%)

medications.mechanism: Binds DNA → generates free radicals (requires iron and O₂) → single- and double-strand DNA breaks; G2 and M phase specific; minimal myelosuppression (unique among chemo agents)
medications.indications: Hodgkin lymphoma (ABVD), testicular cancer (BEP), NHL, malignant pleural effusion (pleurodesis)
medications.dosing:
- ABVD: 10 units/m² IV days 1 and 15, every 28 days
- BEP: 30 units IV days 1, 8, 15 every 21 days (weeks 1–9 only — 9 total doses)
- Pleurodesis: 60 units intrapleurally as single dose
- Lifetime cumulative dose limit: 400 units (pulmonary toxicity threshold)
- Test dose: 1–2 units IV/IM before first dose in lymphoma patients (anaphylactoid risk)
Route: IV, IM, SC, intrapleural
medications.monitoring:
Pulmonary function tests (DLCO) at baseline and periodically; chest X-ray; SpO₂; renal function (dose reduce if CrCl 50)
KEY TOXICITIES: Pulmonary toxicity (dose-limiting — pneumonitis → pulmonary fibrosis; 10% incidence, 1–2% fatal; risk increases with cumulative dose >400 units, age >70, renal impairment, concurrent radiation, high FiO₂), skin toxicity (flagellate hyperpigmentation — pathognomonic; skin thickening, Raynaud), fever/chills (25–50%), anaphylactoid reaction (1% — especially lymphoma patients), minimal myelosuppression
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Pulmonary fibrosis (most severe toxicity — ~10% develop pneumonitis, ~1% fatal). Risk factors: cumulative dose >400 units, age >70, renal impairment, concurrent/prior cisplatin, supplemental O₂, G-CSF (controversial), thoracic radiation.
- Idiosyncratic reaction (~1% of lymphoma patients): Hypotension, mental confusion, fever, chills, wheezing — usually after first or second dose; can be fatal
- Mucocutaneous (50%): Skin hyperpigmentation, erythema, rash, hyperkeratosis, nail changes, stomatitis, alopecia, flagellate hyperpigmentation (pathognomonic)
- Other: Fever/chills (common), Raynaud phenomenon (especially with vinblastine), vascular toxicity
- Minimal myelosuppression (key advantage — allows use in combination regimens without additive marrow toxicity)
medications.watchFor: Minimal myelosuppression — unique advantage in combination regimens. Pulmonary toxicity is the major concern — stop if DLCO decreases >15% from baseline or if new pulmonary symptoms. AVOID high FiO₂ (>30%) during and after bleomycin exposure (even years later) — increases pulmonary toxicity risk; critical for anesthesia teams to know. Flagellate hyperpigmentation is pathognomonic. Dose reduce for renal impairment. In BEP for testicular cancer, bleomycin is given for only 9 weeks (not all cycles)

medications.mechanism: Prodrug converted by carboxylesterase to active metabolite SN-38 → stabilizes topoisomerase I-DNA complex → prevents DNA religation → single-strand breaks → replication fork collapse → apoptosis; S-phase specific
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (FOLFIRI, FOLFOXIRI), pancreatic cancer (liposomal — Onivyde + 5-FU/LV), gastric cancer, SCLC (second-line)
medications.dosing:
- FOLFIRI: 180 mg/m² IV over 90 minutes on day 1, every 2 weeks
- Single agent (weekly): 125 mg/m² IV over 90 minutes weekly × 4 weeks, every 6 weeks
- Single agent (Q3 week): 350 mg/m² IV every 3 weeks
- Liposomal (Onivyde): 70 mg/m² (free base) IV every 2 weeks (with 5-FU/LV)
- UGT1A1 genotyping recommended before initiation
Route: IV
medications.monitoring:
CBC before each dose; LFTs; UGT1A1 genotype; diarrhea assessment (early vs late)
KEY TOXICITIES: Diarrhea — TWO DISTINCT TYPES: (1) Early cholinergic diarrhea (within 24 hours — with cramping, diaphoresis, salivation; treat with atropine 0.25–1 mg IV/SC) and (2) Late diarrhea (>24 hours — secretory, can be severe/life-threatening; treat aggressively with loperamide 4 mg initially then 2 mg Q2H until 12 hours diarrhea-free). Myelosuppression (neutropenia), nausea/vomiting (moderately-highly emetogenic), alopecia, hepatotoxicity
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: Severe diarrhea (early and late) and severe myelosuppression.
- Early diarrhea (within 24 hr): Cholinergic mechanism — accompanied by diaphoresis, abdominal cramping, rhinitis, lacrimation, salivation, miosis. Treat with atropine 0.25–1 mg IV/SC.
- Late diarrhea (>24 hr, typically days 2–10): Dose-limiting — can be severe/life-threatening. Treat aggressively with loperamide 4 mg initially, then 2 mg every 2 hr (or 4 mg every 4 hr at night) until diarrhea-free for 12 hr.
- Myelosuppression: Neutropenia (grade 3–4 in 14–31%), febrile neutropenia
- Other: Nausea/vomiting (moderately emetogenic), alopecia, asthenia, elevated transaminases
medications.watchFor: UGT1A1*28 homozygosity (10% of population) → reduced SN-38 glucuronidation → increased toxicity (neutropenia, diarrhea) — start at reduced dose. Late diarrhea protocol: loperamide 4 mg then 2 mg every 2 hours (4 mg every 4 hours at night) until 12 hours diarrhea-free; if persists >24 hours, add fluoroquinolone. Atropine for early cholinergic syndrome. Do NOT give with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or UGT1A1 inhibitors (ketoconazole, atazanavir)

medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits 26S proteasome → blocks degradation of pro-apoptotic proteins and IκB → NF-κB inhibition → induces apoptosis in myeloma and lymphoma cells; disrupts bone marrow microenvironment
medications.indications: Multiple myeloma (induction, maintenance), mantle cell lymphoma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, AL amyloidosis, antibody-mediated rejection (off-label)
medications.dosing:
- MM induction (VRd): 1.3 mg/m² SC days 1, 4, 8, 11 of 21-day cycle (or days 1, 8, 15, 22 of 35-day cycle)
- MM (with dexamethasone): 1.3 mg/m² SC days 1, 4, 8, 11 every 21 days × 8 cycles, then days 1, 8 every 21 days × 3 cycles
- MCL: 1.3 mg/m² IV/SC days 1, 4, 8, 11 every 21 days (with R-CHOP or VR-CAP)
- SC preferred over IV (less peripheral neuropathy — 24% vs 41%)
Route: SC (preferred), IV
medications.monitoring:
CBC; peripheral neuropathy assessment before each cycle; blood glucose (may cause hypo- or hyperglycemia); viral reactivation screening
KEY TOXICITIES: Peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting — painful sensory neuropathy; 37% any grade; SC route reduces incidence), thrombocytopenia (cyclical — nadir day 11, recovery by day 21), GI (nausea, diarrhea, constipation), herpes zoster reactivation (15–30% without prophylaxis), orthostatic hypotension, fatigue
medications.sideEffects:
- Peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting): 37% all grades, 14% grade 3; primarily sensory (painful paresthesias, burning); SC administration reduces incidence by ~25% vs. IV; weekly dosing also reduces risk; partially reversible with dose modification
- Thrombocytopenia: Cyclical — nadir day 11, recovery by day 21 (due to transient megakaryocyte inhibition, not marrow destruction)
- GI: Nausea (55%), diarrhea (51%), constipation (43%), vomiting (33%)
- Other: Fatigue (61%), herpes zoster reactivation (13% without prophylaxis → acyclovir mandatory), orthostatic hypotension, tumor lysis syndrome (rare), posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (rare), pulmonary toxicity (rare)
medications.watchFor: SC administration reduces peripheral neuropathy by ~50% compared to IV — now standard route. Acyclovir/valacyclovir prophylaxis is MANDATORY (herpes zoster reactivation). Dose reduce or discontinue for grade 2+ neuropathy with pain or grade 3+ neuropathy. Weekly dosing (days 1, 8, 15, 22) is better tolerated than twice-weekly. Thrombocytopenia is cyclical and predictable. Does not require renal dose adjustment — safe in renal impairment including dialysis

medications.mechanism: Binds cereblon (E3 ubiquitin ligase) → promotes degradation of Ikaros (IKZF1) and Aiolos (IKZF3) transcription factors → direct antitumor effect; also enhances T-cell and NK-cell activity, inhibits angiogenesis, modulates bone marrow microenvironment
medications.indications: Multiple myeloma (induction, maintenance), MDS with del(5q), mantle cell lymphoma (maintenance), follicular lymphoma (R²)
medications.dosing:
- MM (VRd induction): 25 mg PO days 1–14 of 21-day cycle
- MM maintenance (post-HCT): 10 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle; increase to 15 mg if tolerated
- MDS del(5q): 10 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle
- MCL maintenance: 10 mg PO daily days 1–21 of 28-day cycle; increase to 15 mg if tolerated
- FL (R²): 20 mg PO days 1–21 of 28-day cycle (with rituximab)
- Dose reduce for renal impairment (CrCl 30–60: 10 mg daily; CrCl 30: 15 mg Q48H; dialysis: 5 mg daily, post-dialysis on dialysis days)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC weekly × 8 weeks then monthly; renal function; LFTs; pregnancy testing (monthly for women of childbearing potential); TSH periodically
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia — dose-limiting, especially in MDS), VTE (DVT/PE — 5–15% without prophylaxis; requires thromboprophylaxis), teratogenicity (REMS program — Revlimid REMS), secondary primary malignancies (AML/MDS, solid tumors — increased with melphalan combination), diarrhea, rash, fatigue, tumor flare reaction (lymphoma)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: (1) Embryo-fetal toxicity — REMS program (Revlimid REMS) required; pregnancy testing mandatory; two forms of contraception required. (2) Hematologic toxicity — neutropenia (grade 3–4: 33–59%), thrombocytopenia (grade 3–4: 17–62%). (3) Venous and arterial thromboembolism — DVT/PE (3–10%); thromboprophylaxis required (aspirin, LMWH, or warfarin based on risk).
- Common: Fatigue, diarrhea, constipation, muscle cramps, rash, pruritus, peripheral edema
- Serious: Second primary malignancies (SPM — 3–7% increased risk, including AML/MDS and solid tumors; risk-benefit still favors use), tumor flare reaction (MCL/CLL), hepatotoxicity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare), tumor lysis syndrome
medications.watchFor: REMS program required — pregnancy prevention program (negative pregnancy test, two forms of contraception, no blood donation). VTE prophylaxis mandatory — aspirin 81–325 mg daily (low risk) or LMWH/warfarin/DOAC (high risk: prior VTE, immobility, concurrent dexamethasone). Renal dose adjustment critical. Secondary malignancy risk should be discussed. Tumor flare (tender lymph node swelling, rash, fever) in ~10% of lymphoma patients — treat with corticosteroids, do not discontinue

medications.mechanism: Inhibits PARP1 and PARP2 → prevents single-strand DNA break repair → synthetic lethality in cells with homologous recombination deficiency (BRCA1/2 mutations, HRD) → double-strand breaks accumulate → apoptosis
medications.indications: Ovarian cancer (BRCA-mutated or HRD+ maintenance after platinum response), breast cancer (germline BRCA-mutated, HER2-negative — adjuvant and metastatic), pancreatic cancer (germline BRCA-mutated maintenance), prostate cancer (HRR gene-mutated mCRPC), fallopian tube/primary peritoneal cancer
medications.dosing:
- Standard: 300 mg PO BID (two 150 mg tablets BID)
- Dose reductions: first to 250 mg BID, second to 200 mg BID
- Ovarian maintenance: continue until progression or unacceptable toxicity (max 2 years for first-line per SOLO-1)
- Breast adjuvant (OlympiA): 300 mg PO BID × 1 year
- Dose reduce with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors to 150 mg BID
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC every month × 12 months then periodically; renal function; signs of MDS/AML; pneumonitis symptoms
KEY TOXICITIES: Myelosuppression (anemia most common — 40% any grade, 20% grade 3+; neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), fatigue (65%), nausea (77% — usually mild, improves over time), MDS/AML (1.5–8% in long-term studies — FDA boxed warning consideration), pneumonitis (rare), VTE
medications.sideEffects:
- WARNINGS: MDS/AML (1–2% — potentially fatal; discontinue if confirmed), pneumonitis (1% — discontinue if confirmed), VTE
- Common: Nausea (58–77%), fatigue/asthenia (37–65%), anemia (27–44%, grade 3–4: 11–22%), vomiting (20–40%), diarrhea (21–33%), decreased appetite, headache, dysgeusia, abdominal pain, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia
- Anemia is the most common reason for dose modification
medications.watchFor: Requires BRCA mutation or HRD testing before use (companion diagnostic). Anemia is the most clinically significant toxicity — may require transfusions or dose reduction. Nausea is very common but usually manageable (anti-emetics, take with food, small meals). MDS/AML risk — monitor CBC monthly; discontinue if confirmed. OlympiA trial: adjuvant olaparib × 1 year improved OS in gBRCA+ early breast cancer. Drug interaction with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (reduce dose) and inducers (avoid). Can be taken with or without food

medications.mechanism: Competitively inhibits BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase (binds inactive conformation) → blocks proliferation of Ph+ leukemia cells; also inhibits KIT (c-kit) and PDGFR
medications.indications: CML (chronic phase — first-line), Ph+ ALL, GIST (KIT+), dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, hypereosinophilic syndrome, systemic mastocytosis
medications.dosing:
- CML chronic phase: 400 mg PO daily (with food and large glass of water)
- CML accelerated/blast phase: 600 mg PO daily
- Ph+ ALL: 600 mg PO daily
- GIST adjuvant: 400 mg PO daily × 3 years (high-risk)
- GIST metastatic: 400 mg PO daily; KIT exon 9 mutation: 800 mg PO daily
- DFSP: 800 mg PO daily
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC weekly × 1 month, biweekly × 1 month, then periodically; LFTs; renal function; molecular response (BCR-ABL by qPCR every 3 months for CML); weight/fluid retention; bone density
KEY TOXICITIES: Fluid retention (periorbital edema, pleural effusion, ascites, weight gain), myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), nausea/diarrhea, muscle cramps, rash, hepatotoxicity, cardiotoxicity (CHF — rare), hypophosphatemia, bone density loss
medications.sideEffects: Edema (periorbital, lower extremity — 60–77%), nausea (50–58%), muscle cramps (49%), musculoskeletal pain (47%), diarrhea (45–56%), rash (38–50%), fatigue (39–69%), vomiting (37%), abdominal pain (37–57%), headache (37%). Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia — more frequent in AP/BC). Hepatotoxicity (5% severe transaminase elevation). CHF/LV dysfunction (especially elderly or cardiac comorbidities). Severe fluid retention (pleural effusion, ascites, pulmonary edema — 1.5–6% grade 3–4). Growth retardation in pediatric patients.
medications.watchFor: Revolutionized CML — 10-year OS ~85% in chronic phase. Take with food to reduce GI side effects. CYP3A4 substrate — avoid grapefruit, strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers. Molecular monitoring (BCR-ABL qPCR) guides treatment milestones — major molecular response (MMR, BCR-ABL ≤0.1%) by 12 months. Treatment-free remission (TFR) may be attempted after sustained deep molecular response (≥MR4) for ≥2 years. Resistance mutations (T315I) require alternative TKIs (ponatinib or asciminib). Generic available — significantly reduced cost

medications.brandNames: Herceptin; biosimilars: Herzuma, Ogivri, Kanjinti, others
medications.mechanism: Binds HER2 extracellular domain IV → inhibits HER2 signaling, prevents receptor dimerization, induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), inhibits HER2 shedding
medications.indications: HER2+ breast cancer (adjuvant, neoadjuvant, metastatic), HER2+ gastric/GEJ cancer (metastatic)
medications.dosing:
- Breast (IV): loading 8 mg/kg IV, then 6 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (or loading 4 mg/kg then 2 mg/kg weekly)
- Breast (SC — Herceptin Hylecta): 600 mg SC every 3 weeks (fixed dose, regardless of weight)
- Adjuvant duration: 1 year (52 weeks) — standard per HERA trial
- Gastric: loading 8 mg/kg IV, then 6 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (with chemotherapy)
Route: IV, SC
medications.monitoring:
LVEF (echocardiogram or MUGA) at baseline, every 3 months during treatment, and at completion; HER2 testing (IHC 3+ or FISH amplified) required before initiation
KEY TOXICITIES: Cardiotoxicity (dose-limiting — LVEF decline, CHF; 4% with trastuzumab alone, 27% with anthracycline combination; usually reversible upon discontinuation — unlike anthracycline cardiotoxicity), infusion reactions (first dose — 40% with chills/fever; premedicate), diarrhea, fatigue, headache
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: (1) Cardiomyopathy — LVEF decline, CHF; 4–6× increased risk vs. chemo alone; highest with concurrent anthracyclines (up to 27% cardiac dysfunction). (2) Infusion reactions — serious/fatal; symptoms within 24 hr. (3) Pulmonary toxicity — dyspnea, interstitial pneumonitis, ARDS; fatal cases reported. (4) Embryo-fetal toxicity — oligohydramnios.
- Common: Fever, nausea, vomiting, infusion reactions (40% first infusion — chills, fever), diarrhea, infections, cough, headache, fatigue, rash, neutropenia (exacerbation of chemo-induced), anemia
- Cardiac: Withhold for ≥16% absolute LVEF decrease from baseline, or LVEF below institutional normal AND ≥10% absolute decrease
medications.watchFor: HER2 testing mandatory — only effective in HER2+ tumors (IHC 3+ or FISH ratio ≥2.0). Cardiotoxicity is usually reversible (unlike anthracyclines) — hold if LVEF drops >16% from baseline or below normal with ≥10% decline; may rechallenge after recovery. Avoid concurrent anthracycline administration (sequential is standard — AC followed by taxane + trastuzumab). 1-year adjuvant duration is standard (HERA, PHARE trials). Multiple biosimilars available. Pertuzumab is often added (dual HER2 blockade) in high-risk adjuvant and metastatic settings

medications.mechanism: Binds VEGF-A → prevents VEGF from binding receptors (VEGFR-1, VEGFR-2) → inhibits angiogenesis → reduces tumor vascularity and interstitial pressure
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (metastatic), NSCLC (non-squamous), ovarian cancer, cervical cancer, glioblastoma (recurrent), renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma (with atezolizumab)
medications.dosing:
- CRC: 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (with FOLFOX/FOLFIRI) or 7.5 mg/kg every 3 weeks
- NSCLC: 15 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (with carboplatin-paclitaxel)
- Ovarian: 15 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks
- Cervical: 15 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (with chemotherapy)
- GBM: 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks
- HCC: 15 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (with atezolizumab)
Route: IV (first infusion over 90 minutes; if tolerated, second over 60 minutes, subsequent over 30 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
Blood pressure (every 2–3 weeks); urinalysis (proteinuria); wound healing assessment; CBC
KEY TOXICITIES: Hypertension (25–40% — dose-dependent; treat with ACE inhibitors/ARBs/CCBs), proteinuria (monitor with urine dipstick; hold if >2 g/24h), bleeding (epistaxis common; serious hemorrhage — especially pulmonary in squamous NSCLC, hence contraindicated), arterial thromboembolic events (stroke, MI — 3.8%), GI perforation (1–3% — higher in CRC with intact primary), wound healing impairment, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES — rare), fistula formation
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: GI perforation (0.3–3%), wound healing complications, hemorrhage (serious/fatal — up to 5× more frequent)
- Class-effect toxicities: Hypertension (25–42% all grades, 5–18% grade 3), proteinuria (17–64%), arterial thromboembolism (3–5% grade 3–5 — MI, stroke), venous thromboembolism (5–11%), hemorrhage (epistaxis most common — 33%; serious pulmonary hemorrhage in squamous NSCLC — 31%), wound healing complications, GI perforation/fistula
- Other: PRES (0.5%), nasal septum perforation, ovarian failure, CHF
- Contraindicated in squamous NSCLC (fatal pulmonary hemorrhage risk)
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in squamous NSCLC (fatal pulmonary hemorrhage). Hold ≥28 days before elective surgery and do not resume until wound fully healed (≥28 days post-op). GI perforation risk — higher with intact primary tumor, recent colonoscopy, bowel obstruction. No proven OS benefit in GBM (PFS benefit only). Hypertension may be a biomarker of efficacy. Does not cross BBB. Multiple biosimilars available

medications.mechanism: Binds EGFR extracellular domain → blocks ligand binding → inhibits receptor activation, downstream RAS/RAF/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling → inhibits proliferation, induces apoptosis, ADCC
medications.indications: Colorectal cancer (RAS wild-type, left-sided — metastatic), HNSCC (with radiation or chemotherapy)
medications.dosing:
- CRC: loading 400 mg/m² IV over 2 hours, then 250 mg/m² IV weekly over 1 hour; or 500 mg/m² IV every 2 weeks
- HNSCC (with radiation): loading 400 mg/m² IV 1 week before radiation, then 250 mg/m² IV weekly during radiation
- HNSCC (with platinum-based chemo): same dosing
- Premedicate with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV
Route: IV
medications.monitoring:
Electrolytes (Mg²⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) weekly during and 8 weeks after treatment; skin toxicity assessment; RAS/RAF mutation testing before CRC use; infusion reaction monitoring
KEY TOXICITIES: Acneiform rash (80% — papulopustular, face/trunk/extremities; correlates with efficacy; manage with doxycycline prophylaxis, topical steroids, moisturizers), hypomagnesemia (55% — can be severe; IV replacement may be needed), infusion reactions (3% severe — more common with cetuximab than panitumumab due to chimeric structure; premedicate), diarrhea, interstitial lung disease (rare), cardiopulmonary arrest (with radiation in HNSCC)
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNINGS: (1) Serious/fatal infusion reactions (2.2% grade 3–4; higher in patients with prior tick bites or alpha-gal allergy). (2) Cardiopulmonary arrest/sudden death (in HNSCC with radiation or platinum-based therapy).
- Dermatologic (signature toxicity): Acneiform rash (82% all grades, 10% grade 3–4 — correlates with efficacy), skin drying/fissuring, paronychia, hypertrichosis
- Hypomagnesemia (55% — due to renal magnesium wasting; can be severe; monitor and replete)
- Other: Diarrhea, headache, infection, fatigue, nausea
- Increased mortality in RAS-mutant CRC — do NOT use
medications.watchFor: RAS wild-type (KRAS/NRAS exons 2–4) is MANDATORY for CRC use — no benefit in RAS-mutant tumors. Left-sided CRC responds better than right-sided (CALGB/SWOG 80405). Acneiform rash correlates with response — do not dose-reduce for rash unless grade 3+. Hypomagnesemia requires monitoring and aggressive replacement. Higher infusion reaction rate than panitumumab (chimeric vs fully human). IgE antibodies to galactose-α-1,3-galactose (alpha-gal) — prevalent in southeastern US — increase anaphylaxis risk

medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits EGFR (ErbB1) intracellular tyrosine kinase domain → blocks downstream RAS/RAF/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling → inhibits proliferation in EGFR-mutant tumors
medications.indications: NSCLC (EGFR-mutant — exon 19 deletion or L858R; largely replaced by osimertinib), pancreatic cancer (with gemcitabine — modest benefit)
medications.dosing:
- NSCLC: 150 mg PO daily on empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after food)
- Pancreatic cancer: 100 mg PO daily (with gemcitabine)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
LFTs periodically; renal function; skin toxicity assessment; ILD symptoms; EGFR mutation testing before NSCLC use
KEY TOXICITIES: Acneiform rash (75% — correlates with efficacy), diarrhea (54%), anorexia, fatigue, interstitial lung disease (1–3% — can be fatal), hepatotoxicity, corneal perforation/ulceration (rare), GI perforation (rare)
medications.sideEffects: Rash (60–85% — acneiform, dose-related), diarrhea (20–62%), anorexia, fatigue, dyspnea, cough, nausea, dry skin, conjunctivitis, paronychia. Serious: ILD/pneumonitis (1% but can be fatal — discontinue immediately), hepatotoxicity (including fatal hepatic failure), GI perforation, renal failure, bullous/exfoliative skin disorders, cerebrovascular events, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
medications.watchFor: Largely replaced by osimertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI) as first-line for EGFR-mutant NSCLC per FLAURA trial (superior OS). Take on empty stomach. Smoking induces CYP1A2 — smokers may need dose increase. Rash correlates with efficacy — manage with doxycycline, topical steroids; do not dose-reduce unless grade 3+. Pancreatic cancer benefit is marginal (median OS improvement ~10 days in PA.3 trial). CYP3A4 substrate — avoid strong inhibitors/inducers

medications.drugClass: Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor (first-generation, irreversible, covalent)
medications.mechanism: Irreversibly binds BTK at Cys-481 → blocks B-cell receptor signaling → inhibits proliferation, adhesion, and migration of malignant B cells; also inhibits off-target kinases (ITK, EGFR, TEC) — responsible for some toxicities
medications.indications: CLL/SLL (first-line and relapsed), mantle cell lymphoma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, marginal zone lymphoma, chronic GVHD
medications.dosing:
- CLL/SLL: 420 mg PO daily (three 140 mg capsules) until progression
- MCL: 560 mg PO daily
- WM: 420 mg PO daily
- MZL: 560 mg PO daily
- cGVHD: 420 mg PO daily
- Take at same time daily; can take with or without food (capsules with water; tablets with food)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
CBC; ECG (atrial fibrillation); blood pressure; bleeding assessment; infection monitoring; LFTs
KEY TOXICITIES: Atrial fibrillation (6–16% — off-target TEC kinase inhibition; higher than acalabrutinib/zanubrutinib), bleeding (petechiae, bruising, major hemorrhage — inhibits platelet aggregation via BTK and off-target effects; hold 3–7 days before surgery), hypertension (19%), diarrhea, arthralgia, infections (pneumonia, opportunistic), second primary malignancies (skin cancer), ventricular arrhythmias (rare — sudden death signal)
medications.sideEffects:
Diarrhea (43–52%), fatigue, musculoskeletal pain (30%), bruising (30%), rash (16.7%), nausea. Atrial fibrillation (15.6–25% — off-target TEC kinase inhibition in cardiac tissue), hypertension, bleeding (bruising, petechiae; serious hemorrhage 4–9% — platelet dysfunction via BTK/TEC inhibition), infections (upper respiratory, pneumonia — especially early in treatment), arthralgia (42%), neutropenia, second primary malignancies (skin cancers)
- Ventricular arrhythmias reported — cardiology evaluation if symptoms
medications.watchFor: Hold 3–7 days before and after surgery (bleeding risk). Avoid with anticoagulants if possible (increased bleeding); if needed, prefer DOACs over warfarin. Atrial fibrillation — rate control preferred; avoid amiodarone (CYP3A4 inhibitor increases ibrutinib levels). CYP3A4 substrate — strong inhibitors require dose reduction (420→140 mg for CLL); strong inducers contraindicated. Second-generation BTK inhibitors (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) have fewer off-target effects (less AF, less bleeding) and are increasingly preferred. Continuous therapy until progression — no fixed duration (except when combined with venetoclax in fixed-duration regimens)

medications.mechanism: Blocks PD-1 receptor on T cells → prevents PD-L1/PD-L2 binding → restores T-cell antitumor immune response; releases immune checkpoint inhibition
medications.indications: NSCLC (first-line ± chemo; PD-L1 ≥1% monotherapy, any PD-L1 with chemo), melanoma (adjuvant and metastatic), HNSCC, urothelial carcinoma, MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors (tissue-agnostic), Hodgkin lymphoma, gastric/GEJ, cervical, endometrial, TNBC, RCC, HCC, Merkel cell, SCLC, esophageal, biliary tract, and many others — broadest FDA-approved indication list of any oncology drug
medications.dosing:
- Adults: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks OR 400 mg IV every 6 weeks (both approved flat doses)
- Pediatric (MSI-H/dMMR, cHL): 2 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (max 200 mg)
- Adjuvant melanoma/RCC: up to 1 year (17 cycles Q3W)
- Metastatic: continue until progression, unacceptable toxicity, or max 2 years (35 cycles Q3W)
Route: IV (infusion over 30 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
Thyroid function (TSH, free T4) every 6 weeks initially then every 12 weeks; LFTs; creatinine; glucose; cortisol if symptoms; irAE assessment at every visit
KEY TOXICITIES: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) — can affect ANY organ system:
- Dermatologic: rash, pruritus, vitiligo (34%)
- Endocrine: hypothyroidism (10–12%), hyperthyroidism (3–8%), adrenal insufficiency (1%), hypophysitis (1%), type 1 diabetes (1%)
- GI: colitis/diarrhea (1–4% grade 3+), hepatitis (1–3% grade 3+)
- Pulmonary: pneumonitis (3–5%; can be fatal)
- Renal: nephritis (1–2%)
- Neurologic: myasthenia gravis, GBS, encephalitis (rare but serious)
- Cardiac: myocarditis (1% — but 50% mortality)
- Infusion reactions (rare)
medications.sideEffects:
- Immune-mediated adverse events (irAEs — 23.7% any grade, 6.3% grade 3–5): Pneumonitis (3.4%), colitis (1.7%), hepatitis (0.7%), nephritis (0.3%), endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism 10–12%, hyperthyroidism 3–8%, adrenal insufficiency 0.8%, hypophysitis 0.6%, type 1 DM 0.2%), dermatologic (rash, vitiligo, SJS/TEN — rare), neurologic (GBS, myasthenia gravis, encephalitis — rare), myocarditis (0.1% but up to 50% mortality)
- Common (non-immune): Fatigue (25–43%), rash (17–24%), pruritus (11–28%), diarrhea (14–20%), nausea (20–22%), decreased appetite (17–25%), cough (16–20%), dyspnea (17–23%), constipation (15–25%), arthralgia (11–14%)
- irAE management: Most managed with corticosteroids (prednisone 1–2 mg/kg); permanently discontinue for grade 4 irAEs (except endocrinopathies managed with hormone replacement)
medications.watchFor: irAE management is grade-dependent: Grade 1 — continue with monitoring; Grade 2 — hold, start prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg; Grade 3 — hold, start prednisone 1–2 mg/kg, consider infliximab for colitis; Grade 4 — permanently discontinue (except endocrinopathies managed with hormone replacement). Myocarditis is rare but has ~50% mortality — check troponin if cardiac symptoms. Endocrinopathies are usually permanent (lifelong hormone replacement). PD-L1 testing (TPS or CPS depending on tumor type) guides monotherapy eligibility in NSCLC and other tumors. MSI-H/dMMR is a tissue-agnostic biomarker for pembrolizumab use

medications.mechanism: Same as pembrolizumab — blocks PD-1 receptor on T cells → restores antitumor immunity
medications.indications: Melanoma (adjuvant and metastatic), NSCLC, RCC, urothelial carcinoma, HNSCC, Hodgkin lymphoma, HCC, esophageal/GEJ, gastric, CRC (MSI-H/dMMR), mesothelioma, adjuvant muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma
medications.dosing:
- Monotherapy: 240 mg IV every 2 weeks OR 480 mg IV every 4 weeks
- With ipilimumab (melanoma): nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses, then nivolumab 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W maintenance
- With ipilimumab (RCC): nivolumab 3 mg/kg + ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance
- With ipilimumab (NSCLC): nivolumab 360 mg Q3W + ipilimumab 1 mg/kg Q6W (+ chemo × 2 cycles)
- Adjuvant (melanoma, urothelial): 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W × 1 year
Route: IV (infusion over 30 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
Same as pembrolizumab — TSH, LFTs, creatinine, glucose, cortisol, irAE assessment
KEY TOXICITIES: Same irAE profile as pembrolizumab. Combination with ipilimumab significantly increases irAE rates (grade 3–4 irAEs: 55–60% with nivo+ipi vs 15–20% with nivo alone). Colitis, hepatitis, and endocrinopathies are more common with combination
medications.sideEffects: Similar irAE profile to pembrolizumab. When combined with ipilimumab: grade 3–4 treatment-related AEs in 55.5%, discontinuation in 35.7%. Most frequent grade 3–4 select AEs: hepatic (17%), GI (16.3%), skin (3.6%), endocrine (3.6%), pulmonary (3.1%), renal (1.6%). Fatal treatment-related AEs 1%. Median time to onset of grade 3–4 irAEs: 3.1 weeks (skin) to 16.3 weeks (renal). Resolution rates 79–100% with immune-modulating agents (except endocrine — usually permanent).
medications.watchFor: Fully human antibody (vs pembrolizumab which is humanized) — no clinically meaningful difference in immunogenicity. Combination nivo+ipi has highest irAE rate of any checkpoint regimen — requires close monitoring. irAE management same as pembrolizumab. CheckMate trials are the landmark studies. Weight-based dosing has been replaced by flat dosing for most indications. Can be given Q4W (480 mg) for convenience in maintenance settings

medications.mechanism: Blocks CTLA-4 on T cells → prevents CTLA-4 from outcompeting CD28 for B7 ligand binding → enhances T-cell activation and proliferation in lymph nodes (earlier in immune response than PD-1)
medications.indications: Melanoma (adjuvant and metastatic — usually with nivolumab), RCC (with nivolumab), NSCLC (with nivolumab ± chemo), CRC (MSI-H/dMMR, with nivolumab), HCC (with nivolumab), mesothelioma (with nivolumab)
medications.dosing:
- Melanoma (with nivolumab): 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses (then nivolumab maintenance)
- Melanoma (monotherapy, adjuvant — historical): 10 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses, then every 12 weeks × 3 years
- RCC (with nivolumab): 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses
- NSCLC (with nivolumab): 1 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks
- CRC MSI-H (with nivolumab): 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks × 4 doses
Route: IV (infusion over 30 minutes)
medications.monitoring:
LFTs before each dose; TSH; creatinine; amylase/lipase; irAE assessment — more intensive monitoring than anti-PD-1 alone
KEY TOXICITIES: Higher irAE rates than anti-PD-1 monotherapy. Colitis/diarrhea (grade 3+: 7–8% monotherapy, up to 15% with nivolumab), hepatitis (grade 3+: 3–9%), dermatitis (grade 3+: 2–3%), hypophysitis (4–6% — more common with ipilimumab than anti-PD-1), adrenal insufficiency, pneumonitis, nephritis, myocarditis, neurotoxicity. Colitis can cause perforation — surgical emergency
medications.sideEffects: Higher irAE rates than anti-PD-1 agents. Monotherapy (3 mg/kg): grade 3–4 irAEs in 20–28%, discontinuation in 9–16%. Colitis/diarrhea (most characteristic — 30–44% any grade, 7–12% grade 3–4), hepatitis (3–9% grade 3–4), dermatitis (rash, pruritus — 24–47%), endocrinopathies (hypophysitis 4–6% — more common than with anti-PD-1; hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency), neuropathy, nephritis. Fatal irAEs ~1% (most commonly colitis, hepatitis).
medications.watchFor: CTLA-4 blockade acts earlier in immune activation (lymph node priming) vs PD-1 blockade (tumor microenvironment) — complementary mechanisms explain combination synergy. Ipilimumab is almost always used in combination with nivolumab now (monotherapy largely historical except adjuvant melanoma). Hypophysitis is more specific to anti-CTLA-4 than anti-PD-1. Colitis management: hold drug, high-dose steroids (1–2 mg/kg prednisone); if refractory, infliximab 5 mg/kg (NOT vedolizumab for hepatitis). 10 mg/kg dosing (adjuvant melanoma) has higher toxicity than 3 mg/kg or 1 mg/kg

medications.mechanism: Competitive antagonist of estrogen at ER in breast tissue → blocks estrogen-driven proliferation; partial agonist in uterus (increases endometrial cancer risk) and bone (protective — reduces osteoporosis in postmenopausal women)
medications.indications: ER+ breast cancer (adjuvant — premenopausal standard; metastatic), DCIS (risk reduction), breast cancer chemoprevention (high-risk women), gynecomastia (off-label)
medications.dosing:
- Breast cancer adjuvant: 20 mg PO daily × 5 years (may extend to 10 years per ATLAS/aTTom trials in high-risk premenopausal)
- DCIS: 20 mg PO daily × 5 years
- Chemoprevention: 20 mg PO daily × 5 years
- Metastatic: 20–40 mg PO daily
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Annual gynecologic exam (endometrial cancer risk); ophthalmologic exam if visual symptoms; lipid panel; CBC; LFTs; bone density (premenopausal — may decrease; postmenopausal — may increase)
KEY TOXICITIES: Hot flashes (most common), VTE (DVT/PE — 2–3× increased risk), endometrial cancer (2–7× increased risk — especially postmenopausal; report any abnormal vaginal bleeding), cataracts/retinopathy, mood changes/depression, vaginal dryness or discharge, hepatotoxicity (rare), teratogenicity
medications.sideEffects: Hot flashes (42.9%), vaginal discharge (10%), vaginal bleeding, menstrual irregularities, nausea, fatigue. Serious: Endometrial cancer (2–7× increased risk — especially >5 years of use and age >50), VTE (DVT 1–2%, PE — 2.5% increase vs. AI), stroke, cataracts/retinopathy, hepatotoxicity (rare). Tumor flare (bone pain, hypercalcemia — early in treatment of metastatic disease).
medications.watchFor: Standard adjuvant endocrine therapy for premenopausal ER+ breast cancer. CYP2D6 converts tamoxifen to active metabolite endoxifen — CYP2D6 poor metabolizers may have reduced efficacy; avoid strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine — use venlafaxine, citalopram, or escitalopram instead for hot flashes/depression). Extended adjuvant (10 years) reduces recurrence but increases side effects — individualize. Postmenopausal women should receive aromatase inhibitor instead (or switch after 2–3 years of tamoxifen). Contraindicated in pregnancy. Reduces contralateral breast cancer risk by ~50%

medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits aromatase (CYP19) → blocks peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogens → reduces circulating estrogen levels by >95% in postmenopausal women
medications.indications: ER+ breast cancer in postmenopausal women (adjuvant, metastatic), breast cancer chemoprevention (IBIS-II trial)
medications.dosing:
- Adjuvant: 1 mg PO daily × 5 years (may extend to 10 years in high-risk)
- Metastatic: 1 mg PO daily until progression
- Chemoprevention: 1 mg PO daily × 5 years
- Premenopausal women: ONLY with ovarian suppression (GnRH agonist) — per SOFT/TEXT trials
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Bone density (DEXA) at baseline and every 1–2 years; lipid panel; arthralgia assessment; fracture risk assessment
KEY TOXICITIES: Musculoskeletal symptoms (arthralgia/myalgia — 35–50%; most common reason for discontinuation), bone loss/osteoporosis/fractures (accelerated bone loss — 2–3% BMD loss/year), hot flashes, vaginal dryness, hyperlipidemia, cardiovascular events (slight increase vs tamoxifen), carpal tunnel syndrome, mood changes
medications.sideEffects: Hot flashes (36%), arthralgia/arthritis (36% — more than tamoxifen), asthenia (19%), nausea (19%), headache (18%), back pain (12%), bone pain (11%), osteoporosis (11%), fractures (10% vs. 7% tamoxifen — spine, hip, wrist), hypertension, depression, insomnia, peripheral edema, rash, elevated cholesterol (9% vs. 3.5% tamoxifen), carpal tunnel syndrome (2.5% vs. 0.7% tamoxifen). Ischemic cardiovascular events increased in patients with pre-existing ischemic heart disease (17% vs. 10% tamoxifen).
medications.watchFor: Only for postmenopausal women (or premenopausal with ovarian suppression) — aromatase inhibitors are ineffective in premenopausal women without ovarian suppression (ovarian estrogen production overwhelms aromatase inhibition). Superior to tamoxifen in postmenopausal women (ATAC trial — lower recurrence, no endometrial cancer risk, no VTE risk). Bone-protective therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab) recommended if T-score -2.0 or if ≥2 risk factors. Arthralgia management: exercise, switch to letrozole or exemestane, duloxetine. Letrozole and exemestane are alternatives in same class

medications.mechanism: Synthetic nonapeptide analog of GnRH. Initially stimulates pituitary LH and FSH release (testosterone flare for 1–3 weeks), then causes GnRH receptor downregulation and desensitization, leading to suppression of LH/FSH secretion and subsequent reduction of gonadal steroid production. Achieves castrate testosterone levels (50 ng/dL) within 2–4 weeks.
medications.indications: FDA-approved: Advanced prostate cancer, central precocious puberty, endometriosis, uterine leiomyomata (fibroids). Off-label: Breast cancer (ovarian suppression in premenopausal women), gender-affirming care, in vitro fertilization protocols
medications.dosing:
- Prostate cancer (Lupron Depot): 7.5 mg IM monthly, 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, 30 mg IM every 4 months, or 45 mg IM every 6 months
- Prostate cancer (Eligard): 7.5 mg SC monthly, 22.5 mg SC every 3 months, 30 mg SC every 4 months, or 45 mg SC every 6 months
- Prostate cancer (Camcevi): 42 mg SC every 6 months
- Endometriosis: 3.75 mg IM monthly or 11.25 mg IM every 3 months × 6 months max
- Uterine fibroids: 3.75 mg IM monthly × 3 months (with iron) or 11.25 mg IM single dose
- Central precocious puberty: 50 mcg/kg/day SC (daily formulation) or depot formulations
Route: IM (Lupron Depot), SC (Eligard, Camcevi)
medications.monitoring: Serum testosterone (confirm castrate levels 50 ng/dL at 12 weeks and periodically), PSA, bone density (long-term use), blood glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel, cardiovascular risk assessment
medications.sideEffects: Hot flashes (55–58%), injection site reactions, fatigue, headache, erectile dysfunction, decreased libido, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy, bone loss/osteoporosis (long-term), metabolic syndrome (weight gain, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia), cardiovascular risk (increased MI/stroke risk with long-term ADT), depression/mood changes, anemia, musculoskeletal pain, cognitive changes. Initial testosterone flare (first 1–3 weeks) — can cause bone pain flare, urinary obstruction, spinal cord compression in patients with metastatic disease.
