medications.watchFor: Testosterone flare is critical — must co-administer a first-generation antiandrogen (bicalutamide, flutamide) for ≥7 days when initiating in patients with metastatic disease in weight-bearing bone or large prostate mass. GnRH antagonists (degarelix, relugolix) do NOT cause testosterone flare — preferred when flare is dangerous. Relugolix (oral GnRH antagonist) showed lower cardiovascular event rate than leuprolide in HERO trial. Long-term ADT adverse effects are cumulative — monitor bone density, metabolic parameters, and cardiovascular health.

medications.mechanism: Competitively binds androgen receptor → blocks testosterone and DHT from activating AR → inhibits androgen-driven prostate cancer growth; does NOT lower testosterone levels (peripheral blockade only)
medications.indications: Prostate cancer (combined androgen blockade with GnRH agonist; antiandrogen flare cover; monotherapy in select cases)
medications.dosing:
- Combined androgen blockade: 50 mg PO daily (with GnRH agonist)
- Flare prevention: 50 mg PO daily starting 7 days before GnRH agonist, continue × 2–4 weeks
- Monotherapy (locally advanced, non-metastatic — EPC trial): 150 mg PO daily
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
LFTs at baseline, monthly × 4 months, then periodically; PSA; breast exam (gynecomastia)
KEY TOXICITIES: Gynecomastia and breast pain (49–72% with monotherapy; less with combined androgen blockade), hot flashes, hepatotoxicity (rare but can be fatal — check LFTs), diarrhea, asthenia, sexual dysfunction (less than GnRH agonists when used as monotherapy)
medications.sideEffects: Hot flashes (53%), pain (35%), back pain (25%), asthenia (22%), constipation (22%), infection (18%), nausea (15%), peripheral edema (13%), dyspnea (13%), diarrhea (12%), hematuria (12%), gynecomastia/breast pain (38–49% as monotherapy, 9% with LHRH agonist). Hepatotoxicity (rare but can be severe/fatal — monitor LFTs). Rarely: interstitial pneumonitis.
medications.watchFor: First-generation antiandrogen — largely replaced by enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide (second-generation) for metastatic disease. Primary role now is flare prevention when starting GnRH agonist. Antiandrogen withdrawal response — ~15–30% of patients have PSA decline when bicalutamide is discontinued (AR mutation makes bicalutamide an agonist). Hepatotoxicity — check LFTs regularly; discontinue if ALT >2× ULN. Gynecomastia can be prevented with prophylactic breast irradiation (single fraction) or tamoxifen

medications.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase and 17,20-lyase) → blocks androgen synthesis in testes, adrenal glands, AND tumor tissue → reduces testosterone to undetectable levels; also inhibits cortisol synthesis → compensatory ACTH rise → mineralocorticoid excess
medications.indications: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (pre- and post-docetaxel), metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (with ADT), high-risk localized prostate cancer (with ADT + radiation — STAMPEDE)
medications.dosing:
- Zytiga: 1000 mg PO daily on empty stomach (2 hours after and 1 hour before food) + prednisone 5 mg PO BID
- Yonsa (fine-particle): 500 mg PO daily (can take with or without food) + methylprednisolone 4 mg PO BID
- Must be taken with low-dose glucocorticoid (prednisone or methylprednisolone) to prevent mineralocorticoid excess
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
LFTs every 2 weeks × 3 months, then monthly; blood pressure; potassium; fluid retention assessment; blood glucose
KEY TOXICITIES: Mineralocorticoid excess (hypertension, hypokalemia, fluid retention/edema — due to CYP17 inhibition of cortisol → ACTH rise → aldosterone/DOC excess; mitigated by concurrent prednisone), hepatotoxicity (ALT elevation — 3–4% grade 3+; rare fatal hepatotoxicity), fatigue, joint pain, cardiac disorders (atrial fibrillation, CHF — especially in patients with pre-existing cardiac disease)
medications.sideEffects: Mineralocorticoid excess (due to CYP17 blockade upstream of cortisol): hypertension (22%), hypokalemia (17%), fluid retention/edema (25–28%). Fatigue (39%), arthralgia (30%), nausea (22%), hot flashes (22%), diarrhea (18%), cough (17%), upper respiratory infection (13%). Hepatotoxicity (ALT/AST elevation — 6% grade 3–4; monitor closely). Cardiac disorders (6–19% — atrial fibrillation, tachycardia, CHF). Adrenal insufficiency (if corticosteroid discontinued abruptly).
medications.watchFor: MUST be taken with prednisone/methylprednisolone — mineralocorticoid excess without glucocorticoid cover causes dangerous hypertension and hypokalemia. Zytiga must be taken on empty stomach (food increases absorption 5–10×, causing toxicity). CYP3A4 substrate and CYP2D6 inhibitor — avoid strong CYP3A4 inducers; caution with CYP2D6 substrates (metoprolol, codeine). LATITUDE and STAMPEDE trials established benefit in mCSPC. Continue GnRH agonist/antagonist concurrently (abiraterone does not suppress testicular androgens alone)

medications.mechanism: Competitively inhibits androgen receptor with higher affinity than bicalutamide → also inhibits AR nuclear translocation, DNA binding, and coactivator recruitment → more complete AR blockade than first-generation antiandrogens; no agonist activity
medications.indications: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, non-metastatic CRPC (high-risk), metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer
medications.dosing:
- All indications: 160 mg PO daily (four 40 mg capsules)
- Can take with or without food
- No concurrent prednisone required (unlike abiraterone)
- Dose reduce to 80 mg daily with strong CYP2C8 inhibitors (gemfibrozil) or if grade 3+ toxicity
Route: PO
medications.monitoring:
Blood pressure; seizure risk assessment; fatigue assessment; CBC; LFTs; thyroid function
KEY TOXICITIES: Fatigue (36% — most common and often dose-limiting), hypertension (12%), seizures (0.5% — lowers seizure threshold; contraindicated in patients with seizure history or predisposing conditions), falls/fractures (12%), cognitive impairment, hot flashes, diarrhea, headache, peripheral edema
medications.sideEffects: Fatigue/asthenia (34–46%), hot flashes (18–33%), hypertension (12–14%), diarrhea (17%), musculoskeletal pain (30%), headache (12%), falls (11–18%), fractures (10–13%), cognitive disorder/memory impairment, dizziness. Seizures (0.5% — dose-related; contraindicated in patients with seizure history or conditions that lower seizure threshold). Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (rare). Ischemic heart disease (3–6%).
medications.watchFor: Does NOT require concurrent prednisone (advantage over abiraterone). Seizure risk — avoid in patients with seizure history, brain metastases, concurrent medications that lower seizure threshold. Strong CYP3A4 inducer — reduces efficacy of many co-administered drugs (warfarin, opioids, midazolam, DOACs — important drug interactions). Also CYP2C8 substrate. PROSPER, ARCHES, and ENZAMET are landmark trials. No cross-resistance with abiraterone (different mechanism) — can be sequenced. Apalutamide and darolutamide are alternatives in same class

medications.mechanism: Binds tubulin → prevents microtubule polymerization → arrests mitosis in M-phase → apoptosis
medications.indications: ALL (POMP, hyper-CVAD), NHL (CHOP), Hodgkin lymphoma (ABVD — uses vinblastine, not vincristine), Wilms tumor, neuroblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, ITP (off-label)
medications.dosing:
- Adults: 1.4 mg/m² IV (MAX 2 mg per dose — absolute cap) on day 1 of each cycle
- Pediatric: 1.5 mg/m² IV (MAX 2 mg per dose) weekly or per protocol
- CHOP: vincristine 1.4 mg/m² (max 2 mg) IV day 1, every 21 days
- Always given as IV push or short infusion (5–10 minutes)
Route: IV ONLY — NEVER intrathecal (FATAL if given intrathecally)
medications.monitoring:
Neurologic exam (deep tendon reflexes, grip strength, foot drop) before each dose; CBC; LFTs (dose reduce for hepatic impairment — bilirubin >3: omit)
KEY TOXICITIES: Peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting — sensory and motor; loss of DTRs is earliest sign; foot drop, wrist drop, jaw pain, constipation/ileus from autonomic neuropathy), constipation/paralytic ileus (autonomic neuropathy — prophylactic bowel regimen mandatory), SIADH, alopecia, myelosuppression (mild compared to other agents), VESICANT
medications.watchFor: ABSOLUTE DOSE CAP OF 2 mg — never exceed regardless of BSA. FATAL IF GIVEN INTRATHECALLY — WHO recommends "For IV use only — fatal if given by other routes" labeling; should never be prepared in a syringe, only in a minibag. Prophylactic bowel regimen (senna + docusate or PEG) for all patients. Dose reduce or hold for significant neuropathy. CYP3A4 substrate — azole antifungals increase toxicity. Liposomal vincristine (Marqibo) approved for relapsed ALL — no 2 mg cap
medications.sideEffects:
- BOXED WARNING: FATAL if given intrathecally — must be administered IV only. Syringes must be labeled "FOR INTRAVENOUS USE ONLY — FATAL IF GIVEN INTRATHECALLY."
- Neurotoxicity (dose-limiting — NOT myelosuppression): Peripheral neuropathy (sensory > motor — paresthesias, loss of DTRs, foot drop, ataxia), autonomic neuropathy (constipation/ileus — very common, paralytic ileus in children), cranial nerve palsies (vocal cord paralysis, ptosis, extraocular muscle weakness), jaw pain, SIADH
- GI: Constipation (prophylactic bowel regimen recommended for ALL patients), paralytic ileus, abdominal cramps, nausea
- Other: Alopecia (common), minimal myelosuppression (key advantage), hepatic VOD (especially pediatric, in combination regimens)

medications.mechanism: Recombinant human IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds TNF-alpha, blocking its interaction with p55 and p75 cell surface TNF receptors, thereby reducing TNF-mediated inflammation.
medications.indications: FDA: RA, JIA, PsA, AS, Crohn's disease (adult/pediatric), UC, plaque psoriasis, hidradenitis suppurativa, non-infectious uveitis. Off-label: sarcoidosis, pyoderma gangrenosum.
medications.dosing:
- RA/PsA/AS: 40 mg SC every 2 weeks
- Crohn's disease (adult): 160 mg day 1, 80 mg day 15, then 40 mg every 2 weeks from day 29
- Plaque psoriasis: 80 mg initial, then 40 mg every other week starting 1 week after initial dose
- Hidradenitis suppurativa: 160 mg day 1, 80 mg day 15, then 40 mg weekly from day 29
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: TB screening (before and during), hepatitis B serology, CBC, LFTs, signs/symptoms of infection
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions, URI, headache, rash. Serious: serious infections (TB, invasive fungal), malignancies (lymphoma, HSTCL in young patients). BOXED WARNING: serious infections and malignancy.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in active serious infections. Screen for latent TB and hepatitis B before initiation. Avoid live vaccines. May worsen or cause new-onset CHF, demyelinating disease, cytopenias, or lupus-like syndrome. Pregnancy category B (limited data). No renal/hepatic dose adjustment.

medications.mechanism: Non-absorbable anion-exchange resin that binds bile acids in the intestine, forming an insoluble complex excreted in feces, thereby increasing hepatic conversion of cholesterol to bile acids and lowering LDL-C.
medications.indications: FDA: primary hypercholesterolemia (adjunct to diet), pruritus associated with partial biliary obstruction. Off-label: bile acid diarrhea, C. difficile toxin binding, Crohn's disease-related diarrhea.
medications.dosing:
- Hypercholesterolemia: 4 g (one packet) PO once or twice daily; maintenance 8–16 g/day in divided doses; max 24 g/day
- Pruritus of biliary obstruction: same dosing range
Route: PO (oral suspension; must be mixed with water or other fluid)
medications.monitoring: Lipid panel, fat-soluble vitamin levels (A, D, E, K), folate, PT/INR if on warfarin
medications.sideEffects: Common: constipation, bloating, nausea, flatulence. Serious: fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, hyperchloremic acidosis, fecal impaction, hypoprothrombinemia (vitamin K deficiency).
medications.watchFor: Binds many co-administered drugs — take other medications ≥1 hour before or 4–6 hours after cholestyramine. Supplement fat-soluble vitamins and folate with chronic use. Contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction. Use caution in renal insufficiency and volume depletion.

medications.mechanism: Human IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody that selectively binds and neutralizes IL-17A, a pro-inflammatory cytokine involved in keratinocyte proliferation and inflammatory cell recruitment.
medications.indications: FDA: moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (≥6 years), PsA (≥2 years), AS, nr-axSpA, enthesitis-related arthritis (≥4 years), hidradenitis suppurativa. Off-label: reactive arthritis.
medications.dosing:
- Plaque psoriasis (adult): 300 mg SC at weeks 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, then every 4 weeks
- PsA: 150 mg SC at weeks 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, then every 4 weeks (may use 300 mg if coexistent psoriasis)
- AS: 150 mg SC at weeks 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, then every 4 weeks (may increase to 300 mg)
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: TB screening before initiation, signs of infection, monitor for IBD symptoms
medications.sideEffects: Common: nasopharyngitis, diarrhea, URI, mucocutaneous candidiasis. Serious: serious infections, exacerbation or new-onset IBD, anaphylaxis (rare).
medications.watchFor: Avoid in active IBD (may exacerbate Crohn's disease). Screen for latent TB. Avoid live vaccines. Caution with latex allergy (needle cap contains latex). Pregnancy: limited data; weigh risks/benefits.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, disrupting transpeptidation and leading to cell lysis. Provides sustained low serum levels over weeks.
medications.indications: FDA: syphilis (all stages), group A streptococcal pharyngitis, rheumatic fever prophylaxis. Off-label: yaws, pinta, bejel.
medications.dosing:
- Primary/secondary/early latent syphilis: 2.4 million units IM × 1 dose
- Late latent/tertiary syphilis: 2.4 million units IM weekly × 3 weeks
- Rheumatic fever prophylaxis: 1.2 million units IM every 3–4 weeks
- Streptococcal pharyngitis: 1.2 million units IM × 1 dose (adults); weight-based for children
Route: IM only (NEVER IV — risk of cardiopulmonary arrest and death)
medications.monitoring: RPR/VDRL titers for syphilis treatment response, monitor for Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site pain. Serious: anaphylaxis, Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (syphilis treatment), Hoigné syndrome (non-allergic embolic reaction).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in penicillin allergy. IM injection only — inadvertent IV administration can be fatal. Not adequate for neurosyphilis (use IV aqueous penicillin G). Pregnancy safe (category B). No dose adjustment for renal impairment for standard indications.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits JAK1, modulating signaling of multiple cytokines involved in inflammation and immune function.
medications.indications: FDA: RA, PsA, atopic dermatitis (≥12 years), UC, Crohn's disease, AS, nr-axSpA, polyarticular JIA (≥2 years), giant cell arteritis — all after inadequate response/intolerance to TNF blockers (except AD).
medications.dosing:
- RA/PsA/AS/nr-axSpA/GCA: 15 mg PO once daily
- Atopic dermatitis: 15 mg or 30 mg PO once daily (adults); 15 mg (12–17 years)
- UC/Crohn's: Induction 45 mg PO once daily × 8 weeks (UC) or 12 weeks (CD); maintenance 15 or 30 mg once daily
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: CBC (lymphocytes, neutrophils, Hgb), LFTs, lipid panel, TB screening, viral hepatitis screening, signs of infection/thrombosis
medications.sideEffects: Common: URI, nausea, acne, headache, elevated CPK. Serious: serious infections, malignancy, MACE, thrombosis (DVT/PE), GI perforation. BOXED WARNING: serious infections, mortality, malignancy, MACE, thrombosis.
medications.watchFor: Avoid with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors at higher doses. Reduce dose in moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe. Reduce dose with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors for certain indications. Avoid live vaccines. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Not recommended with other JAK inhibitors or biologic DMARDs.

medications.mechanism: Competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors (M1–M5), inhibiting parasympathetic effects on smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, secretory glands, and the CNS.
medications.indications: FDA: symptomatic bradycardia, organophosphate/nerve agent poisoning, preoperative antisialagogue, antidote for cholinergic crisis. Off-label: ophthalmic (mydriasis/cycloplegia), amblyopia penalization (low-dose drops).
medications.dosing:
- Bradycardia: 0.5–1 mg IV every 3–5 min; max 3 mg
- Organophosphate poisoning: 2–4 mg IV/IM initially, repeat as needed (may require large cumulative doses)
- Preoperative: 0.4–0.6 mg IV/IM 30–60 min before anesthesia
- Ophthalmic: 1% solution, 1–2 drops
Route: IV, IM, SC, ophthalmic, endotracheal
medications.monitoring: Heart rate, blood pressure, pupil size, urinary output, mental status
medications.sideEffects: Common: dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, tachycardia, constipation, mydriasis. Serious: hyperthermia, delirium, seizures (toxicity), acute angle-closure glaucoma.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma (systemic use), obstructive uropathy, GI obstruction. Use caution in elderly (increased sensitivity). Doses 0.5 mg may cause paradoxical bradycardia. Pregnancy category C.

medications.drugClass: GABA-B receptor agonist / centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant
medications.mechanism: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord, inhibiting monosynaptic and polysynaptic reflexes, thereby reducing muscle spasticity.
medications.indications: FDA: spasticity from multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injuries/diseases. Off-label: alcohol use disorder, trigeminal neuralgia, hiccups.
medications.dosing:
- Spasticity: Start 5 mg PO TID; increase by 5 mg/dose every 3 days; max 80 mg/day in divided doses
- Intrathecal: screening dose 50 mcg; maintenance 12–2000 mcg/day via pump
Route: PO, intrathecal
medications.monitoring: Renal function, signs of withdrawal (if discontinuing), mental status
medications.sideEffects: Common: drowsiness (10–63%), dizziness, weakness, nausea, confusion. Serious: withdrawal syndrome (hallucinations, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, death if abruptly discontinued), respiratory depression, ovarian cysts.
medications.watchFor: NEVER discontinue abruptly — taper gradually to avoid life-threatening withdrawal. Use caution in renal impairment (renally excreted). Additive CNS depression with alcohol/opioids. Avoid in stroke patients (poor tolerability). May exacerbate psychotic disorders, epilepsy, autonomic dysreflexia. Pregnancy: neonatal withdrawal reported.

medications.drugClass: Corticosteroid (high topical potency, low systemic bioavailability)
medications.mechanism: Synthetic glucocorticoid with high affinity for glucocorticoid receptors (~200× cortisol). Undergoes extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism (~90%), limiting systemic effects while providing potent local anti-inflammatory activity.
medications.indications: FDA: mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease (ileal/ascending colon), UC induction (MMX formulation), asthma (inhaled), allergic rhinitis (nasal spray), eosinophilic esophagitis (oral viscous). Off-label: microscopic colitis, autoimmune hepatitis (non-cirrhotic).
medications.dosing:
- Crohn's disease: 9 mg PO once daily × 8 weeks; taper to 6 mg × 2 weeks, then 3 mg × 2 weeks
- UC (Uceris): 9 mg PO once daily × 8 weeks
- Asthma (inhaled): 0.25–1 mg nebulized BID or 180–720 mcg DPI BID (varies by formulation)
- Allergic rhinitis: 1–2 sprays per nostril once daily
Route: PO, inhaled, intranasal, rectal
medications.monitoring: Growth velocity (pediatrics), signs of HPA axis suppression, blood glucose, bone density with prolonged use
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache, nausea, acne, moon face (less than systemic steroids), oral candidiasis (inhaled). Serious: HPA axis suppression, immunosuppression, adrenal insufficiency on withdrawal.
medications.watchFor: Avoid grapefruit juice (CYP3A4 inhibition increases systemic exposure). Reduce dose in moderate hepatic impairment (increased bioavailability). Not suitable for long-term maintenance in UC. Taper when discontinuing — do not stop abruptly. Pregnancy category C; inhaled form generally considered acceptable.

medications.mechanism: Orally active tridentate iron chelator that binds ferric iron (Fe³⁺) with high selectivity, forming a complex excreted primarily in feces.
medications.indications: FDA: chronic iron overload due to blood transfusions (≥2 years), chronic iron overload in non-transfusion-dependent thalassemia (≥10 years).
medications.dosing:
- Transfusional iron overload (Jadenu): 14 mg/kg/day PO once daily; titrate by 3.5–7 mg/kg based on ferritin trends; max 28 mg/kg/day
- NTDT (Jadenu): 7 mg/kg/day PO; max 14 mg/kg/day
- Reduce starting dose by 50% if eGFR 40–60 mL/min/1.73 m²
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Serum creatinine (duplicate at baseline, then weekly × 1 month, then monthly), eGFR, serum ferritin monthly, LFTs (every 2 weeks × 1 month, then monthly), CBC, auditory and ophthalmic exams annually
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, increased creatinine. Serious: acute renal failure, hepatic failure, GI hemorrhage, agranulocytosis, SJS/TEN. BOXED WARNING: renal failure, hepatic failure, GI hemorrhage.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated if eGFR 40 mL/min/1.73 m², severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), or platelet count 50,000/µL. Avoid concomitant nephrotoxic drugs. Avoid aluminum-containing antacids. Pregnancy category C. Elderly and patients with advanced hematologic malignancies at highest risk.

medications.drugClass: Erythroid maturation agent (activin receptor ligand trap)
medications.mechanism: Recombinant fusion protein that acts as a ligand trap for select TGF-β superfamily members, reducing aberrant SMAD2/3 signaling and promoting late-stage erythroid maturation and red blood cell production.
medications.indications: FDA: anemia in adult beta-thalassemia requiring regular RBC transfusions; anemia in very low- to intermediate-risk MDS (ESA-naïve or ESA-refractory with ring sideroblasts).
medications.dosing:
- Beta-thalassemia: 1 mg/kg SC every 3 weeks; may increase to 1.25 mg/kg
- MDS: 1 mg/kg SC every 3 weeks; may titrate up to 1.75 mg/kg
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: CBC/hemoglobin before each dose, blood pressure before each administration, signs of thromboembolism
medications.sideEffects: Common: fatigue, musculoskeletal pain, dizziness, diarrhea, nausea, hypertension, headache. Serious: thromboembolism (DVT, PE, stroke — especially in beta-thalassemia), hypertension, extramedullary hematopoietic masses.
medications.watchFor: Not a substitute for RBC transfusions in acute anemia. Consider thromboprophylaxis in beta-thalassemia patients with risk factors (e.g., splenectomy). Embryo-fetal toxicity — effective contraception required. Response best in SF3B1-mutated MDS.

medications.mechanism: Releases nitric oxide (NO), which activates guanylate cyclase, increasing cGMP in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation of both arteries and veins.
medications.indications: FDA: hypertensive crisis (immediate BP reduction), controlled hypotension during surgery, acute decompensated heart failure.
medications.dosing:
- Start 0.3 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate every few minutes; usual range 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min; max 10 mcg/kg/min (limit max rate to 10 min)
- Renal impairment (eGFR 30): limit to 3 mcg/kg/min; anuric: 1 mcg/kg/min
Route: IV infusion only (protect from light)
medications.monitoring: Continuous arterial blood pressure, cyanide/thiocyanate levels (prolonged use), acid-base status, methemoglobin levels, renal function
medications.sideEffects: Common: hypotension, nausea, diaphoresis, headache. Serious: cyanide toxicity (metabolic acidosis, altered mental status, death), thiocyanate toxicity (tinnitus, seizures), methemoglobinemia. BOXED WARNING: excessive hypotension and cyanide toxicity.
medications.watchFor: Requires continuous BP monitoring. Co-infusion of sodium thiosulfate can reduce cyanide toxicity risk. Avoid prolonged use at high rates. Contraindicated in compensatory hypertension (e.g., aortic coarctation, AV shunting). May increase intracranial pressure. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits PDE-5, preventing degradation of cGMP in pulmonary vascular and corpus cavernosum smooth muscle, resulting in vasodilation.
medications.indications: FDA: pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group I — Revatio), erectile dysfunction (Viagra). Off-label: Raynaud's phenomenon, altitude sickness.
medications.dosing:
- PAH: 20 mg PO TID (4–6 hours apart) or 10 mg IV TID
- Erectile dysfunction: 25–100 mg PO ~1 hour before sexual activity; max once daily
Route: PO, IV
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure, visual/auditory symptoms, pulmonary function (PAH)
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache, flushing, dyspepsia, nasal congestion, visual disturbances (blue tinge). Serious: NAION (sudden vision loss), sudden hearing loss, priapism, hypotension. Increased mortality with higher doses in pediatric PAH.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated with nitrates (severe hypotension) and riociguat. Caution with alpha-blockers, antihypertensives. Not recommended in pediatric PAH (increased mortality at higher doses). Avoid in PVOD. Caution in patients with retinitis pigmentosa. Hepatic impairment: consider lower starting dose. Pregnancy category B.

medications.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, enhances GABA-A activity, antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, and weakly inhibits carbonic anhydrase (types II and IV).
medications.indications: FDA: epilepsy (partial-onset, primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures, Lennox-Gastaut), migraine prophylaxis. Off-label: weight loss (component of Qsymia), essential tremor, binge eating disorder, alcohol use disorder.
medications.dosing:
- Epilepsy (adult): Start 25 mg PO BID; titrate by 25–50 mg/week; target 200–400 mg/day in 2 divided doses
- Migraine prophylaxis: Start 25 mg PO nightly; titrate to 50 mg BID over 4 weeks
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Serum bicarbonate, renal function, intraocular pressure, cognitive function, weight
medications.sideEffects: Common: paresthesias, cognitive dysfunction ("word-finding difficulty"), weight loss, fatigue, dizziness, nephrolithiasis. Serious: metabolic acidosis, acute myopia/angle-closure glaucoma, oligohidrosis/hyperthermia (especially pediatric), suicidality.
medications.watchFor: Teratogenic — associated with cleft lip/palate; effective contraception required (BOXED WARNING for fetal toxicity in some labeling). Reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives at doses ≥200 mg/day. Increase fluid intake to reduce kidney stone risk. Dose adjust in renal impairment (CrCl 70: reduce dose by 50%). Avoid abrupt discontinuation (seizure risk). Avoid with other carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.

medications.mechanism: Binds nuclear retinoid receptors (RAR/RXR), modulating gene expression involved in cell proliferation, differentiation, and keratinization, normalizing epidermal growth in psoriasis.
medications.indications: FDA: severe psoriasis in adults. Off-label: other keratinization disorders (ichthyosis, Darier disease, pityriasis rubra pilaris), cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, chemoprevention of skin cancers in transplant recipients.
medications.dosing:
- Psoriasis: 25–50 mg PO once daily with main meal; adjust based on response and tolerability
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Lipid panel and LFTs monthly × 3 months, then every 3 months; CBC, renal function every 3 months; pregnancy tests monthly during and every 3 months for 3 years after stopping
medications.sideEffects: Common: cheilitis, xerosis, alopecia, pruritus, hypertriglyceridemia, elevated LFTs, arthralgias, dry eyes. Serious: hepatotoxicity, pseudotumor cerebri (especially with tetracyclines), pancreatitis (from hypertriglyceridemia), skeletal hyperostosis (long-term).
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: severe teratogenicity. Females must avoid pregnancy during treatment and for at least 3 YEARS after discontinuation (due to conversion to etretinate with alcohol). Females must not consume ANY alcohol during treatment and for 2 months after (promotes etretinate formation). Two forms of contraception required. Not immunosuppressive — can be used in HIV patients. Avoid concomitant vitamin A, tetracyclines, methotrexate.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits PDE4, increasing intracellular cAMP, which modulates production of pro-inflammatory (TNF-α, IL-23, IL-17) and anti-inflammatory (IL-10) mediators.
medications.indications: FDA: active psoriatic arthritis, moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (adults and pediatric ≥6 years), oral ulcers of Behçet's disease.
medications.dosing:
- All indications: Titrate over 5 days (10 mg → 10 mg BID → 10/20 mg → 20 mg BID → 20/30 mg → 30 mg BID); maintenance 30 mg PO BID
- Severe renal impairment (CrCl 30): 30 mg once daily (use AM titration schedule only)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Weight (discontinue if >5% unexplained loss), mood/depression screening, renal function
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea, nausea, headache, URI, vomiting (most resolve within first month). Serious: depression/suicidal ideation, severe diarrhea (especially elderly), weight loss, hypersensitivity (angioedema, anaphylaxis — postmarketing).
medications.watchFor: No routine lab monitoring required (unlike other systemic psoriasis therapies). Avoid with strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine). Screen for depression history before initiation. Not immunosuppressive. Pregnancy: use only if benefit justifies risk.

medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits JAK1 and JAK2, modulating the JAK-STAT signaling pathway and reducing cytokine-driven inflammation and immune activation.
medications.indications: FDA: moderately-to-severely active RA (after inadequate response to ≥1 TNF blocker), hospitalized COVID-19 (adults requiring supplemental O₂), severe alopecia areata.
medications.dosing:
- RA: 2 mg PO once daily
- COVID-19: 4 mg PO once daily × up to 14 days
- Alopecia areata: 2 mg PO once daily; may increase to 4 mg if inadequate response
- Renal impairment (eGFR 30–60): 1 mg once daily; avoid if eGFR 30
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: CBC (lymphocytes, neutrophils, Hgb), LFTs, lipid panel, TB screening, viral hepatitis screening, signs of infection/thrombosis
medications.sideEffects: Common: URI, nausea, herpes simplex/zoster, elevated cholesterol. Serious: serious infections, malignancy, MACE, thrombosis (DVT/PE), GI perforation. BOXED WARNING: serious infections, mortality, malignancy, MACE, thrombosis.
medications.watchFor: Avoid with other JAK inhibitors, biologic DMARDs, or strong immunosuppressants. Dose reduce in moderate renal impairment. Avoid live vaccines. May cause hypoglycemia in diabetic patients. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors: no dose adjustment needed (unlike some JAK inhibitors).

medications.mechanism: Bismuth component has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties; salicylate component inhibits prostaglandin and chloride secretion in the GI tract. Also binds enterotoxins.
medications.indications: FDA: diarrhea, heartburn, indigestion, nausea, upset stomach. Off-label: H. pylori eradication (component of bismuth quadruple therapy), traveler's diarrhea prophylaxis.
medications.dosing:
- Diarrhea/dyspepsia: 524 mg PO every 30–60 min as needed; max 4.2 g/day (8 doses); max 2 days
- H. pylori (quadruple therapy): 524 mg PO QID × 10–14 days (with metronidazole, tetracycline, PPI)
Route: PO (liquid, chewable tablets)
medications.monitoring: N/A for short-term use; salicylate levels if prolonged use or toxicity suspected
medications.sideEffects: Common: black stools/tongue (harmless), constipation. Serious: salicylate toxicity (tinnitus, encephalopathy — with overuse), neurotoxicity (bismuth encephalopathy — rare, chronic use).
medications.watchFor: Avoid in children/adolescents with viral illness (Reye syndrome risk from salicylate). Avoid with aspirin allergy. Avoid in patients on anticoagulants (salicylate interaction). Avoid in renal impairment (bismuth accumulation). Contraindicated in pregnancy (third trimester). Black stools may be confused with melena — counsel patients.

medications.drugClass: Dopamine D2 receptor agonist / ergot alkaloid
medications.mechanism: Activates dopamine D2 receptors on pituitary lactotrophs (suppressing prolactin secretion), somatotrophs (reducing GH), and nigrostriatal neurons (improving motor function in Parkinson's disease). The Cycloset formulation resets hypothalamic circadian dopamine activity to improve glycemic control.
medications.indications: FDA: hyperprolactinemia-associated dysfunctions (amenorrhea, galactorrhea, infertility, prolactinomas), acromegaly, Parkinson's disease, type 2 diabetes (Cycloset formulation only). Off-label: neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
medications.dosing:
- Hyperprolactinemia: Start 1.25–2.5 mg PO daily; increase by 2.5 mg every 2–7 days; usual 2.5–15 mg/day
- Acromegaly: Start 1.25–2.5 mg PO at bedtime; titrate to 20–30 mg/day; max 100 mg/day
- Parkinson's disease: Start 1.25 mg PO BID; increase by 2.5 mg every 14–28 days; max 100 mg/day
- Type 2 diabetes (Cycloset): Start 0.8 mg PO daily within 2 hours of waking; increase by 0.8 mg/week; usual 1.6–4.8 mg/day
Route: PO (take with food)
medications.monitoring: Prolactin levels, blood pressure, signs of fibrotic complications (long-term high-dose use), blood glucose (Cycloset)
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea, headache, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, fatigue, nasal congestion. Serious: impulse control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality), retroperitoneal/pulmonary fibrosis (high-dose, long-term), stroke/MI (postpartum lactation suppression — contraindicated), syncope.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated for postpartum lactation suppression (risk of stroke, seizures, MI). Caution with antihypertensives (additive hypotension). Avoid in uncontrolled hypertension. Safe in preconception/early pregnancy (more safety data than cabergoline). CYP3A4 substrate — avoid strong inhibitors. Ergot-related fibrosis risk lower at standard prolactinoma doses. Screen for impulse control disorders.

medications.drugClass: Dopamine D2 receptor agonist / ergot derivative
medications.mechanism: Long-acting ergot-derived dopamine D2 receptor agonist with high affinity for D2 receptors on pituitary lactotrophs, directly inhibiting prolactin secretion and reducing prolactinoma tumor size.
medications.indications: FDA: hyperprolactinemic disorders (idiopathic or due to pituitary adenomas) in adults. Off-label: Parkinson's disease, restless legs syndrome, Cushing's disease.
medications.dosing:
- Hyperprolactinemia: Start 0.25 mg PO twice weekly; titrate by 0.25 mg twice weekly at ≥4-week intervals; max 1 mg twice weekly
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Serum prolactin levels, echocardiogram at baseline and every 6–12 months (valvulopathy screening), blood pressure, signs of fibrosis (pleural, pericardial, retroperitoneal)
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea, headache, dizziness, constipation, orthostatic hypotension. Serious: cardiac valvulopathy and pericardial fibrosis (especially at high doses used in Parkinson's disease), pleural/retroperitoneal fibrosis, impulse control disorders, orthostatic hypotension.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in pre-existing valvular heart disease or pericardial fibrosis — obtain baseline echocardiogram before initiation. Avoid for postpartum lactation suppression (risk of hypertension, stroke, seizures). Preferred over bromocriptine for prolactinomas (superior efficacy, better tolerability, less frequent dosing). Valvulopathy risk is dose-dependent and primarily reported at Parkinson's disease doses (>2 mg/day). Less pregnancy safety data than bromocriptine. Monitor for impulse control disorders. Long half-life (63–69 hours).

medications.mechanism: Nonselective adenosine receptor antagonist that stimulates the medullary respiratory center, increases respiratory drive, improves diaphragmatic contractility, and enhances chemoreceptor sensitivity to CO₂.
medications.indications: FDA: short-term treatment of apnea of prematurity in infants between 28 and 33 weeks gestational age.
medications.dosing:
- Loading dose: 20 mg/kg caffeine citrate (= 10 mg/kg caffeine base) IV over 30 minutes × 1 dose
- Maintenance: 5 mg/kg caffeine citrate (= 2.5 mg/kg caffeine base) IV over 10 minutes or PO every 24 hours, beginning 24 hours after loading dose
- May increase maintenance to 10 mg/kg/day if apnea persists
Route: IV, PO
medications.monitoring: Serum caffeine levels (therapeutic 8–20 mg/L), heart rate, signs of NEC, blood glucose
medications.sideEffects: Common: feeding intolerance, jitteriness, restlessness, tachycardia. Serious: necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), seizures (toxicity), cardiac arrhythmias (toxicity), tremors.
medications.watchFor: Caffeine citrate is twice the weight of caffeine base (20 mg caffeine citrate = 10 mg caffeine base) — ensure correct dosing units. Use with caution in neonates with impaired renal or hepatic function. Therapeutic index is wide compared to theophylline. Long half-life in neonates (~100 hours in preterm infants). The CAP trial demonstrated reduced rates of BPD and improved neurodevelopmental outcomes with caffeine therapy.

medications.mechanism: Fully human IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody that selectively binds and neutralizes IL-1β, blocking its interaction with IL-1 receptors and reducing downstream inflammatory signaling.
medications.indications: FDA: CAPS (FCAS, MWS) in patients ≥4 years, TRAPS, HIDS/MKD, FMF (in adults and pediatric patients), active Still's disease (AOSD and SJIA in patients ≥2 years), symptomatic gout flares (in adults where NSAIDs/colchicine are contraindicated/inadequate and repeated corticosteroids are inappropriate). Off-label: cardiovascular risk reduction (CANTOS trial data — not FDA-approved for this).
medications.dosing:
- CAPS (≥4 years, >40 kg): 150 mg SC every 8 weeks; (15–40 kg): 2 mg/kg every 8 weeks
- TRAPS/HIDS/FMF: 150 mg SC every 4 weeks (or 2 mg/kg if ≤40 kg); may increase to 300 mg (or 4 mg/kg)
- SJIA (≥2 years, ≥7.5 kg): 4 mg/kg SC every 4 weeks; max 300 mg
- Gout flares: 150 mg SC × 1 dose
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: CBC (neutrophils), signs of infection, TB screening before initiation, LFTs, lipid panel
medications.sideEffects: Common: nasopharyngitis, diarrhea, rhinitis, nausea, headache, injection site reactions, weight gain. Serious: serious infections (including TB, opportunistic infections), neutropenia, hypersensitivity reactions, macrophage activation syndrome (MAS — in SJIA).
medications.watchFor: Screen for latent TB and hepatitis B before initiation. Avoid live vaccines. Not recommended as first-line for gout (reserved for refractory cases). CANTOS trial showed 15% reduction in MACE at 150 mg every 3 months but increased fatal infections and no all-cause mortality benefit — not approved for cardiovascular indication. Pregnancy: limited data.

medications.mechanism: Competitively inhibits ACE, preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activity. Also inhibits bradykinin degradation.
medications.indications: FDA: hypertension, heart failure (with diuretics/digitalis), left ventricular dysfunction post-MI (EF ≤40%), diabetic nephropathy (type 1 DM with proteinuria). Off-label: hypertensive urgency (sublingual/oral), scleroderma renal crisis, Raynaud's phenomenon.
medications.dosing:
- Hypertension: Start 25 mg PO BID–TID; usual 25–50 mg BID–TID; max 450 mg/day
- Heart failure: Start 6.25–12.5 mg PO TID (if volume depleted) or 25 mg TID; target 50–100 mg TID; max 450 mg/day
- Post-MI LV dysfunction: Start 6.25 mg, then 12.5 mg TID; target 50 mg TID
- Diabetic nephropathy: 25 mg PO TID
Route: PO (take 1 hour before meals — food reduces absorption by 30–40%)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure, serum creatinine, potassium, CBC (for neutropenia — especially with collagen vascular disease/renal impairment), urinalysis (proteinuria)
medications.sideEffects: Common: cough, dysgeusia (metallic taste — unique to captopril's sulfhydryl group), hypotension, rash. Serious: angioedema, hyperkalemia, acute renal failure, neutropenia/agranulocytosis (especially with collagen vascular disease + renal impairment), proteinuria.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: fetal toxicity — contraindicated in pregnancy (all trimesters). Avoid with aliskiren in diabetics. Risk of neutropenia highest in patients with collagen vascular disease and renal impairment — monitor CBC. Reduce dose in renal impairment (renally excreted). Avoid potassium-sparing diuretics/supplements. First-dose hypotension risk in volume-depleted patients. Unique sulfhydryl group causes taste disturbance and rash more than other ACE inhibitors. Short half-life (2 hours) requires TID dosing.

medications.mechanism: Cholinergic agonist that binds muscarinic receptors (particularly M1 and M3 subtypes) on salivary gland epithelial cells, stimulating salivary secretion.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of dry mouth symptoms in patients with Sjögren's syndrome.
medications.dosing:
- 30 mg PO TID
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Clinical response (salivary flow), heart rate, visual acuity (if symptoms develop)
medications.sideEffects: Common: excessive sweating (18.7%), nausea (13.8%), rhinitis (11.2%), diarrhea (10.3%), excessive salivation. Serious: cardiac conduction disturbances (with beta-blockers), visual disturbances, cholinergic crisis (overdose).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma, narrow-angle glaucoma, acute iritis. Use caution with beta-blockers (risk of conduction disturbances), nephrolithiasis, cholelithiasis. Metabolized by CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 — caution with inhibitors. May impair night vision. Counsel patients about dehydration risk from excessive sweating. Compared to pilocarpine, cevimeline has greater M3 selectivity and longer duration of action. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, suppressing inflammatory gene transcription (NF-κB, AP-1), reducing cytokine production, and inhibiting leukocyte infiltration, edema, and capillary permeability.
medications.indications: FDA: short-term treatment of inflammatory and pruritic manifestations of corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses (e.g., psoriasis, eczema, dermatitis). Off-label: alopecia areata, lichen sclerosus, vitiligo, oral lichen planus.
medications.dosing:
- Apply thin film to affected area BID; max 2 consecutive weeks of use; max 50 g/week (cream/ointment)
- Scalp (foam/solution): Apply BID × up to 2 weeks
Route: Topical (cream, ointment, gel, foam, solution, spray, shampoo)
medications.monitoring: Signs of HPA axis suppression (with prolonged/extensive use), skin atrophy, adrenal function (ACTH stimulation test if concern)
medications.sideEffects: Common: burning, stinging, pruritus at application site, skin atrophy, telangiectasia, striae. Serious: HPA axis suppression (especially with occlusion, large surface area, prolonged use), Cushing's syndrome, hyperglycemia, glaucoma/cataracts (periocular use).
medications.watchFor: Limit use to 2 consecutive weeks and ≤50 g/week. Avoid on face, groin, axillae (high absorption, atrophy risk). Do not occlude unless directed. Not recommended in children 12 years (higher surface area-to-weight ratio increases systemic absorption risk). Taper or transition to lower-potency steroid to avoid rebound. Pregnancy category C.

medications.drugClass: Anti-tissue factor pathway inhibitor (anti-TFPI) monoclonal antibody
medications.mechanism: Humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that binds TFPI via the Kunitz-2 domain, preventing TFPI-mediated inactivation of factor Xa, thereby restoring thrombin generation and hemostasis independently of factor VIII or IX.
medications.indications: FDA: routine prophylaxis to prevent or reduce bleeding episodes in patients ≥12 years with hemophilia A (with or without FVIII inhibitors) or hemophilia B (with or without FIX inhibitors).
medications.dosing:
- Day 1: Loading dose 1 mg/kg SC
- Day 2 onward: 0.2 mg/kg SC once daily
- Week 4: Measure concizumab plasma concentration; adjust dose: 4000 ng/mL → 0.15 mg/kg
Route: SC (abdomen or thigh; rotate injection sites daily)
medications.monitoring: Concizumab plasma concentration (at week 4, then at routine follow-ups after ≥8 weeks on stable dose), signs of thromboembolism, bleeding episodes
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions, urticaria. Serious: thromboembolic events (non-fatal events reported in phase 3 trials, leading to temporary trial pause and dose modification).
medications.watchFor: Maintain plasma concentration >200 ng/mL to reduce bleeding risk. If concentration remains 200 ng/mL at two consecutive measurements, consider alternative therapies. Adherence to daily dosing is critical, especially during the first 4 weeks. Missed dose guidance: 1 missed dose → resume; 2–6 missed → double dose then resume; ≥7 missed → contact physician, consider re-loading. Anti-drug antibodies detected in ~26% of patients but none were neutralizing. No dose adjustment for perioperative minor surgery. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.drugClass: Centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant (tricyclic amine)
medications.mechanism: Structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants; acts primarily at the brainstem level to reduce tonic somatic motor activity, decreasing muscle spasm without affecting muscle function.
medications.indications: FDA: adjunct to rest and physical therapy for relief of muscle spasm associated with acute, painful musculoskeletal conditions (short-term use, 2–3 weeks). Off-label: fibromyalgia (low-dose), TMJ disorders.
medications.dosing:
- Immediate-release: 5–10 mg PO TID; max 30 mg/day; use for ≤2–3 weeks
- Extended-release (Amrix): 15–30 mg PO once daily
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Heart rate, mental status (sedation), anticholinergic effects
medications.sideEffects: Common: drowsiness (39%), dry mouth (27%), dizziness (11%), fatigue. Serious: serotonin syndrome (with SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol), cardiac arrhythmias, seizures (overdose).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated with MAOIs (within 14 days), during acute recovery phase of MI, in arrhythmias, heart block, CHF, hyperthyroidism. Avoid in elderly (Beers Criteria — anticholinergic burden, sedation, fall risk). Not effective for spasticity from CNS disease. Additive CNS depression with alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids. Hepatic impairment: use with caution (start 5 mg); avoid in moderate-severe impairment. Pregnancy category B.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits calcineurin-mediated T-cell activation in the lacrimal gland and conjunctiva, reducing local inflammation and allowing recovery of tear production.
medications.indications: FDA: increase tear production in patients with keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eye disease) whose tear production is presumed to be suppressed due to ocular inflammation.
medications.dosing:
- Restasis: 1 drop in each eye BID (~12 hours apart)
- Cequa (0.09%): 1 drop in each eye BID
Route: Ophthalmic
medications.monitoring: Clinical response (Schirmer test, tear break-up time), signs of ocular infection
medications.sideEffects: Common: burning/stinging on instillation (17%), conjunctival hyperemia, discharge, epiphora, eye pain, foreign body sensation. Serious: ocular infections (rare — due to local immunosuppression).
medications.watchFor: Onset of effect may take 3–6 months; counsel patients on delayed benefit. Remove contact lenses before instillation; may reinsert 15 minutes later. Invert vial to obtain uniform emulsion before use. Not absorbed systemically at ophthalmic doses. No systemic immunosuppression. Can be used with artificial tears (separate by 15 minutes). Pregnancy category C. Not indicated for contact lens-related dryness.

medications.mechanism: Chelates copper, promoting urinary copper excretion (Wilson's disease). Also chelates cystine, reducing cystine stone formation. In RA, mechanism is unclear but may involve immunomodulation and reduction of rheumatoid factor.
medications.indications: FDA: Wilson's disease, cystinuria (prevention of cystine stone formation), severe active RA (unresponsive to conventional therapy). Off-label: lead poisoning (second-line), primary biliary cholangitis (historical, largely replaced).
medications.dosing:
- Wilson's disease: 250 mg PO QID (1 g/day); pediatric 20 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses; take on empty stomach
- Cystinuria: 1–4 g/day PO in 4 divided doses; titrate to urinary cystine 100–200 mg/day
- RA: Start 125–250 mg PO daily; increase by 125–250 mg every 1–3 months; max 1.5 g/day
Route: PO (take on empty stomach, 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals)
medications.monitoring: CBC with differential and platelets every 2 weeks × 6 months then monthly, urinalysis (proteinuria) at same intervals, LFTs, renal function, 24-hour urinary copper (Wilson's disease)
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea, anorexia, dysgeusia, rash, proteinuria. Serious: bone marrow suppression (aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia), nephrotic syndrome (membranous nephropathy), myasthenia gravis-like syndrome, Goodpasture syndrome, lupus-like syndrome, pemphigus.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity possible but rare — use with caution). Supplement pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 25 mg/day (penicillamine is a pyridoxine antagonist). Avoid concomitant gold, antimalarials, cytotoxic agents, oxyphenbutazone. Separate from food, antacids, and iron by ≥1 hour. Pregnancy: teratogenic — contraindicated in RA and cystinuria during pregnancy; in Wilson's disease, continue at reduced dose (risk of untreated disease outweighs teratogenic risk).

medications.mechanism: Orally active bidentate iron chelator that binds ferric iron (Fe³⁺) in a 3:1 molar ratio, forming a neutral complex excreted primarily in urine. Particularly effective at removing cardiac iron.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of transfusional iron overload due to thalassemia syndromes when current chelation therapy is inadequate.
medications.dosing:
- 25–33 mg/kg PO TID (75–99 mg/kg/day); max 99 mg/kg/day
- Round to nearest 250 mg (half-tablet)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: ANC weekly (for agranulocytosis), serum ferritin every 2–3 months, LFTs monthly, serum zinc levels, cardiac MRI T2 (cardiac iron)
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, arthralgia, chromaturia (reddish-brown urine — harmless), elevated ALT. Serious: agranulocytosis/neutropenia (BOXED WARNING — can be fatal), hepatotoxicity, zinc deficiency.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: agranulocytosis — obtain ANC before starting and weekly during therapy. Interrupt therapy if ANC 1500/µL; discontinue if ANC 500/µL. Do not rechallenge after agranulocytosis. Avoid concomitant drugs that can cause neutropenia. Supplement zinc if deficient. Particularly useful for cardiac iron overload (superior myocardial iron removal vs. deferoxamine). Often used in combination with deferoxamine. Pregnancy: contraindicated (embryotoxic in animals).

medications.mechanism: Deuterated form of tetrabenazine; its active metabolites (α-HTBZ and β-HTBZ) reversibly inhibit VMAT2, reducing dopamine and other monoamine packaging into synaptic vesicles, thereby decreasing dopaminergic transmission and involuntary movements.
medications.indications: FDA: chorea associated with Huntington's disease, tardive dyskinesia.
medications.dosing:
- HD chorea: Start 6 mg PO BID (or 12 mg XR once daily); titrate by 6 mg/day weekly; max 48 mg/day (or 36 mg/day with strong CYP2D6 inhibitors or in CYP2D6 poor metabolizers)
- Tardive dyskinesia: Same titration schedule
Route: PO (immediate-release with food BID; XR with or without food once daily)
medications.monitoring: Depression/suicidality screening (PHQ-9), CYP2D6 genotyping (before exceeding 36 mg/day), swallowing function, QTc interval
medications.sideEffects: Common: somnolence (11%), diarrhea, dry mouth, fatigue, insomnia, anxiety. Serious: depression/suicidality (BOXED WARNING — in HD patients), neuroleptic malignant syndrome, parkinsonism, akathisia, QTc prolongation, dysphagia.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: increased risk of depression and suicidality in patients with Huntington's disease. Contraindicated in hepatic impairment, with MAOIs (within 14 days), with reserpine (within 20 days), and in suicidal/untreated depression. CYP2D6 poor metabolizers or patients on strong CYP2D6 inhibitors: max 36 mg/day. Obtain CYP2D6 genotype before exceeding 36 mg/day. Better tolerated than tetrabenazine (longer half-life, less peak-trough fluctuation). Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Blocks fast sodium channels (phase 0 depolarization), prolonging action potential duration and effective refractory period. Also has significant negative inotropic and anticholinergic properties.
medications.indications: FDA: documented life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF). Off-label: hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM — reduces LVOT gradient via negative inotropy), vagally mediated atrial fibrillation.
medications.dosing:
- Ventricular arrhythmias: 150 mg PO every 6 hours (600 mg/day); patients 110 lbs: 100 mg every 6 hours (400 mg/day); max 800 mg/day
- Loading dose (if rapid control needed): 300 mg PO
- HOCM (off-label): 100–200 mg PO every 6–8 hours; titrate to symptom relief
- Renal impairment: Reduce dose based on CrCl
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: ECG (QRS width, QTc interval), blood pressure, blood glucose, renal function, anticholinergic symptoms, serum drug levels (therapeutic 2–4 mcg/mL)
medications.sideEffects: Common: dry mouth (32%), urinary hesitancy (14%), constipation (11%), blurred vision. Serious: CHF/hypotension (negative inotropy), QT prolongation/torsades de pointes, proarrhythmia, hypoglycemia, urinary retention, agranulocytosis (rare).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in uncompensated CHF, cardiogenic shock, second/third-degree heart block (without pacemaker), long QT syndrome, glaucoma, myasthenia gravis, urinary retention. CAST trial implications: avoid in non-life-threatening arrhythmias post-MI. Negative inotropy is therapeutically exploited in HOCM (reduces LVOT gradient). Males with BPH at high risk for urinary retention. Hypoglycemia risk increased in CHF, malnutrition, hepatic/renal disease. Renally excreted — dose adjust. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody that binds the IL-4 receptor alpha subunit, inhibiting signaling of both IL-4 and IL-13 — key type 2 inflammatory cytokines driving atopic disease, eosinophilic inflammation, and IgE production.
medications.indications: FDA: moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis (≥6 months), moderate-to-severe asthma (≥6 years, eosinophilic phenotype or OCS-dependent), CRSwNP (adults), eosinophilic esophagitis (≥1 year, ≥15 kg), prurigo nodularis (adults), COPD with type 2 inflammation (adults). Off-label: chronic spontaneous urticaria, allergic contact dermatitis.
medications.dosing:
- Atopic dermatitis (adults): 600 mg SC loading dose, then 300 mg SC every 2 weeks
- Asthma (adults): 400 mg SC loading dose, then 200 mg every 2 weeks (or 600 mg/300 mg for OCS-dependent or comorbid AD/CRSwNP/EoE)
- CRSwNP: 300 mg SC every 2 weeks
- EoE (adults ≥40 kg): 300 mg SC weekly
- Prurigo nodularis: 600 mg loading, then 300 mg every 2 weeks
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: Eosinophil count (may transiently increase), signs of helminth infection, eye symptoms (conjunctivitis/keratitis)
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions, conjunctivitis (AD indication — up to 10%), nasopharyngitis, headache, arthralgia. Serious: conjunctivitis/keratitis (may require ophthalmology referral), eosinophilia (transient, rarely hypereosinophilic syndrome), anaphylaxis (rare), serum sickness-like reactions, helminth infection exacerbation.
medications.watchFor: Not a rescue medication for acute bronchospasm. Do not abruptly discontinue systemic corticosteroids when starting dupilumab for asthma — taper gradually. Treat pre-existing helminth infections before initiation. Conjunctivitis is more common in AD patients (~10%) than asthma patients. No live vaccines during treatment. No dose adjustment for renal/hepatic impairment. Pregnancy: limited data; use if benefit outweighs risk.

medications.mechanism: Elexacaftor and tezacaftor are CFTR correctors that facilitate processing and trafficking of the F508del-CFTR protein to the cell surface. Ivacaftor is a CFTR potentiator that increases the channel open probability of CFTR protein at the cell surface. The triple combination restores significant CFTR function.
medications.indications: FDA: cystic fibrosis in patients ≥2 years with at least one F508del mutation in the CFTR gene (or other responsive mutations per FDA label).
medications.dosing:
- Adults and ≥6 years (≥30 kg): Two morning tablets (each containing elexacaftor 100 mg/tezacaftor 50 mg/ivacaftor 75 mg) + one evening tablet (ivacaftor 150 mg), ~12 hours apart
- 6 to 12 years (30 kg): One morning tablet (elexacaftor 100 mg/tezacaftor 50 mg/ivacaftor 75 mg) + one evening tablet (ivacaftor 75 mg)
- 2 to 6 years: Weight-based dosing with granule formulations
- Take with fat-containing food
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: LFTs (monthly × 6 months, then every 3 months × 12 months, then annually), bilirubin, ophthalmologic exam at baseline and follow-up in pediatric patients (cataracts reported with ivacaftor), ppFEV₁, sweat chloride, BMI/nutritional status
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache (17%), URI (16%), abdominal pain (14%), diarrhea (13%), rash (10%), elevated ALT (10%), elevated AST (9%). Serious: drug-induced liver injury and liver failure (potentially fatal), intracranial hypertension, cataracts (pediatric), anaphylaxis (postmarketing), neuropsychiatric events including suicidal ideation (postmarketing).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers (rifampin, St. John's wort). Dose adjustment required with moderate/strong CYP3A4 inhibitors and in moderate hepatic impairment. Not recommended in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C). Interrupt if ALT/AST >5× ULN. Approximately 90% of CF patients eligible. Long-term data (192 weeks) confirm sustained improvements in ppFEV₁ with no evidence of pulmonary function loss. Pregnancy: limited data.

medications.mechanism: Humanized bispecific monoclonal antibody that bridges activated factor IX (FIXa) and factor X (FX), mimicking the cofactor function of missing activated factor VIII and restoring effective hemostasis.
medications.indications: FDA: routine prophylaxis to prevent or reduce the frequency of bleeding episodes in adult and pediatric patients with hemophilia A (congenital factor VIII deficiency) with or without factor VIII inhibitors.
medications.dosing:
- Loading dose: 3 mg/kg SC once weekly × 4 weeks
- Maintenance (choose one): 1.5 mg/kg SC once weekly, OR 3 mg/kg SC every 2 weeks, OR 6 mg/kg SC every 4 weeks
Route: SC (abdomen, upper arm, or thigh)
medications.monitoring: Bleeding episodes, signs of TMA/thromboembolism (especially with concomitant aPCC), anti-drug antibodies if loss of efficacy suspected
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions (15%), headache (15%), arthralgia (15%). Serious: thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) and thromboembolism — both associated with concomitant aPCC use (>100 U/kg/24 hours), immunogenicity with neutralizing antibodies (1%).
medications.watchFor: Discontinue prophylactic bypassing agents the day before starting emicizumab. If aPCC is needed for breakthrough bleeds, avoid cumulative doses >100 U/kg/24 hours (risk of TMA/thromboembolism). Emicizumab interferes with aPTT-based coagulation assays — aPTT will be artificially shortened; use chromogenic Bethesda assay (bovine) for FVIII inhibitor titers and chromogenic FVIII activity assay. No dose adjustment for renal/hepatic impairment. Pregnancy: limited data. Long-term data (>120 weeks) confirm sustained low ABR with 82% of patients having zero treated bleeds.

medications.mechanism: Competitively inhibits type II 5-alpha reductase, blocking conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) in the prostate, scalp, and other tissues, reducing DHT levels by ~70%.
medications.indications: FDA: symptomatic BPH (5 mg — Proscar), male pattern hair loss/androgenetic alopecia (1 mg — Propecia). Off-label: hirsutism in women (not recommended in pregnancy).
medications.dosing:
- BPH: 5 mg PO once daily (may combine with doxazosin)
- Male pattern hair loss: 1 mg PO once daily; ≥3 months needed before benefit observed
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: PSA (note: finasteride reduces PSA by ~50% — double measured PSA for accurate interpretation), digital rectal exam, signs of sexual dysfunction
medications.sideEffects: Common: decreased libido (6.4%), erectile dysfunction (8.1%), decreased ejaculate volume (3.7%), breast tenderness/enlargement. Serious: post-finasteride syndrome (persistent sexual dysfunction after discontinuation — reported in postmarketing), depression/suicidal ideation (postmarketing), male breast cancer (rare — 4 cases in MTOPS trial), high-grade prostate cancer (increased incidence in PCPT trial).
medications.watchFor: Women who are or may become pregnant must not handle crushed/broken tablets (risk of hypospadias in male fetus). Reduces PSA by ~50% — any confirmed increase from nadir while on therapy should prompt evaluation for prostate cancer. Not indicated for use in women or children. Blood donation should be deferred for ≥1 month after discontinuation. Pregnancy category X.

medications.mechanism: Double-stranded siRNA that targets hepatic antithrombin (AT) mRNA for degradation via RNA interference, reducing plasma AT levels and thereby increasing thrombin generation to rebalance hemostasis independently of factor VIII or IX.
medications.indications: FDA: routine prophylaxis to prevent or reduce the frequency of bleeding episodes in adults and pediatric patients ≥12 years with hemophilia A or B, with or without factor VIII or IX inhibitors.
medications.dosing:
- Starting dose: 50 mg SC once every 2 months
- Dose adjustment based on AT activity levels (target 15–35%): may adjust to 20 mg every 2 months, or 20/50/80 mg monthly as needed
Route: SC
medications.monitoring: AT activity levels (target 15–35%; measure before each dose during first 6 months, then periodically), LFTs (monthly × 6 months after initiation and after dose increases, then periodically), signs of thromboembolism, gallbladder symptoms (ultrasound if symptomatic), homocysteine levels
medications.sideEffects: Common: elevated ALT (19–32%), injection site reactions, headache, arthralgia. Serious: thrombotic events (including fatal cerebral venous sinus thrombosis at higher doses), gallbladder disease (cholecystitis, cholelithiasis — 13–15%, some requiring cholecystectomy), hepatotoxicity. BOXED WARNING: thrombotic events and acute/recurrent gallbladder disease.
medications.watchFor: The 80 mg once monthly dose is NOT approved or recommended (associated with higher thrombotic risk). Persistent AT activity 15% is a risk factor for thrombosis — if AT 15% at two consecutive measurements, dose reduction or discontinuation required. Discontinue prophylactic BPA/CFC no later than 7 days after starting fitusiran. For breakthrough bleeds, use lowest effective dose of CFC/BPA per specific guidelines. Contraindicated in patients with established thrombophilia or history of thrombosis (generally excluded from trials). After discontinuation, AT activity remains suppressed for ~6 months. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.drugClass: Gabapentin prodrug (voltage-gated calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligand)
medications.mechanism: Prodrug of gabapentin that is actively transported via intestinal nutrient transporters, providing improved and more predictable bioavailability compared to gabapentin. After absorption, it is rapidly hydrolyzed to gabapentin, which binds the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, modulating neurotransmitter release.
medications.indications: FDA: moderate-to-severe primary restless legs syndrome (RLS) in adults, postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) in adults.
medications.dosing:
- RLS: 600 mg PO once daily at approximately 5 PM
- PHN: 600 mg PO once daily in the morning × 3 days, then 600 mg PO BID starting day 4
- Renal impairment: dose adjustment required (CrCl 30–59: RLS 300 mg/day, PHN 300 mg BID; CrCl 15–29: RLS 300 mg/day, PHN 300 mg daily; CrCl 15: RLS 300 mg every other day, PHN 300 mg every other day; hemodialysis: not recommended)
Route: PO (swallow whole with food; do not crush, split, or chew)
medications.monitoring: Renal function, signs of CNS depression, suicidality screening
medications.sideEffects: Common: somnolence/sedation (20%), dizziness (13%), headache (12%), fatigue (6%), nausea (6%), peripheral edema. Serious: suicidal behavior/ideation (class warning for antiepileptics), respiratory depression (with opioids/CNS depressants), withdrawal symptoms (seizures, anxiety if abruptly discontinued).
medications.watchFor: NOT interchangeable with other gabapentin products (different pharmacokinetics). Must be taken with food. Avoid abrupt discontinuation — taper over ≥1 week. Additive CNS depression with opioids, alcohol, benzodiazepines. Driving impairment may occur. Schedule V controlled substance. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that binds calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), preventing its binding to the CGRP receptor and blocking CGRP-mediated vasodilation and neurogenic inflammation involved in migraine and cluster headache pathophysiology.
medications.indications: FDA: preventive treatment of migraine in adults, treatment of episodic cluster headache in adults.
medications.dosing:
- Migraine prevention: 240 mg SC loading dose (two 120 mg injections), then 120 mg SC once monthly
- Episodic cluster headache: 300 mg SC (three 100 mg injections) at onset of cluster period, then 300 mg SC monthly until end of cluster period
Route: SC (abdomen, thigh, back of upper arm, or buttocks)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure, signs of constipation, clinical response (migraine diary)
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions (18%), injection site pain (8%), nasopharyngitis, URI. Serious: anaphylaxis/angioedema (postmarketing), constipation with serious complications (postmarketing), hypertension (postmarketing), Raynaud's phenomenon (postmarketing).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in patients with serious hypersensitivity to galcanezumab. Different dosing for migraine vs. cluster headache (120 mg vs. 300 mg monthly). Allow 3 months to assess efficacy for migraine. No dose adjustment for renal/hepatic impairment. No significant drug interactions. Pregnancy: limited data; CGRP antibodies cross the placenta — weigh risks/benefits. Not studied in chronic cluster headache.

medications.mechanism: N-acetylgalactosamine-conjugated siRNA that is selectively taken up by hepatocytes and targets ALAS1 (aminolevulinic acid synthase 1) mRNA for degradation via RNA interference, reducing production of the neurotoxic heme precursors ALA and PBG.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of adults with acute hepatic porphyria (AHP).
medications.dosing:
- 2.5 mg/kg SC once monthly
- If severe transaminase elevations occur: reduce to 1.25 mg/kg once monthly; may re-escalate to 2.5 mg/kg if tolerated
Route: SC (administered by healthcare professional)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (monthly × 6 months, then as clinically indicated), serum creatinine/eGFR (periodically), homocysteine levels (before and during treatment — supplement B vitamins if elevated), amylase/lipase, urinary ALA and PBG levels
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions (25%), nausea (27%), elevated ALT (15%), fatigue, elevated creatinine (15%). Serious: anaphylaxis, hepatotoxicity (ALT ≥3× ULN in 15%), renal toxicity (increased creatinine, decreased eGFR), pancreatitis, hyperhomocysteinemia.
medications.watchFor: Interrupt or discontinue for ALT/AST >5× ULN or with signs of liver injury. Homocysteine elevation is common — supplement with vitamin B6, folate, and B12. In the ENVISION trial, givosiran reduced porphyria attacks by 70% vs. placebo. Long-term data (36 months) show sustained attack reduction with 86–92% of patients attack-free at end of study. Pregnancy: not recommended (limited data). No dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal impairment; not studied in severe renal impairment.

medications.mechanism: Mimics endogenous prostacyclin (PGI₂), activating prostacyclin receptors (IP receptors) on vascular smooth muscle and platelets, causing pulmonary vasodilation, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and antiproliferative effects on vascular smooth muscle cells.
medications.indications: FDA: pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group I, NYHA class III–IV) to improve exercise tolerance and symptoms and delay clinical deterioration. Off-label: Raynaud's phenomenon (IV formulation, outside US), digital ulcers in systemic sclerosis.
medications.dosing:
- Inhaled: Start 2.5 mcg/inhalation; if tolerated, increase to 5 mcg/inhalation; administer 6–9 times daily (no more than every 2 hours) during waking hours via adaptive aerosol delivery device
- Each inhalation session takes ~4–10 minutes
Route: Inhaled (via I-neb or Prodose AAD nebulizer systems)
medications.monitoring: Vital signs (blood pressure — risk of syncope), pulmonary function tests (before initiation — risk of bronchospasm), 6-minute walk distance, functional class, signs of pulmonary edema
medications.sideEffects: Common: cough (39%), headache (30%), flushing (27%), flu-like syndrome, nausea, trismus/jaw pain (12%), hypotension (11%), insomnia. Serious: syncope, bronchospasm, pulmonary edema (if PVOD suspected — discontinue), bleeding (antiplatelet effect).
medications.watchFor: Avoid in patients with systolic BP 85 mmHg. Avoid mixing with other medications in the nebulizer. Requires dedicated nebulizer system. Short duration of action necessitates frequent dosing (6–9 times/day), which limits adherence. Avoid abrupt withdrawal. Additive hypotension with antihypertensives. May increase bleeding risk with anticoagulants. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic catecholamine that stimulates both β₁ and β₂ adrenergic receptors, increasing heart rate, contractility, and conduction velocity (β₁) while causing bronchodilation and peripheral vasodilation (β₂).
medications.indications: FDA: mild or transient episodes of heart block not requiring pacemaker or electric shock, serious episodes of heart block and Adams-Stokes attacks (except when caused by VT/VF), cardiac arrest until definitive therapy available, bronchospasm during anesthesia. Off-label: pharmacologic provocation in Brugada syndrome diagnosis, beta-blocker overdose, torsades de pointes (rate-dependent suppression).
medications.dosing:
- Bradycardia/heart block: 2–10 mcg/min IV infusion; titrate to heart rate response
- Cardiac arrest: 0.02–0.06 mg IV bolus, then 5 mcg/min infusion
- Brugada provocation: 1–3 mcg/min IV infusion
Route: IV (infusion or bolus)
medications.monitoring: Continuous ECG, heart rate, blood pressure, urine output
medications.sideEffects: Common: tachycardia, palpitations, headache, flushing, tremor, nausea. Serious: ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF), myocardial ischemia/infarction, severe hypotension (from β₂-mediated vasodilation), hypokalemia.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in tachyarrhythmias, digitalis-induced arrhythmias, angina pectoris. Use with extreme caution in coronary artery disease. Not a first-line agent for bradycardia (atropine and pacing preferred per ACLS). Largely replaced by transcutaneous/transvenous pacing. Increases myocardial oxygen demand. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Reduces sebaceous gland size and sebum production by ~90%, normalizes follicular keratinization, has anti-inflammatory properties, and indirectly reduces Cutibacterium acnes colonization. The only acne medication that targets all four pathogenic factors.
medications.indications: FDA: severe recalcitrant nodular acne unresponsive to conventional therapy (including systemic antibiotics). Off-label: moderate acne unresponsive to other treatments, rosacea (low-dose), hidradenitis suppurativa, prevention of skin cancers in high-risk patients.
medications.dosing:
- 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO in 1–2 divided doses × 15–20 weeks; some use cumulative dose target of 120–150 mg/kg
- Low-dose regimens (0.25–0.5 mg/kg/day) used off-label for moderate acne
- Take with fatty food (increases absorption by 2×; Absorica can be taken without food)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Pregnancy test (two negative tests before starting, monthly during, and 1 month after), lipid panel and LFTs at baseline, 1 month, then every 1–2 months, CBC, mood/depression screening
medications.sideEffects: Common: cheilitis (>90%), xerosis, dry eyes, epistaxis, myalgias, elevated triglycerides, elevated LFTs, photosensitivity. Serious: teratogenicity (BOXED WARNING), depression/suicidality (debated but FDA-mandated warning), pseudotumor cerebri (especially with tetracyclines), inflammatory bowel disease (controversial association), pancreatitis (from hypertriglyceridemia), premature epiphyseal closure.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: severe birth defects — iPLEDGE REMS program mandatory. Females of reproductive potential must use two forms of contraception starting 1 month before, during, and 1 month after treatment. Monthly pregnancy tests required. Cannot donate blood during and for 1 month after treatment. Avoid concomitant tetracyclines (pseudotumor cerebri risk), vitamin A supplements, and waxing/laser procedures (scarring risk). Night vision impairment may occur. Pregnancy category X.

medications.mechanism: Binds to the CFTR protein at the cell surface and increases the probability of channel opening (gating), thereby enhancing chloride ion transport in patients with CFTR gating mutations (e.g., G551D) or other responsive mutations.
medications.indications: FDA: cystic fibrosis in patients ≥1 month with at least one mutation in the CFTR gene that is responsive to ivacaftor potentiation (based on clinical and/or in vitro assay data — currently ~97 mutations).
medications.dosing:
- ≥6 years: 150 mg PO every 12 hours with fat-containing food
- 6 months to 6 years: weight-based (25–75 mg every 12 hours)
- 4 to 6 months: 25 mg every 12 hours
- 1 to 4 months: 5.8–13.4 mg every 12 hours
Route: PO (with fat-containing food)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (every 3 months × 1 year, then annually), ophthalmologic exam at baseline and annually in pediatric patients (cataracts), ppFEV₁, sweat chloride, weight/BMI
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache, oropharyngeal pain, URI, nasal congestion, abdominal pain, nasopharyngitis, diarrhea, rash, nausea, elevated transaminases. Serious: elevated transaminases (ALT/AST >5× ULN — interrupt dosing), cataracts (non-congenital lens opacities in pediatric patients), intracranial hypertension (postmarketing), anaphylaxis (postmarketing), neuropsychiatric events (postmarketing).
medications.watchFor: Only ~5% of CF patients carry gating mutations responsive to ivacaftor monotherapy; most F508del patients require triple combination (Trikafta). Dose reduction required with moderate/strong CYP3A4 inhibitors. Avoid strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, St. John's wort). Avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges. Hepatic impairment: reduce dose in moderate (Child-Pugh B); avoid in severe (Child-Pugh C). Pregnancy: limited data.

medications.mechanism: Prodrug of dextroamphetamine; after oral absorption, lisdexamfetamine is enzymatically cleaved in red blood cells to release active d-amphetamine, which blocks reuptake and promotes release of norepinephrine and dopamine in the CNS.
medications.indications: FDA: ADHD in adults and pediatric patients ≥6 years, moderate-to-severe binge eating disorder (BED) in adults. Not indicated for weight loss.
medications.dosing:
- ADHD: Start 30 mg PO once daily in the morning; titrate by 10–20 mg/week; max 70 mg/day
- BED: Start 30 mg PO once daily; titrate by 20 mg/week; target 50–70 mg/day; max 70 mg/day
- Severe renal impairment (eGFR 15–29): max 50 mg/day; ESRD (eGFR 15): max 30 mg/day
Route: PO (capsules can be opened and mixed with yogurt, water, or orange juice)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure, heart rate, height/weight (pediatric — growth suppression), psychiatric symptoms (psychosis, mania, aggression), signs of peripheral vasculopathy
medications.sideEffects: Common: decreased appetite (27–39%), insomnia (19–27%), dry mouth (26%), nausea, diarrhea, anxiety, irritability, weight loss. Serious: sudden death (in patients with structural cardiac abnormalities), MI/stroke, psychosis/mania, serotonin syndrome (with serotonergic agents), peripheral vasculopathy (Raynaud's), growth suppression. BOXED WARNING: abuse, misuse, and addiction; CNS stimulants have high potential for abuse and dependence.
medications.watchFor: Schedule II controlled substance. Contraindicated with MAOIs (within 14 days), known hypersensitivity to amphetamines, and advanced arteriosclerosis/symptomatic cardiovascular disease. Prodrug design reduces (but does not eliminate) abuse potential compared to immediate-release amphetamines. Avoid afternoon dosing (insomnia). Screen for bipolar disorder before initiation. May exacerbate tics. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Exact mechanism unknown; modulates multiple intracellular signaling pathways including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase (depleting inositol), inhibition of glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK-3β), and modulation of neurotransmitter release (serotonin, dopamine, glutamate).
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of manic episodes of bipolar disorder, maintenance therapy for bipolar disorder. Off-label: bipolar depression (augmentation), treatment-resistant depression (augmentation), cluster headache prophylaxis, schizoaffective disorder, suicide prevention.
medications.dosing:
- Acute mania: 900–1800 mg/day PO in 2–3 divided doses (IR) or 900–1800 mg/day in 1–2 doses (ER); titrate to therapeutic level
- Maintenance: 600–1200 mg/day PO; target serum level 0.6–1.0 mEq/L (maintenance) or 0.8–1.2 mEq/L (acute mania)
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Serum lithium level (12 hours post-dose; weekly during titration, then every 1–3 months), renal function (BUN, creatinine, eGFR every 6 months), thyroid function (TSH every 6 months), calcium/PTH (annually — hyperparathyroidism risk), ECG (baseline), urinalysis, weight, pregnancy test
medications.sideEffects: Common: tremor, polyuria/polydipsia (nephrogenic DI), weight gain, nausea, diarrhea, hypothyroidism (up to 20%), cognitive dulling, acne.
