Serious: lithium toxicity (>1.5 mEq/L — ataxia, coarse tremor, confusion, seizures, renal failure, death), nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, chronic kidney disease, cardiac conduction abnormalities (Brugada-like pattern, sinus node dysfunction), hyperparathyroidism/hypercalcemia, Ebstein anomaly (first trimester teratogenicity — risk lower than historically estimated).
medications.watchFor: Narrow therapeutic index (0.6–1.2 mEq/L). Toxicity risk increased by dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, low-sodium diet. Maintain adequate hydration and sodium intake. Avoid in severe renal impairment. Dose reduce in elderly. Lithium is the only mood stabilizer with demonstrated anti-suicidal properties. Pregnancy: associated with Ebstein anomaly (absolute risk ~0.1–0.2%); fetal echocardiography recommended if exposed in first trimester. Breastfeeding: generally avoided (excreted in breast milk).
medications.onset: PO — peak 0.5–2 hrs (IR); 4–12 hrs (ER); clinical effect 1–3 weeks (mania); full prophylactic effect may take months
medications.halfLife: ~18–36 hrs (mean ~24 hrs); longer in elderly and renal impairment; shorter in younger patients
medications.metabolism: NOT metabolized — excreted entirely unchanged by kidneys
medications.excretion: Renal (95% — filtered at glomerulus, 80% reabsorbed in proximal tubule alongside sodium; anything that increases sodium reabsorption increases lithium reabsorption → toxicity risk)
medications.maxDose: Individualized by serum levels; typical 900–1,800 mg/day (divided BID-TID for IR; once-twice daily for ER); elderly: lower doses
medications.renalHepaticDosing: Renal impairment: REDUCE dose and monitor closely (lithium is entirely renally excreted); avoid in severe renal impairment if possible; GFR decline is a known long-term effect; no hepatic adjustment needed (not hepatically metabolized)
medications.pregnancyLactation: Category D; Ebstein's anomaly risk ~1:1000 (lower than historically believed); weigh risks vs benefits carefully; levels fluctuate in pregnancy (increased GFR → lower levels; postpartum GFR decrease → toxicity risk)
medications.contraindications: Significant renal impairment (relative); Brugada syndrome; severe debilitation/dehydration; sodium-depleted patients. DRUG INTERACTIONS causing toxicity: NSAIDs (reduce renal lithium clearance by 15–20%), thiazides (increase proximal tubule reabsorption), ACE inhibitors/ARBs (reduce GFR), metronidazole. Loop diuretics are safer (less effect on lithium levels). Only medication proven to reduce suicide risk. Gold standard for bipolar disorder prophylaxis.

medications.mechanism: Allosteric, reversible inhibitor of cardiac myosin ATPase that reduces the number of actin-myosin cross-bridges during systole, shifting myosin to an energy-sparing super-relaxed state. This decreases excessive contractility and LVOT obstruction in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of adults with symptomatic NYHA class II–III obstructive HCM to improve functional capacity and symptoms.
medications.dosing:
- Starting dose: 5 mg PO once daily
- Titrate based on echocardiographic LVEF and Valsalva LVOT gradient at weeks 4, 8, 12, then every 12 weeks
- Dose range: 2.5 mg, 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once daily; max 15 mg/day
- Interrupt if LVEF 50% at any visit
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Echocardiogram (LVEF and Valsalva LVOT gradient) at baseline, weeks 4, 8, 12, then every 12 weeks; clinical status; NT-proBNP; pregnancy testing. Available only through CAMZYOS REMS program.
medications.sideEffects: Common: dizziness (27%), syncope (6%), heart failure symptoms. Serious: heart failure due to systolic dysfunction (LVEF 50% in ~6% of patients — reversible with dose adjustment/discontinuation). BOXED WARNING: risk of heart failure due to systolic dysfunction.
medications.watchFor: REMS program required — prescribers, pharmacies, and patients must be enrolled. Do not initiate if LVEF 55%. Contraindicated with strong CYP2C19 inhibitors, moderate-to-strong CYP2C19 or CYP3A4 inducers. CYP2C19 metabolizer status affects exposure (poor metabolizers have higher levels). Avoid concomitant disopyramide, ranolazine, verapamil + beta-blocker, or diltiazem + beta-blocker. Embryo-fetal toxicity — effective contraception required; avoid combined hormonal contraceptives containing progestins other than norethindrone (CYP interaction). Intercurrent illness or new AF increases risk of systolic dysfunction — consider temporary interruption. In EXPLORER-HCM, mavacamten significantly improved exercise capacity, NYHA class, and LVOT gradients vs. placebo.

medications.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (preferentially inactivated state), shortening the action potential duration and effective refractory period. Structurally similar to lidocaine but orally bioavailable.
medications.indications: FDA: documented life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (VT). Off-label: myotonia (non-dystrophic myotonias, myotonic dystrophy), neuropathic pain, long QT syndrome type 3 (LQT3 — late sodium current blocker).
medications.dosing:
- Ventricular arrhythmias: Start 200 mg PO every 8 hours with food; may increase by 50–100 mg every 2–3 days; max 1200 mg/day; loading dose 400 mg may be used for rapid control
- Myotonia (off-label): 150–200 mg PO BID–TID
- LQT3 (off-label): 3–8 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses
Route: PO (take with food to reduce GI effects)
medications.monitoring: ECG (QRS duration, QTc), hepatic function, CBC, serum drug levels (therapeutic 0.5–2 mcg/mL)
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea (40%), dizziness (26%), tremor (13%), coordination difficulties (10%), GI distress (41%). Serious: proarrhythmia, hepatotoxicity (acute hepatic necrosis — rare), blood dyscrasias (agranulocytosis — rare), DRESS, SJS/TEN (rare), seizures. BOXED WARNING: increased mortality with antiarrhythmic drugs in non-life-threatening arrhythmias (CAST trial implications).
medications.watchFor: Initiate treatment in hospital. Reserve for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. Hepatic impairment prolongs half-life — use with caution. Urinary pH affects excretion (alkaline urine increases half-life). Avoid in second/third-degree heart block without pacemaker. CYP1A2 and CYP2D6 substrate. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Selectively activates beta-3 adrenergic receptors on detrusor smooth muscle, promoting bladder relaxation during the storage phase and increasing functional bladder capacity without affecting voiding contractions.
medications.indications: FDA: overactive bladder (OAB) with symptoms of urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and urinary frequency in adults; neurogenic detrusor overactivity (NDO) in pediatric patients ≥3 years.
medications.dosing:
- OAB (adults): Start 25 mg PO once daily; may increase to 50 mg once daily after 4–8 weeks
- NDO (pediatric ≥35 kg): 25 mg PO once daily; may increase to 48 mg (granules) once daily
- Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): do not exceed 25 mg; severe renal impairment (eGFR 15–29): do not exceed 25 mg; ESRD: not recommended
Route: PO (swallow whole; do not crush or chew)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure (may increase by 3–10 mmHg), post-void residual (if BOO suspected), urinary symptoms
medications.sideEffects: Common: hypertension (8–11%), nasopharyngitis (3–4%), UTI (3–4%), headache (2–3%), constipation, diarrhea. Serious: urinary retention, hypertensive crisis (rare), angioedema (rare), atrial fibrillation (0.2%).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in severe uncontrolled hypertension. Monitor BP periodically. Avoid in ESRD or severe hepatic impairment at doses >25 mg. Inhibits CYP2D6 — may increase levels of metoprolol, desipramine, and other CYP2D6 substrates. Increases digoxin levels — monitor. Advantage over antimuscarinics: significantly lower incidence of dry mouth and cognitive effects, making it preferable in elderly patients. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Inhibits multiple receptor tyrosine kinases including PDGFR α/β, FGFR 1–3, VEGFR 1–3, and CSF1R, blocking intracellular signaling cascades involved in fibrotic tissue remodeling in interstitial lung diseases.
medications.indications: FDA: idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), chronic fibrosing interstitial lung diseases (ILDs) with a progressive phenotype, systemic sclerosis-associated ILD (SSc-ILD).
medications.dosing:
- All indications: 150 mg PO BID approximately 12 hours apart with food
- Mild hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A): 100 mg PO BID
- Moderate/severe hepatic impairment: not recommended
- Dose reduction to 100 mg BID for adverse reactions; may re-escalate to 150 mg BID
Route: PO (swallow whole with food; do not crush or chew)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin) before initiation, at regular intervals during first 3 months, then periodically; pregnancy testing
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea (62%), nausea (24%), abdominal pain (15%), vomiting (12%), liver enzyme elevation (14%), decreased appetite (11%), weight loss (10%). Serious: drug-induced liver injury (including fatal cases — postmarketing), arterial thromboembolic events (MI 1.5%), bleeding, GI perforation (rare), nephrotic-range proteinuria (rare).
medications.watchFor: Embryo-fetal toxicity — effective contraception required during and for ≥3 months after last dose. Manage diarrhea early with hydration and loperamide. Discontinue if ALT/AST >3× ULN with clinical signs of liver injury or bilirubin >2× ULN. Use caution in patients at higher cardiovascular risk. Slows FVC decline by ~50% in IPF. Concomitant use with pirfenidone is feasible. P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4 substrate. Pregnancy: contraindicated.

medications.mechanism: Primarily exerts pharmacologic activity through its active metabolite (10-monohydroxy derivative, MHD), which blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, stabilizing hyperexcited neural membranes and inhibiting repetitive neuronal firing.
medications.indications: FDA: partial-onset (focal) seizures — monotherapy or adjunctive therapy in adults; monotherapy in children ≥4 years; adjunctive therapy in children ≥2 years. Off-label: trigeminal neuralgia, bipolar disorder.
medications.dosing:
- Adults (IR): Start 600 mg/day PO in 2 divided doses; increase by 600 mg/day weekly; target 1200 mg/day; max 2400 mg/day
- Adults (XR): Start 600 mg PO once daily on empty stomach; increase weekly; target 1200–2400 mg/day
- Pediatric: 8–10 mg/kg/day; max 60 mg/kg/day
- Renal impairment (CrCl 30): start at half the usual dose; titrate slowly
Route: PO (IR: with or without food; XR: on empty stomach, swallow whole)
medications.monitoring: Serum sodium (especially first 3 months and with concomitant sodium-lowering drugs), CBC, signs of hypersensitivity, mood/suicidality
medications.sideEffects: Common: dizziness (22–49%), somnolence (20–36%), diplopia (14–40%), nausea (15–29%), vomiting (7–36%), ataxia (5–31%), headache, fatigue, nystagmus, tremor. Serious: hyponatremia (125 mmol/L in 2.5% — SIADH mechanism), SJS/TEN, DRESS, aplastic anemia, anaphylaxis, angioedema, suicidal ideation.
medications.watchFor: 25–30% cross-reactivity with carbamazepine hypersensitivity — question patients about prior carbamazepine reactions. HLA-B1502 screening recommended in patients of Southeast Asian ancestry (risk of SJS/TEN). Induces CYP3A4 — reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, cyclosporine, and other CYP3A4 substrates. Does NOT require routine drug level monitoring (unlike carbamazepine). Avoid abrupt discontinuation. Pregnancy: may cause fetal harm; monitor MHD levels during pregnancy (levels may decrease).

medications.mechanism: Competitively antagonizes muscarinic (M1–M3) receptors on detrusor smooth muscle, reducing involuntary bladder contractions. Also has direct antispasmodic effects on smooth muscle and local anesthetic properties.
medications.indications: FDA: overactive bladder (OAB) with symptoms of urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency; neurogenic detrusor overactivity (pediatric ≥6 years for ER; ≥5 years for IR). Off-label: hyperhidrosis (topical).
medications.dosing:
- IR (adults): 5 mg PO BID–TID; max 20 mg/day; frail elderly: start 2.5 mg BID–TID
- ER (adults): 5–10 mg PO once daily; max 30 mg/day
- Transdermal patch (Oxytrol): 3.9 mg/day patch applied twice weekly
- Topical gel (Gelnique): 1 g (10%) applied daily to thigh, abdomen, upper arm, or shoulder
- Pediatric (≥5 years, IR): 5 mg PO BID; max 15 mg/day
Route: PO (IR, ER), transdermal, topical gel
medications.monitoring: Post-void residual (if BOO suspected), anticholinergic side effects, cognitive function (especially elderly)
medications.sideEffects: Common: dry mouth (20–70%), constipation (9–15%), somnolence, blurred vision, dizziness, headache, cognitive impairment. Serious: urinary retention, angioedema, heat prostration (decreased sweating), hallucinations/delirium (especially elderly), QT prolongation (rare).
medications.watchFor: Beers Criteria: avoid in elderly (high anticholinergic burden — associated with cognitive decline, dementia risk, falls). Transdermal and topical formulations have lower systemic anticholinergic effects than oral IR. Contraindicated in uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention, gastric retention. Use caution in myasthenia gravis, GI obstructive disorders, ulcerative colitis. Additive effects with other anticholinergics. May worsen dementia symptoms in patients on cholinesterase inhibitors. Pregnancy category B.

medications.mechanism: Non-peptide small molecule that selectively agonizes SSTR2 (>4000-fold selectivity over other SST receptor subtypes), suppressing growth hormone (GH) and insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) secretion similarly to injectable somatostatin receptor ligands.
medications.indications: FDA (approved September 2025): treatment of adults with acromegaly who had an inadequate response to surgery and/or for whom surgery is not an option.
medications.dosing:
- Start 40 mg PO once daily on an empty stomach (≥6 hours after a meal, ≥1 hour before next meal)
- After 2–4 weeks, titrate to 60 mg once daily based on IGF-1 levels
- May reduce to 20 mg once daily if needed for tolerability
Route: PO (first oral treatment for acromegaly)
medications.monitoring: IGF-1 levels (at baseline, 2–4 weeks, then periodically), GH levels, gallbladder ultrasound (baseline and periodically), fasting blood glucose/HbA1c, TSH/free T4, ECG (bradycardia risk), vitamin B12 levels
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea (23–33%), abdominal pain (19%), nausea (9–13%), sinus bradycardia (7%), hyperglycemia (7%), decreased appetite (10%), palpitations (7%). Serious: cholelithiasis and complications (6% — including cholecystitis, biliary colic, obstructive pancreatitis requiring cholecystectomy), hyperglycemia/diabetes, cardiac conduction abnormalities (PR prolongation, sinus arrest, AV block), hypothyroidism, steatorrhea/fat malabsorption.
medications.watchFor: First-in-class oral SSTR2 agonist — eliminates need for injectable SRLs. Must be taken on empty stomach for adequate absorption. In PATHFNDR-1 trial, 83.3% of patients switching from injectable SRLs maintained IGF-1 control. In PATHFNDR-2, 55.6% of uncontrolled patients achieved IGF-1 normalization vs. 5.3% placebo. Monitor for gallstones (class effect of somatostatin analogs). Adjust antidiabetic medications as needed. Caution with drugs that cause bradycardia (beta-blockers). Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.drugClass: PEGylated recombinant phenylalanine ammonia lyase (enzyme substitution therapy)
medications.mechanism: PEGylated form of the enzyme phenylalanine ammonia lyase (PAL) derived from Anabaena variabilis, which converts phenylalanine to trans-cinnamic acid and ammonia, thereby reducing blood phenylalanine levels independently of the deficient phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) enzyme.
medications.indications: FDA: reduction of blood phenylalanine concentrations in patients ≥12 years with phenylketonuria (PKU) who have uncontrolled blood Phe >600 µmol/L on existing management.
medications.dosing:
- Induction: 2.5 mg SC once weekly × 4 weeks
- Titration: 2.5 mg SC twice weekly × 1 week, then 10 mg SC once weekly × 4 weeks, then increase to 20 mg SC once daily
- Maintenance: 20 mg SC once daily for ≥24 weeks; may increase to 40 mg or 60 mg once daily based on blood Phe response
Route: SC (abdomen, thigh, or upper arm; rotate injection sites)
medications.monitoring: Blood phenylalanine levels (periodically — target 360 µmol/L; avoid sustained 30 µmol/L), signs of anaphylaxis (observe ≥60 min after first dose and after dose increases), complement C3/C4, CRP, nutritional status (protein intake adequacy)
medications.sideEffects: Common: injection site reactions (88%), arthralgia (74%), hypersensitivity reactions (54%), headache (36%), generalized skin reactions (21–41%), nausea (18–31%), alopecia (19%), fatigue, pruritus. Serious: anaphylaxis (10% of patients — BOXED WARNING), injection site infections (abscess, cellulitis, necrosis), hypophenylalaninemia (65% had Phe 30 µmol/L). BOXED WARNING: anaphylaxis — may occur at any time during treatment.
medications.watchFor: Available only through REMS program (Palynziq REMS). Patients must carry injectable epinephrine at all times. Observe patients for ≥60 minutes after first dose. Consider risks/benefits of readministration after anaphylaxis. Hypophenylalaninemia can cause neurological adverse effects — monitor Phe and adjust dietary protein intake. Arthralgia and hypersensitivity reactions are immune-mediated and typically decrease over time. Complement C3 decreases are common but clinical significance is unclear. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Stimulates muscarinic receptors (primarily M3) on exocrine glands (salivary, lacrimal, sweat) and smooth muscle, increasing secretions and causing miosis and ciliary muscle contraction in the eye.
medications.indications: FDA (oral): dry mouth from Sjögren's syndrome; radiation-induced xerostomia. FDA (ophthalmic): open-angle glaucoma, ocular hypertension, acute angle-closure glaucoma, induction of miosis, presbyopia (Vuity 1.25%). Off-label: drug-induced dry mouth.
medications.dosing:
- Xerostomia (oral): 5 mg PO TID; may increase to 10 mg TID; max 30 mg/day
- Glaucoma (ophthalmic): 1 drop of 1–4% solution up to QID
- Acute angle-closure glaucoma: 1 drop of 1–2% up to 3 times over 30 minutes
- Presbyopia (Vuity): 1 drop of 1.25% in each eye once daily
Route: PO, ophthalmic
medications.monitoring: Heart rate, blood pressure, visual acuity, intraocular pressure (ophthalmic), sweating/hydration status
medications.sideEffects: Common (oral): sweating (29%), nausea (6%), rhinitis (5–14%), chills, flushing, urinary frequency, dizziness, headache. Common (ophthalmic): brow ache, accommodative spasm, blurred vision, miosis, eye irritation. Serious: bradycardia, bronchospasm, retinal detachment (ophthalmic — rare), cholinergic crisis (overdose).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma, narrow-angle glaucoma (systemic), acute iritis (ophthalmic). Use caution in cardiovascular disease, COPD, cholelithiasis, nephrolithiasis. Onset of oral effect: 20–60 minutes; duration: 3–5 hours. Compared to cevimeline, pilocarpine is less M3-selective and has shorter duration. Antidote for overdose: atropine. Ophthalmic use: caution in patients with retinal disease (risk of retinal detachment). Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Selectively binds and blocks presynaptic histamine H3 autoreceptors, enhancing histamine synthesis and release in the brain. Also increases activity of other wake-promoting neurotransmitters (acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine) via H3 heteroreceptors on non-histaminergic neurons, without activating accumbal dopaminergic neurons (low abuse potential).
medications.indications: FDA: excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) in adults with narcolepsy; cataplexy in adults with narcolepsy; EDS in pediatric patients ≥6 years with narcolepsy.
medications.dosing:
- Adults: Start 8.9 mg PO once daily upon waking; increase at weekly intervals to 17.8 mg, then 35.6 mg once daily; max 35.6 mg/day
- Pediatric (≥6 years, ≥40 kg): same as adult dosing
- Pediatric (40 kg): Start 8.9 mg; max 17.8 mg/day
- Hepatic impairment (moderate, Child-Pugh B): max 17.8 mg/day; severe (Child-Pugh C): not recommended
- Renal impairment (eGFR 15–89): max 17.8 mg/day; ESRD: not recommended
- CYP2D6 poor metabolizers: max 17.8 mg/day
Route: PO (take upon waking; avoid late-day dosing)
medications.monitoring: Signs of QTc prolongation (avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs), mood/psychiatric symptoms, hepatic function
medications.sideEffects: Common: insomnia (6–12%), headache (12–18%), nausea (5–6%), anxiety (7%), upper abdominal pain. Serious: QTc prolongation (dose-dependent), insomnia (may be severe).
medications.watchFor: Not a controlled substance (unique among narcolepsy treatments — no abuse potential). Avoid with strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine, quinidine) — max 17.8 mg/day. Strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) may reduce efficacy. Avoid concomitant H1 antihistamines (may reduce efficacy). May reduce efficacy of hormonal contraceptives — use alternative contraception. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Selectively stimulates dopamine D2 and D3 receptors (with preferential affinity for D3) in the striatum and limbic system, compensating for dopamine deficiency in Parkinson's disease and modulating sensorimotor circuits in restless legs syndrome.
medications.indications: FDA: Parkinson's disease (early and advanced), moderate-to-severe primary restless legs syndrome (RLS). Off-label: treatment-resistant depression (augmentation).
medications.dosing:
- Parkinson's disease (IR): Start 0.125 mg PO TID; increase every 5–7 days; max 4.5 mg/day in 3 divided doses
- Parkinson's disease (ER): Start 0.375 mg PO once daily; increase every 5–7 days; max 4.5 mg/day
- RLS: Start 0.125 mg PO once daily 2–3 hours before bedtime; increase every 4–7 days; max 0.5 mg/day
- Renal impairment: CrCl 30–50: start 0.125 mg BID (PD) or 0.125 mg daily (RLS); CrCl 15–29: start 0.125 mg daily (PD); CrCl 15: not studied
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure (orthostatic hypotension), daytime somnolence, impulse control behaviors, mental status (hallucinations), skin examination (melanoma risk — theoretical)
medications.sideEffects: Common (early PD): nausea (28%), dizziness (25%), somnolence (22%), insomnia (17%), constipation (14%), hallucinations (9%). Common (RLS): nausea, somnolence, fatigue, headache. Serious: impulse control disorders (pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive spending, binge eating — 3–4%), sleep attacks (falling asleep during activities), hallucinations/psychosis, orthostatic hypotension, withdrawal-emergent hyperpyrexia (NMS-like), augmentation (RLS — worsening of symptoms with chronic use).
medications.watchFor: Screen for impulse control disorders at every visit. Avoid abrupt discontinuation — taper gradually (risk of withdrawal syndrome: apathy, anxiety, depression, pain, sweating). Augmentation in RLS is a significant long-term concern — consider dose reduction or switching. Additive sedation with alcohol, CNS depressants. Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) may reduce efficacy. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Blocks fast sodium channels (phase 0), prolonging action potential duration and effective refractory period. Also has anticholinergic activity (increases sinus rate), negative inotropic effects, and peripheral alpha-adrenergic antagonism (vasodilation).
medications.indications: FDA: conversion of atrial fibrillation/flutter, maintenance of sinus rhythm after cardioversion, suppression of life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias, life-threatening Plasmodium falciparum malaria (quinidine gluconate IV). Off-label: Brugada syndrome (suppression of VF storms), short QT syndrome.
medications.dosing:
- AF/flutter conversion (sulfate): 400 mg PO every 6 hours; adjust based on response
- Maintenance (sulfate): 200 mg PO every 6 hours
- AF/flutter conversion (gluconate ER): 324–648 mg PO every 8 hours
- Ventricular arrhythmias: individualized dosing
- Severe malaria (gluconate IV): 6.25 mg base/kg IV over 1–2 hours, then 0.0125 mg base/kg/min continuous infusion
Route: PO, IV (gluconate only)
medications.monitoring: ECG (QTc — discontinue if QTc >500 ms or increases >25%), serum drug levels (therapeutic 2–5 mcg/mL), electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺), CBC, LFTs, blood pressure
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea (35%), nausea, GI distress (22%), lightheadedness (15%), headache (7%), cinchonism (tinnitus, hearing loss, blurred vision, vertigo, confusion). Serious: torsades de pointes (QT prolongation), proarrhythmia, thrombocytopenia (immune-mediated), hepatotoxicity, hemolytic anemia, lupus-like syndrome, syncope. BOXED WARNING: increased mortality in meta-analyses of AF/flutter treatment; reserve for life-threatening arrhythmias.
medications.watchFor: Increases digoxin levels by ~2× — reduce digoxin dose by 50% when starting quinidine. Potent CYP2D6 inhibitor — increases levels of codeine (blocks conversion to morphine), tamoxifen, metoprolol. Correct hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia before use (increases torsades risk). Contraindicated in myasthenia gravis (anticholinergic effects), complete heart block, thrombocytopenic purpura from prior quinidine. Combined with dextromethorphan as Nuedexta for pseudobulbar affect. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Reduces presynaptic glutamate release by inhibiting voltage-gated sodium channels on glutamatergic neurons, thereby limiting excitotoxicity — the overactivation of glutamate receptors on motor neurons that may accelerate neuronal death in ALS.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) to extend survival or time to tracheostomy.
medications.dosing:
- 50 mg PO twice daily, taken at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after a meal
- Available as tablets, oral suspension (Tiglutik), and oral film (Exservan — dissolves on tongue, suitable for patients with dysphagia)
Route: PO (tablets, oral suspension, oral film)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (ALT) before and during treatment (monthly × 3 months, then every 3 months × first year, then periodically), CBC (neutrophils), signs of hepatotoxicity, interstitial lung disease
medications.sideEffects: Common: nausea (16%), asthenia/malaise (19%), dizziness (4%), diarrhea (5%), abdominal pain, elevated ALT (10–19%). Serious: hepatotoxicity (ALT >5× ULN in ~2%; rare cases of fatal hepatitis), neutropenia, interstitial lung disease (rare), anaphylaxis/angioedema (rare).
medications.watchFor: Prolongs survival by approximately 2–3 months in clinical trials (NNT = 11 to delay 1 death at 12 months); observational data suggest potentially greater benefit (6–21 months). Discontinue if ALT >5× ULN. Avoid in patients with baseline hepatic impairment. CYP1A2 substrate — smoking induces CYP1A2 (may reduce levels); ciprofloxacin, fluvoxamine inhibit CYP1A2 (may increase levels). Take on empty stomach (food reduces bioavailability). Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits Janus kinase 3 (JAK3) and the TEC family of kinases by blocking the ATP binding site. This inhibits cytokine-induced STAT phosphorylation mediated by JAK3-dependent receptors and signaling of immune receptors dependent on TEC kinase family members, reducing the autoimmune attack on hair follicles.
medications.indications: FDA: severe alopecia areata in adults and adolescents ≥12 years.
medications.dosing:
- 50 mg PO once daily with or without food
- Optional 200 mg loading dose daily × 4 weeks, then 50 mg daily (may accelerate response)
Route: PO (swallow capsules whole)
medications.monitoring: CBC (lymphocytes — dose-dependent decrease in CD4, CD8, NK cells), LFTs, lipid panel, TB screening before initiation, viral hepatitis screening, signs of infection, immunizations (update before starting)
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache (21%), diarrhea, nasopharyngitis, URI, acne, urticaria, pyrexia, SARS-CoV-2 infection. Serious: herpes zoster (reported in ~1–2%), serious infections (rare), malignancies (rare — 3 cases in long-term data), MACE (rare — 3 cases in long-term data).
medications.watchFor: Unlike JAK1/JAK2 inhibitors (baricitinib, tofacitinib), ritlecitinib does NOT carry the JAK class boxed warning for MACE, malignancy, thrombosis, or mortality — its selective JAK3/TEC mechanism avoids JAK1/JAK2-related effects (no significant cholesterol elevation, less thrombocytopenia/anemia). Response may take 6–12 months; up to 11% of patients require >1 year for response. At 3 years, 65% of patients achieved SALT ≤20 (observed data). Not recommended with other JAK inhibitors or potent immunosuppressants. Avoid live vaccines. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.drugClass: Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) activator / synthetic BH4 cofactor
medications.mechanism: Synthetic form of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), the naturally occurring cofactor for phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH). In BH4-responsive PKU patients, sapropterin enhances residual PAH enzyme activity, increasing conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine and lowering blood Phe levels.
medications.indications: FDA: reduction of blood phenylalanine (Phe) levels in patients ≥1 month with hyperphenylalaninemia (HPA) due to BH4-responsive phenylketonuria (PKU), in conjunction with a Phe-restricted diet.
medications.dosing:
- Pediatric 1 month to 6 years: 10 mg/kg PO once daily with a meal
- Patients ≥7 years: Start 10 mg/kg PO once daily; if blood Phe does not decrease at 1 week, may increase to 20 mg/kg once daily
- Evaluation period: Check blood Phe at 1 week and periodically during first month; discontinue if no biochemical response (no Phe decrease) after 1 month at 20 mg/kg/day
- Once responsiveness established, adjust dose within 5–20 mg/kg/day based on blood Phe levels
Route: PO (dissolve tablets in water or apple juice; take with a meal)
medications.monitoring: Blood phenylalanine levels (weekly during first month, then periodically), nutritional status (dietary protein/Phe intake), growth parameters in pediatric patients
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache (15%), rhinorrhea (11%), pharyngolaryngeal pain (10%), diarrhea (8%), vomiting (8%), cough (7%), nasal congestion (7%). Serious: upper GI mucosal inflammation (esophageal stricture, ulcers, bleeding — postmarketing), hypophenylalaninemia (especially in children 7 years at 20 mg/kg/day), hyperactivity.
medications.watchFor: Only ~25–50% of PKU patients are BH4-responsive — a BH4 loading test or genotyping is required to identify responders before committing to long-term therapy. Must be used in conjunction with a Phe-restricted diet — do not modify dietary protein/Phe during the evaluation period. Hypophenylalaninemia (blood Phe too low) can cause catabolism and adverse developmental outcomes — monitor and adjust diet accordingly. Caution with levodopa (may cause seizures, overstimulation in patients with neurological disorders). Pregnancy: can be used during pregnancy in known BH4 responders if dietary treatment alone is insufficient.

medications.mechanism: Selectively inhibits PDE5, preventing degradation of cGMP in smooth muscle cells of the corpus cavernosum, pulmonary vasculature, and prostate/bladder. This enhances nitric oxide-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, resulting in penile erection (with sexual stimulation), pulmonary vasodilation, and lower urinary tract smooth muscle relaxation.
medications.indications: FDA: erectile dysfunction (Cialis), signs and symptoms of BPH (Cialis 5 mg), ED with concomitant BPH (Cialis 5 mg), pulmonary arterial hypertension (Adcirca 40 mg). Off-label: Raynaud's phenomenon, altitude sickness prophylaxis.
medications.dosing:
- ED (as needed): Start 10 mg PO prior to sexual activity; adjust to 5 mg or 20 mg; max once daily
- ED (once daily): 2.5–5 mg PO once daily
- BPH or ED + BPH: 5 mg PO once daily at approximately the same time
- PAH (Adcirca): 40 mg PO once daily
- Renal impairment (CrCl 30–50): start 5 mg as needed, max 10 mg q48h; CrCl 30 or HD: max 5 mg as needed
- Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B): max 10 mg as needed; severe (Child-Pugh C): not recommended
- With strong CYP3A4 inhibitors: max 10 mg q72h (as needed) or 2.5 mg daily
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure, signs of visual/hearing changes, priapism, cardiovascular status
medications.sideEffects: Common: headache (15%), dyspepsia (7–13%), back pain (6–12%), myalgia (4–5%), nasopharyngitis, flushing, nasal congestion. Serious: priapism, NAION (non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy — rare), sudden hearing loss, hypotension (with nitrates), SJS/TEN (rare postmarketing).
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated with organic nitrates in any form (risk of severe hypotension) — wait ≥48 hours after tadalafil before administering nitrates in emergencies. Contraindicated with GC stimulators (riociguat). Caution with alpha-blockers (additive hypotension — stabilize on alpha-blocker first). Long half-life (~17.5 hours) distinguishes tadalafil from sildenafil/vardenafil. CYP3A4 substrate. Screen for cardiovascular risk before prescribing for ED. Pregnancy: not indicated in women (Adcirca for PAH: category B).

medications.mechanism: Binds selectively to the thyroxine-binding sites of the TTR tetramer with high affinity, kinetically stabilizing the native tetrameric structure and preventing dissociation into monomers that misfold and aggregate into amyloid fibrils.
medications.indications: FDA: cardiomyopathy of wild-type or hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (ATTR-CM) in adults, to reduce cardiovascular mortality and cardiovascular-related hospitalization.
medications.dosing:
- Tafamidis 61 mg (Vyndamax) PO once daily — this is the current formulation (bioequivalent to tafamidis meglumine 80 mg [4 × 20 mg Vyndaqel capsules])
- Swallow whole; do not crush or cut
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Echocardiography (LVEF, wall thickness), NT-proBNP or BNP, 6-minute walk test, NYHA functional class, signs of heart failure progression, renal function
medications.sideEffects: Tafamidis was well tolerated in ATTR-ACT with adverse event rates similar to placebo. Reported: diarrhea, constipation, UTI, abdominal pain — all mild to moderate and less common than placebo. Permanent discontinuation due to adverse events was lower with tafamidis (0.8%) than placebo (2.3%).
medications.watchFor: Greatest benefit observed in earlier-stage disease (NYHA class I–II); patients with NYHA class III had higher cardiovascular hospitalizations initially but long-term (58.5-month) data showed survival advantage even in class III. Benefit not observed in NYHA class IV, severe aortic stenosis, or eGFR 25. Caution with rosuvastatin >20 mg or atorvastatin >40 mg (risk of rhabdomyolysis due to BCRP inhibition). In ATTR-ACT, NNT = 7.5 to prevent 1 death over 30 months. Acoramidis (Attruby) is a newer TTR stabilizer FDA-approved in 2024. Pregnancy: limited data.

medications.mechanism: Selectively blocks alpha-1A adrenergic receptors in the prostate, bladder neck, and prostatic urethra, relaxing smooth muscle and improving urine flow without significant effects on vascular alpha-1B receptors (less orthostatic hypotension than non-selective alpha-blockers).
medications.indications: FDA: signs and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Off-label: ureteral stone expulsion (medical expulsive therapy), female voiding dysfunction.
medications.dosing:
- 0.4 mg PO once daily, approximately 30 minutes after the same meal each day
- May increase to 0.8 mg once daily if inadequate response after 2–4 weeks
- If therapy interrupted for several days, restart at 0.4 mg
Route: PO (take 30 minutes after a meal)
medications.monitoring: Blood pressure (orthostatic hypotension), urinary symptoms (IPSS/AUA-SS), post-void residual, PSA (screen for prostate cancer)
medications.sideEffects: Common: dizziness (15–17%), rhinitis (13–18%), abnormal ejaculation (8–18%), headache, asthenia, somnolence. Serious: syncope (0.2%), priapism (rare), intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (IFIS — important for cataract/glaucoma surgery), orthostatic hypotension.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole). Caution with moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors, strong/moderate CYP2D6 inhibitors, and CYP2D6 poor metabolizers. Do not combine with other alpha-blockers. Caution with PDE5 inhibitors (additive hypotension). IFIS: inform ophthalmologist before cataract surgery — IFIS can occur even after tamsulosin discontinuation. Not indicated for hypertension. No dose adjustment for renal/hepatic impairment. Pregnancy: not indicated in women.

medications.mechanism: Recombinant analogue of human GLP-2 that binds GLP-2 receptors on intestinal epithelial cells, promoting intestinal mucosal growth (villus height, crypt depth), increasing intestinal absorptive surface area, and enhancing nutrient and fluid absorption.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of adults and pediatric patients ≥1 year with short bowel syndrome (SBS) who are dependent on parenteral support.
medications.dosing:
- Adults and pediatric patients ≥1 year: 0.05 mg/kg SC once daily
- Moderate to severe renal impairment (CrCl 50): 0.025 mg/kg SC once daily
Route: SC (abdomen, thigh, or upper arm; rotate injection sites)
medications.monitoring: Colonoscopy (with removal of polyps) before starting and at end of year 1, then every 5 years; bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase (every 6 months — gallbladder/biliary disease); lipase and amylase (every 6 months — pancreatic disease); parenteral support volume and composition (adjust as absorption improves); fluid balance; stoma output
medications.sideEffects: Common: abdominal pain (30%), nausea (23%), URI (21%), abdominal distension (20%), injection site reactions (13%), vomiting (12%), fluid overload (12%), hypersensitivity (10%), flatulence (9%), decreased appetite (7%). Serious: acceleration of neoplastic growth (theoretical — GLP-2 is trophic), intestinal obstruction, cholecystitis/cholelithiasis, pancreatitis, fluid overload, GI stoma complications (42% in stoma patients vs. 14% placebo).
medications.watchFor: Screen for colorectal polyps before initiation — remove any polyps. Contraindicated in active GI malignancy. Reduce parenteral support cautiously as intestinal absorption improves (risk of dehydration if reduced too quickly, fluid overload if not reduced). In clinical trials, 32% of pediatric patients were weaned completely off PN at 48 weeks. Citrulline levels may serve as a biomarker of intestinal adaptation. Pregnancy: limited data.

medications.mechanism: Synthetic vasopressin analogue that is cleaved in vivo to release lysine-vasopressin, which acts on V1 receptors in splanchnic vascular smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction. This reduces portal blood inflow, decreases portal hypertension, and redistributes blood volume from the splanchnic to the systemic circulation, improving renal perfusion in hepatorenal syndrome.
medications.indications: FDA: improvement of kidney function in adults with hepatorenal syndrome with rapid reduction in kidney function (HRS-AKI).
medications.dosing:
- Days 1–3: 0.85 mg IV every 6 hours
- Day 4: If SCr has decreased ≥30% from baseline, continue 0.85 mg q6h; if SCr decreased 30%, increase to 1.7 mg q6h
- Maximum duration: 14 days, or until two consecutive SCr values ≤1.5 mg/dL at least 2 hours apart
- Administer with albumin (1 g/kg day 1, then 40–50 g/day)
Route: IV (bolus; can also be given as continuous infusion off-label at 2 mg/day, titrated up to 12 mg/day — associated with fewer side effects)
medications.monitoring: Continuous pulse oximetry (SpO₂), serum creatinine (daily), signs of ischemia (cardiac, digital, intestinal), fluid status (avoid volume overload), ECG (bradycardia), blood pressure
medications.sideEffects: Common: abdominal pain (19.5%), nausea (16%), respiratory failure (15.5%), diarrhea (13%), dyspnea (12.5%), fluid overload (8.5%), bradycardia (5%). Serious: respiratory failure (BOXED WARNING — 11% respiratory death in terlipressin group vs. 2% placebo in CONFIRM trial), ischemic events (cardiac, digital, intestinal, skin), sepsis.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: serious or fatal respiratory failure — do not initiate if SpO₂ 90%. Contraindicated in patients with ongoing coronary, peripheral, or mesenteric ischemia. Use with caution in ACLF grade 3 (≥3 organ failures) — highest risk of respiratory failure. Benefits may not outweigh risks if SCr >5 mg/dL or MELD ≥35. Predictors of response: baseline bilirubin 10 mg/dL, baseline SCr 5 mg/dL, lower ACLF stage. Does not improve survival — serves as bridge to liver transplant. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits VMAT2, reducing packaging of dopamine and other monoamines into synaptic vesicles, thereby decreasing dopaminergic transmission and suppressing involuntary choreiform movements.
medications.indications: FDA: chorea associated with Huntington's disease.
medications.dosing:
- Start 12.5 mg PO once daily; increase to 12.5 mg BID after 1 week
- Titrate by 12.5 mg/day at weekly intervals
- CYP2D6 genotyping required before exceeding 50 mg/day: EMs/IMs max 100 mg/day (max single dose 37.5 mg); PMs max 50 mg/day (max single dose 25 mg)
Route: PO (with or without food)
medications.monitoring: Depression/suicidality screening (at every visit), CYP2D6 genotype (before exceeding 50 mg/day), swallowing function, signs of parkinsonism, akathisia
medications.sideEffects: Common: sedation/somnolence (31%), fatigue (22%), insomnia (22%), depression (19%), akathisia (19%), anxiety (15%), nausea (13%), parkinsonism. Serious: depression/suicidality (BOXED WARNING), neuroleptic malignant syndrome, dysphagia, QTc prolongation.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: increased risk of depression and suicidality in patients with Huntington's disease — contraindicated in patients who are actively suicidal or have untreated/inadequately treated depression. Contraindicated with MAOIs (within 14 days), with reserpine (within 20 days), and in hepatic impairment. CYP2D6 PMs have substantially higher metabolite levels (~3-fold α-HTBZ, ~9-fold β-HTBZ). Deutetrabenazine (Austedo) is preferred by many clinicians due to better tolerability, less frequent dosing, and lower peak-trough fluctuation. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Exact mechanism incompletely understood; inhibits TNF-α production, modulates T-cell co-stimulation, has anti-angiogenic properties (inhibits bFGF and VEGF-mediated angiogenesis), and modulates cytokine production. In ENL, suppresses TNF-α-driven inflammation; in myeloma, anti-angiogenic and immunomodulatory effects contribute to anti-tumor activity.
medications.indications: FDA: newly diagnosed multiple myeloma (in combination with dexamethasone), acute treatment and maintenance therapy for cutaneous manifestations of moderate-to-severe erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL). Off-label: graft-versus-host disease, myelodysplastic syndromes, Waldenström macroglobulinemia.
medications.dosing:
- Multiple myeloma: 200 mg PO once daily with dexamethasone 40 mg on days 1–4, 9–12, 17–20 of each 28-day cycle
- ENL: 100–300 mg PO once daily (up to 400 mg/day for severe cases); taper by 50 mg every 2–4 weeks once controlled
- Take at bedtime, at least 1 hour after evening meal
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: Pregnancy testing (before, during, and after treatment — per REMS), CBC with differential (ongoing — neutropenia risk), signs of VTE, peripheral neuropathy assessment (monthly × 3 months, then periodically), nerve conduction studies (baseline and every 6 months), HIV viral load (if HIV+)
medications.sideEffects: Common (MM): fatigue, constipation, peripheral sensory neuropathy, somnolence, tremor, edema, dizziness, DVT (12%), rash, neutropenia, leukopenia. Common (ENL): somnolence, rash, headache. Serious: teratogenicity (BOXED WARNING), VTE including DVT and PE (BOXED WARNING — especially in MM), peripheral neuropathy (may be irreversible), neutropenia, bradycardia, SJS/TEN, seizures, tumor lysis syndrome.
medications.watchFor: BOXED WARNING: (1) Severe birth defects or embryo-fetal death — even a single dose can cause phocomelia. Available only through THALOMID REMS program. (2) VTE — thromboprophylaxis recommended in MM patients (aspirin, LMWH, or warfarin based on risk). Do not initiate with ANC 750/mm³. Discontinue immediately if neuropathy develops. Do not add pembrolizumab to thalidomide analogue + dexamethasone (increased mortality). Pregnancy: absolutely contraindicated (category X). Males must use condoms.

medications.mechanism: Intrathecally administered antisense oligonucleotide that binds SOD1 mRNA, promoting its degradation via RNase H, thereby reducing synthesis of the toxic mutant SOD1 protein in motor neurons and the CNS.
medications.indications: FDA: treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) in adults who have a mutation in the superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) gene.
medications.dosing:
- Loading: 100 mg (15 mL) intrathecal injection × 3 doses at 14-day intervals
- Maintenance: 100 mg intrathecal injection every 28 days thereafter
Route: Intrathecal (lumbar puncture — administered by healthcare professionals experienced in LP)
medications.monitoring: CSF analysis (WBC, protein — pleocytosis common), neurofilament light chain (plasma — biomarker of neurodegeneration), SOD1 protein levels (CSF), neurological exam (for myelitis, radiculitis, papilledema), signs of intracranial hypertension
medications.sideEffects: Common: pain (42% — includes back pain, pain in extremity), fatigue (17%), arthralgia (14%), CSF WBC increased (14%), myalgia (14%), CSF protein increased (8%). Serious: myelitis and radiculitis (may present as new weakness, sensory changes, bowel/bladder dysfunction), papilledema and elevated intracranial pressure, aseptic meningitis — all occurred in ~7% of tofersen recipients; all were reversible.
medications.watchFor: Approved via accelerated approval based on reduction in plasma neurofilament light chains (biomarker); long-term data (VALOR + OLE, up to 3.5 years) demonstrate earlier initiation of tofersen is associated with less functional decline and prolonged survival vs. delayed start. CSF pleocytosis is nearly universal (~58% with WBC >10) and is a pharmacodynamic effect, not predictive of serious neurologic events. Only ~2% of ALS cases are SOD1-related — genetic testing required. Serious neurologic adverse events were reversible and few led to discontinuation. Pregnancy: insufficient data.

medications.mechanism: Polyamine chelator that binds copper ions, forming a stable complex excreted in the urine, thereby reducing systemic copper burden. May also inhibit intestinal copper absorption.
medications.indications: FDA (Syprine): treatment of Wilson's disease in patients intolerant of penicillamine. FDA (Cuvrior): treatment of adult patients with stable Wilson's disease who are de-coppered and tolerant to penicillamine.
medications.dosing:
- Initial treatment (dihydrochloride): 15–20 mg/kg/day PO in 2–3 divided doses; max 2000 mg/day; ramp up over 2–3 weeks
- Maintenance: 10–15 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses
- Pediatric: 20 mg/kg/day rounded to nearest 250 mg in 2–3 divided doses
- Cuvrior (tetrahydrochloride): 300–3000 mg/day PO in 2 divided doses (dose not directly interchangeable with dihydrochloride — bioequivalence not established)
- Take on empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after meals) with ample fluid
Route: PO
medications.monitoring: 24-hour urinary copper excretion (target 150–500 µg/24h on treatment; 100 µg suggests overtreatment), serum non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper (NCC), LFTs, CBC (pancytopenia — rare), serum iron and zinc levels, neurological status
medications.sideEffects: Common: gastritis, nausea, esophageal irritation. Serious: sideroblastic anemia, pancytopenia (rare), colitis, lupus-like reaction (mostly in patients previously on penicillamine), paradoxical neurological worsening at treatment initiation (10–15%).
medications.watchFor: Fewer side effects than D-penicillamine — preferred in penicillamine-intolerant patients. Paradoxical neurological worsening occurs in 10–15% at treatment initiation (similar to penicillamine, though possibly less common). Chelates iron — do not coadminister with iron supplements (toxic complex). Dihydrochloride formulation requires refrigeration. Reduce dose by 25–50% during pregnancy and for surgery (impairs wound healing). Signs of overtreatment: urinary copper 100 µg/24h, sideroblastic anemia, leukopenia, iron accumulation.

medications.mechanism: At low concentrations (2–10%), acts as a humectant by binding water in the stratum corneum, enhancing skin hydration. At higher concentrations (20–40%), disrupts hydrogen bonds between keratin chains, promoting desquamation and keratolysis. Also used as an osmotic diuretic (IV, rarely) and to reduce intracranial/intraocular pressure (historical).
medications.indications: FDA (topical): hyperkeratotic conditions (xerosis, ichthyosis, keratosis pilaris, psoriasis, eczema, calluses, corns, damaged/ingrown nails). Off-label: SIADH (oral urea for hyponatremia), elevated intracranial pressure (IV — largely historical).
medications.dosing:
- Topical (dermatologic): Apply to affected area 1–3 times daily; concentrations range from 2% (moisturizer) to 40–50% (keratolytic for nails/calluses)
- SIADH (oral, off-label): 15–60 g/day PO in divided doses (dissolved in water/juice — unpleasant taste)
Route: Topical (cream, lotion, ointment, gel, foam, solution), PO (off-label for SIADH)
medications.monitoring: Topical: clinical response, skin irritation. Oral (SIADH): serum sodium, serum osmolality, renal function.
medications.sideEffects: Topical: stinging/burning on application (especially on broken skin or at higher concentrations), local irritation, erythema. Oral: nausea, vomiting (due to taste), GI upset, headache. IV (historical): thrombophlebitis, hemolysis, electrolyte disturbances.
medications.watchFor: Topical urea is generally well tolerated even long-term with no systemic absorption at standard dermatologic concentrations. Avoid application to broken, inflamed, or infected skin (stinging). Effects are concentration-dependent: ≤10% = hydration; ~20% = hydration + mild keratolysis; ≥30% = significant keratolysis. For SIADH, oral urea is effective but limited by poor palatability — can be mixed with flavored syrup. No sensitization reported despite widespread use.

medications.mechanism: Naturally occurring hydrophilic bile acid that displaces hepatotoxic hydrophobic bile acids from the bile acid pool, stimulates hepatobiliary secretion (choleresis), protects cholangiocytes and hepatocytes from bile acid-induced apoptosis, and has anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties.
medications.indications: FDA: primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), dissolution of radiolucent noncalcified gallbladder stones ≤20 mm in patients who refuse or are poor candidates for surgery, prevention of gallstone formation in obese patients undergoing rapid weight loss. Off-label: intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP — first-line per SMFM/AGA/AASLD), primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC — improves biochemistries but no proven survival benefit), cystic fibrosis-associated liver disease, biliary atresia (post-Kasai).
medications.dosing:
- PBC: 13–15 mg/kg/day PO in 2–4 divided doses with food; lifelong therapy
- Gallstone dissolution: 8–10 mg/kg/day PO in 2–3 divided doses
- Gallstone prevention (rapid weight loss): 300 mg PO BID
- ICP (off-label): 10–15 mg/kg/day PO in 2–3 divided doses; may titrate to 21 mg/kg/day if pruritus unrelieved
Route: PO (with food for PBC; with or without food for gallstone indications)
medications.monitoring: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin) at baseline and periodically; for PBC: assess biochemical response at 12 months (ALP, bilirubin — multiple validated criteria); for gallstone dissolution: gallbladder ultrasound every 6 months
medications.sideEffects: Common: diarrhea/loose stools (2–9%), nausea, dyspepsia, headache, mild weight gain (1–2 kg), hair thinning. Postmarketing: abdominal pain, constipation, vomiting, pruritus, rash, elevated transaminases, jaundice (aggravation of pre-existing), myalgia, dizziness, alopecia, peripheral edema.
medications.watchFor: Generally very well tolerated with an excellent long-term safety profile. Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine, colestipol) and aluminum-based antacids reduce UDCA absorption — administer UDCA ≥1 hour before or 4 hours after these agents. Estrogens, oral contraceptives, and clofibrate may counteract gallstone dissolution efficacy by increasing hepatic cholesterol secretion. No dose adjustment needed for renal or hepatic impairment. Approximately 20–40% of PBC patients have an inadequate biochemical response to UDCA after 1 year — these patients may benefit from second-line therapy (elafibranor, seladelpar). In PBC, 10-year transplant-free survival is ~80% with UDCA vs. ~61% without (NNT = 11 to prevent 1 transplant/death at 5 years). Pregnancy: considered safe; recommended as first-line for ICP by multiple guidelines.

medications.brandNames: Leuprolide [Lupron], Goserelin [Zoladex], Triptorelin [Trelstar], Nafarelin [Synarel], Histrelin [Supprelin LA, Vantas]
medications.mechanism: Synthetic GnRH analogues that initially stimulate pituitary gonadotropin release (LH/FSH "flare"), followed by sustained receptor desensitization and downregulation, resulting in suppression of gonadotropin secretion and subsequent reduction of gonadal sex steroids (testosterone/estradiol) to castrate/postmenopausal levels within 2–4 weeks.
medications.indications: FDA: advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids (preoperative shrinkage/bridge therapy), central precocious puberty (CPP), breast cancer (premenopausal, in combination with other agents), endometrial thinning before ablation. Off-label: gender-affirming hormone therapy (puberty suppression), ovarian suppression during chemotherapy (fertility preservation), in vitro fertilization protocols, premenstrual syndrome (severe/refractory).
medications.dosing:
- Leuprolide (prostate cancer): 7.5 mg IM monthly, 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, 30 mg IM every 4 months, or 45 mg IM every 6 months
- Leuprolide (endometriosis): 3.75 mg IM monthly or 11.25 mg IM every 3 months × 6 months max
- Leuprolide (CPP): 7.5–15 mg IM monthly (weight-based) or 11.25–30 mg IM every 3 months
- Goserelin: 3.6 mg SC implant every 28 days or 10.8 mg SC every 12 weeks
- Histrelin (CPP): 50 mg SC implant every 12 months
- Nafarelin (endometriosis): 200 mcg intranasal BID × 6 months
Route: IM, SC (depot injection or implant), intranasal (nafarelin)
medications.monitoring: Testosterone or estradiol levels (confirm castrate levels), LH/FSH, PSA (prostate cancer), bone mineral density (DXA — especially if >6 months of therapy), fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel, cardiovascular risk assessment, signs of tumor flare (first 1–2 weeks).
medications.sideEffects: Common: hot flashes (57–80%), injection site reactions, headache, decreased libido, erectile dysfunction (men), vaginal dryness (women), mood changes, fatigue, arthralgia, bone mineral density loss (2–3%/year). Serious: tumor flare (first 1–2 weeks — spinal cord compression, ureteral obstruction in prostate cancer; vaginal bleeding in endometriosis), cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, sudden cardiac death — small increased risk in men), QT prolongation, diabetes/hyperglycemia, osteoporotic fractures (long-term use), anaphylaxis, seizures (rare — leuprolide).
medications.watchFor: Tumor flare: in prostate cancer, consider antiandrogen (bicalutamide) cover for first 2–4 weeks to prevent flare; GnRH antagonists (degarelix, relugolix) avoid flare entirely. Bone loss: limit use to 6 months without add-back therapy; norethindrone acetate 5 mg/day is FDA-approved add-back for endometriosis (extends safe use to 12 months). Monitor glucose in patients with diabetes. Cardiovascular risk: counsel men about small increased risk of MI/stroke. In women, fertility typically returns within months of discontinuation. Pregnancy: contraindicated (category X).

medications.mechanism: Provides elemental calcium (9.3 mg Ca²⁺ per 100 mg calcium gluconate) to restore normal serum calcium levels; calcium is essential for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, cardiac function, and coagulation.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for acute symptomatic hypocalcemia in pediatric and adult patients. Key off-label uses include hyperkalemia (cardiac membrane stabilization), hydrofluoric acid burns (topical gel or IV), calcium channel blocker overdose, and magnesium sulfate toxicity reversal.
medications.dosing:
- Acute hypocalcemia (adult): 1000–2000 mg IV bolus (max rate 200 mg/min); may repeat q6h or initiate continuous infusion at 5.4–21.5 mg/kg/hr
- Acute hypocalcemia (pediatric >1 mo to <17 yr): 29–60 mg/kg IV bolus (max rate 100 mg/min); may repeat q6h or continuous infusion at 8–13 mg/kg/hr
- Acute hypocalcemia (neonate ≤1 mo): 100–200 mg/kg IV bolus (max rate 100 mg/min); may repeat q6h or continuous infusion at 17–33 mg/kg/hr
- Hyperkalemia (off-label, adult): 1000–3000 mg (10–30 mL of 10% solution) IV over 2–5 min; repeat as needed based on ECG
- Renal impairment: initiate at lowest recommended dose for all age groups; monitor serum calcium q4h
Route: IV (bolus or continuous infusion); dilute in D5W or NS to 10–50 mg/mL for bolus, 5.8–10 mg/mL for infusion
medications.monitoring: Serum calcium q4–6h (intermittent) or q1–4h (continuous infusion); ECG during administration; vitals
medications.sideEffects: Most common — hypotension, flushing, nausea, bradycardia with rapid infusion. Most serious — cardiac arrest (with too-rapid IV push), tissue necrosis/calcinosis cutis from extravasation. No boxed warning.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in hypercalcemia and digoxin toxicity (calcium potentiates digoxin). Do NOT mix with ceftriaxone (fatal precipitates in neonates), phosphate-containing, or bicarbonate-containing solutions. Not for intradermal injection. Contains benzyl alcohol in some formulations — avoid in neonates when possible. 10% calcium gluconate = 100 mg/mL = 9.3 mg elemental Ca²⁺/mL (vs. calcium chloride 10% = 27.2 mg elemental Ca²⁺/mL — not interchangeable). Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, translocates to the nucleus, and modulates gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses; inhibits phospholipase A₂, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis.
medications.indications: FDA-approved (IM) for severe allergic conditions, dermatologic diseases, rheumatic disorders, adrenal insufficiency, MS exacerbations, nephrotic syndrome, sarcoidosis, and others. FDA-approved (intra-articular/soft tissue) for acute gouty arthritis, bursitis, tenosynovitis, epicondylitis, RA, OA synovitis. Off-label: plantar fasciitis, trigger finger, carpal tunnel syndrome, keloids.
medications.dosing:
- Systemic (IM): 40–80 mg deep gluteal injection; range 20–100 mg; may repeat based on response (adrenal suppression lasts 30–40 days after 60–100 mg)
- MS exacerbation: 160 mg/day IM × 1 week, then 64 mg every other day × 1 month
- Intra-articular (large joints): 5–15 mg per injection; up to 40 mg for larger areas
- Intra-articular (small joints): 2.5–5 mg per injection
- Soft tissue (tendon sheath, bursa): 2.5–10 mg per injection
- Total multi-joint dose: up to 80 mg across several joints in one session
- Pediatric (systemic): 0.11–1.6 mg/kg/day in 3–4 divided doses
Route: IM, intra-articular, soft tissue injection. NOT for intradermal, IV, intrathecal, or epidural use.
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (especially diabetics — transient hyperglycemia common); blood pressure; signs of infection at injection site; HPA axis function with repeated systemic doses; bone density with chronic use
medications.sideEffects: Most common — injection site pain/atrophy, skin depigmentation (especially darker skin), transient steroid flare (post-injection pain 24–48h), hyperglycemia. Most serious — HPA axis suppression, tendon rupture (avoid direct tendon injection), joint infection (septic arthritis), osteonecrosis, anaphylaxis (rare). No boxed warning. Contains benzyl alcohol — caution in neonates.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in systemic fungal infections and in patients with hypersensitivity to any component. Potency equivalence: 4 mg triamcinolone = 4 mg methylprednisolone = 5 mg prednisone = 20 mg hydrocortisone. No mineralocorticoid activity. Intra-articular injections generally limited to 3–4 per year per joint. Taper if used systemically for >2 weeks. Avoid live vaccines during systemic use. Pregnancy category C. Formulation is a suspension — shake well before use; do not use if agglomerated.

medications.drugClass: Anthracycline topoisomerase II inhibitor (pegylated liposomal formulation)
medications.mechanism: Doxorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell death; encapsulation in PEGylated (STEALTH) liposomes prolongs circulation time, reduces cardiac uptake, and enhances tumor accumulation via the EPR effect.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for (1) ovarian cancer after failure of platinum-based chemotherapy, (2) AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma after failure of prior systemic chemotherapy, (3) multiple myeloma in combination with bortezomib (≥1 prior therapy, bortezomib-naïve). Key off-label uses include breast cancer, endometrial cancer, and platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer (with carboplatin).
medications.dosing:
- Ovarian cancer: 50 mg/m² IV over 60 min every 28 days (in practice, 40 mg/m² often used to reduce toxicity)
- AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma: 20 mg/m² IV over 60 min every 21 days
- Multiple myeloma: 30 mg/m² IV over 60 min on day 4 of each 21-day cycle (after bortezomib), for up to 8 cycles
- Infusion: start at 1 mg/min; if no reaction, increase to complete over 1 hour. Never give as bolus.
Route: IV infusion only (never bolus, never undiluted)
medications.monitoring: LVEF (echocardiogram or MUGA) at baseline and periodically; CBC with differential before each cycle; LFTs; monitor for hand-foot syndrome and stomatitis at each visit
medications.sideEffects: Most common — hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, ~20% grade 3/4 at 50 mg/m²), stomatitis/mucositis, nausea, fatigue, neutropenia. Most serious — BOXED WARNINGS: (1) cardiomyopathy including acute LV failure (11% risk at cumulative anthracycline dose 450–550 mg/m²); (2) serious/life-threatening/fatal infusion-related reactions (11% in solid tumors).
medications.watchFor: Do NOT substitute for conventional doxorubicin — different dosing, pharmacokinetics, and toxicity profile. Cumulative anthracycline dose limit applies (account for prior doxorubicin/epirubicin exposure). Dose-reduce by 25% for grade 3–4 HFS, stomatitis, or grade 4 hematologic toxicity; do not re-escalate after reduction. No specific renal/hepatic dosing in label, but use caution. Pregnancy category D (embryotoxic). Extravasation risk — administer via secure IV line.

medications.mechanism: Paclitaxel stabilizes microtubules by preventing depolymerization, arresting cells in G₂/M phase and inducing apoptosis; albumin-bound nanoparticle formulation eliminates the need for Cremophor EL solvent, allowing higher intratumoral drug delivery via albumin-mediated transcytosis (gp60/SPARC pathway).
medications.indications: FDA-approved for (1) metastatic breast cancer after failure of combination chemotherapy or relapse within 6 months of adjuvant therapy, (2) first-line NSCLC in combination with carboplatin (patients not candidates for curative surgery/radiation), (3) first-line metastatic adenocarcinoma of the pancreas in combination with gemcitabine.
medications.dosing:
- Metastatic breast cancer: 260 mg/m² IV over 30 min every 21 days
- NSCLC: 100 mg/m² IV over 30 min on days 1, 8, 15 of each 21-day cycle (with carboplatin AUC 6 on day 1)
- Pancreatic adenocarcinoma: 125 mg/m² IV over 30–40 min on days 1, 8, 15 of each 28-day cycle (with gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² immediately after)
- Hepatic impairment (MBC/NSCLC): moderate-to-severe (bilirubin >1.5–5× ULN) — reduce to 200 mg/m² (MBC) or 80 mg/m² (NSCLC); not recommended if AST >10× ULN or bilirubin >5× ULN. Not recommended for pancreatic cancer with moderate-to-severe hepatic impairment.
Route: IV infusion over 30 min (no in-line filter required; no premedication routinely required unlike solvent-based paclitaxel)
medications.monitoring: CBC with differential prior to each dose (ANC ≥1500 and platelets ≥100,000 on day 1); peripheral neuropathy assessment; LFTs at baseline; monitor for extravasation
medications.sideEffects: Most common — neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, alopecia, fatigue, nausea, diarrhea. Most serious — severe neutropenia (grade 4), severe sensory neuropathy, pneumonitis, sepsis. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur but are less frequent than with solvent-based paclitaxel. No boxed warning.
medications.watchFor: Do NOT substitute for or with solvent-based paclitaxel (Taxol) — different doses and formulation. No Cremophor EL = no routine steroid/antihistamine premedication required. Dose reductions for MBC: first reduction to 220 mg/m², second to 180 mg/m². Contraindicated if baseline ANC <1500. Insufficient data for severe renal impairment (CrCl <30). Pregnancy: can cause fetal harm. No specific drug interactions via CYP3A4 listed, but caution with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers (paclitaxel is a CYP3A4 substrate).

medications.drugClass: Topoisomerase II inhibitor (podophyllotoxin derivative; water-soluble prodrug of etoposide)
medications.mechanism: Prodrug that is rapidly and completely dephosphorylated in vivo to etoposide; etoposide interacts with DNA-topoisomerase II to induce DNA strand breaks and free radical formation, causing cell cycle arrest primarily at G₂ phase and cell death.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for (1) refractory testicular tumors in combination with other chemotherapy, (2) small cell lung cancer (SCLC) in combination with cisplatin as first-line treatment. Off-label: widely used in the same settings as etoposide (AML induction, lymphomas, HSCT conditioning, other solid tumors).
medications.dosing:
- Refractory testicular tumors: 50–100 mg/m²/day IV on days 1–5, or 100 mg/m² IV on days 1, 3, 5 of each 21- or 28-day cycle
- SCLC (first-line, with cisplatin): 35 mg/m²/day IV × 4 days, or 50 mg/m²/day IV × 5 days
- Renal impairment (CrCl 15–50 mL/min): reduce dose to 75% of recommended; insufficient data for CrCl <15
- Infusion: administer over 5 min to 3.5 hours; do NOT give by bolus injection
Route: IV infusion only
medications.monitoring: CBC with differential before each cycle and at nadir (~day 15–19); renal function (CrCl); LFTs; blood pressure during infusion
medications.sideEffects: Most common — myelosuppression (dose-limiting; neutropenia nadir day 15–19), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, anorexia, stomatitis, fatigue. Most serious — severe leukopenia/neutropenia, secondary acute myeloid leukemia (with cumulative exposure), anaphylactic reactions (rare). Hypotension and allergic reactions are less common than with conventional etoposide due to absence of polysorbate 80/Cremophor. No boxed warning on current label, but secondary leukemia risk is a class warning.
medications.watchFor: Pharmacokinetically equivalent to etoposide — doses are expressed as etoposide equivalents. Advantage over conventional etoposide: water-soluble, can be given in smaller volumes and shorter infusion times (5 min vs. 30–60 min), no polysorbate 80 (lower hypotension/allergy risk). Dextran 40 excipient in Etopophos may cause acute kidney injury at high doses (e.g., HSCT conditioning at 60 mg/kg) — consider substituting conventional etoposide in that setting. CYP3A4 substrate — caution with strong inhibitors/inducers. Pregnancy: can cause fetal harm.

medications.drugClass: Topoisomerase I inhibitor (liposomal formulation of irinotecan)
medications.mechanism: Irinotecan encapsulated in lipid bilayer liposomes; irinotecan and its active metabolite SN-38 bind the topoisomerase I–DNA complex, preventing re-ligation of single-strand breaks, leading to double-strand DNA damage and cell death. Liposomal encapsulation prolongs circulation and enhances intratumoral drug exposure via the EPR effect.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for (1) first-line metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma in combination with oxaliplatin, fluorouracil, and leucovorin (NALIRIFOX), and (2) metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma after progression on gemcitabine-based therapy, in combination with fluorouracil and leucovorin. Not indicated as a single agent. Off-label: under investigation in metastatic colorectal cancer and other GI malignancies.
medications.dosing:
- First-line metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (NALIRIFOX): 50 mg/m² IV over 90 min every 2 weeks (with oxaliplatin 60 mg/m², leucovorin 400 mg/m², 5-FU 2400 mg/m² over 46h)
- Post-gemcitabine metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (with 5-FU/LV): 70 mg/m² IV over 90 min every 2 weeks; homozygous UGT1A1*28: start at 50 mg/m², may increase to 70 mg/m² as tolerated
- No recommended dose for patients with serum bilirubin above ULN
Route: IV infusion over 90 min; dilute in 500 mL D5W or NS
medications.monitoring: CBC with differential periodically (withhold if ANC <1500); LFTs at baseline; monitor for diarrhea (early and late); UGT1A1 genotyping recommended before initiation
medications.sideEffects: Most common — diarrhea (early cholinergic and late), nausea, vomiting, fatigue, neutropenia, anemia. Most serious — BOXED WARNINGS: (1) severe/life-threatening neutropenia (including fatal neutropenic sepsis/fever); (2) severe/life-threatening diarrhea. Hand-foot syndrome and neurocerebellar toxicity can occur.
medications.watchFor: Do NOT substitute for conventional irinotecan HCl — different pharmacokinetics, dosing, and toxicity. Premedicate with a corticosteroid and antiemetic 30 min before each infusion. Treat late diarrhea with loperamide; treat early (cholinergic) diarrhea with atropine if not contraindicated. Contraindicated in bowel obstruction. UGT1A1*28 homozygotes: increased neutropenia risk — start at reduced dose. Dose reductions for toxicity: first to 40 mg/m², then 32.5 mg/m², then 25 mg/m², then discontinue (NALIRIFOX regimen). Pregnancy: can cause fetal harm.

medications.mechanism: Mycophenolic acid (MPA) is a selective, reversible, non-competitive inhibitor of inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH), blocking the de novo pathway of guanosine nucleotide synthesis; lymphocytes are critically dependent on this pathway, so MPA exerts a relatively selective cytostatic effect on T and B lymphocytes.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for prophylaxis of organ rejection in adult kidney transplant recipients and in pediatric patients ≥5 years who are ≥6 months post kidney transplant, in combination with cyclosporine and corticosteroids. Off-label: lupus nephritis, other solid organ transplants (heart, liver, lung), myasthenia gravis, autoimmune hepatitis, vasculitis.
medications.dosing:
- Adult kidney transplant: 720 mg PO twice daily (1440 mg/day total)
- Pediatric kidney transplant (≥5 yr, ≥6 mo post-transplant): 400 mg/m² PO twice daily (max 720 mg twice daily)
- Take on empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after food); swallow tablets whole — do not crush, chew, or cut (enteric-coated)
Route: PO (delayed-release enteric-coated tablets: 180 mg, 360 mg)
medications.monitoring: CBC with differential weekly during first month, twice monthly for months 2–3, then monthly through first year; LFTs; renal function; monitor for signs of infection and GI bleeding
medications.sideEffects: Most common — diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, leukopenia, anemia, UTI. Most serious — BOXED WARNINGS (class-wide for mycophenolate products): (1) increased risk of infections and lymphoma/malignancy (especially skin) due to immunosuppression; (2) increased risk of first-trimester pregnancy loss and congenital malformations. Additional serious risks: GI perforation/hemorrhage, PML (JC virus), pure red cell aplasia, BK virus nephropathy.
medications.watchFor: NOT interchangeable with mycophenolate mofetil (MMF/CellCept) without physician supervision — different absorption rates (Myfortic 720 mg ≈ MMF 1000 mg in MPA exposure, but rate differs). Contraindicated in hypersensitivity to mycophenolate sodium, MPA, or any component. Pregnancy: CONTRAINDICATED (REMS program required; negative pregnancy test before starting; two forms of contraception required). Males: use contraception during treatment and for 90 days after. No specific hepatic dose adjustment; renal impairment does not significantly alter MPA exposure but markedly increases MPAG (inactive metabolite). Avoid live vaccines. Drug interactions: antacids with Mg/Al hydroxide decrease absorption; cholestyramine decreases MPA levels; acyclovir/ganciclovir compete for tubular secretion.

medications.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, leukotrienes, cytokines) and immune cell activation; the acetate ester creates a depot effect with slow local absorption and prolonged anti-inflammatory action at the injection site.
medications.indications: FDA-approved (intra-articular/soft tissue) for rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis synovitis, acute gouty arthritis, bursitis, tenosynovitis, epicondylitis, acute nonspecific tenosynovitis. FDA-approved (IM) for adrenocortical insufficiency, allergic states, dermatologic diseases, rheumatic disorders, collagen diseases. FDA-approved (intralesional) for keloids, alopecia areata, discoid lupus, granuloma annulare, lichen planus. Off-label: carpal tunnel syndrome, trigger finger, plantar fasciitis, Morton neuroma, de Quervain tenosynovitis, trochanteric bursitis.
medications.dosing:
- Intra-articular (large joints — knee, ankle, shoulder): 20–80 mg per injection
- Intra-articular (medium joints — elbow, wrist): 10–40 mg per injection
- Intra-articular (small joints — MCP, IP, AC): 4–10 mg per injection
- Bursitis (subdeltoid, prepatellar, olecranon): 20–40 mg
- Tendon sheath (tenosynovitis, trigger finger): 4–10 mg
- Ganglion cyst: 10–20 mg
- Carpal tunnel syndrome (off-label): 20–40 mg (some evidence supports 40–80 mg for longer duration of effect)
- Plantar fasciitis (off-label): 20–40 mg, often mixed with 1–2 mL lidocaine or bupivacaine
- Systemic (IM, deep gluteal): 40–120 mg; adrenal suppression may last 1–4 weeks after a single dose
- Intralesional (keloids, dermatologic): 20–60 mg per lesion, may repeat at 1–4 week intervals
Route: Intra-articular, soft tissue (bursa, tendon sheath), intralesional, IM. NOT for IV, intrathecal, epidural, or intradermal use.
medications.monitoring: Blood glucose (transient hyperglycemia common, especially in diabetics — may last 2–5 days post-injection); blood pressure; injection site for signs of infection, skin atrophy, or depigmentation; HPA axis function with repeated systemic (IM) doses; bone density with chronic use.
medications.sideEffects: Most common — injection site pain, post-injection steroid flare (24–48h), skin atrophy and depigmentation at injection site (especially in darker skin), transient hyperglycemia, facial flushing. Most serious — tendon rupture (with peritendinous injection), septic arthritis (rare, ~1:10,000–50,000), HPA axis suppression (with repeated IM use), osteonecrosis, anaphylaxis (rare). No boxed warning.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in systemic fungal infections, infection at the injection site, and hypersensitivity to any component. Potency equivalence: 4 mg methylprednisolone = 4 mg triamcinolone = 5 mg prednisone = 20 mg hydrocortisone; no clinically significant mineralocorticoid activity. Steroid flare rate is higher than triamcinolone acetonide (~23% vs. ~4% in one shoulder injection study).  Intra-articular injections generally limited to 3–4 per year per joint (minimum 3-month interval recommended by ASRA/AAPM guidelines).  Available as 20 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, and 80 mg/mL suspensions — shake vigorously before use. Some formulations contain benzyl alcohol (avoid in neonates) or polyethylene glycol. Do not inject directly into tendons. Avoid live vaccines during systemic immunosuppressive doses. Pregnancy category C. Distinct product from methylprednisolone sodium succinate (Solu-Medrol), which is the IV formulation — these are NOT interchangeable.

medications.mechanism: Leucovorin (folinic acid) is the calcium salt of 5-formyltetrahydrofolic acid, a metabolically active reduced folate that bypasses dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) and directly enters the one-carbon folate pool; it rescues normal cells from methotrexate toxicity by replenishing tetrahydrofolate cofactors, and it potentiates 5-fluorouracil cytotoxicity by stabilizing the ternary complex of FdUMP–thymidylate synthase–methylenetetrahydrofolate.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for (1) leucovorin rescue after high-dose methotrexate therapy, (2) treatment of megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency when oral folic acid is not feasible, (3) palliative treatment of advanced colorectal cancer in combination with 5-fluorouracil. Key off-label uses include methanol/formic acid poisoning (adjunctive folate repletion), component of FOLFOX/FOLFIRI/FLOT chemotherapy regimens, rescue from trimethoprim or pyrimethamine toxicity, and inadvertent methotrexate overdose.
medications.dosing:
- Methotrexate rescue (high-dose MTX): 15 mg (approximately 10 mg/m²) IV/IM/PO every 6 hours × 10 doses, starting 24 hours after MTX infusion begins; adjust based on serum MTX levels (if 24h MTX >5 × 10⁻⁶ M or 48h MTX >9 × 10⁻⁷ M, increase to 150 mg IV q3h until MTX <5 × 10⁻⁸ M)
- Inadvertent MTX overdose: begin leucovorin as soon as possible; 10 mg/m² IV/IM/PO q6h until serum MTX <10⁻⁸ M; if 24h serum creatinine ≥50% above baseline, increase to 100 mg/m² IV q3h
- Advanced colorectal cancer (with 5-FU): 200 mg/m² IV over ≥2 min, followed by 5-FU 370 mg/m² IV daily × 5 days, repeated q28 days; OR 20 mg/m² IV followed by 5-FU 425 mg/m² IV daily × 5 days q28 days
- FOLFOX/FOLFIRI component: 400 mg/m² IV over 2 hours on day 1 of each 14-day cycle (per modified de Gramont schedule)
- FLOT component: 200 mg/m² IV on day 1 of each 14-day cycle
- Methanol poisoning (off-label): 50 mg IV every 4–6 hours (typically 1 mg/kg IV q4–6h is also cited); continue until formate is cleared and acidosis resolves
- Megaloblastic anemia: up to 1 mg/day IM; no more than 1 mg/day (higher doses do not enhance hematologic response)
Route: IV, IM, PO. IV administration: doses >10 mg/m² must be given parenterally (not PO) due to saturable oral absorption. Inject IV no faster than 160 mg/min (due to calcium content). Do not administer intrathecally.
medications.monitoring: Methotrexate rescue — serum MTX levels at 24, 48, and 72 hours; serum creatinine q24h (MTX nephrotoxicity delays clearance and necessitates leucovorin dose escalation); CBC; urine pH (maintain ≥7.0 with hydration/bicarbonate). Colorectal cancer — CBC with differential and electrolytes before each cycle. Methanol poisoning — serum formate, arterial pH, anion gap, osmolal gap.
