medications.sideEffects: Most common — generally well tolerated; nausea, vomiting, stomatitis, diarrhea (primarily when combined with 5-FU, where leucovorin enhances both efficacy and GI/hematologic toxicity). Most serious — allergic/anaphylactoid reactions (rare); seizures in susceptible patients (leucovorin may lower seizure threshold — reported primarily in patients receiving intrathecal MTX or with CNS pathology). When combined with 5-FU, leucovorin significantly increases the rate of severe mucositis, diarrhea, and myelosuppression compared to 5-FU alone. No boxed warning.
medications.watchFor: NOT a substitute for folic acid in pernicious anemia or B₁₂-deficient megaloblastic anemia — leucovorin may improve hematologic parameters while masking irreversible neurologic damage from B₁₂ deficiency. Leucovorin rescue must begin within 24 hours of MTX administration and is ineffective if delayed >40 hours. Impaired MTX elimination (renal dysfunction, third-space fluid, GI obstruction) requires prolonged leucovorin rescue with higher doses guided by MTX levels. Leucovorin and folic acid are NOT pharmacologically identical — leucovorin bypasses DHFR and is the preferred agent for MTX rescue and methanol poisoning; folic acid requires enzymatic reduction and is an acceptable but theoretically inferior substitute for methanol poisoning. Levoleucovorin (Fusilev) is the pharmacologically active L-isomer and is dosed at half the leucovorin dose (e.g., 200 mg/m² levoleucovorin = 400 mg/m² racemic leucovorin). No renal or hepatic dose adjustment required. Pregnancy category C.

medications.mechanism: Binds penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) in the bacterial cell wall, inhibiting transpeptidation and disrupting peptidoglycan synthesis, leading to cell lysis and death; the isoxazolyl side chain confers resistance to staphylococcal β-lactamase (penicillinase), preserving activity against MSSA.
medications.indications: FDA-approved for infections caused by penicillinase-producing staphylococci, including bacteremia/septicemia, bone and joint infections (osteomyelitis), endocarditis, skin and soft tissue infections, pneumonia, meningitis, and urinary tract infections. Used interchangeably with nafcillin for MSSA infections. Not active against MRSA.
medications.dosing:
- MSSA bacteremia/endocarditis (adult): 2 g IV every 4 hours (12 g/day) × 4–6 weeks
- MSSA osteomyelitis (adult): 2 g IV every 4 hours × 4–6 weeks (may transition to oral after initial IV course)
- MSSA skin/soft tissue, pneumonia (adult): 1–2 g IV every 4–6 hours
- Meningitis (adult): 2 g IV every 4 hours
- Pediatric (mild-moderate infections): 50 mg/kg/day IV/IM in equally divided doses q6h
- Pediatric (severe infections): 100–200 mg/kg/day IV in equally divided doses q4–6h (max 12 g/day)
- Neonates (≤2000 g, 0–7 days): 25–50 mg/kg/dose IV q12h; (>7 days): q8h; (>2000 g, 0–7 days): q8h; (>7 days): q6h
Route: IV (preferred), IM. Oral formulation historically available but rarely used (poor bioavailability; dicloxacillin preferred for oral antistaphylococcal therapy).
medications.monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine) — especially with prolonged courses (>2 weeks); urinalysis (interstitial nephritis); CBC with differential (neutropenia with prolonged use); LFTs periodically; monitor for signs of hypersensitivity.
medications.sideEffects: Most common — phlebitis at IV site, rash, diarrhea, nausea. Most serious — acute interstitial nephritis (infrequent — LOWER risk than nafcillin), anaphylaxis, neutropenia/agranulocytosis (with prolonged courses >2 weeks), C. difficile-associated diarrhea, seizures (at very high doses or in renal failure). Hepatotoxicity — HIGHER signal than nafcillin, particularly at doses >6 g/day (pediatric OPAT series 22% vs nafcillin 0%). No boxed warning.
medications.watchFor: Contraindicated in penicillin hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity with other β-lactams ~1–5%). Nafcillin preferred over oxacillin in patients with hepatic impairment (oxacillin carries higher hepatotoxicity risk, particularly at high doses). Oxacillin preferred over nafcillin in patients with renal impairment or AKI risk (nafcillin carries significantly higher rates of AKI and hypokalemia). Approximately 30–45% renally excreted unchanged — no formal dose adjustment recommended for renal impairment, but monitor closely. Highly protein-bound (~94%). Probenecid increases oxacillin levels (blocks tubular secretion). Neonates: half-life is significantly prolonged (1.5–5.5 hours vs. 0.4–0.7 hours in adults) — use extended dosing intervals. Pregnancy category B. Sodium content: ~2.8–3.1 mEq Na⁺ per gram — consider in sodium-restricted patients.

organisms.name: Staphylococcus aureus
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in clusters (grape-like); catalase-positive, coagulase-positive (distinguishes from S. epidermidis); golden-yellow colonies on blood agar; mannitol-fermenting (yellow on mannitol salt agar)
organisms.features: Protein A (binds IgG Fc → prevents opsonization); coagulase (converts fibrinogen to fibrin → abscess formation); numerous toxins — TSST-1 (toxic shock syndrome), Panton-Valentine leukocidin/PVL (necrotizing pneumonia, skin abscesses — associated with CA-MRSA), exfoliative toxins (scalded skin syndrome), enterotoxins (food poisoning — heat-stable, preformed); biofilm formation on prosthetic devices; most common cause of skin/soft tissue infections, bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, prosthetic joint infections
organisms.source: Human skin and nares (30% colonization rate); healthcare settings; community (CA-MRSA)

presentations.symptoms: Folliculitis, furuncles, carbuncles, cellulitis, impetigo, wound infections, abscesses
presentations.clues: Purulent drainage, fluctuance; CA-MRSA often presents as "spider bite"

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills, new murmur, septic emboli (Janeway lesions, Osler nodes), metastatic abscesses
presentations.clues: Persistent bacteremia (>72 hrs); always obtain TTE/TEE; mortality 20–25% for MRSA

presentations.symptoms: Bone pain, fever, elevated ESR/CRP; joint swelling, warmth, restricted ROM
presentations.clues: Hematogenous in children; contiguous/post-surgical in adults

presentations.symptoms: Necrotizing pneumonia (PVL+), empyema, post-influenza pneumonia
presentations.clues: Cavitary lesions on imaging; rapid deterioration post-influenza

presentations.symptoms: High fever, diffuse macular rash (sunburn-like), desquamation (1–2 weeks later), hypotension, multi-organ involvement
presentations.clues: Tampon-associated (menstrual) or wound-associated; TSST-1 mediated

presentations.symptoms: Sudden-onset nausea, vomiting (predominant), abdominal cramps, diarrhea
presentations.clues: Very rapid onset; self-limited (24 hrs); no fever; associated with custards, meats, salads left at room temperature

presentations.symptoms: Diffuse erythema, bullae, Nikolsky sign, desquamation
presentations.clues: Neonates/young children; exfoliative toxin; no mucosal involvement (unlike TEN)

coverages.note: first-line (MSSA) — IV; gold standard for serious MSSA infections
first-line (MSSA) — IV; may have lower mortality and fewer adverse effects than nafcillin; preferred by many for MSSA bacteremia
first-line (MSSA, oral) — Outpatient skin/soft tissue infections
first-line (MRSA) — IV; target AUC 400–600; inferior to beta-lactams for MSSA
alternative (MRSA) — IV; 6–10 mg/kg; do NOT use for pneumonia (inactivated by surfactant)
alternative (MRSA) — PO/IV; 100% bioavailability; monitor platelets
first-line (CA-MRSA, outpatient) — PO; for uncomplicated skin infections
alternative (CA-MRSA, outpatient) — PO; for uncomplicated skin infections
alternative (CA-MRSA) — PO/IV; check for inducible resistance (D-test); also inhibits toxin production

organisms.name: Staphylococcus epidermidis
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in clusters; catalase-positive, coagulase-NEGATIVE (key distinction from S. aureus); novobiocin-sensitive (distinguishes from S. saprophyticus)
organisms.features: Biofilm formation on prosthetic devices (most important virulence factor); most common cause of prosthetic device infections (prosthetic valves, joint replacements, CSF shunts, intravascular catheters); normal skin flora — most blood culture isolates are contaminants (must distinguish true infection from contamination by ≥2 positive cultures from separate sites)
organisms.source: Normal human skin flora (ubiquitous)

presentations.symptoms: Fever, new/changing murmur, embolic phenomena
presentations.clues: Early PVE (60 days) often S. epidermidis; indolent course

presentations.symptoms: Fever, catheter site erythema/purulence
presentations.clues: Most common cause of CRBSI; often methicillin-resistant

presentations.symptoms: Joint pain, loosening, sinus tract
presentations.clues: Biofilm-mediated; often requires device removal

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, shunt malfunction
presentations.clues: Most common cause of VP shunt infections

coverages.note: first-line — Most strains are methicillin-resistant (~80%); vancomycin is empiric choice
adjunct — Added for prosthetic device infections (biofilm penetration); never use as monotherapy (rapid resistance)
alternative — For vancomycin-intolerant patients
alternative — For vancomycin-intolerant patients

organisms.name: Streptococcus pyogenes
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in chains; catalase-negative; beta-hemolytic (complete hemolysis on blood agar); Lancefield Group A; bacitracin-sensitive; PYR-positive
organisms.features: M protein (major virulence factor — anti-phagocytic; >200 serotypes; molecular mimicry → rheumatic fever); streptolysin O (ASO titer); streptolysin S (beta-hemolysis); streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins/SPE (scarlet fever, streptococcal toxic shock); hyaluronidase, streptokinase, DNase B; NO penicillin resistance has EVER been documented
organisms.source: Human pharynx and skin; respiratory droplets; direct contact

presentations.symptoms: Sudden sore throat, fever, tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, palatal petechiae; absence of cough
presentations.clues: Centor/McIsaac criteria; 20–30% of pediatric sore throats; 5–15% of adult

presentations.symptoms: Diffuse sandpaper-like erythematous rash, strawberry tongue, Pastia lines (linear petechiae in skin folds), circumoral pallor
presentations.clues: Rash due to erythrogenic/pyrogenic exotoxin; desquamation follows

presentations.symptoms: Honey-crusted lesions on face/extremities
presentations.clues: Non-bullous; can trigger post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (NOT rheumatic fever)

presentations.symptoms: Erysipelas: sharply demarcated, raised, erythematous; cellulitis: spreading erythema, warmth, tenderness
presentations.clues: Erysipelas involves superficial dermis/lymphatics; raised border

presentations.symptoms: Severe pain out of proportion to exam, rapidly spreading erythema, crepitus, bullae, systemic toxicity
presentations.clues: Surgical emergency; "flesh-eating bacteria"; often with streptococcal toxic shock

presentations.symptoms: Jones criteria: carditis, polyarthritis (migratory, large joints), chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
presentations.clues: Post-pharyngeal ONLY (not post-skin); prevented by treating pharyngitis with antibiotics

presentations.incubation: 1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis or 3–6 weeks post-skin infection
presentations.symptoms: Hematuria, edema, hypertension, low C3
presentations.clues: NOT prevented by antibiotics; self-limited in children

coverages.note: first-line — 10-day course for pharyngitis; NO resistance ever documented
first-line (IM) — Single dose; ensures compliance
alternative (non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy) — 10 days
alternative (anaphylactic penicillin allergy) — Significant resistance in some US areas
alternative — Also inhibits toxin production; useful in necrotizing fasciitis (add to penicillin)
first-line (necrotizing fasciitis/toxic shock) — Clindamycin inhibits toxin production; penicillin for bactericidal activity

organisms.name: Streptococcus agalactiae
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in chains; catalase-negative; beta-hemolytic; Lancefield Group B; bacitracin-resistant; CAMP test positive; hippurate hydrolysis positive
organisms.features: Polysaccharide capsule (10 serotypes); colonizes GI and GU tracts (~25% of pregnant women); leading cause of neonatal sepsis and meningitis; also causes infections in elderly and immunocompromised adults (UTI, pneumonia, skin/soft tissue, bacteremia, endocarditis)
organisms.source: Human GI/GU tract; vertical transmission during delivery

presentations.symptoms: Sepsis, pneumonia, respiratory distress, meningitis
presentations.clues: Acquired during passage through birth canal; prevented by intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP)

presentations.symptoms: Meningitis (most common), bacteremia
presentations.clues: Acquired from mother or community; NOT prevented by IAP

presentations.symptoms: UTI, pneumonia, skin/soft tissue, bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis
presentations.clues: Elderly, diabetics, immunocompromised

coverages.note: first-line — IV; for neonatal disease and IAP
first-line — IV; for neonatal disease and IAP; add gentamicin for synergy in meningitis
alternative (IAP) — For penicillin-allergic patients (low anaphylaxis risk)
alternative (IAP) — For penicillin-allergic patients (high anaphylaxis risk); must confirm susceptibility
alternative (IAP) — For penicillin-allergic patients with clindamycin-resistant GBS

organisms.name: Streptococcus pneumoniae
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci; catalase-negative; alpha-hemolytic (partial/green hemolysis on blood agar); optochin-sensitive; bile-soluble; quellung reaction positive (capsular swelling)
organisms.features: Polysaccharide capsule (>100 serotypes — major virulence factor; basis for vaccines PCV13/15/20, PPSV23); IgA protease; pneumolysin (pore-forming toxin); most common cause of bacterial CAP, bacterial meningitis (adults), otitis media, sinusitis; asplenic patients at high risk for overwhelming sepsis
organisms.source: Human nasopharynx (5–10% adult carriage; up to 60% children); respiratory droplets

presentations.symptoms: Acute onset fever, productive cough (rusty sputum), pleuritic chest pain, rigors
presentations.clues: Lobar consolidation on CXR; most common bacterial cause of CAP

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status, photophobia
presentations.clues: Most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults; CSF: high WBC (PMN), high protein, low glucose, positive Gram stain

presentations.symptoms: Ear pain, fever, bulging TM, decreased hearing
presentations.clues: Most common bacterial cause of AOM in children

presentations.symptoms: Facial pain/pressure, purulent nasal discharge, fever
presentations.clues: Common bacterial cause

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills, hypotension
presentations.clues: Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) — fulminant, high mortality

coverages.note: first-line (outpatient CAP, AOM) — 90 mg/kg/day (pediatric); 1 g TID (adult)
first-line (hospitalized, non-meningeal, susceptible) — IV; high-dose overcomes intermediate resistance
first-line (hospitalized CAP, meningitis) — IV; for meningitis add vancomycin empirically until susceptibilities known
first-line (meningitis, empiric) — Added to ceftriaxone for meningitis until susceptibilities confirmed
alternative — For beta-lactam-allergic patients; reserve to prevent resistance
alternative/adjunct — Resistance >30% in US; use only in combination or where resistance 25%

organisms.name: Viridans streptococci
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in chains; catalase-negative; alpha-hemolytic (partial hemolysis); optochin-RESISTANT (distinguishes from S. pneumoniae); bile-INSOLUBLE
organisms.features: Heterogeneous group including S. mutans (dental caries), S. mitis, S. sanguinis, S. salivarius, S. anginosus group (abscess formation); normal oral flora; most common cause of subacute bacterial endocarditis (native valve); S. anginosus group (S. anginosus, S. intermedius, S. constellatus) — associated with abscess formation (brain, liver, lung)
organisms.source: Human oral cavity, GI tract, GU tract

presentations.symptoms: Low-grade fever, malaise, weight loss, new murmur, Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, splinter hemorrhages
presentations.clues: Most common cause of native valve endocarditis; insidious onset; often on previously damaged valves

presentations.symptoms: Tooth decay
presentations.clues: S. mutans; produces acid from sucrose → enamel demineralization

presentations.symptoms: Fever, focal symptoms depending on location
presentations.clues: S. anginosus group; tendency to form abscesses

coverages.note: first-line — IV; for endocarditis (4 weeks monotherapy if MIC ≤0.12; add gentamicin for 2 weeks if MIC 0.12–0.5)
first-line — IV; for endocarditis; once-daily dosing advantage
alternative — For penicillin-allergic patients

organisms.name: Enterococcus faecalis / E. faecium
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in chains or pairs; catalase-negative; gamma-hemolytic (no hemolysis) or alpha-hemolytic; grows in 6.5% NaCl, bile esculin positive, PYR-positive; grows at 10–45°C
organisms.features: Intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins (ALL generations), aminoglycosides (low-level), TMP-SMX (in vivo), clindamycin; E. faecalis causes ~97% of enterococcal endocarditis; E. faecium more likely to be vancomycin-resistant (VRE); bacteriostatic effect of cell-wall agents alone (need synergy for bactericidal activity); common cause of UTI, intra-abdominal infections, bacteremia, endocarditis; third most common cause of IE
organisms.source: Human GI tract (normal flora); healthcare settings (VRE)

presentations.symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, urgency; often catheter-associated
presentations.clues: Second most common cause of healthcare-associated UTI

presentations.symptoms: Fever, abdominal pain
presentations.clues: Usually polymicrobial; pathogenic role debated in polymicrobial infections

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills; often catheter-related or biliary/GU source
presentations.clues: Mortality 20–30%; VRE associated with higher mortality

presentations.symptoms: Subacute presentation similar to viridans strep; ~10% of IE cases
presentations.clues: Requires prolonged combination therapy (6 weeks); E. faecalis >>E. faecium

coverages.note: first-line (E. faecalis, susceptible) — Preferred over all other agents when susceptible
first-line (endocarditis, susceptible) — 6 weeks; synergistic bactericidal activity; nephrotoxicity risk
first-line (endocarditis, alternative) — 6 weeks; double beta-lactam synergy; less nephrotoxic than aminoglycoside combination; preferred for aminoglycoside-resistant strains
alternative (penicillin-allergic or resistant) — For E. faecalis resistant to ampicillin; E. faecium often VRE
first-line (VRE) — PO/IV; bacteriostatic; monitor platelets
first-line (VRE) — IV; bactericidal; higher doses (8–12 mg/kg) for endocarditis
first-line (VRE UTI) — For uncomplicated cystitis only

organisms.name: Listeria monocytogenes
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod (short); catalase-positive; beta-hemolytic (narrow zone); tumbling motility at 20–25°C (actin rocket propulsion intracellularly); facultative intracellular pathogen; grows at 4°C (refrigerator temperatures — cold enrichment)
organisms.features: Intracellular pathogen (survives in macrophages); cell-to-cell spread via actin polymerization (ActA); crosses blood-brain barrier, placental barrier, and intestinal barrier; T-cell mediated immunity required for clearance (NOT antibody); at-risk populations: neonates, elderly, pregnant women, immunocompromised (especially cell-mediated immunodeficiency — transplant, steroids, HIV)
organisms.source: Soil, water, animals; contaminated food — unpasteurized dairy (soft cheeses), deli meats, hot dogs, raw vegetables, cantaloupe
organisms.notes: Cephalosporins have NO activity against Listeria — empiric meningitis regimens must include ampicillin to cover Listeria in at-risk patients (neonates, elderly, immunocompromised)

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status; may have brainstem involvement (rhombencephalitis — cranial nerve palsies, ataxia)
presentations.clues: Third most common cause of bacterial meningitis; affects neonates, elderly, immunocompromised; CSF may show monocyte predominance

presentations.symptoms: Fever, myalgias, GI prodrome
presentations.clues: Immunocompromised patients

presentations.symptoms: Granulomatosis infantiseptica (disseminated granulomas); sepsis, meningitis
presentations.clues: Transplacental or birth canal transmission; associated with preterm labor, stillbirth

presentations.symptoms: Fever, watery diarrhea, nausea
presentations.clues: Self-limited in immunocompetent; may not require treatment

presentations.symptoms: Flu-like illness in mother; devastating fetal effects (miscarriage, stillbirth, preterm labor, neonatal sepsis)
presentations.clues: Pregnant women 10× more susceptible; maternal illness often mild

coverages.note: first-line — IV; often combined with gentamicin for synergy in meningitis
first-line (meningitis) — Synergistic bactericidal activity
alternative (penicillin allergy) — IV; good CNS penetration

organisms.name: Bacillus cereus
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod (large, box-car shaped); aerobic/facultative anaerobic; spore-forming (central/subterminal spores); motile; catalase-positive; beta-hemolytic
organisms.features: Two distinct food poisoning syndromes — emetic (cereulide toxin — preformed, heat-stable) and diarrheal (enterotoxins — heat-labile, produced in vivo); spores survive cooking; also causes ocular infections (post-traumatic endophthalmitis — rapidly destructive)
organisms.source: Soil, dust, vegetation; contaminated food — FRIED RICE (emetic type), meats, vegetables, sauces (diarrheal type)
organisms.notes: Intrinsically resistant to penicillins and cephalosporins (beta-lactamase production)

presentations.symptoms: Nausea, vomiting (predominant), abdominal cramps
presentations.clues: Associated with reheated FRIED RICE; similar to S. aureus food poisoning; self-limited

presentations.symptoms: Watery diarrhea, abdominal cramps, nausea
presentations.clues: Toxin produced in vivo; similar to C. perfringens; self-limited

presentations.symptoms: Rapid vision loss, eye pain, chemosis
presentations.clues: Post-traumatic or post-surgical; rapidly destructive; may require vitrectomy

coverages.note: first-line (food poisoning) — Self-limited; antibiotics not indicated
first-line (systemic/ocular) — For serious infections; intrinsically resistant to beta-lactams (produces beta-lactamase)
alternative — For systemic infections

organisms.name: Clostridioides difficile (formerly Clostridium difficile)
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod; obligate anaerobe; spore-forming (spores resistant to alcohol-based hand sanitizers, heat, many disinfectants — use bleach/sporicidal agents); motile
organisms.features: Toxin A (enterotoxin — causes fluid secretion and mucosal damage) and Toxin B (cytotoxin — more potent; causes cell rounding and death); binary toxin CDT (in hypervirulent strains); hypervirulent strain NAP1/BI/027 (produces more toxin, fluoroquinolone-resistant); leading cause of healthcare-associated diarrhea; risk factors: antibiotics (clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins — highest risk), age >65, hospitalization, PPI use, immunosuppression
organisms.source: Healthcare environments (spores persist on surfaces); fecal-oral transmission; can be part of normal flora in infants

presentations.incubation: During or up to 3 months after antibiotic use
presentations.symptoms: Watery diarrhea (≥3 loose stools/24 hrs), abdominal pain/cramping, fever, leukocytosis (often WBC >15,000; can be >40,000)
presentations.clues: Diagnosis: stool toxin testing (GDH screen → toxin EIA or NAAT); do NOT test formed stool; do NOT test for cure

presentations.symptoms: Severe diarrhea, abdominal distension, fever, leukocytosis; pseudomembranes on colonoscopy (yellow-white plaques)
presentations.clues: Pathognomonic finding on colonoscopy

presentations.symptoms: Ileus (may have decreased diarrhea paradoxically), toxic megacolon, bowel perforation, septic shock
presentations.clues: WBC >15,000, creatinine >1.5, lactate >2.2; may require colectomy

presentations.symptoms: Same as initial episode
presentations.clues: 20–25% recurrence after first episode; 40–65% after second recurrence

coverages.note: first-line (all severity) — PO 200 mg BID × 10 days; narrow-spectrum (spares normal flora); lower recurrence than vancomycin (~13% vs ~27%)
first-line (alternative) — PO 125 mg QID × 10 days; for initial and recurrent episodes; NOT absorbed systemically (local GI effect)
first-line (fulminant) — Add rectal

organisms.name: Clostridium perfringens
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod (large, box-car shaped); anaerobic (but aerotolerant); spore-forming; NON-motile (distinguishes from other clostridia); double-zone beta-hemolysis on blood agar; Nagler reaction positive (lecithinase/alpha toxin)
organisms.features: Alpha toxin (phospholipase C/lecithinase — destroys cell membranes → gas gangrene/myonecrosis); enterotoxin (food poisoning — produced during sporulation in intestine); theta toxin (perfringolysin O — pore-forming); produces gas in tissues (crepitus); most common cause of gas gangrene/clostridial myonecrosis
organisms.source: Soil, GI tract of humans and animals; contaminated food (meats, gravies)
organisms.notes: Resistant to aminoglycosides and sulfonamides. Susceptible to penicillins, clindamycin, metronidazole, carbapenems, vancomycin. Surgical debridement is the most critical intervention for gas gangrene.

presentations.symptoms: Watery diarrhea, abdominal cramps; vomiting uncommon; NO fever
presentations.clues: Associated with reheated meats/gravies; self-limited (24 hrs); enterotoxin produced during sporulation

presentations.symptoms: Severe pain out of proportion, rapid swelling, bronze/dusky skin discoloration, hemorrhagic bullae, crepitus (gas in tissues), foul-smelling discharge, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, fever, shock)
presentations.clues: Surgical emergency; Gram stain shows large GPR with few WBCs (leukocyte destruction by toxins); X-ray shows gas in soft tissues

presentations.symptoms: Severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, bowel necrosis
presentations.clues: Beta toxin; associated with protein-poor diets; rare in developed countries

coverages.note: first-line (gas gangrene) — Clindamycin inhibits toxin production; penicillin for bactericidal activity; emergent surgical debridement is essential
first-line (food poisoning) — Self-limited; antibiotics not indicated
alternative (polymicrobial/uncertain) — Broad-spectrum coverage when diagnosis uncertain

organisms.name: Clostridium botulinum
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod; obligate anaerobe; spore-forming; motile
organisms.features: Botulinum toxin (most potent biological toxin known; 7 serotypes A–G; blocks acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junction by cleaving SNARE proteins → flaccid paralysis); toxin is heat-labile (destroyed at 85°C × 5 min) but spores are heat-resistant; three clinical forms: foodborne (preformed toxin), infant (in vivo toxin production from ingested spores), wound (in vivo production from wound contamination)
organisms.source: Soil, dust; contaminated food (home-canned vegetables, honey — infant botulism); wound contamination (injection drug use)
organisms.notes: Do NOT give antibiotics for infant botulism (bacterial lysis may release more toxin). Supportive care with mechanical ventilation is critical. Antitoxin must be given early — it neutralizes circulating toxin only.

presentations.symptoms: Symmetric DESCENDING flaccid paralysis starting with cranial nerves (diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, ptosis), then descending to limbs and respiratory muscles; NO sensory deficits; NO fever; autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention); clear sensorium
presentations.clues: Descending paralysis (opposite of Guillain-Barré which is ascending); bilateral cranial nerve involvement; dilated unreactive pupils

presentations.symptoms: "Floppy baby" — poor feeding, weak cry, constipation, hypotonia, loss of head control, descending weakness
presentations.clues: Age 1 year; associated with honey ingestion; most common form in US

presentations.symptoms: Same as foodborne but without GI prodrome
presentations.clues: Injection drug users (black tar heroin); wound contamination

coverages.note: first-line (foodborne/wound) — Equine-derived; give as early as possible; does not reverse existing paralysis but prevents progression; obtain from CDC
first-line (infant botulism) — Human-derived; reduces hospital stay from ~6 weeks to ~3 weeks
adjunct (wound botulism) — Wound debridement essential; antibiotics adjunctive; do NOT use aminoglycosides (worsen neuromuscular blockade)

organisms.name: Clostridium tetani
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod; obligate anaerobe; spore-forming (terminal spores — "drumstick" or "tennis racket" appearance); motile
organisms.features: Tetanospasmin (tetanus neurotoxin/TeNT — second most potent biological toxin after botulinum); TeNT undergoes retrograde axonal transport to CNS → blocks release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA and glycine) from presynaptic terminals → unopposed excitatory motor neuron activity → muscle rigidity and spasms; spores ubiquitous in soil; vaccine-preventable (Td/Tdap)
organisms.source: Soil, animal feces, rusty objects; contaminated wounds

presentations.incubation: 3–21 days (average 7–10 days; shorter incubation = worse prognosis)
presentations.symptoms: Trismus (lockjaw — earliest and most common), risus sardonicus (sardonic smile from facial muscle spasm), opisthotonus (arched back), generalized muscle rigidity, painful reflex spasms (triggered by stimuli — noise, touch, light), dysphagia, autonomic instability (labile BP, tachycardia, diaphoresis, arrhythmias)
presentations.clues: Descending rigidity pattern; clear sensorium; NO sensory deficits; diagnosis is CLINICAL

presentations.symptoms: Rigidity/spasms confined to muscles near wound
presentations.clues: May progress to generalized; better prognosis

presentations.symptoms: Trismus, cranial nerve palsies (especially CN VII), dysphagia
presentations.clues: Head/face wounds; often progresses to generalized

presentations.symptoms: Poor feeding, rigidity, spasms
presentations.clues: Unclean umbilical cord care; unvaccinated mother

coverages.note: first-line — 500 IU IM (some recommend 3,000–6,000 IU); neutralizes unbound toxin; does NOT reverse bound toxin
first-line (antibiotic) — 500 mg IV q6-8h × 7–10 days; preferred over penicillin (penicillin is a GABA antagonist — may worsen spasms)
essential — Remove foreign bodies and necrotic tissue
first-line (spasm control) — GABA agonists; high doses often needed; magnesium sulfate as adjunct
prophylaxis/treatment — Active immunization (tetanus does NOT confer natural immunity — must vaccinate after recovery); Tdap booster q10 years

organisms.name: Corynebacterium diphtheriae
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod; club-shaped; arranged in V/L formations ("Chinese letter" pattern); non-spore-forming; non-motile; metachromatic (volutin) granules on Albert stain; grows on Löffler medium and tellurite agar (black colonies)
organisms.features: Diphtheria toxin (AB exotoxin — encoded by beta-prophage; inhibits protein synthesis by ADP-ribosylating EF-2/elongation factor 2); only toxigenic strains cause disease; Elek test confirms toxin production; toxin causes local tissue necrosis (pseudomembrane) and systemic effects (myocarditis, neuropathy); vaccine-preventable (DTaP/Tdap)
organisms.source: Human respiratory tract; direct contact or respiratory droplets

presentations.symptoms: Sore throat, low-grade fever, malaise, cervical lymphadenopathy ("bull neck"), adherent gray-white pseudomembrane on pharynx/tonsils (bleeds when removed), stridor (laryngeal involvement)
presentations.clues: Pseudomembrane is pathognomonic; can cause airway obstruction

presentations.symptoms: Arrhythmias (heart block, bundle branch block), heart failure, cardiogenic shock
presentations.clues: Most common cause of death; toxin-mediated

presentations.symptoms: Palatal paralysis (nasal voice, regurgitation), cranial nerve palsies, peripheral polyneuropathy (ascending — resembles GBS)
presentations.clues: Usually reversible over weeks to months

presentations.symptoms: Non-healing ulcer with gray membrane
presentations.clues: More common in tropics; less systemic toxicity

coverages.note: first-line — Equine-derived; give immediately (neutralizes unbound toxin); dose based on severity (20,000–120,000 units); do NOT wait for culture confirmation
first-line (antibiotic) — 500 mg QID × 14 days; eliminates organism and prevents transmission
alternative (antibiotic) — 600,000 U IM q12h × 14 days
prophylaxis — Active immunization; disease does NOT confer immunity — must vaccinate after recovery

organisms.name: Bacillus anthracis
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive rod (large, box-car shaped); aerobic; spore-forming (central spores — highly resistant; can persist in soil for decades); NON-motile (distinguishes from B. cereus); NON-hemolytic on blood agar (distinguishes from B. cereus); encapsulated (poly-D-glutamic acid capsule — anti-phagocytic; unique polypeptide capsule)
organisms.features: Three virulence factors: (1) protective antigen (PA — binds cell receptors, forms pore for toxin entry); (2) lethal factor (LF — metalloprotease; PA+LF = lethal toxin); (3) edema factor (EF — adenylate cyclase; PA+EF = edema toxin); bioterrorism agent (Category A); spores used as biological weapon (2001 anthrax letters)
organisms.source: Soil (spores); infected animals (cattle, sheep, goats); contaminated animal products (hides, wool, bone meal)
organisms.notes: Do NOT use cephalosporins (intrinsically resistant due to inducible beta-lactamases). Penicillin historically used but resistance reported. Post-exposure prophylaxis: ciprofloxacin or doxycycline × 60 days + anthrax vaccine.

presentations.symptoms: Painless papule → vesicle → black eschar (malignant pustule) with surrounding edema; regional lymphadenopathy
presentations.clues: Most common form (95%); painless black eschar is characteristic; mortality 1% with treatment

presentations.symptoms: Biphasic: initial flu-like prodrome (fever, malaise, myalgias) → abrupt deterioration with dyspnea, stridor, shock, mediastinal widening on CXR (hemorrhagic mediastinitis)
presentations.clues: Widened mediastinum on CXR is classic; hemorrhagic pleural effusions; mortality >45% even with treatment

presentations.symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, ascites
presentations.clues: From ingesting contaminated meat; rare

presentations.symptoms: Severe soft tissue infection, deep abscess without eschar
presentations.clues: Injection drug users; described in Europe

coverages.note: first-line (all forms, empiric) — 400 mg IV q12h (systemic) or 500 mg PO BID (cutaneous); part of combination therapy for systemic disease
first-line (inhalation/systemic) — Triple combination for systemic anthrax; linezolid inhibits toxin production
alternative — 100 mg IV/PO BID; for cutaneous or as part of combination
adjunct (inhalation) — Monoclonal antibodies against protective antigen; FDA-approved for inhalation anthrax

organisms.name: Neisseria meningitidis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative diplococci (kidney-bean shaped); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive; maltose-fermenting (distinguishes from N. gonorrhoeae which ferments glucose only); encapsulated (polysaccharide capsule — 13 serogroups; A, B, C, W, Y cause most disease)
organisms.features: Polysaccharide capsule (major virulence factor); IgA protease; pili (adhesion); lipooligosaccharide/LOS (endotoxin — triggers DIC, purpura); complement deficiency (C5–C9/terminal complement) predisposes to recurrent meningococcal disease; asplenia increases risk; vaccines: MenACWY (conjugate) and MenB (protein-based)
organisms.source: Human nasopharynx (5–10% carriage); respiratory droplets; close contact

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status; petechial/purpuric rash (in ~50%)
presentations.clues: Second most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults; CSF: high WBC (PMN), high protein, low glucose

presentations.symptoms: Fever, petechial/purpuric rash (rapidly progressive), hypotension, DIC, Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage → acute adrenal crisis)
presentations.clues: Fulminant; can progress from well to dead in hours; purpura fulminans

presentations.symptoms: Shock, DIC, bilateral adrenal hemorrhage, purpura fulminans
presentations.clues: Pathognomonic for meningococcal sepsis (but can occur with other organisms)

coverages.note: first-line (meningitis/meningococcemia) — 2 g IV q12h; empiric for bacterial meningitis
alternative (if susceptible) — 4 million U IV q4h; increasing resistance reported
essential — Rifampin 600 mg PO q12h × 2 days, OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO × 1 dose (preferred in adults), OR ceftriaxone 250 mg IM × 1 dose (preferred in pregnancy)
prophylaxis — MenACWY at age 11–12 with booster at 16; MenB for high-risk groups and outbreaks

organisms.name: Neisseria gonorrhoeae
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative diplococci (kidney-bean shaped); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive; ferments glucose ONLY (not maltose — distinguishes from N. meningitidis); grows on Thayer-Martin (chocolate agar with antibiotics); intracellular within PMNs on Gram stain
organisms.features: Pili (antigenic variation — evades immune response); Opa proteins (adhesion); IgA protease; LOS (endotoxin); NO polysaccharide capsule; NO vaccine available; remarkable ability to develop antibiotic resistance; second most common notifiable STI in US
organisms.source: Human genital/rectal/pharyngeal mucosa; sexual transmission; vertical transmission (ophthalmia neonatorum)
organisms.notes: Azithromycin monotherapy NO LONGER recommended (rising resistance >5%). Fluoroquinolones NOT recommended (>35% resistance). Test-of-cure for pharyngeal infections. Treat all partners within 60 days.

presentations.symptoms: Purulent urethral discharge, dysuria
presentations.clues: Symptomatic in ~90% of males; Gram stain shows intracellular GN diplococci within PMNs

presentations.symptoms: Mucopurulent cervical discharge, intermenstrual bleeding; often ASYMPTOMATIC (up to 50%)
presentations.clues: Asymptomatic carriage common; can ascend to PID

presentations.symptoms: Lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, fever
presentations.clues: Complication of untreated cervicitis; can cause infertility, ectopic pregnancy

presentations.symptoms: Triad: migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, dermatitis (painless pustules on distal extremities); may progress to septic arthritis (usually monoarticular — knee)
presentations.clues: More common in females; complement deficiency predisposes

presentations.symptoms: Bilateral purulent conjunctivitis, eyelid edema
presentations.clues: Can cause corneal perforation and blindness; prevented by erythromycin eye ointment at birth

presentations.symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; sore throat
presentations.clues: Harder to eradicate; test-of-cure recommended

coverages.note: first-line (all sites) — Single dose; 1 g if ≥150 kg; add doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days if chlamydia not excluded

coverages.medication: Gentamicin 240 mg IM + azithromycin 2 g PO
coverages.note: alternative (cephalosporin allergy) — Single dose of each
alternative (if ceftriaxone unavailable) — Single dose; NOT recommended for pharyngeal infection
first-line (DGI) — For 7 days; transition to PO cefixime after improvement

organisms.name: Moraxella catarrhalis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative diplococci (kidney-bean shaped — resembles Neisseria); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive; DNase-positive; does NOT ferment sugars (distinguishes from Neisseria)
organisms.features: Nearly 100% produce beta-lactamase (BRO-1 and BRO-2); common cause of otitis media, sinusitis, and COPD exacerbations; third most common cause of otitis media (after S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae)
organisms.source: Human upper respiratory tract (normal flora in children)
organisms.notes: Amoxicillin ALONE is ineffective (~100% beta-lactamase production). However, amoxicillin remains first-line for AOM because most M. catarrhalis AOM resolves spontaneously.

presentations.symptoms: Ear pain, fever, bulging TM
presentations.clues: Third most common cause in children (after S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae)

presentations.symptoms: Facial pain, purulent nasal discharge
presentations.clues: Common bacterial cause

presentations.symptoms: Increased dyspnea, sputum purulence, sputum volume
presentations.clues: Second most common bacterial cause of COPD exacerbation

presentations.symptoms: Cough, fever, dyspnea
presentations.clues: Usually in elderly or immunocompromised

coverages.note: first-line — Nearly all strains produce beta-lactamase → amoxicillin alone is ineffective
alternative — Cefuroxime, cefdinir, ceftriaxone
alternative — Good activity
alternative — Good activity
alternative — Reserve for resistant cases

organisms.name: Escherichia coli
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; lactose-fermenting (pink colonies on MacConkey agar); motile; catalase-positive; oxidase-negative; most common gram-negative rod in clinical specimens
organisms.features: Multiple pathotypes — EHEC/STEC (Shiga toxin-producing; O157:H7 most common serotype), ETEC (heat-labile LT and heat-stable ST enterotoxins — traveler's diarrhea), EPEC (attaching/effacing — pediatric diarrhea), EIEC (invasion — dysentery), EAEC (aggregative adherence — persistent diarrhea); also causes UTI (most common cause), neonatal meningitis (K1 capsule), bacteremia, intra-abdominal infections
organisms.source: Human/animal GI tract; contaminated food (undercooked beef — EHEC; contaminated water — ETEC); fecal-oral
organisms.notes: ESBL-producing E. coli increasing — carbapenems for ESBL infections. Check local antibiograms for empiric UTI therapy.

presentations.symptoms: Bloody diarrhea (hemorrhagic colitis), severe abdominal cramps, NO fever typically; HUS develops in 5–15% (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI)
presentations.clues: O157:H7 does NOT ferment sorbitol (sorbitol-MacConkey agar); HUS most common in children 5 yrs; Shiga toxin damages endothelium

presentations.symptoms: Watery diarrhea (no blood), abdominal cramps, nausea; NO fever
presentations.clues: Most common cause of traveler's diarrhea; LT (cholera-like, activates adenylate cyclase) and ST (activates guanylate cyclase)

presentations.symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, urgency; flank pain and fever (pyelonephritis)
presentations.clues: Most common cause of UTI (75–95% of uncomplicated UTI); uropathogenic E. coli (UPEC)

presentations.symptoms: Fever, irritability, bulging fontanelle, seizures
presentations.clues: K1 capsular antigen; second most common cause of neonatal meningitis (after GBS)

coverages.note: first-line — Supportive care and hydration; antibiotics may INCREASE risk of HUS (Shiga toxin release from bacterial lysis); avoid anti-motility agents
first-line — For moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea; rifaximin for non-dysenteric TD
first-line (uncomplicated cystitis) — Per local resistance patterns
first-line (pyelonephritis) — Fluoroquinolone or 3rd-gen cephalosporin

coverages.medication: Neonatal meningitis — ceftriaxone or cefotaxime + ampicillin
coverages.note: first-line — Empiric neonatal meningitis coverage

organisms.name: Klebsiella pneumoniae
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; lactose-fermenting; NON-motile; heavily encapsulated (thick mucoid polysaccharide capsule — "mucoid" colonies); oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Thick capsule (anti-phagocytic — major virulence factor); mucoid colonies; common cause of healthcare-associated infections (UTI, pneumonia, bacteremia); classic association with "currant jelly" sputum in alcoholics/debilitated patients; increasing ESBL and carbapenemase (KPC) production; hypervirulent strains (hvKP — community-acquired liver abscess, especially in East Asia)
organisms.source: Human GI tract; healthcare environments
organisms.notes: Intrinsically resistant to ampicillin (chromosomal beta-lactamase). ESBL rates increasing globally. KPC (Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase) is the most common carbapenemase in US. Hypervirulent strains may be susceptible to standard antibiotics but cause severe invasive disease.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, productive cough with thick "currant jelly" sputum (bloody, mucoid), dyspnea
presentations.clues: Classic: upper lobe cavitary pneumonia in alcoholics/debilitated; "bulging fissure" sign on CXR

presentations.symptoms: Dysuria, frequency
presentations.clues: Common cause of healthcare-associated UTI; often catheter-associated

presentations.symptoms: Fever, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly
presentations.clues: Hypervirulent K. pneumoniae (hvKP); associated with diabetes; can metastasize to eyes (endophthalmitis) and CNS

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills, hypotension
presentations.clues: Healthcare-associated; high mortality with carbapenem-resistant strains

coverages.note: first-line (susceptible) — For community-acquired infections
first-line (healthcare-associated, susceptible) — Empiric for healthcare-associated infections
first-line (ESBL-producing) — For ESBL-producing strains
first-line (KPC-producing/CRE) — For carbapenemase-producing Enterobacterales (KPC)
alternative (KPC-producing) — For KPC-producing strains

organisms.name: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; obligate aerobe; NON-lactose-fermenting (colorless on MacConkey); oxidase-positive; motile (single polar flagellum); produces blue-green pigment (pyocyanin — blue; pyoverdin/fluorescein — yellow-green); grape-like/fruity odor; grows at 42°C
organisms.features: Intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics (efflux pumps, porin mutations, AmpC beta-lactamase); biofilm formation (CF lungs, prosthetic devices); exotoxin A (inhibits EF-2 like diphtheria toxin); elastase, phospholipase C; most common cause of nosocomial pneumonia; common in CF, burns, neutropenic patients
organisms.source: Water, soil, hospital environments (sinks, ventilators, humidifiers); moist environments
organisms.notes: Always prefer non-carbapenem beta-lactams over carbapenems when susceptible (carbapenem-sparing strategy reduces resistance emergence). Extended-infusion dosing improves pharmacodynamic target attainment. IDSA does not routinely recommend combination therapy for susceptible Pseudomonas. For DTR isolates, ceftolozane-tazobactam or ceftazidime-avibactam are preferred first; cefiderocol is preferred if MBL-producing. Imipenem (not meropenem) has activity with relebactam. Ertapenem has NO antipseudomonal activity.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, purulent sputum, new infiltrate
presentations.clues: Most common cause of VAP; high mortality (30–40%)

presentations.symptoms: Wound discoloration (blue-green), sepsis
presentations.clues: Leading cause of death in burn patients

presentations.symptoms: Chronic productive cough, progressive lung function decline
presentations.clues: Mucoid phenotype (alginate biofilm); chronic colonization; most common cause of morbidity/mortality in CF

presentations.symptoms: Ear pain, discharge, tragal tenderness
presentations.clues: Most common cause of otitis externa

presentations.symptoms: Severe ear pain, granulation tissue at bone-cartilage junction, cranial nerve palsies
presentations.clues: Elderly diabetics; osteomyelitis of temporal bone

coverages.note: first-line (empiric and definitive, susceptible isolates) — 4.5 g IV q6h (extended infusion over 4 hrs preferred); preferred over carbapenems when susceptible to preserve carbapenem activity
first-line (empiric and definitive, susceptible isolates) — 2 g IV q8h (extended infusion over approx 3 hrs); preferred non-carbapenem beta-lactam
first-line (empiric and definitive, susceptible isolates) — 2 g IV q8h; comparable outcomes to carbapenems and pip-tazo for bacteremia
first-line (empiric, severe infections) — 1-2 g IV q8h (extended infusion); reserve for resistant isolates or critically ill; higher risk of selecting resistance than non-carbapenem beta-lactams
first-line (empiric combination or step-down) — 400 mg IV q8h or 750 mg PO BID; often combined with antipseudomonal beta-lactam for empiric coverage
alternative (empiric combination or step-down) — 750 mg IV/PO daily; used in combination with antipseudomonal beta-lactam
alternative (combination therapy or UTI) — 5-7 mg/kg IV daily (extended-interval dosing); useful for UTI or as part of combination therapy; monitor levels and renal function
alternative (combination therapy or UTI) — 15-20 mg/kg IV daily; retains activity against some tobramycin-resistant isolates; useful for DTR Pseudomonas UTI
alternative (penicillin-allergic patients) — 2 g IV q8h; monobactam with antipseudomonal activity; safe in penicillin allergy; can be combined with avibactam for MBL-producing strains
first-line (DTR Pseudomonas) — 3 g IV q8h (for HAP/VAP); 1.5 g IV q8h (other infections); preferred for DTR Pseudomonas pneumonia; no MBL activity
first-line (DTR Pseudomonas) — 2.5 g IV q8h; comparable to ceftolozane-tazobactam for non-pneumonia infections; no MBL activity; can be combined with aztreonam for MBL producers
first-line (DTR Pseudomonas, non-MBL) — 1.25 g IV q6h; may retain activity when ceftolozane-tazobactam and ceftazidime-avibactam are resistant; no MBL activity

first-line (DTR Pseudomonas, including MBL producers) — 2 g IV q8h (3-hr infusion); siderophore cephalosporin; preferred agent when metallo-beta-lactamase (MBL) is present; active against nearly all Pseudomonas
last resort (pan-resistant Pseudomonas) — Colistin: 5 mg/kg IV loading then 2.5 mg/kg IV q12h; Polymyxin B: 1.25-1.5 mg/kg IV q12h; significant nephrotoxicity; use only when no other options

organisms.name: Proteus mirabilis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; NON-lactose-fermenting; highly motile ("swarming" motility on agar — concentric rings); urease-positive (splits urea → ammonia → alkaline urine → struvite stones); oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Urease production (major virulence factor — alkalinizes urine → magnesium ammonium phosphate/struvite stones); swarming motility; common cause of complicated UTI (especially catheter-associated); staghorn calculi; wound infections
organisms.source: Human GI tract; soil; water; healthcare environments
organisms.notes: Intrinsically resistant to nitrofurantoin and tetracyclines. P. vulgaris and P. penneri are intrinsically resistant to ampicillin (unlike P. mirabilis). Struvite stones require complete surgical removal — antibiotics alone insufficient.

presentations.symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, foul-smelling alkaline urine, flank pain
presentations.clues: Second most common cause of complicated UTI; urease → alkaline urine (pH >7) → struvite/staghorn stones

presentations.symptoms: Flank pain, recurrent UTI, staghorn calculi on imaging
presentations.clues: Alkaline urine + struvite stones = think Proteus; requires stone removal + antibiotics

presentations.symptoms: Purulent drainage, cellulitis
presentations.clues: Often polymicrobial

coverages.note: first-line (susceptible) — Most P. mirabilis susceptible (unlike other Proteus spp.)
first-line (UTI) — Good activity against P. mirabilis
alternative — Good activity
alternative — Ceftriaxone for complicated infections

organisms.name: Enterobacter spp. (E. cloacae, E. aerogenes/Klebsiella aerogenes)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; lactose-fermenting; motile; oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Chromosomal AmpC beta-lactamase (inducible — can be derepressed during therapy with 3rd-gen cephalosporins → resistance emergence); ESKAPE pathogen; common cause of healthcare-associated infections (pneumonia, UTI, bacteremia, wound infections); "SPACE" organisms (Serratia, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Citrobacter, Enterobacter) — all have inducible AmpC
organisms.source: Human GI tract; healthcare environments
organisms.notes: Do NOT use 3rd-gen cephalosporins (ceftriaxone, ceftazidime) even if initially susceptible — inducible AmpC beta-lactamase can be derepressed during therapy → treatment failure ("SPACE bugs" or "AmpC trap"). Cefepime is stable to AmpC. Piperacillin-tazobactam controversial — some guidelines recommend against for serious Enterobacter infections.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, cough, new infiltrate
presentations.clues: Often ventilator-associated

presentations.symptoms: Dysuria, frequency
presentations.clues: Often catheter-associated

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills
presentations.clues: Often line-related or biliary/GU source

presentations.symptoms: Purulent drainage, erythema
presentations.clues: Often polymicrobial

coverages.note: first-line — Stable to AmpC beta-lactamase (unlike ceftriaxone/ceftazidime)
first-line (severe/ESBL) — For severe infections or ESBL-producing strains
alternative — If susceptible
alternative (UTI) — If susceptible

organisms.name: Salmonella enterica
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; NON-lactose-fermenting; motile (peritrichous flagella); produces H₂S on TSI (black colonies on Hektoen/XLD agar); oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Two major clinical groups: (1) Typhoidal — S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi (enteric/typhoid fever; human-restricted); (2) Non-typhoidal (NTS) — S. Enteritidis, S. Typhimurium (gastroenteritis; animal reservoir); intracellular pathogen (survives in macrophages); sickle cell disease predisposes to Salmonella osteomyelitis
organisms.source: Typhoidal: humans only (fecal-oral, contaminated water); NTS: poultry, eggs, reptiles, contaminated food
organisms.notes: Increasing multidrug resistance globally. Fluoroquinolone resistance >85% in South Asian S. Typhi. XDR typhoid (resistant to ampicillin, chloramphenicol, TMP-SMX, fluoroquinolones, ceftriaxone) emerging from Pakistan. Always guide therapy by susceptibilities.

presentations.symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, watery or bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps, fever
presentations.clues: Most common Salmonella presentation; usually self-limited (4–7 days); antibiotics NOT recommended for uncomplicated cases (may prolong carriage)

presentations.symptoms: Gradual onset fever (stepwise/staircase pattern), headache, malaise, relative bradycardia, rose spots (salmon-colored blanching macules on trunk), hepatosplenomegaly, constipation (more common than diarrhea in adults)
presentations.clues: Complications in week 2–3: intestinal perforation, hemorrhage; blood culture sensitivity ~60%; bone marrow culture gold standard (~80%)

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills
presentations.clues: More common in immunocompromised (HIV with low CD4, sickle cell, extremes of age); NTS bacteremia in sub-Saharan Africa

presentations.symptoms: Bone pain, fever
presentations.clues: Classic association with sickle cell disease (Salmonella is most common cause of osteomyelitis in SCD)

presentations.symptoms: Asymptomatic shedding
presentations.clues: ~1–4% of typhoid patients; associated with gallstones; "Typhoid Mary"

coverages.note: first-line — Antibiotics NOT recommended for uncomplicated NTS gastroenteritis (may prolong carriage); treat only if: age 50, immunocompromised, prosthetic implants, bacteremia

coverages.medication: NTS (when treatment indicated) — fluoroquinolone or azithromycin
coverages.note: first-line — Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 5–7 days; azithromycin alternative

first-line — Azithromycin 1 g day 1 then 500 mg × 5–7 days (uncomplicated); ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily × 10–14 days (complicated); ciprofloxacin if susceptible (NOT for South Asian origin — high resistance)
first-line — Azithromycin for uncomplicated; meropenem for complicated XDR typhoid

organisms.name: Shigella spp. (S. dysenteriae, S. flexneri, S. boydii, S. sonnei)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; facultative anaerobe; NON-lactose-fermenting; NON-motile (distinguishes from Salmonella); does NOT produce H₂S; oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Very low infectious dose (~10–100 organisms — highly contagious); invades colonic epithelium (intracellular pathogen); S. dysenteriae type 1 produces Shiga toxin (can cause HUS); S. sonnei most common in US; S. flexneri most common worldwide; person-to-person transmission; increasing multidrug resistance including XDR strains
organisms.source: Humans only (no animal reservoir); fecal-oral; contaminated food/water; sexual transmission (MSM)
organisms.notes: Antibiotics shorten illness by 1–2 days and reduce transmission. Increasing XDR Shigella in US, especially among MSM. Always obtain susceptibilities. Do NOT use anti-motility agents in dysentery.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, abdominal cramps, tenesmus, bloody mucoid diarrhea (dysentery); initially watery then bloody
presentations.clues: Small volume, frequent bloody stools with mucus; fecal leukocytes; left lower quadrant tenderness; self-limited in 5–7 days in immunocompetent

presentations.symptoms: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI
presentations.clues: Shiga toxin-mediated; similar to EHEC-HUS

presentations.symptoms: Asymmetric oligoarthritis, conjunctivitis, urethritis
presentations.clues: HLA-B27 associated; post-infectious

coverages.note: first-line (if susceptible) — 500 mg BID × 3–5 days; BUT ~60% resistance in US (2023); check MIC
alternative — 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg × 4 days; ~34% resistance in US; not for bacteremia
first-line (severe/parenteral) — 1–2 g IV daily; for severe disease or bacteremia
alternative (if susceptible) — Widespread resistance limits use
first-line (XDR Shigella) — For extensively drug-resistant strains (resistant to azithromycin + ciprofloxacin + ceftriaxone + TMP-SMX + ampicillin)

organisms.name: Campylobacter jejuni
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative curved/spiral rod ("gull-wing" or "S-shaped"); microaerophilic (grows in 5% O₂, 10% CO₂); motile (single polar flagellum — darting motility); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive; grows at 42°C (thermophilic — selective advantage)
organisms.features: Most common bacterial cause of gastroenteritis in developed countries; associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome (molecular mimicry — lipo-oligosaccharide resembles gangliosides GM1/GD1a); also associated with reactive arthritis; low infectious dose (~500 organisms)
organisms.source: Poultry (most common — undercooked chicken), unpasteurized milk, contaminated water; animal reservoir (birds, cattle, dogs)
organisms.notes: Antibiotics shorten illness by 1–2 days if given early. Fluoroquinolone resistance increasing (~29% in US). Erythromycin historically used but azithromycin preferred (better tolerated). Do NOT use antibiotics empirically for bloody diarrhea without considering EHEC (antibiotics contraindicated in EHEC).

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome of fever, headache, myalgias → bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps (can mimic appendicitis — RLQ pain), fever
presentations.clues: Most common bacterial cause of diarrhea in US; bloody diarrhea common; usually self-limited (5–7 days)

presentations.symptoms: Ascending symmetric flaccid paralysis, areflexia, albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF
presentations.clues: Most common infectious trigger of GBS (~30% of cases); molecular mimicry with gangliosides

presentations.symptoms: Asymmetric oligoarthritis
presentations.clues: HLA-B27 associated

coverages.note: first-line — 500 mg daily × 3 days; treat only moderate-severe disease or high-risk patients; most cases self-limited
alternative — 500 mg BID × 5–7 days; BUT ~29% resistance in US; NOT first-line
first-line (mild) — Most cases self-limited; antibiotics only if severe, prolonged, or immunocompromised

organisms.name: Vibrio cholerae
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative curved rod ("comma-shaped"); oxidase-positive; motile (single polar flagellum); grows on TCBS agar (yellow colonies — sucrose-fermenting); halophilic (salt-tolerant but can grow without NaCl — unlike other Vibrio spp.)
organisms.features: Cholera toxin (AB₅ toxin — B subunit binds GM1 ganglioside; A subunit ADP-ribosylates Gs alpha → permanently activates adenylate cyclase → ↑cAMP → massive Cl⁻ and water secretion into intestinal lumen); serogroups O1 (El Tor biotype — current pandemic) and O139 cause epidemic cholera; "rice-water" stool; can lose up to 1 L/hr of fluid
organisms.source: Contaminated water and food; human fecal-oral; endemic in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, Haiti

presentations.symptoms: Sudden onset profuse watery diarrhea ("rice-water" stools — gray, slightly fishy odor, flecks of mucus), vomiting, rapid severe dehydration, muscle cramps, hypovolemic shock
presentations.clues: Can lose 10–20 L/day; death from dehydration within hours if untreated; 75% of infections are asymptomatic or mild
presentations.severity: Mild to life-threatening (mortality 1% with treatment; up to 50% without)

coverages.note: first-line — WHO-ORS solution; cornerstone of treatment; has reduced mortality from ~50% to 1%
first-line (severe dehydration) — Aggressive IV rehydration for severe cases
first-line (antibiotic, adults) — 300 mg single dose; shortens diarrhea duration by ~50% and reduces fluid loss; recommended for moderate-severe disease
first-line (antibiotic, children/pregnant) — 1 g single dose (adults); 20 mg/kg (children); preferred in pregnancy and children
alternative — 1 g single dose; resistance increasing
adjunct (children 5) — Reduces duration and severity
prophylaxis — 2 doses; protection for 2–3 years

organisms.name: Vibrio parahaemolyticus
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative curved rod; oxidase-positive; motile; halophilic (requires NaCl for growth — marine organism); grows on TCBS agar (green colonies — does NOT ferment sucrose)
organisms.features: Kanagawa phenomenon positive (beta-hemolysis on Wagatsuma agar — thermostable direct hemolysin/TDH); most common cause of Vibrio gastroenteritis worldwide; associated with raw/undercooked SEAFOOD (especially oysters, shellfish)
organisms.source: Marine/estuarine environments; raw/undercooked seafood (oysters, shrimp, crab)

presentations.symptoms: Watery diarrhea (occasionally bloody), abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, fever
presentations.clues: Associated with raw seafood consumption; usually self-limited (2–3 days)

presentations.symptoms: Cellulitis from seawater exposure
presentations.clues: Less severe than V. vulnificus wound infections

coverages.note: first-line — Self-limited; antibiotics not usually needed
first-line (severe) — For severe gastroenteritis or wound infections

organisms.name: Vibrio vulnificus
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative curved rod; oxidase-positive; halophilic; lactose-fermenting (unusual for Vibrio)
organisms.features: Most lethal foodborne pathogen in US (mortality 50% for septicemia); encapsulated; produces metalloprotease, cytolysin; risk factors: liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis), iron overload (hemochromatosis), immunosuppression, diabetes
organisms.source: Warm coastal waters (Gulf of Mexico); raw oysters

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills, hypotension, hemorrhagic bullae on extremities
presentations.clues: Occurs in patients with liver disease/iron overload; mortality ~50%

presentations.symptoms: Rapidly progressive cellulitis, hemorrhagic bullae, necrotizing fasciitis
presentations.clues: Can progress to sepsis; may require amputation

coverages.note: first-line — Combination therapy essential; doxycycline 100 mg IV BID + ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV daily × ≥2 weeks; combination associated with 90–100% survival vs 20–50% with cephalosporin alone
alternative — If cephalosporin unavailable
essential — Early surgical consult; may require fasciotomy or amputation

organisms.name: Helicobacter pylori
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative curved/spiral rod; microaerophilic; motile (multiple polar flagella — corkscrew motility through gastric mucus); urease-positive (major virulence factor); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive
organisms.features: Urease (hydrolyzes urea → ammonia → neutralizes gastric acid → creates alkaline microenvironment for survival); CagA (cytotoxin-associated gene A — oncogene; injected via type IV secretion system → inflammation, cell proliferation); VacA (vacuolating cytotoxin); colonizes gastric epithelium; infects ~50% of world population; WHO Group 1 carcinogen (gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma)
organisms.source: Human stomach; person-to-person (oral-oral, fecal-oral); contaminated water
organisms.notes: Test-and-treat strategy for dyspepsia in patients 60 without alarm features. Confirm eradication ≥4 weeks after completing therapy (urea breath test or stool antigen — NOT serology). Clarithromycin resistance ~20–30% in US → bismuth quadruple therapy increasingly preferred as first-line. Doxycycline is NOT equivalent to tetracycline for H. pylori.

presentations.symptoms: Often asymptomatic; epigastric pain, dyspepsia
presentations.clues: Most infected individuals have chronic active gastritis; only 10–20% develop clinical disease

presentations.symptoms: Epigastric pain, nausea; duodenal ulcer (pain relieved by eating) vs gastric ulcer (pain worsened by eating)
presentations.clues: Causes ~60–70% of gastric ulcers and ~90% of duodenal ulcers

presentations.symptoms: Weight loss, early satiety, GI bleeding
presentations.clues: 1–3% lifetime risk in infected individuals; intestinal-type adenocarcinoma; CagA+ strains higher risk

presentations.symptoms: Dyspepsia, GI bleeding
presentations.clues: Low-grade B-cell lymphoma; 60–80% regress with H. pylori eradication alone

coverages.note: first-line (if clarithromycin resistance 15%) — PPI + clarithromycin 500 mg BID + amoxicillin 1 g BID × 14 days
first-line (if clarithromycin resistance >15% or prior macrolide exposure) — PPI + bismuth subsalicylate + metronidazole 500 mg TID-QID + tetracycline 500 mg QID × 10–14 days
alternative — PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin + metronidazole × 10–14 days
second-line/salvage — PPI + levofloxacin 500 mg daily + amoxicillin 1 g BID × 10–14 days
salvage — PPI + rifabutin + amoxicillin × 14 days; reserve for multiply-failed regimens

organisms.name: Haemophilus influenzae
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus (pleomorphic); facultative anaerobe; requires factors X (hemin) and V (NAD) for growth (grows on chocolate agar but NOT blood agar — unless satelliting around S. aureus); oxidase-positive; catalase-positive
organisms.features: Encapsulated (6 serotypes a–f; type b/Hib most virulent — polysaccharide capsule) and non-typeable (NTHi — unencapsulated; most common cause of disease in post-vaccine era); Hib vaccine dramatically reduced invasive Hib disease (>99% reduction); NTHi causes otitis media, sinusitis, COPD exacerbations, pneumonia; IgA protease
organisms.source: Human nasopharynx (NTHi carriage common); respiratory droplets
organisms.notes: ~25–30% produce beta-lactamase (TEM-1) → resistant to ampicillin/amoxicillin. Beta-lactamase-negative ampicillin-resistant (BLNAR) strains increasing (PBP3 mutations) — may be resistant to amoxicillin-clavulanate and some cephalosporins. Hib vaccine is one of the most successful vaccines in history.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status
presentations.clues: Was most common cause of bacterial meningitis in children pre-vaccine; now rare in vaccinated populations; mortality 3–6%

presentations.symptoms: Acute onset sore throat, drooling, stridor, "tripod" positioning, "hot potato" voice, high fever
presentations.clues: Pediatric emergency (now rare due to Hib vaccine); lateral neck X-ray: "thumbprint sign"; do NOT examine throat (risk of complete obstruction)

presentations.symptoms: Ear pain, fever, bulging TM
presentations.clues: Second most common cause of AOM (after S. pneumoniae); NTHi increasingly common

presentations.symptoms: Facial pain, purulent nasal discharge
presentations.clues: Common bacterial cause

presentations.symptoms: Increased dyspnea, sputum purulence
presentations.clues: Common cause of acute exacerbation

presentations.symptoms: Cough, fever, dyspnea
presentations.clues: NTHi in adults with COPD/chronic lung disease; Hib in unvaccinated children

coverages.note: first-line (AOM, sinusitis) — ~25–30% produce beta-lactamase → amoxicillin alone may fail; high-dose amoxicillin (90 mg/kg/day) still first-line for AOM (most NTHi AOM resolves spontaneously)
first-line (meningitis, invasive) — 2 g IV q12h (meningitis); 3rd-gen cephalosporins are standard for invasive disease
first-line (susceptible, non-meningeal) — If beta-lactamase negative
alternative — For respiratory infections in penicillin-allergic patients
alternative — Levofloxacin, moxifloxacin for adults
prophylaxis — For household contacts of invasive Hib disease (if unvaccinated children 4 yrs in household)

organisms.name: Bordetella pertussis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus (small); strict aerobe; non-motile; grows on Bordet-Gengou agar (potato-glycerol-blood agar) or Regan-Lowe agar; slow-growing (3–7 days); "mercury drop" colonies
organisms.features: Pertussis toxin (PT — ADP-ribosylates Gi protein → ↑cAMP; causes lymphocytosis by preventing lymphocyte migration from blood to lymph nodes); filamentous hemagglutinin (FHA — adhesion); tracheal cytotoxin (damages ciliated epithelium); adenylate cyclase toxin; pertactin; highly contagious (attack rate 80–90% in household contacts); vaccine-preventable (DTaP/Tdap)
organisms.source: Humans only (no animal reservoir); respiratory droplets; most contagious during catarrhal phase
organisms.notes: Antibiotics are primarily given to reduce transmission, NOT to improve symptoms (a Cochrane review found no significant reduction in symptom duration). Treatment is most beneficial if started during the catarrhal phase (first 1-2 weeks). Treat all patients diagnosed within 3 weeks of cough onset. Post-exposure prophylaxis with the same regimens is recommended for all close contacts, especially households with infants or pregnant women. Macrolide resistance has emerged in some regions - TMP-SMX may be considered.

presentations.symptoms: THREE PHASES: (1) Catarrhal (1–2 weeks): rhinorrhea, mild cough, low-grade fever — most contagious, indistinguishable from URI; (2) Paroxysmal (2–

coverages.note: first-line (treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis) — Adults: 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg PO days 2-5 (5-day course) OR 500 mg PO daily x 3 days; Children: 10 mg/kg PO day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2-5; Infants under 1 month: 10 mg/kg PO daily x 5 days (preferred macrolide for neonates - erythromycin associated with pyloric stenosis)
first-line (alternative macrolide) — Adults: 500 mg PO BID x 7 days; Children over 1 month: 7.5 mg/kg PO BID x 7 days; NOT recommended for infants under 1 month

alternative (older macrolide) — Adults: 500 mg PO QID x 14 days; effective but poorly tolerated (frequent GI side effects, QID dosing); associated with infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis in neonates - avoid in infants under 1 month

alternative (macrolide-intolerant or macrolide-resistant) — Adults: 1 DS tablet (160/800 mg) PO BID x 14 days; Children over 2 months: TMP 8 mg/kg/day divided BID x 14 days; contraindicated in infants under 2 months and in pregnancy (near term)

organisms.name: Legionella pneumophila
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod (poorly staining on Gram stain — use silver stain/Dieterle); facultative intracellular pathogen; grows on buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar with iron and L-cysteine (requires both for growth); does NOT grow on standard media; oxidase-positive; catalase-positive
organisms.features: Survives and replicates within alveolar macrophages; transmitted via aerosolized water (NOT person-to-person); associated with contaminated water systems (cooling towers, hot water tanks, showers, fountains, hospital water supplies); risk factors: age >50, smoking, chronic lung disease, immunosuppression (especially corticosteroids/transplant), diabetes; causes ~2–9% of CAP; urine antigen test detects serogroup 1 only (~70–80% of cases)
organisms.source: Freshwater environments; man-made water systems (cooling towers, hot tubs, plumbing); NOT transmitted person-to-person
organisms.notes: Beta-lactams and aminoglycosides are INEFFECTIVE (intracellular pathogen). Empiric CAP regimens should include atypical coverage (macrolide or fluoroquinolone) to cover Legionella. Diagnosis: urine antigen (rapid but only detects serogroup 1), culture on BCYE (gold standard), PCR. No person-to-person transmission → no isolation needed.

presentations.symptoms: High fever (often >40°C), cough (initially dry → productive), dyspnea, GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting — present in ~20–40%), headache, confusion/encephalopathy, relative bradycardia
presentations.clues: Atypical pneumonia with systemic features; hyponatremia (SIADH); elevated LFTs; CXR: rapidly progressive infiltrates (often multilobar); can mimic severe CAP

presentations.symptoms: Self-limited flu-like illness (fever, myalgias, headache); NO pneumonia
presentations.clues: Self-limited within 2–5 days; no treatment needed

coverages.note: first-line — 500 mg IV/PO daily × 3–5 days (may extend to 10 days for severe/immunocompromised)
first-line — 750 mg IV/PO daily × 5–10 days; some guidelines prefer for severe disease; preferred in transplant patients (fewer drug interactions than macrolides)
alternative — 100 mg BID; good intracellular penetration

organisms.name: Yersinia enterocolitica
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod (coccobacillus); facultative anaerobe; NON-lactose-fermenting; motile at 25°C (not at 37°C); grows at 4°C (cold enrichment — like Listeria); oxidase-negative; urease-positive
organisms.features: Type III secretion system (T3SS — injects Yop effector proteins into host cells); invades Peyer's patches and mesenteric lymph nodes; causes mesenteric adenitis that mimics appendicitis (pseudoappendicitis); associated with iron overload states (hemochromatosis, deferoxamine therapy, sickle cell — iron enhances virulence); can contaminate blood products (grows at 4°C → transfusion-associated sepsis)
organisms.source: Pigs (primary reservoir — raw/undercooked pork); unpasteurized milk; contaminated water; blood products
organisms.notes: Intrinsically resistant to penicillins and 1st-gen cephalosporins (chromosomal beta-lactamases). Stool shedding can persist for weeks after acute illness. Transfusion-associated Yersinia sepsis is a recognized complication of stored RBC products.

presentations.symptoms: Diarrhea (watery or bloody), abdominal pain (often RLQ), fever; duration 1–3 weeks (longer than most bacterial gastroenteritis)
presentations.clues: Children: enterocolitis predominates; adults: mesenteric adenitis/pseudoappendicitis predominates; pharyngitis can be accompanying symptom (diagnostic clue)

presentations.symptoms: RLQ pain, fever, vomiting, elevated WBC
presentations.clues: Mimics acute appendicitis; surgery reveals inflamed terminal ileum and mesenteric lymph nodes with normal appendix; more common in adolescents/young adults

presentations.symptoms: Asymmetric oligoarthritis, usually large joints
presentations.clues: HLA-B27 associated; self-limiting; erythema nodosum also occurs

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills, shock
presentations.clues: Iron overload states (hemochromatosis, deferoxamine, sickle cell); transfusion-associated (contaminated blood products stored at 4°C)

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated) — Most cases self-limited; antibiotics NOT indicated for uncomplicated enterocolitis
first-line (severe/complicated) — 500 mg BID; for septicemia, immunocompromised, or severe disease
alternative — For mild-moderate cases requiring treatment
first-line (septicemia) — IV; for bacteremia/sepsis
alternative (septicemia) — For severe systemic infections

organisms.name: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
organisms.morphology: NO cell wall (smallest free-living organism); pleomorphic; cannot be Gram-stained; does NOT grow on standard media (requires enriched media with sterols); facultative anaerobe
organisms.features: No cell wall → intrinsically resistant to ALL beta-lactams and vancomycin; attaches to respiratory epithelium via P1 adhesin protein; causes "walking pneumonia" (atypical CAP); most common cause of CAP in children 5–18 years and young adults; epidemic waves every 3–7 years; can cause extrapulmonary manifestations (erythema multiforme, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, hemolytic anemia — cold agglutinins IgM, neurologic — encephalitis, transverse myelitis, GBS)
organisms.source: Human respiratory tract; respiratory droplets; close contact (schools, military barracks, dormitories)
organisms.notes: Beta-lactams are INEFFECTIVE (no cell wall). Most cases are self-limited even without treatment. Macrolide resistance increasing globally. Doxycycline or fluoroquinolones for macrolide-resistant strains. Post-COVID resurgence of M. pneumoniae observed globally in 2023–2024.

presentations.symptoms: Gradual onset; dry/hacking cough (most prominent symptom), low-grade fever, headache, malaise, sore throat; CXR often worse than clinical appearance (diffuse interstitial/patchy infiltrates)
presentations.clues: Absence of improvement after 48 hrs of beta-lactam therapy is a diagnostic clue; cold agglutinins positive in ~50%; PCR is preferred diagnostic test

presentations.symptoms: Pharyngitis, tracheobronchitis
presentations.clues: More common than pneumonia

presentations.symptoms: Erythema multiforme (most common skin manifestation), Stevens-Johnson syndrome (Mycoplasma-induced rash and mucositis/MIRM), hemolytic anemia (cold agglutinins — IgM anti-I), myocarditis, pericarditis, encephalitis, transverse myelitis, GBS, arthritis
presentations.clues: Can occur without pneumonia; autoimmune-mediated

coverages.note: first-line — 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2–5 (adults); 10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2–5 (children); macrolide resistance 0–15% in US, >90% in China
alternative (adults, children >8 yrs) — 100 mg BID × 5–7 days
alternative (adults) — For macrolide-resistant cases or macrolide-intolerant patients

organisms.name: Chlamydia trachomatis
organisms.morphology: Cannot be Gram-stained; obligate intracellular pathogen; two forms: elementary body (EB — infectious, extracellular, metabolically inert) and reticulate body (RB — intracellular, metabolically active, replicating); cannot be cultured on standard media (requires cell culture)
organisms.features: Multiple serovars with different disease associations: D–K (urogenital infections — most common), L1–L3 (lymphogranuloma venereum/LGV — invasive), A–C (trachoma — leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide); most common bacterial STI in US (~1.8 million cases/year); often asymptomatic (up to 70% in women, 50% in men)
organisms.source: Human genital/rectal/pharyngeal mucosa; sexual transmission; vertical transmission (neonatal conjunctivitis, pneumonia)
organisms.notes: Screen sexually active women 25 annually. Treat all partners within 60 days. Test-of-cure not routinely needed (except pregnancy). Doxycycline now preferred over azithromycin per 2021 CDC guidelines due to superior efficacy, especially for rectal infection.
