presentations.symptoms: Males: dysuria, mucoid/clear urethral discharge (less purulent than gonorrhea); Females: often ASYMPTOMATIC, mucopurulent cervical discharge, intermenstrual bleeding, dysuria
presentations.clues: Most common bacterial STI; often co-infection with gonorrhea; asymptomatic carriage common

presentations.symptoms: Lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever
presentations.clues: Ascending infection from cervix; can cause tubal scarring → infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain

presentations.symptoms: Stage 1: painless genital papule/ulcer; Stage 2: painful inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes — may suppurate and form fistulae; "groove sign" = inguinal and femoral nodes separated by inguinal ligament); Stage 3: chronic proctocolitis, rectal strictures, genital elephantiasis
presentations.clues: Increasingly recognized in MSM as proctitis; can mimic IBD

presentations.symptoms: Triad: arthritis + urethritis + conjunctivitis ("can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree"); asymmetric oligoarthritis
presentations.clues: HLA-B27 associated; most common cause of reactive arthritis

presentations.symptoms: Bilateral purulent conjunctivitis
presentations.clues: Most common cause of neonatal conjunctivitis; later onset than gonococcal (2–5 days)

presentations.symptoms: Staccato cough, tachypnea, NO fever, eosinophilia
presentations.clues: Afebrile pneumonia in infant; "staccato cough" is characteristic

presentations.symptoms: Chronic follicular conjunctivitis → corneal scarring → blindness
presentations.clues: Leading infectious cause of preventable blindness worldwide; endemic in developing countries

coverages.note: first-line (urogenital/rectal) — 100 mg PO BID × 7 days; superior to azithromycin especially for rectal chlamydia (cure rate 97–100% vs 71–76%)
alternative — 1 g PO × 1 dose; preferred in pregnancy; lower cure rate for rectal infection
alternative — 500 mg PO daily × 7 days

organisms.name: Chlamydophila pneumoniae (formerly Chlamydia pneumoniae)
organisms.morphology: Same biphasic lifecycle as C. trachomatis (EB/RB); obligate intracellular; cannot be cultured on standard media
organisms.features: Causes atypical pneumonia (similar to Mycoplasma); responsible for ~10% of CAP; also causes pharyngitis, bronchitis, sinusitis; associated with atherosclerosis (controversial); transmitted person-to-person via respiratory droplets (unlike C. trachomatis which is sexually transmitted); no animal reservoir
organisms.source: Human respiratory tract; respiratory droplets
organisms.notes: Beta-lactams are INEFFECTIVE (obligate intracellular). Often co-infection with S. pneumoniae. Empiric CAP regimens with macrolide or fluoroquinolone provide coverage. Diagnosis difficult — serology most common but limited utility; PCR available.

presentations.symptoms: Gradual onset; persistent dry cough, low-grade fever, hoarseness, pharyngitis, headache
presentations.clues: Often biphasic illness (pharyngitis → pneumonia); CXR: subsegmental infiltrate; milder than Legionella; often indistinguishable from Mycoplasma

presentations.symptoms: Sore throat, hoarseness, persistent cough
presentations.clues: More common than pneumonia

presentations.symptoms: Facial pain, nasal congestion
presentations.clues: Less common

coverages.note: first-line — Same dosing as for Mycoplasma pneumoniae
alternative — 100 mg BID × 10–14 days
alternative — Levofloxacin or moxifloxacin

organisms.name: Bacteroides fragilis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; obligate anaerobe; NON-spore-forming; non-motile; encapsulated (polysaccharide capsule — major virulence factor; promotes abscess formation)
organisms.features: Most common anaerobe isolated from clinical infections despite being a minor component of normal flora (90% resistant to penicillin); superoxide dismutase (aerotolerant for an anaerobe); most common cause of intra-abdominal anaerobic infections and anaerobic bacteremia
organisms.source: Human colon (normal flora); released during bowel perforation, surgery, or trauma
organisms.notes: Resistant to penicillin (>90% — beta-lactamase), aminoglycosides (all anaerobes), and most cephalosporins. Clindamycin resistance increasing (~40–60% in US) — no longer reliable empirically. Cefoxitin susceptibility declining. Metronidazole resistance remains very rare (1–4%). For serious infections, obtain anaerobic susceptibility testing.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, abdominal pain, peritonitis, abscess formation
presentations.clues: Usually polymicrobial (with E. coli and other Enterobacterales); post-surgical, perforated appendicitis, diverticulitis

presentations.symptoms: Fever, chills
presentations.clues: Often from intra-abdominal or pelvic source; mortality 20–30%

presentations.symptoms: Pelvic pain, fever, vaginal discharge
presentations.clues: Post-surgical or post-partum; tubo-ovarian abscess

presentations.symptoms: Purulent drainage, foul-smelling (anaerobic)
presentations.clues: Post-surgical; bite wounds

presentations.symptoms: Headache, focal neurologic deficits, fever
presentations.clues: Often from contiguous spread (sinusitis, otitis) or hematogenous

coverages.note: first-line — 500 mg IV/PO q8h; nearly 100% susceptibility; excellent anaerobic coverage; often combined with a cephalosporin for polymicrobial coverage
first-line — >90% susceptibility; covers both aerobes and anaerobes; may be preferred empiric monotherapy
first-line (severe) — >90% susceptibility; broadest coverage; reserve for severe infections
alternative — Good activity but susceptibility declining in some regions

organisms.name: Treponema pallidum
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative spirochete (thin, tightly coiled); too thin to be seen on Gram stain — use darkfield microscopy or immunofluorescence; cannot be cultured on artificial media; motile (endoflagella/axial filaments)
organisms.features: Outer membrane lacks LPS (unlike most gram-negatives); evades immune system by antigenic variation and limited surface protein expression; crosses placental barrier (congenital syphilis); crosses blood-brain barrier (neurosyphilis at any stage); NO penicillin resistance has EVER been documented; serologic testing: non-treponemal (RPR, VDRL — screening, follow response to treatment) and treponemal (FTA-ABS, TP-PA — confirmatory, remain positive for life)
organisms.source: Humans only; sexual contact; vertical transmission (transplacental)
organisms.notes: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction occurs in ~30% within 24 hrs of treatment (fever, rigors, myalgias — from spirochete lysis; treat with antipyretics). Pregnant patients with penicillin allergy should undergo desensitization (no alternative is adequate for fetal treatment). Follow RPR/VDRL titers to monitor response (4-fold decline expected). Screen all pregnant patients at first prenatal visit, third trimester, and delivery. Doxycycline post-exposure prophylaxis (doxy-PEP) 200 mg within 72 hrs after sex recommended for MSM/transgender women with recent STI history.

presentations.symptoms: Painless chancre (clean-based ulcer with raised, indurated borders) at site of inoculation (genital, anal, oral); painless regional lymphadenopathy
presentations.clues: Chancre is highly infectious; heals spontaneously in 3–6 weeks even without treatment

presentations.incubation: 6–8 weeks after primary (4–10 weeks after chancre heals)
presentations.symptoms: Diffuse maculopapular rash (classically involves palms and soles), condylomata lata (moist, flat, gray-white lesions in intertriginous areas — highly infectious), mucous patches (oral), generalized lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise, weight loss, patchy alopecia ("moth-eaten")
presentations.clues: Rash on palms/soles is classic; condylomata lata are pathognomonic; resolves spontaneously

presentations.symptoms: Asymptomatic; positive serology only
presentations.clues: Early latent (1 year): lower infectivity

presentations.symptoms: Gummas (granulomatous lesions of skin, bone, viscera), cardiovascular syphilis (aortitis → ascending aortic aneurysm, aortic regurgitation), late neurosyphilis
presentations.clues: Occurs in 15–40% of untreated patients; now rare with treatment

presentations.symptoms: Early: meningitis, cranial nerve palsies (CN VII, VIII), uveitis, hearing loss, stroke (meningovascular); Late: general paresis (dementia, personality changes), tabes dorsalis (posterior column degeneration → ataxia, lightning pains, Argyll Robertson pupils — "accommodate but don't react")
presentations.clues: Can occur at ANY stage; CSF VDRL is specific but not sensitive; CSF FTA-ABS is sensitive but not specific

presentations.symptoms: Early (2 yrs): Hutchinson teeth, interstitial keratitis, CN VIII deafness (Hutchinson triad), saddle nose, saber shins
presentations.clues: Up to 40% stillborn or die in infancy if untreated; preventable with maternal screening and treatment

coverages.note: first-line (primary, secondary, early latent) — Single dose; NO penicillin resistance ever documented

coverages.medication: Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM weekly × 3 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (late latent, tertiary) — Three doses, one week apart

coverages.medication: Aqueous crystalline penicillin G 18–24 MU/day IV
coverages.note: first-line (neurosyphilis) — 3–4 MU IV q4h × 10–14 days
alternative (penicillin allergy, non-pregnant) — 100 mg PO BID × 14 days (early) or 28 days (late)
alternative — 1–2 g IV/IM daily × 10–14 days; some cross-reactivity risk with penicillin allergy

organisms.name: Borrelia burgdorferi
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative spirochete (loosely coiled, larger than Treponema); motile (endoflagella); microaerophilic; grows on Barbour-Stoenner-Kelly (BSK) medium (specialized, rarely used clinically)
organisms.features: Transmitted by Ixodes tick bite (deer tick/black-legged tick); requires tick attachment for ≥36–48 hours for transmission; outer surface proteins (OspA — tick midgut, OspC — mammalian infection) undergo antigenic variation; most common tick-borne disease in Northern Hemisphere; endemic in Northeast, upper Midwest, and Pacific coast US; diagnosis: clinical (erythema migrans) + two-tier serology (ELISA → Western blot or ELISA → ELISA)
organisms.source: Ixodes ticks; white-footed mouse (reservoir); white-tailed deer (tick host)
organisms.notes: Azithromycin is second-line only (lower efficacy). Prolonged/repeated antibiotic courses NOT beneficial for post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (persistent symptoms after adequate treatment). Two-tier serology may be negative in early disease — treat EM clinically. IgM alone positive after 30 days is likely false positive.

presentations.symptoms: Erythema migrans (EM): expanding erythematous macule/patch at tick bite site, ≥5 cm; may have central clearing ("bull's-eye") but most are uniformly erythematous; flu-like symptoms (fever, fatigue, myalgias, arthralgias, headache)
presentations.clues: EM is pathognomonic — clinical diagnosis, no serology needed (often seronegative at this stage); present in ~70–80% of infected patients

presentations.symptoms: Multiple EM lesions (hematogenous spread), cranial nerve palsy (CN VII/Bell palsy — bilateral in ~25%), lymphocytic meningitis, radiculopathy, cardiac involvement (AV block — typically 1st or 2nd degree, rarely 3rd degree; myocarditis)
presentations.clues: Lyme carditis can cause sudden death from complete heart block; neurologic Lyme (neuroborreliosis)

presentations.symptoms: Lyme arthritis: intermittent or persistent monoarthritis or oligoarthritis (knee most common — large, warm, swollen); acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans (ACA — Europe only); late neuroborreliosis (encephalomyelitis, peripheral neuropathy — rare)
presentations.clues: Lyme arthritis may persist despite antibiotics (autoimmune mechanism); ACA is pathognomonic for European Lyme

coverages.note: first-line (EM, early disseminated without CNS, Lyme arthritis) — 100 mg PO BID × 10–21 days (EM); 28 days (arthritis); also covers Anaplasma co-infection; preferred in adults
first-line (children 8, pregnant) — 500 mg PO TID × 14–21 days (adults); 50 mg/kg/day divided TID (children)
alternative — 500 mg PO BID × 14–21 days
first-line (neuroborreliosis with CNS involvement, severe carditis) — 2 g IV daily × 14–21 days (meningitis); 14–21 days (carditis requiring hospitalization)
prophylaxis — Single dose within 72 hrs of tick removal; only if: Ixodes tick, attached ≥36 hrs, endemic area, and can start within 72 hrs

organisms.name: Leptospira spp. (L. interrogans most common pathogenic species)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative spirochete (thin, tightly coiled with hooked ends — "question mark" shape); aerobic; motile (endoflagella); visualized by darkfield microscopy; grows on specialized media (EMJH medium)
organisms.features: Zoonosis with worldwide distribution; transmitted through contact with urine of infected animals or contaminated water/soil entering through skin abrasions or mucous membranes; occupational risk (farmers, sewer workers, veterinarians) and recreational risk (freshwater swimming, flooding); biphasic illness: leptospiremic phase (week 1) → immune phase (week 2+); Weil's disease = severe form with jaundice + renal failure + hemorrhage
organisms.source: Infected animal urine (rats, dogs, cattle, pigs); contaminated freshwater, soil; flooding events
organisms.notes: Start antibiotics as early as possible - most effective within 4 days of symptom onset. Antibiotics reduce defervescence time but have not been definitively shown to reduce mortality in severe disease. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension within 24 hrs of first dose) can occur. Severe leptospirosis (Weil disease: jaundice + AKI + hemorrhage) requires ICU-level care.

presentations.symptoms: Biphasic: Phase 1 (leptospiremic, 3–7 days): sudden high fever, severe headache, myalgias (especially calves and lumbar — characteristic), conjunctival suffusion

coverages.note: first-line (mild leptospirosis) — 100 mg PO BID x 7 days; also used for chemoprophylaxis (200 mg PO weekly during exposure period); drug of choice for mild disease
alternative (mild leptospirosis) — 500 mg PO TID x 7 days; alternative for patients who cannot take doxycycline (e.g., children under 8, pregnant women)
alternative (mild leptospirosis) — 500 mg PO QID x 7 days; alternative oral option
alternative (mild leptospirosis) — 500 mg PO daily x 3 days; comparable efficacy to doxycycline in network meta-analysis; suitable alternative for macrolide-tolerant patients

first-line (severe leptospirosis) — 1.5 million units IV q6h x 7 days; drug of choice for severe disease (Weil disease, pulmonary hemorrhage, meningitis); Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction may occur within 24 hrs
alternative (severe leptospirosis) — 1 g IV daily x 7 days; non-inferior to IV penicillin in clinical trials; convenient once-daily dosing
alternative (severe leptospirosis) — 1 g IV q6h x 7 days; comparable efficacy to penicillin; may have fastest defervescence time in network meta-analysis

organisms.name: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
organisms.morphology: Acid-fast rod (Ziehl-Neelsen stain — red/pink on blue background); obligate aerobe; slow-growing (doubling time 15–20 hrs; culture takes 2–8 weeks on Löwenstein-Jensen medium); non-motile; non-spore-forming; waxy cell wall (mycolic acids — resists Gram staining, desiccation, many disinfectants); cord factor (trehalose dimycolate — virulence factor)
organisms.features: Facultative intracellular pathogen (survives within macrophages); granuloma formation (caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells); transmitted via airborne droplet nuclei (1–5 μm); latent TB infection (LTBI) in ~25% of world population; reactivation risk highest in first 2 years and with immunosuppression (HIV, TNF inhibitors, transplant); apical lung segments preferred (high O₂ tension); Ghon complex (primary TB — Ghon focus + hilar lymphadenopathy); diagnosis: TST, IGRA, AFB smear/culture, GeneXpert MTB/RIF (rapid molecular)
organisms.source: Humans (respiratory droplets); airborne transmission; NOT transmitted by fomites
organisms.notes: Add pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 25–50 mg daily with isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy (especially in HIV, diabetes, pregnancy, malnutrition, renal failure). Monitor LFTs monthly (hepatotoxicity risk with INH, RIF, PZA). Ethambutol: monitor visual acuity (optic neuritis). Rifampin: potent CYP450 inducer — many drug interactions. DOT recommended for all active TB. Notify public health department.

presentations.incubation: Weeks to months (reactivation can occur years after primary infection)
presentations.symptoms: Chronic cough (>2–3 weeks), hemoptysis, night sweats, fever, weight loss, anorexia, fatigue
presentations.clues: Upper lobe cavitary disease on CXR (reactivation); miliary pattern (hematogenous dissemination); AFB smear positive in ~50–60% of culture-confirmed cases

presentations.symptoms: Usually asymptomatic or mild; Ghon complex on CXR (lower/middle lobe infiltrate + hilar lymphadenopathy); pleural effusion
presentations.clues: Most common in children; usually self-limited in immunocompetent

presentations.symptoms: Asymptomatic; positive TST or IGRA; normal CXR
presentations.clues: 5–10% lifetime risk of reactivation (higher with HIV — 5–15% per year); not infectious

presentations.symptoms: Lymphadenitis (most common extrapulmonary — cervical "scrofula"), pleural TB, skeletal TB (Pott's disease — vertebral), genitourinary TB, meningitis (basilar meningitis with cranial nerve palsies), peritoneal TB, pericardial TB, miliary TB (disseminated)
presentations.clues: Occurs in ~15–20% of TB cases; higher in HIV+ (up to 50%)

presentations.symptoms: Subacute headache, fever, cranial nerve palsies (CN VI most common), altered mental status, hydrocephalus
presentations.clues: CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, very high protein, very low glucose; basilar meningeal enhancement on MRI; mortality 20–50%

coverages.note: first-line (drug-susceptible) — Intensive phase (2 months): Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol (RIPE); Continuation phase (4 months): Rifampin + Isoniazid (RI); total 6 months
first-line (LTBI) — 12 weekly doses of isoniazid + rifapentine by DOT; or isoniazid 300 mg daily × 9 months; or rifampin 600 mg daily × 4 months
alternative (drug-susceptible pulmonary) — Rifapentine + moxifloxacin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide × 8 weeks, then rifapentine + moxifloxacin × 9 weeks; non-inferior to standard 6-month regimen
specialized — BPaL regimen: Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid (6 months); or longer regimens with fluoroquinolones, injectable agents

organisms.name: Mycobacterium avium complex (M. avium, M. intracellulare)
organisms.morphology: Acid-fast rod; slow-growing; non-motile; non-spore-forming; similar cell wall to M. tuberculosis (mycolic acids)
organisms.features: Most common NTM causing pulmonary disease; ubiquitous in environment (water, soil); NOT transmitted person-to-person; two major clinical presentations: (1) Pulmonary MAC — nodular-bronchiectatic (Lady Windermere syndrome — elderly thin women, RML/lingula) or fibrocavitary (resembles TB — upper lobe); (2) Disseminated MAC — in advanced HIV (CD4 50); diagnosis requires ATS/IDSA criteria: compatible symptoms + radiographic abnormalities + microbiologic confirmation (≥2 positive sputum cultures, or 1 positive BAL culture, or lung biopsy with granulomas + positive culture)
organisms.source: Environmental (water systems, soil, biofilms in plumbing); NOT person-to-person
organisms.notes: Clarithromycin can substitute for azithromycin (500 mg BID). Macrolide susceptibility testing essential — macrolide resistance predicts treatment failure. Do NOT use macrolide monotherapy (rapid resistance). Rifabutin preferred over rifampin in HIV patients (fewer drug interactions with ART). Treatment is prolonged (12+ months beyond culture conversion) and has frequent side effects.

presentations.symptoms: Chronic cough, fatigue, weight loss; CT: RML/lingula nodules + bronchiectasis ("tree-in-bud" pattern)
presentations.clues: "Lady Windermere syndrome" — classically elderly thin women; indolent course; may not require immediate treatment

presentations.symptoms: Cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, fever; CT: upper lobe cavitary disease (resembles TB)
presentations.clues: More aggressive; usually in older men with underlying lung disease (COPD, prior TB); requires treatment

presentations.symptoms: Fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hepatosplenomegaly, anemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase
presentations.clues: Occurs when CD4 50; blood cultures positive; high organism burden

coverages.note: first-line (nodular-bronchiectatic) — Three times weekly (MWF); azithromycin 500 mg + rifampin 600 mg + ethambutol 25 mg/kg; continue 12 months beyond sputum culture conversion
first-line (cavitary) — Daily dosing; consider IV amikacin for first 2–3 months for cavitary or severe disease; continue 12 months beyond culture conversion
first-line (refractory) — For treatment-refractory MAC-PD (cultures positive after ≥6 months of guideline-based therapy); added to existing regimen
first-line (disseminated MAC in HIV) — Azithromycin 500 mg daily + ethambutol 15 mg/kg daily ± rifabutin 300 mg daily; treat ≥12 months and until CD4 >100 for ≥6 months on ART
prophylaxis (HIV, CD4 100 for ≥3 months on ART

organisms.name: Influenza A/B
organisms.morphology: Orthomyxovirus; enveloped; segmented negative-sense ssRNA (8 segments); Influenza A: subtypes based on hemagglutinin (H1–H18) and neuraminidase (N1–N11); Influenza B: two lineages (Victoria, Yamagata)
organisms.features: Hemagglutinin (HA — binds sialic acid receptors, mediates viral entry; target of neutralizing antibodies and vaccines); Neuraminidase (NA — cleaves sialic acid, releases progeny virions; target of oseltamivir/zanamivir); Antigenic drift (point mutations → seasonal epidemics); Antigenic shift (reassortment of genome segments between different subtypes → pandemics; Influenza A only); M2 ion channel (target of amantadine/rimantadine — no longer recommended due to universal resistance)
organisms.source: Humans (respiratory droplets, contact); animal reservoirs for Influenza A (birds, pigs — zoonotic potential)
organisms.notes: Amantadine/rimantadine NO LONGER recommended (universal resistance in circulating strains). Antiviral treatment recommended for: all hospitalized patients, high-risk outpatients, and any patient within 48 hrs of onset. Do NOT give aspirin to children/adolescents with influenza (Reye syndrome risk).

presentations.symptoms: Abrupt onset fever, cough, myalgias, headache, malaise, sore throat, rhinorrhea, chills/sweats
presentations.clues: Hallmark is ABRUPT onset of systemic symptoms; duration 5–7 days; cough may persist 2+ weeks

presentations.symptoms: Primary viral pneumonia (bilateral infiltrates, ARDS) or secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. aureus, S. pneumoniae — biphasic illness with initial improvement then worsening)
presentations.clues: High-risk groups: age >65, age 5, pregnancy, chronic diseases, immunosuppression

presentations.symptoms: Myocarditis, encephalitis, myositis/rhabdomyolysis, Reye syndrome (aspirin + influenza in children — do NOT give aspirin to children with influenza)
presentations.clues: Reye syndrome: fatty liver + encephalopathy

coverages.note: first-line — 75 mg PO BID × 5 days; start within 48 hrs of symptom onset (benefit greatest within 24 hrs); reduces illness duration by ~18–25 hrs; reduces complications and hospitalization; can be given >48 hrs in hospitalized/high-risk patients

first-line (alternative) — Single dose (40 mg if 80 kg, 80 mg if ≥80 kg); cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor; similar efficacy to oseltamivir; greater efficacy against influenza B; NOT recommended in pregnancy
alternative — 10 mg (2 inhalations) BID × 5 days; contraindicated in asthma/COPD
alternative — 600 mg IV × 1 dose; for patients unable to take oral/inhaled medications
prophylaxis — Recommended for all persons ≥6 months; IIV (inactivated) or LAIV (live attenuated — nasal spray, age 2–49, not pregnant/immunocompromised)

organisms.name: RSV
organisms.morphology: Paramyxovirus; enveloped; non-segmented negative-sense ssRNA; two subtypes (A and B)
organisms.features: F protein (fusion — mediates viral entry and syncytia formation; target of palivizumab and nirsevimab); G protein (attachment); most common cause of bronchiolitis and pneumonia in infants 1 year; nearly all children infected by age 2; seasonal (fall–spring in temperate climates); increasingly recognized as cause of severe disease in elderly and immunocompromised adults
organisms.source: Humans; respiratory droplets and fomites (survives on surfaces for hours)
organisms.notes: Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are NOT recommended for RSV bronchiolitis. Diagnosis is clinical — routine viral testing not recommended. High-flow nasal cannula or CPAP for respiratory support if needed.

presentations.symptoms: Initial URI symptoms (rhinorrhea, congestion, low-grade fever) → lower respiratory tract involvement (cough, wheezing, tachypnea, retractions, poor feeding, apnea in young infants)
presentations.clues: Most common cause of bronchiolitis; peak severity days 3–5; most cases self-limited; hospitalization in ~1–3%

presentations.symptoms: Cough, fever, hypoxia, respiratory distress
presentations.clues: Can cause severe disease in premature infants, elderly, immunocompromised, cardiopulmonary disease

presentations.symptoms: Common cold symptoms; can trigger asthma/COPD exacerbations
presentations.clues: Usually mild in healthy adults

coverages.note: first-line — Supplemental O₂ to maintain SpO₂ >90%; nasal suctioning; IV/NG fluids if poor feeding; NO routine bronchodilators, epinephrine, corticosteroids, or antibiotics (not beneficial)
prophylaxis (infants) — Long-acting monoclonal antibody against F protein; single IM dose for all infants entering first RSV season and high-risk children up to 24 months in second season
prophylaxis (high-risk infants) — Monthly IM injections during RSV season; for premature infants 29 weeks, chronic lung disease of prematurity, hemodynamically significant CHD
prophylaxis (adults ≥60, maternal) — Abrysvo approved for pregnant women (32–36 weeks) to protect newborns; Arexvy for adults ≥60
rarely used — Aerosolized; sporadically used for severe RSV in immunocompromised (transplant); limited evidence of efficacy

organisms.name: SARS-CoV-2
organisms.morphology: Betacoronavirus; enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA (largest RNA virus genome ~30 kb); spike (S) protein (binds ACE2 receptor — mediates entry; target of vaccines and monoclonal antibodies)
organisms.features: Transmitted via respiratory droplets and aerosols; incubation 2–14 days (average 5); can be spread by asymptomatic/presymptomatic individuals; variants of concern (Alpha, Delta, Omicron sublineages); mRNA and viral vector vaccines available; rapid antigen and RT-PCR testing
organisms.source: Humans; respiratory droplets/aerosols; possible fomite transmission
organisms.notes: Treatment landscape evolves with new variants. Monoclonal antibodies (bebtelovimab, sotrovimab, etc.) have lost efficacy against current Omicron sublineages. Paxlovid: check drug interactions (ritonavir is potent CYP3A4 inhibitor); renal dose adjustment needed. Vaccination remains cornerstone of prevention.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, cough, fatigue, myalgias, headache, sore throat, anosmia/ageusia (64–80%), rhinorrhea, diarrhea (15–39%)
presentations.clues: Anosmia/ageusia relatively specific for COVID-19; most cases self-limited

presentations.symptoms: Dyspnea, hypoxia (SpO₂ 94%), bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT, lymphopenia, elevated CRP/D-dimer/LDH/ferritin
presentations.clues: Cytokine storm/hyperinflammation; ARDS; thrombotic complications (PE, DVT, stroke)

presentations.symptoms: ARDS, septic shock, multiorgan failure, cardiac injury
presentations.clues: ICU admission; mortality up to 40% in ICU patients

presentations.incubation: Weeks to months after acute infection
presentations.symptoms: Fatigue, brain fog, dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, joint pain, depression
presentations.clues: Affects 10–30% of infected individuals; can persist for months

presentations.symptoms: Fever, multiorgan inflammation (cardiac, GI, mucocutaneous — resembles Kawasaki disease), elevated inflammatory markers
presentations.clues: Rare; more common in unvaccinated

coverages.note: first-line (outpatient, high-risk) — 300 mg nirmatrelvir + 100 mg ritonavir PO BID × 5 days; start within 5 days of symptom onset; ritonavir is CYP3A4 inhibitor — many drug interactions
first-line (hospitalized; outpatient alternative) — Hospitalized: 200 mg IV day 1, then 100 mg IV daily × 5 days (up to 10 days if no improvement); Outpatient high-risk: 3-day course
first-line (hospitalized requiring O₂) — 6 mg IV/PO daily × 10 days; reduces 28-day mortality in patients requiring supplemental O₂ or mechanical ventilation; do NOT use in patients NOT requiring O₂
adjunct (severe, on O₂ + corticosteroids) — IL-6 receptor antagonist (tocilizumab) or JAK inhibitor (baricitinib); added to dexamethasone in patients with rapid respiratory deterioration

organisms.name: Rhinovirus
organisms.morphology: Picornavirus; non-enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA; >100 serotypes (3 species: A, B, C)
organisms.features: Most common cause of the common cold (30–50% of cases); optimal replication at 33°C (nasal passages); transmitted via respiratory droplets and fomites (survives on surfaces for hours); can trigger asthma and COPD exacerbations; no vaccine available (too many serotypes)
organisms.source: Humans; respiratory droplets; fomite transmission (hand-to-nose/eye)
organisms.notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for the common cold. OTC cold medications should NOT be used in children 4 years. Vitamin C and echinacea have NOT shown consistent benefit. Hand hygiene is the best prevention strategy.

presentations.symptoms: Rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sneezing, sore throat, cough, low-grade fever, malaise
presentations.clues: Self-limited (7–10 days; cough may persist 2+ weeks); most common viral URI

presentations.symptoms: Wheezing, dyspnea, increased sputum
presentations.clues: Most common viral trigger of asthma exacerbations in children and adults

coverages.note: first-line — Symptomatic treatment only; NO antibiotics; NO specific antiviral therapy

adjunct — Combination antihistamine-decongestant-analgesic (NNT ~4); nasal decongestants (topical or oral); NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain/fever; ipratropium nasal spray (rhinorrhea); zinc lozenges within 24 hrs may reduce duration; honey (children >1 yr) for cough

organisms.name: Norovirus
organisms.morphology: Calicivirus; non-enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA; multiple genogroups (GI, GII — GII.4 most common cause of outbreaks)
organisms.features: Most common cause of viral gastroenteritis worldwide (all ages); extremely contagious (infectious dose as low as 18 viral particles); resistant to many disinfectants (non-enveloped — alcohol-based hand sanitizers less effective; use soap and water + bleach); causes outbreaks in closed settings (cruise ships, nursing homes, hospitals, schools); no vaccine currently available
organisms.source: Humans; fecal-oral; contaminated food/water; fomites; aerosolized vomitus
organisms.notes: No vaccine currently available (candidates in clinical trials). No specific antiviral therapy. Chronic norovirus shedding can occur in immunocompromised patients. Alcohol-based hand sanitizers are LESS effective than soap and water against norovirus (non-enveloped virus).

presentations.symptoms: Acute onset nausea, vomiting (often projectile — more prominent than diarrhea), watery non-bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps, low-grade fever, body aches
presentations.clues: "Stomach flu"; self-limited (1–3 days in healthy; 4–6 days in elderly/hospitalized); vomiting more prominent than in bacterial gastroenteritis
presentations.severity: Mild; self-limited (can be severe in elderly/immunocompromised)

coverages.note: first-line — Oral rehydration; IV fluids if severe dehydration; NO antivirals, NO antibiotics, NO anti-emetics routinely needed

essential — Contact precautions; hand washing with soap and water (alcohol-based sanitizers less effective); bleach-based surface disinfection; isolate symptomatic patients; exclude food handlers until 48 hrs after symptom resolution

organisms.name: Rotavirus
organisms.morphology: Reovirus; non-enveloped; segmented double-stranded RNA (11 segments — "wheel-like" appearance on EM); Group A most common cause of disease
organisms.features: Was the most common cause of severe gastroenteritis in children 5 before vaccine introduction; transmitted fecal-oral; very stable in environment; vaccines (RotaTeq — pentavalent, Rotarix — monovalent) have dramatically reduced disease burden (49–89% reduction in hospitalizations)
organisms.source: Humans; fecal-oral; fomites
organisms.notes: Contraindication: history of intussusception (small increased risk with vaccine — ~1–5 per 100,000). Vaccine has reduced rotavirus hospitalizations by 49–89% in countries with routine vaccination. Natural infection does not confer complete immunity.

presentations.symptoms: Vomiting (often precedes diarrhea), profuse watery diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain; duration 3–8 days
presentations.clues: Peak age 6–24 months; can cause severe dehydration; nearly universal infection by age 5; now much less common due to vaccination

coverages.note: first-line — Oral rehydration (ORS); IV fluids for severe dehydration; zinc supplementation (children in developing countries); NO antibiotics, NO antivirals

prophylaxis — RotaTeq: 3 doses at 2, 4, 6 months; Rotarix: 2 doses at 2, 4 months; first dose by 15 weeks, series complete by 8 months; LIVE oral vaccine — contraindicated in SCID and history of intussusception

organisms.name: Hepatitis A virus
organisms.morphology: Picornavirus (Hepatovirus); non-enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA
organisms.features: Fecal-oral transmission; does NOT cause chronic infection; self-limited; vaccine-preventable; incidence decreased >90% in US after vaccine introduction; outbreaks in unvaccinated populations (homeless, PWID, MSM)
organisms.source: Humans; fecal-oral (contaminated food/water — shellfish, produce); person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome: fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea, abdominal pain → jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stools, hepatomegaly, pruritus
presentations.clues: Children 6 yrs often asymptomatic (70%); adults symptomatic (70%); does NOT cause chronic hepatitis; fulminant hepatic failure rare (1%)

coverages.note: first-line — Self-limited; no specific antiviral therapy; avoid hepatotoxins (alcohol, acetaminophen); monitor for fulminant hepatic failure (rare)
prophylaxis — 2-dose series (0, 6–12 months); recommended for all children 12–23 months, travelers to endemic areas, MSM, PWID, chronic liver disease, homeless
prophylaxis — HAV vaccine (preferred if age 1–40 and healthy) within 2 weeks of exposure; immune globulin (IG) for age 40, immunocompromised, chronic liver disease

organisms.name: Hepatitis B virus
organisms.morphology: Hepadnavirus; enveloped; partially double-stranded circular DNA; uses reverse transcriptase
organisms.features: Transmitted via blood, sexual contact, vertical (perinatal); can cause acute and CHRONIC hepatitis; chronic infection risk inversely related to age at infection (90% if infected at birth, 5% if infected as adult); associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and membranous nephropathy; serologic markers: HBsAg (active infection), anti-HBs (immunity), HBeAg (high replication/infectivity), anti-HBc IgM (acute), anti-HBc IgG (prior exposure); vaccine-preventable
organisms.source: Humans; blood/body fluids; sexual; vertical (perinatal); percutaneous
organisms.notes: Lamivudine, adefovir, and telbivudine are obsolete due to high resistance rates and should NOT be first-line. Treatment is usually lifelong, especially in cirrhosis - discontinuation risks severe hepatitis flare. Goals: suppress HBV DNA to undetectable, normalize ALT, prevent cirrhosis/HCC. HCC surveillance (ultrasound +/- AFP q6 months) for all patients with cirrhosis and select non-cirrhotic patients.

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome: fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea → jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain; serum sickness-like syndrome (arthralgias, urticaria, fever) in prodrome
presentations.clues: Adults: symptomatic in ~70%; fulminant hepatic failure in ~1%; 95% of adults clear infection spontaneously

presentations.symptoms: Often asymptomatic for years; can progress to cirrhosis (15–40% lifetime risk), HCC, hepatic decompensation
presentations.clues: Risk of chronicity: 90% (neonatal), 25–50% (children 1–5 yrs), 5% (adults); HCC can occur even without cir

coverages.note: first-line (chronic HBV, nucleos(t)ide-naive) — 0.5 mg PO daily (1 mg if lamivudine-experienced); high barrier to resistance (1.2% at 5 yrs); over 95% achieve undetectable HBV DNA; avoid if prior lamivudine resistance; dose adjust for CrCl under 50

first-line (chronic HBV) — 300 mg PO daily; no resistance reported through 7+ years; effective against lamivudine-resistant HBV; preferred in pregnancy and HIV coinfection; long-term use associated with renal tubular damage and decreased bone mineral density
first-line (chronic HBV) — 25 mg PO daily; improved renal and bone safety vs TDF; preferred if age over 60, renal impairment, or osteoporosis risk; may cause weight gain and lipid elevation

alternative (select patients, finite therapy) — 180 mcg SC once weekly x 48 weeks; immunomodulatory + antiviral; HBeAg seroconversion approx 30%; best response in young patients with high ALT, low HBV DNA, genotype A/B; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis, pregnancy, autoimmune disease, severe psychiatric illness

organisms.name: Hepatitis C virus
organisms.morphology: Flavivirus; enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA; 6 major genotypes (GT1 most common in US)
organisms.features: Transmitted via blood (IVDU most common in US, transfusion pre-1992); chronic infection in ~55–85% (unlike HAV/HBV); leading cause of liver transplantation in US; associated with HCC, cryoglobulinemia, membranoproliferative GN, porphyria cutanea tarda; NO vaccine available; curable with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) — SVR12 >95%
organisms.source: Humans; blood/body fluids; percutaneous (IVDU, needlestick); less commonly sexual or vertical
organisms.notes: "Treat all" strategy — all patients with chronic HCV should be treated regardless of fibrosis stage. SVR12 (undetectable viral load 12 weeks post-treatment) = cure. No pretreatment genotyping needed for pangenotypic regimens (simplified treatment). Screen all adults 18+ at least once. Lifelong HCC surveillance for patients with cirrhosis even after SVR. Check for HBV coinfection before treatment (risk of HBV reactivation).

presentations.symptoms: Usually asymptomatic (70–80%); if symptomatic: jaundice, fatigue, nausea, RUQ pain
presentations.clues: Spontaneous clearance in 15–45%; most progress to chronic infection

presentations.symptoms: Often asymptomatic for decades; fatigue; progressive fibrosis → cirrhosis (10–20% over 20 years) → HCC (1–4% per year in cirrhotics); extrahepatic: cryoglobulinemia, MPGN, PCT, lymphoma
presentations.clues: Leading indication for liver transplant; HCC can develop only in setting of advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis

coverages.note: first-line (pangenotypic) — 3 tablets (300/120 mg) PO daily; 8 weeks (without cirrhosis or with compensated cirrhosis); SVR12 >97%; no restriction based on renal function; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)
first-line (pangenotypic) — 1 tablet (400/100 mg) PO daily; 12 weeks; SVR12 >95%; can be used in decompensated cirrhosis (with ribavirin); approved for eGFR ≤30 and hemodialysis
first-line (DAA-experienced) — 1 tablet PO daily × 12 weeks; for patients who failed prior DAA therapy

organisms.name: HIV-1/HIV-2
organisms.morphology: Lentivirus (Retroviridae); enveloped; two copies of positive-sense ssRNA; reverse transcriptase (RNA → DNA); integrase (integrates into host genome); protease (cleaves polyproteins)
organisms.features: Infects CD4+ T cells, macrophages, dendritic cells via gp120 binding to CD4 and coreceptors (CCR5 or CXCR4); progressive CD4 depletion → immunodeficiency → opportunistic infections; transmitted via sexual contact, blood, vertical; acute retroviral syndrome (2–4 weeks post-exposure); window period for antibody testing ~2–4 weeks (4th-gen Ag/Ab combo test detects p24 antigen earlier); AIDS defined as CD4 200 or AIDS-defining illness
organisms.source: Humans; sexual contact; blood; vertical (perinatal, breastfeeding)
organisms.notes: Start ART as soon as possible after diagnosis, ideally same day or within 7 days. ART is recommended for ALL people with HIV regardless of CD4 count. Monitor HIV RNA and CD4 at baseline, 2–8 weeks after starting, then q3–6 months. Goal: undetectable viral load (50 copies/mL). U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable). Screen for OIs based on CD4 count. If prior CAB-LA PrEP use, avoid empiric INSTI-based regimen (possible INSTI resistance).

presentations.symptoms: Fever, lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis, rash (maculopapular), myalgias, headache, mucosal ulcers, diarrhea
presentations.clues: Resembles mononucleosis; high viral load, very infectious; 4th-gen Ag/Ab test may be positive (p24 antigen); antibody may be negative (window period)

presentations.symptoms: Asymptomatic or persistent generalized lymphadenopathy; gradual CD4 decline
presentations.clues: Viral replication continues; infectious throughout

presentations.symptoms: Opportunistic infections (PCP, toxoplasmosis, MAC, CMV, cryptococcal meningitis, Kaposi sarcoma, NHL, cervical cancer, wasting syndrome, HIV encephalopathy)
presentations.clues: CD4 200 cells/μL or AIDS-defining illness; without ART, survival ~3 years after AIDS diagnosis

coverages.note: first-line (initial ART) — 1 tablet PO daily; INSTI-based single-tablet regimen; high barrier to resistance; excellent tolerability
first-line (initial ART) — DTG 50 mg daily + dual NRTI backbone; equivalent efficacy to Biktarvy
first-line (2-drug regimen) — 1 tablet PO daily; for patients with no prior ART, HBV-negative, HIV RNA 500,000, no known resistance
first-line (long-acting injectable) — IM injections every 2 months; for virologically suppressed patients; improves adherence
PrEP (oral) — 1 tablet PO daily; for HIV-negative individuals at risk; Descovy not studied for receptive vaginal sex
PrEP (long-acting injectable) — IM every 2 months; superior to oral TDF/FTC for PrEP

organisms.name: HSV-1 / HSV-2
organisms.morphology: Herpesviridae (Alphaherpesvirinae); enveloped; large linear dsDNA; establishes latency in sensory ganglia (trigeminal for HSV-1, sacral/lumbosacral for HSV-2)
organisms.features: HSV-1: primarily orolabial herpes ("cold sores"), increasingly causes genital herpes (especially in young adults), herpes keratitis, and encephalitis (temporal lobe); HSV-2: primarily genital herpes, neonatal herpes, aseptic meningitis (Mollaret meningitis); lifelong latent infection with periodic reactivation; asymptomatic shedding common (transmission can occur without visible lesions); most common cause of genital ulcers worldwide
organisms.source: Humans; direct contact with lesions or secretions; sexual transmission; vertical (neonatal — during delivery)
organisms.notes: Antivirals do NOT cure HSV — they reduce symptoms, shedding, and recurrences. Topical antivirals are NOT recommended (inferior to systemic). Suppressive therapy reduces transmission to partners by ~50%. Condoms reduce transmission by ~30%. Screening for HSV in asymptomatic individuals is NOT recommended. Acyclovir-resistant HSV (rare, immunocompromised) → foscarnet.

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome (tingling, burning) → grouped vesicles on erythematous base on lips/perioral area → ulcerate → crust; painful; recurrences common
presentations.clues: "Cold sores" or "fever blisters"; recurrences milder and shorter than primary

presentations.symptoms: Painful grouped vesicles/ulcers on genitalia, dysuria, inguinal lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise; can have urinary retention; duration 2–3 weeks
presentations.clues: Primary episode more severe than recurrences; HSV-2 recurs more frequently than HSV-1 genitally

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome → fewer lesions, shorter duration (5–10 days), less pain
presentations.clues: HSV-2: average 4–5 recurrences/year (first year); HSV-1 genital: average 1 recurrence/year

presentations.symptoms: Fever, headache, altered mental status, focal neurologic deficits (temporal lobe — personality changes, aphasia, seizures)
presentations.clues: Most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis; MRI: temporal lobe involvement; CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, HSV PCR positive; mortality 70% untreated, 20% with acyclovir

presentations.symptoms: Three forms: (1) SEM (skin, eye, mouth); (2) CNS (encephalitis); (3) Disseminated (sepsis-like, hepatitis, DIC)
presentations.clues: Usually acquired during delivery from maternal genital HSV; highest risk with primary maternal infection near delivery

coverages.note: first-line (primary genital) — 1 g PO BID × 7–10 days
alternative — 400 mg PO TID × 7–10 days (primary); 800 mg PO TID × 2 days (recurrent episodic); 400 mg PO BID (suppressive); less convenient dosing
alternative — 250 mg PO TID × 7–10 days (primary); 1 g PO BID × 1 day (recurrent); 250 mg PO BID (suppressive)
first-line (encephalitis, severe/disseminated, neonatal) — 10 mg/kg IV q8h × 14–21 days (encephalitis/neonatal CNS); 5–10 mg/kg IV q8h (severe mucocutaneous)

organisms.name: Varicella-zoster virus
organisms.morphology: Herpesviridae (Alphaherpesvirinae); enveloped; large linear dsDNA; establishes latency in dorsal root ganglia and cranial nerve ganglia
organisms.features: Primary infection = varicella (chickenpox); reactivation = herpes zoster (shingles); highly contagious (airborne + contact with vesicle fluid); vaccine-preventable (Varivax for varicella, Shingrix for zoster)
organisms.source: Humans; airborne transmission (varicella); direct contact with vesicle fluid
organisms.notes: Antivirals for zoster are most beneficial if started within 72 hrs of rash onset, but can be started later if new lesions are still forming. Always treat: age >50, immunocompromised, ophthalmic involvement, severe pain, complicated zoster. Varicella is infectious until all lesions have crusted. Pregnant women with varicella exposure should receive VZIG within 10 days.

presentations.symptoms: Fever, malaise → generalized pruritic rash: macules → papules → vesicles ("dewdrop on rose petal") → pustules → crusts; lesions in different stages simultaneously (crops); centripetal distribution (trunk > extremities)
presentations.clues: Highly contagious; usually mild in children; more severe in adults, neonates, immunocompromised; complications: secondary bacterial skin infection, pneumonia (adults), cerebellar ataxia, encephalitis

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome (2–3 days): pain, burning, tingling in dermatomal distribution → unilateral vesicular rash confined to single dermatome (does NOT cross midline); thoracic dermatomes most common
presentations.clues: Lifetime risk 30%; risk increases with age and immunosuppression; most common complication: postherpetic neuralgia (PHN — pain persisting >90 days)

presentations.symptoms: V1 (ophthalmic division of trigeminal) involvement; vesicles on nose tip (Hutchinson sign — nasociliary branch involvement → high risk of ocular disease); keratitis, uveitis, acute retinal necrosis
presentations.clues: Ophthalmology emergency; can cause vision loss

presentations.symptoms: Herpes zoster of geniculate ganglion (CN VII); vesicles in ear canal/auricle + ipsilateral facial nerve palsy + sensorineural hearing loss ± vertigo
presentations.clues: Worse prognosis for facial nerve recovery than Bell palsy

presentations.symptoms: Burning, stabbing, or aching pain in affected dermatome; allodynia; can be debilitating
presentations.clues: Occurs in ~10–20% of zoster patients; risk increases with age (>30% in patients >60)

coverages.note: first-line (zoster) — 1 g PO TID × 7 days; start within 72 hrs of rash onset; preferred over acyclovir (better bioavailability, less frequent dosing)
alternative (zoster) — 500 mg PO TID × 7 days
alternative (zoster) — 800 mg PO 5 times daily × 7 days; less convenient; preferred IV for severe/disseminated disease
first-line (severe/disseminated/immunocompromised) — 10 mg/kg IV q8h × 7–10 days
symptomatic — Gabapentin (300–3600 mg/day), pregabalin (150–600 mg/day), TCAs (nortriptyline/amitriptyline 25–150 mg/day), topical lidocaine 5% patch, topical capsaicin 8% patch
prophylaxis — 2 doses IM (0, 2–6 months); recommended for all adults ≥50 and immunocompromised adults ≥19; >90% efficacy against zoster and PHN; preferred over Zostavax (live, discontinued)
prophylaxis — 2 doses SC (12–15 months, 4–6 years); for all children; also for susceptible adults (2 doses, 4–8 weeks apart)

organisms.name: Cytomegalovirus
organisms.morphology: Herpesviridae (Betaherpesvirinae); enveloped; largest human herpesvirus; linear dsDNA; establishes latency in myeloid progenitor cells
organisms.features: Seroprevalence 50–80% in adults; primary infection usually asymptomatic in immunocompetent; significant pathogen in immunocompromised (transplant, HIV) and neonates (congenital CMV — leading infectious cause of congenital sensorineural hearing loss and intellectual disability); "owl's eye" intranuclear inclusions on histopathology; transmitted via body fluids (saliva, urine, breast milk, blood, sexual contact, transplanted organs)
organisms.source: Humans; body fluids; transplanted organs; vertical (transplacental, perinatal)
organisms.notes: CMV retinitis in HIV: start ART but delay 2 weeks to reduce immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) risk. Maintenance therapy can be discontinued when CD4 >100 for ≥3–6 months on ART. Ganciclovir resistance: UL97 mutations (most common) → consider foscarnet; UL54 mutations → may confer cross-resistance. Acyclovir/valacyclovir have minimal activity against CMV.

presentations.symptoms: Prolonged fever, fatigue, malaise, myalgias, atypical lymphocytosis; pharyngitis and lymphadenopathy LESS prominent than EBV mono; hepatitis (elevated transaminases) common
presentations.clues: Heterophile-negative mononucleosis (negative monospot); usually self-limited

presentations.symptoms: Painless vision loss, floaters, scotomata; fundoscopy: "pizza pie" or "cottage cheese and ketchup" appearance (hemorrhages + exudates); can cause retinal detachment
presentations.clues: Most common CMV end-organ disease in AIDS; bilateral in 30–50%

presentations.symptoms: Diarrhea (watery or bloody), abdominal pain, fever, weight loss
presentations.clues: Immunocompromised; endoscopy: ulcerations; biopsy: owl's eye inclusions

presentations.symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, hypoxia, bilateral interstitial infiltrates
presentations.clues: Most lethal CMV disease in HSCT recipients (mortality 30–50% even with treatment)

presentations.symptoms: 90% asymptomatic at birth; symptomatic: petechiae, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications, chorioretinitis, SNHL
presentations.clues: Leading infectious cause of congenital SNHL and intellectual disability; ~10% of asymptomatic infants develop late-onset SNHL

coverages.note: first-line (treatment, non-severe) — 900 mg PO BID × 21 days (induction), then 900 mg PO daily (maintenance); for retinitis, colitis, non-severe disease

first-line (severe disease, pneumonitis) — 5 mg/kg IV q12h × 14–21 days (induction), then 5 mg/kg IV daily or valganciclovir PO (maintenance); dose adjust for renal function; major toxicity: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia
alternative (ganciclovir-resistant or intolerant) — 60 mg/kg IV q8h or 90 mg/kg IV q12h; nephrotoxic; electrolyte abnormalities
first-line (refractory/resistant CMV post-transplant) — 400 mg PO BID; UL97 kinase inhibitor; FDA-approved for post-transplant CMV refractory to ganciclovir/foscarnet/cidofovir
prophylaxis (HSCT) — 480 mg PO/IV daily (240 mg if on cyclosporine); CMV terminase inhibitor; for CMV-seropositive allogeneic HSCT recipients

organisms.name: Epstein-Barr virus (HHV-4)
organisms.morphology: Herpesviridae (Gammaherpesvirinae); enveloped; linear dsDNA; establishes latency in B lymphocytes
organisms.features: Infects >95% of adults worldwide; transmitted via saliva ("kissing disease"); causes infectious mononucleosis; associated with multiple malignancies: Burkitt lymphoma (endemic — jaw; sporadic — abdomen), Hodgkin lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD), gastric carcinoma; also associated with oral hairy leukoplakia (HIV); heterophile antibody test (Monospot) for diagnosis
organisms.source: Humans; saliva; close personal contact
organisms.notes: Do NOT give amoxicillin or ampicillin (causes diffuse maculopapular rash in ~70–100% of mono patients — not a true allergy). Do NOT give aspirin (risk of Reye syndrome in children). Avoid contact sports for ≥3 weeks (splenic rupture risk). No vaccine available. EBV has been strongly linked to multiple sclerosis (32-fold increased risk).

presentations.symptoms: Classic triad: fever, pharyngitis (exudative), posterior cervical lymphadenopathy; also: fatigue (can persist weeks-months), splenomegaly (50%), hepatomegaly, palatal petechiae, periorbital edema; maculopapular rash if given amoxicillin/ampicillin (~70–100%)
presentations.clues: Peak age 15–24 years; atypical lymphocytes >10% on CBC; heterophile antibody (Monospot) sensitivity 87%, specificity 91%; false-negative in first week and children 5

presentations.symptoms: Splenic rupture (0.1–0.2% — rare but potentially fatal), airway obstruction (most common cause of hospitalization in children), hepatitis, hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, GBS, encephalitis, myocarditis
presentations.clues: Splenic rupture risk highest weeks 2–3; avoid contact sports for ≥3 weeks from symptom onset

presentations.symptoms: Burkitt lymphoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, PTLD, gastric carcinoma, NK/T-cell lymphoma
presentations.clues: EBV is oncogenic; linked to ~200,000 cancers/year worldwide

coverages.note: first-line — Rest, hydration, analgesics (acetaminophen/NSAIDs), avoid contact sports for ≥3 weeks; NO specific antiviral therapy; antivirals (acyclovir) reduce oropharyngeal shedding but do NOT improve clinical outcomes

NOT routinely recommended — May be considered for impending airway obstruction, severe thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia; short course (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 5–7 days); insufficient evidence for routine use

organisms.name: Measles virus
organisms.morphology: Paramyxovirus (Morbillivirus); enveloped; non-segmented negative-sense ssRNA
organisms.features: One of the most contagious diseases known (R₀ = 12–18); transmitted via airborne droplet nuclei (can remain airborne for 2 hours); vaccine-preventable (MMR); causes transient immune suppression ("immune amnesia" — can last 2–3 years); notifiable disease
organisms.source: Humans only; airborne transmission; infectious 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset
organisms.notes: Airborne precautions required (negative pressure room). Reportable disease. Immune amnesia: measles infection can erase existing immune memory → increased susceptibility to other infections for 2–3 years. Vitamin A deficiency increases measles severity and mortality.

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome (2–4 days): high fever (up to 40.6°C), cough, coryza, conjunctivitis (the "3 Cs"), Koplik spots (pathognomonic — blue-white spots on buccal mucosa opposite molars, appear 1–2 days before rash); Rash: erythematous maculopapular, starts on face/hairline → spreads cephalocaudally to trunk and extremities over 3 days; fever peaks with rash onset
presentations.clues: Complications: otitis media (most common), pneumonia (most common cause of death), encephalitis (1 in 1000), SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis — rare, fatal, years later)
presentations.severity: Mild to life-threatening (mortality 1–2 per 1000 in developed countries; up to 25% in malnourished)

coverages.note: first-line — No specific antiviral; hydration, antipyretics; treat secondary bacterial infections (pneumonia, otitis media) with antibiotics

first-line (adjunct) — 200,000 IU PO × 2 doses (day 1 and day 2) for children ≥12 months; 100,000 IU for 6–11 months; 50,000 IU for 6 months; reduces mortality and complications; recommended for all hospitalized children with measles
prophylaxis — 2 doses (12–15 months, 4–6 years); 97% effective after 2 doses; post-exposure: MMR within 72 hrs of exposure or immune globulin within 6 days

organisms.name: Mumps virus
organisms.morphology: Paramyxovirus (Rubulavirus); enveloped; non-segmented negative-sense ssRNA
organisms.features: Transmitted via respiratory droplets and saliva; vaccine-preventable (MMR); incubation 16–18 days; infectious 2 days before to 5 days after parotitis onset
organisms.source: Humans; respiratory droplets; saliva

presentations.symptoms: Prodrome: fever, headache, malaise, myalgias → bilateral (or unilateral) parotid gland swelling (parotitis — tender, "chipmunk cheeks"); ear pain worsened by chewing
presentations.clues: 30–40% of infections are asymptomatic; parotitis is the hallmark

presentations.symptoms: Orchitis (15–30% of post-pubertal males — usually unilateral; rarely causes sterility), oophoritis (5% of post-pubertal females), aseptic meningitis (1–10%), encephalitis (rare), pancreatitis, sensorineural hearing loss (usually unilateral, may be permanent)
presentations.clues: Orchitis is most common complication in adults

coverages.note: first-line — No specific antiviral; analgesics, warm/cold compresses for parotitis, scrotal support for orchitis
prophylaxis — 2

organisms.name: Rubella virus
organisms.morphology: Togavirus (Rubivirus); enveloped; positive-sense ssRNA
organisms.features: Generally mild disease in children and adults; primary concern is congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) when infection occurs in early pregnancy (especially first trimester — risk of CRS up to 85%); vaccine-preventable (MMR); 25–50% of infections are asymptomatic; transmitted via respiratory droplets
organisms.source: Humans; respiratory droplets
organisms.notes: Rubella is a reportable disease. Droplet precautions for 7 days after rash onset. Congenital rubella infants can shed virus for months. No treatment for CRS — prevention through vaccination is key. All pregnant women should be screened for rubella immunity at first prenatal visit.

presentations.symptoms: Low-grade fever, generalized erythematous maculopapular rash (starts on face → trunk → extremities, lasts ~3 days — "3-day measles"), posterior auricular/posterior cervical/suboccipital lymphadenopathy (characteristic — often precedes rash), arthralgias/arthritis (up to 70% of adult women)
presentations.clues: Milder than measles; lymphadenopathy pattern is distinguishing feature; 25–50% asymptomatic; complications rare: thrombocytopenic purpura (1:3000), encephalitis (1:6000)

presentations.symptoms: Classic triad: sensorineural hearing loss (most common), congenital heart disease (PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis), cataracts; also: microcephaly, intellectual disability, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenic purpura ("blueberry muffin" rash), jaundice
presentations.clues: Risk of CRS: ~85% if maternal infection in first trimester, ~25% in second trimester, rare in third trimester; ~100,000 cases/year worldwide

coverages.note: first-line — No specific antiviral therapy; self-limited; analgesics for arthralgias

prophylaxis — 2 doses (12–15 months, 4–6 years); highly effective; contraindicated in pregnancy (live vaccine — advise avoiding pregnancy for 4 weeks after vaccination); check rubella immunity in all women of childbearing age

organisms.name: Candida albicans
organisms.morphology: Oval budding yeast; forms pseudohyphae and true hyphae (dimorphic — yeast at normal temperatures, hyphae during tissue invasion); Gram-positive on Gram stain; grows on Sabouraud agar; forms germ tubes in serum at 37°C (germ tube test — distinguishes C. albicans from most other Candida species)
organisms.features: Normal flora of skin, GI tract, and vagina; most common cause of fungal infections in humans; opportunistic — causes disease when host defenses are impaired (immunosuppression, antibiotics, diabetes, indwelling catheters, ICU); C. auris is an emerging multidrug-resistant species of concern; biofilm formation on medical devices
organisms.source: Endogenous (normal flora); nosocomial (catheters, devices)
organisms.notes: Remove all central venous catheters in candidemia when feasible. Ophthalmologic exam for all patients with candidemia (endophthalmitis in ~10–15%). Echinocandins have NO activity against Cryptococcus. C. glabrata and C. krusei have intrinsic or acquired fluconazole resistance — echinocandins preferred. C. auris: often multidrug-resistant — check susceptibilities.

presentations.symptoms: Vulvar pruritus (most prominent), burning, soreness, dyspareunia, external dysuria; thick white "cottage cheese" discharge; vulvar erythema, edema, fissures
presentations.clues: 75% of women have ≥1 episode; C. albicans causes ~85–90%; recurrent VVC (RVVC) = ≥3 episodes/year; risk factors: antibiotics, pregnancy, diabetes, OCP

presentations.symptoms: White, curd-like plaques on buccal mucosa, tongue, palate that can be scraped off revealing erythematous base; pain, dysphagia
presentations.clues: Common in HIV (CD4 200), neonates, denture wearers, inhaled corticosteroid users, chemotherapy

presentations.symptoms: Odynophagia, dysphagia, retrosternal pain
presentations.clues: AIDS-defining illness; often without oral thrush; diagnosis by endoscopy (white plaques, biopsy shows yeast/pseudohyphae)

presentations.symptoms: Fever unresponsive to antibiotics, sepsis, end-organ involvement (endophthalmitis, endocarditis, hepatosplenic candidiasis, osteomyelitis)
presentations.clues: Risk factors: central lines, TPN, broad-spectrum antibiotics, ICU, abdominal surgery, neutropenia; mortality 30–40%

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated VVC) — Single dose; >90% response; alternatives: topical azoles (miconazole, clotrimazole) × 3–7 days

coverages.medication: Fluconazole 150 mg PO q72h × 3 doses, then 150 mg weekly × 6 months
coverages.note: first-line (recurrent VVC) — Suppressive therapy; oteseconazole (Vivjoa) FDA-approved for RVVC prevention in non-reproductive-potential females
first-line (oropharyngeal) — Alternatives: clotrimazole troches, nystatin suspension
first-line (esophageal) — Alternatives: echinocandin if fluconazole-refractory

first-line (candidemia/invasive) — Micafungin 100 mg IV daily; caspofungin 70 mg IV load then 50 mg daily; anidulafungin 200 mg IV load then 100 mg daily; treat ≥14 days after first negative blood culture

alternative (candidemia, stable, non-neutropenic) — Step-down from echinocandin once species identified and susceptible; preferred for CNS and ocular candidiasis (echinocandins have poor CNS/eye penetration)

organisms.name: Aspergillus spp. (A. fumigatus most common pathogen)
organisms.morphology: Septate hyphae with acute-angle (45°) dichotomous branching (vs. Mucor: wide-angle, non-septate); conidiophore with uniseriate phialides producing chains of conidia; grows rapidly on Sabouraud agar; ubiquitous in environment
organisms.features: Transmitted by inhalation of airborne conidia (spores); NOT transmitted person-to-person; causes spectrum of disease depending on immune status: allergic (ABPA), saprophytic (aspergilloma), invasive (IPA — immunocompromised); risk factors for IPA: prolonged neutropenia, HSCT, solid organ transplant, high-dose corticosteroids, CGD; diagnosis: galactomannan antigen (serum/BAL), beta-D-glucan, CT (halo sign early, air crescent sign late), culture, histopathology
organisms.source: Environmental (soil, decaying vegetation, construction sites, air ducts); inhalation of conidia
organisms.notes: Echinocandins have NO activity against Mucor/Rhizopus (important differential for non-septate hyphae). Voriconazole: monitor trough levels, visual disturbances, hepatotoxicity, photosensitivity, periostitis. Azole resistance in A. fumigatus is increasing (environmental exposure to agricultural azoles). If azole-resistant Aspergillus suspected, use liposomal amphotericin B.

presentations.symptoms: Fever unresponsive to antibiotics, cough, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, dyspnea
presentations.clues: CT: halo sign (ground-glass opacity surrounding nodule — early), air crescent sign (late — cavitation); occurs in severely immunocompromised (neutropenia, HSCT, transplant); mortality 30–95% depending on host

presentations.symptoms: Poorly controlled asthma, wheezing, cough, expectoration of brownish mucus plugs, fleeting pulmonary infiltrates, bronchiectasis
presentations.clues: Occurs in asthma and CF; elevated total IgE (>1000 IU/L), Aspergillus-specific IgE, eosinophilia, positive skin prick test

presentations.symptoms: Often asymptomatic; hemoptysis (can be massive/life-threatening)
presentations.clues: Saprophytic colonization of pre-existing lung cavity (TB, sarcoidosis); CXR: mobile intracavitary mass with air crescent sign (Monod sign)

presentations.symptoms: Chronic cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, fatigue
presentations.clues: Occurs in patients with underlying lung disease but NOT severely immunocompromised; progressive cavitation

coverages.note: first-line (IPA) — 6 mg/kg IV q12h × 1 day (loading), then 4 mg/kg IV q12h; oral: 200–300 mg q12h; TDM recommended (target trough 1–5.5 μg/mL); duration: minimum 6–12 weeks, until clinical/radiographic resolution and immune reconstitution

first-line (IPA, alternative) — 200 mg IV/PO q8h × 6 doses (loading), then 200 mg daily; non-inferior to voriconazole; fewer drug interactions; favorable safety profile (no QTc prolongation, less hepatotoxicity); may have optimal efficacy-safety balance
first-line (IPA, alternative) — 300 mg PO/IV BID day 1, then 300 mg daily; non-inferior to voriconazole in recent trial
alternative (IPA) — 3–5 mg/kg/day IV; for azole-intolerant or azole-resistant cases

salvage therapy — NOT recommended as monotherapy for primary treatment; may be added to voriconazole for severe cases (voriconazole + anidulafungin combination showed trend toward benefit in subgroup of proven IPA)
first-line (ABPA) — 200 mg PO BID; adjunct to corticosteroids; reduces exacerbations and steroid requirements; voriconazole or posaconazole if itraconazole fails
first-line (ABPA) — Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day for acute exacerbations; taper over 3 months; controls immune-mediated inflammation

organisms.name: Cryptococcus neoformans
organisms.morphology: Encapsulated yeast; round, narrow-based budding; thick polysaccharide capsule (major virulence factor — antiphagocytic); visualized with India ink stain (clear halo around yeast against dark background); mucicarmine stain (red capsule); grows on Sabouraud agar; urease-positive; does NOT form hyphae
organisms.features: Causes meningoencephalitis primarily in immunocompromised (HIV with CD4 25 cmH₂O in >50%), lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose; India ink positive in ~60–80% of HIV-associated cases
organisms.source: Environmental — pigeon droppings, soil; inhalation of desiccated yeast or basidiospores; NOT transmitted person-to-person
organisms.notes: Manage elevated ICP aggressively — serial therapeutic lumbar punctures (remove CSF until opening pressure 20 cmH₂O or 50% reduction); elevated ICP is the leading cause of early death. Echinocandins have NO activity against Cryptococcus. Delay ART initiation 4–6 weeks after starting antifungal therapy (reduce IRIS risk — COAT trial). Serum CrAg screening in HIV patients with CD4 100 can identify subclinical infection for preemptive treatment.

presentations.symptoms: Headache (most common), fever, neck stiffness (may be absent in HIV), altered mental status, cranial nerve palsies, visual changes, nausea/vomiting
presentations.clues: Most common cause of meningitis in HIV/AIDS; elevated ICP is major cause of morbidity/mortality; CSF CrAg sensitivity >95%; serum CrAg often positive before symptoms

presentations.symptoms: Cough, dyspnea, chest pain; CXR: nodules, infiltrates, cavitation, pleural effusion
presentations.clues: May be asymptomatic in immunocompetent; can disseminate in immunocompromised

presentations.symptoms: Skin lesions (papules resembling molluscum contagiosum in HIV), fungemia, bone, prostate (reservoir for relapse)
presentations.clues: Occurs with severe immunosuppression

coverages.note: first-line (induction, meningitis) — Liposomal AmB 3–4 mg/kg/day IV + flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h × 2 weeks; most effective induction regimen (fastest CSF sterilization)

coverages.medication: Single high-dose liposomal AmB (10 mg/kg) + flucytosine + fluconazole
coverages.note: first-line (WHO-recommended, resource-limited) — Single dose AmB + flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day × 14 days + fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 14 days; AMBITION trial demonstrated non-inferiority
first-line (consolidation) — × 8 weeks after successful induction (negative CSF culture, clinical improvement); then step down to 200 mg/day for maintenance
first-line (maintenance/secondary prophylaxis) — Continue until CD4 >100 for ≥3 months on ART and undetectable/very low HIV viral load and ≥1 year of antifungal therapy

organisms.name: Histoplasma capsulatum
organisms.morphology: Dimorphic: mold at 25°C (septate hyphae with tuberculate macroconidia — large, round, with finger-like projections), yeast at 37°C (small, oval, intracellular yeast within macrophages); visualized on GMS or PAS stain
organisms.features: Endemic to Ohio and Mississippi River valleys, Central America, Caribbean; found in soil contaminated with bird or bat droppings (caves, chicken coops, old buildings); inhaled conidia convert to yeast in lungs → phagocytosed by macrophages → can disseminate; most infections asymptomatic or self-limited; disseminated disease in immunocompromised (HIV CD4 150); diagnosis: urine/serum Histoplasma antigen (most useful for disseminated), serology (complement fixation, immunodiffusion), culture, histopathology
organisms.source: Soil enriched with bird/bat droppings; Ohio/Mississippi River valleys; caves; endemic areas
organisms.notes: Fluconazole is LESS effective than itraconazole for histoplasmosis — not recommended as first-line. Echinocandins have NO activity against Histoplasma. Monitor urine Histoplasma antigen during and after treatment (decline indicates response; rise indicates relapse). Itraconazole has many drug interactions (CYP3A4 inhibitor). Mild acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in immunocompetent patients does NOT require treatment (self-limited).

presentations.symptoms: Fever, cough, chest pain, malaise, myalgias; CXR: hilar lymphadenopathy ± patchy infiltrates
presentations.clues: Most cases self-limited in immunocompetent; heavy exposure → diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates, ARDS

presentations.symptoms: Chronic cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, fever; CXR: upper lobe cavitary disease (resembles TB)
presentations.clues: Occurs in patients with underlying emphysema; progressive without treatment

presentations.symptoms: Fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, mucosal ulcers (oral/GI), adrenal insufficiency, skin lesions
presentations.clues: Occurs in HIV (CD4 150), transplant, immunosuppression; can mimic miliary TB; mortality high without treatment

presentations.symptoms: Mediastinal lymphadenitis, mediastinal granuloma, mediastinal fibrosis (rare, progressive, no effective treatment)
presentations.clues: Fibrosis can cause SVC syndrome, airway/esophageal compression

coverages.note: first-line (mild-moderate pulmonary and disseminated) — 200 mg PO TID × 3 days (loading), then 200 mg PO BID; ≥12 weeks for acute pulmonary, ≥12 months for disseminated; monitor serum levels (target 1–2 μg/mL); liquid formulation preferred (better absorption)
first-line (severe/disseminated) — 3 mg/kg/day IV × ≥2 weeks or until clinical improvement, then step down to itraconazole

organisms.name: Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii)
organisms.type: Fungus (atypical — classified as fungus by rRNA analysis but does not respond to most antifungals)
organisms.morphology: Cannot be cultured on standard media; cysts (contain intracystic bodies) and trophic forms; visualized with GMS stain (cysts — "crushed ping-pong balls"), DFA, or Giemsa stain (trophic forms); cell wall contains beta-D-glucan (useful diagnostic marker) but lacks ergosterol (azoles and amphotericin B are INEFFECTIVE)
organisms.features: Causes Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) in immunocompromised — primarily HIV (CD4 95%), PCR; classic CXR: bilateral diffuse ground-glass/interstitial infiltrates; elevated LDH
organisms.source: Humans; airborne transmission; ubiquitous
organisms.notes: Azoles and amphotericin B are INEFFECTIVE against Pneumocystis (lacks ergosterol). Beta-D-glucan is a useful adjunctive diagnostic test (sensitivity >95%). Patients who develop PCP despite TMP-SMX prophylaxis can still be treated with TMP-SMX at therapeutic doses. Start ART within 2 weeks of PCP treatment. Discontinue prophylaxis when CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART.

presentations.incubation: Weeks (subacute in HIV; more acute in non-HIV)
presentations.symptoms: Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxia (often out of proportion to exam findings); CXR: bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities (can be normal early); CT: ground-glass opacities, cystic changes
presentations.clues: HIV: subacute onset over weeks; Non-HIV: more acute, worse prognosis; elevated LDH (>500 suggests PCP); beta-D-glucan elevated; exercise desaturation

coverages.note: first-line (treatment) — TMP 15–20 mg/kg/day + SMX 75–100 mg/kg/day IV/PO divided q6–8h × 21 days; or 2 DS tablets PO TID for mild-moderate disease; dose adjust for renal function

first-line (moderate-severe PCP) — Prednisone 40 mg PO BID days 1–5, 40 mg daily days 6–10, 20 mg daily days 11–21; indicated if PaO₂ 70 mmHg or A-a gradient ≥35 mmHg; start within 72 hrs of PCP treatment; reduces mortality
alternative (TMP-SMX intolerant) — Primaquine 30 mg base PO daily + clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h or 450 mg PO q6h × 21 days; check G6PD before primaquine
alternative (severe, TMP-SMX intolerant) — 4 mg/kg IV daily × 21 days; nephrotoxic, hypoglycemia, pancreatitis
alternative (mild-moderate only) — 750 mg PO BID with food × 21 days; less effective than TMP-SMX but fewer side effects
first-line (prophylaxis) — For HIV with CD4 200 (or 100 regardless of viral load); also covers Toxoplasma; alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, aerosolized pentamidine

organisms.name: Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton)
organisms.morphology: Septate hyphae; keratinophilic (digest keratin in skin, hair, nails); identified by KOH prep (branching septate hyphae), culture on Sabouraud agar (with cycloheximide), or PCR; T. rubrum is the most common species worldwide
organisms.features: Classified by body site: tinea corporis (body), tinea pedis (feet), tinea cruris (groin), tinea capitis (scalp), tinea unguium/onychomycosis (nails), tinea manuum (hands), tinea faciei (face); transmitted by direct contact with infected persons, animals, or fomites; T. indotineae is an emerging terbinafine-resistant strain
organisms.source: Humans (anthropophilic), animals (zoophilic), soil (geophilic); direct contact; fomites
organisms.notes: Topical therapy alone is INSUFFICIENT for tinea capitis and onychomycosis — oral therapy required. Add antifungal shampoo (ketoconazole 2% or selenium sulfide) for tinea capitis to reduce transmission. Confirm onychomycosis diagnosis before starting oral therapy (many mimics). T. indotineae: emerging terbinafine-resistant strain — consider itraconazole.

presentations.symptoms: Annular, erythematous, scaly plaque with raised border and central clearing; pruritic
presentations.clues: Most common in children; differential: eczema, psoriasis, granuloma annulare

presentations.symptoms: Interdigital maceration/scaling, moccasin-type (diffuse plantar scaling), vesiculobullous type
presentations.clues: Most common dermatophyte infection in adults; T. rubrum most common cause

presentations.symptoms: Erythematous, scaly plaques in inguinal folds; spares scrotum (unlike Candida)
presentations.clues: More common in males; often coexists with tinea pedis

presentations.symptoms: Scalp scaling, alopecia, broken hairs ("black dot" — endothrix; "gray patch" — ectothrix); kerion (boggy, inflammatory mass — can cause scarring alopecia)
presentations.clues: Most common in prepubertal children; T. tonsurans most common in US; requires ORAL therapy (topicals do not penetrate hair follicle)

presentations.symptoms: Thickened, discolored, dystrophic nails; subungual debris
presentations.clues: Most common nail disorder; T. rubrum most common cause; confirm diagnosis before treating (KOH, culture, or PAS of nail clipping)

coverages.note: first-line (corporis, cruris, pedis) — Apply daily × 1–2 weeks (corporis/cruris) or 2–4 weeks (pedis); allylamines superior to azoles (37% relative reduction in treatment failure)
alternative (corporis, cruris, pedis) — Apply BID × 2–4 weeks; OTC; effective but slightly less than terbinafine
first-line (capitis — Trichophyton spp., onychomycosis) — Capitis: weight-based dosing × 4–6 weeks; Onychomycosis: 250 mg daily × 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails); monitor LFTs
first-line (capitis — Microsporum spp.) — 20–25 mg/kg/day (microsized) × 6–12 weeks; preferred for Microsporum infections; take with fatty food
alternative (capitis, onychomycosis) — Pulse dosing for onychomycosis: 200 mg BID × 1 week/month × 2–3 months; continuous: 200 mg daily; effective for both Trichophyton and Microsporum

organisms.name: Giardia lamblia
organisms.morphology: Flagellated protozoan; two forms: trophozoite (pear-shaped, "face-like" appearance with 2 nuclei, 4 pairs of flagella, ventral sucking disk — attaches to duodenal/jejunal mucosa) and cyst (oval, 4 nuclei — infectious form, resistant to chlorination)
organisms.features: Most common intestinal parasite worldwide; transmitted fecal-oral (contaminated water — "backpacker's diarrhea," daycare centers, MSM); cysts survive in cold water for months; does NOT invade mucosa (no blood in stool); causes malabsorption (fat-soluble vitamins, lactose); diagnosis: stool antigen (most sensitive), O&P (trophozoites or cysts), PCR
organisms.source: Contaminated water (streams, lakes, municipal water); fecal-oral; daycare centers; animals (beavers — "beaver fever")

presentations.symptoms: Acute: watery, foul-smelling diarrhea (non-bloody), bloating, flatulence, abdominal cramps, nausea, steatorrhea; Chronic: malabsorption, weight loss, failure to thrive (children), lactose intolerance
presentations.clues: Self-limited in 2–6 weeks in immunocompetent; can become chronic; no blood or mucus in stool (non-invasive); no eosinophilia

coverages.note: first-line — 2 g PO × 1 dose (adults); 50 mg/kg × 1 dose (children ≥3 yrs); cure rate >90%; best efficacy in meta-analyses
first-line (alternative) — 500 mg PO BID × 3 days (adults); FDA-approved for children ≥1 yr; comparable to metronidazole
