alternative — 250 mg PO TID × 5–7 days (adults); 15 mg/kg/day divided TID × 5–7 days (children); widely available but more GI side effects; NOT FDA-approved for giardiasis

organisms.name: Entamoeba histolytica
organisms.morphology: Two forms: trophozoite (motile, 10–60 μm, single nucleus with central karyosome, pseudopodia; pathognomonic finding: ingested erythrocytes within trophozoite) and cyst (10–15 μm, spherical, 4 nuclei when mature — infectious form); morphologically identical to non-pathogenic E. dispar (must differentiate with antigen testing or PCR)
organisms.features: Causes amebic dysentery and liver abscess; transmitted fecal-oral (contaminated water/food); invades colonic mucosa → flask-shaped ulcers (undermined edges); can disseminate hematogenously to liver (most common extraintestinal site); diagnosis: stool antigen (E. histolytica-specific), PCR, serology (positive in >90% of liver abscess), O&P (cannot distinguish from E. dispar); endemic in developing countries; risk groups: travelers, immigrants, MSM, institutionalized
organisms.source: Humans; fecal-oral; contaminated water/food
organisms.notes: ALL invasive amebiasis (colitis or liver abscess) must be followed by a luminal agent (paromomycin or iodoquinol) to eliminate intraluminal cysts and prevent relapse — tissue amebicides (metronidazole/tinidazole) do NOT reliably eradicate cysts. Asymptomatic carriers of confirmed E. histolytica should be treated with a luminal agent alone. E. dispar does NOT require treatment. Corticosteroids are CONTRAINDICATED (risk of fulminant colitis/perforation). Stool microscopy alone cannot distinguish E. histolytica from E. dispar — use antigen testing or PCR.

presentations.symptoms: Gradual onset bloody diarrhea (heme-positive), abdominal pain/cramping, tenesmus; fever in minority; can mimic IBD
presentations.clues: Flask-shaped ulcers on colonoscopy; cecum and ascending colon most commonly affected; complications: toxic megacolon, perforation, ameboma (mass lesion mimicking cancer); distinguish from bacterial dysentery (Shigella — more acute)

presentations.incubation: Weeks to months after intestinal infection
presentations.symptoms: Fever, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, right shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation); usually single, large, right lobe abscess; "anchovy paste" aspirate (necrotic hepatocytes, not true pus — few WBCs)
presentations.clues: Most common extraintestinal manifestation; 10:1 male predominance; serology positive >90%; concurrent intestinal infection in only ~30%; stool may be negative

presentations.symptoms: No symptoms; passes cysts in stool
presentations.clues: Must treat to prevent invasive disease and transmission; must confirm E. histolytica (not E. dispar) before treating

coverages.note: first-line (invasive/symptomatic) — 2 g PO daily × 3 days (colitis) or × 5 days (liver abscess); more effective and better tolerated than metronidazole in meta-analyses
first-line (alternative, invasive) — 500–750 mg PO/IV TID × 7–10 days; widely available; more GI side effects than tinidazole
first-line (luminal agent — MUST follow tissue amebicide) — 25–35 mg/kg/day PO divided TID × 7 days; eliminates intraluminal cysts; prevents relapse; also used alone for asymptomatic carriers
alternative (luminal agent) — 650 mg PO TID × 20 days; alternative to paromomycin
adjunct (liver abscess) — Consider if: no clinical response to antiparasitic therapy within 5–7 days, large abscess (>5–10 cm), left lobe abscess (risk of pericardial rupture), or imminent rupture

organisms.name: Plasmodium spp.
organisms.morphology: Intracellular parasite of erythrocytes; identified by Giemsa-stained thick and thin blood smears; P. falciparum: multiple ring forms per RBC, banana-shaped gametocytes, no schizonts in peripheral blood (sequestration); P. vivax: enlarged RBCs, Schüffner dots, ameboid trophozoites; P. ovale: oval RBCs, fimbriated edges; P. malariae: band-form trophozoites, rosette schizonts
organisms.features: Transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito (injection of sporozoites); life cycle: sporozoites → liver (exoerythrocytic stage, 1–2 weeks) → merozoites → RBCs (erythrocytic cycle — causes symptoms); P. vivax and P. ovale form hypnozoites (dormant liver stage → relapses weeks to years later); P. falciparum: sequestration of infected RBCs in microvasculature → severe/cerebral malaria; diagnosis: thick smear (detection), thin smear (speciation), rapid diagnostic test (HRP2 for P. falciparum, pLDH for all species); ~2000 cases/year in US (mostly travelers)
organisms.source: Female Anopheles mosquito; rarely: transfusion, vertical, needlestick
organisms.notes: Malaria is a medical emergency — diagnose and treat promptly. Thick and thin blood smears remain the gold standard (repeat q12–24h × 3 if initial negative). All patients with P. falciparum or severe disease should be hospitalized. IV quinidine is no longer available in the US. CDC Malaria Hotline available 24/7 for consultation. Post-artesunate delayed hemolysis: monitor hemoglobin and hemolytic markers weekly × 4 weeks. Pregnant women with severe malaria: IV artesunate is indicated in all trimesters.

presentations.incubation: P. falciparum: 10–14 days; P. vivax: 2–3 weeks (or months with some strains); P. malariae: ≥18 days
presentations.symptoms: Fever (paroxysmal — classically q48h for falciparum/vivax/ovale, q72h for malariae, but often irregular), chills, rigors, sweats, headache, myalgias, malaise, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea
presentations.clues: Thrombocytopenia (very common), anemia, splenomegaly, mild jaundice; ~80% of US cases are uncomplicated

presentations.symptoms: Cerebral malaria (altered consciousness, seizures, coma), severe anemia (Hb 5%, jaundice
presentations.clues: Mortality up to 40% for cerebral malaria; can progress from uncomplicated to severe within hours; P. falciparum causes >90% of severe cases

presentations.incubation: Weeks to years after initial infection
presentations.symptoms: Recurrence of fever, chills, anemia
presentations.clues: Due to hypnozoites (dormant liver stage); requires specific treatment with 8-aminoquinolines (primaquine or tafenoquine) to prevent relapse

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated, all species) — 4 tablets (80/480 mg) PO at 0, 8, 24, 36, 48, 60 hrs (6 doses total over 3 days); take with fatty food; ACT is WHO-recommended first-line for all uncomplicated malaria
first-line (alternative, uncomplicated) — 4 tablets (1000/400 mg) PO daily × 3 days; effective against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum; also used for chemoprophylaxis

first-line (chloroquine-sensitive areas only) — 600 mg base PO, then 300 mg at 6, 24, 48 hrs; effective for P. vivax (except Papua New Guinea/Indonesia), P. ovale, P. malariae; P. falciparum: only in Haiti, Central America west of Panama Canal

first-line (SEVERE malaria, all species) — 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24 hrs, then daily; transition to oral ACT when parasitemia 1% and patient can take PO; 35% mortality reduction vs quinine in adults; monitor for post-artesunate delayed hemolysis × 4 weeks
first-line (antirelapse, P. vivax/P. ovale) — 0.25–0.5 mg/kg base PO daily × 14 days; CHECK G6PD before prescribing (hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency); kills hypnozoites

first-line (antirelapse, alternative) — 300 mg PO × 1 dose; single-dose alternative to 14-day primaquine; CHECK G6PD (quantitative test required); contraindicated in G6PD deficiency, pregnancy, breastfeeding, age 16, psychiatric disorders

prophylaxis — Atovaquone-proguanil (daily, start 1–2 days before, continue 7 days after); Doxycycline (daily, start 1–2 days before, continue 30 days after); Mefloquine (weekly, start 1–2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after — FDA black box for neuropsychiatric effects); Chloroquine (weekly — chloroquine-sensitive areas only); Tafenoquine (loading then weekly — check G6PD)

organisms.name: Toxoplasma gondii
organisms.morphology: Three forms: tachyzoite (rapidly dividing, crescent-shaped — causes acute disease), bradyzoite (slowly dividing, within tissue cysts — latent infection), oocyst (produced only in cat intestine — environmental contamination); infects any nucleated cell
organisms.features: Definitive host: cats (sexual reproduction in intestinal epithelium → oocysts shed in feces); intermediate hosts: humans, mammals, birds; transmitted via: (1) ingestion of oocysts from cat feces/contaminated soil/water, (2) ingestion of tissue cysts in undercooked meat (pork, lamb, venison), (3) vertical transmission (transplacental — congenital toxoplasmosis), (4) organ transplant/blood transfusion (rare); seroprevalence ~11% in US, up to 80% in some countries; most infections asymptomatic in immunocompetent; reactivation in immunocompromised (HIV CD4 100) → toxoplasma encephalitis; diagnosis: serology (IgG = prior exposure, IgM = acute/recent), brain MRI (ring-enhancing lesions), PCR, brain biopsy if no response to empiric therapy
organisms.source: Cats (definitive host — oocysts in feces); undercooked meat (tissue cysts); soil/water contaminated with oocysts
organisms.notes: Empiric treatment for TE in HIV patients with ring-enhancing lesions and positive Toxoplasma IgG is standard — brain biopsy reserved for non-responders at 10–14 days. ~90% of patients respond clinically within 14 days. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) only if significant mass effect — discontinue ASAP. Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist — always give with leucovorin (NOT folic acid). Monitor CBC weekly during acute therapy (myelosuppression). Start ART within 2 weeks of TE treatment. Prevention: cook meat to safe temperatures, wash fruits/vegetables, avoid cat litter (pregnant women/immunocompromised).

presentations.symptoms: Usually asymptomatic (80–90%); if symptomatic: cervical lymphadenopathy (most common manifestation — non-tender, bilateral), low-grade fever, malaise, myalgias, hepatosplenomegaly
presentations.clues: Self-limited in immunocompetent; resembles mononucleosis; heterophile-negative

presentations.symptoms: Blurred vision, scotomata, eye pain, photophobia; fundoscopy: focal necrotizing retinitis ("headlight in the fog" — white/yellow lesion adjacent to old pigmented scar), vitritis
presentations.clues: Most common cause of posterior uveitis worldwide; can occur in immunocompetent (reactivation of congenital infection) or immunocompromised

presentations.symptoms: Headache, confusion, fever, focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia, ataxia), seizures, altered mental status
presentations.clues: MRI: multiple ring-enhancing lesions with surrounding edema (basal ganglia most common location); differential: primary CNS lymphoma (usually single lesion, periventricular); empiric treatment trial — clinical/radiographic response expected within 7–14 days; if no response by 10–14 days → brain biopsy; mortality 20–60% at 1 year despite treatment

presentations.symptoms: Classic triad: chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications (diffuse/scattered — vs. CMV which has periventricular calcifications); also: seizures, hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, thrombocytopenia, microcephaly
presentations.clues: Risk of transmission increases with gestational age (10–15% first trimester → 60–80% third trimester), but severity is inversely related (first trimester → most severe); many asymptomatic at birth but develop sequelae later (chorioretinitis, learning disabilities)

coverages.note: first-line (TE, acute treatment) — Pyrimethamine: 200 mg PO loading dose, then 50 mg (60 kg) or 75 mg (≥60 kg) PO daily; Sulfadiazine: 1000 mg (60 kg) or 1500 mg (≥60 kg) PO q6h; Leucovorin: 10–25 mg PO daily (prevent pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow suppression); × 6 weeks minimum (longer if extensive disease or incomplete response)
first-line (alternative, sulfa-allergic) — Clindamycin 600 mg IV/PO q6h; replaces sulfadiazine; must add separate PCP prophylaxis (clindamycin does not cover Pneumocystis)
alternative (TE) — TMP 5 mg/kg + SMX 25 mg/kg IV/PO BID; similar efficacy to pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine in small RCT; better tolerated; only IV option; increasingly used as primary alternative

after acute treatment — Pyrimethamine 25–50 mg PO daily + sulfadiazine 500–1000 mg PO q6h + leucovorin 10–25 mg PO daily; continue until CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART and completed initial therapy and asymptomatic
primary prophylaxis — For HIV with CD4 100 and positive Toxoplasma IgG; also provides PCP prophylaxis; alternative: dapsone + pyrimethamine + leucovorin, or atovaquone ± pyrimethamine + leucovorin
first-line (maternal acute toxoplasmosis, first trimester) — 1 g PO q8h; reduces vertical transmission by ~60%; available in US through FDA (IND protocol); does NOT treat established fetal infection

organisms.name: Trichomonas vaginalis
organisms.morphology: Flagellated protozoan, pear-shaped trophozoite with 4 anterior flagella and undulating membrane; no cyst form
organisms.features: Most prevalent nonviral STI (~3.7 million in US). Motile trophozoites on wet mount (51–65% sensitivity). NAATs are preferred diagnostic test. "Strawberry cervix" (colpitis macularis) in ~5% of women. Significant racial disparities (African Americans >4× more likely). In vitro nitroimidazole resistance ~4–10%.
organisms.source: Sexually transmitted; human-only reservoir

presentations.symptoms: dysuria, vaginal discharge (frothy yellow-green), vulvovaginal irritation; men often asymptomatic
presentations.clues: strawberry cervix pathognomonic; associated with increased HIV acquisition, preterm birth, cervical cancer
presentations.severity: mild-moderate but significant sequelae in women

coverages.note: first-line (women) — CDC 2021 recommended; multidose superior to single dose in women
first-line (men) — CDC 2021 recommended
alternative (women and men) — longer half-life, equivalent or superior to metronidazole; fewer GI side effects

coverages.medication: Metronidazole or tinidazole 2 g PO daily × 7 days
coverages.note: persistent/resistant infection — higher dose for treatment failure
avoid — does not reach therapeutic levels in urethra/perivaginal glands; less efficacious than oral

organisms.name: Cryptosporidium parvum/hominis
organisms.morphology: Intracellular protozoan; oocysts 4–6 μm, acid-fast positive; attaches to intestinal epithelial brush border
organisms.features: Transmitted via fecal-oral route (contaminated water, person-to-person). Oocysts resistant to chlorine disinfection. Self-limited in immunocompetent (1–2 weeks). Chronic/severe in immunocompromised (especially CD4 100). Can cause biliary tract disease in AIDS.
organisms.source: Contaminated water, person-to-person, animal contact; low infectious dose (~10 oocysts)

presentations.symptoms: profuse watery diarrhea (can exceed 10 L/day in AIDS), abdominal cramps, nausea, low-grade fever
presentations.clues: self-limited in immunocompetent; chronic/life-threatening in AIDS with CD4 100

presentations.symptoms: RUQ pain, cholangitis, sclerosing cholangiopathy
presentations.clues: may require ERCP/sphincterotomy

coverages.note: first-line (immunocompetent) — FDA-approved; 88% clinical response in children
first-line (HIV/AIDS) — mainstay of treatment; no antiparasitic consistently effective without ART

coverages.medication: Nitazoxanide 500–1000 mg PO BID × ≥14 days + ART
coverages.note: adjunctive (HIV, CD4 >50) — CIII recommendation; variable efficacy in severe immunosuppression

coverages.medication: Paromomycin 500 mg PO QID × 14–21 days + ART
coverages.note: alternative (HIV) — CIII; inconsistent results

organisms.name: Babesia microti
organisms.morphology: Intraerythrocytic piroplasm; ring forms on blood smear resembling Plasmodium but with "Maltese cross" (tetrad) pathognomonic; no pigment (hemozoin)
organisms.features: Transmitted by Ixodes scapularis tick (same vector as Lyme/Anaplasma). Also via blood transfusion (now screened). Endemic in NE and upper Midwest US. Severe in asplenic, elderly, immunocompromised (especially rituximab). Coinfection with Lyme/Anaplasma common. Diagnosis: blood smear + PCR.
organisms.source: Ixodes scapularis tick bite; blood transfusion; rare transplacental

presentations.incubation: 1–4 weeks after tick bite (up to 9 weeks post-transfusion)
presentations.symptoms: fever, chills, sweats, myalgia, fatigue, hemolytic anemia
presentations.clues: Maltese cross on smear; no hemozoin (unlike malaria); can cause splenic infarct/rupture
presentations.severity: mild in immunocompetent; fatal in asplenic/immunocompromised

coverages.medication: Atovaquone 750 mg PO BID + azithromycin 500–1000 mg PO day 1 then 250 mg daily
coverages.note: first-line (mild-moderate) — 7–10 days; fewer adverse effects than clindamycin/quinine

coverages.medication: Clindamycin 600 mg IV q6h + quinine 650 mg PO q6–8h
coverages.note: first-line (severe) — 7–10 days; 72% adverse effect rate; consider IV azithromycin + atovaquone as alternative

coverages.medication: Extended therapy ≥6 weeks (until blood smear negative × 2 weeks)
coverages.note: immunocompromised — higher azithromycin doses (500–1000 mg daily); multi-drug regimens may be needed
adjunctive (parasitemia ≥10%, severe hemolysis, organ compromise) — target ≥90% reduction in parasitemia

organisms.name: Strongyloides stercoralis
organisms.morphology: Filariform (L3) larvae penetrate skin; rhabditiform larvae in stool. Adult female ~2 mm in small intestinal mucosa. Unique autoinfective cycle allows indefinite persistence.
organisms.features: Infects 300–600 million worldwide. Autoinfection cycle → chronic asymptomatic infection (often with eosinophilia). Hyperinfection triggered by corticosteroids, HTLV-1, transplant immunosuppression. Hyperinfection mortality >60%. Enteric gram-negative bacteremia/meningitis accompanies dissemination. Screen before immunosuppression.
organisms.source: Soil-transmitted; larvae penetrate skin (bare feet); endemic in tropics/subtropics; autochthonous in rural US Southeast

presentations.symptoms: intermittent diarrhea, abdominal pain, urticaria (larva currens — serpiginous, rapidly migrating), eosinophilia
presentations.clues: may persist decades via autoinfection

presentations.symptoms: massive larval burden → GI bleeding, respiratory failure, intestinal obstruction; dissemination to CNS, liver; gram-negative sepsis/meningitis
presentations.clues: case fatality >60–87%

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated) — 88% cure rate with single dose; preferred over albendazole

coverages.medication: Ivermectin 200 μg/kg PO daily until stool/sputum negative × 2 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (hyperinfection) — continue until clearance; reduce immunosuppression; add antibiotics for secondary infections
alternative — less efficacious than ivermectin

organisms.name: Cyclospora cayetanensis
organisms.morphology: Oocysts 8–10 μm (twice the size of Cryptosporidium); acid-fast variable (pink to unstained); autofluorescent under UV
organisms.features: Foodborne outbreaks (fresh produce — berries, basil, lettuce). Seasonal (rainy season/summer). Oocysts require 1–2 weeks in environment to sporulate (not immediately infectious person-to-person). Worldwide prevalence ~3.55%.
organisms.source: Contaminated fresh produce and water; not directly person-to-person (oocysts need environmental maturation)

presentations.symptoms: protracted watery diarrhea (weeks to months if untreated), anorexia, weight loss, fatigue, bloating, nausea
presentations.clues: relapsing course; prolonged/severe in immunocompromised
presentations.severity: moderate; can be fatal in immunosuppressed

coverages.note: first-line (immunocompetent) — CDC recommended; resolves symptoms in 1–2 days

coverages.medication: TMP-SMX (160/800 mg) PO QID × 10 days → TMP-SMX 3×/week (secondary prophylaxis)
coverages.note: first-line (HIV/immunocompromised) — high recurrence rate without prophylaxis
alternative (sulfa-intolerant) — less effective than TMP-SMX
alternative (sulfa-intolerant + cipro-resistant) — limited data; not always effective

organisms.name: Naegleria fowleri
organisms.morphology: Exists as trophozoite, flagellate, and cyst forms. Trophozoite 10–35 μm with single nucleus and large karyosome. Enters via nasal mucosa → olfactory nerve → brain.
organisms.features: Causes primary amebic meningoencephalitis (PAM). Fatality rate >97%. Found in warm freshwater (lakes, hot springs, poorly maintained pools). Rare (~0–8 cases/year in US). Rapid progression (death within 5–7 days of symptom onset). Diagnosis: motile trophozoites in CSF, CSF PCR.
organisms.source: Warm freshwater exposure (swimming, diving, nasal irrigation with contaminated water); NOT transmitted person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: sudden severe frontal headache, fever, nausea/vomiting, stiff neck, altered mental status, seizures → rapid coma and death
presentations.clues: fulminant meningoencephalitis; hemorrhagic brain necrosis

coverages.medication: High-dose amphotericin B deoxycholate 1.5 mg/kg/day IV + intrathecal amphotericin B
coverages.note: first-line — based on rare survivor case reports

coverages.medication: Miltefosine + amphotericin B + azithromycin + fluconazole + rifampin
coverages.note: combination (survivor protocols) — CDC recommended multi-drug regimen; miltefosine available via CDC
adjunctive — to control cerebral edema

organisms.name: Acanthamoeba
organisms.morphology: Trophozoite 15–45 μm with acanthopodia (spine-like projections); double-walled cyst form highly resistant to disinfection
organisms.features: Ubiquitous in soil, water, air. Contact lens wear is primary risk factor for keratitis. Granulomatous amebic encephalitis (GAE) in immunocompromised. Cyst form resistant to most disinfectants including chlorine. Diagnosis: corneal scraping with culture on non-nutrient agar with E. coli overlay; confocal microscopy.
organisms.source: Ubiquitous in environment (soil, freshwater, tap water); contact lens contamination; NOT transmitted person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: severe eye pain (disproportionate to findings), photophobia, tearing, ring-shaped corneal infiltrate
presentations.clues: contact lens wearers; can mimic herpes keratitis

presentations.symptoms: headache, altered mental status, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, cranial nerve palsies
presentations.clues: immunocompromised (HIV/AIDS, transplant); subacute/chronic course (unlike Naegleria)

coverages.note: first-line (keratitis) — topical hourly initially → taper over months; prolonged treatment needed due to cyst resistance
first-line (GAE) — multi-drug combination; poor outcomes despite treatment

organisms.name: Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm)
organisms.morphology: Small white worms; female 8–13 mm, male 2–5 mm; eggs are oval, flattened on one side, 50–60 μm
organisms.features: Most common helminth in US/Europe. Primarily affects children. Scotch tape test (perianal) for diagnosis — eggs deposited at night. Autoinfection common. Highly contagious within households.
organisms.source: Fecal-oral; ingestion of eggs from contaminated hands, fomites, bedding; eggs viable on surfaces for 2–3 weeks

presentations.symptoms: intense perianal/perineal pruritus (worse at night), insomnia, irritability; rarely appendicitis, vulvovaginitis in girls
presentations.clues: visible worms at anus at night

coverages.medication: Mebendazole 100 mg PO single dose, repeat in 2 weeks
coverages.note: first-line — treats all household members simultaneously

coverages.medication: Albendazole 400 mg PO single dose, repeat in 2 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (alternative) — equally effective

coverages.medication: Pyrantel pamoate 11 mg/kg PO single dose (max 1 g), repeat in 2 weeks
coverages.note: alternative — OTC available

organisms.name: Ascaris lumbricoides
organisms.morphology: Largest intestinal roundworm; adult female 20–35 cm, male 15–30 cm; eggs are round with thick mammillated shell, 45–75 μm
organisms.features: Most common helminth worldwide (~800 million infected). Larvae migrate through lungs (Löffler syndrome — eosinophilic pneumonitis). Heavy burden → intestinal obstruction, biliary obstruction, pancreatitis. Diagnosis: eggs in stool; adult worms may be passed or vomited.
organisms.source: Soil-transmitted; ingestion of embryonated eggs from contaminated soil/food; endemic in tropics with poor sanitation

presentations.symptoms: cough, dyspnea, wheezing, fever, eosinophilia, transient pulmonary infiltrates
presentations.clues: during larval lung migration

presentations.symptoms: often asymptomatic; abdominal pain, malnutrition in children; heavy burden → intestinal obstruction, biliary obstruction, pancreatitis
presentations.clues: worm bolus on imaging
presentations.severity: mild to severe depending on burden

coverages.note: first-line — WHO recommended; >95% cure rate

coverages.medication: Mebendazole 500 mg PO single dose or 100 mg BID × 3 days
coverages.note: first-line (alternative)
alternative

organisms.name: Hookworm (Ancylostoma duodenale / Necator americanus)
organisms.morphology: Adult worms 7–13 mm; Ancylostoma has teeth, Necator has cutting plates at buccal capsule; eggs are thin-shelled, oval, 60–75 μm with visible morula
organisms.features: ~470 million infected worldwide. Larvae penetrate skin (bare feet) → lung migration → swallowed → attach to small intestinal mucosa and feed on blood. Major cause of iron deficiency anemia in endemic areas. Ancylostoma more pathogenic (0.2 mL blood/worm/day vs. 0.03 mL for Necator).
organisms.source: Soil-transmitted; filariform larvae penetrate skin from contaminated soil; endemic in tropics/subtropics

presentations.incubation: at site of larval skin penetration
presentations.symptoms: pruritic erythematous papules at entry site (usually feet)
presentations.clues: immediate

presentations.symptoms: iron deficiency anemia (microcytic, hypochromic), fatigue, pica, protein malnutrition, growth retardation in children; eosinophilia
presentations.clues: chronic blood loss from intestinal attachment

coverages.note: first-line — WHO recommended

coverages.medication: Mebendazole 500 mg PO single dose or 100 mg BID × 3 days
coverages.note: first-line (alternative) — less effective than albendazole for hookworm
adjunctive — essential for anemia correction

organisms.name: Schistosoma (S. mansoni, S. haematobium, S. japonicum)
organisms.morphology: Adult worms 1–2 cm; male has gynecophoral canal holding female. Eggs species-specific: S. mansoni — lateral spine; S. haematobium — terminal spine; S. japonicum — small lateral knob. Eggs in stool (mansoni/japonicum) or urine (haematobium).
organisms.features: ~250 million infected worldwide, 90% in sub-Saharan Africa. Cercariae from freshwater snails penetrate skin. Pathology driven by granulomatous response to eggs trapped in tissues. S. haematobium associated with squamous cell carcinoma of bladder. Diagnosis: eggs in stool/urine, serology, POC-CCA urine test.
organisms.source: Freshwater exposure; cercariae released by snail intermediate hosts penetrate skin

presentations.symptoms: fever, urticaria, myalgia, cough, eosinophilia, hepatosplenomegaly
presentations.clues: in non-immune travelers; hypersensitivity to parasite antigens

presentations.disease: Chronic intestinal schistosomiasis (S. mansoni, S. japonicum)
presentations.symptoms: abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea → hepatic fibrosis (Symmers pipestem fibrosis), portal hypertension, splenomegaly, variceal bleeding, ascites
presentations.clues: egg granulomas in liver

presentations.symptoms: dysuria, hematuria → hydronephrosis, renal failure, bladder calcification, squamous cell carcinoma of bladder
presentations.clues: eggs in bladder wall

coverages.medication: Praziquantel 40 mg/kg PO in 2 divided doses (S. mansoni/haematobium) or 60 mg/kg in 3 doses (S. japonicum)
coverages.note: first-line — single-day treatment; 60–70% cure rate; inexpensive ($0.15/treatment)
first-line (Katayama fever) — steroids first to control hypersensitivity; early praziquantel may worsen symptoms
alternative (S. mansoni only) — available in South America

organisms.name: Taenia solium (cysticercosis)
organisms.morphology: Adult tapeworm 2–7 m in intestine; cysticerci (larval cysts) are fluid-filled, 5–20 mm with invaginated scolex; eggs are round, 30–40 μm, radially striated
organisms.features: Humans are definitive host (tapeworm from undercooked pork) AND accidental intermediate host (cysticercosis from egg ingestion). Neurocysticercosis is most common parasitic CNS infection worldwide; causes ~29% of seizures in endemic areas. ~2000 US cases/year. Cysts go through viable → degenerating → calcified stages.
organisms.source: Tapeworm: ingestion of undercooked pork containing cysticerci. Cysticercosis: fecal-oral ingestion of T. solium eggs (from tapeworm carrier, NOT from pork)

presentations.symptoms: usually asymptomatic; passage of proglottids in stool
presentations.clues: mild

presentations.incubation: months to years after egg ingestion
presentations.symptoms: seizures (most common), headache, intracranial hypertension, chronic meningitis, focal neurologic deficits
presentations.clues: parenchymal cysts on MRI/CT; ring-enhancing lesions when degenerating

coverages.medication: Albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (max 1200 mg) PO in 2 divided doses × 10–14 days + dexamethasone
coverages.note: first-line (viable parenchymal NCC) — corticosteroids to prevent inflammatory reaction from cyst death
combination (>2 viable cysts) — IDSA/ASTMH 2017 guidelines; superior cyst resolution
symptomatic (seizures) — initiate before antiparasitic therapy; may need long-term

coverages.medication: Avoid antiparasitic therapy if diffuse cerebral edema or hydrocephalus
coverages.note: contraindicated — neurosurgery for hydrocephalus first

organisms.name: Echinococcus (E. granulosus, E. multilocularis)
organisms.morphology: E. granulosus: unilocular cysts with germinal layer, laminated layer, and pericyst; daughter cysts and protoscoleces ("hydatid sand"). E. multilocularis: infiltrative, tumor-like alveolar cysts without clear boundaries.
organisms.features: E. granulosus causes cystic echinococcosis (CE); E. multilocularis causes alveolar echinococcosis (AE). Liver most common site (60–70%). Cyst rupture → anaphylaxis. WHO-IWGE ultrasound classification (CE1–CE5) guides treatment. AE mimics hepatic malignancy. Diagnosis: imaging + serology.
organisms.source: E. granulosus: dogs (definitive host) → sheep/livestock (intermediate host); humans infected by ingesting eggs from dog feces. E. multilocularis: foxes → rodents; humans accidental intermediate host.

presentations.symptoms: often incidental finding; hepatomegaly, RUQ pain/fullness; cyst rupture → anaphylaxis, secondary seeding; lung cysts → cough, chest pain
presentations.clues: unilocular cyst with daughter cysts on imaging

presentations.symptoms: mimics hepatic malignancy; progressive hepatic destruction, biliary obstruction, metastasis-like spread to lungs/brain
presentations.clues: infiltrative mass on imaging

coverages.medication: Albendazole 10–15 mg/kg/day (max 800 mg) PO in 2 divided doses
coverages.note: first-line (medical) — continuous dosing (cyclical no longer recommended); parasitostatic; monitor LFTs and CBC
first-line (CE1/CE3a, >5 cm) — percutaneous; start albendazole before procedure

coverages.medication: Surgery (total pericystectomy or hepatectomy) + peri-operative albendazole
coverages.note: first-line (large/complicated cysts) — curative; albendazole 1–6 months post-op
combination — higher scolicidal activity than albendazole alone
inactive cysts (CE4/CE5) — surveillance imaging; no active treatment needed

organisms.name: Trichinella spiralis
organisms.morphology: Larvae encyst in striated muscle forming nurse cells; encysted larvae are coiled, 1 mm; adults are small (1–4 mm) in small intestinal mucosa
organisms.features: Acquired from undercooked pork or wild game (bear, boar). Outbreaks linked to specific meals. Periorbital edema + eosinophilia + myalgia is classic triad. Diagnosis: serology (antibodies appear 2–3 weeks after infection); muscle biopsy shows encysted larvae. Elevated CPK/LDH.
organisms.source: Ingestion of undercooked meat containing encysted larvae (pork, wild boar, bear, walrus)

presentations.incubation: Phase 1 (intestinal, 1–2 days): diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea. Phase 2 (muscle invasion, 2–8 weeks): periorbital/facial edema, myalgia, fever, eosinophilia (often >50%), splinter hemorrhages, conjunctival hemorrhage
presentations.symptoms: classic triad: periorbital edema + eosinophilia + myalgia
presentations.clues: mild to severe; myocarditis and CNS involvement can be fatal

coverages.note: first-line — most effective early in infection (intestinal phase)

coverages.medication: Mebendazole 200–400 mg PO TID × 3 days then 400–500 mg TID × 10 days
coverages.note: alternative
adjunctive — for severe myositis, myocarditis, CNS involvement; taper over 2 weeks

organisms.name: Trypanosoma cruzi (Chagas disease)
organisms.morphology: Trypomastigotes in blood (C-shaped with undulating membrane and kinetoplast); amastigotes (intracellular, no flagellum) in tissues
organisms.features: Endemic in Latin America; ~300,000 infected in US (immigration). Transmitted by triatomine ("kissing bug") vector. Also via blood transfusion, organ transplant, congenital, oral (contaminated food). Cardiac involvement in 20–30% of chronically infected. Leading cause of infectious cardiomyopathy worldwide. Diagnosis: serology (2 different assays); PCR for acute phase.
organisms.source: Triatomine bug feces (rubbed into bite wound or mucous membranes); blood transfusion; organ transplant; congenital; oral (contaminated food/juice)

presentations.symptoms: fever, malaise, hepatosplenomegaly; Romaña sign (unilateral periorbital edema at inoculation site); chagoma (skin nodule); rarely myocarditis/meningoencephalitis
presentations.clues: often unrecognized; self-resolves in 4–8 weeks

presentations.symptoms: asymptomatic; positive serology; normal ECG/imaging
presentations.clues: 60–70% remain indeterminate lifelong

presentations.symptoms: dilated cardiomyopathy (CHF, arrhythmias, thromboembolism, sudden death); megaesophagus (dysphagia, aspiration); megacolon (constipation, volvulus)
presentations.clues: apical aneurysm on echo; RBBB + LAFB on ECG

coverages.medication: Benznidazole 5 mg/kg/day PO in 2 divided doses × 60 days
coverages.note: first-line — FDA-approved; best efficacy in acute/early chronic; GI and dermatologic side effects common

coverages.medication: Nifurtimox 8–10 mg/kg/day PO in 3–4 divided doses × 60–90 days
coverages.note: alternative — more side effects than benznidazole; used when benznidazole unavailable

coverages.medication: Treatment benefit uncertain in established chronic cardiomyopathy
coverages.note: BENEFIT trial showed parasitological clearance but no reduction in cardiac outcomes over 5 years

organisms.name: Leishmania (L. donovani, L. tropica, L. braziliensis, L. major)
organisms.morphology: Amastigotes (2–4 μm, intracellular in macrophages, with kinetoplast visible on Giemsa stain); promastigotes (flagellated, in sandfly vector)
organisms.features: Transmitted by sandfly (Phlebotomus in Old World, Lutzomyia in New World). Three clinical forms based on species and host immunity. Visceral (kala-azar) is fatal without treatment. Diagnosis: tissue biopsy/aspirate showing amastigotes, PCR, rK39 rapid test (visceral). Endemic in >90 countries.
organisms.source: Sandfly bite; rare: blood transfusion, congenital, needle-sharing

presentations.incubation: weeks to months after sandfly bite
presentations.symptoms: painless papule → ulcer with raised borders and clean base; single or multiple; heals spontaneously in months (Old World) but may scar
presentations.clues: L. major, L. tropica (Old World); L. braziliensis, L. mexicana (New World)

presentations.symptoms: destructive inflammation of nasal/oral/pharyngeal mucosa; nasal septal perforation, palate destruction
presentations.clues: L. braziliensis (New World)

presentations.symptoms: fever, massive hepat

coverages.medication: Liposomal amphotericin B 3 mg/kg/day IV days 1–5, 14, 21 (total 21 mg/kg)
coverages.note: first-line (visceral, immunocompetent) — FDA-approved; >95% cure rate; preferred in US/Europe
first-line (visceral, oral option) — FDA-approved; teratogenic — contraindicated in pregnancy

coverages.medication: Sodium stibogluconate (pentavalent antimony) 20 mg/kg/day IV/IM × 28 days
coverages.note: first-line (Old World CL, WHO) — cardiotoxic; resistance increasing in India

coverages.medication: Intralesional sodium stibogluconate or cryotherapy or topical paromomycin
coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated Old World CL) — local therapy for small lesions
first-line (visceral in HIV) — higher doses and secondary prophylaxis needed; high relapse rate without ART

organisms.name: Gardnerella vaginalis
organisms.morphology: Gram-variable (thin-walled, appears gram-negative but structurally gram-positive); small pleomorphic rod/coccobacillus
organisms.features: Key organism in bacterial vaginosis (BV); found in ~95% of BV cases. Forms tenacious polymicrobial biofilm on vaginal epithelium. "Clue cells" (vaginal epithelial cells studded with adherent bacteria) are pathognomonic for BV. Also found in healthy vaginal flora — not pathogenic alone. Diagnosis of BV: Amsel criteria (≥3 of 4: thin homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells) or Nugent score on Gram stain. NAATs increasingly used.
organisms.source: Normal vaginal flora; sexually associated but not a classic STI; biofilm transmission between partners
organisms.notes: G. vaginalis has poor in vitro susceptibility to metronidazole, but the hydroxy-metabolite is active; clinical efficacy is established. Recurrence rate ~50% within 12 months due to biofilm persistence.

presentations.symptoms: thin, homogeneous, grayish-white malodorous ("fishy") vaginal discharge; often asymptomatic
presentations.clues: clue cells on wet mount; pH >4.5; positive whiff test
presentations.severity: mild but associated with preterm birth, PID, increased HIV acquisition

coverages.note: first-line — CDC 2021 recommended
first-line (alternative) — topical
first-line (alternative) — topical
first-line (alternative) — FDA-approved; convenient single dose

organisms.name: Staphylococcus saprophyticus
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in clusters; coagulase-negative; novobiocin-resistant (key distinguishing feature from other CoNS)
organisms.features: Second most common cause of uncomplicated UTI in young sexually active women (5–20% of community UTIs). Seasonal peak in late summer/early fall. Novobiocin resistance distinguishes it from S. epidermidis. Rarely causes complicated infections. Intrinsically resistant to fosfomycin.
organisms.source: GI/GU flora; colonizes perineum and urogenital tract; associated with sexual activity in young women

presentations.symptoms: dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; occasionally hematuria
presentations.clues: young sexually active women; second most common uropathogen after E. coli

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated UTI) — excellent activity
first-line (alternative) — good activity; check local resistance
alternative — if nitrofurantoin/TMP-SMX not tolerated
avoid — intrinsic resistance

organisms.name: Acinetobacter baumannii
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus (often appears as diplococci); strictly aerobic; non-fermenting; oxidase-negative
organisms.features: Major nosocomial pathogen, especially in ICU and ventilated patients. Survives on surfaces for weeks. Rapidly acquires resistance — carbapenem-resistant A. baumannii (CRAB) is CDC "urgent threat." Intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics. OXA-type carbapenemases are primary resistance mechanism. Often colonizer — must distinguish from true infection.
organisms.source: Hospital environment (surfaces, equipment, hands of HCWs); rarely community-acquired; associated with trauma/burns, military wounds

presentations.symptoms: fever, purulent secretions, new infiltrate on CXR
presentations.clues: ICU patients; high mortality (40–70% for CRAB)

presentations.symptoms: sepsis, surgical site infections, catheter-associated UTI
presentations.clues: immunocompromised, ICU, burns

coverages.note: first-line (CRAB) — sulbactam component has intrinsic activity against A. baumannii
first-line (CRAB, alternative) — nephrotoxic; often used in combination
alternative (CRAB) — siderophore cephalosporin; FDA-approved for HABP/VABP; clinical data mixed for A. baumannii

coverages.medication: Combination therapy recommended for CRAB (e.g., high-dose ampicillin-sulbactam + polymyxin ± meropenem or minocycline)
coverages.note: preferred approach — monotherapy associated with worse outcomes

organisms.name: Rickettsia rickettsii (Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus; obligate intracellular; cannot be cultured on standard media; tropism for vascular endothelium
organisms.features: Most lethal tickborne disease in the Americas. Case fatality 20–25% without treatment, 5% with early doxycycline. Transmitted by Dermacentor variabilis (American dog tick) and D. andersoni (Rocky Mountain wood tick). Rash appears day 3–5 (starts wrists/ankles → centripetal spread, becomes petechial). Classic triad: fever + headache + rash (present in only 60–70% at presentation). Diagnosis: serology (IFA, acute + convalescent); treat empirically — do NOT wait for confirmation.
organisms.source: Tick bite (Dermacentor spp.); endemic in SE/south-central US; peak spring-summer
organisms.notes: Beta-lactams, aminoglycosides, macrolides, and fluoroquinolones are NOT effective against Rickettsia

presentations.symptoms: abrupt high fever, severe headache, myalgia → maculopapular rash (wrists/ankles → trunk, becomes petechial) → vasculitis → multiorgan failure (encephalitis, ARDS, DIC, renal failure)
presentations.clues: classic triad: fever + headache + rash; may lack rash early ("spotless RMSF")

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV BID × 7 days (or ≥3 days after defervescence)
coverages.note: first-line (ALL ages, including children) — CDC recommended; do NOT delay for lab confirmation; use of other antibiotics increases mortality
alternative (only if severe doxycycline allergy) — inferior outcomes; risk of aplastic anemia

organisms.name: Bartonella henselae (cat-scratch disease)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative, small curved rod; fastidious; requires prolonged culture (2–6 weeks) on blood agar with CO₂; Warthin-Starry silver stain highlights organisms in tissue
organisms.features: Transmitted by cat scratch or bite (especially kittens); cat flea (Ctenocephalides felis) is vector between cats. Most common cause of chronic lymphadenopathy in children/young adults. Also causes bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis hepatis in immunocompromised (HIV). Diagnosis: serology (IFA IgM/IgG), PCR of lymph node aspirate, histopathology (stellate granulomas).
organisms.source: Cat scratch/bite (especially kittens 1 year); cat flea vector; cats are reservoir (bacteremic)

presentations.incubation: 3–10 days after scratch → papule at inoculation site; 1–3 weeks later → regional lymphadenopathy (tender, may suppurate)
presentations.symptoms: self-limited in immunocompetent (resolves 2–4 months); fever in ~30%
presentations.clues: mild-moderate

presentations.symptoms: vascular proliferative lesions (skin, liver, spleen); can mimic Kaposi sarcoma
presentations.clues: severe without treatment

coverages.note: first-line — self-limited; antibiotics do not clearly shorten course

coverages.medication: Azithromycin 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg daily × 4 days
coverages.note: first-line (if treating CSD) — only RCT showing benefit (lymph node volume reduction)

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + rifampin 300 mg PO BID × ≥3 months
coverages.note: first-line (bacillary angiomatosis/peliosis, immunocompromised) — prolonged treatment needed

organisms.name: Pasteurella multocida (animal bites)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus; bipolar staining ("safety pin" appearance on Wright stain); facultative anaerobe; oxidase-positive
organisms.features: Most common pathogen in cat bite infections (75%); also dog bites. Rapid onset of infection (24 hours after bite). Can cause cellulitis, abscess, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and rarely meningitis or endocarditis. Intrinsically resistant to first-generation cephalosporins, clindamycin, erythromycin, and vancomycin.
organisms.source: Normal oral flora of cats (70–90%) and dogs (50–65%); transmitted by bite, scratch, or licking of wounds

presentations.symptoms: intense pain, erythema, swelling, purulent drainage at bite site; lymphangitis; can progress to septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, tenosynovitis
presentations.clues: rapid onset distinguishes from other bite pathogens
presentations.severity: moderate; can be severe if deep structures involved

coverages.note: first-line (prophylaxis and treatment) — covers Pasteurella + anaerobes + streptococci
first-line (severe/hospitalized) — IV equivalent

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID or TMP-SMX DS PO BID (+ metronidazole for anaerobic coverage)
coverages.note: alternative (penicillin-allergic)
avoid — intrinsically resistant; common prescribing error

organisms.name: Anaplasma phagocytophilum / Ehrlichia chaffeensis
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative; obligate intracellular; form morulae (microcolonies) within host cells. Anaplasma: infects neutrophils (morulae in granulocytes). Ehrlichia: infects monocytes/macrophages (morulae in monocytes).
organisms.features: Tickborne. Anaplasma: transmitted by Ixodes scapularis (same as Lyme/Babesia); NE and upper Midwest US. Ehrlichia: transmitted by Amblyomma americanum (lone star tick); SE and south-central US. Coinfection with Lyme/Babesia possible (Anaplasma). Diagnosis: morulae on blood smear (sensitivity ~20–80%), PCR (preferred acute), serology (convalescent). Leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated transaminases are classic lab findings.
organisms.source: Tick bite; Anaplasma — Ixodes scapularis; Ehrlichia — Amblyomma americanum
organisms.notes: Beta-lactams, aminoglycosides, macrolides, TMP-SMX are NOT effective. Use of TMP-SMX increases mortality risk.

presentations.disease: Human granulocytic anaplasmosis (HGA) / Human monocytic ehrlichiosis (HME)
presentations.symptoms: fever, headache, myalgia, malaise; leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated AST/ALT
presentations.clues: rash uncommon in anaplasmosis (~10%); rash more common in ehrlichiosis (~30%, especially children)
presentations.severity: mild-moderate; severe in elderly/immunocompromised (HLH, meningoencephalitis, ARDS)

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV BID × 10–14 days (or ≥3 days after defervescence)
coverages.note: first-line (ALL ages) — CDC recommended; empiric treatment on clinical suspicion; do NOT delay
alternative (pregnancy, severe doxycycline allergy) — limited data; used in pregnancy

organisms.name: Fusobacterium necrophorum (Lemierre syndrome)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative, obligate anaerobic, pleomorphic rod (filamentous); non-spore-forming
organisms.features: Normal oropharyngeal flora. Causes Lemierre syndrome (septic thrombophlebitis of internal jugular vein following pharyngitis). Primarily affects healthy adolescents/young adults. Classic sequence: pharyngitis → peritonsillar infection → internal jugular vein thrombosis → septic emboli (lungs most common). Mortality ~5% with treatment. Diagnosis: blood cultures (anaerobic bottles), CT neck with contrast showing IJV thrombosis.
organisms.source: Normal oropharyngeal flora; endogenous infection following pharyngitis/peritonsillar abscess

presentations.symptoms: high spiking fevers, rigors, neck pain/swelling (IJV thrombosis), septic pulmonary emboli (bilateral nodular infiltrates, cavitation), metastatic abscesses (joints, liver, brain)
presentations.clues: "postanginal sepsis"; healthy young adults

coverages.medication: Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h
coverages.note: first-line — excellent anaerobic coverage

coverages.medication: Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h + third-generation cephalosporin
coverages.note: first-line (alternative combination) — metronidazole has excellent Fusobacterium activity
alternative — for severe/refractory cases
controversial — no RCTs; consider for extensive IJV thrombosis or progressive thrombosis despite antibiotics
avoid — increasing resistance in F. necrophorum

organisms.name: Stenotrophomonas maltophilia
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; strictly aerobic; non-fermenting; oxidase-negative (unlike Pseudomonas); motile with polar flagella
organisms.features: Nosocomial opportunist, especially in immunocompromised and ICU patients. Intrinsically resistant to carbapenems (L1 and L2 beta-lactamases), aminoglycosides, and most beta-lactams. Often colonizer — must distinguish from true infection. Mortality >50% in HCT recipients. Susceptibility testing is challenging.
organisms.source: Hospital environment (water systems, respiratory equipment); soil and plants; rarely community-acquired

presentations.incubation: in immunocompromised, prolonged ICU stay, prior broad-spectrum antibiotics (especially carbapenems)
presentations.symptoms: fever, respiratory decline, bacteremia
presentations.clues: high mortality in neutropenic/HCT patients

coverages.medication: TMP-SMX 8–12 mg/kg/day (trimethoprim component) IV divided q6–12h
coverages.note: first-line — historical standard; resistance emerging

coverages.medication: Combination therapy preferred for serious infections: TMP-SMX + cefiderocol or levofloxacin or minocycline
coverages.note: first-line (severe) — IDSA 2024 recommends ≥2 active agents initially
alternative — limited clinical data but in vitro activity
alternative — near 100% in vitro susceptibility; limited clinical data
avoid — intrinsic resistance

organisms.name: Serratia marcescens
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative rod; Enterobacteriaceae; motile; some strains produce red pigment (prodigiosin) at room temperature
organisms.features: Nosocomial pathogen. Produces AmpC beta-lactamase (inducible) — can develop resistance to third-generation cephalosporins during therapy ("AmpC derepression"). Associated with contaminated IV solutions, respiratory equipment, and hand transmission. Can cause outbreaks in NICUs. Intrinsically resistant to colistin/polymyxins.
organisms.source: Hospital environment (contaminated solutions, equipment); soil, water; GI colonization

presentations.symptoms: UTI, pneumonia, bloodstream infection, wound infection, meningitis (neonates)
presentations.clues: red-pigmented colonies on culture (not always)

coverages.note: first-line (serious infections) — not affected by AmpC
first-line (alternative, if susceptible) — check susceptibility
first-line (alternative) — stable to AmpC (unlike ceftriaxone)
alternative (UTI) — if susceptible
use with caution — risk of AmpC derepression and treatment failure; avoid for serious infections
avoid — intrinsic resistance

organisms.name: Nocardia (N. asteroides complex, N. brasiliensis, N. farcinica)
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive, weakly acid-fast (modified Kinyoun stain), filamentous branching rods; aerobic; slow-growing (2–14 days on culture)
organisms.features: Ubiquitous soil saprophyte. Opportunistic — primarily affects immunocompromised (transplant, chronic steroids, HIV, malignancy). Pulmonary nocardiosis most common. Hematogenous dissemination to brain (brain abscess) in ~30% of disseminated cases. Must speciate — N. farcinica is more resistant. Diagnosis: modified acid-fast stain, culture (hold cultures ≥2 weeks), 16S rRNA sequencing for speciation.
organisms.source: Soil; inhalation of airborne organisms; rarely direct inoculation (cutaneous)

presentations.symptoms: cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss; cavitary or nodular infiltrates on imaging
presentations.clues: mimics TB, lung cancer, or fungal infection

presentations.symptoms: brain abscess (ring-enhancing lesions), skin nodules/abscesses, disseminated disease
presentations.clues: immunocompromised; brain imaging recommended for all pulmonary cases

presentations.symptoms: cellulitis, mycetoma, lymphocutaneous (sporotrichoid) spread
presentations.clues: tropical/subtropical; immunocompetent

coverages.medication: TMP-SMX 15 mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV divided q6–8h
coverages.note: first-line — backbone of therapy; oral step-down when improving
first-line (severe/disseminated, especially N. farcinica) — triple therapy until clinical improvement
alternative (sulfa-intolerant or resistant) — excellent CNS penetration; monitor for toxicity

coverages.medication: Duration: 6–12 months (pulmonary); ≥12 months (CNS/disseminated); lifelong suppression if ongoing immunosuppression

organisms.name: Actinomyces israelii
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive, non-acid-fast, filamentous branching rods; anaerobic to microaerophilic; slow-growing; forms "sulfur granules" (yellow macroscopic granules = colonies of organisms)
organisms.features: Normal flora of oropharynx, GI tract, and female genital tract. Causes chronic, indolent infections that cross tissue planes and form draining sinuses. Three classic forms: cervicofacial (most common, 50%), thoracic, abdominal/pelvic. Associated with IUD use (pelvic actinomycosis). "Sulfur granules" in discharge are pathognomonic. Often polymicrobial (with companion bacteria). Diagnosis: culture (anaerobic, hold 2–3 weeks), histopathology showing sulfur granules.
organisms.source: Endogenous flora; infection follows disruption of mucosal barriers (dental procedures, trauma, IUD, aspiration)
organisms.notes: Metronidazole has NO activity against Actinomyces. Surgery may be needed for extensive disease, fistulae, or diagnostic uncertainty.

presentations.incubation: weeks to months after dental procedure/trauma
presentations.symptoms: painless or mildly tender jaw/neck mass → draining sinus tracts with sulfur granules; crosses tissue planes
presentations.clues: most common form

presentations.symptoms: cough, chest pain, weight loss; mass lesion crossing fissures/chest wall; may form empyema or chest wall sinus
presentations.clues: mimics lung cancer or TB

presentations.symptoms: pelvic mass, tubo-ovarian abscess, fistulae
presentations.clues: associated with IUD use (especially >5 years)

coverages.medication: Penicillin G 18–24 million units/day IV divided q4h × 2–6 weeks → amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID × 6–12 months
coverages.note: first-line — prolonged treatment essential to prevent relapse
alternative (penicillin-allergic)
alternative — good activity

organisms.name: Brucella (B. melitensis, B. abortus, B. suis, B. canis)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative, small coccobacillus; facultative intracellular; non-motile; slow-growing (culture 5–21 days); BSL-3 pathogen (lab notification required)
organisms.features: Zoonotic — most common bacterial zoonosis worldwide. B. melitensis (goats/sheep) most virulent. Transmitted via unpasteurized dairy, direct animal contact, or inhalation. Undulant fever pattern. Can cause any organ involvement. Chronic relapsing course without adequate treatment. Diagnosis: blood cultures (hold 4 weeks), serology (SAT ≥1:160), PCR. Lab personnel must be notified (BSL-3 hazard).
organisms.source: Unpasteurized dairy products (cheese, milk); direct contact with infected animals (cattle, goats, sheep, pigs, dogs); inhalation (occupational — farmers, veterinarians, lab workers)

presentations.symptoms: undulant fever (evening rise, morning fall), drenching sweats, arthralgias/myalgias, hepatosplenomegaly, fatigue, weight loss
presentations.clues: "undulant fever"; can affect any organ

presentations.symptoms: sacroiliitis/spondylitis (most common focal), epididymo-orchitis, neurobrucellosis, endocarditis (rare but leading cause of death)
presentations.clues: osteoarticular involvement in 20–40%

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 6 weeks + streptomycin 1 g IM daily × 2–3 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (WHO recommended) — lowest relapse rate (~5%)

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 6 weeks + rifampin 600–900 mg PO daily × 6 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (alternative, all-oral) — higher relapse rate (~15%) than doxy + streptomycin
first-line (spondylitis, neurobrucellosis, endocarditis) — triple therapy for focal/complicated disease; longer duration (3–6 months)
avoid — unacceptably high relapse rates

organisms.name: Francisella tularensis (tularemia)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative, tiny coccobacillus; facultative intracellular; fastidious (requires cysteine-enriched media); BSL-3 pathogen; CDC Category A bioterrorism agent
organisms.features: Extremely low infectious dose (~10 organisms). Multiple transmission routes: tick bite, animal contact (rabbits, rodents), inhalation, contaminated water. Six clinical forms based on portal of entry. Ulceroglandular most common (75%). Diagnosis: serology (acute + convalescent), PCR, culture (notify lab — BSL-3 hazard).
organisms.source: Tick bite (Dermacentor, Amblyomma); contact with infected animals (rabbits, hares, rodents); deer fly bite; inhalation; contaminated water

presentations.symptoms: painful ulcer at inoculation site + tender regional lymphadenopathy (may suppurate)
presentations.clues: most common form (75%)

presentations.symptoms: fever, cough, substernal chest tightness, bilateral infiltrates
presentations.clues: inhalation exposure or hematogenous spread; bioterrorism concern

presentations.symptoms: high fever, prostration, no localizing signs
presentations.clues: diagnosis of exclusion

coverages.medication: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h × 10 days
coverages.note: first-line — preferred aminoglycoside
first-line (historical gold standard) — limited availability
first-line (mild-moderate, oral option) — higher relapse rate than aminoglycosides; longer course needed
alternative — good clinical data; oral option
avoid — clinical failures despite in vitro susceptibility

organisms.name: Coxiella burnetii (Q fever)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative, small coccobacillus; obligate intracellular; survives in acidic phagolysosome; phase variation (phase I = virulent, phase II = avirulent); extremely resistant to environmental conditions
organisms.features: Zoonotic — cattle, sheep, goats are primary reservoirs. Transmitted by inhalation of contaminated aerosols (birth products, manure). Extremely low infectious dose (1 organism). Acute Q fever is usually self-limited. Chronic Q fever (endocarditis) develops in ~5% of acute cases, especially with pre-existing valvular disease. Diagnosis: serology (IFA) — phase II antibodies in acute, phase I antibodies in chronic (phase I IgG ≥1:800 = chronic).
organisms.source: Inhalation of contaminated aerosols from infected livestock (parturient animals); unpasteurized dairy; rarely tick-transmitted

presentations.symptoms: high fever, severe headache, myalgia, pneumonia (atypical), hepatitis; ~50% asymptomatic
presentations.clues: no rash (unlike other rickettsial diseases); "culture-negative" pneumonia

presentations.symptoms: culture-negative endocarditis (most common manifestation); vascular graft infection; hepatitis
presentations.clues: pre-existing valvular disease is major risk factor; phase I IgG ≥1:800

coverages.note: first-line (acute Q fever) — most effective if started within 3 days of symptom onset

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID + hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO TID × ≥18 months
coverages.note: first-line (chronic Q fever/endocarditis) — hydroxychloroquine alkalinizes phagolysosome, enhancing doxycycline activity; monitor for retinal toxicity
alternative (pregnancy, first trimester) — doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy

organisms.name: Haemophilus ducreyi (chancroid)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus; forms short chains ("school of fish" or "railroad track" pattern on Gram stain); fastidious; requires special culture media (sensitivity 80%); non-motile
organisms.features: Causes chancroid, a sexually transmitted genital ulcer disease. Declining prevalence globally but still endemic in parts of Africa and Caribbean. Also recently identified as cause of chronic cutaneous ulcers in children in yaws-endemic Pacific regions. Diagnosis is often clinical (probable diagnosis if painful ulcer + negative syphilis/HSV testing). No FDA-cleared NAAT available in US. Culture sensitivity 80%.
organisms.source: Sexually transmitted; direct contact with ulcer exudate; no animal reservoir
organisms.notes: HIV-infected patients may have slower healing and higher treatment failure rates with single-dose regimens; close follow-up recommended. Fluctuant buboes should be aspirated or incised and drained.

presentations.symptoms: one or more painful, soft genital ulcers with ragged undermined edges and purulent base; tender inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo) in ~50%, may suppurate and drain
presentations.clues: painful ulcer (vs. painless syphilitic chancre); soft base (vs. indurated syphilis)
presentations.severity: moderate; genital scarring and fistulae in advanced cases

coverages.note: first-line — CDC 2021 recommended
first-line (alternative) — CDC 2021 recommended
alternative — CDC 2021 recommended

coverages.medication: Erythromycin base 500 mg PO TID × 7 days
coverages.note: alternative — CDC 2021 recommended

organisms.name: Ureaplasma urealyticum / Mycoplasma genitalium
organisms.morphology: Smallest free-living organisms; lack cell wall (inherently resistant to beta-lactams and vancomycin); Ureaplasma: urease-positive; M. genitalium: flask-shaped, extremely slow-growing (weeks to months in culture)
organisms.features: M. genitalium: established cause of nongonococcal urethritis (NGU) in men (30–40% of persistent/recurrent NGU) and cervicitis/PID in women. Prevalence ~10% at urogenital sites. Macrolide resistance 30–100% depending on region (23S rRNA mutations). FDA-cleared NAATs available. Screening of asymptomatic persons NOT recommended. Ureaplasma: common colonizer of GU tract; pathogenic role debated; associated with NGU, chorioamnionitis, neonatal pneumonia/meningitis in preterm infants.
organisms.source: Sexually transmitted (M. genitalium); GU colonizer (Ureaplasma — vertical transmission to neonates)

presentations.symptoms: dysuria, penile irritation, urethral discharge; persistent/recurrent after chlamydia treatment
presentations.clues: 30–40% of persistent NGU

presentations.symptoms: cervicitis, postcoital bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding, lower abdominal pain; linked to infertility
presentations.clues: mild-moderate

presentations.symptoms: pneumonia, meningitis, bronchopulmonary dysplasia in preterm infants
presentations.clues: preterm neonates

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7 days → moxifloxacin 400 mg PO daily × 7 days
coverages.note: first-line (M. genitalium, CDC 2021 recommended sequential therapy) — doxycycline reduces bacterial load before moxifloxacin

coverages.medication: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7 days → azithromycin 1 g PO day 1 then 500 mg daily × 3 days
coverages.note: alternative (M. genitalium, if macrolide-susceptible) — resistance-guided therapy preferred if available
first-line (Ureaplasma urethritis)
alternative (Ureaplasma)
avoid — no cell wall; inherently resistant

organisms.name: Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis biotype I)
organisms.morphology: Gram-positive cocci in chains; group D streptococcus (Lancefield); catalase-negative; bile-esculin positive; grows in 6.5% NaCl (variable)
organisms.features: Strong association with colorectal neoplasia — colonoscopy is mandatory after any S. gallolyticus bacteremia or endocarditis. Patients with S. gallolyticus bacteremia have ~5–8% incidence of CRC and ~60% have adenomas/carcinomas on colonoscopy. Also a leading cause of streptococcal infective endocarditis in elderly. HR for CRC diagnosis after bacteremia: 5.73–10.3 compared to controls. Taxonomic reclassification from S. bovis has caused confusion — speciation is clinically important.
organisms.source: Normal GI flora (colonizes colon); associated with colonic mucosal disruption from neoplasia

presentations.symptoms: fever, chills; often without obvious source
presentations.clues: must evaluate for endocarditis AND colorectal neoplasia

presentations.symptoms: fever, new murmur, embolic phenomena; often affects native valves in elderly
presentations.clues: S. gallolyticus (biotype I) has OR 16.6 for IE vs. biotype II

coverages.medication: Penicillin G 12–18 million units/day IV divided q4h × 4 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (endocarditis, penicillin-susceptible) — per AHA/IDSA IE guidelines
first-line (endocarditis, synergistic short-course) — gentamicin × 2 weeks, penicillin × 4 weeks
alternative (endocarditis) — convenient dosing
alternative (penicillin-allergic)

coverages.medication: Mandatory colonoscopy after any S. gallolyticus infection
coverages.note: screening — even if recent colonoscopy was normal; repeat may be warranted

organisms.name: Mycobacterium leprae (leprosy / Hansen disease)
organisms.morphology: Acid-fast bacillus; obligate intracellular; cannot be cultured in vitro; grows in armadillo footpads and nude mouse models; doubling time ~14 days (slowest of all known bacteria); optimal growth at 30–33°C (cooler body sites)
organisms.features: Causes leprosy (Hansen disease). Infects Schwann cells of peripheral nerves and skin macrophages. Spectrum determined by host cell-mediated immunity: tuberculoid (paucibacillary, strong CMI) to lepromatous (multibacillary, weak CMI). Nine-banded armadillo is animal reservoir in US. ~200,000 new cases/year worldwide (India, Brazil, Indonesia). Diagnosis: clinical (cardinal signs) + skin biopsy/slit-skin smear. Leprosy reactions (type 1 reversal, type 2 ENL) can cause acute nerve damage.
organisms.source: Prolonged close contact with untreated multibacillary patients (nasal droplet); armadillo contact (US Gulf states); NOT highly contagious

presentations.symptoms: few well-defined hypopigmented/erythematous skin patches with raised borders, anesthetic (loss of sensation); enlarged peripheral nerves (ulnar, peroneal); few or no bacilli on smear
presentations.clues: strong CMI response; few lesions (≤5)

presentations.symptoms: numerous symmetric skin lesions (papules, nodules, plaques); diffuse skin thickening; leonine facies; madarosis (loss of eyebrows/eyelashes); nasal congestion/epistaxis; glove-and-stocking neuropathy; many bacilli on smear
presentations.clues: weak CMI; >5 lesions

presentations.symptoms: acute inflammation of existing skin lesions and nerves; pain, tenderness, edema; can cause acute nerve damage → disability
presentations.clues: borderline forms

presentations.symptoms: tender erythematous nodules, fever, malaise, neuritis, iritis, orchitis, arthritis
presentations.clues: lepromatous/borderline lepromatous

coverages.medication: Rifampicin 600 mg PO monthly + dapsone 100 mg PO daily × 6 months
coverages.note: first-line (paucibacillary, WHO MDT) — supervised monthly rifampicin

coverages.medication: Rifampicin 600 mg PO monthly + dapsone 100 mg PO daily + clofazimine 300 mg PO monthly + 50 mg PO daily × 12 months
coverages.note: first-line (multibacillary, WHO MDT) — US NHDP may extend to 24 months

coverages.medication: Prednisone 40–60 mg/day PO → taper over months
coverages.note: first-line (type 1 reversal reaction) — to prevent nerve damage
first-line (type 2 ENL, males/non-childbearing) — FDA-approved for ENL; teratogenic — strict REMS program
first-line (type 2 ENL, females of childbearing potential) — thalidomide contraindicated

organisms.name: Yersinia pestis (plague)
organisms.morphology: Gram-negative coccobacillus; bipolar staining ("safety pin" appearance on Wright/Giemsa stain); facultative anaerobe; non-motile (unlike other Yersinia); grows on standard media but slowly; BSL-3 pathogen; CDC Category A bioterrorism agent
organisms.features: Causes plague — one of the deadliest infectious diseases in history. Three clinical forms: bubonic (most common, 80–90%), septicemic, pneumonic. Transmitted by flea bite (Xenopsylla cheopis) from rodent reservoir. Pneumonic plague is person-to-person transmissible via respiratory droplets. Case fatality: bubonic 17%, pneumonic 27–31%, septicemic 38% with treatment. Diagnosis: blood/bubo aspirate culture, PCR, serology. Treat empirically — do NOT delay.
organisms.source: Flea bite from infected rodents (rats, prairie dogs, ground squirrels); direct contact with infected animals; respiratory droplets (pneumonic); endemic in western US, Africa, Asia

presentations.symptoms: sudden fever, chills, headache, malaise → exquisitely tender, swollen lymph node (bubo) in groin/axilla/cervical region; bubo may suppurate
presentations.clues: most common form (80–90%); can progress to septicemic/pneumonic

presentations.incubation: 1–3 days incubation (primary) or secondary from bubonic
presentations.symptoms: fever, cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, chest pain → rapid respiratory failure
presentations.clues: person-to-person transmission; bioterrorism concern; droplet precautions required
presentations.severity: very severe; nearly 100% fatal without treatment within 24h

presentations.symptoms: high fever, prostration, DIC, purpura, acral gangrene ("Black Death"), multiorgan failure
presentations.clues: may lack bubo (primary septicemic)

coverages.medication: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h × 10 days
coverages.note: first-line — CDC 2021 recommended
first-line (historical gold standard) — limited availability

coverages.medication: Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h or 500 mg PO BID × 10 days
coverages.note: first-line (alternative) — FDA-approved under Animal Rule; preferred for mass casualty
first-line (alternative, mild-moderate bubonic) — FDA-approved; also used for post-exposure prophylaxis × 7 days
avoid — associated with 75% case fatality rate in aggregate data

organisms.name: Candida auris
organisms.morphology: Oval yeast cells; does not form true hyphae or pseudohyphae (unlike C. albicans); grows on standard fungal media but frequently misidentified as C. haemulonii, C. duobushaemulonii, or other species by conventional methods
organisms.features: First identified 2009 (Japan). Present on all continents except Antarctica. Five clades with distinct geographic distributions. CDC "urgent threat." Persists on skin and surfaces for weeks. Colonization → candidemia in up to 25% of critically ill colonized patients. >90% resistant to fluconazole; some strains pan-resistant. MALDI-TOF MS with updated databases required for accurate identification. Contact precautions mandatory. Mortality ~30–60%.
organisms.source: Healthcare-associated; person-to-person and environment-to-person transmission; persists on hospital surfaces; colonizes skin (axilla, groin, nares)

presentations.symptoms: fever, sepsis, hemodynamic instability; catheter-associated BSI most common; also wound infections, UTI, osteomyelitis, meningitis
presentations.clues: immunocompromised, ICU, prior antifungals, indwelling devices

coverages.medication: Echinocandins (caspofungin 70 mg load → 50 mg daily; micafungin 100 mg daily; anidulafungin 200 mg load → 100 mg daily)
coverages.note: first-line (CDC recommended) — pending susceptibility testing; primary echinocandin resistance uncommon but emerging
alternative (echinocandin-resistant) — ~37% of strains susceptible to amphotericin B; check susceptibility
avoid — >90% resistant
first-line — echinocandins have poor CNS penetration
essential — source control; 2 weeks of therapy after blood culture clearance

organisms.name: Coccidioides immitis / C. posadasii (Valley fever)
organisms.morphology: Mold in environment (hyphae → arthroconidia); spherules containing endospores in tissue (pathognomonic); arthroconidia 2–5 μm (highly infectious); spherules 20–200 μm
organisms.features: Endemic in southwestern US (Arizona, California San Joaquin Valley), northern Mexico, Central/South America. Causes 15–29% of CAP in endemic areas. ~60% of infections asymptomatic. Risk factors for dissemination: Filipino, African American, immunosuppressed, pregnancy (3rd trimester), diabetes. Diagnosis: serology (IgM EIA → confirm with immunodiffusion; CF titers correlate with disease severity), culture (BSL-3), histopathology (spherules). Erythema nodosum/multiforme common.
organisms.source: Inhalation of airborne arthroconidia from disturbed soil in endemic areas; NOT person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: fever, cough, chest pain, fatigue, arthralgias ("desert rheumatism"), erythema nodosum/multiforme; CXR: infiltrate, hilar adenopathy, pleural effusion
presentations.clues: 40% symptomatic; self-limited in most immunocompetent

presentations.symptoms: persistent cough, weight loss, hemoptysis; thin-walled cavities, nodules
presentations.clues: can mimic TB or lung cancer

presentations.symptoms: meningitis (basilar, most feared), skin lesions, bone/joint involvement, lymphadenopathy
presentations.clues: immunocompromised, Filipino, African American; CF titer ≥1:16 suggests dissemination

coverages.note: first-line (uncomplicated primary, immunocompetent, low risk) — observation with serial serology and CXR

coverages.medication: Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily or itraconazole 200 mg PO BID × 3–12 months
coverages.note: first-line (symptomatic pulmonary, risk factors for dissemination) — azoles are mainstay
first-line (coccidioidal meningitis) — lifelong therapy; intrathecal amphotericin B if refractory
first-line (severe/rapidly progressive, pregnancy) — azoles teratogenic in pregnancy
alternative/salvage — limited data but used for refractory disease

organisms.name: Mucor / Rhizopus (mucormycosis)
organisms.morphology: Broad (6–25 μm), ribbon-like, pauciseptate (few or no septa) hyphae branching at right angles (90°); Rhizopus has rhizoids; rapid growth on culture (fills plate in days); non-pigmented
organisms.features: Angioinvasive — causes tissue infarction and necrosis. Risk factors: uncontrolled diabetes/DKA (most common), hematologic malignancy, transplant, deferoxamine therapy, iron overload, corticosteroids, voriconazole prophylaxis (breakthrough). Rhino-orbital-cerebral form most common in DKA. Pulmonary form in neutropenic patients. Diagnosis: tissue biopsy showing characteristic hyphae (culture often negative); CT/MRI. Mortality 40–80% depending on form. Voriconazole has NO activity.
organisms.source: Ubiquitous in environment (soil, decaying organic matter, bread mold); inhalation of sporangiospores; direct inoculation (trauma, burns); NOT person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: facial pain/swelling, nasal congestion/discharge (black eschar on palate or nasal turbinates), periorbital edema, proptosis, vision loss, cranial nerve palsies → cavernous sinus thrombosis, brain invasion
presentations.clues: DKA most common setting; black eschar pathognomonic

presentations.symptoms: fever, cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain; reverse halo sign on CT
presentations.clues: neutropenic patients, transplant; angioinvasive → hemorrhagic infarction

presentations.symptoms: rapidly progressive necrotic wound, black eschar
presentations.clues: trauma, burns, surgical sites

coverages.note: first-line (strongly recommended) — full dose from day 1; 10 mg/kg for CNS involvement; continue until clinical/radiographic resolution + immune reconstitution

coverages.medication: Isavuconazole 200 mg IV/PO q8h × 6 doses (loading) → 200 mg daily
coverages.note: first-line (alternative) or step-down/salvage — FDA-approved for mucormycosis; similar efficacy to amphotericin B in matched analysis

coverages.medication: Posaconazole DR tablets 300 mg PO BID day 1 → 300 mg daily
coverages.note: alternative/step-down — moderately recommended; oral maintenance after amphotericin B
essential (in parallel with antifungals) — aggressive debridement of necrotic tissue; repeat as needed
avoid — NO activity against Mucorales

coverages.medication: Correct underlying condition (DKA correction, reduce immunosuppression, stop deferoxamine)
coverages.note: essential — reversal of predisposing factors critical for survival

organisms.name: Blastomyces dermatitidis
organisms.morphology: Mold in environment (hyphae with conidia at 25°C); large thick-walled yeast (8–15 μm) with single broad-based bud in tissue at 37°C (pathognomonic); "double-contoured" cell wall
organisms.features: Endemic in Great Lakes region, Ohio/Mississippi River valleys, St. Lawrence River. Infection via inhalation of conidia from disturbed soil (near waterways). Pneumonia most common; disseminates to skin (verrucous or ulcerative lesions), bone, GU tract (prostate, epididymis). "Great mimicker" — can resemble cancer, TB, or other infections. Diagnosis: culture (gold standard), direct visualization of broad-based budding yeast, urine/serum Blastomyces antigen (>80% sensitivity, cross-reacts with Histoplasma). All patients should be treated.
organisms.source: Inhalation of conidia from soil near waterways in endemic areas; NOT person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: cough, fever, myalgia, chest pain; CXR: mass-like consolidation (mimics lung cancer), diffuse infiltrates, or miliary pattern; can progress to ARDS
presentations.clues: can be acute or chronic

presentations.symptoms: skin lesions (verrucous papules/plaques or ulcers, especially face/extremities), osteomyelitis (vertebrae, ribs, long bones), GU involvement (prostatitis, epididymitis), CNS (meningitis, brain abscess)
presentations.clues: immunocompromised at higher risk

coverages.medication: Itraconazole 200 mg PO daily (or BID for severe) × 6–12 months
coverages.note: first-line (mild-moderate) — 90–95% cure rate

coverages.medication: Liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg/day IV × 1–2 weeks → step-down to itraconazole × 6–12 months
coverages.note: first-line (severe, including ARDS, CNS) — longer itraconazole for CNS/bone
alternative (itraconazole-intolerant) — less effective than itraconazole
alternative (CNS involvement) — good CNS penetration; limited data

organisms.name: Candida glabrata (Nakaseomyces glabratus)
organisms.morphology: Small oval yeast cells (1–4 μm); does NOT form pseudohyphae or true hyphae (unlike C. albicans); haploid; grows on standard media
organisms.features: Second most common cause of candidemia in many regions. Intrinsically reduced susceptibility to fluconazole (dose-dependent susceptibility, not resistant per se, but clinical failures common). Increasing echinocandin resistance (FKS mutations) — test for echinocandin susceptibility. Associated with prior azole exposure, elderly, ICU patients. Mortality comparable to or higher than C. albicans candidemia.
organisms.source: Endogenous (GI tract colonizer); healthcare-associated; prior azole exposure selects for C. glabrata

presentations.symptoms: fever, sepsis, catheter-associated BSI; also UTI, peritonitis, hepatosplenic candidiasis
presentations.clues: elderly, ICU, prior azole exposure, abdominal surgery

presentations.symptoms: oropharyngeal or vulvovaginal candidiasis, especially in patients with prior azole exposure
presentations.clues: often azole-refractory

coverages.note: first-line (invasive, strong recommendation) — IDSA 2016; test for echinocandin susceptibility (FKS mutations)

coverages.medication: Higher-dose fluconazole 800 mg (12 mg/kg) daily or voriconazole 200–300 mg BID
coverages.note: step-down (only if isolate confirmed susceptible) — do NOT use empirically for C. glabrata
alternative (echinocandin-resistant + azole-resistant) — high susceptibility maintained
