avoid as empiric therapy — intrinsically reduced susceptibility; clinical failures common

organisms.name: Sporothrix schenckii
organisms.morphology: Mold in environment (septate hyphae with "rosette" or "daisy" clusters of conidia at 25°C); cigar-shaped yeast cells in tissue at 37°C (2–6 μm); asteroid bodies (Splendore-Hoeppli phenomenon) on histopathology in 30% of cases
organisms.features: Causes sporotrichosis ("rose gardener's disease"). Acquired by traumatic inoculation from soil, plants (rose thorns, sphagnum moss), or zoonotic transmission (cats, especially in Brazil). Lymphocutaneous form most common (nodules tracking along lymphatics). Disseminated/pulmonary forms in immunocompromised (alcoholism, HIV, TNF inhibitors). Diagnosis: culture (gold standard — dimorphism must be demonstrated); histopathology has limited sensitivity in immunocompetent.
organisms.source: Traumatic inoculation from soil, plants (rose thorns, hay, sphagnum moss); zoonotic (cat scratch/bite, especially in Brazil); rarely inhalation (pulmonary)

presentations.symptoms: painless papule/nodule at inoculation site → ulcerates; sequential nodules develop along draining lymphatics (sporotrichoid spread)
presentations.clues: classic ascending nodular lymphangitis; "rose gardener's disease"

presentations.symptoms: single plaque, nodule, or ulcer at inoculation site without lymphatic spread
presentations.clues: limited disease

presentations.symptoms: chronic cavitary pneumonia (mimics TB); osteoarticular involvement; widespread skin lesions
presentations.clues: immunocompromised (alcoholism, HIV, TNF inhibitors)

coverages.medication: Itraconazole 200 mg PO daily × 3–6 months (2–4 weeks after lesion resolution)
coverages.note: first-line (cutaneous/lymphocutaneous) — 80–100% response rate
alternative (cutaneous/lymphocutaneous) — comparable efficacy to itraconazole

coverages.medication: Liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg/day IV → step-down to itraconazole 200 mg PO BID × ≥12 months
coverages.note: first-line (disseminated/severe pulmonary)
alternative (cutaneous, resource-limited) — 70–89% response; poorly tolerated (dysgeusia, GI, acneiform)
avoid — high MICs; not effective

organisms.name: Malassezia (M. furfur, M. globosa) — tinea versicolor
organisms.morphology: Round yeast cells (3–8 μm) with short hyphae — "spaghetti and meatballs" appearance on KOH prep; lipid-dependent (requires long-chain fatty acids for growth); difficult to culture on standard media (requires lipid-supplemented media)
organisms.features: Normal skin flora; causes tinea versicolor (pityriasis versicolor) when overgrowth occurs. Also associated with Malassezia folliculitis, seborrheic dermatitis, and neonatal pustulosis. Predisposing factors: heat, humidity, hyperhidrosis, oily skin, immunosuppression. Diagnosis: clinical appearance + KOH prep ("spaghetti and meatballs"); Wood lamp shows yellowish-green fluorescence. High recurrence rate.
organisms.source: Normal skin flora (commensal); overgrowth in warm, humid conditions; NOT transmitted person-to-person

presentations.symptoms: round to oval hypo- or hyperpigmented macules/patches with fine scale on upper trunk, neck, proximal arms; coalescing "map-like" pattern; usually asymptomatic or mildly pruritic
presentations.clues: pigment changes persist weeks to months after treatment (not treatment failure)

presentations.symptoms: pruritic monomorphic papules/pustules on trunk, shoulders, upper arms
presentations.clues: often misdiagnosed as acne; does not respond to antibiotics

coverages.medication: Ketoconazole 2% cream daily × 2 weeks or ketoconazole 2% shampoo as body wash
coverages.note: first-line (topical) — 80–90% clearance

coverages.medication: Selenium sulfide 2.5% lotion or shampoo as body wash
coverages.note: first-line (topical, alternative) — apply 10 min before rinsing daily × 1–2 weeks

coverages.medication: Fluconazole 300 mg PO weekly × 2 weeks or itraconazole 200 mg PO daily × 5–7 days
coverages.note: first-line (extensive disease or topical failure) — systemic azoles for widespread involvement

coverages.medication: Fluconazole 300 mg PO monthly or ketoconazole shampoo weekly
coverages.note: prophylaxis (recurrence prevention) — high recurrence rate without maintenance
avoid (oral) — poor efficacy against Malassezia (lipophilic yeast, not dermatophyte)

organisms.name: Human Papillomavirus (HPV)
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, double-stranded circular DNA virus; icosahedral capsid ~55 nm; >150 genotypes identified; infects basal epithelial cells through microabrasions
organisms.features: Most common STI worldwide — most sexually active persons acquire HPV during their lifetime. Low-risk types (6, 11) cause anogenital warts and recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. High-risk types (16, 18, 31, 33, 45) cause cervical, anal, oropharyngeal, penile, vulvar, and vaginal cancers. HPV 16/18 responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. Most infections clear spontaneously within 1–2 years. Persistent high-risk infection is the strongest risk factor for cancer. Diagnosis: clinical (warts), Pap smear/HPV DNA testing (cervical screening), NAATs. No treatment for the virus itself — only for conditions it causes.
organisms.source: Sexual contact (vaginal, anal, oral, genital-to-genital); vertical transmission (rare, during delivery → recurrent respiratory papillomatosis in infant)

presentations.symptoms: flesh-colored, exophytic papules/plaques on genitalia, perianal area; usually painless; can be flat, papular, or cauliflower-like
presentations.clues: HPV 6 and 11

presentations.incubation: years to decades of persistent infection
presentations.symptoms: often asymptomatic until advanced; abnormal Pap smear → CIN → invasive carcinoma; postcoital bleeding, vaginal discharge
presentations.severity: progressive; preventable with screening and vaccination

presentations.symptoms: sore throat, dysphagia, neck mass; rising incidence in young men
presentations.clues: HPV 16 predominant

presentations.symptoms: hoarseness, stridor, airway obstruction; juvenile-onset (vertical transmission) or adult-onset
presentations.clues: HPV 6, 11

coverages.note: prevention (ages 9–45) — 2 doses if started 90% efficacy against targeted types
first-line (external warts, patient-applied) — apply at bedtime 3×/week × up to 16 weeks
first-line (external warts, patient-applied) — apply BID × 3 days, then 4 days off; repeat up to 4 cycles
first-line (provider-applied) — repeat q1–2 weeks
first-line (provider-applied) — weekly application
alternative (large/refractory warts)

coverages.medication: No antiviral therapy exists for HPV infection itself
coverages.note: supportive — treat conditions caused by HPV

organisms.name: Rabies virus
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded negative-sense RNA virus; bullet-shaped virion ~180 × 75 nm; neurotropic — travels via peripheral nerves to CNS
organisms.features: Nearly 100% fatal once clinical symptoms appear (~59,000 deaths/year worldwide, mostly Asia/Africa). Transmitted by bite of infected mammal (dogs worldwide; bats in US). Incubation highly variable (weeks to years, typically 1–3 months). Negri bodies (eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions in neurons) are pathognomonic. Entirely preventable with timely post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Diagnosis: direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test on animal brain; in humans — nuchal skin biopsy DFA, saliva RT-PCR, serum/CSF antibodies.
organisms.source: Bite or scratch from infected mammal (dogs worldwide; bats, raccoons, skunks, foxes in US); rarely aerosolized in bat caves or organ transplantation

presentations.symptoms: prodrome: pain/paresthesia at bite site, fever, malaise → agitation, hydrophobia (pharyngeal spasms triggered by swallowing), aerophobia, hypersalivation, autonomic instability, fluctuating consciousness → coma → death
presentations.clues: 80% of cases; hydrophobia is pathognomonic

presentations.symptoms: ascending flaccid paralysis (mimics GBS), sphincter dysfunction, minimal hydrophobia → respiratory failure → death
presentations.clues: 20% of cases; often misdiagnosed

coverages.medication: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) — previously unvaccinated: wound washing with soap/water × 15 min + human rabies immunoglobulin (HRIG) 20 IU/kg infiltrated at wound site + rabies vaccine IM on days 0, 3, 7, 14
coverages.note: first-line (prevention) — must be given before symptom onset; nearly 100% effective

coverages.medication: PEP — previously vaccinated: wound washing + rabies vaccine IM on days 0 and 3 only
coverages.note: first-line (previously immunized) — NO HRIG needed

coverages.medication: Pre-exposure prophylaxis: rabies vaccine IM on days 0, 7, 21
coverages.note: prevention (high-risk: veterinarians, lab workers, travelers to endemic areas)
supportive/palliative — Milwaukee protocol (therapeutic coma) has had extremely rare success; not recommended as standard

organisms.name: Dengue virus (DENV 1–4)
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; ~50 nm; 4 serotypes (DENV 1–4); infection with one serotype provides lifelong immunity to that serotype but increases risk of severe dengue upon secondary infection with different serotype (antibody-dependent enhancement)
organisms.features: Most common arboviral disease worldwide (~390 million infections/year). Transmitted by Aedes aegypti and A. albopictus mosquitoes. Endemic in tropics/subtropics; expanding range. Three clinical phases: febrile → critical (at defervescence, days 4–7) → recovery. Warning signs predict progression to severe dengue. NS1 antigen detectable early (days 1–5). Diagnosis: NS1 antigen (acute), IgM/IgG serology, RT-PCR.
organisms.source: Aedes mosquito bite; NOT person-to-person; rare: blood transfusion, organ transplant, perinatal

presentations.symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia/arthralgia ("breakbone fever"), maculopapular rash, minor hemorrhagic manifestations
presentations.clues: self-limited in most; leukopenia, thrombocytopenia

presentations.disease: Severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever / dengue shock syndrome)
presentations.symptoms: plasma leakage (hemoconcentration, pleural effusion, ascites), severe hemorrhage (hematemesis, melena), organ impairment (hepatitis, encephalitis, myocarditis)
presentations.clues: warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, rising hematocrit with falling platelets
presentations.severity: severe; 2.5% mortality without treatment, 1% with proper fluid management

coverages.note: first-line (all dengue) — acetaminophen for fever/pain; oral rehydration; monitor hematocrit and platelets q12–24h
first-line (severe dengue / plasma leakage) — guided by hematocrit; colloids if refractory shock
avoid — increased bleeding risk
prevention (ages 9–16, only if prior confirmed dengue infection) — FDA-approved; NOT for dengue-naïve individuals (increased severe dengue risk)

organisms.name: Zika virus
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; ~40 nm; closely related to dengue; neurotropic — crosses placental barrier
organisms.features: 50–80% of infections asymptomatic. Major concern: congenital Zika syndrome (microcephaly, brain abnormalities) and Guillain-Barré syndrome in adults. Transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes AND sexually (unique among arboviruses). Diagnosis complicated by cross-reactivity with dengue serology; RT-PCR in serum/urine (acute), Zika IgM with confirmatory PRNT.
organisms.source: Aedes mosquito bite; sexual transmission (persists in semen up to 6 months); vertical (transplacental); blood transfusion (rare)

presentations.symptoms: low-grade fever, maculopapular pruritic rash (prominent), conjunctivitis (non-purulent), arthralgia, myalgia, headache
presentations.clues: milder than dengue; rash and conjunctivitis more prominent

presentations.symptoms: microcephaly, intracranial calcifications, ventriculomegaly, arthrogryposis, ocular abnormalities
presentations.clues: first/second trimester infection highest risk

presentations.incubation: days to weeks after Zika infection
presentations.symptoms: ascending flaccid paralysis, areflexia
presentations.clues: associated with Zika outbreaks

coverages.note: first-line — rest, fluids, acetaminophen; avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue excluded

coverages.medication: No specific antiviral or vaccine available
coverages.note: prevention relies on mosquito avoidance, safe sex practices, travel advisories for pregnant women

organisms.name: Chikungunya virus
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; ~70 nm; transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes (same vectors as dengue/Zika)
organisms.features: "Chikungunya" means "that which bends up" — refers to stooped posture from severe joint pain. Distinguishing feature: severe, debilitating polyarthralgia/polyarthritis that can persist for months to years (chronic phase in 25–50%). Diagnosis: RT-PCR (acute, first 5 days), IgM serology (after day 5).
organisms.source: Aedes aegypti and A. albopictus mosquito bite; NOT person-to-person; endemic in Africa, Asia, Americas

presentations.symptoms: abrupt high fever, severe bilateral symmetric polyarthralgia (small joints of hands/feet, wrists, ankles), maculopapular rash, headache, myalgia
presentations.clues: arthralgia more severe and prominent than dengue; joint swelling common

presentations.symptoms: persistent or relapsing polyarthralgia/polyarthritis; morning stiffness; can mimic rheumatoid arthritis
presentations.clues: 25–50% of patients; more common in older adults, women

coverages.note: first-line (acute) — rest, fluids, acetaminophen; avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue excluded
first-line (arthralgia/arthritis) — ibuprofen or naproxen for joint pain
alternative (chronic inflammatory arthritis refractory to NSAIDs) — limited evidence; rheumatology referral
prevention (ages ≥18, FDA-approved 2023) — single dose IM

organisms.name: West Nile virus
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; ~50 nm; neurotropic flavivirus; closely related to Japanese encephalitis virus
organisms.features: Leading cause of mosquito-borne disease in contiguous US (~1300 neuroinvasive cases/year). Transmitted by Culex mosquitoes. ~80% asymptomatic, ~20% West Nile fever, 1% neuroinvasive disease. Mortality ~10% for neuroinvasive disease (20% in age ≥70). Long-term sequelae in >50% of hospitalized patients. Blood supply screened since 2003. Diagnosis: serum/CSF IgM (may be negative early); RT-PCR more sensitive in immunocompromised.
organisms.source: Culex mosquito bite; birds are reservoir; rare: blood transfusion, organ transplant, transplacental, breastmilk

presentations.symptoms: fever, headache, fatigue, myalgia, weakness, maculopapular rash (trunk/extremities), lymphadenopathy
presentations.clues: self-limited; prolonged fatigue common

presentations.symptoms: meningitis (headache, stiff neck, photophobia), encephalitis (confusion, tremor, myoclonus, parkinsonism, coma), acute flaccid myelitis (asymmetric limb weakness, areflexia — mimics polio)
presentations.clues: elderly and immunocompromised at highest risk; tremor is characteristic

coverages.note: first-line — no specific antiviral therapy proven effective; ICU monitoring for encephalitis (airway protection, ICP management, seizure control)
investigational — no proven benefit in clinical studies
prevention relies on mosquito avoidance (repellents, protective clothing, limiting dusk-to-dawn exposure)

organisms.name: Parvovirus B19
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, single-stranded DNA virus; icosahedral; ~22–26 nm (one of smallest DNA viruses); tropism for erythroid progenitor cells (binds P antigen/globoside receptor)
organisms.features: Causes erythema infectiosum (fifth disease) in children. Arthropathy predominates in adults (especially women). Transient aplastic crisis in patients with hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell, thalassemia). Chronic pure red cell aplasia in immunocompromised. Hydrops fetalis in pregnancy (risk ~3% if infected in first 20 weeks). Biphasic illness: viremia (infectious) → immune-mediated rash/arthritis (no longer infectious). Diagnosis: IgM serology (immunocompetent), PCR (immunocompromised — may not mount antibody response).
organisms.source: Respiratory droplets; hand-to-mouth contact; blood products; vertical (transplacental); infectious before rash onset (no longer infectious once rash appears)

presentations.symptoms: prodromal fever/malaise → "slapped cheek" facial erythema → lacy reticular rash on trunk/extremities; rash may wax/wane with sun/heat/bathing
presentations.clues: children; self-limited

presentations.symptoms: symmetric polyarthralgia/polyarthritis (hands, wrists, knees); can persist weeks to months; mimics RA
presentations.clues: adults, especially women; rash less common in adults

presentations.symptoms: sudden severe anemia (reticulocyte count near zero), fatigue, pallor; Hgb can drop precipitously
presentations.clues: patients with sickle cell disease, thalassemia, spherocytosis

presentations.symptoms: persistent anemia, absent reticulocytes
presentations.clues: immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy); cannot clear virus

presentations.symptoms: fetal anemia, high-output cardiac failure, generalized edema, pleural/pericardial effusions
presentations.clues: risk ~3% with maternal infection 20 weeks; can cause fetal death

coverages.note: first-line (immunocompetent) — self-limited; acetaminophen/NSAIDs for arthralgia; transfusion for aplastic crisis

coverages.medication: IVIG 400 mg/kg/day × 5 days or 1 g/kg/day × 2–3 days
coverages.note: first-line (immunocompromised with chronic infection/pure red cell aplasia) — provides neutralizing antibodies; may relapse → repeat courses
adjunctive (transplant recipients) — allow immune clearance
first-line (severe fetal hydrops) — cordocentesis with packed RBC transfusion; reduces fetal mortality from ~30% to ~18%

organisms.name: Adenovirus
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus; icosahedral with fiber projections; ~90 nm; >50 serotypes grouped into 7 species (A–G); extremely stable in environment
organisms.features: Ubiquitous; causes wide spectrum of disease depending on serotype. Respiratory illness (types 3, 4, 7, 14, 21), pharyngoconjunctival fever (types 3, 7), epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (types 8, 19, 37), gastroenteritis (types 40, 41), hemorrhagic cystitis (types 11, 34, 35 — post-HCT). Severe disseminated disease in immunocompromised (especially pediatric HCT recipients — mortality 50–80%). Diagnosis: PCR (preferred), viral culture, rapid antigen testing.
organisms.source: Respiratory droplets, fecal-oral, direct contact (conjunctival), fomites, contaminated water (swimming pools); highly contagious

presentations.symptoms: fever, cough, pharyngitis, rhinitis; can cause pneumonia (especially types 4, 7, 14 in military recruits)
presentations.clues: all ages; outbreaks in close quarters

presentations.symptoms: fever, pharyngitis, bilateral conjunctivitis, cervical lymphadenopathy
presentations.clues: children; swimming pool outbreaks

presentations.symptoms: severe unilateral → bilateral conjunctivitis, tearing, photophobia, subepithelial corneal infiltrates
presentations.clues: highly contagious; nosocomial outbreaks

presentations.symptoms: watery diarrhea, vomiting, fever
presentations.clues: types 40, 41; children 2 years

presentations.symptoms: pneumonia, hepatitis, hemorrhagic cystitis, colitis, encephalitis, multiorgan failure
presentations.clues: HCT recipients, especially pediatric; mortality 50–80%

coverages.note: first-line (immunocompetent) — self-limited; no specific antiviral needed

coverages.medication: Cidofovir 5 mg/kg IV weekly (with probenecid and hydration)
coverages.note: first-line (severe/disseminated in immunocompromised) — nephrotoxic; monitor renal function
alternative (disseminated) — less nephrotoxic; FDA-approved for smallpox but used off-label
adjunctive (HCT recipients) — emerging therapy; promising results

organisms.name: Coxsackievirus / Enterovirus
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; icosahedral; ~30 nm; includes Coxsackievirus A (23 types), Coxsackievirus B (6 types), Echoviruses, Enterovirus D68, Enterovirus A71
organisms.features: Fecal-oral and respiratory transmission. Peak incidence summer/fall. Neonatal enteroviral sepsis can be fatal. EV-D68 causes severe respiratory illness and acute flaccid myelitis (AFM) in children. EV-A71 causes hand-foot-mouth disease with CNS complications. Diagnosis: RT-PCR (CSF, throat, stool, blood); viral culture.
organisms.source: Fecal-oral, respiratory droplets; highly contagious; children are primary reservoir

presentations.symptoms: fever, oral vesicles/ulcers (painful), vesicular rash on palms, soles, buttocks
presentations.clues: Coxsackievirus A16 (classic), EV-A71 (can cause CNS complications)

presentations.symptoms: fever, sore throat, small vesicles/ulcers on posterior oropharynx (soft palate, tonsillar pillars)
presentations.clues: Coxsackievirus A; children

presentations.symptoms: fever, headache, stiff neck, photophobia; CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose
presentations.clues: enteroviruses are most common cause of viral meningitis

presentations.symptoms: chest pain, dyspnea, heart failure; dilated cardiomyopathy
presentations.clues: Coxsackievirus B3, B5

presentations.symptoms: severe paroxysmal chest/abdominal pain, fever
presentations.clues: Coxsackievirus B

presentations.symptoms: acute onset limb weakness, areflexia; anterior horn cell involvement on MRI
presentations.clues: EV-D68; children; biennial outbreaks

presentations.symptoms: fever/hypothermia, poor feeding, hepatitis, myocarditis, meningoencephalitis, DIC
presentations.clues: vertical transmission; Coxsackievirus B, Echovirus

coverages.note: first-line (most enteroviral infections) — self-limited; acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain/fever
first-line (neonatal enteroviral sepsis, agammaglobulinemia) — provides neutralizing antibodies
under study — oral antiviral for enterovirus; compassionate use
investigational — capsid inhibitor; limited availability

organisms.name: Parainfluenza virus (HPIV 1–4)
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded negative-sense RNA virus; pleomorphic; ~150–300 nm; 4 types: HPIV-1 and HPIV-2 (croup), HPIV-3 (bronchiolitis/pneumonia), HPIV-4 (mild URI)
organisms.features: Second most common cause of lower respiratory tract infection in young children (after RSV). HPIV-1 is the most common cause of croup. Seasonal: HPIV-1 (fall, odd years), HPIV-2 (fall), HPIV-3 (spring/summer). Can cause severe pneumonia in immunocompromised (HCT recipients). Diagnosis: RT-PCR (respiratory panel), rapid antigen testing.
organisms.source: Respiratory droplets, direct contact; highly contagious; children are primary reservoir

presentations.symptoms: barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness, low-grade fever; worse at night
presentations.clues: HPIV-1 (most common cause), HPIV-2; children 6 months–3 years

presentations.symptoms: cough, wheezing, tachypnea, hypoxia
presentations.clues: HPIV-3; infants 6 months

presentations.symptoms: progressive cough, dyspnea, diffuse infiltrates
presentations.clues: HCT recipients; mortality 10–30%

coverages.note: first-line — humidified air, hydration
first-line (croup) — reduces severity and need for intubation; can repeat × 1
first-line (moderate-severe croup) — 0.5 mL of 2.25% solution; observe ≥2 hours for rebound

coverages.medication: No specific antiviral therapy or vaccine available

organisms.name: Human metapneumovirus (hMPV)
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded negative-sense RNA virus; pleomorphic; ~150–600 nm; closely related to RSV; 2 major genotypes (A, B)
organisms.features: Discovered 2001. Causes respiratory illness similar to RSV. Peak incidence late winter/early spring. Affects all ages but most severe in young children (5 years), elderly (≥65), and immunocompromised. Accounts for 5–15% of pediatric respiratory hospitalizations. Can cause severe pneumonia in HCT recipients. Diagnosis: RT-PCR (respiratory panel); rapid antigen tests less sensitive.
organisms.source: Respiratory droplets, direct contact; peak late winter/spring

presentations.symptoms: cough, rhinorrhea, fever, sore throat
presentations.clues: all ages

presentations.symptoms: wheezing, cough, tachypnea, hypoxia; clinically indistinguishable from RSV
presentations.clues: children 5 years, elderly, immunocompromised

presentations.symptoms: progressive respiratory failure
presentations.clues: HCT recipients; mortality up to 40% without treatment

coverages.note: first-line — oxygen, hydration, bronchodilators (limited evidence)
investigational/off-label (severe, immunocompromised) — limited evidence; used in HCT recipients

coverages.medication: No specific antiviral therapy or vaccine currently approved

organisms.name: HHV-6 (roseola) / HHV-8 (Kaposi sarcoma)
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, double-stranded DNA viruses. HHV-6: ~200 nm; 2 species (HHV-6A, HHV-6B); infects CD4+ T cells; can integrate into host chromosomes (chromosomally integrated HHV-6, ciHHV-6). HHV-8: ~120–150 nm; also called Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV); infects B cells and endothelial cells.
organisms.features: HHV-6B: causes roseola infantum (exanthem subitum/sixth disease); >90% seropositive by age 2. Reactivation in HCT recipients causes encephalitis and delayed engraftment. ciHHV-6 present in ~1% of population (high viral loads without active infection — do not treat). HHV-8: causes Kaposi sarcoma (AIDS-defining), primary effusion lymphoma, multicentric Castleman disease. Diagnosis: HHV-6 — PCR (distinguish ciHHV-6 from active infection); HHV-8 — biopsy (spindle cells, latent nuclear antigen LANA), PCR.
organisms.source: HHV-6: saliva (primary route); nearly universal childhood infection. HHV-8: saliva, sexual contact; endemic in sub-Saharan Africa, Mediterranean
organisms.notes: Must distinguish chromosomally integrated HHV-6 (ciHHV-6, ~1% of population) from active infection — ciHHV-6 produces persistently high viral loads without active disease; do NOT treat

presentations.symptoms: high fever (often >40°C) × 3–5 days → sudden defervescence → diffuse maculopapular rash (trunk → extremities); febrile seizures in ~10%
presentations.clues: infants 6–24 months

presentations.symptoms: confusion, memory loss, seizures; limbic encephalitis; MRI: mesial temporal lobe signal abnormality
presentations.clues: HCT recipients (especially cord blood)

presentations.symptoms: violaceous papules/plaques/nodules on skin, oral mucosa; visceral involvement (lungs, GI); lymphedema
presentations.clues: HIV/AIDS (CD4 200); also classic (elderly Mediterranean men), endemic (Africa), iatrogenic (transplant)

presentations.symptoms: body cavity effusions
generalized lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, fevers, cytopenias, hypoalbuminemia, elevated CRP/IL-6
presentations.clues: HIV/AIDS

coverages.note: first-line (roseola, immunocompetent) — self-limited; antipyretics for fever

coverages.medication: Foscarnet 90 mg/kg IV q12h or ganciclovir 5 mg/kg IV q12h
coverages.note: first-line (HHV-6B encephalitis, post-HCT) — ASTCT 2025 guidelines; choice based on risk profile (myelosuppression with ganciclovir vs. nephrotoxicity with foscarnet)
consider (severe/refractory HHV-6B encephalitis) — limited data suggesting improved neurologic outcomes; balance against toxicity

coverages.medication: Routine HHV-6B monitoring/preemptive therapy NOT recommended
coverages.note: ASTCT 2025 — insufficient evidence for efficacy; no established viral load thresholds
first-line (limited cutaneous KS) — immune reconstitution may induce remission; ART alone may suffice for T0 disease
first-line (advanced/T1 Kaposi sarcoma) — NCCN/NIH OI guidelines recommended
alternative (KS, if liposomal doxorubicin unavailable or recurrent) — comparable response rates

coverages.medication: Pomalidomide 5 mg PO daily (days 1–21 of 28-day cycle) + ART + aspirin 81 mg
coverages.note: alternative (KS, FDA-approved for AIDS-related and HIV-negative KS) — 67% response rate in phase 1/2
avoid in KS — associated with KS progression; all formulations including inhaled/topical

organisms.name: Hepatitis D virus (HDV)
organisms.morphology: Smallest known animal virus; single-stranded circular negative-sense RNA (~1.7 kb); requires HBV surface antigen (HBsAg) envelope for assembly and entry — obligate satellite virus of HBV; encodes only one protein (HDAg)
organisms.features: Affects ~12–72 million people worldwide. Only infects persons with HBV (HBsAg-positive). Two patterns: coinfection (simultaneous HDV + HBV acquisition → 95% clear both) vs. superinfection (HDV acquired by chronic HBV carrier → >90% develop chronic HDV). Chronic hepatitis D causes the most severe form of viral hepatitis — faster progression to cirrhosis than HBV alone. 30–70% have cirrhosis at diagnosis. HBV vaccine prevents HDV by preventing HBV. All HBsAg-positive persons should be screened for HDV. Diagnosis: anti-HDV antibody (screening) → HDV RNA (confirmation of active infection).
organisms.source: Parenteral (injection drug use, needlestick), sexual, perinatal (rare); same transmission routes as HBV

presentations.symptoms: acute hepatitis (often biphasic ALT elevation), jaundice, fatigue, nausea; can cause fulminant hepatitis (higher rate than HBV alone)
presentations.clues: usually self-limited; ~95% clear both viruses
presentations.severity: moderate; fulminant hepatitis risk higher than HBV alone

presentations.symptoms: chronic hepatitis, accelerated fibrosis/cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma; may present as unexplained hepatitis flare in chronic HBV carrier
presentations.clues: >90% develop chronic infection; >50% die of liver disease within 10 years of diagnosis if cirrhotic

coverages.medication: Pegylated interferon alfa-2a 180 μg SC weekly × 48 weeks
coverages.note: first-line (chronic hepatitis D) — only ~30% achieve sustained virologic response; significant side effects (fatigue, depression, cytopenias)
first-line (chronic hepatitis D, EU-approved; FDA under review) — entry inhibitor blocking HBV/HDV receptor (NTCP); used alone or with PEG-IFN; well-tolerated; bile acid elevation expected
ineffective against HDV — do not inhibit HDV replication; use only for HBV DNA suppression if indicated
prevention — prevents HBV → prevents HDV; no vaccine for HDV in persons already HBV-infected
first-line (most countries) — 180 μg SC weekly × 48 weeks
first-line (Europe) — 2 mg SC daily

organisms.name: Hepatitis E virus (HEV)
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped (in stool) or quasi-enveloped (in blood), single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; ~27–34 nm; 4 major genotypes infecting humans (GT1–4)
organisms.features: Leading cause of acute viral hepatitis worldwide (~20 million infections/year). GT1/GT2: fecal-oral (contaminated water), developing countries, epidemic; GT3/GT4: zoonotic (undercooked pork/game), developed countries, sporadic. Usually self-limited. Fulminant hepatitis in pregnancy (GT1/GT2) with 20–25% mortality in third trimester. Chronic hepatitis E in immunocompromised (GT3 — transplant recipients, HIV). Diagnosis: anti-HEV IgM (acute), HEV RNA (PCR, preferred in immunocompromised).
organisms.source: GT1/GT2: fecal-oral (contaminated water); GT3/GT4: zoonotic (undercooked pork, deer, wild boar); blood transfusion (GT3); vertical transmission (GT1)

presentations.symptoms: jaundice, malaise, nausea, abdominal pain, hepatomegaly; elevated ALT/AST
presentations.clues: self-limited in 2–6 weeks in immunocompetent
presentations.severity: mild-moderate; fulminant in pregnancy (GT1/GT2, 20–25% mortality in 3rd trimester)

presentations.symptoms: persistent hepatitis, progressive fibrosis/cirrhosis
presentations.clues: GT3 in immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, HIV, hematologic malignancy); defined as HEV RNA positive >3 months

presentations.symptoms: neurologic (GBS, neuralgic amyotrophy, meningoencephalitis), renal (membranoproliferative GN, cryoglobulinemia), hematologic (thrombocytopenia)
presentations.clues: GT3 most common

presentations.symptoms: acute liver failure, coagulopathy, encephalopathy
presentations.clues: HEV1 in third trimester; mortality up to 25%; also high fetal mortality

coverages.note: first-line (acute, immunocompetent) — self-limited; avoid hepatotoxic drugs
first-line (chronic HEV in transplant recipients) — achieves viral clearance in ~30% of cases; reduce tacrolimus/MMF
first-line (chronic HEV, if immunosuppression reduction fails) — 80–85% sustained virologic response; monitor for hemolytic anemia; contraindicated in pregnancy
alternative (chronic HEV, non-transplant) — cannot use in transplant recipients (risk of rejection)
prevention (China only, approved) — GT1; not widely available outside China
first-line (chronic HEV in transplant recipients) — ~30% achieve viral clearance with IS reduction alone
first-line (chronic HEV) — 600–800 mg/day PO × 12 weeks (extend to 24 weeks if needed)

organisms.name: JC virus (JCPyV) — Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML)
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, double-stranded circular DNA virus; icosahedral; ~40 nm; infects oligodendrocytes (causes demyelination) and astrocytes; seroprevalence 50–65% in adults
organisms.features: Primary infection in childhood, usually asymptomatic. Reactivation in immunocompromised → PML, a devastating demyelinating disease of the CNS. Risk factors: HIV/AIDS (CD4 200), natalizumab (anti-VLA-4, used in MS — risk ~1:250 to 1:1000 in JCV antibody-positive patients), rituximab, other immunosuppressants, hematologic malignancies. No effective antiviral exists — treatment depends on restoring immune competence. Diagnosis: MRI (multifocal white matter lesions without mass effect or enhancement), CSF JCV PCR (sensitivity ~75–90%, specificity >95%), brain biopsy if PCR negative. Mortality ~50% at 1 year even with treatment.
organisms.source: Ubiquitous; primary infection via respiratory or fecal-oral route in childhood; reactivation from latent infection in kidneys/lymphoid tissue

presentations.symptoms: progressive focal neurologic deficits: hemiparesis, visual field deficits (homonymous hemianopia), cognitive decline, ataxia, aphasia; NO fever, NO headache (unlike most CNS infections); relentless progression
presentations.clues: MRI: multifocal asymmetric white matter lesions (subcortical U-fibers involved), no mass effect, no contrast enhancement (unless IRIS)
presentations.severity: severe; median survival 6 months without immune reconstitution

coverages.note: first-line (essential) — HIV: initiate/optimize ART (improves survival from 14 to 64 weeks median); natalizumab: discontinue + plasma exchange/immunoadsorption to accelerate drug clearance; other immunosuppressants: discontinue/reduce

coverages.medication: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab 2 mg/kg IV q4–6 weeks)
coverages.note: emerging therapy (non-HIV PML) — ~50–60% 1-year survival; variable response; no predictive biomarkers established; risk of IRIS
emerging therapy — 69% 1-year survival in one study vs. 45% with best supportive care; investigational
for severe IRIS (paradoxical worsening after immune reconstitution) — balance benefit vs. re-immunosuppression risk
NOT routinely recommended — no proven efficacy in clinical studies

investigational/emerging — rationale: block PD-1 on exhausted JCV-specific T cells to reinvigorate antiviral immunity; 1-year survival ~52% in treated patients; immune-related adverse events in ~30%, IRIS in ~19%; not yet standard of care; NIH OI guidelines do not recommend in HIV setting
investigational/emerging — most promising strategy; limited to expert centers; case reports of PML resolution
investigational — stimulates T-cell proliferation including JCV-specific cells; lower IRIS rate (~5.8%) than checkpoint inhibitors
NOT recommended — clinical trials showed no benefit; NIH OI guidelines explicitly recommend against all of these
supportive — corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for severe PML-IRIS with mass effect/edema; balance immunosuppression vs immune reconstitution

organisms.name: BK virus (BKPyV)
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, double-stranded circular DNA virus; icosahedral; ~40–45 nm; closely related to JC virus; seroprevalence ~80% in adults; infects uroepithelial and renal tubular cells
organisms.features: Primary infection in childhood, usually asymptomatic. Reactivation in immunocompromised, especially kidney transplant recipients. BKPyV-associated nephropathy (BKPyVAN) occurs in 3–5% of kidney transplant recipients and can cause graft loss in ~50% if untreated. Viruria (25–35%) → viremia (10–12%) → nephropathy (3–5%). No effective antiviral treatment — management relies on reducing immunosuppression. Screening with plasma BKPyV PCR recommended monthly in first year post-transplant. Diagnosis: plasma BKPyV DNA >10,000 copies/mL (presumptive BKPyVAN); kidney biopsy (gold standard — "decoy cells" in urine, viral cytopathic changes, SV40 immunostain positive).
organisms.source: Ubiquitous; primary infection in childhood (respiratory/fecal-oral); reactivation from latent infection in uroepithelium; donor-derived in transplant

presentations.symptoms: often asymptomatic; rising creatinine, declining graft function; may mimic acute rejection on biopsy
presentations.clues: kidney transplant recipients on tacrolimus + MMF at highest risk
presentations.severity: severe; graft loss in ~50% without intervention

presentations.symptoms: gross hematuria, dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain, bladder clots
presentations.clues: allogeneic HCT recipients; late-onset (>2 weeks post-HCT)

presentations.symptoms: hydronephrosis, rising creatinine
presentations.clues: kidney transplant recipients

coverages.note: first-line (BKPyVAN) — stepwise: reduce tacrolimus trough to 4–6 ng/mL, reduce/discontinue MMF; switch tacrolimus to cyclosporine or add mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus/everolimus); monitor BKPyV viral load q2–4 weeks

coverages.medication: Screening: plasma BKPyV PCR monthly × 9 months post-transplant, then q3 months through first year
coverages.note: recommended (KDIGO, AST) — early detection allows preemptive immunosuppression reduction
adjunctive (refractory BKPyVAN) — limited evidence; may provide neutralizing antibodies

coverages.medication: Cidofovir (low-dose 0.25–1 mg/kg IV q1–2 weeks without probenecid)
coverages.note: adjunctive (refractory) — limited and conflicting evidence; nephrotoxic
adjunctive (refractory) — limited evidence; monitor for hepatotoxicity and myelosuppression
supportive — management centers on immunosuppression reduction and monitoring

first-line (BKPyVAN prevention and treatment) — screen plasma BKPyV DNA monthly × 9 months then every 3 months × 2 years; reduce immunosuppression for sustained viremia >1000 copies/mL or rising to >10,000 copies/mL; common strategy: withdraw antimetabolite (MMF) + reduce CNI dose by 50%
adjunctive (limited evidence) — 0.25–1 mg/kg IV every 1–2 weeks without probenecid; nephrotoxic; AST guidelines do not generally recommend due to lack of RCT evidence
NOT recommended — AST guidelines explicitly state fluoroquinolones are not recommended for prophylaxis or therapy of BKPyV
first-line (HCT setting) — continuous bladder irrigation, pain management; reduce immunosuppression; cidofovir intravesical instillation in refractory cases

organisms.name: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV)
organisms.morphology: Large, enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus; brick-shaped; ~200 × 300 nm; 4 subtypes (MCV-1 most common); replicates in epidermal keratinocytes; produces characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies ("Henderson-Patterson bodies" or "molluscum bodies")
organisms.features: Most common viral skin infection in children (prevalence 5–12%). Spread by direct skin contact, fomites, autoinoculation, and sexual contact (adults — genital lesions). Self-limited in immunocompetent (resolves in 6–12 months, sometimes up to 2–4 years). Extensive/refractory in HIV/immunocompromised. Risk factors: atopic dermatitis, swimming, immunosuppression. Diagnosis: clinical (characteristic umbilicated papules); dermoscopy; biopsy if atypical.
organisms.source: Direct skin-to-skin contact; fomites (towels, pool equipment); autoinoculation; sexual contact (genital lesions in adults)

presentations.symptoms: discrete, dome-shaped, flesh-colored, umbilicated papules (1–5 mm); central waxy core; trunk, axillae, extremities, face (children); genital/inguinal (adults, sexually transmitted); usually painless; eczematous halo ("molluscum dermatitis") common
presentations.clues: self-limited in immunocompetent; can be extensive in HIV (face, >100 lesions)

coverages.note: first-line (immunocompetent, asymptomatic) — self-limited; resolves in 6–12 months; avoids treatment-related pain/scarring
first-line (if treatment desired) — 97.8% clearance; quick; may require topical anesthetic (EMLA); minor scarring risk
first-line (alternative) — effective; may require multiple sessions q2–3 weeks
first-line (alternative, especially pediatric) — painless at application; blistering occurs 24–48h later; wash off in 4–6 hours
first-line (alternative, home-applied) — apply to each lesion daily until inflammation; 74% clearance
FDA-approved (2024, ages ≥1 year) — topical nitric oxide-releasing agent; apply to lesions daily × 12 weeks; favorable safety profile in pediatric patients
alternative — off-label; limited evidence of efficacy; can cause significant local irritation
essential — immune reconstitution leads to resolution
first-line (children, immunocompetent) — self-limited; resolves in 6–12 months; avoids painful treatments
first-line (if treatment desired) — liquid nitrogen to each lesion; repeat every 2–3 weeks
first-line (provider-applied, painless at application) — apply to each lesion, wash off in 2–6 hours; blistering agent; repeat every 2–4 weeks
second-line — limited evidence; imiquimod may be useful for genital lesions
first-line (HIV-associated extensive molluscum) — immune reconstitution leads to resolution

organisms.name: Poliovirus
organisms.type: Virus (RNA virus, Picornaviridae, genus Enterovirus, species Enterovirus C)
organisms.morphology: Non-enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus; icosahedral; ~30 nm; 3 serotypes (PV1, PV2, PV3); PV2 and PV3 wild-type eradicated; PV1 remains endemic in Afghanistan and Pakistan
organisms.features: ~99.5% of infections are asymptomatic or cause minor illness. ~1 in 200 infections leads to irreversible paralysis. Fecal-oral transmission. Near-global eradication achieved through vaccination (OPV and IPV). Vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) outbreaks still occur from OPV reversion. Last US wild poliovirus case: 1979. 2022: VDPV2 case in unvaccinated adult in New York with community transmission detected in wastewater. Diagnosis: stool viral culture (gold standard), RT-PCR; AFP surveillance is cornerstone of polio eradication.
organisms.source: Fecal-oral (primary); respiratory droplets (minor); contaminated water/food; highly contagious

presentations.symptoms: fever, malaise, sore throat, headache, nausea, vomiting
presentations.clues: ~4–8% of infections; self-limited in 2–3 days

presentations.symptoms: fever, headache, stiff neck, back pain, meningismus
presentations.clues: ~1–2% of infections; self-limited

presentations.incubation: 7–21 days (biphasic: minor illness → brief improvement → paralysis)
presentations.symptoms: asymmetric acute flaccid paralysis (lower limbs > upper); fasciculations; absent reflexes; sensation preserved; bulbar involvement (dysphagia, dysarthria, respiratory failure) in 5–35% of paralytic cases
presentations.clues: 1 in 200 infections; 5–10% mortality when respiratory muscles involved

presentations.symptoms: new progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, muscle atrophy, pain in previously affected and unaffected limbs
presentations.clues: 25–40% of paralytic polio survivors

presentations.symptoms: fever, malaise, sore throat, headache, nausea
presentations.clues: ~5% of infections; self-limited

presentations.symptoms: fever, headache, stiff neck; CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis
presentations.clues: ~1–2% of infections

coverages.note: supportive — bed rest, analgesics, physical therapy, respiratory support (mechanical ventilation if bulbar/respiratory involvement); orthopedic management for residual paralysis
prevention (standard in US and most developed countries) — 4 doses: 2, 4, 6–18 months, 4–6 years; >99% seroconversion after 3 doses
prevention (used in eradication campaigns, developing countries) — live attenuated; risk of VDPV reversion (~1 in 2.7 million doses); novel OPV2 (nOPV2) developed with reduced reversion risk
recommended if traveling to endemic/outbreak areas — IPV series (0, 1–2 months, 6–12 months)
supportive — respiratory support, physical therapy, pain management
prevention (cVDPV2 outbreaks) — genetically stabilized to reduce reversion risk; WHO Emergency Use Listing

organisms.name: Hantavirus (Sin Nombre virus, Andes virus, and others)
organisms.morphology: Enveloped, single-stranded negative-sense RNA virus (tri-segmented genome: L, M, S); spherical; ~80–120 nm; multiple species with distinct geographic distributions and rodent hosts
organisms.features: Two major clinical syndromes: hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS, Americas — Sin Nombre virus, Andes virus) and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS, Europe/Asia — Hantaan, Seoul, Puumala viruses). HCPS mortality 35–40%. Transmitted by inhalation of aerosolized rodent excreta. Andes virus is unique — person-to-person transmission documented. Diagnosis: serology (IgM/IgG by ELISA — nearly all patients seropositive at presentation), RT-PCR; classic lab triad: thrombocytopenia + leukocytosis with left shift (immunoblasts) + hemoconcentration.
organisms.source: Inhalation of aerosolized urine/droppings/saliva from infected rodents (deer mouse for SNV in US); NOT typically person-to-person (except Andes virus); rural/peridomestic exposure; cleaning rodent-infested areas

presentations.symptoms: prodrome (2–7 days): fever, myalgia, headache, GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain — can mimic acute abdomen) → cardiopulmonary phase: abrupt onset cough, dyspnea, tachycardia → rapid progression to non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, cardiogenic shock within hours
presentations.clues: thrombocytopenia + hemoconcentration + immunoblasts on smear; bilateral infiltrates on CXR
presentations.severity: very severe; 35–40% mortality; most deaths within 24–48h of cardiopulmonary phase onset

presentations.symptoms: five phases: febrile → hypotensive → oliguric (AKI, hemorrhage) → diuric → convalescent; headache, back pain, abdominal pain, petechiae, conjunctival injection
presentations.clues: Hantaan virus (severe, 5–15% mortality), Puumala virus (mild "nephropathia epidemica," 1% mortality)

presentations.symptoms: prodrome (3–5 days): fever, myalgia, headache, GI symptoms → cardiopulmonary phase: abrupt onset noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, bilateral infiltrates, respiratory failure, cardiogenic shock (myocardial depression); thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, left shift
presentations.clues: Sin Nombre virus (Americas); mortality 35–40%; survivors recover rapidly

coverages.note: first-line (HCPS) — early ICU admission essential; careful fluid management (AVOID aggressive IV fluids — worsens pulmonary edema); vasopressors (norepinephrine) for shock; mechanical ventilation
rescue therapy (refractory cardiogenic shock/respiratory failure) — may improve survival in severe HCPS; initiate early if available
NOT recommended for HCPS — no proven benefit (RCT showed no survival advantage); may have role in HFRS (limited data)
avoid in HCPS — worsens pulmonary edema due to capillary leak; use judicious fluid management with vasopressors

coverages.medication: No vaccine or specific antiviral available
coverages.note: prevention relies on rodent control, avoiding exposure to rodent excreta, proper cleaning of rodent-infested areas (wet down with bleach solution before sweeping)

first-line (HPS) — early ICU admission; careful fluid management (avoid overhydration — worsens pulmonary edema); vasopressors for shock; mechanical ventilation; ECMO for refractory cardiogenic shock (survival benefit reported)

coverages.medication: No specific antiviral therapy for HPS
coverages.note: supportive — no vaccine available; prevention: rodent control, avoid sweeping/vacuuming rodent-contaminated areas (wet-clean instead), seal entry points
